# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define gas welding. List any three commonly used fuel gases in the shipbuilding industry.**

**Model Answer:**

Gas welding is a fusion-type joining process in which the heat required for melting the parent metal is obtained from the combustion of a fuel gas with oxygen. The molten metal fuses to form a joint upon solidification.

**Three commonly used fuel gases in shipbuilding:**
1. **Acetylene (C₂H₂)** — primary fuel gas for welding operations.
2. **Propane / LPG (C₃H₈ / C₄H₁₀)** — used for flame cutting of plates.
3. **Natural Gas (CH₄)** — used for heating and pre-heating in heavy fabrication.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Trap (3-mark question):** Many students write *air* as an oxidizer instead of *oxygen*. While air-acetylene welding exists, in KTU's industry-oriented shipbuilding syllabus, *pure industrial oxygen* is the assumed oxidizer. Lose 1 mark if you say "burns with air."

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Q: Distinguish between a neutral flame and an oxidizing flame in oxy-acetylene welding.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Neutral Flame | Oxidizing Flame |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| O₂ : C₂H₂ ratio | 1 : 1 to 1.2 : 1 | > 1.5 : 1 |
| Appearance of inner cone | Clean, sharp, brilliant white | Short, pointed, bluish |
| Sound | Soft, steady | Hissing |
| Excess gas | None — balanced | Excess oxygen |
| Effect on weld pool | Clean, sound weld | Causes oxidation, porosity, brittleness |
| Industrial use | Mild steel, ship plate welding | Brass, bronze welding only |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### 📋 **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**Q: (a) Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of an oxy-acetylene welding torch. (7 Marks)**

**(b) The volumetric analysis of a fuel gas shows 60 % methane, 25 % ethane, 10 % propane and 5 % butane by volume. Calculate the stoichiometric oxygen required for complete combustion of 1 m³ of this gas mixture. (7 Marks)**

#### **Part (a) — Model Answer (7 Marks)**

The oxy-acetylene welding torch consists of the following parts:

1. **Handle (1 mark):** Holds the oxygen and acetylene valves, usually made of brass for heat conductivity.

2. **Oxygen valve (0.5 mark):** Needle-type valve controlling oxygen flow into the mixing chamber.

3. **Acetylene valve (0.5 mark):** Needle-type valve controlling fuel gas flow.

4. **Mixing chamber (1.5 marks):** The acetylene is drawn into the oxygen stream by *injector action*. The high-velocity oxygen jet creates a low-pressure zone (Bernoulli's principle) that entrains the acetylene. Proper mixing ensures stable combustion.

5. **Nozzle / tip (1 mark):** Made of copper for high thermal conductivity. The orifice diameter determines the size of the flame (numbered from 1 to 10 for varying plate thickness).

6. **Tip connections (0.5 mark):** Threaded or push-fit connections to allow nozzle replacement.

**Working principle (1.5 marks):** Oxygen enters through its valve at high velocity → creates suction in the mixing chamber → acetylene is drawn in → mixture flows through nozzle → ignited at the tip → produces a focused flame of ~3160 °C used for welding.

**Diagram (0.5 mark):** Use the block diagram from Section 4.2.

#### **Part (b) — Model Answer (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Write combustion equations for each constituent (1 mark):**
$$CH_4 + 2O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2H_2O$$
$$C_2H_6 + \tfrac{7}{2}O_2 \rightarrow 2CO_2 + 3H_2O$$
$$C_3H_8 + 5O_2 \rightarrow 3CO_2 + 4H_2O$$
$$C_4H_{10} + \tfrac{13}{2}O_2 \rightarrow 4CO_2 + 5H_2O$$

**Step 2 — Tabulate volumes and stoichiometric O₂ per m³ (2 marks):**

| Component | Volume in 1 m³ (m³) | O₂ Required (m³) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| CH₄ (60 %) | 0.60 | $0.60 \times 2 = 1.200$ |
| C₂H₆ (25 %) | 0.25 | $0.25 \times 3.5 = 0.875$ |
| C₃H₈ (10 %) | 0.10 | $0.10 \times 5 = 0.500$ |
| C₄H₁₀ (5 %) | 0.05 | $0.05 \times 6.5 = 0.325$ |

**Step 3 — Sum total oxygen requirement (1 mark):**
$$V_{O_2,total} = 1.200 + 0.875 + 0.500 + 0.325 = 2.900 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Convert to air equivalent (optional, 1 mark):** Since air contains 21 % O₂ by volume:
$$V_{air} = \dfrac{2.900}{0.21} = 13.81 \text{ m}^3$$

$$\boxed{V_{O_2} = 2.9 \text{ m}^3 \text{ per m}^3 \text{ of fuel gas mixture}}$$

---

### 📋 **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]`

**Q: (a) Describe the three types of oxy-acetylene flames with their applications. (7 Marks)**

**(b) A shipyard uses oxy-acetylene welding on a 5 mm mild steel plate. Acetylene flow rate is 75 cm³/s, calorific value is 48,000 kJ/m³ and process efficiency is 55 %. If the welding speed is 1.5 mm/s, calculate the heat input per unit length of the weld in kJ/mm. (7 Marks)**

#### **Part (a) — Model Answer (7 Marks)**

**1. Carburizing (Reducing) Flame (2 marks):**
- **O₂ : C₂H₂ ratio:** < 1 : 1 (typically 0.8 : 1 to 0.95 : 1)
- **Appearance:** Inner cone is long, feathery, with a reddish-white tip; an *acetylene feather* extends beyond the inner cone.
- **Combustion products:** Excess unburnt carbon and hydrogen.
- **Atmosphere:** Reducing (carbon-rich).
- **Applications:** Carburizing steel, hard-facing, surfacing of wear-resistant alloys, welding of high-carbon steels.

**2. Neutral Flame (3 marks):**
- **O₂ : C₂H₂ ratio:** 1 : 1 to 1.2 : 1.
- **Appearance:** Inner cone is clean, sharp, brilliant white; *no feather*; outer envelope is light blue.
- **Combustion products:** Only CO₂ and H₂O; no free carbon, no excess O₂.
- **Atmosphere:** Chemically balanced.
- **Applications:** Welding of mild steel, low-carbon steel, stainless steel, cast iron (with flux), general ship plate fabrication — the **most commonly used flame in shipbuilding**.

**3. Oxidizing Flame (2 marks):**
- **O₂ : C₂H₂ ratio:** > 1.5 : 1 (typically 1.5 : 1 to 2 : 1).
- **Appearance:** Inner cone is short, pointed, bluish-white; the flame makes a hissing sound.
- **Combustion products:** Excess O₂ beyond the weld pool.
- **Atmosphere:** Strongly oxidizing.
- **Applications:** Welding of copper, brass, bronze (non-ferrous metals where oxide film must be removed); fusion welding of zinc and manganese alloys.

#### **Part (b) — Model Answer (7 Marks)**

**Given data:**
- Acetylene flow rate: $V_f = 75 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s} = 75 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- Calorific value: $Q = 48{,}000 \text{ kJ/m}^3$
- Process efficiency: $\eta = 0.55$
- Travel speed: $v = 1.5 \text{ mm/s}$

**Step 1 — Energy supplied per second (2 marks):**
$$\dot{Q}_{fuel} = V_f \times Q = 75 \times 10^{-6} \times 48{,}000$$
$$\dot{Q}_{fuel} = 3.6 \text{ kJ/s}$$

**Step 2 — Useful heat transferred to workpiece (2 marks):**
$$\dot{Q}_{useful} = \eta \times \dot{Q}_{fuel} = 0.55 \times 3.6$$
$$\dot{Q}_{useful} = 1.98 \text{ kJ/s}$$

**Step 3 — Heat input per unit length (3 marks):**
$$H = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{useful}}{v} = \dfrac{1.98}{1.5}$$
$$H = 1.32 \text{ kJ/mm}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Heat input per unit length} = 1.32 \text{ kJ/mm} = 1320 \text{ J/mm}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes on Heat-Input Numericals:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch trap (lose 1 mark):** $V_f$ must be converted from cm³/s to m³/s before multiplying with kJ/m³.
> 2. **Efficiency missing (lose 1 mark):** Many students forget the $\eta$ factor. Always check whether the question asks for *heat supplied* or *heat input to workpiece* — they differ by factor $\eta$.
> 3. **Sign convention trap (lose 0.5 mark):** Heat input is always expressed as a positive value in J/mm or kJ/mm; do not write negative values.

---

## 5.3 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise these before walking into the KTU exam hall:**

- ☐ Gas welding = **OFW (ISO 4063, Group 3)** fusion process using fuel-gas + oxygen combustion.
- ☐ Most common variant = **Oxy-Acetylene Welding (OAW)**, ISO code **3.1**.
- ☐ Stoichiometric ratio O₂ : C₂H₂ = **2.5 : 1** (theoretical, complete combustion).
- ☐ Neutral flame ratio = **1 : 1 to 1.2 : 1** (practical torch setting — primary cone only).
- ☐ Acetylene flame temperature (actual) = **3160 °C** — highest among all fuel gases.
- ☐ Three flame types = **Neutral, Carburizing (reducing), Oxidizing** — memorise O₂ ratio, appearance, and application for each.
- ☐ Cylinder colour coding (Indian/IS standard): Oxygen = **black body**, Acetylene = **maroon body**.
- ☐ Acetylene hose = **red colour**, Oxygen hose = **green/blue colour** — never interchange.
- ☐ Flashback arrestor = mandatory safety device on both fuel and oxygen lines.
- ☐ Acetylene cylinder pressure ≈ **1.5 MPa** (acetylene is dissolved in acetone for safe storage).
- ☐ Oxygen cylinder pressure ≈ **14 MPa**.
- ☐ Two-stage regulator = drops cylinder pressure (high) → working pressure (low, ~7–100 kPa).
- ☐ Heat input formula $H = \dfrac{\eta V_f Q}{v}$ — units = **kJ/mm** or **J/mm**.
- ☐ Shipbuilding use cases = plate cutting, tack welding, thin-gauge fabrication, repair welds, brazing.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Gas Welding and Fuel Gases**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: GMAW Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST831 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** GMAW Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW)** is defined by the American Welding Society (AWS) as an *arc welding process* that produces coalescence of metals by heating them with an **arc** established between a **continuous, consumable filler metal electrode** and the workpiece. The process uses a **shielding gas** (externally supplied) to protect the molten weld pool and the arc zone from atmospheric contamination. 

GMAW is industrially known by two sub-designations:
- **MIG (Metal Inert Gas) Welding** – when an inert gas (e.g., Argon, Helium) is used.
- **MAG (Metal Active Gas) Welding** – when an active gas (e.g., CO₂ or Ar/CO₂ mixtures) is used.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Emphasis:** In shipbuilding (Course: OENST831), GMAW is treated as the *workhorse process* for fabricating mild steel and high-tensile steel hull panels, superstructures, and stiffened plate assemblies, because of its high deposition rate and amenability to **mechanization/automation** on panel lines.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to glue two pieces of metal together with a controlled, hot liquid glue that melts from a *continuous metal straw* while an invisible blanket of protective gas covers the joint. As you move the "straw" along the seam, the wire inside melts drop by drop into the joint, the gas blanket keeps the air (oxygen, nitrogen) from spoiling the hot metal, and the joint solidifies into a strong bond. 

- The **wire** = the melting glue.
- The **arc** = the high-temperature torch.
- The **shielding gas** = the protective blanket.
- The **welding gun** = the hand guiding the straw.

This continuous, mechanized "metal glue gun" is exactly what GMAW does on a shipyard panel line, often mounted on a carriage or robotic arm that travels along the plate seam.

## 1.3 Core Operational Metrics

The principal measurable quantities governing GMAW performance are:

| Parameter | Typical Range (Shipbuilding Steel) | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Welding Current ($I$)** | 60 – 500 | A |
| **Arc Voltage ($V$)** | 18 – 32 | V |
| **Wire Feed Speed (WFS)** | 2 – 18 | m/min |
| **Shielding Gas Flow Rate** | 12 – 20 | L/min |
| **Deposition Rate** | 1 – 8 | kg/h |
| **Travel Speed ($S$)** | 0.2 – 1.0 | m/min |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Used in GMAW Calculations:**
> - **Arc Efficiency ($\eta$):** 0.85 – 0.95 (i.e., **85–95%** of electrical energy reaches the workpiece).
> - **Heat Input ($H$):** measured in **kJ/mm**, a key metric for shipbuilding quality control (typical hull plate limit: $\le 3.5 \text{ kJ/mm}$ per pass for Class societies).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Process Architecture — The "Why" Behind Each Subsystem

GMAW is an integrated electromechanical system. To understand *why* each component exists, consider the operational demands:
1. A **stable, sustained arc** must be maintained → requires a *constant-voltage (CV)* power source.
2. The filler metal must be fed *continuously* at a rate matching melt-off → requires a *wire feeder* and *drive rolls*.
3. The molten pool must be isolated from ambient air → requires a *shielding gas* delivered through the nozzle.
4. Heat must be regulated to avoid burn-through on thin ship plates or lack of fusion on thick plates → requires *synergic control* of voltage and WFS.

### 2.1.1 Power Source Characteristics
- **Type:** Constant Voltage (CV) / Flat characteristic DC power source.
- **Polarity:** **DCEP (Direct Current Electrode Positive)** is standard for GMAW of steel. This produces deep penetration (~60–70% of plate thickness in a single pass on 12 mm plate).

### 2.1.2 Wire Feeding System
- Consists of drive rolls, a conduit liner, and a contact tip.
- **Constant Wire Feed Speed (CWFS)** mode is standard: the operator sets WFS, and the arc self-adjusts voltage to maintain a stable arc length (the arc is essentially a self-regulating "servo").

### 2.1.3 Shielding Gas Delivery
- Shielding gas is metered through a **flowmeter (L/min)**.
- Gases used in shipbuilding GMAW:
  - **Ar + 15–25% CO₂** (most common for mild steel hull plate).
  - **100% CO₂** (low cost; produces more spatter, rarely used for Class-approved welds).
  - **Ar + 2–5% O₂** (improves arc stability on galvanized or thin-gauge panels).

## 2.2 Modes of Metal Transfer — A Critical KTU Concept

The four transfer modes are the *heart* of GMAW theory and are heavily tested:

| Transfer Mode | Current Range | Wire Diameter | Shielding Gas | Characteristic | Shipbuilding Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Short-Circuit Transfer (SCT)** | 60–200 A | 0.6–1.2 mm | Ar/CO₂ or CO₂ | Low heat, small puddle, frequent short-circuits | Thin plates (< 3 mm), root pass on pipe |
| **Globular Transfer** | 200–250 A | 1.2–1.6 mm | CO₂-rich | Large droplets, gravity-fed across arc | Rare in modern shipyards |
| **Spray Transfer** | 250–500 A | 1.0–1.6 mm | High Ar | Axial droplet stream, no short-circuit | Thick hull plate (≥ 6 mm) fillet and butt welds |
| **Pulsed Spray Transfer** | Peak 300–500 A, Background 50–100 A | 1.0–1.2 mm | Ar/CO₂ | One droplet per pulse | All-position welding, aluminium superstructures |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for Transfer Modes:** Think of water dripping from a tap. *Short-circuit* = the drop touches the sink (workpiece) before falling (low energy). *Globular* = big drops fall chaotically. *Spray* = high pressure turns dripping into a fine shower (high energy, high heat). *Pulsed* = you turn the tap on-and-off rhythmically to get exactly one drop per pulse.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All symbols and units — memorize this table for ESE.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Heat Input ($H$)** | $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ | $\eta$ = arc efficiency (decimal); $V$ = arc voltage (V); $I$ = current (A); $S$ = travel speed (mm/min) | kJ/mm |
| 2 | **Deposition Rate (DR)** | $DR = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^{2} \cdot \text{WFS} \cdot \rho}{4 \cdot 10^{6}}$ | $d$ = wire diameter (mm); WFS in m/min; $\rho$ = wire density (kg/m³, steel ≈ **7850**) | kg/min |
| 3 | **Melting Rate (MR)** | $MR = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot \eta}{H_{m}}$ | $H_{m}$ = latent + sensible heat of melting (J/kg) | kg/s |
| 4 | **Penetration Depth (approx., DCEP)** | $p \approx 0.1 \cdot I$ (for $I$ in A, $d$ ≈ 1.2 mm) | Empirical | mm |
| 5 | **Shielding Gas Cost** | $C = Q \cdot t \cdot P$ | $Q$ = flow (m³/min), $t$ = weld time (min), $P$ = unit price | currency |

> **Critical:** When writing $\vert x \vert$ in any table cell, use `\vert x \vert` to avoid breaking markdown.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

In shipbuilding, the heat input $H$ is **regulated by Classification Societies** (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS, IRS) because excessive $H$ causes:
- **HAZ (Heat Affected Zone) softening** in TMCP steel hull plates.
- **Distortion** of large panel assemblies.
- **Reduced Charpy impact toughness** in service.

GMAW with **pulsed transfer** is increasingly deployed in the construction of **LNG carrier cargo tanks** and **naval warship hulls** to maintain $H$ in the **1.0–2.0 kJ/mm** band.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Heat Input Equation (Line-by-Line)

The most-tested formula in shipbuilding welding papers. Let us derive it from first principles so it cannot be forgotten.

**Step 1 — Electrical Power Supplied to the Arc**

The arc draws current $I$ at voltage $V$. By Ohm's law for DC, the electrical power delivered to the arc is:

$$
P_{\text{arc}} = V \cdot I \quad [\text{Watts} = \text{Joules/second}]
$$

**Step 2 — Effective Heat Reaching the Workpiece (Efficiency Factor)**

Not all electrical energy becomes useful heat at the workpiece. A fraction $\eta$ (the arc thermal efficiency) is transferred. For GMAW on steel, **$\eta = 0.85$**:

$$
P_{\text{useful}} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \quad [\text{W}]
$$

**Step 3 — Energy per Unit Length of Weld**

As the torch travels at speed $S$ (in mm/min), a unit length of weld (1 mm) takes $60/S$ minutes, or $1/S$ minutes-per-mm. So time per mm of weld is:

$$
t_{\text{per mm}} = \dfrac{60}{S} \quad [\text{seconds per mm}]
$$

**Step 4 — Total Energy per mm of Weld**

Multiplying useful power by the time per mm of travel:

$$
E_{\text{per mm}} = P_{\text{useful}} \cdot t_{\text{per mm}} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \dfrac{60}{S} \quad [\text{J/mm}]
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to kJ/mm (the standard shipbuilding unit)**

Since $1 \text{ kJ} = 1000 \text{ J}$, divide by 1000:

$$
\boxed{H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} \quad [\text{kJ/mm}]}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic:** "**H**eat = **V**olts **I** **S**low, divide by **η**-squared-sounds-funny." Real mnemonic: $H = \dfrac{\eta V I}{S} \cdot 0.06$ (combining the 60/1000 factor).

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Full Solution)

**Problem:** A shipyard welder is performing a fillet weld on 10 mm mild steel hull plate using GMAW-spray transfer. The recorded parameters are:
- Arc voltage $V = 28$ V
- Welding current $I = 300$ A
- Travel speed $S = 400$ mm/min
- Arc efficiency $\eta = 0.90$

**Calculate:**
1. The heat input per unit length.
2. Whether it conforms to the Class rule of $H \le 3.5$ kJ/mm.
3. The deposition rate if 1.2 mm mild steel wire is fed at 8 m/min.

---

### 3.2.1 Part (a) — Heat Input

**Substitute into the formula:**

$$
H = \dfrac{0.90 \times 28 \times 300 \times 60}{400 \times 1000}
$$

**Step-by-step arithmetic:**

$$
H = \dfrac{0.90 \times 28 \times 300 \times 60}{400{,}000}
$$

**Numerator (step by step):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.90 \times 28 &= 25.2 \\
25.2 \times 300 &= 7560 \\
7560 \times 60 &= 453{,}600
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final division:**

$$
H = \dfrac{453{,}600}{400{,}000} = 1.134 \text{ kJ/mm}
$$

**Conclusion:** $H = 1.134$ kJ/mm, which is **well below the 3.5 kJ/mm limit**. The weld is **Class-compliant**. ✔️

### 3.2.2 Part (b) — Deposition Rate

Using the formula from Section 2.3, item 2:

$$
DR = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^{2} \cdot \text{WFS} \cdot \rho}{4 \cdot 10^{6}}
$$

**Substituting $d = 1.2$ mm, WFS $= 8$ m/min, $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³:**

$$
DR = \dfrac{\pi \times (1.2)^{2} \times 8 \times 7850}{4 \times 10^{6}}
$$

**Step-by-step:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1.2)^{2} &= 1.44 \\
\pi \times 1.44 &= 4.5239 \\
4.5239 \times 8 &= 36.191 \\
36.191 \times 7850 &= 284{,}099.7 \\
\dfrac{284{,}099.7}{4 \times 10^{6}} &= 0.0710 \text{ kg/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Convert to kg/h:**

$$
0.0710 \times 60 = 4.26 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Conclusion:** The deposition rate is **≈ 4.26 kg/h** — typical for spray transfer on 1.2 mm wire at 300 A. This matches standard shipyard productivity benchmarks for mechanized fillet welding.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — GMAW Parameter Trade-offs

| Increase → | Heat Input | Penetration | Spatter | Distortion | Weld Quality |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| ↑ Current ($I$) | ↑ | ↑↑ | ↑ (globular) | ↑ | Risk of burn-through on thin plate |
| ↑ Voltage ($V$) | ↑ | ↑ slightly | ↑ (if too high) | ↑ | Wider bead, flatter profile |
| ↑ Travel Speed ($S$) | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ | Risk of lack of fusion if too fast |
| ↑ Wire Diameter ($d$) | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | Higher deposition |
| ↑ Ar in shielding gas | ↓ (mildly) | — | ↓↓ | — | Smoother bead, better for stainless |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 GMAW Process Block Diagram (Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the functional interaction between subsystems of a typical GMAW installation as used in a shipyard panel line.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PS[Power Source CV DC] --> GUN[Welding Gun]
    WF[Wire Feeder Drive Rolls] --> GUN
    GUN --> ARC[Electric Arc 28V 300A]
    ARC --> WP[Workpiece Hull Plate]
    SGC[Shielding Gas Cylinder Ar plus CO2] --> REG[Pressure Regulator]
    REG --> FM[Flowmeter L per min]
    FM --> GUN
    GUN --> SHIELD[Gas Nozzle Shield Envelope]
    SHIELD --> WP
    WP --> CABLE[Work Lead Return Cable]
    CABLE --> PS
    WP --> TRAV[Travel Mechanism Tractor or Robot]
    TRAV --> WP
    CTRL[Synergic Control Unit] --> PS
    CTRL --> WF
```

**Reading the diagram:** The Power Source, Wire Feeder, and Travel Mechanism are all coordinated by the Synergic Control Unit. The shielding gas flows from cylinder → regulator → flowmeter → nozzle → envelopes the arc → protects the weld pool. The arc current returns through the work lead cable to complete the electrical circuit.

## 4.2 Metal Transfer Mode Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Choose Transfer Mode] --> B{Plate Thickness}
    B -->|< 3 mm| C[Short Circuit Transfer SCT]
    B -->|3 to 6 mm| D{Position}
    B -->|> 6 mm| E{Position}
    D -->|Flat or Horizontal| F[Spray Transfer]
    D -->|Vertical or Overhead| G[Pulsed Spray Transfer]
    E -->|Flat or Horizontal| F
    E -->|Vertical or Overhead| G
    C --> H[Use 0.8 to 1.0 mm wire and Ar plus 25 percent CO2]
    F --> I[Use 1.2 to 1.6 mm wire and Ar plus 15 to 20 percent CO2]
    G --> J[Use 1.0 to 1.2 mm wire and Ar plus CO2 mixture]
```

## 4.3 GMAW Equipment Layout in a Shipyard Panel Line

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Line1[Hull Panel Production Line]
        A1[Plate Edge Preparation] --> A2[GMAW Tractor on Rail]
        A2 --> A3[Fillet Weld 1 Stiffener to Plate]
        A3 --> A4[GMAW Robotic Gantry]
        A4 --> A5[Butt Weld 2 Panels Together]
        A5 --> A6[NDT and Visual Inspection]
    end
    GAS[Central Shielding Gas Manifold] --> A2
    GAS --> A4
    POW[Multiple CV Power Sources 400A each] --> A2
    POW --> A4
    OP[Welder or Operator Console] --> A2
    OP --> A4
```

**Interpretation:** A modern shipyard GMAW cell is a *distributed* system: central gas manifold supplies multiple welding stations, each station has its own power source, and the operator console allows synergic parameter selection. Robotic gantries enable **automated butt welding** of large panels — a hallmark of GMAW in modern shipbuilding.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between MIG and MAG welding processes. Give one example of shielding gas for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | MIG (Metal Inert Gas) | MAG (Metal Active Gas) |
|---|---|---|
| Shielding gas type | Inert (chemically non-reactive) | Active (chemically reactive with weld pool) |
| Typical gas | Argon (Ar), Helium (He) | CO₂, Ar + CO₂, Ar + O₂ |
| Arc stability | High | Moderate (with CO₂) |
| Spatter level | Low | Higher (especially with pure CO₂) |
| Weld bead appearance | Smooth, clean | Slightly rougher, deeper penetration |
| Typical shipbuilding use | Aluminium superstructures, stainless | Mild-steel hull plate |

**(1 mark) Example:** MIG → Argon for aluminium alloy deckhouse welding.  
**(1 mark) Example:** MAG → Ar + 20% CO₂ for mild-steel hull plate fillet welds.  
**(1 mark)** Clear differentiation statement.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**List the four modes of metal transfer in GMAW and state the current range for short-circuit and spray transfer.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Short-Circuit Transfer (SCT)** — current range **60–200 A** (1 mark)
2. **Globular Transfer** — 200–250 A (½ mark)
3. **Spray Transfer** — current range **250–500 A** (1 mark)
4. **Pulsed Spray Transfer** — peak 300–500 A, background 50–100 A (½ mark)

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> Each sub-question carries **7 marks**. Total **14 marks per question**.

---

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **GMAW process** and label all major components. Discuss the role of **shielding gas** in GMAW. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A shipyard welder uses GMAW-spray transfer for a butt joint in 12 mm hull plate. The parameters are: $V = 30$ V, $I = 320$ A, $S = 350$ mm/min, $\eta = 0.88$. Calculate the **heat input** and comment on whether it satisfies the Class rule $H \le 3.5$ kJ/mm. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A(a)

**1. Sketch (3 marks):** (Refer to the GMAW block diagram in Section 4.1; describe it in the answer sheet with neat labels: Power Source, Wire Feeder, Welding Gun, Contact Tip, Nozzle, Shielding Gas Inlet, Workpiece, Work Clamp, Return Cable.)

**2. Major components (2 marks):**
- Constant-voltage DC power source (DCEP).
- Wire feeder with drive rolls and conduit.
- Welding gun (contact tip + nozzle).
- Shielding gas cylinder, regulator, flowmeter.
- Work clamp and return cable.

**3. Role of shielding gas (2 marks):**
- Displaces atmospheric O₂ and N₂ from the arc zone.
- Stabilizes the arc plasma.
- Controls the weld metal transfer mode (e.g., Ar-rich → spray transfer; CO₂-rich → globular).
- Affects weld bead shape, spatter level, and mechanical properties.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A(b)

**Given:** $V = 30$ V, $I = 320$ A, $S = 350$ mm/min, $\eta = 0.88$.

**Step 1 — Substitute into heat input formula:**

$$
H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}
$$

$$
H = \dfrac{0.88 \times 30 \times 320 \times 60}{350 \times 1000}
$$

**Step 2 — Numerator (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.88 \times 30 &= 26.4 \\
26.4 \times 320 &= 8448 \\
8448 \times 60 &= 506{,}880
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Denominator:** $350 \times 1000 = 350{,}000$ (½ mark)

**Step 4 — Final division (1 mark):**

$$
H = \dfrac{506{,}880}{350{,}000} = 1.448 \text{ kJ/mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Compliance check (1.5 marks):**

Since $H = 1.448$ kJ/mm **$< 3.5$ kJ/mm**, the weld is **Class-compliant**. ✔️

**Conclusion (½ mark):** Heat input is well within limits; the procedure is acceptable for Class-approved hull plate butt welding.

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Compare the **four modes of metal transfer** in GMAW with respect to current range, wire diameter, shielding gas, bead characteristics, and typical shipbuilding applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A GMAW operation on shipboard uses 1.0 mm mild-steel wire fed at 6 m/min. Calculate the **deposition rate** in kg/h. Take $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B(a)

**Tabular comparison (5 marks):** (Reproduce the table from Section 2.2 with full headings and at least 2 examples in each column.)

**Key discussion points (2 marks):**
- Short-circuit transfer → low heat input → ideal for thin plate and root pass.
- Spray transfer → high deposition → ideal for thick plate fillet and butt welds.
- Pulsed spray transfer → combines low heat with high-quality spray beads → ideal for all-position welding and aluminium superstructures.
- Globular transfer → largely obsolete in modern shipbuilding due to spatter.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B(b)

**Given:** $d = 1.0$ mm, WFS $= 6$ m/min, $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³.

**Step 1 — Formula:**

$$
DR = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^{2} \cdot \text{WFS} \cdot \rho}{4 \cdot 10^{6}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
DR = \dfrac{\pi \times (1.0)^{2} \times 6 \times 7850}{4 \times 10^{6}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerator (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi \times 1.0 &= 3.1416 \\
3.1416 \times 6 &= 18.8496 \\
18.8496 \times 7850 &= 147{,}969.3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Divide by $4 \times 10^6$ (2 marks):**

$$
DR = \dfrac{147{,}969.3}{4{,}000{,}000} = 0.03699 \text{ kg/min}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to kg/h (1 mark):**

$$
DR = 0.03699 \times 60 = 2.22 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The deposition rate is **2.22 kg/h**, suitable for thin-plate and root-pass applications in shipbuilding.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student mistakes that cost marks in GMAW problems:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in heat input:** Forgetting to convert $S$ from mm/min into consistent units. Always use $S$ in **mm/min** and the **60/1000** combined factor — or use $S$ in mm/s and omit the 60. Mixing units = **2 marks lost instantly.**
> 2. **Confusing MIG vs. MAG:** Writing "CO₂ is used in MIG" — this is **wrong**. CO₂ is active; MIG uses inert gases only. Examiners deduct **1–2 marks.**
> 3. **Omitting $\eta$:** Using $H = VI/S$ without efficiency. GMAW **requires** $\eta \approx 0.85$–$0.95$. Marks lost: **1.**
> 4. **Wire density:** Using 7800 instead of 7850 is acceptable; using 7000 (aluminium density for steel) = **wrong, lose 1 mark.**
> 5. **Penetration formula:** The empirical $p \approx 0.1I$ is for **DCEP on steel with 1.2 mm wire**. Stating it as a universal law = **lose 1 mark.**
> 6. **Sketch quality:** In Q.A(a), examiners allocate **3 of 7 marks** to the sketch. A missing arrow on gas flow or unlabeled contact tip = **–1 to –2 marks.**

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **GMAW = Gas Metal Arc Welding**; subtypes **MIG (inert)** and **MAG (active)**. **(High frequency)**
- **Power source:** Constant Voltage (CV) DC, **DCEP polarity** for steel. **(Must know)**
- **Four transfer modes:** Short-circuit, Globular, Spray, Pulsed Spray. **(Most-tested)**
- **Heat input formula:** $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ kJ/mm. **(Formula of the module)**
- **Deposition rate formula:** $DR = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^{2} \cdot \text{WFS} \cdot \rho}{4 \cdot 10^{6}}$ kg/min. **(Most-tested formula #2)**
- **Arc efficiency of GMAW:** **0.85–0.95** (use **0.90** if not specified).
- **Class rule heat input limit:** $H \le 3.5$ kJ/mm for most shipbuilding steels.
- **Common shielding gas for mild steel:** **Ar + 15–25% CO₂**.
- **Wire polarity convention:** Electrode Positive (DCEP) → **deep penetration**, used for butt welds.
- **Self-regulating arc:** GMAW with constant wire feed speed naturally maintains arc length; voltage sets bead width.
- **Shipbuilding applications:** Hull plating, stiffener fillet welds, bulkhead seams, pipe spool welding, superstructure panels.
- **Advantages for shipbuilding:** High deposition rate, mechanizable, good for long straight seams, suits robotic panel lines.
- **Disadvantages / limitations:** Sensitive to wind (cannot be used outdoors without shelter), expensive equipment, requires clean plate surface, not ideal for very thin plate without SCT.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute revision mantra:** *"GMAW = CV + DCEP + wire + gas. Modes: S–G–S–P. Heat: $\eta V I / S$ in kJ/mm. Class limit: 3.5. Memorize — pass with flying colours."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Different Metal Transfer Modes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST831 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Different Metal Transfer Modes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW)** — also classified by the American Welding Society (AWS) and used extensively in KTU-aligned shipbuilding practice — **Metal Transfer** is defined as the controlled mechanism by which molten filler metal droplets are detached from the consumable electrode tip and propelled across the arc plasma into the weld pool. The **mode of metal transfer** is the geometric and physical manner in which this detachment and transfer occurs, governed primarily by the welding current, arc voltage, electrode diameter, shielding gas composition, and the balance of electromagnetic, gravitational, and surface-tension forces acting on the molten droplet at the electrode tip.

The **AWS A5.1 / A5.18** classification framework and the **ISO 4063** process codes recognize **four principal transfer modes** in GMAW that shipbuilding students must master:

1. **Short-Circuit Transfer (SCT)** — also called *Dip Transfer* or *Contact Transfer*.
2. **Globular Transfer (GT)** — also called *Drop Transfer*.
3. **Conventional Spray Transfer (CST)** — also called *Axial Spray Transfer*.
4. **Pulsed Spray Transfer (PST)** — also called *Pulsed Arc Transfer*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For OENST831, students are required to compare transfer modes in terms of current range, voltage range, droplet size, heat input, shielding gas, weld position suitability, and shipbuilding application. Pure recall of names without numerical ranges fetches **zero marks** in Part A.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are **watering a plant using a tap with a hose attached** (a fitting analogy for a shipyard welder operating a GMAW torch):

- **Short-Circuit Transfer** → You repeatedly squeeze the hose end against the plant pot to release a small squirt, then release, then squeeze again. Each "squeeze" pushes a tiny amount of water. This is **fast, low, and controlled** — used for thin plates or overhead welding in a ship's superstructure.

- **Globular Transfer** → You hold the hose loosely, and big heavy drops of water fall from the end under gravity alone. The drops are large, irregular, and fall straight down. This is **slow, rough, and gravity-dependent** — rarely desired in modern shipbuilding.

- **Spray Transfer** → You turn the tap fully on, and a fine cone-shaped mist of water shoots forward axially with great force. This is **fast, high-deposition, and directional** — used for thick plate seams in flat (1G/1F) positions on a hull panel.

- **Pulsed Spray Transfer** → You pulse the tap — *burst* of high pressure, *pause* of low pressure, *burst* again — sending one precisely-sized droplet per pulse. This gives **spray-like quality at lower average heat input** — ideal for all-position welding of a ship's curved shell plates.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Density of molten steel** ≈ **7,200 kg/m³** at 1,540 °C (relevant for gravity/buoyancy force calculations on droplets).
- **Surface tension of molten steel (Ar shielding)** ≈ **1.2 N/m**.
- **Standard shielding gases**: **Argon (Ar)**, **Carbon Dioxide (CO₂)**, **Ar + 5–25 % CO₂**, **Ar + 2–5 % O₂**.
- **Reference standard**: **AWS C5.1 / ISO 4063** for GMAW (Process Number **131, 135, 136, 137**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** Transfer mode is essentially a *forced detachment phenomenon*. As the welding current increases, the **electromagnetic pinch force** (Lorentz body force) grows as $I^2$ and progressively overcomes the **surface-tension retaining force** at the droplet neck, changing the mode from contact-based (SCT) → gravity-based (Globular) → electromagnetic-spray (Spray) → pulsed-controlled (Pulsed Spray).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Transfer Modes — Structured Logic Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Short-Circuit Transfer (SCT / Dip Transfer)
- **Voltage range:** **15 – 22 V** (typically 18 V).
- **Current range:** **50 – 200 A** (low current density, ≈ 100 A/mm² for 1.0 mm wire).
- **Wire diameter:** 0.6 – 1.2 mm.
- **Droplet size:** Comparable to wire diameter; transfer occurs only on *physical contact* with the pool.
- **Shielding gas:** Pure CO₂ or Ar + 25 % CO₂.
- **Mechanism:** Electrode touches weld pool → short circuit → current surge → pinch force ruptures the liquid bridge → arc re-ignites → cycle repeats at **50 – 200 Hz** (short-circuit frequency).
- **Heat input:** **Low** → minimal distortion, suitable for thin plates (1 – 3 mm).
- **Positions:** All positions (1F, 2F, 3F, 4F, 1G, 2G, 3G, 4G) — *outstanding for shipyard overhead and vertical welds*.
- **Shipbuilding application:** Welding of **bulb flats, stiffeners, internal bulkhead panels, and thin deck plating** during ship outfitting.

### 2.1.2 Globular Transfer (GT)
- **Voltage range:** **22 – 28 V**.
- **Current range:** **150 – 300 A** (above transition current but below spray threshold).
- **Droplet size:** **2× to 4× the wire diameter** (large, irregular globules).
- **Shielding gas:** Pure **CO₂** (Ar-rich mixtures suppress this mode).
- **Mechanism:** Droplet grows until **gravity overcomes surface tension**. Detachment is *non-axial*, *erratic*, and *low-frequency* (typically 10 – 20 droplets/sec).
- **Drawbacks:** Heavy spatter, poor bead shape, deep penetration, slag inclusion risk.
- **Shipbuilding application:** **Rarely used** in modern shipbuilding; only legacy CO₂ semi-automatic stations for thick-plate butt joints where spatter is acceptable.

### 2.1.3 Conventional Spray Transfer (CST)
- **Voltage range:** **28 – 35 V** (above the transition voltage).
- **Current range:** **> 200 A** (above **transition current** $I_t$).
- **Wire diameter:** 1.0 – 1.6 mm.
- **Droplet size:** *Equal to or smaller than* wire diameter.
- **Droplet rate:** **> 100 droplets/sec** (visually a stream).
- **Shielding gas:** Argon-rich (≥ 80 % Ar) — usually **Ar + 2 – 5 % O₂** or **Ar + 5 – 25 % CO₂**.
- **Mechanism:** **Electromagnetic pinch force** dominates; droplets detach axially, projected in a fine stream.
- **Heat input:** **High** → risk of distortion and burn-through on thin plates.
- **Positions:** **Flat (1G/1F) and horizontal (2G/2F) only** — gravity-defying positions not feasible.
- **Shipbuilding application:** **Long butt welds of thick hull plates (8 – 25 mm)**, panel-line seams on the flat bottom and bilge.

### 2.1.4 Pulsed Spray Transfer (PST)
- **Voltage:** Pulsed between a **background level (~18 – 20 V)** and a **pulse level (30 – 40 V)**.
- **Current:** Pulses between a **background current ($I_b$)** and a **peak pulse current ($I_p$)**.
- **Wire diameter:** 0.8 – 1.6 mm.
- **Droplet size:** One droplet per pulse (synergic control).
- **Mechanism:** Each current pulse provides enough pinch force to detach exactly one droplet; the background current maintains the arc with minimum heat.
- **Heat input:** **Controlled, lower than CST** while retaining spray quality.
- **Positions:** **All positions** — the workhorse of robotic shipbuilding welding.
- **Shipbuilding application:** **Out-of-position robotic welding of curved shell plates, T-joints in stiffened panels, and aluminium superstructures (Al-Mg / Al-Si alloys)**.

## 2.2 The Three Governing Forces on a Molten Droplet

For a detached droplet hanging at the electrode tip, the *vertical force balance* is:

$$
F_{EM} + F_{gravity} \; = \; F_{ST} + F_{drag}
$$

Where:
- $F_{EM}$ = Electromagnetic (Lorentz) pinch force.
- $F_{gravity} = \rho \, V \, g$ (weight).
- $F_{ST}$ = Surface-tension retaining force at the neck.
- $F_{drag}$ = Aerodynamic/plasma drag.

The **transition current $I_t$** (Globular → Spray) is empirically given by:

$$
I_t \; \approx \; k \cdot d_w
$$

Where $d_w$ = wire diameter (mm), and $k \approx 30$–$40$ A/mm for steel in Argon-rich shielding. So a 1.2 mm wire transitions to spray at roughly **$I_t \approx 36$–$48$ A/mm × 1.2 mm = 43 – 58 A** of cross-sectional current density? **No** — the correct relation (Lesnewich, AWS C5.1) is:

$$
I_t \; (\text{A}) \; \approx \; 30 \times d_w \; (\text{mm}) \times 10^1 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{For } d_w = 1.2 \text{ mm, } I_t \approx 360 \text{ A}
$$

## 2.3 Heat Input Formula (Universal — KTU 2024 High-Yield)

$$
H \; = \; \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{v \cdot 1000} \quad \left[ \dfrac{\text{kJ}}{\text{mm}} \right]
$$

Where:
- $\eta$ = arc efficiency (**0.75 – 0.85 for GMAW**).
- $V$ = arc voltage (V).
- $I$ = welding current (A).
- $v$ = travel speed (mm/min).

For **deposition rate** (mass of weld metal laid per unit time):

$$
R_{dep} \; = \; \dfrac{\pi \, d_w^2 \, v_f \, \rho}{4 \times 10^6} \quad \left[ \dfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{min}} \right]
$$

Where $v_f$ = wire feed speed (mm/min), $\rho$ = wire density (kg/m³), $d_w$ in mm.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Markdown Table)

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|--------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Heat Input | $H$ | $H = \dfrac{\eta V I \cdot 60}{v \cdot 1000}$ | kJ/mm | $\eta \approx 0.8$ for GMAW |
| 2 | Deposition Rate | $R_{dep}$ | $R_{dep} = \dfrac{\pi d_w^2 v_f \rho}{4 \times 10^6}$ | kg/min | 7,200 kg/m³ for steel wire |
| 3 | Transition Current | $I_t$ | $I_t \approx 30 \, d_w \times 10^{1}$ | A | Empirical (Lesnewich) |
| 4 | Current Density | $J$ | $J = \dfrac{4I}{\pi d_w^2}$ | A/mm² | SCT ≈ 100; CST > 200 |
| 5 | Droplet Reynolds No. | $Re_d$ | $Re_d = \dfrac{\rho v_p d_d}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | Indicates transfer stability |
| 6 | Pinch (Constriction) Pressure | $P_{EM}$ | $P_{EM} \approx \dfrac{\mu_0 I^2}{4 \pi^2 R^2}$ | Pa | Lorentz body force |
| 7 | Wire Extension Resistive Heat | $Q_{ext}$ | $Q_{ext} = I^2 R_{ext}$ | W | Pre-heats electrode in CST |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pitfall:** When writing the heat input equation in the exam, students often write $\dfrac{V I}{v}$ without the constants 60 and 1000. This changes the units silently and **costs 2 marks** in KTU valuation. Always show: $H = \dfrac{\eta V I}{v}$ *with $v$ in mm/min and result in J/mm* OR include the conversion to **kJ/mm**.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Shipbuilding automation:** Robotic GMAW systems using **Pulsed Spray Transfer** dominate the *Panamax*, *Capesize*, and *LNG carrier* fabrication lines (e.g., Daewoo, Hyundai Heavy Industries) for one-sided welding of **DH36/EH36 grade hull plates**.
- **Offshore structures:** Jackets and decks for fixed platforms use **Spray Transfer** for thick tubular joints.
- **Submarine fabrication:** Austenitic stainless steel (SS 304L/316L) is welded using **Pulsed Spray with Ar + 2 % CO₂** to prevent sensitization.
- **Defence shipbuilding:** Cordite-grade welding of torpedo tubes and pressure hulls requires **SCT (low heat input)** to minimize HAZ softening.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Transition Current $I_t$ (Globular → Spray)

The **pinch effect** is described by the **Bennett constriction** of a current-carrying cylindrical liquid column. The **electromagnetic pressure** acting on a droplet of radius $R$ carrying current $I$ through a converging nozzle geometry is given by:

$$
P_{EM} \; = \; \dfrac{\mu_0 \, I^2}{4 \pi^2 R^2}
$$

For detachment, this must overcome the **Laplace pressure** (surface tension) at the neck:

$$
P_{ST} \; = \; \dfrac{2 \gamma}{R}
$$

Setting $P_{EM} \geq P_{ST}$ for the *incipient detachment condition*:

$$
\dfrac{\mu_0 \, I^2}{4 \pi^2 R^2} \; = \; \dfrac{2 \gamma}{R}
$$

Solving for $I$:

$$
I^2 \; = \; \dfrac{8 \pi^2 \gamma R}{\mu_0}
$$

$$
I \; = \; \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{8 \gamma R}{\mu_0}}
$$

Substituting $R \propto d_w / 2$ (the droplet radius scales with the wire radius):

$$
I_t \; \propto \; \sqrt{d_w}
$$

**Numerical substitution** for steel in Argon (γ = 1.2 N/m, μ₀ = 4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m, R = 0.6 mm = 6 × 10⁻⁴ m for 1.2 mm wire):

$$
I_t \; = \; \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{8 \times 1.2 \times 6 \times 10^{-4}}{4\pi \times 10^{-7}}}
$$

$$
I_t \; = \; \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{5.76 \times 10^{-3}}{1.2566 \times 10^{-6}}}
$$

$$
I_t \; = \; \pi \sqrt{4584.0} \; = \; \pi \times 67.7 \; \approx \; 212.7 \; \text{A}
$$

**Note:** This is the *idealized theoretical* value. In practice, the empirical Lesnewich constant gives higher values (≈ 350 A) due to **arc-root wandering** and **non-axisymmetric droplet formation**.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Heat Input Comparison (KTU-typical 14-mark problem)

**Question:** A shipyard welds a **12 mm thick EH36 hull plate** using GMAW in two different transfer modes. Compare the heat input per unit length.

**Case A — Short-Circuit Transfer:** $V = 19$ V, $I = 150$ A, $v = 350$ mm/min.
**Case B — Spray Transfer:** $V = 32$ V, $I = 300$ A, $v = 450$ mm/min.

Take $\eta = 0.8$ for both.

### Solution — Case A (SCT):

$$
H_A \; = \; \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{v \cdot 1000}
$$

$$
H_A \; = \; \dfrac{0.8 \times 19 \times 150 \times 60}{350 \times 1000}
$$

$$
H_A \; = \; \dfrac{136{,}800}{350{,}000} \; = \; 0.391 \; \text{kJ/mm}
$$

### Solution — Case B (Spray):

$$
H_B \; = \; \dfrac{0.8 \times 32 \times 300 \times 60}{450 \times 1000}
$$

$$
H_B \; = \; \dfrac{460{,}800}{450{,}000} \; = \; 1.024 \; \text{kJ/mm}
$$

### Comparison and Inference:

$$
\dfrac{H_B}{H_A} \; = \; \dfrac{1.024}{0.391} \; \approx \; 2.62
$$

**Inference:** Spray transfer delivers **2.62× the heat per mm** of weld compared to short-circuit mode. For the same 12 mm plate:
- **SCT** is preferred for the **second-side sealing run** (low heat, less distortion).
- **Spray** is preferred for the **root + hot pass** in the flat position (high deposition, deep penetration).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mode Selection Decision Tree for Shipbuilding

| Plate Thickness (mm) | Position | Preferred Mode | Justification |
|----------------------|----------|----------------|---------------|
| 1 – 3 | All | **SCT** | Low HAZ, no burn-through |
| 3 – 6 | All | **SCT or PST** | PST for robotic uniformity |
| 6 – 12 | Flat / Horizontal | **Spray** | High deposition, full penetration |
| 6 – 12 | Vertical / Overhead | **PST** | Controlled droplet, stable pool |
| > 12 | Flat (butt) | **Spray + multi-pass** | Productivity-driven |
| Stainless / Al | All | **PST with Ar-rich** | Cleaner bead, no oxidation |
| Clad / Coated | Any | **PST** | Reduced spatter on coating |

## 3.4 Derivation: Deposition Rate for a Robotic Pulsed Spray Cell

Given: $d_w = 1.2$ mm, $v_f = 8$ m/min = **8,000 mm/min**, $\rho = 7,200$ kg/m³.

$$
R_{dep} \; = \; \dfrac{\pi \times (1.2)^2 \times 8{,}000 \times 7{,}200}{4 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
R_{dep} \; = \; \dfrac{3.1416 \times 1.44 \times 8{,}000 \times 7{,}200}{4 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
R_{dep} \; = \; \dfrac{2.605 \times 10^8}{4 \times 10^6} \; = \; 65.1 \; \text{kg/h}
$$

**Inference:** A single 1.2 mm pulsed-spray robotic torch can lay down **65 kg of weld metal per hour** — enough to weld approximately **6 – 8 metres of 6 mm fillet per hour** in a ship panel line. This is the productivity basis for modern panel-line automation in Korean and Japanese shipyards.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Force Balance and Transfer Mode Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: GMAW Process Initiated] --> B{Is Wire Contacting Pool?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Short Circuit Detected]
    B -- No --> D{Current vs Transition Current I sub t}
    C --> E[Current Surge via Inductance]
    E --> F[Pinch Force Ruptures Bridge]
    F --> G[Arc Re-ignites]
    G --> H[Short Circuit Transfer SCT]
    D -- Below I sub t --> I{Droplet Detachment Cause?}
    D -- Above I sub t --> J[Electromagnetic Pinch Dominates]
    I -- Gravity --> K[Globular Transfer GT]
    I -- Pulsed Current --> L[Pulsed Spray Transfer PST]
    J --> M[Spray Transfer CST]
    H --> N[Low Heat Input Thin Plates All Positions]
    K --> O[High Spatter Legacy CO2]
    L --> P[All Position Robotic Stainless Al]
    M --> Q[High Deposition Thick Flat Plates]
    N --> R[Shipbuilding Application Decision]
    O --> R
    P --> R
    Q --> R
    R --> S[End: Optimal Mode Selected]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of a Synergic Pulsed Spray System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Power Source Inverter] --> B[Waveform Generator]
    B --> C[Background Current Control]
    B --> D[Pulse Current Control]
    C --> E[Arc Length Feedback Sensor]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Wire Feed Motor Drive]
    F --> G[Consumable Electrode]
    G --> H[Arc Plasma Column]
    H --> I[Weld Pool]
    I --> J[Solidified Weld Bead]
    E --> K[Synergic Controller CPU]
    K --> A
    K --> F
    H --> L[Shielding Gas Ar plus CO2]
    L --> H
```

## 4.3 Comparative Schematic of Droplet Sizes (Functional Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph SCT
    S1[Wire 1.0 mm] --> S2[Droplet equals wire diameter]
    S2 --> S3[Contact-based detachment]
    end
    subgraph GT
    G1[Wire 1.2 mm] --> G2[Droplet 2x to 4x wire diameter]
    G2 --> G3[Gravity-based detachment]
    end
    subgraph CST
    C1[Wire 1.2 mm] --> C2[Droplet smaller than wire]
    C2 --> C3[Pinch-based axial detachment]
    end
    subgraph PST
    P1[Wire 1.0 mm] --> P2[One droplet per pulse]
    P2 --> P3[Controlled per-pulse detachment]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note:** The block diagrams above intentionally abstract the physical droplet geometry to comply with the mermaid compilation safeguard (no reserved keywords as node IDs, raw uppercase alphanumeric labels). For physical droplet sketches, students should draw freehand in the answer script: an oval for globular, a teardrop for spray, and a dotted bridge for short-circuit neck rupture.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define short-circuit transfer mode in GMAW. Mention any two characteristic features.**

**Model Answer:**
Short-Circuit Transfer (SCT) is a mode of metal transfer in Gas Metal Arc Welding in which the molten filler metal is transferred from the consumable electrode to the weld pool only when the electrode physically contacts (short-circuits) the weld pool. Upon contact, the current surges through the liquid bridge, electromagnetic pinch force ruptures the bridge, and the molten droplet is deposited. The arc re-ignites, and the cycle repeats at 50–200 Hz.

**Two characteristic features:**
1. **Low voltage (15 – 22 V) and low current (50 – 200 A)** operation.
2. **Suitable for all welding positions** including overhead and vertical — making it indispensable for shipbuilding stiffener and bulkhead welding.

*(2 marks for definition + 1 mark for features = 3 marks)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Distinguish between globular transfer and spray transfer in GMAW. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Globular Transfer | Spray Transfer |
|-----------|------------------|----------------|
| Voltage | 22 – 28 V | 28 – 35 V |
| Droplet size | 2× to 4× wire diameter | Smaller than wire diameter |
| Detachment force | Gravity-dominated | Electromagnetic pinch-dominated |
| Spatter | Heavy | Minimal |
| Position suitability | Flat only | Flat & horizontal |

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for distinction + 1 mark for position = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) **[7 marks]** Explain the four different modes of metal transfer in GMAW with suitable sketches. Compare their current and voltage ranges.

**Model Solution:**

Metal transfer in GMAW refers to the manner in which the molten electrode tip is detached and transferred to the weld pool. Four principal modes exist:

**1. Short-Circuit Transfer (SCT):**
- Voltage: 15 – 22 V; Current: 50 – 200 A.
- Mechanism: Electrode contacts pool → current surge → pinch ruptures bridge → arc re-ignites.
- Applications: Thin plates, all positions.

**2. Globular Transfer (GT):**
- Voltage: 22 – 28 V; Current: 150 – 300 A.
- Mechanism: Large droplets (2 – 4 × wire diameter) detach under gravity.
- Applications: Legacy CO₂ welding, thick plate (rare in modern shipbuilding).

**3. Spray Transfer (CST):**
- Voltage: 28 – 35 V; Current: > 200 A (above transition current).
- Mechanism: Electromagnetic pinch force detaches fine droplets axially at > 100 drops/sec.
- Applications: Thick plates, flat/horizontal positions, high deposition.

**4. Pulsed Spray Transfer (PST):**
- Voltage/Current pulsed between background and peak levels.
- Mechanism: One droplet per pulse; synergic control.
- Applications: All positions, robotic shipbuilding, stainless steel, aluminium.

**Comparison Table (must be drawn in the exam):**

| Mode | Voltage (V) | Current (A) | Droplet Size | Position |
|------|------------|------------|--------------|----------|
| SCT | 15–22 | 50–200 | = wire dia. | All |
| GT | 22–28 | 150–300 | 2–4× wire | Flat |
| CST | 28–35 | > 200 | < wire dia. | Flat/Horiz. |
| PST | Pulsed | Pulsed | 1 per pulse | All |

*(3 marks for explanation + 2 marks for sketches + 2 marks for comparison = 7 marks)*

#### (b) **[7 marks]** A 10 mm thick AH36 ship plate is welded by GMAW using spray transfer at V = 30 V, I = 280 A, travel speed = 400 mm/min. Calculate (i) heat input per mm, and (ii) deposition rate if wire diameter is 1.2 mm at feed rate 6 m/min. Assume arc efficiency = 0.80 and wire density = 7,200 kg/m³.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Heat Input per mm:**

$$
H \; = \; \dfrac{\eta \, V \, I \, 60}{v \, 1000}
$$

$$
H \; = \; \dfrac{0.80 \times 30 \times 280 \times 60}{400 \times 1000}
$$

$$
H \; = \; \dfrac{403{,}200}{400{,}000} \; = \; 1.008 \; \text{kJ/mm}
$$

**(ii) Deposition Rate:**

Wire feed speed = 6 m/min = 6,000 mm/min.

$$
R_{dep} \; = \; \dfrac{\pi d_w^2 v_f \rho}{4 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
R_{dep} \; = \; \dfrac{3.1416 \times (1.2)^2 \times 6{,}000 \times 7{,}200}{4 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
R_{dep} \; = \; \dfrac{3.1416 \times 1.44 \times 6{,}000 \times 7{,}200}{4 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
R_{dep} \; = \; \dfrac{1.954 \times 10^8}{4 \times 10^6} \; = \; 48.86 \; \text{kg/h}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- Heat input = **1.008 kJ/mm** (exceeds typical 1.0 kJ/mm cap for AH36 — risk of HAZ softening, examiner accepts comment).
- Deposition rate = **48.86 kg/h**.

*(2 marks for heat input substitution + 2 marks for answer + 1 mark for deposition formula + 2 marks for final answer = 7 marks)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### (a) **[7 marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the electromagnetic pinch force theory responsible for spray transfer in GMAW. Derive the condition for droplet detachment.

**Model Solution:**

When current $I$ flows through a converging molten metal column (the droplet neck at the electrode tip), a self-induced magnetic field encircles the column. The interaction between the current and its own field produces an **inward radial Lorentz force** ($F = J \times B$), which constricts the column — the **pinch effect**.

**Force balance on a droplet of radius $R$ carrying current $I$:**

- Inward electromagnetic pressure: $P_{EM} = \dfrac{\mu_0 I^2}{4 \pi^2 R^2}$
- Outward Laplace (surface tension) pressure: $P_{ST} = \dfrac{2 \gamma}{R}$

**Detachment condition** ($P_{EM} \geq P_{ST}$):

$$
\dfrac{\mu_0 I^2}{4 \pi^2 R^2} \;\geq\; \dfrac{2 \gamma}{R}
$$

$$
I \;\geq\; \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{8 \gamma R}{\mu_0}} \;=\; I_t
$$

**Sketch description (in answer script):** Draw a vertical electrode, a teardrop-shaped droplet at the tip, magnetic field lines encircling the neck, and an arrow pointing inward labelled "F_EM = J × B". Indicate surface tension arrow outward at the neck.

**Inference:** As $I$ increases, the pinch force grows as $I^2$, and the droplet detaches axially in a fine stream — this is **spray transfer**.

*(2 marks for theory + 2 marks for sketch + 3 marks for derivation = 7 marks)*

#### (b) **[7 marks]** Compare pulsed spray transfer with conventional spray transfer in terms of (i) heat input control, (ii) positional capability, (iii) spatter, (iv) shipbuilding application, and (v) shielding gas. State the principle of synergic control.

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Conventional Spray (CST) | Pulsed Spray (PST) |
|-----------|-------------------------|-------------------|
| (i) Heat input | High, fixed by current | Variable, controlled per pulse |
| (ii) Position | Flat/Horiz. only | All positions |
| (iii) Spatter | Low (in Ar) | Very low |
| (iv) Shipbuilding use | Thick plate butt, flat | Robotic all-position, Al/SS |
| (v) Shielding gas | Ar + 2–5 % O₂ | Ar + 2 % CO₂ / pure Ar |

**Principle of Synergic Control:**
Synergic control is an **integrated electronic control** in modern GMAW power sources where a single parameter (e.g., wire feed speed or average current set by the operator) automatically adjusts all other interdependent variables — pulse frequency, pulse current, background current, pulse width, and arc voltage — to maintain a **stable one-droplet-per-pulse transfer** across a wide operating range. It was pioneered by **Kemppi** (Finland) and is the technological foundation of all modern shipyard robotic welding cells.

*(5 marks for comparison table + 2 marks for synergic principle = 7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deductions in OENST831 — Metal Transfer Modes:**
> 1. **Writing the heat input formula without the 60/1000 conversion factors** (costs 2 marks in 14-mark Q). Always state units explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing "globular" with "spray"** by stating globular occurs in Ar-rich gas (it is a CO₂ phenomenon). Examiner deducts 1 mark.
> 3. **Forgetting the "transition current" concept** when explaining why spray doesn't occur below 200 A — this is a 2-mark "why" question, not a "what" question.
> 4. **Not drawing the electrode–arc–pool geometry** in 7-mark questions. Even a labelled box-diagram fetches 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Using SI units inconsistently** (e.g., mixing mm/min and m/s in the same calculation). Always show the unit conversion step.
> 6. **Stating pulsed spray is "expensive and not used in shipbuilding"** — this is **factually incorrect**; it is the *dominant* robotic mode in Korean/Japanese shipyards.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Four transfer modes:** SCT, Globular, Spray, Pulsed Spray — *must be memorized in voltage and current ranges.*
- **Short-Circuit Transfer:** 15–22 V, 50–200 A, all positions, thin plates.
- **Globular Transfer:** 22–28 V, gravity-driven, large droplets, heavy spatter, **CO₂ gas**, largely obsolete.
- **Conventional Spray Transfer:** 28–35 V, > 200 A, axial droplet stream, flat/horizontal only, thick plates.
- **Pulsed Spray Transfer:** Pulsed current/voltage, **one droplet per pulse**, all positions, robotic shipbuilding, Al/SS.
- **Transition current $I_t$** is the boundary between globular and spray — defined by the **pinch force / surface tension balance**.
- **Heat input formula:** $H = \dfrac{\eta V I \times 60}{v \times 1000}$ kJ/mm with $\eta \approx 0.8$ for GMAW.
- **Deposition rate formula:** $R_{dep} = \dfrac{\pi d_w^2 v_f \rho}{4 \times 10^6}$ kg/h.
- **Synergic control** = single-knob multi-parameter electronic control of PST systems.
- **Pinpoint pinch force** in spray transfer: $P_{EM} \approx \dfrac{\mu_0 I^2}{4 \pi^2 R^2}$.
- **Shipbuilding decision rule:** thin / out-of-position → SCT or PST; thick / flat → CST; stainless / aluminium → PST.
- **AWS / ISO reference:** AWS C5.1, AWS A5.18, ISO 4063 (Process 131/135/136/137).
- **Forces governing detachment:** Lorentz pinch ($I^2$ growth), gravity ($\rho V g$), surface tension ($2\gamma/R$), plasma drag.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Source
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST831 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Power Source

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Joining Techniques in Shipbuilding**, a **Power Source** (also called a *welding power source* or *welding generator/set*) is defined as the **electrical or thermal energy supply unit** that delivers controlled voltage and current of a specific waveform (AC, DC, or pulsed) to the welding arc, electrode, or joining tool, in order to melt and fuse the base metals and/or filler material to produce a sound welded joint.

As per **ISO 857-1** and **AWS A3.0** terminology, a welding power source is *"equipment which supplies the electric current and voltage necessary for arc welding or resistance welding, with the required characteristics."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> A shipbuilding welding power source is **not just a transformer**. It is a complete **energy conversion + control system** whose output volt-ampere (V–A) characteristic must match the *static* and *dynamic* demands of the chosen joining process (SMAW, GTAW, GMAW, FCAW, SAW, PAW, RSW, etc.) and the metallurgical demands of the marine-grade steels (AH36, DH36, EH36, SS 304/316, etc.) used in hull fabrication.

## 1.2 Classification of Power Sources (High-Level Map)

Power sources used in shipbuilding joining are classified as follows:

### A. By Energy Form Delivered
| Type | Typical Process | Shipbuilding Use |
|------|-----------------|------------------|
| **Electrical (Arc)** | SMAW, GTAW, GMAW, FCAW, SAW, PAW | Hull plates, stiffeners, pipe welds |
| **Electrical (Resistance)** | Spot, Projection, Seam, Stud | Deck panels, thin sheet stiffeners |
| **Thermal (Gas/Chemical)** | Oxy-fuel (OFW), Air-acetylene | Heating, cutting, brazing |
| **Mechanical** | Friction Stir Welding (FSW) | Aluminum superstructures, decks |
| **Beam (Concentrated Energy)** | Laser Beam (LBW), Electron Beam (EBW) | Specialized thin-section joints |

### B. By Conversion Technology
1. **Static Power Sources** — Transformer, Rectifier, Inverter (solid-state, no moving parts).
2. **Rotary Power Sources** — Engine-driven or motor-driven welding generator (DC welding generator).
3. **Hybrid/Static-Rotary** — Engine-driven inverter (e.g., diesel-driven inverter for site work).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Power Source is the Welding Heart"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy (Think of it this way):**
> Imagine a **water tap** that supplies water to a garden hose. If you open it fully, water gushes out with high pressure but low control (hard to direct). If you open it just a little, you get a steady, controllable stream. A welding power source works the **same way**:
> - The **tap** = the power source.
> - The **water pressure** = open-circuit voltage.
> - The **flow rate** = welding current.
> - The **sprinkler at the end** = the welding arc.
> 
> Just as different garden jobs (watering delicate flowers vs. washing a car) need **different tap settings**, different welding processes (TIG on thin stainless vs. SAW on thick hull plate) need **different V–A characteristics** from the power source.

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (Must-Memorize for KTU)

- **Open-Circuit Voltage (OCV)** — Voltage at the output terminals **with no arc struck** (typically **50–100 V** for SMAW, **60–80 V** for GMAW, **70–95 V** for SAW).
- **Arc Voltage (Working Voltage)** — Voltage **across the arc** during welding (typically **18–32 V** for most arc processes).
- **Welding Current** — Current flowing through the arc (**50 A – 1000 A+** depending on process and plate thickness).
- **Duty Cycle** — Percentage of a **10-minute cycle** the source can deliver rated current (typically **60 %** at **40 °C** ambient per IEC 60974-1).
- **No-Load Power Consumption** — Power drawn when idle (important for shipyard energy audits).
- **Efficiency (η)** — Ratio of output welding power to input electrical power, typically **0.70–0.90** for modern inverters.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept: How a Welding Power Source Works

A power source performs **three core functions** in sequence:

1. **Energy Conversion** — Converts incoming mains power (e.g., 3-phase 415 V, 50 Hz AC) into a form suitable for welding (low-voltage, high-current AC or DC).
2. **Regulation / Control** — Maintains the desired V–A relationship (constant current, constant voltage, or drooping) despite arc length fluctuations.
3. **Delivery** — Feeds the regulated power to the arc via heavy-duty cables, electrode holder, and work lead.

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Functional Logic (Generic)

- **Step 1:** Mains supply → input rectifier/filter (in inverter types) → DC bus.
- **Step 2:** DC bus → high-frequency switching (IGBT/MOSFET at **20–100 kHz**) → step-down transformer → output rectifier → DC welding output.
- **Step 3:** Feedback loop senses arc voltage and current → compares with setpoint → adjusts switching duty cycle → maintains desired V–A curve.
- **Step 4:** The arc, behaving as a **non-linear resistive load** (voltage rises with current then falls), is kept stable by the dynamic response of the source.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the high frequency (20–100 kHz)?**
> Transformer size is inversely proportional to frequency. At **50 Hz**, a 400 A welding transformer weighs **~150 kg**; at **50 kHz**, the same power transformer weighs **<15 kg**. This is why **inverter power sources** dominate modern shipyards — they are portable, ship-deck-friendly, and energy-efficient.

## 2.2 Static vs. Rotary Power Sources — Engineering Trade-Off

| Parameter | Static (Transformer/Rectifier/Inverter) | Rotary (Engine/Motor Generator) |
|-----------|------------------------------------------|----------------------------------|
| **Weight** | Low (inverter ~25 kg for 400 A) | High (engine-driven set ~300–800 kg) |
| **Efficiency** | **0.85–0.92** | **0.50–0.70** |
| **Maintenance** | Minimal (no brushes, no engine) | High (engine oil, brushes, bearings) |
| **Site Mobility** | Excellent (portable) | Limited (needs fuel/trailer) |
| **Dynamic Response** | **Excellent** (kHz-level control) | Slower (mechanical inertia) |
| **Output Quality** | Clean, stable DC | Slight ripple; DC commutator |
| **Initial Cost** | Higher | Lower (but high running cost) |
| **KTU shipyard choice** | **Preferred** for enclosed workshops, automated lines, robotic cells | Used for **field erection, dry-dock, repair** where mains is unavailable |

## 2.3 The V–A (Volt-Ampere) Characteristic — The Heart of Power Source Selection

The V–A curve defines how the source's terminal voltage changes as welding current changes. This is the **single most important concept** in power source engineering.

### 2.3.1 Three Canonical Shapes

1. **Constant Current (CC) — "Drooping" characteristic**
   - Voltage drops sharply as current rises.
   - Used for **SMAW (stick), GTAW (TIG), PAW (plasma)**.
   - Why? These processes use **constant-current** to prevent the arc from self-destructing if the welder varies the arc length (electrode-to-work distance).

2. **Constant Voltage (CV) — "Flat" characteristic**
   - Voltage remains nearly constant; current self-adjusts with arc length.
   - Used for **GMAW (MIG/MAG), FCAW, SAW** with **constant wire-feed speed**.
   - Why? In wire-feed processes, the welder cannot manually adjust current; the arc length is controlled by burn-off rate, so voltage must stay flat.

3. **Rising Characteristic** — Voltage rises with current.
   - Rare; used in some **electroslag** and **electrogas** processes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Trick — "Drooping for Manual, Flat for Automatic":**
> **D**rooping = **D**rawn by hand (SMAW, GTAW)
> **F**lat = **F**eed-controlled (GMAW, FCAW, SAW)

### 2.3.2 Mathematical Form of the V–A Curve (for Examination Use)

A typical CC source can be modelled as a **Thevenin equivalent**:

$$V_{terminal} = V_{OC} - I \cdot R_{internal}$$

where:

- $V_{OC}$ = **Open-circuit voltage** (intercept on the V-axis, in **V**)
- $I$ = **Welding current** (in **A**)
- $R_{internal}$ = **Internal slope resistance** (in **Ω**), controlling the steepness of the droop.

For a CV source:

$$V_{terminal} \approx V_{set} \quad (\text{independent of } I)$$

A more realistic CV model includes small regulation:

$$V_{terminal} = V_{set} - k \cdot (I - I_0)$$

where $k$ is a small **regulation constant** (V/A), and $I_0$ is the no-load reference current.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Used For / Context |
|---|---------------------|------------|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | Open-circuit voltage | $V_{OC}$ | **V** | Initiation of arc; IEC 60974-1 safety limits |
| 2 | Working/arc voltage | $V_a = V_{OC} - I \cdot R_{int}$ | **V** | Actual voltage during welding |
| 3 | Heat input (H.I.) | $H.I. = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \cdot 1000}$ | **kJ/mm** | Critical in shipbuilding (H.I. limits for AH/DH/EH steels) |
| 4 | Power output | $P_{out} = V \cdot I$ | **W** (or **kW**) | Sizing power source |
| 5 | Power input | $P_{in} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{\eta}$ | **W** | Energy audit; cable sizing |
| 6 | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ | **%** | Comparison of source types |
| 7 | Duty cycle derating | $I_{actual} = I_{rated} \sqrt{\dfrac{D_{rated}}{D_{actual}}}$ | **A** | Derating a source for higher duty |
| 8 | Power source weight rule (inverter) | $W \propto \dfrac{1}{f_{sw}}$ | **kg** | Why high-frequency switching reduces weight |
| 9 | Arc energy (per unit length) | $E = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{v}$ | **J/mm** | Metallurgical control of HAZ |
| 10 | Cable voltage drop | $\Delta V = \dfrac{2 \cdot \rho \cdot L \cdot I}{A}$ | **V** | Sizing welding cables on ship |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In a real shipyard, the power source selection impacts **heat input**, which directly governs the **HAZ microstructure, hardness, and toughness** of marine steel welds. A poorly chosen source (e.g., excessive OCV or unstable arc) can cause **undercut, porosity, lack of fusion, or HAZ embrittlement** — all of which are major IACS / classification society (DNV, LR, ABS, BV) rejection criteria.

## 2.5 Real-World Shipbuilding Applications

| Shipyard Operation | Recommended Power Source | Why |
|--------------------|--------------------------|-----|
| Hull plate butt welds (SAW) | **CV Inverter + Wire feeder** (e.g., 1000 A) | High deposition, long beads, automated |
| Hull plate fillet welds (FCAW) | **CV Inverter** | Self-shielded flux-cored wire; mobile |
| Pipe welding (GTAW root + SMAW fill) | **CC Inverter (TIG) + CC Rectifier (stick)** | Root pass quality, then deposition rate |
| Aluminum superstructure (FSW or GMAW) | **Pulsed MIG inverter or FSW spindle** | Low heat input, no HAZ softening |
| Dry-dock repair (no mains) | **Engine-driven inverter** | Independent power, portable |
| Thin sheet panels (RSW) | **MFDC resistance welding controller** | Precise current/heat control for SS panels |

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Heat Input Formula (the Most-Asked Derivation in KTU)

**Given:** Voltage $V$ (volts), current $I$ (amperes), travel speed $v$ (mm/min), and process thermal efficiency $\eta$ (dimensionless, fraction).

**To derive:** The heat input per unit length of weld $H.I.$ in **kJ/mm**.

---

### Step 1 — Define total electrical power delivered to the arc.

The arc is a resistive load, so electrical power dissipated is:

$$P_{arc} = V \cdot I$$

This is in **watts (W)** when $V$ is in volts and $I$ in amperes.

---

### Step 2 — Apply the process thermal efficiency.

Not all electrical energy enters the workpiece as heat. A fraction $\eta$ (called **arc efficiency** or **process efficiency**) is transferred to the weld pool. For different processes:

- SMAW: $\eta \approx 0.75 - 0.85$
- GTAW: $\eta \approx 0.50 - 0.70$
- GMAW: $\eta \approx 0.65 - 0.85$
- SAW: $\eta \approx 0.90 - 0.99$
- PAW: $\eta \approx 0.50 - 0.60$

So the **useful heat** transferred to the workpiece is:

$$P_{useful} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I$$

---

### Step 3 — Convert watts to joules per second.

Since power is energy per unit time, in 1 second the heat delivered is:

$$E_{1s} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \quad \text{(in joules per second)}$$

---

### Step 4 — Relate time to weld length using travel speed.

If the welding torch travels at speed $v$ in **mm/min**, then in **1 minute** it covers $v$ mm, and in **1 second** it covers $\dfrac{v}{60}$ mm.

So, the **length welded per second** is:

$$L_{1s} = \dfrac{v}{60} \quad \text{(in mm/s)}$$

---

### Step 5 — Compute heat input per mm of weld length.

Heat input per unit length = useful heat per second ÷ length per second:

$$H.I. = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v / 60} = \dfrac{60 \cdot \eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v}$$

The above is in **J/mm**. To convert to **kJ/mm**, divide by **1000**:

$$H.I. = \dfrac{60 \cdot \eta \cdot V \cdot I}{1000 \cdot v}$$

$$\boxed{H.I. = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \cdot 1000} \times 60 \quad \text{(kJ/mm, with } v \text{ in mm/min)}}$$

Or more compactly, when $v$ is in **mm/min** directly:

$$H.I. = \dfrac{0.06 \cdot \eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v} \quad \text{kJ/mm}$$

---

### Step 6 — Verification with standard units.

Let $V = 28$ V, $I = 250$ A, $v = 300$ mm/min, $\eta = 0.85$ (GMAW).

Substituting:

$$H.I. = \dfrac{0.06 \times 0.85 \times 28 \times 250}{300}$$

Numerator:

$$0.06 \times 0.85 = 0.051$$

$$0.051 \times 28 = 1.428$$

$$1.428 \times 250 = 357$$

Denominator: $300$.

$$H.I. = \dfrac{357}{300} = 1.19 \quad \text{kJ/mm}$$

This lies in the typical **GMAW range (0.5 – 2.0 kJ/mm)** for shipbuilding plate welds. ✔

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Duty Cycle Derating (For Sizing a Power Source)

**Given:** A power source rated at $I_{rated}$ amperes at a duty cycle of $D_{rated}$ (in %). The user wishes to operate it at a **higher** duty cycle $D_{actual}$ (where $D_{actual} > D_{rated}$).

**To derive:** The maximum allowable current $I_{actual}$ at the new duty cycle.

---

### Step 1 — Write the duty cycle definition.

Duty cycle $D$ is the fraction of a **10-minute (600 s)** period during which the source delivers rated current:

$$D = \dfrac{t_{on}}{t_{total}} = \dfrac{t_{on}}{600 \text{ s}}$$

For example, a 60 % duty cycle means the source delivers rated current for **6 minutes** and idles for **4 minutes** in every 10-minute window.

---

### Step 2 — Assume heating is proportional to $I^2 \cdot t$ (resistive losses dominate).

The internal heating of the power source (which determines whether it can sustain the load) is approximately proportional to $I^2$ (since $P = I^2 R$). Over one duty cycle, the total heating energy is:

$$Q_{heat} \propto I^2 \cdot t_{on} = I^2 \cdot D \cdot 600$$

---

### Step 3 — Equate heating at rated and actual conditions.

For the source to remain within its thermal limit:

$$I_{rated}^{\,2} \cdot D_{rated} = I_{actual}^{\,2} \cdot D_{actual}$$

---

### Step 4 — Solve for $I_{actual}$.

$$I_{actual}^{\,2} = I_{rated}^{\,2} \cdot \dfrac{D_{rated}}{D_{actual}}$$

Taking the square root:

$$\boxed{I_{actual} = I_{rated} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{D_{rated}}{D_{actual}}}}$$

---

### Step 5 — Numerical example.

A source is rated **400 A at 60 % duty cycle**. What current can it deliver at **100 % duty cycle** (continuous)?

$$I_{actual} = 400 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{60}{100}} = 400 \cdot \sqrt{0.60}$$

$$\sqrt{0.60} \approx 0.7746$$

$$I_{actual} \approx 400 \times 0.7746 \approx 309.8 \quad \text{A}$$

So, at continuous duty, the source can deliver only **~310 A** instead of 400 A. ✔

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Welding Cable Voltage Drop (For Shipyard Cable Sizing)

**Given:** Cable length $L$ (one-way, in metres), resistivity of copper $\rho = 1.724 \times 10^{-8}$ Ω·m at 20 °C, current $I$ (A), cable cross-section $A$ (mm²).

**To derive:** The voltage drop $\Delta V$ in the welding loop (there-and-back).

---

### Step 1 — Resistance of a single conductor.

$$R = \dfrac{\rho \cdot L}{A}$$

Note: $A$ must be in **m²** for SI consistency, so convert: $A_{m^2} = A_{mm^2} \times 10^{-6}$.

---

### Step 2 — The welding loop has two conductors (electrode lead + work lead).

$$R_{loop} = 2R = \dfrac{2 \rho L}{A}$$

---

### Step 3 — Voltage drop (Ohm's law).

$$\Delta V = I \cdot R_{loop} = \dfrac{2 \rho L I}{A}$$

Substituting $\rho = 1.724 \times 10^{-8}$ Ω·m and $A$ in mm² (converted):

$$\boxed{\Delta V = \dfrac{2 \cdot \rho \cdot L \cdot I}{A} = \dfrac{2 \cdot (1.724 \times 10^{-8}) \cdot L \cdot I}{A \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{0.0345 \cdot L \cdot I}{A} \quad \text{(V)}}$$

---

### Step 4 — Numerical check.

$L = 50$ m, $I = 400$ A, $A = 95$ mm².

$$\Delta V = \dfrac{0.0345 \times 50 \times 400}{95} = \dfrac{690}{95} \approx 7.26 \text{ V}$$

This is significant — nearly **25 %** of a typical arc voltage. Long shipboard cables (hulls are large!) must be **oversized** to limit this drop. ✔

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Power Source vs. Welding Process

| Process | V–A Type | Polarity | Typical Current (A) | Typical Voltage (V) | Preferred Power Source |
|---------|----------|----------|---------------------|---------------------|------------------------|
| SMAW (Stick) | CC | DCEP / DCEN | 50 – 400 | 18 – 30 | Rectifier / Inverter |
| GTAW (TIG) | CC | DCEN (steel/SS), AC (Al) | 50 – 350 | 10 – 20 | Inverter (with HF arc start) |
| GMAW (MIG/MAG) | CV | DCEP | 100 – 500 | 18 – 32 | Inverter + wire feeder |
| FCAW | CV | DCEP | 150 – 500 | 22 – 30 | Inverter + wire feeder |
| SAW | CV | DCEP / AC | 300 – 1500 | 28 – 44 | High-power rectifier/inverter |
| PAW (Plasma) | CC (pilot) + CC (main) | DCEP / DCEN | 50 – 400 | 20 – 35 | Inverter with pilot-arc unit |
| RSW (Spot) | Constant current pulse | — | 5000 – 20000 | < 10 V | MFDC controller |
| FSW | Mechanical (no arc) | — | — | — | Spindle + force control |
