| Aluminium (Thick) | > 20 | GMAW (MIG) | Ar + 50 % He | Deep, fast |
| Copper-Nickel (Heat Exchanger) | 2 – 6 | GTAW | Pure Ar | Narrow, controlled |
| High-Strength Steel (Class DH36) | 10 – 30 | GMAW | Ar + 5–10 % CO₂ | Controlled, low-H |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Shielding Gas Delivery in GMAW

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Gas Cylinder Bank] --> A2[Pressure Regulator]
    A2 --> A3[Floater Flow Meter]
    A3 --> A4[Gas Hose to Welding Torch]
    A4 --> A5[Torch Nozzle Exit]
    A5 --> A6[Shielding Envelope around Arc]
    A6 --> A7[Weld Pool Surface]
    A7 --> A8[Solidified Weld Bead]
    A6 --> A9[Arc Plasma Column]
    A9 --> A10[Consumable Wire Electrode]

    B1[Cylinder: Ar] --> A1
    B2[Cylinder: CO2] --> A1
    B3[Cylinder: Ar He Mix] --> A1

    C1[Moisture Separator] -.in-line.- A3
    C2[Heating Unit to Prevent Freeze] -.CO2 only.- A2

    A6 -.protects.- D1[Atmospheric N2]
    A6 -.protects.- D2[Atmospheric O2]
    A6 -.protects.- D3[Atmospheric H2O Vapor]
```

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Classification Diagram — Gas Taxonomy in Metal Arc Welding

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G[Gas in Metal Arc Welding]

    G --> G1[Inert Gases]
    G --> G2[Active Oxidizing Gases]
    G --> G3[Reducing Gases]
    G --> G4[Fuel Gases Oxy Fuel]

    G1 --> G1A[Argon Ar]
    G1 --> G1B[Helium He]

    G2 --> G2A[Carbon Dioxide CO2]
    G2 --> G2B[Oxygen O2 minor additive]

    G3 --> G3A[Hydrogen H2 minor additive]

    G4 --> G4A[Acetylene C2H2]
    G4 --> G4B[Propane C3H8]
    G4 --> G4C[Natural Gas CH4]

    G1A --> U1[Shielding in MIG TIG of Al SS]
    G1B --> U2[Deep penetration thick Al]
    G2A --> U3[Carbon steel MIG MAG hull plate]
    G2B --> U4[Arc stabilization]
    G3A --> U5[Austenitic SS enhanced fluidity]
    G4A --> U6[Oxy acetylene cutting]
    G4B --> U7[Preheat large steel sections]
    G4C --> U8[Preheat pipe joints]
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Process Flow — Oxy-Acetylene Cutting of Hull Plate

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Oxygen Cylinder High Pressure] --> P2[Oxygen Regulator set to 3 bar]
    P3[Acetylene Cylinder] --> P4[Acetylene Regulator set to 0.7 bar]
    P2 --> P5[Blowpipe Mixing Chamber]
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> P6[Heating Flame 3100 C]
    P6 --> P7[Steel Plate brought to red hot]
    P7 --> P8[High Purity Oxygen Jet Knocks Out Molten Oxide]
    P8 --> P9[Slag Blown Away]
    P9 --> P10[Clean Cut Kerf in Hull Plate]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
> *List any three inert gases used as shielding gases in Metal Arc Welding and state one specific shipbuilding application for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Argon (Ar):** Used in MIG/GMAW and TIG/GTAW welding of **aluminium superstructure components** and **stainless steel pipework** in ships. It is chemically inert and produces a stable arc.
2. **Helium (He):** Used in MIG welding of **thick aluminium marine plates** (>15 mm) where its high thermal conductivity produces deeper penetration and faster travel speeds.
3. *(Alternative — Argon-Helium Mixture)* Ar + 25–50 % He is used for **high-strength marine aluminium alloy** (e.g., 5083, 5086) plate welding in fast patrol vessels.

*(Award 1 mark per gas + application pair. Maximum 3.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
> *Why is pure Argon preferred over pure CO₂ as a shielding gas when welding austenitic stainless steel pipes on shipboard?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Pure CO₂ cannot be used for austenitic stainless steel welding because:
1. CO₂ dissociates in the arc into CO and O, releasing free oxygen that **oxidizes chromium**, destroying the **Cr-oxide passive film** which gives stainless steel its corrosion resistance. *(1 mark)*
2. This leads to **intergranular corrosion** in stainless fuel and seawater piping systems. *(1 mark)*
3. Pure Argon, being chemically inert, does not react with chromium and preserves the passive layer; for added fluidity, a small percentage of **2–5 % Hydrogen (H₂)** is sometimes mixed with Argon. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### ⭐ QUESTION A — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model — Q Choice Based on Module 1]**
> **(a)** With neat sketches, explain the **classification of shielding gases used in Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW)**. Discuss the effect of **CO₂ content** in Ar + CO₂ mixtures on weld bead shape, penetration, and mechanical properties of the weld joint.
> **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A shipyard performs GMAW on **12 mm thick DH36 grade mild steel hull plates** using a shielding gas mixture of **Ar + 15 % CO₂**. Given: Voltage $V = 26 \, \text{V}$, Current $I = 230 \, \text{A}$, Travel speed $v = 5 \, \text{mm/s}$, and arc efficiency $\eta = 0.78$. Calculate the **heat input per unit length** and **comment on its suitability** with respect to IACS standards.
> **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.A(a) — Classification of Shielding Gases (7 marks):**

**1. Classification Table:**

| Type | Examples | Behaviour in Arc |
|------|----------|-------------------|
| Inert | Argon, Helium | Do not react with weld pool |
| Oxidizing | CO₂, O₂ (≤5 %) | React with weld pool — produce oxides |
| Reducing | H₂ (2–5 %) | Reduces existing oxides |
| Mixed | Ar + CO₂, Ar + He, Ar + H₂ | Tailored performance |

*(2 marks for classification)*

**2. Effect of CO₂ content on weld bead characteristics:**

| CO₂ % | Bead Shape | Penetration | Spatter | Toughness |
|-------|------------|-------------|---------|-----------|
| 0 % (Pure Ar) | Convex, narrow | Shallow | Very low | Excellent (but only suitable for non-ferrous) |
| 5–10 % | Slightly convex | Moderate | Low | Good |
| 15–20 % | Flat to slightly concave | Deep | Moderate | Adequate for hull plates |
| 25–50 % | Concave | Very deep | High | Reduced impact toughness |
| 100 % (Pure CO₂) | Concave, undercut risk | Excessive | Very high | Poor; high oxygen inclusions |

*(3 marks for trend analysis)*

**3. Key engineering observation:** The **Ar + 15 % CO₂** mix is widely used in shipyards because it balances arc stability, bead wetting, and impact toughness at sub-zero temperatures (Class DH36 requirement: ≥ **34 J at −20 °C** per IACS UR W17).

*(2 marks for shipbuilding-specific conclusion)*

---

**Solution to Q.A(b) — Heat Input Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** State the formula.
$$H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \times 1000}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values.
\begin{aligned}
H &= \dfrac{0.78 \times 26 \times 230}{5 \times 1000} \\
  &= \dfrac{4664.4}{5000} \\
  &= 0.9329 \, \text{kJ/mm}
\end{aligned}

*(4 marks for substitution and arithmetic)*

**Step 3:** Interpretation.
\begin{aligned}
H &= 0.933 \, \text{kJ/mm}
\end{aligned}

According to IACS UR W17 for **DH36 hull plate GMAW**, the recommended heat input range is **0.8 – 1.8 kJ/mm** for plate thickness 10–20 mm. Our value of **0.933 kJ/mm** lies within the acceptable range, ensuring:
- Adequate fusion without lack of penetration.
- Controlled HAZ grain size for low-temperature toughness.
- Acceptable distortion in 12 mm plates.

*(3 marks for compliance commentary)*

---

#### ⭐ QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model — Alternative Module 1 Choice]**
> **(a)** Describe in detail the **role of gases in Oxy-Acetylene (Oxy-Fuel) cutting** as applied in shipbuilding. Explain why **oxygen** alone cannot sustain the cutting process and why **acetylene** is the preferred fuel. Include the relevant combustion equation.
> **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A shipyard uses pure Argon for TIG root-pass welding of a **stainless steel pipe (Grade 316L)** with nozzle exit diameter **12 mm** and gas exit velocity **10 m/s**. Calculate the **mass flow rate of Argon** and the **volume of Argon consumed per 8-hour shift**. Given: $\rho_{Ar} = 1.784 \, \text{kg/m}^3$.
> **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.B(a) — Oxy-Acetylene Cutting in Shipbuilding (7 marks):**

**1. The Three-Stage Process:**
- **Stage 1 — Preheating:** The steel plate is brought to its ignition temperature (~**870 °C** for mild steel) using the oxygen-acetylene flame. *(1 mark)*
- **Stage 2 — Cutting Oxygen Jet:** A separate high-pressure oxygen jet strikes the preheated steel, oxidizing it exothermically:
$$Fe + \tfrac{1}{2}\,O_2 \rightarrow FeO + \text{Heat (}\sim 200 \, \text{kJ/mol}\text{)}$$
The heat released sustains the reaction without further preheating. *(2 marks)*
- **Stage 3 — Slag Removal:** The molten iron oxide (FeO) is blown out of the kerf by the high-velocity oxygen jet, producing a clean cut. *(1 mark)*

**2. Why Oxygen Alone Cannot Cut:**
Pure oxygen at room temperature does not react with solid steel — the activation energy barrier is too high. A flame (from a fuel gas) is required to first raise the steel temperature above its ignition point.

**3. Why Acetylene is Preferred:**
- Highest practical flame temperature with oxygen (~**3,480 °C**).
- Combustion equation:
$$2\,C_2H_2 + 5\,O_2 \rightarrow 4\,CO_2 + 2\,H_2O + 1300 \, \text{kJ/mol}$$
*(2 marks for equation + explanation)*
- Compact, controllable flame; portable cylinders; rapid ignition.

**4. Shipbuilding Application:** Cutting hull plates up to ~**100 mm** thickness; flame-straightening deformed plate sections; bevel preparation for heavy plate welding. *(1 mark)*

---

**Solution to Q.B(b) — Argon Mass Flow Rate for TIG (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Compute nozzle exit area.
\begin{aligned}
A_{nozzle} &= \dfrac{\pi \cdot d_n^2}{4} \\
           &= \dfrac{\pi \times (0.012)^2}{4} \\
           &= 1.131 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^2
\end{aligned}

*(2 marks)*

**Step 2:** Compute mass flow rate.
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{Ar} &= \rho_{Ar} \times A_{nozzle} \times v_g \\
             &= 1.784 \times 1.131 \times 10^{-4} \times 10 \\
             &= 2.017 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{kg/s}
\end{aligned}

*(2 marks)*

**Step 3:** Compute volume flow rate.
\begin{aligned}
\dot{V}_{Ar} &= \dfrac{\dot{m}_{Ar}}{\rho_{Ar}} = A_{nozzle} \times v_g \\
             &= 1.131 \times 10^{-4} \times 10 \\
             &= 1.131 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s} \\
             &= 1.131 \, \text{L/s}
\end{aligned}

*(1 mark)*

**Step 4:** Volume consumed per 8-hour shift.
\begin{aligned}
V_{shift} &= 1.131 \, \text{L/s} \times 8 \times 3600 \, \text{s} \\
          &= 32572.8 \, \text{L} \\
          &= 32.57 \, \text{m}^3 \text{ of Argon per shift}
\end{aligned}

*(2 marks)*

**Engineering Note:** At STP, 1 liquid liter of Argon ≈ **700 gaseous liters**. Hence liquid Argon consumption ≈ **46.5 L of liquid per shift**, justifying shipyard investment in bulk cryogenic Argon tanks.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Examinations:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert travel speed (mm/s → m/s)** when using the heat input formula → causes 10× error. *(Loses 2–3 marks instantly.)*
> 2. **Confusing ionization energy with thermal conductivity** when justifying Helium's deep penetration. *(Examiner deducts 1 mark for unclear physics.)*
> 3. **Writing the acetylene combustion equation incorrectly** — students frequently write $C_2H_2 + O_2$ without balancing. The correct stoichiometry is **2 C₂H₂ + 5 O₂ → 4 CO₂ + 2 H₂O**.
> 4. **Stating "Argon is used for stainless steel" without naming a specific grade** (e.g., 316L for marine piping) → loses 1 mark on application specificity.
> 5. **Omitting units in final numerical answers** → KTU strict policy: *marks reserved separately for unit notation.*

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **Argon (Ar)** = Inert shield → Aluminium + Stainless Steel (GMAW/GTAW); density **1.784 kg/m³**.
- ⭐ **Helium (He)** = Hotter arc → thick-section Al; mixed with Ar typically at **25–50 %**.
- ⭐ **CO₂** = Cheap deep-penetration shield for mild steel hull plates; cannot be used on Al or SS.
- ⭐ **O₂ (1–5 %)** = Arc stabilizer, wetting agent.
- ⭐ **H₂ (2–5 %)** = Austenitic SS only — **never** for carbon steel (embrittlement).
- ⭐ **C₂H₂ + O₂** = Oxy-acetylene cutting — flame temp **~3,480 °C**.
- ⭐ **Heat input formula:** $H = \dfrac{\eta V I}{v \times 1000}$ in **kJ/mm** — **memorize**.
- ⭐ **IACS UR W17 range for DH36 plate GMAW:** **0.8 – 1.8 kJ/mm**.
- ⭐ **Mass flow equation:** $\dot{m} = \rho A v_g$ — applies to any shielding gas.
- ⭐ **Acetylene combustion equation (must balance):** $2 C_2H_2 + 5 O_2 \rightarrow 4 CO_2 + 2 H_2O$.
- ⭐ **Standard shipbuilding gas mix for hull plate:** **Ar + 8–20 % CO₂**.
- ⭐ **Standard shipbuilding gas mix for austenitic SS pipe:** **Pure Ar (root) + Ar + 2–5 % H₂ (cap)**.
- ⭐ **Classification Society Reference:** IACS UR W17, W11, W13 govern ship hull welding parameters.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Different Types of Oxy-Acetylene Flames
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OENST831 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Different Types of Oxy-Acetylene Flames ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition An **Oxy-Acetylene Flame** is the combustion product produced when **Acetylene (C₂H₂)** gas — a hydrocarbon fuel — is burned in the presence of **pure Oxygen (O₂)** inside a specially designed blowpipe or welding torch. By varying the precise volumetric ratio of Oxygen to Acetylene supplied to the torch nozzle, the resulting flame assumes three distinct physical and chemical personalities, each possessing unique temperature profiles, flame-zone geometries, and metallurgical effects on the parent metal. These three flame categories form the foundational knowledge block of Module 1 of the Joining Techniques in Shipbuilding syllabus. In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, the formal study of oxy-acetylene flames falls under the Course Outcome framework as follows: > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Course Outcome Alignment (OENST831)** > - **CO1:** *Recall the fundamental principles of various joining processes used in ship construction, including the classification and behaviour of oxy-fuel flames.* > - **Bloom's Level:** *L1 (Remember) → L2 (Understand)* The three canonical flame types recognized universally by classification societies (IRS, Lloyd's Register, DNV, BV) and codified under **ISO 5171 / ASME Section IX** are: | # | Flame Type | Alternate Name | Oxygen-to-Acetylene Ratio (by volume) | |---|------------|----------------|----------------------------------------| | 1 | **Neutral Flame** | Balanced / Stoichiometric Flame | **1 : 1** | | 2 | **Carburizing Flame** | Reducing / Carbonizing Flame | **< 1 : 1** (Acetylene-rich) | | 3 | **Oxidizing Flame** | Oxidising Flame | **> 1 : 1** (Oxygen-rich) | ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Gas Stove Knob" Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Intuitive Real-World Analogy:** > Imagine you are cooking rice on a domestic LPG stove. If you open the gas knob fully but only half-open the air-intake shutter, you get a **sooty, yellow, lazy flame** that blackens the vessel — this is analogous to the **Carburizing Flame** (too much fuel, not enough oxygen → unburnt carbon deposits). > If you open the air-shutter fully but only half-open the gas, you get a **hissing, harsh, blue, roaring flame** that scorches the vessel — this is analogous to the **Oxidizing Flame** (excess oxygen → metal becomes brittle and burns away). > When both the gas and air are in perfect chemical balance, you get a **clean, sharp, blue inner cone with a soft outer envelope** — this is the **Neutral Flame**, the metallurgist's "Goldilocks zone," used for nearly all critical shipbuilding welds. ### 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics - **Stoichiometric Combustion Temperature of Oxy-Acetylene:** **~3,160 °C** (the highest flame temperature of any commercially used fuel-gas combination). - **Acetylene Flame Propagation Speed in Oxygen:** **~2,480 m/s** (compared to ~2.9 m/s in air — which is why pure O₂ is mandatory). - **Ignition Temperature of Acetylene in O₂:** **~300 °C**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Combustion Chemistry — Foundation Layer Acetylene combustion proceeds in **two sequential reaction stages**. Understanding these two stages is the key to differentiating the three flame types. **Stage 1 — Primary Combustion (Inner Cone Reaction):** $$ \text{C}_2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; 2\text{CO} + \text{H}_2 \;+\; \Delta H_1 $$ This reaction occurs in the **bright luminous inner cone** (also called the *white cone* or *working cone*). It is only **partial oxidation** — carbon monoxide (CO) and hydrogen gas (H₂) are produced, not yet the final combustion products. **Stage 2 — Secondary Combustion (Outer Envelope Reaction):** The CO and H₂ produced in Stage 1 mix with additional atmospheric (or supplementary) oxygen: $$ 2\text{CO} + \text{O}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; 2\text{CO}_2 \;+\; \Delta H_2 $$ $$ \text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{H}_2\text{O} \;+\; \Delta H_3 $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why Two Stages?** > The two-stage chemistry is the *theoretical justification* for why neutral, carburizing, and oxidizing flames exist. The **ratio** of O₂ supplied to the torch nozzle determines whether Stage 1, Stage 2, or both stages go to completion. ### 2.2 Stage-by-Stage Breakdown of Each Flame Type #### 2.2.1 Neutral Flame (Stoichiometric) - **Volumetric Ratio:** O₂ : C₂H₂ = **1 : 1** (exactly 1.0 volumes of O₂ per 1.0 volume of C₂H₂). - **Chemistry Status:** *Stage 1 completes fully; sufficient O₂ remains in Stage 2 to convert all CO and H₂ into CO₂ and H₂O.* - **Visual Appearance:** - A short, sharp, well-defined **bright white inner cone**. - A **faint blue outer envelope** (almost invisible). - A **barely perceptible acetylene feather** (a wispy blue haze around the inner cone) — its *absence* confirms a true neutral flame. - **Temperature:** **~3,100 – 3,200 °C** in the inner cone tip (the working point). - **Net Chemical Effect on Weld Pool:** *None* — neither oxidising nor reducing. - **Shipbuilding Applications:** Mild steel plate welding, structural stiffener fabrication, pressure-vessel shell plate joints, general-purpose fabrication under **IACS standards**. #### 2.2.2 Carburizing Flame (Reducing Flame) - **Volumetric Ratio:** O₂ : C₂H₂ **< 1 : 1** (commonly **0.85 : 1** to **0.95 : 1**). Acetylene is in **excess**. - **Chemistry Status:** *Stage 1 completes fully; insufficient O₂ remains for Stage 2. Free carbon, unburnt C₂H₂ fragments, and excess H₂ remain in the flame envelope.* - **Visual Appearance:** - Inner cone is **slightly elongated and feathered**. - A **prominent reddish-white "acetylene feather"** or **"carbon feather"** extends 2 – 3 times the length of the inner cone. - The outer envelope is **bright yellow-orange** due to glowing soot particles. - **Temperature:** **~2,900 – 3,100 °C** (slightly lower than neutral due to incomplete combustion cooling). - **Net Chemical Effect on Weld Pool:** **Carbon is added** to the molten weld pool. The flame is *reducing* in nature (it pulls oxygen *out* of any metal oxides). - **Shipbuilding Applications:** - **Hard-facing** of propeller shaft liners. - **Brazing** of copper-nickel condenser tubes (with brass/silver filler). - **Surfacing** high-carbon steel rollers and wear plates. - Welding of **cast iron** (where carbon replenishment prevents white-hard brittle martensite). #### 2.2.3 Oxidizing Flame - **Volumetric Ratio:** O₂ : C₂H₂ **> 1 : 1** (commonly **1.1 : 1** to **1.5 : 1**). Oxygen is in **excess**. - **Chemistry Status:** *Stage 1 completes fully; excess O₂ remains in Stage 2 AND additional O₂ attacks the parent metal directly.* - **Visual Appearance:** - Inner cone is **shorter, sharper, more pointed** than neutral. - The **acetylene feather is absent**. - The outer envelope is **deeper blue, shorter, and hissing** (often with a slight purple tint). - **Temperature:** **~3,300 – 3,500 °C** (highest of the three — excess O₂ forces CO₂ and H₂O formation, releasing maximum heat). - **Net Chemical Effect on Weld Pool:** **Oxygen is added** to the molten metal, forming **iron oxides and slag inclusions**; the weld becomes **porous, brittle, and low in ductility**. - **Shipbuilding Applications:** *Almost never used for ship-hull welding.* Limited use in **fusion welding of brass and bronze**, where a thin oxide film (ZnO) is desired, and in **cutting of heavy plate** (where the excess O₂ jet sustains the cut). ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL EXAMINATION TABLE — Memorise before every KTU ESE:** | Parameter | Neutral Flame | Carburizing Flame | Oxidizing Flame | |-----------|---------------|-------------------|-----------------| | **O₂ : C₂H₂ Ratio** | **1 : 1** | **< 1 : 1** | **> 1 : 1** | | **Inner Cone Length** | 1× (reference) | 1.5× – 2× (elongated) | 0.5× – 0.8× (shortened) | | **Acetylene Feather** | Absent | **Prominent (reddish-white)** | Absent | | **Outer Envelope Color** | Faint blue | Yellow-orange (sooty) | Deep blue, hissing | | **Flame Temperature** | **~3,160 °C** | **~2,900 °C** | **~3,500 °C** | | **Chemical Effect** | Neutral | **Reducing (Carbon addition)** | **Oxidising (Oxygen addition)** | | **Weld Pool Behavior** | Calm, no sparking | Sparking, sooty deposit | Violent boiling, sparks | | **Suitable Metals** | Mild steel, low-alloy steel | Cast iron, high-C steel, brass, bronze brazing | Brass, bronze fusion; NOT for steel | | **Suitable for Shipbuilding?** | **YES — primary choice** | Selective (hard-facing, brazing) | **NO (avoid)** | ### 2.4 Stoichiometric Ratio Derivation (Molecular Basis) The *exact* volume ratio of 1 : 1 is derived from molecular stoichiometry: **C₂H₂ + O₂ → 2CO + H₂** → 1 mole of C₂H₂ reacts with **1 mole of O₂**. By **Avogadro's Law**, equal moles occupy equal volumes at constant temperature and pressure. Hence, **1 volume of C₂H₂ needs exactly 1 volume of O₂** for the primary reaction to proceed completely. The secondary reactions consume the *additional* atmospheric oxygen drawn in around the inner cone. --- ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility in Shipbuilding - **Hull Plate Fabrication:** Neutral flame is mandated by IACS UR W17 for shell-plating manual oxy-acetylene welding on vessels ≤ 12 mm thickness. - **Pipe Spool Welding:** Carburizing flame with low-melting silver brazing filler is used for joining copper-nickel (Cu-Ni 90/10 and 70/30) cooling pipes and condenser assemblies. - **Repair & Salvage:** Neutral flame remains the workhorse for in-situ repair of cracked hull plates in dry-dock conditions. - **Cutting Operations:** Although pure cutting uses a separate *oxy-propane* or *oxy-acetylene cutting torch*, the *oxidizing-flame principle* is conceptually reused for steel plate edge preparation (bevel cutting) prior to welding. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Stoichiometric O₂ : C₂H₂ Ratio We begin with the molecular formula of acetylene: **C₂H₂** (molar mass = **26 g/mol**). **Step 1 — Write the complete balanced combustion equation for acetylene:** The *full* combustion of acetylene produces CO₂ and H₂O: $$ \text{C}_2\text{H}_2 \;+\; x\,\text{O}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; a\,\text{CO}_2 \;+\; b\,\text{H}_2\text{O} $$ **Step 2 — Balance the carbon atoms.** Each C₂H₂ molecule has 2 carbon atoms, so: $$ a = 2 $$ **Step 3 — Balance the hydrogen atoms.** Each C₂H₂ has 2 H atoms, so: $$ 2b = 2 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; b = 1 $$ **Step 4 — Balance the oxygen atoms.** Total O atoms on RHS = $2a + b = 2(2) + 1 = 5$. Hence: $$ 2x = 5 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; x = 2.5 $$ **Step 5 — Final balanced equation for complete combustion:** $$ \text{C}_2\text{H}_2 \;+\; 2.5\,\text{O}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; 2\,\text{CO}_2 \;+\; \text{H}_2\text{O} $$ **Step 6 — Apply Avogadro's Law (Equal volumes at same T & P for gases):** $$ \frac{V_{\text{O}_2}}{V_{\text{C}_2\text{H}_2}} \;=\; \frac{n_{\text{O}_2}}{n_{\text{C}_2\text{H}_2}} \;=\; \frac{2.5}{1} \;=\; 2.5 $$ **Conclusion:** *For complete (theoretical) combustion of 1 volume of acetylene, 2.5 volumes of O₂ are required.* > [!NOTE] > **But the "Neutral Flame" uses a 1 : 1 ratio. Why the apparent contradiction?** > Because in the **oxy-acetylene blowpipe**, only the **primary (inner-cone) reaction** is fuel-rich: $$ \text{C}_2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO} + \text{H}_2 $$ > The remaining **1.5 volumes of O₂** needed for *complete* combustion are drawn **passively from the surrounding atmosphere** around the outer envelope (Stage 2 reactions). Therefore, the *primary* ratio of **1 : 1** is correct for what the torch nozzle supplies, and the *total* stoichiometric ratio is **2.5 : 1**. ### 3.2 Derivation of Flame Temperature (Adiabatic Flame Temperature) The adiabatic flame temperature $T_{\text{ad}}$ can be approximated using the enthalpy balance: $$ \sum_i n_i \, h_i(T_{\text{ad}}) \;=\; \sum_j n_j \, h_j(T_{\text{ref}}) \;+\; \Delta H_{\text{reaction}} $$ **Step 1 — Identify reaction enthalpies (at 298 K):** $$ \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{C}_2\text{H}_2) = +226.7 \text{ kJ/mol} $$ $$ \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{CO}) = -110.5 \text{ kJ/mol} $$ $$ \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{CO}_2) = -393.5 \text{ kJ/mol} $$ $$ \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{H}_2\text{O, g}) = -241.8 \text{ kJ/mol} $$ **Step 2 — Reaction enthalpy for the primary inner-cone reaction:** $$ \Delta H_1 = [2(-110.5) + 0] - [226.7 + 0] = -221.0 - 226.7 = -447.7 \text{ kJ/mol} $$ **Step 3 — Apply the enthalpy balance to find $T_{\text{ad}}$:** Using mean specific heat $\bar{c}_p \approx 35$ J/(mol·K) for the products (2CO + H₂): $$ T_{\text{ad}} \;=\; T_{\text{ref}} + \frac{-\Delta H_1}{n_{\text{products}} \cdot \bar{c}_p} $$ $$ T_{\text{ad}} = 298 + \frac{447{,}700 \text{ J/mol}}{3 \text{ mol} \times 35 \text{ J/(mol·K)}} = 298 + 4{,}262 = 4{,}560 \text{ K} $$ **Step 4 — Convert to °C and apply a *real-flame correction factor*** (heat losses via radiation, dissociation, and incomplete mixing typically reduce the theoretical temperature by ~30 %): $$ T_{\text{ad,\,real}} \approx 0.70 \times 4{,}560 = 3{,}192 \text{ K} \approx 3{,}160 \text{ °C} $$ This theoretical estimate matches the experimentally observed neutral-flame temperature of **~3,160 °C** within engineering tolerance. ### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Each Flame > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examination Decision Framework:** | Parent Metal | Recommended Flame | Justification | |---------------|--------------------|---------------| | Mild Steel (≤ 0.25 % C) | **Neutral** | Avoids C pickup (carburising) and oxide inclusions (oxidising) | | Medium Carbon Steel (0.25 – 0.5 % C) | **Slightly Carburising** | Replenishes carbon lost in the weld pool; prevents decarburisation | | High Carbon Steel (> 0.5 % C) | **Carburising** | Prevents brittle martensite formation; allows slow cooling | | Cast Iron | **Carburising (with preheat)** | Carbon replenishment + reducing atmosphere prevents white iron | | Stainless Steel (18/8) | **Neutral (strict)** | Any C pickup destroys corrosion resistance; any O pickup causes intergranular corrosion | | Copper & Copper-Nickel | **Neutral or slightly Oxidising** | Surface oxide (Cu₂O) helps dissolve impurities | | Brass & Bronze | **Oxidising** | Zn evaporation compensated; prevents Zn-loss porosity | | Aluminium & Alloys | **Neutral (special flux)** | Strictly avoid both C and O pickup | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Oxy-Acetylene Flame Type Selection Logic ```mermaid flowchart TD A([Shipbuilder Initiates Oxy-Acetylene Process]) --> B{What is the
Parent Metal?} B -->|Mild Steel| C[Use Neutral Flame
O2 to C2H2 = 1 to 1] B -->|Cast Iron| D[Use Carburizing Flame
O2 to C2H2 less than 1 to 1] B -->|Stainless Steel| E[Use Neutral Flame
Strict Carbon control] B -->|Brass or Bronze| F[Use Slightly Oxidizing
O2 to C2H2 greater than 1 to 1] B -->|Hard Facing| G[Use Carburizing
Carbon enrichment] C --> H([Stable Weld Pool
No Spatter]) D --> I([Sparking Weld Pool
Carbon Replenished]) E --> J([Bright Clean Weld
Corrosion Resistant]) F --> K([Zn Loss Compensated
Porosity Free]) G --> L([Hard Wear Surface
Achieved]) H --> M([Quality Weld Joint]) I --> M J --> M K --> M L --> M ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Two-Stage Combustion Chemistry ```mermaid flowchart LR A([Acetylene C2H2
+ Oxygen O2
enters nozzle]) --> B{Stage 1
Inner Cone
Reaction} B --> C[C2H2 + O2
produces 2CO + H2] C --> D{Stage 2
Outer Envelope
Atmospheric O2} D -->|If O2 Stoichiometric| E[Neutral Flame
CO and H2 converted
to CO2 and H2O] D -->|If O2 Insufficient| F[Carburizing Flame
Excess C2H2 remains
Carbon feather forms] D -->|If O2 Excessive| G[Oxidizing Flame
Excess O2 attacks metal
Oxide inclusions form] E --> H([Stable Shipbuilding Weld]) F --> I([Hard Facing or Brazing]) G --> J([Metal Oxidation
and Porosity]) ``` ### 4.3 Schematic Description of Flame Zones ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph FLAME[Oxy-Acetylene Flame Cross Section] direction TB A1([Bright White
Inner Cone
Stage 1 Reaction]) --> B1([Bluish Translucent
Outer Envelope
Stage 2 Reaction]) A1 --> C1([Acetylene Feather
if Carburizing]) end subgraph NOZZ[Blowpipe Tip] direction TB D1([Welding Nozzle
O2 + C2H2 Inlet]) end NOZZ --> FLAME FLAME --> WELD([Weld Pool
on Parent Metal]) ``` ### 4.4 Comparison Matrix — Visual Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD N([NEUTRAL FLAME]) --> N1[Ratio 1 to 1] N --> N2[Temp 3160 C] N --> N3[No Feather] C([CARBURIZING FLAME]) --> C1[Ratio less than 1 to 1] C --> C2[Temp 2900 C] C --> C3[Long Reddish Feather] O([OXIDIZING FLAME]) --> O1[Ratio greater than 1 to 1] O --> O2[Temp 3500 C] O --> O3[Short Sharp Cone] N1 --- C1 --- O1 N2 --- C2 --- O2 N3 --- C3 --- O3 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Q1, 3 Marks]** **Q1.** *Differentiate between a neutral flame and a carburizing flame with respect to their volumetric O₂ : C₂H₂ ratio, flame temperature, and a typical shipbuilding application.* **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Neutral Flame | Carburizing Flame | |-----------|---------------|-------------------| | O₂ : C₂H₂ ratio | **1 : 1** | **< 1 : 1** (typically 0.85 : 1) | | Flame temperature | **~3,160 °C** | **~2,900 °C** | | Acetylene feather | **Absent** | **Present (reddish-white, 2–3× inner cone)** | | Net effect | Neutral | Reducing (carbon added) | | Shipbuilding use | Mild steel hull plate welding | Hard-facing of propeller shafts; cast-iron repair | **[3 Marks: 1 for ratio, 1 for temperature, 1 for application]** --- > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Q2, 3 Marks]** **Q2.** *Why is an oxidizing flame generally avoided for welding mild steel plates in ship hull construction?* **Model Answer:** An oxidizing flame has excess oxygen (O₂ : C₂H₂ > 1 : 1). The surplus oxygen **combines with iron in the weld pool to form iron oxides (FeO, Fe₂O₃)** which become trapped as **slag inclusions**, leading to **porosity, brittleness, and reduced ductility**. This compromises the **impact toughness and fatigue life** required by IACS standards for ship hulls, hence oxidizing flames are avoided. **[3 Marks]** --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Model, 14 Marks Module Choice]** #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Choose either (a) or (b)* **(a) [7 Marks]** *With the help of a neat labelled sketch, explain the construction and working of an oxy-acetylene blowpipe. How does the blowpipe generate the three different flame types?* **(b) [7 Marks]** *Explain the two-stage combustion chemistry of an oxy-acetylene flame. Derive the volumetric O₂ : C₂H₂ ratio for a stoichiometric (neutral) flame and state its temperature.* **Model Answer for (a) [7 Marks]:** 1. **Construction [3 Marks]:** An oxy-acetylene blowpipe (welding torch) consists of: - **Oxygen inlet valve** (connected to a high-pressure O₂ cylinder via regulator). - **Acetylene inlet valve** (connected to an acetylene cylinder via regulator). - **Mixing chamber** (where O₂ and C₂H₂ are premixed in the correct ratio). - **Flashback arrester** (safety device to prevent flame travelling back into the hoses). - **Nozzle / Tip** (orifice that shapes and accelerates the mixed gas jet). 2. **Working [2 Marks]:** The two gases enter the mixing chamber at regulated pressures (typically O₂ ~ 2–3 bar, C₂H₂ ~ 0.5–1 bar). The mixed gas exits through the nozzle, where it is ignited. The flame's character depends on the **ratio** in which the two gases were mixed inside the chamber. 3. **Generation of three flames [2 Marks]:** - **Equal pressure setting** → Neutral flame. - **Higher C₂H₂ valve opening** → Carburizing flame. - **Higher O₂ valve opening** → Oxidizing flame. **Model Answer for (b) [7 Marks]:** 1. **Two-stage chemistry [3 Marks]:** - *Stage 1 (inner cone):* C₂H₂ + O₂ → 2CO + H₂. - *Stage 2 (outer envelope):* 2CO + O₂ → 2CO₂ ; H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O. 2. **Ratio derivation [2 Marks]:** From Stage 1, 1 mole C₂H₂ requires 1 mole O₂. By Avogadro's Law, this is a **1 : 1 volumetric ratio** for the torch inlet. The remaining 1.5 volumes of O₂ needed for full combustion are drawn from the surrounding atmosphere in Stage 2. 3. **Temperature [2 Marks]:** Adiabatic flame temperature of the neutral oxy-acetylene flame is **~3,160 °C**, the highest of any commercially used fuel-gas combination, making it ideal for fusion welding. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative choice* **(a) [7 Marks]** *Compare and contrast the visual appearance, chemical action on the weld pool, and shipbuilding applications of (i) neutral, (ii) carburizing, and (iii) oxidizing oxy-acetylene flames.* **(b) [7 Marks]** *A shipyard is tasked with the oxy-acetylene welding of 10 mm thick mild-steel hull plates. (i) Which flame type would you select and why? (ii) Explain the consequences of mistakenly using a strongly oxidizing flame for this job. (iii) Mention the relevant IACS / classification society guideline applicable.* **Model Answer for (a) [7 Marks]:** | Criterion | Neutral | Carburizing | Oxidizing | |-----------|---------|-------------|-----------| | Ratio | 1 : 1 | < 1 : 1 | > 1 : 1 | | Visual | Sharp white inner cone, no feather | Elongated cone, red-white feather | Short sharp cone, deep blue hissing | | Chemical | No change to weld pool | Carbon added (reducing) | Oxygen added (oxidising) | | Application | Mild-steel hull plate, structural work | Cast iron, hard-facing, brazing | Brass/bronze fusion; cutting | **[7 Marks: ~2 Marks per criterion + 1 for synthesis]** **Model Answer for (b) [7 Marks]:** 1. **Flame selection [2 Marks]:** For 10 mm mild-steel hull plate, **neutral flame (O₂ : C₂H₂ = 1 : 1)** is selected because it provides maximum heat (~3,160 °C) without carbon pickup or oxygen contamination, satisfying IACS UR W17 requirements for hull plate welding. 2. **Consequences of oxidizing flame [3 Marks]:** - Formation of **iron oxide slag inclusions** in the weld bead. - **Porosity** due to dissolved O₂ evolving during solidification. - **Loss of ductility and impact toughness** — dangerous for a hull plate subjected to wave-loading fatigue. - The weld would **fail radiographic and ultrasonic NDT** required by classification societies. 3. **IACS guideline [2 Marks]:** **IACS UR W17** (Unified Requirements for Welding) and **ASME Section IX** govern the procedure qualification for manual oxy-acetylene welding of mild-steel ship hull components. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Read Carefully:** > 1. **Confusing primary and total stoichiometric ratios.** Many students write "the ratio for a neutral flame is 2.5 : 1" without realising that the *torch-inlet* ratio is **1 : 1** and the remaining 1.5 volumes are atmospheric. *(−2 Marks)* > 2. **Stating "carburizing flame is hotter than neutral."** This is *false* — the **oxidizing flame is the hottest**, and the **carburizing flame is the coolest** of the three. *(−1 Mark)* > 3. **Forgetting the acetylene feather.** Examiners look for explicit mention of the **reddish-white feather** when describing the carburizing flame; omitting it loses marks. > 4. **Application mismatch.** Writing that oxidizing flame is "used for ship-hull welding" is a textbook error — it is **never used** for hull plates. *(−2 Marks)* > 5. **Not labelling flame zones.** Always draw the **inner cone**, **outer envelope**, and (where relevant) the **acetylene feather** in any sketch question. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1:** - ☐ Oxy-acetylene flame is produced by burning **C₂H₂** in pure **O₂**. - ☐ Three flame types: **Neutral (1:1)**, **Carburizing (<1:1)**, **Oxidizing (>1:1)**. - ☐ Combustion is a **two-stage process**: inner cone (primary) and outer envelope (secondary). - ☐ Primary reaction: **C₂H₂ + O₂ → 2CO + H₂**. - ☐ Secondary reactions: **2CO + O₂ → 2CO₂** and **H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O**. - ☐ **Neutral flame** temperature: **~3,160 °C** — highest practical flame temperature. - ☐ **Carburizing flame** has a **reddish-white acetylene feather**; adds carbon; used for cast iron and hard-facing. - ☐ **Oxidizing flame** has a **hissing deep-blue envelope**; causes porosity and brittleness; avoided in ship-hull welding. - ☐ Torch-inlet ratio for neutral = **1:1** (NOT 2.5:1 — that is total stoichiometric including atmospheric O₂). - ☐ **IACS UR W17** and **ASME Section IX** are the governing standards for oxy-fuel welding in shipbuilding. - ☐ **Avogadro's Law** is the bridge between molar ratio and volumetric ratio. - ☐ Always visually inspect the **inner cone length** and **acetylene feather** to identify flame type on the shop floor.

topics.title: Forehand and Backhand Welding Techniques and Function of Fillers and Fluxes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST831 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Forehand and Backhand Welding Techniques and Function of Fillers and Fluxes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1 — FORE HAND & BACK HAND WELDING, ROLE OF FILLERS & FLUXES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Forehand Welding (Forward / Pushing Technique)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (OENST831 — Module 1):**
> *Forehand welding* is a manual arc-welding technique in which the electrode is held inclined **in the direction of weld travel**, and the arc is pushed **forward into the unwelded base metal**, depositing weld metal ahead of the molten pool.

- The welder's hand motion is **forward** (away from the body), hence the name "forehand" or "push" technique.
- Electrode travel angle is generally **5°–15° from vertical**, tilted in the direction of progress.
- The arc force presses the molten puddle **into the joint ahead**, giving a **flat, wide, shallow bead profile**.
- Heat input is spread over a larger area of the base metal ahead of the arc — therefore **penetration is shallow**, ideal for **thin plates and surfacing work**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy (Real-World):**
> Imagine you are painting a wall with a roller, **pushing** the paint ahead of you. The paint spreads out flat and thin. Forehand welding does exactly the same — the molten weld metal is "pushed" ahead, spreading thinly across the surface with shallow penetration.

---

### 1.2 Backhand Welding (Backward / Trailing / Pulling Technique)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (OENST831 — Module 1):**
> *Backhand welding* is a manual arc-welding technique in which the electrode is held inclined **opposite to the direction of weld travel**, so the arc is directed **backward onto the previously deposited weld bead**, melting the base metal behind the puddle.

- The welder pulls the electrode **toward the body**, hence "backhand" or "pull" technique.
- Electrode travel angle is **5°–15° from vertical**, tilted **backward**.
- The arc force and heat concentrate **behind the puddle**, digging deep into the base metal — giving **deep, narrow penetration**.
- This is the **preferred technique for thick plates, root passes, and structural ship-hull welding**, where deep fusion is mandatory.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy (Real-World):**
> Imagine using a garden shovel to dig a trench while **pulling the soil toward you**. The deeper, concentrated force of the shovel digs into the earth. Backhand welding pulls the heat back into the base metal in the same way — concentrating energy to dig deep into the parent plate.

---

### 1.3 Why the Distinction Matters in Shipbuilding

In a ship hull, plates range from **4 mm (superstructure) to 50+ mm (hull bottom plating)**. The welder must therefore switch between forehand (cosmetic + thin plates) and backhand (deep penetration in thick plates) — and this distinction is a **favourite KTU viva and 3-mark question**.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Memory Hook (For KTU Viva):**
> - **F**orehand → **F**lat & wide (like a fan) → Fast, shallow, thin plate
> - **B**ackhand → **B**evel & deep (like a plough) → Slow, deep, thick plate

---

### 1.4 Function of Fillers in Welding

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> A *filler* (filler metal / filler rod / electrode core wire) is the consumable material added to the weld joint to **fill the groove, bridge the gap between parent plates, and supply alloying elements** that determine the mechanical and chemical properties of the joint.

In shipbuilding (SMAW, GMAW, GTAW, FCAW, SAW), the filler is the **load-bearing component** of the welded joint.

**Primary Functions of Fillers:**

1. **Joint Filling** — Mechanically fills the bevel/gap between two plates (volume replacement).
2. **Strength Contribution** — Transfers tensile, shear, and impact loads across the joint.
3. **Alloying Addition** — Provides elements like Mn, Ni, Cr, Mo to match base-metal strength (e.g., AH36/DH36 ship steel fillers).
4. **Deoxidation** — Contains Si, Mn, Ti, Al which scavenge dissolved oxygen.
5. **Weld-Metal Refinement** — Controls grain size and toughness of the solidified weld.
6. **Dilution Control** — Balances chemical composition between base metal and filler.

---

### 1.5 Function of Fluxes in Welding

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> A *flux* is a granular or coated chemical compound that, on heating, decomposes to **shield the weld pool, cleanse the metal of oxides, stabilize the arc, and form a protective slag** over the solidifying weld.

Fluxes are used in **SMAW (coating), SAW (granular), FCAW (tubular core), and brazing**.

**Primary Functions of Fluxes:**

1. **Atmospheric Shielding** — Produces a CO/CO₂/H₂ gas envelope that displaces O₂ and N₂ from the arc zone.
2. **Slag Formation** — Forms a molten slag layer that floats on the weld pool, protecting it from atmospheric oxidation until solidification.
3. **Deoxidization / Cleansing** — Reacts with oxides (FeO, SiO₂) and reduces them back to pure metal.
4. **Arc Stabilization** — Ionizes easily (contains K, Na, Ca compounds) to keep the arc smooth and continuous.
5. **Alloying Carrier** — Adds Mn, Si, Cr to the weld pool as it dissolves.
6. **Slag Detachability** — Controls the coefficient of thermal expansion of slag so it peels off easily (crucial for multi-pass shipbuilding welds).
7. **Weld-Bead Shaping** — Modifies surface tension of the molten pool to give a smooth, concave bead.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy for Flux:**
> Think of flux as the **"umbrella + detergent + alloy delivery boy"** for the weld pool. The **umbrella** shields it from rain (oxygen), the **detergent** cleans the dirty oxides, and the **delivery boy** drops in alloying elements for strength.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Forehand vs Backhand — Operational Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Forehand (Push) Welding — Step Logic

- **Electrode Orientation:** Tilted **forward** (5°–15°) in line with travel.
- **Arc Position:** Arc strikes **ahead** of the puddle → pushes molten metal forward.
- **Heat Distribution:** Heat is dispersed over a **wider, thinner area** of the base metal.
- **Penetration Profile:** **Shallow and wide** — bead looks like a wide, flat ribbon.
- **Slag Behavior:** Slag tends to run ahead of the puddle → easier to control for cosmetic work.
- **Speed:** Generally **faster** because the puddle flows easily into the joint.
- **Applications in Shipbuilding:**
  - Thin plate welding (< 6 mm)
  - Cosmetic cap passes (final layer) on butt joints
  - Horizontal and flat (1G/1F) positions
  - Lap and fillet welds on superstructure panels

### 2.1.2 Backhand (Pull) Welding — Step Logic

- **Electrode Orientation:** Tilted **backward** (5°–15°) opposite to travel.
- **Arc Position:** Arc strikes **behind** the puddle, melting the previously deposited bead.
- **Heat Distribution:** Heat is **concentrated** in a small area directly under the arc → deep melting.
- **Penetration Profile:** **Deep and narrow** — bead looks like a tall, narrow V-shape.
- **Slag Behavior:** Slag flows back over the solidified bead → cleaner root fusion.
- **Speed:** Slower, because deeper fusion requires controlled heat input.
- **Applications in Shipbuilding:**
  - Thick plate welding (> 10 mm) — hull bottom, side shell, deck plating
  - Root passes in V-groove butt welds
  - Vertical-up (3G) and overhead (4G) positions
  - Heavy structural joints where IACS UR rules demand deep penetration

---

### 2.2 Comparison Matrix — Forehand vs Backhand

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Favourite Comparison Table — Memorize for 14-Mark Questions:**

| Parameter | Forehand (Push) | Backhand (Pull) |
|---|---|---|
| Electrode Angle | 5°–15° **forward** of vertical | 5°–15° **backward** of vertical |
| Arc Direction | Ahead of puddle | Behind puddle |
| Heat Concentration | Dispersed / spread | Concentrated / focused |
| Penetration | Shallow, wide | Deep, narrow |
| Bead Shape | Flat, wide, smooth | Convex / tall, narrow |
| Welding Speed | Faster | Slower |
| Slag Flow | Runs ahead | Runs behind |
| Typical Use | Thin plates, cap passes | Thick plates, root passes |
| Position Preference | Flat (1G), Horizontal (2F) | All positions incl. 3G/4G |
| Skill Required | Lower | Higher |
| Heat Input per unit length | Lower | Higher |

---

### 2.3 Function of Fillers — Detailed Mechanistic Breakdown

| Function | Mechanism | Engineering Benefit |
|---|---|---|
| Joint Filling | Filler metal fills the bevel/gap volume | Replaces the removed base metal |
| Load Transfer | Fuses with base metal (metallurgical bond) | Transfers tensile/shear loads |
| Alloying | Adds Mn, Ni, Cr, Mo from filler chemistry | Matches AH36 / DH36 / EH36 steel properties |
| Deoxidation | Si, Mn in filler react with dissolved O₂ | Removes blowholes & porosity |
| Grain Refinement | Ti, Al, Nb act as inoculants | Improves impact toughness |
| Dilution Control | Adjusts the %mix of base + filler | Compensates for parent-metal loss |

**Example (Shipbuilding):**
For welding **EH36 grade hull steel** (yield ≥ 355 MPa), the typical filler is an **AWS E7018** or **E8018-C1** low-hydrogen electrode, where the "C1" denotes controlled Ni addition for low-temperature impact toughness (important for ships sailing in Arctic waters).

---

### 2.4 Function of Fluxes — Detailed Mechanistic Breakdown

| Function | Mechanism | Chemical Example |
|---|---|---|
| Shielding Gas Generation | Flux decomposes → releases CO, CO₂, H₂ | Cellulose, carbonates |
| Slag Formation | SiO₂, TiO₂, Al₂O₃ fuse into molten slag | Rutile (TiO₂) coatings |
| Deoxidation | FeO + Mn → MnO + Fe (slag floats) | Ferromanganese in coating |
| Arc Stabilization | K₂O, Na₂O, CaO have low ionization potential | Potassium titanate |
| Alloy Addition | Mn, Si, Cr released into weld pool | Ferro-silicon, ferro-manganese |
| Slag Detachability | CaO + Al₂O₃ tuning for thermal expansion match | Limestone (CaCO₃) |
| Bead Shaping | Surface tension modifiers | TiO₂ (rutile) for smooth beads |

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Welding Process Calculations)

> [!NOTE]
> These formulas are **commonly tested** in the KTU OENST831 ESE for filler/flux/heat-input related problems.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Heat Input | $HI = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ | kJ/mm | V=voltage, I=current, S=speed (mm/min) |
| 2 | Deposition Rate | $DR = k \cdot I$ | kg/h | k = constant for process |
| 3 | Filler Weight (per joint) | $W_f = A \cdot L \cdot \rho$ | kg | A=cross-section, L=length, ρ=density |
| 4 | Filler Wire Feed Rate | $W_{fr} = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot v_f \cdot \rho \cdot 60$ | kg/h | d=wire dia, v_f=feed speed (m/s) |
| 5 | Slag Volume Fraction | $V_s = \dfrac{m_{slag}}{m_{filler}}$ | % | Typical 5–15% for SMAW |
| 6 | Arc Force (approximate) | $F = k_f \cdot I$ | N | k_f ≈ 0.5–1.0 mN/A |
| 7 | Penetration Depth (empirical) | $P = a \cdot \dfrac{HI^{0.7}}{t^{0.5}}$ | mm | t=plate thickness |
| 8 | Dilution Ratio | $D = \dfrac{A_{base}}{A_{base} + A_{filler}} \times 100$ | % | Critical for ship plate welds |
| 9 | Preheat Temp (ASTM) | $T_p = \dfrac{350 \cdot [\%C]_{eq} + 75$ | °C | For thick ship plate |
| 10 | Carbon Equivalent (IIW) | $CE = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni+Cu}{15}$ | — | For ship plate classification |

> [!TIP]
> **Most likely 7-mark sub-question:** A numerical problem on **Heat Input (HI)** given V, I, S — practice it until automatic.

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility in Shipbuilding

- **IACS UR W11 / UR W31** compliance demands controlled heat input, dilution, and filler chemistry for hull welds.
- **Forehand technique** ensures cosmetic finish on **visible deck plating and superstructure welds** (aesthetic + corrosion-resist).
- **Backhand technique** ensures **root-pass penetration** in **sub-assembly panel welds** (structural integrity).
- **Filler selection** (e.g., E7018, E8018-C1, E11018-G) directly decides whether the vessel meets **Class Society rules (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS, BV)**.
- **Flux selection** (rutile, basic, cellulosic, acid) determines whether the weld is **X-ray quality** — essential for **butt-welded seams in pressure hulls and LNG carriers**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Heat Input Derivation (Most Expected KTU Numerical)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Heat Input (HI)** is the single most important parameter in any KTU welding numerical. The derivation below is the **board-exam standard** approach.

### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Definition:**
Heat input is the total thermal energy supplied by the arc per unit length of weld.

**Step 2 — Electrical Power in the Arc:**
The arc acts as an electrical load between the electrode and workpiece.

$$
P_{arc} = V \times I \quad \text{[Watts]}
$$

Where:
- $V$ = Arc voltage (Volts)
- $I$ = Welding current (Amperes)

**Step 3 — Energy per Unit Time:**
Since Power = Energy / time, the energy delivered per second is:

$$
E_{sec} = V \cdot I \quad \text{[Joules/second]}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Energy per Minute:**
Multiply by 60 seconds:

$$
E_{min} = V \cdot I \cdot 60 \quad \text{[Joules/minute]}
$$

**Step 5 — Energy per Unit Length of Weld:**
Welding speed $S$ is given in mm/min. Energy per mm of weld:

$$
HI = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S} \quad \text{[J/mm]}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to kJ/mm:**

$$
HI = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} \quad \text{[kJ/mm]}
$$

**Step 7 — Apply Thermal Efficiency Factor $\eta$:**
In practice, not all arc energy reaches the workpiece. A thermal efficiency factor is applied:

$$
\boxed{HI = \eta \cdot \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} \quad \text{[kJ/mm]}}
$$

Typical $\eta$ values:
- **SMAW:** $\eta \approx 0.75$–$0.85$
- **GMAW:** $\eta \approx 0.70$–$0.85$
- **SAW:** $\eta \approx 0.90$–$0.99$
- **GTAW:** $\eta \approx 0.50$–$0.70$

---

### 3.1.2 Worked Numerical — KTU Exam Style

> **Question:** A welder uses the **backhand technique** on 20 mm thick ship-hull plate with $V = 25$ V, $I = 220$ A, travel speed $S = 180$ mm/min. The process is SMAW with $\eta = 0.80$. Determine the **Heat Input**.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
HI &= \eta \cdot \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} \\
   &= 0.80 \times \dfrac{25 \times 220 \times 60}{180 \times 1000} \\
   &= 0.80 \times \dfrac{330{,}000}{180{,}000} \\
   &= 0.80 \times 1.833 \\
   &= 1.467 \;\text{kJ/mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $HI \approx 1.47$ kJ/mm.

**Engineering Interpretation:**
For 20 mm AH36 plate, the IACS allowable heat input for grade A is typically **1.5–3.5 kJ/mm** — so $1.47$ kJ/mm is **within limits**, indicating a sound backhand weld with adequate (but not excessive) penetration.

---

### 3.2 Dilution Ratio Derivation (Filler Function Numerical)

**Step 1 — Definition:**
Dilution is the percentage of base metal melted into the weld pool relative to the total weld metal area.

**Step 2 — Visualize the Weld Cross-Section:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{total} &= A_{base} + A_{filler} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Definition:**

$$
D = \dfrac{A_{base}}{A_{base} + A_{filler}} \times 100 \; \%
$$

**Step 4 — Worked Numerical:**
A weld cross-section reveals:
- Base metal melted area: $A_{base} = 30 \;\text{mm}^2$
- Filler metal deposited area: $A_{filler} = 20 \;\text{mm}^2$

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= \dfrac{30}{30 + 20} \times 100 \\
  &= \dfrac{30}{50} \times 100 \\
  &= 60 \; \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** 60% dilution means the final weld chemistry is **60% parent plate + 40% filler** — a **backhand deep-penetration weld** (high dilution, ideal for thick ship plate). A forehand weld would show ~25–35% dilution.

---

### 3.3 Carbon Equivalent (CE) Calculation — Filler Selection Aid

**Step 1 — IIW Formula:**

$$
CE_{IIW} = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni+Cu}{15}
$$

**Step 2 — Worked Numerical for AH36 Ship Plate:**
Given composition (typical AH36):
- C = 0.16%, Mn = 1.40%, Cr = 0.05%, Mo = 0.02%, V = 0.005%, Ni = 0.02%, Cu = 0.03%

$$
\begin{aligned}
CE_{IIW} &= 0.16 + \dfrac{1.40}{6} + \dfrac{0.05 + 0.02 + 0.005}{5} + \dfrac{0.02 + 0.03}{15} \\
         &= 0.16 + 0.2333 + \dfrac{0.075}{5} + \dfrac{0.05}{15} \\
         &= 0.16 + 0.2333 + 0.0150 + 0.0033 \\
         &= 0.4116
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**
Since $CE > 0.40$, **preheating is mandatory** (typically 100–150 °C) and a **low-hydrogen filler** (e.g., E7018) must be used to avoid hydrogen-induced cracking in the weld — a critical requirement per **IACS UR W31** for hull welding.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection Decision Tree for Shipbuilding

| Plate Thickness | Position | Recommended Technique | Recommended Filler | Recommended Flux Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| < 6 mm | Flat / Horizontal | **Forehand** | E6013 rutile | Light rutile coating |
| 6–12 mm | Flat / Horizontal | **Forehand (cap) + Backhand (root)** | E7018 basic | Basic low-hydrogen |
| 12–25 mm | All positions | **Backhand** | E8018-C1 / E7018 | Basic flux |
| > 25 mm (hull bottom) | All positions | **Backhand + SAW** | Filler wire + SAW flux | Fused flux (e.g., Lincoln 880) |
| Austenitic SS (LNG tank) | All positions | **Backhand GTAW root** | ER308L / ER316L | Argon backing gas |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This decision matrix is a **high-yield last-day revision chart** for KTU ESE preparation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Electrode Angle & Bead Profile

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Welder Hand] -->|Holds Electrode at 10° from vertical| B1[Forehand: Electrode Tilted FORWARD]
    A -->|Holds Electrode at 10° from vertical| B2[Backhand: Electrode Tilted BACKWARD]

    B1 --> C1[Arc Strikes AHEAD of Puddle]
    B2 --> C2[Arc Strikes BEHIND Puddle]

    C1 --> D1[Heat Spread Wide - Shallow Penetration]
    C2 --> D2[Heat Concentrated - Deep Penetration]

    D1 --> E1[Wide Flat Bead - Thin Plate Use]
    D2 --> E2[Narrow Tall Bead - Thick Plate Use]

    E1 --> F1[Ship Superstructure and Cap Passes]
    E2 --> F2[Hull Bottom and Root Passes]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of Filler + Flux in Weld Pool

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT STREAM"]
        A1[Parent Plate Base Metal]
        A2[Filler Rod or Electrode Core]
        A3[Flux Coating or Granular Flux]
    end

    subgraph ARC["ARC ZONE 4000-7000 C"]
        B1[Arc Heat Melts Base Metal]
        B2[Arc Heat Melts Filler]
        B3[Flux Decomposes to Shielding Gas]
    end

    subgraph POOL["MOLTEN WELD POOL"]
        C1[Deoxidation Reactions Mn Si scavenge O]
        C2[Alloying Elements Transfer from Filler and Flux]
        C3[Slag Floats Above Molten Metal]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["SOLIDIFIED WELD"]
        D1[Welded Joint with Mechanical Strength]
        D2[Slag Layer Peels Off After Cooling]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B2
    A3 --> B3
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    A2 --> C2
    A3 --> C2
    C1 --> D1
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D2
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Forehand vs Backhand Bead Profile (Cross-Section)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FH["FOREHAND WELD BEAD PROFILE"]
        F1[Base Plate]
        F2[Weld Metal - Wide and Shallow]
        F3[Penetration Depth - Small]
    end

    subgraph BH["BACKHAND WELD BEAD PROFILE"]
        B1[Base Plate]
        B2[Weld Metal - Narrow and Tall]
        B3[Penetration Depth - Large]
    end

    F1 --- F2
    F2 --- F3
    B1 --- B2
    B2 --- B3
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block — Flux Action Flow During Welding

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Flux Heated by Arc] --> P2[Decomposes to CO CO2 H2 Shielding Gas]
    P1 --> P3[Forms Molten Slag Layer]
    P2 --> P4[Displaces O2 and N2 from Weld Pool]
    P3 --> P5[Traps Oxides and Impurities]
    P4 --> P6[Clean Weld Pool with Low Porosity]
    P5 --> P6
    P6 --> P7[Solidifies to Sound Weld Metal]
    P3 --> P8[Slag Solidifies on Bead Surface]
    P8 --> P9[Slag Detaches on Cooling]
```

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU Exam:** If you cannot draw a sketch, the Mermaid-based "block-level functional architecture" shown above is **fully acceptable** to the examiner and is increasingly used as a substitute for free-hand sketches.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Differentiate between forehand and backhand welding techniques. Mention any three points.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Sl. No. | Forehand Welding | Backhand Welding |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Electrode inclined **in direction** of weld travel | Electrode inclined **opposite** to weld travel |
| 2 | Arc directed **ahead** of puddle → shallow penetration | Arc directed **behind** puddle → deep penetration |
| 3 | Bead is **wide and flat**, suitable for thin plates | Bead is **narrow and deep**, suitable for thick plates |
| 4 | Faster welding speed | Slower welding speed |
| 5 | Used for cap passes and cosmetic joints | Used for root passes and thick hull welds |

*(Any 3 well-explained points = 3 marks)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**List any six functions of flux in welding.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Shielding of weld pool** from atmospheric oxygen and nitrogen by releasing gases like CO and CO₂.
2. **Slag formation** — forms a molten layer that protects the solidifying weld.
3. **Deoxidation** — removes dissolved oxides (e.g., FeO) from the weld pool.
4. **Arc stabilization** — alkali compounds (K₂O, Na₂O) lower ionization potential.
5. **Alloying** — adds Mn, Si, Cr to the weld metal.
6. **Improves bead shape** — modifies surface tension for smooth finish.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **Forehand vs Backhand + Heat Input Numerical**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Compare forehand and backhand welding techniques with respect to (i) electrode angle, (ii) penetration, (iii) bead shape, (iv) slag flow, (v) applications in shipbuilding. Add a neat labelled sketch of electrode orientation for both techniques.**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

1. **Electrode Angle:**
   - *Forehand:* 5°–15° tilted **forward** (in direction of travel).
   - *Backhand:* 5°–15° tilted **backward** (opposite to travel).

2. **Penetration:**
   - *Forehand:* **Shallow and wide** — arc force spreads heat over a larger area.
   - *Backhand:* **Deep and narrow** — arc force concentrates heat on parent metal behind puddle.

3. **Bead Shape:**
   - *Forehand:* Flat, wide, smooth, fish-scale appearance.
   - *Backhand:* Convex, narrow, taller crown.

4. **Slag Flow:**
   - *Forehand:* Slag runs **ahead** of puddle → easier visual control.
   - *Backhand:* Slag runs **backward** over solidifying bead → helps root cleansing.

5. **Shipbuilding Applications:**
   - *Forehand:* Superstructure panels (4–6 mm), deck plating cap passes, cosmetic fillet welds.
   - *Backhand:* Hull bottom (>12 mm), root pass of butt welds, vertical-up (3G) and overhead (4G) welds per **IACS UR W11**.

6. **Sketch:** (Draw or describe)
   - *Forehand:* Electrode tilted forward → arrow showing travel direction → arc striking ahead.
   - *Backhand:* Electrode tilted backward → arc striking behind puddle.

*(1 mark per correctly explained point + 1 mark for neat sketch = 7 marks)*

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A welder in a shipyard uses the backhand technique on 18 mm thick AH36 plate with V = 24 V, I = 200 A, travel speed = 150 mm/min. For SMAW, take $\eta = 0.85$. Calculate (i) heat input per mm, (ii) deposition rate assuming k = 0.06 kg/h·A for the electrode, and (iii) comment on whether preheating is required if $CE_{IIW} = 0.42$.**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**(i) Heat Input (3 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
HI &= \eta \cdot \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} \\
   &= 0.85 \times \dfrac{24 \times 200 \times 60}{150 \times 1000} \\
   &= 0.85 \times \dfrac{288{,}000}{150{,}000} \\
   &= 0.85 \times 1.92 \\
   &= 1.632 \;\text{kJ/mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Deposition Rate (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DR &= k \cdot I \\
   &= 0.06 \times 200 \\
   &= 12 \;\text{kg/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Preheat Requirement (2 Marks):**
Since $CE_{IIW} = 0.42 > 0.40$, preheating **IS required**. Using the empirical relation:
$T_p \approx 350 \cdot CE - 75$
$T_p \approx 350 \times 0.42 - 75 = 72 \;°C$

So preheat to **~75 °C minimum** with low-hydrogen E7018 electrode to prevent hydrogen cracking (per **IACS UR W31**).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **Functions of Fillers and Fluxes**

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain in detail the functions of fillers and fluxes in arc welding. State at least four functions each.**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Functions of Fillers (3.5 Marks):**

1. **Joint filling:** Mechanically fills the bevel/gap between parent plates.
2. **Strength contribution:** Provides the tensile, fatigue, and impact strength of the joint.
3. **Alloying addition:** Supplies Mn, Si, Ni, Cr, Mo to match parent-metal chemistry (e.g., E7018 supplies Mn + Si; E8018-C1 supplies Ni for low-temp toughness).
4. **Deoxidation:** Contains deoxidizers (Mn, Si) that scavenge dissolved oxygen.
5. **Dilution control:** Filler volume controls how much parent metal is mixed into the weld.
6. **Weld-metal refinement:** Ti, Al in filler refine grain structure → better toughness.

**Functions of Fluxes (3.5 Marks):**

1. **Shielding:** Releases CO, CO₂, H₂ that displace atmospheric O₂ and N₂.
2. **Slag formation:** Forms molten slag blanket that protects the weld pool.
3. **Deoxidation / cleansing:** MnO, SiO₂ in flux react with FeO to form slag.
4. **Arc stabilization:** Alkali oxides (K₂O, Na₂O) provide easy arc ignition and stability.
5. **Alloy carrier:** Adds Mn, Si, Cr to the weld metal through slag-metal reaction.
6. **Slag detachability:** Flux composition tuned so slag flakes off cleanly after cooling.
7. **Bead shaping:** Modifies surface tension to give a smooth, concave bead profile.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A shipbuilding company welds 20 mm thick EH36 hull plate using SAW. The weld length is 5 m. Calculate (i) volume of weld metal if cross-section area = 80 mm², (ii) weight of filler required if density = 7850 kg/m³, (iii) approximate weight of slag if slag fraction = 8% of filler weight.**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**(i) Volume of Weld Metal (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= A \cdot L \\
  &= 80 \;\text{mm}^2 \times 5000 \;\text{mm} \\
  &= 400{,}000 \;\text{mm}^3 \\
  &= 400 \;\text{cm}^3 = 0.0004 \;\text{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Weight of Filler (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_f &= V \cdot \rho \\
    &= 0.0004 \;\text{m}^3 \times 7850 \;\text{kg/m}^3 \\
    &= 3.14 \;\text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Weight of Slag (3 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{slag} &= 0.08 \times W_f \\
         &= 0.08 \times 3.14 \\
         &= 0.251 \;\text{kg} \approx 251 \;\text{grams}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Practical Note:** This slag must be **removed after each pass** before the next pass is deposited — a routine QA step in IACS-certified shipyards.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks (Forehand/Backhand & Filler/Flux Questions):**

1. **Confusing direction of electrode tilt** — many students write "backhand means electrode goes back" but then tilt it the wrong way in the sketch. **Always recheck** the arrow direction.
2. **Confusing penetration depth** — backhand = deeper, forehand = shallower. **Mnemonic:** "**P**ull = **P**lough deep".
3. **Mixing up functions of filler vs flux** — filler provides **strength & alloying**, flux provides **shielding & slag**. Examiners deduct 1 mark if these are swapped.
4. **Forgetting the thermal efficiency factor $\eta$** in heat input calculations — common 1-mark loss.
5. **Wrong unit conversion** — travel speed must be in mm/min (NOT mm/sec) for the formula $HI = V \cdot I \cdot 60 / (S \cdot 1000)$.
6. **Skipping the IACS / Class Society reference** — adding "as per IACS UR W11 / W31" gives the examiner a reason to award the **full 14 marks** rather than 12.
7. **Not mentioning slag detachability** in flux functions — this is a **syllabus favourite** often missed.

---

## 📝 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Day Revision Flash-Card — KTU OENST831 Module 1**

- ☐ **Forehand = Push technique** → electrode tilted **forward** → arc ahead of puddle → **shallow** penetration → wide flat bead → used for **thin plates and cap passes**.
- ☐ **Backhand = Pull technique** → electrode tilted **backward** → arc behind puddle → **deep** penetration → narrow tall bead → used for **thick plates and root passes**.
- ☐ **Forehand speed > Backhand speed**, but backhand strength > forehand strength.
- ☐ **Heat Input formula:** $HI = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60 / (S \cdot 1000)$ — in **kJ/mm**.
- ☐ **Dilution Ratio:** $D = A_{base} / (A_{base} + A_{filler}) \times 100$ — high in backhand, low in forehand.
- ☐ **Filler Functions:** Fill, Strengthen, Alloy, Deoxidize, Refine grain.
- ☐ **Flux Functions:** Shield, Slag, Deoxidize, Stabilize arc, Alloy, Detach, Shape bead.
- ☐ **Key shipbuilding fillers:** E6013 (rutile), E7018 (basic LH), E8018-C1 (Ni for low-temp), Filler wire + SAW flux for thick plate.
- ☐ **CE > 0.40 → Preheat mandatory**; always use **low-hydrogen electrode**.
- ☐ **IACS rules UR W11 (welding) and UR W31 (hatch cover/sealing)** are the international benchmark for ship welds.
- ☐ **Backhand = penetration = structural weld**, **Forehand = cosmetic weld**.
- ☐ Slag detachability is critical in **multi-pass ship welds** — never skip slag-removal between passes.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Arc Welding: Various Arc Welding Processes and Carbon Arc Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OENST831 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Arc Welding: Various Arc Welding Processes and Carbon Arc Welding # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Arc Welding — The Foundational Principle > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > *Arc welding is a fusion welding process in which coalescence of metals is produced by heating them to the welding temperature with an electric arc established between a filler/consumable electrode (or a non-consumable electrode plus separate filler) and the workpiece. Shielding of the molten weld pool is provided by a gas, vapour, or slag generated from a flux, an externally supplied gas, or both.* The **electric arc** itself is a sustained, high-current electrical discharge through a normally non-conductive ionized gas column (plasma). Temperatures within the arc column routinely reach **6,000 °C to 20,000 °C**, which is more than sufficient to melt almost every engineering metal used in **shipbuilding** — mild steel, AH36/DH36 hull plates, stainless steel (304/316L for propellers and seawater systems), aluminium alloys (5083/6082 for superstructures and fast craft), and copper-nickel alloys for piping. ### Conceptual Analogy — The "Lightning Matchstick" Imagine striking an enormous **lightning bolt between two conductors and holding it steady**. Where the bolt touches the metal, the metal instantly melts into a small liquid puddle (the **weld pool**). If you slowly drag this controlled "lightning matchstick" along the joint, the puddle trails behind you, freezes, and welds the two plates into one continuous plate. That is exactly what an arc welder does — but instead of using a thundercloud, they use a **welding power source** (transformer, inverter, or rectifier) to force current through a small gap, ionizing the air into a **plasma channel**. In shipbuilding, this process is the *backbone* of hull fabrication: **over 90% of all joints in a modern merchant ship** are joined using some form of arc welding. ### 1.2 How an Arc is Established — The Physical Mechanism For an arc to strike, the **air gap** between the electrode tip and the workpiece must become electrically conductive. This happens in three stages: 1. **Short-Circuit Touch (Touch Start):** The electrode is momentarily touched to the workpiece. A high short-circuit current flows, and contact resistance heating causes the tip to melt slightly and release thermionic electrons. 2. **Withdrawal / Lift-Off:** The electrode is withdrawn by **2–4 mm**. The molten bridge stretches, necks, and ruptures — releasing a burst of ionized vapour. 3. **Arc Sustenance:** The ionized vapour column (plasma) has very low resistance. Electrons accelerate from the **cathode (−)** toward the **anode (+)** (or vice versa for AC). Their impact on the anode workpiece, plus resistive (I²R) heating in the column, generates the intense heat. > [!NOTE] > **Three Heat Generation Zones in an Arc:** > - **Cathode zone** (heat ~ 36% of total): emits electrons by thermionic/field emission. > - **Anode zone** (heat ~ 64% of total): absorbs the electron kinetic energy — *this is why the workpiece usually melts faster than the electrode in DCEN (Electrode Negative) polarity.* > - **Arc column (plasma)**: convective and radiative transfer; the brightest visible region. ### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU 2024 — Must Memorize) | Term | Definition | Typical Value (Shipbuilding) | |---|---|---| | **Open Circuit Voltage (OCV)** | Voltage at the output terminals with no arc | 50 – 80 V (DC), 70 – 100 V (AC) | | **Arc Voltage (Varc)** | Voltage measured across the arc while welding | 18 – 32 V | | **Welding Current (I)** | Current flowing through the arc | 80 – 600 A (typical) | | **Heat Input (E)** | Arc energy per unit length of weld | **0.6 – 1.5 kJ/mm** (IACS limits for ship plate) | | **Travel Speed (v)** | Speed of the torch/electrode along the joint | 4 – 15 mm/s | | **Electrode Polarity** | DCEP / DCEN / AC | DCEP for MMA, DCEN for TIG | | **Duty Cycle** | % of a 10-min cycle the machine can deliver rated current | 60 – 100% | ### 1.4 The Two Broad Families of Arc Welding **1. Consumable Electrode Processes** — the electrode itself becomes part of the weld metal: - Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW / MMA / Stick) - Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW / MIG / MAG) - Flux-Cored Arc Welding (FCAW) - Submerged Arc Welding (SAW) **2. Non-Consumable Electrode Processes** — the electrode is inert (tungsten); a separate filler rod may or may not be added: - Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW / TIG) - Plasma Arc Welding (PAW) - **Carbon Arc Welding (CAW)** — the subject of deep study in this module. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The General Arc Welding Process — Six Logical Sub-Systems Every arc welding process, regardless of type, comprises six logical sub-systems that must be controlled simultaneously: 1. **Power Source** — delivers V-I characteristics matched to the process (constant-current for TIG/MMA; constant-voltage for MIG/SAW). 2. **Arc Initiation & Stabilization** — touch start, scratch start, high-frequency (HF) start, or pilot arc. 3. **Electrode Feed System** — manual (sticks), gravity (SAW), push-pull (FCAW), or wire-drive motor (MIG). 4. **Shielding Delivery** — flux coating decomposition (MMA), external gas (TIG/MIG), flux-cored powder (FCAW), granular flux blanket (SAW), or gas from a carbon arc vaporizing the electrode. 5. **Travel / Manipulation Mechanics** — hand-held, tractor, column-and-boom, or robotic gantry. 6. **Weld Pool Solidification Control** — heat input, preheat, interpass temperature, and post-weld heat treatment (PWHT). > [!NOTE] > **Why "Shielding"?** At arc temperatures, molten steel greedily absorbs **oxygen, nitrogen, and hydrogen** from the atmosphere. Just 30 ppm of dissolved nitrogen can halve the impact toughness of a weld. The shielding mechanism (gas/slag/vapour) physically displaces the air from the arc zone. ## 2.2 Why Each Process Exists — A Logical "Why" Map | Engineering Need | Resulting Process | |---|---| | Cheap, portable, field repairable | **SMAW (Stick)** — flux-coated electrode carries its own shielding | | High deposition rate, continuous, automation | **GMAW (MIG/MAG)** — solid wire + shielding gas (Ar/CO₂) | | Very high deposition on thick plate (double-sided) | **SAW** — granular flux submerges the arc | | Excellent slag removal + high productivity (shipyards love it) | **FCAW** — tubular wire filled with flux powder | | Highest quality, no spatter, austenitic stainless & aluminium | **GTAW (TIG)** — tungsten electrode, inert gas | | Even higher energy density than TIG (narrow, deep welds) | **PAW** — constricted arc through a tungsten orifice | | Brazing, preheating, no filler | **Carbon Arc Welding (CAW)** — carbon/graphite electrode | ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All quantities below are examinable. Memorize the units exactly.** | # | Formula / Relationship | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $E = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{v \cdot 1000}$ | Heat Input per unit length (kJ/mm) | kJ/mm | | 2 | $E = \dfrac{0.06 \cdot V \cdot I}{v}$ | Equivalent form using travel speed in mm/s | kJ/mm | | 3 | $P = V \cdot I$ | Arc Power (gross) | W (or kW) | | 4 | $\eta = \dfrac{E_{abs}}{E_{gross}}$ | Heat transfer efficiency (process dependent) | dimensionless | | 5 | $E_{abs} = \eta \cdot E$ | Actual heat delivered to plate (for cooling-rate calcs) | kJ/mm | | 6 | $t_{8/5} = f\!\left(E_{abs},\, T_{pre},\, \text{plate thickness}\right)$ | Cooling time 800→500 °C — governs HAZ hardness | s | | 7 | $R = \dfrac{V_{oc}}{I_{sc}}$ | Static load ratio of power source | Ω | | 8 | $D = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{on}+T_{off}} \times 100\%$ | Duty cycle | % | | 9 | $V_{drop} = I \cdot R_{cable}$ | Voltage drop in welding cable (must be < 4% of OCV) | V | | 10 | $H_{fusion} = m \cdot c_{p} \cdot \Delta T + m \cdot L_{f}$ | Energy to raise mass *m* to melting and fuse it | J | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Shipbuilding Constants (IACS / Class Rules):** > - Maximum heat input for **DH36/EH36 high-tensile hull plate**: typically **1.5 – 2.5 kJ/mm** (process dependent). > - Maximum interpass temperature: **200 – 250 °C** for TMCP steels. > - Shielding gas purity for austenitic stainless (316L): **Argon 99.99% minimum**; dew point **< –60 °C**. ## 2.4 Static vs. Dynamic Power Source Characteristics - **Constant Current (CC) / Drooping V-I curve** — used for **TIG, MMA, PAW**. Current remains nearly constant even if arc length (and thus V) varies, giving stable hand welding. - **Constant Voltage (CV) / Flat V-I curve** — used for **MIG, FCAW, SAW**. Voltage is held constant; the wire-feed speed self-adjusts the current (arc length self-regulation). ## 2.5 Real-World Shipbuilding Utility In a modern Korean or Japanese shipyard (Hyundai, Tsuneishi, etc.), the steel plate is first **shot-blasted and primed**, then cut by **NC plasma/oxy**, formed into **sub-assemblies** (double-bottom tanks, side-shell panels, deck plates) by **one-sided FCAW + back-gouging + SAW**. The final **block-to-block erection** uses **FCAW with CO₂ shield** in the open yard, while **critical joints** (e.g., stem, stern tube, propeller boss) use **GTAW for root + SAW for fill** to ensure radiographic quality. This multi-process orchestration is exactly what the **IACS UR W11 / W12** rules require. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Heat Input (E) — The Most Tested KTU Formula This is the single most important derivation in the entire arc welding module. **Board questions on heat input appear almost every semester.** ### Statement of the Problem The welding operator measures: - Arc voltage, $V = 28\ \text{V}$ - Welding current, $I = 250\ \text{A}$ - The plate moves (or the torch moves) at a travel speed, $v = 6\ \text{mm/s}$ Determine the **gross heat input per unit length of weld** in kJ/mm. ### Step-by-Step Working **Step 1 — Identify the physical meaning of "heat input per unit length".** This is *arc energy deposited per second* ÷ *length of weld produced per second*. The quotient has units of energy/length, i.e. **J/mm** (which we will convert to kJ/mm). $$ E = \frac{\text{Energy per second}}{\text{Length per second}} = \frac{P}{v} = \frac{V \cdot I}{v} $$ with $P = V \cdot I$ in **watts** (J/s) and $v$ in **mm/s**, giving **J/mm**. **Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values.** $$ P = V \cdot I = 28\ \text{V} \times 250\ \text{A} = 7000\ \text{W} = 7000\ \text{J/s} $$ $$ E = \frac{P}{v} = \frac{7000\ \text{J/s}}{6\ \text{mm/s}} $$ **Step 3 — Perform the division.** $$ E = \frac{7000}{6}\ \text{J/mm} = 1166.67\ \text{J/mm} $$ **Step 4 — Convert to the KTU-preferred unit of kJ/mm.** $$ E = \frac{1166.67}{1000} = 1.167\ \text{kJ/mm} $$ **Step 5 — Cross-check with the IACS typical limit.** Since $1.167\ \text{kJ/mm} < 1.5\ \text{kJ/mm}$, this weld is *within* the typical Class-rule envelope for ordinary mild-steel hull plate.
