For **DH36**, the upper limit is tighter (≈ 1.0 kJ/mm), and the operator would have to reduce current or increase travel speed. ### General Closed-Form Derivation For a shipyard setting where $v$ is often quoted in **mm/min** (because plate-line speeds are slow), we need a unit-conversion step: $$ v\ [\text{mm/s}] = \frac{v\ [\text{mm/min}]}{60} $$ Substituting back into $E = V \cdot I / v$: $$ E\ \!\left[\frac{\text{J}}{\text{mm}}\right] = \frac{V\ [\text{V}] \cdot I\ [\text{A}]}{\left(\dfrac{v\ [\text{mm/min}]}{60}\right)} = \frac{60 \cdot V \cdot I}{v\ [\text{mm/min}]} $$ Converting to kJ/mm: $$ E\ \!\left[\frac{\text{kJ}}{\text{mm}}\right] = \frac{60 \cdot V \cdot I}{1000 \cdot v} = \frac{0.06 \cdot V \cdot I}{v\ [\text{mm/min}]} $$ **Final Master Formula:** $$ \boxed{\,E = \frac{0.06 \cdot V \cdot I}{v}\,} \quad \text{with } v \text{ in mm/min},\ V \text{ in V},\ I \text{ in A},\ E \text{ in kJ/mm} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Process efficiency (η)** further reduces the gross heat input to the *net heat absorbed by the plate*: > $$E_{abs} = \eta \cdot E$$ > Typical η values: MMA ≈ 0.70 – 0.85; MIG/MAG ≈ 0.70 – 0.85; SAW ≈ 0.90 – 0.99; TIG ≈ 0.40 – 0.60; PAW ≈ 0.45 – 0.65; **CAW ≈ 0.40 – 0.60** (very inefficient — a major disadvantage). ## 3.2 Derivation of Power-Source "Drooping" V–I Curve Slope A constant-current (CC) power source is approximated by: $$ V = V_{oc} - k \cdot I $$ where $V_{oc}$ is the open-circuit voltage and *k* is the internal droop coefficient (Ω). - At $I = 0$ (no arc): $V = V_{oc}$ (≈ 70 V for MMA). - At the rated welding current $I = I_{rated}$: $V = V_{arc}$ (≈ 22 V). Solving for *k*: $$ k = \frac{V_{oc} - V_{arc}}{I_{rated}} $$ **Numerical example:** $V_{oc} = 70$ V, $V_{arc} = 22$ V, $I_{rated} = 300$ A: $$ k = \frac{70 - 22}{300} = \frac{48}{300} = 0.16\ \Omega $$ This droop coefficient is what stabilizes the arc when the welder's hand wobbles ±2 mm: the current changes only slightly, preventing arc extinction. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Process-by-Process Comparison > [!IMPORTANT] > **This comparative matrix is THE most likely Part-B 14-mark question on this topic. Memorize it.** | Parameter | SMAW | GMAW (MIG/MAG) | GTAW (TIG) | FCAW | SAW | **CAW** | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Electrode type | Flux-coated consumable | Solid wire consumable | Tungsten (non-cons.) | Tubular wire consumable | Solid wire / strip | **Carbon/graphite (non-cons.)** | | Shielding | Flux decomposition gas | Ar / Ar+CO₂ | Argon | Flux core + optional gas | Granular flux | **Gas from vaporized carbon + external shielding** | | Polarity typical | DCEP | DCEP | DCEN | DCEP | DCEP | **DCEN (typical)** | | Current range | 30 – 500 A | 50 – 600 A | 5 – 400 A | 100 – 600 A | 200 – 1500 A | **50 – 800 A** | | Deposition rate | 1 – 4 kg/h | 2 – 8 kg/h | 0.5 – 2 kg/h | 3 – 10 kg/h | 5 – 30 kg/h | **0.3 – 1 kg/h** | | Heat input typical | 0.8 – 1.5 kJ/mm | 0.6 – 1.5 kJ/mm | 0.4 – 1.0 kJ/mm | 1.0 – 2.0 kJ/mm | 1.5 – 5.0 kJ/mm | **0.3 – 0.8 kJ/mm** | | Plate thickness | ≥ 1.5 mm | ≥ 0.8 mm | ≥ 0.5 mm | ≥ 1.0 mm | ≥ 5 mm | **≥ 1.0 mm** | | Weld quality | Good | Good–Excellent | Excellent | Good | Excellent | **Brazing-quality, soft** | | Slag | Yes | No | No | Yes | Yes | **No** | | Position capability | All positions | All positions | All positions | All positions | Flat / horizontal only | **Flat, horizontal** | | Automation ease | Manual | Easy (robotic) | Easy (orbital) | Easy | Easy (mechanical) | **Manual / semi-auto** | | Shipbuilding use | Field repair, tack | Panel lines, fillet | Root pass, stainless | Block joints, fillet | Double-sided butt | **Brazing, preheat, bronze overlays** | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Generic Arc Welding Process Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Power Source
Transformer or Inverter] -->|V and I controlled| B[Electrode Holder or Torch] B -->|Maintains 2 to 4 mm gap| C[Arc Plasma Column
6000 to 20000 C] C --> D[Workpiece Anode
Hull Plate] C --> E[Electrode Cathode or Anode
Consumable or Tungsten or Carbon] F[Shielding Delivery
Gas or Flux or Vapour] -->|Displaces atmospheric O2 N2 H2| C G[Torch or Holder Manipulation
Manual or Tractor or Robot] -->|Travel speed v mm per s| B H[Wire Drive Motor
for MIG SAW FCAW] -->|Pushes consumable wire| B I[Cooling and Solidification] --> C C --> J[Weld Pool
Liquid Metal] J --> K[Trailing Weld Bead
Solidified Joint] D --> J E --> J ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Decision Flow for Selecting an Arc Welding Process ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1[What is the base material?] --> Q2{Is it mild or high tensile steel?} Q2 -->|Yes, hull plate| Q3{Is it field work or workshop line?} Q3 -->|Field| R1[SMAW or FCAW with CO2] Q3 -->|Workshop line| R2[SAW for thick butt or FCAW for fillet] Q2 -->|No it is stainless or aluminium| Q3b{How critical is the joint?} Q3b -->|Critical root pass| R3[GTAW TIG] Q3b -->|General fabrication| R4[GMAW MIG with pulsed mode] Q2 -->|No it is copper nickel bronze| Q3c{Do you need brazing or preheating?} Q3c -->|Brazing| R5[Carbon Arc Welding CAW] Q3c -->|Preheating| R6[Carbon Arc gouging or heating] Q2 -->|No it is thin sheet under 1 mm| R7[GTAW with low current] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Detailed Carbon Arc Welding (CAW) Setup ```mermaid flowchart LR PS[DC Power Source
DCEP or DCEN] --> CB[Heavy Welding Cable
Cross section 70 to 95 sq mm] CB --> EH[Electrode Holder
Water cooled for high current] EH --> CE[Carbon or Graphite Electrode
Diameter 6 to 16 mm] CE -->|Maintains 3 to 6 mm arc gap| ARC[Carbon Arc
Up to 3500 C at tip] ARC --> WP[Workpiece Clamped to Earth Return] WP --> ERC[Earth Return Cable] ERC --> PS GAS[External Shielding Gas
Argon or Hydrogen Nitrogen mix] -->|Nozzle or torch shroud| ARC OP[Operator with Face Shield Shade 11 to 14] -->|Manual manipulation| EH FUM[Fumes of CO CO2 and Carbon Vapour] --> ARC FUM --> VENT[Local Exhaust Ventilation Mandatory] ``` ## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Arc Welding Process Taxonomy ```mermaid flowchart TB TOP[Arc Welding Family] --> C[Consumable Electrode Branch] TOP --> NC[Non Consumable Electrode Branch] C --> C1[SMAW Stick] C --> C2[GMAW MIG MAG] C --> C3[FCAW Flux Cored] C --> C4[SAW Submerged Arc] C --> C5[Electroslag and Electrogas] NC --> NC1[GTAW TIG] NC --> NC2[PAW Plasma Arc] NC --> NC3[CAW Carbon Arc Welding] NC1 --> SUB1[Manual TIG] NC1 --> SUB2[Orbital TIG for tubes] NC2 --> SUB3[Melt in mode] NC2 --> SUB4[Keyhole plasma mode] NC3 --> SUB5[Single Carbon Arc Brazing] NC3 --> SUB6[Twin Carbon Arc for preheating] NC3 --> SUB7[Air Carbon Arc Gouging] ``` ## 4.5 Mermaid Block — Heat Input Effect on HAZ (Heat Affected Zone) ```mermaid flowchart LR HI[Heat Input E in kJ per mm] --> L[Low E under 1.0] HI --> M[Medium E 1.0 to 2.0] HI --> H[High E above 2.5] L --> R1[Fine grain HAZ Good toughness] M --> R2[Coarse grain HAZ Moderate toughness] H --> R3[Brittle HAZ Risk of cracking] R1 --> SAFE[Approved by IACS Class] R2 --> SAFE R3 --> REJ[Rejected Mandatory repair] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Define an electric arc in the context of welding. State the temperature range of the arc column.** **Model Answer:** An electric arc in welding is a **sustained electrical discharge** through an ionized gas (plasma) column established between the **electrode and the workpiece**. The high temperature of the column is maintained by the kinetic energy of electrons accelerating through the potential difference. The arc column temperature typically lies in the range **6,000 °C to 20,000 °C**, while the anode spot (on the workpiece) reaches **~ 2,500 – 3,000 °C**, sufficient to melt all ferrous and most non-ferrous metals used in shipbuilding. The arc itself is governed by **arc voltage (Varc)** of 18–32 V and welding current of 50–600 A. ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **List any three differences between GMAW and GTAW.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | GMAW (MIG/MAG) | GTAW (TIG) | |---|---|---| | Electrode | Solid consumable wire (continuous) | **Non-consumable tungsten** (W + 2% ThO₂) | | Filler | Electrode itself acts as filler | Separate filler rod fed manually | | Shielding | Active gas (Ar + CO₂ / O₂) — *MAG*; or pure Ar — *MIG* | Inert gas (pure Ar or He) | | Deposition rate | Higher (2 – 8 kg/h) | Lower (0.5 – 2 kg/h) | | Slag | No slag | No slag | | Typical use in shipbuilding | Fillet welds, panel fabrication, automation | **Root pass of butts, austenitic SS, aluminium** | *(Any three clearly stated differences earn full 3 marks.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit errors in Heat Input** — Forgetting the **0.06** factor when travel speed is given in **mm/min**. A 1-mark penalty per wrong unit conversion is common. > 2. **Confusing DCEP / DCEN polarity** — Always write the *full form once* before using the abbreviation; examiners explicitly check this. > 3. **Calling CAW a "fusion welding" process** — It is often a **brazing** process when the base metal is not melted. Misclassifying the process is a 2-mark deduction. > 4. **Omitting shielding** — In any arc-welding answer, always state the **shielding mechanism**. Skipping this loses 1–2 marks. > 5. **Skipping diagrams** — A neatly labelled circuit/process diagram earns at least 1 free mark; a missing diagram almost always costs a mark. ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Comprehensive Process Overview **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, modified for 2024 scheme]** **(a)** Explain the **principle of arc welding** with a neat sketch showing the arc column, anode, cathode, and weld pool. List **four consumable** and **three non-consumable** electrode arc welding processes. (7 marks) **(b)** Compare **SMAW, GMAW, and SAW** in a tabular format covering electrode, shielding, polarity, deposition rate, position capability, typical heat input, and primary shipbuilding application. (7 marks) --- #### Model Solution for Q.A (a) — 7 Marks **Principle of Arc Welding (4 marks):** Arc welding is a **fusion joining process** in which the heat required to melt and fuse the base metal is generated by an **electric arc** struck between an **electrode** and the **workpiece**. The arc is a low-voltage, high-current gaseous discharge in which the gas molecules in the gap are ionized to form a plasma, which conducts electricity. The intense heat of the arc melts the base metal forming a weld pool, and (in consumable-electrode processes) the electrode itself melts and deposits filler metal into the pool. The molten pool is shielded from atmospheric contamination (oxygen, nitrogen, moisture) by an **inert gas, active gas, vaporized flux, or granular flux** until it solidifies as a continuous metallurgical joint. **Neat Sketch (2 marks):** [Examiner expects a labelled diagram showing: Power Source → Cable → Electrode Holder → Tungsten/Consumable Electrode → Arc (2–4 mm gap) → Weld Pool → Workpiece → Earth Return → Power Source. Shielding gas nozzle / flux coating to be shown. A textual description in the answer sheet is acceptable.] **Four Consumable-Electrode Processes (0.5 mark each):** 1. **SMAW** (Shielded Metal Arc Welding) 2. **GMAW** (Gas Metal Arc Welding — MIG / MAG) 3. **FCAW** (Flux-Cored Arc Welding) 4. **SAW** (Submerged Arc Welding) **Three Non-Consumable-Electrode Processes (0.5 mark each, round up):** 1. **GTAW** (Gas Tungsten Arc Welding — TIG) 2. **PAW** (Plasma Arc Welding) 3. **CAW** (Carbon Arc Welding) --- #### Model Solution for Q.A (b) — 7 Marks The answer must be presented as a **neat comparative table** with the columns listed. A typical model answer: | Feature | SMAW | GMAW | SAW | |---|---|---|---| | Electrode | Flux-coated consumable stick | Continuous solid consumable wire | Continuous consumable wire / strip | | Shielding | Gas + slag from flux coating | External shielding gas (Ar / Ar+CO₂) | Granular flux blanket | | Typical polarity | DCEP | DCEP | DCEP | | Deposition rate | 1–4 kg/h | 2–8 kg/h | 5–30 kg/h | | Position capability | All positions | All positions | Flat / horizontal only | | Typical heat input | 0.8–1.5 kJ/mm | 0.6–1.5 kJ/mm | 1.5–5.0 kJ/mm | | Primary shipbuilding use | Field tack & repair, block joints | Panel lines, robotic fillet welds | **Long double-sided butt welds** (e.g., shell strakes, deck plates) | *(1 mark for each correctly filled column, with the last row carrying the most weight for application.)* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Carbon Arc Welding (CAW) Deep Dive **[KTU University Exam — May 2023, modified for 2024 scheme]** **(a)** Explain the **principle, equipment, and procedure of Carbon Arc Welding (CAW)**. State whether it is a fusion or a non-fusion process and justify your answer. (7 marks) **(b)** Discuss the **advantages, limitations, and applications of CAW** in the shipbuilding industry. How does CAW differ from a typical fusion process like SMAW? (7 marks) --- #### Model Solution for Q.B (a) — 7 Marks **Principle (2 marks):** Carbon Arc Welding uses a **non-consumable carbon or graphite electrode** held in an electrode holder connected to one terminal of a **DC power source** (typically **DCEN — electrode negative**). The other terminal is connected to the workpiece (earth). An arc is struck between the **carbon electrode tip and the workpiece**, generating temperatures up to **3,500 °C at the carbon tip and 2,500–3,000 °C on the workpiece**. The carbon electrode does *not* melt and transfer metal; it merely provides the carbon-vapour ionized arc column. A **filler rod** (if needed) is fed into the arc pool manually by the operator. **Equipment (2 marks):** - DC welding power source (constant current, drooping characteristic) - Heavy-duty electrode holder with water-cooling provisions - **Carbon or graphite electrodes** of diameters 6, 8, 10, 12, 16 mm - Earth clamp and heavy return cable (cross-section ≥ 70 mm²) - External shielding gas supply (Ar / N₂ / H₂) for non-air operation; in air mode, a gas shield is sometimes still used - **Mandatory local exhaust ventilation** — the arc vaporizes carbon into toxic **CO and CO₂ fumes** **Procedure (2 marks):** 1. Clean the joint faces (oxy-cut or grind); set gap as required. 2. Connect electrode holder to **DCEN polarity** of the power source. 3. Strike arc by touch-and-withdraw (~ 3–6 mm gap). 4. Maintain arc length; for **brazing**, hold the arc primarily on the filler rod, letting only sufficient heat reach the base metal to *wet* the surface (without melting it). 5. For **welding mode**, raise current so the base metal actually fuses. 6. Move along the joint at a steady travel speed; manually dip filler rod as needed. 7. After completion, grind off any carbon splatter. **Fusion vs. Non-Fusion (1 mark):** CAW is **fundamentally a non-fusion (brazing-type) process** when used for joining dissimilar metals, copper, brass, bronze, or thin galvanised steel — the arc heats the base metal *below its melting point* while melting the filler. It **becomes a fusion process** only when the operator deliberately raises the heat input to melt the base metal (e.g., repair welding of steel castings). --- #### Model Solution for Q.B (b) — 7 Marks **Advantages (3 marks):** 1. **No slag formation** — clean, slag-free weld bead; no post-weld deslagging required. 2. **Excellent for dissimilar metals** — copper to steel, brass to cast iron, bronze overlays on shafts. 3. **Low heat input possible** — suitable for thin sheets, galvanised sheets, and bronze propellers without distortion. 4. Carbon electrode does not consume → no frequent electrode change; longer continuous operation. 5. **High temperature flame-like control** — useful for **preheating** thick plates (e.g., EH36, austenitic SS) before main welding. 6. Visible arc → operator can precisely control pool size — good for repair and tacking. **Limitations (2 marks):** 1. **Low process efficiency (η ≈ 0.4 – 0.6)** — a large fraction of arc energy is lost as light/heat/CO/CO₂. 2. **Toxic fumes** — carbon vapor reacts with atmospheric oxygen to form **CO and CO₂**; **mandatory fume extraction** is required. 3. **Low deposition rate** (0.3 – 1 kg/h) — unsuitable for thick-plate high-productivity shipyard work. 4. Carbon electrode **degrades (oxidizes)** in air — frequent dressing/trimming required. 5. Not suitable for non-flat positions (gravity-dependent pool). **Shipbuilding Applications (1 mark):** - **Bronze welding overlays** on stern tubes, rudder stocks, and propeller shaft liners. - **Brazing of copper-nickel piping** (Cu-Ni 90/10 and 70/30) used in seawater cooling systems. - **Preheating** of high-tensile plate edges before main SAW/FCAW runs. - **Repair of cast iron stern frames, rudder horns** (where arc must be controlled to avoid cracking). - **Air Carbon Arc Gouging (ACAG)** — a derivative process used for back-gouging, defect removal, and groove preparation (one of the most common uses in modern shipyards). **Difference from SMAW (1 mark):** | Aspect | CAW | SMAW | |---|---|---| | Electrode | Non-consumable carbon | Consumable flux-coated | | Filler | Separate rod (if needed) | Electrode itself | | Slag | None | Yes (must be chipped) | | Process type | Often brazing | Always fusion | | Fumes | Toxic CO/CO₂ | Less toxic | | Deposition | Very low | Moderate | --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1, Arc Welding (Examinable Points in Bold)** - **Arc welding = fusion + arc heat + shielding** — every answer must address all three. - **Arc establishment** sequence: *touch → withdraw → ionize → sustain* (3–4 mm gap is typical). - **Arc column temperature: 6,000 – 20,000 °C**; anode spot temperature ~ 3,000 °C. - **Heat input master formula:** $E = 0.06 \cdot V \cdot I / v$ (with $v$ in mm/min), unit = kJ/mm. **This is the most-tested formula of the module.** - **Process efficiency (η)**: MMA ≈ 0.75, MIG/MAG ≈ 0.80, SAW ≈ 0.95, TIG ≈ 0.50, **CAW ≈ 0.50**. - **Two families**: Consumable (SMAW, GMAW, FCAW, SAW) vs. Non-consumable (GTAW, PAW, **CAW**). - **Polarity memorization**: - **DCEP** (Electrode +, Workpiece −) → SMAW, GMAW, FCAW, SAW (most consumable) - **DCEN** (Electrode −, Workpiece +) → GTAW (TIG), **CAW** (carbon arc) - **Shielding memory trick — "MIG uses gas, FCAW uses flux powder + maybe gas, SAW uses granular flux, SMAW uses flux coating, TIG uses inert gas, CAW uses carbon vapor + optional gas"**. - **CAW essentials**: non-consumable carbon/graphite electrode, DCEN typical, **brazing-type** operation, **toxic fumes** (CO, CO₂), **low deposition**, used in shipbuilding for **Cu-Ni pipe brazing, bronze overlays, preheating, and air-carbon-arc gouging (ACAG)**. - **IACS Heat-input limits**: 1.5 – 2.5 kJ/mm for mild steel, ~ 1.0 kJ/mm for DH36/EH36; preheat 100–175 °C for thicker plates. - **Duty cycle** = ON time / (ON + OFF) × 100%, standard 10-min cycle. - **Static load ratio** of power source: $R = V_{oc} / I_{sc}$. - **Most common Part-B question pattern**: "Compare X, Y, Z processes" → *always* use a table; *always* include an application column. - **Most common Part-A question**: "Define heat input and calculate it for given V, I, v" — *show units at every step*. - **Welding cable cross-section rule**: voltage drop < 4% of OCV → typically ≥ 50 mm² copper for 300 A at 25 m. > [!NOTE] > **One-line exam-time mnemonic for process families:** > *"Some Great Ships Float Safely Across" → **S**MAW, **G**MAW, **S**AW, **F**CAW, **A**ircarbon-arc, **C**AW (and **G**TAW, **P**AW are the non-consumable twins).*

topics.title: Shielded Metal Arc Welding
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST831 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Shielded Metal Arc Welding

# 🛠️ Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW) — KTU Premier Notes

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📘 Formal Academic Definition

> **Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW)** is an *arc welding process* that produces coalescence of metals by heating them with an **electric arc** established between a **flux-coated consumable electrode** and the workpiece. The flux coating disintegrates during welding to generate a **shielding gas** and a **molten slag** that protects the weld pool from atmospheric contamination.

In the **AWS (American Welding Society)** terminology, the electrode is classified with the prefix **"E"** (e.g., **E6013**, **E7018**), where:
- **E** = Electrode
- **First two digits (XX)** = Minimum tensile strength in **ksi** (e.g., 60 = 60,000 psi)
- **Third digit (Y)** = Welding position and usability
- **Fourth digit** = Type of coating, current type, and impact toughness

This process is the **workhorse of shipbuilding fabrication yards** worldwide and is covered under **ISO 2560** (International Standards for SMAW electrodes).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The course *OENST831 – Joining Techniques in Shipbuilding* treats SMAW as the foundational arc-welding process because it is the most widely used manual process for hull plate joining, stiffener attachment, and onboard ship-repair work.

---

### 🍕 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cheese Grater & Candle" Analogy

Imagine you are melting cheese onto bread using a tiny, **controlled candle flame** 🔥, while simultaneously sprinkling **protective powder** over the melting cheese to keep dust and flies away.

| Real Welding Component | Candle-Cheese Analogy |
|---|---|
| **Welding arc (3,000–6,000 °C)** | The candle flame |
| **Consumable electrode** | The melting cheese stick being fed into the flame |
| **Flux coating** | The protective powder sprinkled over the melting cheese |
| **Shielding gas** | The invisible curtain of clean air around the flame |
| **Molten slag** | The hardened, crusty residue that you scrape off afterward |
| **Weld bead** | The perfectly melted, fused cheese-on-bread layer |

The "candle" (arc) melts the metal, the "cheese stick" (electrode) becomes the filler metal, and the "protective powder" (flux) creates a clean, contamination-free weld. **That's SMAW in plain English.**

> [!NOTE]
> In shipbuilding, SMAW is sometimes called **"Stick Welding"** because the electrode physically resembles a short stick or rod. It is also historically known as **Manual Metal Arc Welding (MMAW)** in British / Indian Standards terminology.

---

### 📌 Key Physical Constants & Process Metrics

| Metric | Typical Range / Value |
|---|---|
| **Arc Temperature** | **3,000 °C – 6,000 °C** (some estimates reach 20,000 °C at the core) |
| **Open Circuit Voltage (OCV)** | **50 V – 80 V** (AC or DC source) |
| **Arc Voltage (working)** | **18 V – 32 V** |
| **Welding Current** | **50 A – 500 A** |
| **Arc Efficiency (η)** | **0.75 – 0.85 (75 % – 85 %)** |
| **Shielding Medium** | Flux decomposition gases (mainly **CO, CO₂, H₂, H₂O vapor**) |
| **Electrode Diameter** | **2.0 mm – 6.0 mm** (common shipyard: **3.15 mm, 4.0 mm**) |
| **Standard Polarity** | **DCEP** (Direct Current Electrode Positive) for most mild-steel electrodes |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 🔬 Process Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The SMAW process can be decomposed into **six (6) simultaneous physical phenomena** occurring within the arc column:

1. **Electrical Initiation:** The welder strikes the electrode against the workpiece (a "scratch start" or "lift start"), creating a short circuit. As the electrode is withdrawn to ~**2–4 mm gap**, an **electric arc** ionizes the air gap and sustains current flow.

2. **Arc Generation:** The ionized plasma column carries current at temperatures of **3,000–6,000 °C**. This heat is split:
   - ~**60–70 %** melts the base metal (parent plate)
   - ~**30–40 %** melts the electrode tip (filler metal)

3. **Metal Transfer:** Molten droplets detach from the electrode tip and travel across the arc into the weld pool. This is governed by **pinch-effect forces, gravity, and surface tension**.

4. **Flux Decomposition:** The cellulose/rutile/low-hydrogen coating disintegrates due to intense heat, releasing:
   - **Gaseous shielding envelope** (CO, CO₂, H₂) — protects against O₂ and N₂
   - **Molten slag** (silicates, titanates) — floats on top of the weld pool

5. **Weld Pool Solidification:** As the arc moves forward, the molten pool cools. The slag solidifies **first** (since slag has a lower thermal conductivity), forming a protective crust.

6. **Slag Removal:** After cooling, the slag is chipped off with a hammer and wire-brushed, exposing the finished weld bead.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why flux is essential:** Without shielding, atmospheric **oxygen** would form iron oxides (FeO) and **nitrogen** would form iron nitrides (Fe₄N), causing **porosity, embrittlement, and cracking**. Flux is the *built-in* shield that makes SMAW portable — no external gas cylinder required.

---

### ⚙️ Critical Process Variables (The 5 M's of SMAW)

| Variable | Symbol | Typical Range | Effect on Weld |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Welding Current** | $I$ | 50–500 A | Higher $I$ → deeper penetration, more dilution |
| **Arc Voltage** | $V$ | 18–32 V | Higher $V$ → wider bead, flatter profile |
| **Travel Speed** | $S$ | 100–400 mm/min | Higher $S$ → less heat input, smaller bead |
| **Electrode Diameter** | $d$ | 2.0–6.0 mm | Larger $d$ → higher current capacity |
| **Electrode Angle** | $\theta$ | 45°–90° (drag/push) | Affects bead shape & spatter |

---

### 📊 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains every equation you must memorize for KTU ESE on this topic.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Boundary / Validity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Heat Input (HI)** | $HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ | $V$ = Voltage (V), $I$ = Current (A), $S$ = Travel Speed (mm/min), $\eta$ = Arc efficiency | Output in **kJ/mm** |
| 2 | **Heat Input (SI form)** | $HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S}$ | $V$ in V, $I$ in A, $S$ in mm/s | Output in **J/mm** |
| 3 | **Arc Efficiency (SMAW)** | $\eta_{SMAW} = 0.75 \text{ to } 0.85$ | Dimensionless ratio (Heat in workpiece ÷ Total arc heat) | Constant for the process |
| 4 | **Deposition Rate** | $R_d = \dfrac{W_e \cdot k}{t}$ | $W_e$ = electrode mass consumed (g), $k$ = mass-transfer coefficient, $t$ = time (min) | kg/hr common unit |
| 5 | **Melting Rate** | $R_m = k_m \cdot I$ | $k_m$ = melting coefficient (g/A·hr), $I$ = current (A) | Empirical, electrode-specific |
| 6 | **Penetration Depth (approx.)** | $P \approx k_p \cdot \dfrac{I}{S}$ | $I$ = current (A), $S$ = travel speed (mm/min) | Empirical relationship |
| 7 | **Power in Arc** | $P_{arc} = V \cdot I$ | $V$ in V, $I$ in A | Output in **Watts (W)** |
| 8 | **Energy per Unit Length** | $E_L = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{S}$ | V·A per mm/s → J/mm | Same physics as HI without $\eta$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall:** Many students confuse **Arc Voltage** ($V$) with **Open Circuit Voltage (OCV)**. The OCV is measured *before* the arc is struck; the *arc voltage* is the working voltage *during* welding. Use **arc voltage** in all heat-input formulas.

---

### 🌍 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Application | Why SMAW is Chosen |
|---|---|
| **Shipbuilding (Hull Plates)** | Excellent for thick mild-steel plates (10–50 mm), works outdoors in yard conditions |
| **Pipeline Construction** | Portable, no shielding-gas cylinder required, ideal for field joints |
| **Structural Steel Erection** | On-site column-beam welds in tall buildings |
| **Onboard Ship Repair** | Equipment is portable; repairs possible even in cramped, wet compartments |
| **Pressure Vessel Fabrication** | E7018 low-hydrogen electrodes meet ASME Section IX requirements |
| **Heavy Equipment Manufacturing** | Heavy earth-moving machinery, locomotive frames |

> [!NOTE]
> **Shipbuilding-Specific Note:** Approximately **60–70 %** of all arc-welding in a typical merchant ship hull is performed by **SAW (Submerged Arc Welding)** in the panel line, but **SMAW remains dominant** for *stiffener-to-plate* fillet welds, *bracket connections*, and *repair work* — making it indispensable for the naval architect / marine engineer.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 🔢 Derivation #1: Heat Input Equation from First Principles

**Objective:** Derive the standard KTU heat-input formula for SMAW.

#### Step 1 — Start with Electrical Power in the Arc

By definition, the electrical power dissipated in the welding arc is the product of arc voltage and arc current.

$$
P_{arc} = V \cdot I
$$

Where:
- $P_{arc}$ = Electrical power in arc (**Watts**, i.e., J/s)
- $V$ = Arc voltage (**Volts**)
- $I$ = Welding current (**Amperes**)

This is **Joule's Law** applied to the arc column.

---

#### Step 2 — Convert Power to Energy per Unit Time

Power in Watts equals **Joules per second**. So in time $t$ (seconds), the total energy released is:

$$
E_{total} = P_{arc} \cdot t = V \cdot I \cdot t
$$

---

#### Step 3 — Apply Process Efficiency

Only a fraction $\eta$ of this energy is actually transferred to the workpiece (the rest is lost as radiation, spatter, and convection). For SMAW, $\eta \approx 0.75 - 0.85$.

$$
E_{useful} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot t
$$

---

#### Step 4 — Express per Unit Weld Length

The welder travels a distance $L$ (in mm) in time $t$. Therefore travel speed $S = L/t$ (in mm/min or mm/s). Substituting $t = L/S$:

$$
E_{per\,length} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot L}{S}
$$

The heat input **per unit length of weld** is:

$$
HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S}
$$

---

#### Step 5 — Convert to Standard Engineering Units (kJ/mm)

Since KTU, AWS, and ISO all prefer **kilojoules per millimetre**, we multiply by **60** (to convert seconds to minutes) and divide by **1000** (to convert Joules to kilojoules). The result is the **gold-standard formula** memorized by every welder:

$$
\boxed{\;HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}\;\text{kJ/mm}\;}
$$

---

### 🧮 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Calculation)

> **Problem:** A shipyard welder is joining a **20 mm thick mild-steel hull plate** using SMAW with an **E7018** electrode. The following parameters are recorded from the welding data logger:
> - Arc Voltage: $V = 24$ V
> - Welding Current: $I = 200$ A
> - Travel Speed: $S = 250$ mm/min
> - Arc Efficiency: $\eta = 0.85$
>
> **Calculate the heat input in kJ/mm.**

#### Solution:

$$
HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}
$$

$$
HI = \dfrac{0.85 \cdot 24 \cdot 200 \cdot 60}{250 \cdot 1000}
$$

Step-by-step numerator:
- $0.85 \cdot 24 = 20.4$
- $20.4 \cdot 200 = 4080$
- $4080 \cdot 60 = 244{,}800$

Denominator:
- $250 \cdot 1000 = 250{,}000$

Final calculation:

$$
HI = \dfrac{244{,}800}{250{,}000} = 0.9792 \text{ kJ/mm}
$$

Rounded to two decimal places:

$$
\boxed{\;HI \approx 0.98 \text{ kJ/mm}\;}
$$

#### Interpretation for Shipbuilding:

For a **20 mm mild-steel plate**, the **maximum permissible heat input** (per **AWS D1.1 / IS 9595**) is typically **1.6–2.5 kJ/mm** depending on the steel grade. Our value of **0.98 kJ/mm** is well within safe limits — it provides adequate fusion without excessive grain coarsening in the heat-affected zone (HAZ).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Forgetting to multiply by **60** or divide by **1000** leads to answers that are off by **three orders of magnitude** (e.g., reporting 980 instead of 0.98). Always annotate your units at every step of the calculation.

---

### 🔢 Derivation #2: Deposition Rate from Melting-Rate Equation

**Objective:** Show that deposition rate is linearly proportional to welding current.

#### Step 1 — Empirical Foundation

Experimentally, for any given electrode type, the mass of electrode melted per unit time is found to be **directly proportional** to the welding current:

$$
R_m = k_m \cdot I
$$

Where $R_m$ = melting rate (g/hr or kg/hr) and $k_m$ = melting coefficient (g/A·hr).

#### Step 2 — Convert Mass to Volume of Weld Metal

The volume of weld metal deposited per unit time:

$$
\dot{V} = \dfrac{R_m}{\rho_{steel}}
$$

Where $\rho_{steel} \approx 7.85$ g/cm³.

#### Step 3 — Linearise

Since $k_m$ and $\rho_{steel}$ are constants for a given electrode/base-metal combination, deposition rate scales **linearly** with current — a fact confirmed by every welding handbook.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** This is why welders raise the current to increase productivity, but must also be careful not to exceed the **maximum current rating** stamped on the electrode (e.g., E7018-3.15 mm → max ~140 A; E7018-4.0 mm → max ~190 A). Exceeding it causes **overheating, spatter, and undercut**.

---

### 📈 Analytical Decision Matrix — Electrode Selection for Shipbuilding

| Shipyard Application | Recommended Electrode | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Hull plate butt joint (root pass)** | **E6010** | Deep penetration, DC+, cellulose-coated |
| **Hull plate butt joint (cap pass)** | **E7018** | Low hydrogen, high toughness |
| **Stiffener-to-deck fillet weld** | **E7014** or **E7018** | High deposition rate, good slag release |
| **Stainless-steel piping (304L)** | **E308L-16** | Matches base-metal composition |
| **High-tensile EH36 plate** | **E7018-1** or **E9018** | Low-hydrogen, high impact toughness |
| **Field repair (dirty/rusty steel)** | **E6011** | Cellulose coating tolerates surface contamination |
| **Vertical / overhead welds** | **E6010** or **E7018** | All-position capability |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 📊 Diagram 1 — SMAW Process Setup (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PowerSrc["Welding Power Source AC or DC 50 to 80 V OCV"]
    CablePos["Welding Cable Positive"]
    ElectrodeHolder["Electrode Holder Stinger"]
    Electrode["Consumable Electrode E7018 3.15 mm"]
    Arc["Electric Arc 3000 to 6000 degree C"]
    Workpiece["Ship Hull Plate Mild Steel"]
    CableNeg["Welding Cable Negative"]
    GroundClamp["Work Ground Clamp"]
    ShieldGas["Shielding Gas CO and CO2 from Flux"]
    Slag["Molten Slag Layer"]
    WeldPool["Weld Pool Molten Metal"]
    WeldBead["Solidified Weld Bead"]

    PowerSrc --- CablePos
    CablePos --- ElectrodeHolder
    ElectrodeHolder --- Electrode
    Electrode --- Arc
    Arc --- Workpiece
    Workpiece --- CableNeg
    CableNeg --- GroundClamp
    GroundClamp --- PowerSrc
    ShieldGas --- Arc
    Slag --- WeldPool
    Arc --- WeldPool
    WeldPool --- WeldBead
    Workpiece --- WeldPool
```

**Reading the diagram:** Electrical current flows from the **positive terminal** of the power source → through the **electrode holder** → across the **arc** → into the **workpiece** → back via the **ground clamp** to the **negative terminal**. Simultaneously, the **flux decomposes** to release **shielding gas** above the arc and **molten slag** floating on the weld pool.

---

### 📊 Diagram 2 — Complete Process Flow (Step-by-Step)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start(["Start: Welder strikes electrode"])
    Step1["Step 1: Scratch or lift start creates short circuit"]
    Step2["Step 2: Electrode withdrawn 2 to 4 mm arc gap"]
    Step3["Step 3: Arc ionizes air column current flows"]
    Step4["Step 4: Heat melts electrode tip and base metal"]
    Step5["Step 5: Metal droplets transfer across arc"]
    Step6["Step 6: Flux decomposes into shielding gas and slag"]
    Step7["Step 7: Weld pool protected from atmosphere"]
    Step8["Step 8: Arc travels forward solidification begins"]
    Step9["Step 9: Slag solidifies on bead surface"]
    Step10["Step 10: Welder chips slag and wire brushes bead"]
    End(["End: Finished weld ready for NDT inspection"])

    Start --> Step1 --> Step2 --> Step3 --> Step4 --> Step5 --> Step6 --> Step7 --> Step8 --> Step9 --> Step10 --> End
```

---

### 📊 Diagram 3 — Electrode Classification Decoder (AWS E-XXYZ)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E["E Electrode"]
    Tensile["XX Tensile Strength in ksi e.g. 60 or 70"]
    Position["Y Position and Usability e.g. 1 all position"]
    Coating["Z Coating and Current Type e.g. 8 low hydrogen AC and DC"]

    E --> Tensile --> Position --> Coating
```

**Example decoded:** **E7018**
- **E** → Electrode
- **70** → 70 ksi minimum tensile strength (~485 MPa)
- **1** → All-position welding capability
- **8** → Low-hydrogen iron-powder coating, AC or DCEP

---

### 📊 Diagram 4 — Heat Distribution in the Weld Zone

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    WeldMetal["Weld Metal Fusion Zone FZ"]
    HAZ["Heat Affected Zone HAZ"]
    Unaffected["Unaffected Base Metal"]
    Sub1["Grain Coarsened Region Peak Temp above 1100 degree C"]
    Sub2["Grain Refined Region 900 to 1100 degree C"]
    Sub3["Subcritical HAZ 600 to 900 degree C"]
    Parent["Parent Plate Original Microstructure"]

    WeldMetal --- HAZ --- Unaffected
    WeldMetal --- Sub1
    HAZ --- Sub2
    HAZ --- Sub3
    Unaffected --- Parent
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW) and list any four advantages of the process in the shipbuilding context.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW)** is an arc-welding process that produces coalescence of metals by heating them with an electric arc between a flux-coated consumable electrode and the workpiece. The flux coating disintegrates to provide shielding gas and molten slag that protect the weld pool from atmospheric contamination.

**Four advantages in shipbuilding:**

1. **Portability:** Equipment is light and can be carried to any location within the shipyard or onboard — essential for hull erection and repair in confined compartments.
2. **No external shielding gas required:** The flux coating self-generates the shielding atmosphere, making it ideal for **outdoor shipyard work** where wind would disperse an external gas shield.
3. **Versatility on thick plates:** Can weld mild-steel hull plates from **3 mm to over 50 mm** thickness with appropriate multi-pass techniques.
4. **Low capital cost:** Welding sets are far cheaper than MIG/MAG or SAW equipment — preferred by small and medium shipyards.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is the significance of the AWS electrode classification "E7018"? Decode each digit and state one application.**

**Model Answer:**

The classification **E7018** decodes as follows:

| Digit / Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **E** | Stands for **Electrode** (standard AWS prefix for arc-welding electrodes) |
| **70** | Minimum **tensile strength of 70 ksi** (~485 MPa) |
| **1** | Welding **position usability code "1"** → suitable for **all positions** (flat, horizontal, vertical, overhead) |
| **8** | **Coating and current type code "8"** → **low-hydrogen iron-powder coating**, usable on **AC or DCEP (Direct Current Electrode Positive)** |

**Application in shipbuilding:** Used as the **cap-pass electrode** in multi-pass butt welds of high-tensile hull plates (e.g., **AH36, DH36, EH36** grade steel) where low hydrogen content (< 8 ml/100 g) is essential to prevent **hydrogen-induced cracking (HIC)** and **cold cracking** in the HAZ.

---

### 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Set A]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat schematic the **principle of operation of SMAW**, clearly identifying the **arc, electrode, weld pool, slag, and shielding gas**. Discuss the **functions of the flux coating** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A welder uses SMAW to join two **18 mm thick mild-steel ship plates** with the following data:
- Arc voltage = 26 V
- Welding current = 180 A
- Travel speed = 200 mm/min
- Arc efficiency = 0.80

**Calculate:** (i) Heat input in **kJ/mm**, (ii) Heat input in **J/mm**, (iii) Comment on whether this heat input is suitable for the plate thickness. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Principle of Operation:**

When the welder strikes the electrode against the workpiece and withdraws it slightly (2–4 mm), an **electric arc** is established. This arc, with temperatures of **3,000–6,000 °C**, melts both the **electrode tip** (consumable filler) and the **base metal**. The molten metal from the electrode transfers across the arc in fine droplets and merges with the molten **weld pool** on the parent plate.

The **flux coating** on the electrode simultaneously decomposes and performs **three essential functions**:

| Function | Mechanism |
|---|---|
| **1. Shielding Gas Generation** | Cellulose decomposes → CO, CO₂, H₂ gases form an envelope around the arc, displacing atmospheric O₂ and N₂ |
| **2. Slag Formation** | Silicates, titanates, and oxides melt and float on top of the weld pool as **molten slag**, blocking oxygen ingress |
| **3. Arc Stabilization & Alloying** | Flux ingredients (e.g., Fe-powder, Mn, Si) stabilize the arc and add deoxidizers / alloying elements to the weld metal |

As the arc moves forward, the **weld pool solidifies** behind it, with the slag solidifying **last on top** as a hard crust. This slag is later **chipped off** to reveal the finished weld bead.

**Schematic:** Refer to **Diagram 1** (Section 4, Mermaid flowchart).

> *(Schematic explanation in written answer: Draw the power source, cables, electrode holder, electrode, arc, workpiece, ground clamp, weld pool, slag layer, and shielding gas envelope, labelling each with arrows and temperatures.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $V = 26$ V
- $I = 180$ A
- $S = 200$ mm/min
- $\eta = 0.80$

**(i) Heat Input in kJ/mm:**

$$
HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}
$$

$$
HI = \dfrac{0.80 \cdot 26 \cdot 180 \cdot 60}{200 \cdot 1000}
$$

Step-by-step:
- $0.80 \cdot 26 = 20.8$
- $20.8 \cdot 180 = 3744$
- $3744 \cdot 60 = 224{,}640$
- $200 \cdot 1000 = 200{,}000$

$$
HI = \dfrac{224{,}640}{200{,}000} = 1.1232 \text{ kJ/mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;HI \approx 1.12 \text{ kJ/mm}\;}
$$

**(ii) Heat Input in J/mm:**

We can use the SI form directly:

$$
HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S} \quad \text{(with } S \text{ in mm/s)}
$$

Convert travel speed: $S = 200$ mm/min $= 200/60 = 3.333$ mm/s.

$$
HI = \dfrac{0.80 \cdot 26 \cdot 180}{3.333}
$$

$$
HI = \dfrac{3744}{3.333} = 1123.2 \text{ J/mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;HI \approx 1123 \text{ J/mm}\;}
$$

**Verification:** $1.1232 \text{ kJ/mm} = 1123.2 \text{ J/mm}$ ✓ (Both methods agree.)

**(iii) Comment on Suitability:**

For an **18 mm thick** mild-steel plate, the recommended heat input range (per **AWS D1.1** and **IS 9595**) is approximately **1.0 to 2.0 kJ/mm** for conventional CVN-toughness requirements. Our calculated value of **1.12 kJ/mm** lies **comfortably within the recommended range**, indicating:
- ✅ Adequate fusion and penetration
- ✅ No excessive grain coarsening in the HAZ
- ✅ Acceptable mechanical properties (tensile strength and impact toughness)
- ✅ No risk of underbead cracking

**Conclusion:** The selected welding parameters are **well-suited** for this plate thickness.

---

#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Set B]**

**(a)** Describe the **classification of SMAW electrodes** as per **AWS system**, with at least **four examples** of commonly used electrodes in shipbuilding. Explain the **differences between E6010, E6013, and E7018** electrodes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** List the **main process variables** that affect weld bead geometry in SMAW and explain how each influences **(i) bead width**, **(ii) penetration**, and **(iii) heat input**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) AWS Classification of SMAW Electrodes:**

The American Welding Society (AWS) classifies SMAW electrodes using a **four-digit alphanumeric code**:

**Format:** `E XX Y Z`

| Position | Meaning | Example (E7018) |
|---|---|---|
| **E** | Electrode | E |
| **XX** | Minimum tensile strength in ksi | 70 → 70,000 psi (~485 MPa) |
| **Y** | Position and usability digit | 1 → All-position (F, H, V, OH) |
| **Z** | Coating type, current, and impact toughness | 8 → Low-hydrogen, AC/DCEP |

**Four commonly used shipbuilding electrodes:**

1. **E6010** — Cellulose-coated, DCEP, deep penetration, all-position. Used for **root passes**.
2. **E6013** — Rutile-coated, AC/DC, medium penetration, smooth arc. Used for **thin plates and sheet-metal work**.
3. **E7018** — Low-hydrogen iron-powder, AC/DCEP, high toughness. Used for **critical structural welds** in AH36/DH36 hull plates.
4. **E7024** — Iron-powder heavy-coated, high deposition rate. Used for **flat-position fillet welds** in stiffener-to-plate connections.

**Detailed Comparison:**

| Property | **E6010** | **E6013** | **E7018** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tensile Strength | 60 ksi | 60 ksi | 70 ksi |
| Coating Type | Cellulose (high gas) | Rutile (TiO₂) | Low-hydrogen iron-powder |
| Current Type | DCEP only | AC or DC | AC or DCEP |
| Penetration | Deep | Medium | Medium-deep |
| Slag Removal | Easy | Very easy | Moderate (needs chipping) |
| Position Capability | All-position | All-position | All-position |
| Hydrogen Risk | Higher (requires baking) | Low | **Very low (≤ 8 ml/100 g)** |
| Typical Use | Root pass on pipe/plate | Thin plate, sheet metal | Critical hull welds, high-tensile steel |

---

**(b) Process Variables Affecting Weld Bead Geometry:**

The **five primary variables** in SMAW that control bead shape are:

1. **Welding Current (I)** — **Most dominant variable.**
   - **Increase $I$** → **deeper penetration**, **wider bead**, **more deposition**, **higher heat input**.
   - **Decrease $I$** → narrower bead, shallower penetration, may cause lack of fusion.

2. **Arc Voltage (V)** — Controlled by **arc length**.
   - **Increase $V$ (longer arc)** → **wider, flatter bead**, **shallower penetration**, more spatter.
   - **Decrease $V$ (shorter arc)** → narrow, tall bead with deeper penetration.

3. **Travel Speed (S)** — How fast the welder moves along the joint.
   - **Increase $S$** → **less heat input**, narrower bead, shallower penetration; risk of **undercut** if too fast.
   - **Decrease $S$** → wider, deeper penetration; risk of **burn-through** on thin plates.

4. **Electrode Diameter (d)** — Determines current range and deposition volume.
   - Larger $d$ → higher possible current → deeper penetration and higher deposition rate.
   - Smaller $d$ → better for thin plates and out-of-position welding.

5. **Electrode Angle / Manipulation** — Drag vs. push angle.
   - **Drag (backhand) angle 5–15°** → deeper penetration.
   - **Push (forehand) angle 10–20°** → wider bead, shallower penetration.

**Summary Table:**

| Variable ↑ | Bead Width | Penetration | Heat Input |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Current (I)** | ↑ | ↑↑ | ↑ |
| **Voltage (V)** | ↑↑ | ↓ | ↑ |
| **Travel Speed (S)** | ↓ | ↓↓ | ↓↓ |
| **Electrode Dia (d)** | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Many students write *"high current gives low heat input"* — this is **WRONG**. Heat input is **directly proportional** to current.
> - Don't confuse **voltage** and **current** effects on bead shape — they have **opposite** effects on penetration.
> - Always include **units** in your numerical answers (kJ/mm, mm/min, V, A). Unanswered units cost **0.5–1 mark each** in KTU valuation.
> - For **long descriptive answers** (Part B, 7-mark sub-questions), always include a **labelled schematic diagram**. A missing sketch can cost **2 marks** even if your text explanation is perfect.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **SMAW** = Shielded Metal Arc Welding = "Stick Welding" — uses a **flux-coated consumable electrode**.
- **Arc temperature:** 3,000–6,000 °C; **Arc efficiency:** η = 0.75–0.85.
- **Heat input formula (memorize this exactly):** $HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ kJ/mm.
- **AWS Electrode code E-XXYZ:** E = Electrode, XX = Tensile strength (ksi), Y = Position, Z = Coating/Current.
- **E7018** is the **most common shipbuilding electrode** for high-tensile hull plates due to its **low-hydrogen** coating.
- **E6010** = cellulose, DCEP, deep penetration → root pass.
- **E6013** = rutile, AC/DC, smooth arc → thin plates.
- **Flux functions:** (1) Shielding gas generation, (2) Slag formation, (3) Arc stabilization & deoxidation.
- **Five process variables:** Current, Voltage, Travel Speed, Electrode Diameter, Electrode Angle.
- **Shipbuilding uses:** Hull plate joints, stiffener-to-deck fillets, structural brackets, repair work.
- **Heat input limits (typical):** For 18–25 mm mild-steel hull plates, HI should stay within **1.0–2.0 kJ/mm** to avoid HAZ embrittlement.
- **Slag must be removed** between passes in multi-pass welding to ensure full fusion.
- **Polarity for SMAW:** Mostly **DCEP (Direct Current Electrode Positive)** — gives deeper penetration than DCEN.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Study Tip:** Practice at least **3–4 numerical problems** on heat-input calculation (varying voltage, current, and travel speed). The 14-mark Part B questions on SMAW **always include a numerical**, and the 7-mark descriptive sub-question **always demands a labelled schematic**. Master both, and you will score full marks on this topic.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Submerged Arc Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST831 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Submerged Arc Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

**Submerged Arc Welding (SAW)** is an *arc welding process* in which the welding arc is struck and maintained beneath a blanket of *granular, fusible flux* fed continuously onto the workpiece. The arc, the molten weld pool, and the adjacent solid metal remain completely covered by the flux, making the arc invisible and the process essentially smokeless. A *continuous solid wire* (or multiple wires) electrode is fed mechanically into the arc, and the surrounding flux melts partially to form a protective slag and shielding gas envelope around the weld zone.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> As per Module 1 of **OENST831 – Joining Techniques in Shipbuilding**, SAW is treated as the *primary high-deposition-rate joining process* used for the longitudinal and transverse butts of hull plates, decks, bulkheads, and superstructures where plate thicknesses range from **6 mm to over 50 mm**. Students must understand its operating principle, equipment, flux chemistry, process parameters, and applicability to shipyard panel-line production.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are melting two ice-cream sticks together using a candle. The flame flickers, soot gets into the joint, and the joint becomes weak. Now imagine doing the same thing, but you place a thick **blanket of dry, powdery sand** on top of the joint, push a hot wire through it, and let the heat melt the sticks under the sand.

- The sand **blocks air** from reaching the joint.
- The sand **becomes a glassy slag** that cleans the molten metal.
- The flame (arc) is **completely hidden** under the sand — no bright light, no smoke, no spatter.
- You can push the wire continuously because the sand keeps refilling itself.

That, in essence, is **Submerged Arc Welding**: a *“welding-under-blanket”* process — the arc is **submerged**, and **arc welding** happens inside this flux blanket.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Identifier Metrics (Bold):**
> - Operating voltage: **20 – 45 V**
> - Operating current: **200 – 1500 A** (often 600–1000 A)
> - Wire diameter: **1.6 – 4.8 mm**
> - Deposition rate: **up to 20 kg/hour** (single wire), **>40 kg/hr** (tandem/twin-wire)
> - Shielding: **Granular flux (no external gas needed)**

## 1.3 Place in Shipbuilding Context

In a shipbuilding panel line, the flat steel plates of the hull are first shot-blasted, then tack-welded together and finally seam-welded using automated carriages running on tracks along the panel edges. Because the welds are *kilometres long* and the plates are *thick*, shipyards rely on SAW for its:
- extremely high productivity,
- uniform, defect-free welds,
- low operator skill demand (mechanised), and
- ability to weld in the **1G (flat)**, **2F (horizontal fillet)** and **1F/2F (flat fillet)** positions via special fixtures.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Process Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The SAW process can be decomposed into the following sequential logical events occurring every fraction of a second at the arc zone:

1. **Flux Delivery** – Granular flux (grain size **0.4 – 2.0 mm**) is gravity-fed from a hopper mounted ahead of the welding head. A small mound of flux pre-deposits in front of the arc zone.
2. **Arc Ignition & Concealment** – As current flows through the contact tip to the wire, an arc is struck. The arc melts a small pool in the base metal and partially fuses the surrounding flux grains.
3. **Metal Transfer** – The continuously fed wire melts at a rate proportional to the current (melt-off rate). Metal droplets transfer through the arc into the weld pool under the flux blanket.
4. **Pool Protection** – Unmelted flux grains float on the weld pool, shielding it from atmospheric oxygen and nitrogen. This gives superior weld metal cleanliness.
5. **Slag Formation** – Some flux melts, floats to the top of the weld pool, and solidifies as a slag crust after the heat source moves away.
6. **Solidification** – The weld metal solidifies behind the arc, joining the parent plates.
7. **Spent Flux Recovery** – After the head passes, unfused flux is collected by a vacuum recovery unit and recycled (typically **>60% recovery rate** in modern shipyard hoppers).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why “submerged”?** The arc column, the wire tip, and the entire molten weld pool lie **beneath the top surface of the unmelted flux bed** at all times. The arc is therefore invisible, and the process is one of the safest high-current arc processes for shipyard operators (no UV glare, low fume emission).

## 2.2 Equipment Architecture

A typical **SAW workstation** for shipbuilding panel-line production consists of:

- **Power Source**: Constant-voltage (CV) transformer-rectifier or inverter, DC+/DC− or AC output. Modern sources provide **>60% electrical efficiency**.
- **Wire Feed Unit (WFU)**: Constant-speed feed rolls deliver wire at controlled speed (typically **1.5 – 12 m/min**).
- **Flux Hopper & Delivery Tube**: Gravity-fed; some systems use pressurised air to blow flux.
- **Contact Tip & Welding Head**: Conducts current and guides the wire into the flux bed.
- **Travel Carriage / Tractor**: Moves the head along the joint at a programmed travel speed (often **0.3 – 1.5 m/min**).
- **Flux Recovery Unit**: Suction-based reclaim system.
- **Control Console**: Sets voltage, wire feed rate, travel speed, and on/off sequencing.
- **Work-piece Manipulation**: Rotators, turning rolls, or fixed-position jigs (1G welding).

## 2.3 Flux – The Heart of SAW

The flux is more than just shielding. It actively participates in the welding metallurgy. Typical flux classifications:

| Flux Type | Composition Idea | Use in Shipbuilding |
|---|---|---|
| **Fused (acidic)** | High SiO₂ + MnO | General structural work |
| **Sintered / bonded (basic)** | CaO + MgO + Al₂O₃ + FeO | High-toughness, critical ship plates (e.g., EH36, DH36) |
| **Alloy-bearing** | Fe-Mn, Fe-Si, Fe-Cr additions | Single-pass welds requiring matching chemistry |
| **Neutral** | Al₂O₃ + CaO balanced | Multi-pass welding |

**Key Flux Functions:**
1. Shielding atmosphere (releases shielding gases when heated).
2. Slag formation — dissolves and traps oxides, sulphides, phosphides.
3. Deoxidation — Mn and Si in the flux reduce FeO from the pool.
4. Alloying — adds Mn, Si, Cr, Mo, Ni to the weld metal.
5. Shape control — slag viscosity affects bead contour.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every numerical KTU problem on SAW will use one or more of the following formulae. Memorise the units.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Heat Input (per unit length) | $HI = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S}$ | **J/mm** or **kJ/mm** |
| 2 | Wire Melt-Off Rate | $MOR = \dfrac{I \cdot k}{1000}$ | **kg/hr** (k ≈ 0.08–0.12 g/A·min typical for steel) |
| 3 | Travel Speed | $S = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{HI}$ | **mm/min** |
| 4 | Deposition Rate | $DR = \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \cdot v_{wire} \cdot \rho \cdot 60$ | **kg/hr** |
| 5 | Penetration (empirical, mm) | $P \approx 0.0015 \cdot I + 0.5$ (rough approx., varies) | **mm** |
| 6 | Bead Area (cross-section) | $A = w \cdot h$ or from bead profile | **mm²** |
| 7 | % Dilution | $\%\text{Dil} = \dfrac{\text{Area of melted base metal}}{\text{Area of melted base metal + deposited wire area}} \times 100$ | **%** |

Where:
- $V$ = arc voltage (V)
- $I$ = welding current (A)
- $S$ = travel speed (mm/min)
- $d$ = wire diameter (mm)
- $v_{wire}$ = wire feed speed (m/min)
- $\rho$ = density of steel ≈ **7.85 g/cm³ = 7.85 × 10⁻³ g/mm³**

**Note on Table Pipes:** To respect markdown syntax, every `|` symbol in a formula has been written as `\vert` or replaced by `·`. For example, `|V|` would be rendered as $\vert V \vert$.

## 2.5 Advantages & Limitations for Shipyards

| Aspect | Comment |
|---|---|
| **+ Deposition rate** | Single-wire up to **~15 kg/hr**, tandem up to **~40 kg/hr** — perfect for long hull seams. |
| **+ Penetration** | Deep penetration even on **25 mm plate** in single pass. |
| **+ Weld quality** | Low hydrogen, low porosity, smooth bead, low spatter. |
| **+ Operator comfort** | No arc glare, low fume, mechanised. |
| **– Position limitation** | Only **1G / 2F** generally; vertical & overhead rarely done. |
| **– Flux handling** | Requires flux recovery & drying. |
| **– Plate preparation** | Demands accurate fit-up (gap ≤ 1–2 mm). |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivations are **model solutions** showing the exact valuation key style a KTU board examiner follows. Each algebraic transition is shown step-by-step with explanatory text rows. No step is skipped.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Heat Input Formula from First Principles

**KTU-style problem:**
*"Derive the expression for arc heat input per unit length of weld in SAW."*

**Step-by-Step Reasoning:**

Let $V$ be the arc voltage in volts and $I$ the welding current in amperes. The electrical power developed at the arc is:

$$
P = V \cdot I \quad \text{(Watts)}
$$

For a constant travel speed $S$ measured in **mm/min**, the time taken by the arc to traverse **1 mm** of weld length is:

$$
t = \dfrac{1}{S} \quad \text{(minutes)} = \dfrac{60}{S} \quad \text{(seconds)}
$$

The arc energy deposited over 1 mm of weld is therefore:

$$
HI = P \cdot t = (V \cdot I) \cdot \dfrac{60}{S}
$$

Expressed in standard form:

$$
HI = \dfrac{60 \cdot V \cdot I}{S} \quad \text{J/mm}
$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by 1000 gives the **kJ/mm** form preferred in KTU exam scripts:

$$
HI = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{S} \cdot \dfrac{60}{1000} \quad \text{kJ/mm}
$$

> **Engineering meaning:** A typical shipyard SAW panel-line runs at $V = 32$ V, $I = 800$ A, $S = 500$ mm/min. Therefore $HI = (32 \times 800 \times 60)/500 = 3072$ J/mm ≈ **3.07 kJ/mm**. Going above ~3.5 kJ/mm risks grain-coarsening in EH36 grade plates; below ~1.5 kJ/mm risks lack of fusion.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Deposition Rate from Wire Geometry

**Problem:** *A SAW job uses a solid wire of diameter 3.2 mm and density 7.85 g/cm³. The wire is fed at 4 m/min. Find the deposition rate in kg/hr.*

**Step 1:** Cross-sectional area of the wire:

$$
A_{w} = \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times (3.2)^{2}}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times 10.24}{4} = 8.04 \;\text{mm}^{2}
$$

**Step 2:** Volume of metal deposited per metre of wire fed:

$$
v_{per\,m} = A_{w} \times 1000\,\text{mm} = 8.04 \times 1000 = 8040 \;\text{mm}^{3}
$$

**Step 3:** Mass per metre using density (convert g/cm³ → g/mm³):

$$
\rho = 7.85 \;\dfrac{\text{g}}{\text{cm}^{3}} = 7.85 \times 10^{-3} \;\dfrac{\text{g}}{\text{mm}^{3}}
$$

$$
m_{per\,m} = 8040 \times 7.85 \times 10^{-3} = 63.114 \;\text{g/m}
$$

**Step 4:** For wire feed at $v_{wire} = 4$ m/min, mass per minute:

$$
m_{per\,min} = 63.114 \times 4 = 252.456 \;\text{g/min}
$$

**Step 5:** Convert to **kg/hr**:

$$
DR = 252.456 \times 60 \times 10^{-3} = 15.15 \;\text{kg/hr}
$$

> **Conclusion:** A single-wire SAW job with a 3.2 mm wire at 4 m/min feed will deposit approximately **15 kg/hr** of weld metal. Shipyard planners use this to estimate welding time per panel joint.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Finding Travel Speed for a Target Heat Input

**Problem:** *For a hull plate butt weld, the welding procedure specification (WPS) demands a heat input of 2.0 kJ/mm. If the set voltage is 30 V and current is 700 A, calculate the required travel speed in mm/min and m/min.*

**Step 1:** Rearrange the heat-input equation:

$$
S = \dfrac{60 \cdot V \cdot I}{HI}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $HI = 2.0$ kJ/mm = $2000$ J/mm:

$$
S = \dfrac{60 \times 30 \times 700}{2000} = \dfrac{1\,260\,000}{2000} = 630 \;\text{mm/min}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert to m/min:

$$
S = 630 \times 10^{-3} = 0.63 \;\text{m/min}
$$

> **Engineering meaning:** The carriage driver must therefore set **630 mm/min** to honour the WPS heat input of 2 kJ/mm. Exceeding this speed (e.g., 800 mm/min) lowers heat input to ~1.575 kJ/mm, risking lack of fusion in 25 mm plate.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Percentage Dilution Calculation

**Problem:** *In a single-pass SAW on 20 mm plate, the bead cross-section measured after polishing is 60 mm² total. The melted base-metal portion occupies 18 mm². Calculate the percentage dilution.*

**Step 1:** Identify the two zones of the bead:

- Area of **melted base metal** = $18 \;\text{mm}^{2}$
- Area of **deposited wire metal** = $60 - 18 = 42 \;\text{mm}^{2}$

**Step 2:** Apply dilution formula:

$$
\%\text{Dil} = \dfrac{\text{Melted base metal area}}{\text{Total bead area}} \times 100
$$

$$
\%\text{Dil} = \dfrac{18}{60} \times 100 = 30\%
$$

> **Interpretation:** 30% dilution is typical of SAW in butt joints on mild/EH-grade steel. In **fillets**, dilution can rise to 40–60%, demanding closer attention to WPS chemistry.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Quick Parameter Map for the Board Examiner

| Parameter | Too Low | Optimum (single-wire, 1G butt) | Too High |
|---|---|---|---|
| Current $I$ | Lack of fusion, low penetration | **600 – 900 A** | Undercut, burn-through |
| Voltage $V$ | Bead humping, porosity | **28 – 35 V** | Wide bead, concave profile |
| Travel Speed $S$ | Excessive heat input → coarse grain | **300 – 600 mm/min** | Cold lap, undercut |
| Flux Grain Size | Poor shielding, irregular bead | **0.4 – 2.0 mm** | Arc shorting (too coarse) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power Source CV-DC+AC] --> B[Wire Feed Unit]
    B --> C[Contact Tip & Welding Head]
    C --> D[Arc Zone Hidden Under Flux Bed]
    D --> E[Weld Pool - Molten Metal]
    E --> F[Solidified Weld Metal]
    D --> G[Slag Crust Forms Above Pool]
    H[Flux Hopper] --> D
    D --> I[Spent Flux Discharge]
    I --> J[Vacuum Flux Recovery Unit]
    J --> K[Recycled Flux to Hopper]
    L[Travel Carriage Tractor] --> C
    M[Operator Console CV and S Set] --> A
    M --> B
    M --> L
```

**Interpretation:** The diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of a single-wire SAW station. Electrical energy from the **Power Source** combines with wire and flux delivered mechanically. The arc, weld pool, and slag are all resident in the **Arc Zone** under the flux blanket. The recovered flux loop closes the **material cycle**, critical in modern shipyards where flux cost is significant.

## 4.2 Equipment Layout for a Shipyard Panel Line (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Plate Edge Preparation] --> P2[Tack Welding Station]
    P2 --> P3[SAW Carriage on Track]
    P3 --> P4[Flux Hopper Above Track]
    P4 --> P5[Vacuum Recovery Trolley]
    P5 --> P6[Flux Recycling Bin]
    P3 --> P7[Control Panel Room]
    P3 --> P8[Welded Panel Output]
```

## 4.3 SAW Variants Comparison Map (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SAW[Submerged Arc Welding SAW] --> SW[Single Wire - Standard]
    SAW --> TW[Twin Wire - Two Electrodes One Head]
    SAW --> TA[Tandem Arc - Two Arcs Sequential]
    SAW --> MW[Multi-Wire - 3 or 4 Arcs]
    SAW --> ST[Strip Cladding - Wide Electrode Surfacing]
    SW --> APP1[Panel Line Butt Welds]
    TW --> APP2[Deck Plate Long Seams]
    TA --> APP3[Heavy Plate Shipbuilding]
    MW --> APP4[Pipe Mills Storage Tanks]
    ST --> APP5[Corrosion Resistant Surfacing]
```

## 4.4 Process Parameter Inter-Relationship (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V[Voltage V] --> BE[Bead Width]
    I[Current I] --> PE[Penetration Depth]
    S[Travel Speed S] --> HI[Heat Input kJ per mm]
    FW[Wire Feed m per min] --> DR[Deposition Rate kg per hr]
    HI --> Q1[Microstructure Grain Size]
    HI --> Q2[Distortion Risk]
    DR --> Q3[Productivity]
    BE --> Q4[Leg Length in Fillet]
    PE --> Q5[Root Fusion Quality]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read the diagrams:** Each block is a process input, variable, or outcome. The arrows show causal influence — for example, increasing **Travel Speed S** reduces **Heat Input HI**, which directly affects the **Grain Size** of the weld HAZ (Heat-Affected Zone). Shipyard engineers use such maps during WPS approval.

---

## 4.5 Physical Schematic Description (Text-Based Block Drawing)

If a physical sketch is required in the exam script, the candidate should draw and label:

1. **Base plates** with a square-butt or V-groove edge preparation.
2. **Flux bed** drawn as a hatched layer of thickness ~**25–40 mm** on top of the joint.
3. **Electrode wire** entering from the welding head at an angle of typically **60–80° from horizontal** (drag angle).
4. **Arc column** drawn as a small hidden region inside the flux layer, between the wire tip and the weld pool.
5. **Weld pool** as an ellipse under the flux, with arrows indicating **travel direction**.
6. **Slag crust** floating on top of the weld pool.
7. **Solidified bead** behind the travel direction.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Define Submerged Arc Welding (SAW). Why is it called “submerged”? List any two advantages of using SAW in shipbuilding.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Distribution):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Submerged Arc Welding is an arc welding process in which a continuous solid or cored wire electrode is fed into the weld zone while a blanket of **granular fusible flux** is deposited over the joint. The arc strikes and remains entirely **beneath the flux**, melting the wire and base metal to form the weld pool.
- **Why "Submerged" (1 mark):** Because the arc column, the molten weld pool, and the wire tip are all located **underneath the top surface** of the unmelted flux layer — the arc is not visible from outside.
- **Advantages in shipbuilding (1 mark — any two):**
  1. Very high **deposition rate** (up to ~15 kg/hr single-wire, ~40 kg/hr tandem), making it ideal for long hull seams.
  2. Excellent weld **soundness** with low hydrogen content — suitable for **EH36/DH36** ship steel grades.
  3. Mechanised travel along tracks gives uniform, defect-free welds with low operator fatigue.

### Question A.2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: What are the functions of the granular flux in Submerged Arc Welding?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Distribution — one mark per function, any three):**
1. **Atmospheric shielding** — melts to release shielding gases and forms a slag blanket that prevents oxygen and nitrogen ingress into the weld pool.
2. **Deoxidation** — Mn and Si oxides in the flux reduce FeO from the weld pool, cleaning the metal.
3. **Slag formation** — forms a protective and cosmetic slag crust that supports the molten pool and shapes the bead.
4. **Alloying** — can supply Mn, Si, Cr, Mo, Ni to the weld metal to achieve required mechanical properties of the joint.
5. **Arc stabilisation** — provides ionisable vapour that stabilises the arc and reduces spatter.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU ESE pattern for a 14-mark question in Module 1, the question carries two sub-parts **(a) of 7 marks** and **(b) of 7 marks**. **Attempt either Question A or Question B in full.**

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Modified]`

**Q: (a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **principle of operation of Submerged Arc Welding**, identifying all the major zones in the process. State clearly the role of each zone. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A shipyard panel-line SAW job uses the following parameters: arc voltage = **32 V**, welding current = **750 A**, travel speed = **450 mm/min**, and wire diameter = **3.15 mm**. Calculate:
1. The **heat input** in J/mm.
2. The **deposition rate** in kg/hr (assume density = **7.85 g/cm³**, wire feed ≈ deposition rate per geometry).
3. Comment on whether the heat input is **safe for an EH36 grade ship plate** (WPS upper limit = **3.5 kJ/mm**). **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — (a) Principle of Operation [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Identification of Zones (2 marks)**
The student should draw and label:
- **Electrode wire** (continuous, fed at constant speed).
- **Contact tip** (current-conducting guide).
- **Flux bed** (granular, gravity-fed, ~25–40 mm thick).
- **Arc column** (hidden under the flux — invisible).
- **Weld pool** (molten metal).
- **Slag crust** (frozen flux on top of bead).
- **Solidified weld metal** (behind the arc).
- **Base plates** (parent metal with edge preparation).
- **Recovery suction hose** (returns spent flux).

**Step 2: Description of Process (3 marks)**
- The flux hopper continuously deposits a granular layer of flux ahead of the welding head.
- The wire feed unit pushes a consumable electrode at a preset rate (e.g., 4 m/min).
- The contact tip conducts current from the CV power source to the wire.
- An arc is established between the wire tip and the parent metal — completely **submerged** under the flux.
- The arc melts the wire and the base metal, forming a **weld pool**.
- Some flux melts and floats to form a **slag crust**; unmelted flux forms the protective blanket.
- The molten metal solidifies behind the arc into a **dense, defect-free weld**.
- The **vacuum recovery** collects unfused flux for recycling.

**Step 3: Role of Each Zone (2 marks)**
- **Flux bed** → shields pool from atmosphere, supplies deoxidizers, forms slag.
- **Arc zone** → primary heat source, melts wire and base metal.
- **Weld pool** → site of metallurgical reactions, fusion, and dilution.
- **Slag crust** → covers and supports the slow-cooling weld pool, shapes bead contour.
- **Travel carriage** → ensures uniform bead width and consistent heat input.

---

#### Model Solution — (b) Numerical [7 Marks]

**Given:** $V = 32$ V, $I = 750$ A, $S = 450$ mm/min, $d = 3.15$ mm, $\rho = 7.85$ g/cm³.

**Part 1: Heat Input (2 marks)**

$$
HI = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S} = \dfrac{32 \times 750 \times 60}{450}
$$

$$
HI = \dfrac{1\,440\,000}{450} = 3200 \;\text{J/mm} = 3.2 \;\text{kJ/mm}
$$

**Part 2: Deposition Rate — Approach via wire geometry (3 marks)**

Wire cross-section area:

$$
A_{w} = \dfrac{\pi \times (3.15)^{2}}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times 9.9225}{4} = 7.79 \;\text{mm}^{2}
$$

Assuming the wire feed rate approximately equals the wire consumption rate of **3 m/min** (typical SAW value matching 750 A on 3.15 mm wire):

Volume per metre:

$$
V_{per\,m} = 7.79 \times 1000 = 7790 \;\text{mm}^{3}
$$

Mass per metre:

$$
m_{per\,m} = 7790 \times 7.85 \times 10^{-3} = 61.15 \;\text{g/m}
$$

Per minute (3 m/min):

$$
m_{per\,min} = 61.15 \times 3 = 183.45 \;\text{g/min}
$$

Per hour:

$$
DR = 183.45 \times 60 \times 10^{-3} = 11.0 \;\text{kg/hr}
$$

**Part 3: Safety Comment (2 marks)**

WPS upper limit for EH36 plate = **3.5 kJ/mm**.
Calculated $HI = 3.2$ kJ/mm $\Rightarrow$ **SAFE**, with a margin of **0.3 kJ/mm**. The procedure is acceptable. **Recommendation:** the supervisor may attempt to raise the travel speed to ~520 mm/min to improve productivity while still keeping HI within the WPS window.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Modified]`

**Q: (a)** Compare **Single-Wire SAW** with **Twin-Wire / Tandem-Arc SAW** in terms of deposition rate, heat input, distortion control, and applicability in shipbuilding. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A SAW job on **20 mm thick hull plate** requires the bead area in cross-section to be **75 mm²**. If the melted base-metal area in the bead is **22 mm²**, calculate the **percentage dilution**. If the wire feed rate is **2.5 m/min** and wire diameter is **4.0 mm**, find the **deposition rate** in kg/hr (density 7.85 g/cm³). Comment on whether such dilution is **acceptable for an EH36 grade** (typical acceptable range: 20–40%). **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — (a) Comparison Table [7 Marks]

| Criterion | Single-Wire SAW | Twin-Wire / Tandem-Arc SAW |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of arcs** | One | Two (twin) or two sequential (tandem) |
| **Deposition rate** | ~10–15 kg/hr | ~25–45 kg/hr |
| **Heat input per unit length** | Moderate, easier to control | High — needs **interpass temperature** control |
| **Distortion control** | Better — less heat | Worse — high heat can warp thin plates |
| **Weld bead shape** | Smooth, narrower | Wider, may need repositioning |
| **Equipment cost** | Lower | Higher (two power sources, twin-wire feeder) |
| **Operator skill** | Moderate | High (tandem arc synchronisation) |
| **Shipbuilding use** | General panel-line butt welds, 1G | Heavy plate deck/side-shell, very long seams, sub-assemblies |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** For ship hull panel lines under 30 mm thickness, single-wire SAW gives better control and is the industry default. For ultra-thick plates (>30 mm) or extremely long longitudinal seams in super-tankers, tandem-arc SAW provides the necessary productivity.

---

#### Model Solution — (b) Numerical [7 Marks]

**Part 1: Dilution (2 marks)**

$$
\%\text{Dil} = \dfrac{\text{Melted base metal area}}{\text{Total bead area}} \times 100 = \dfrac{22}{75} \times 100 = 29.33\%
$$

**Part 2: Deposition Rate (3 marks)**

$$
A_{w} = \dfrac{\pi \times (4.0)^{2}}{4} = 12.566 \;\text{mm}^{2}
$$

Mass per metre of wire:

$$
m_{per\,m} = 12.566 \times 1000 \times 7.85 \times 10^{-3} = 98.64 \;\text{g/m}
$$

Per minute (2.5 m/min):

$$
m_{per\,min} = 98.64 \times 2.5 = 246.6 \;\text{g/min}
$$

Per hour:

$$
DR = 246.6 \times 60 \times 10^{-3} = 14.8 \;\text{kg/hr}
$$

**Part 3: Acceptability Comment (2 marks)**

Calculated dilution = **29.3%**, which lies inside the **20–40%** acceptable range for EH36 grade ship-plate butt welds. The procedure is **acceptable**, ensuring good base-metal-to-weld-metal fusion without excessive base-metal dilution that could embrittle the joint.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students typically lose marks on SAW questions:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion in Heat Input** — forgetting to multiply by 60 to convert mm/min to seconds. Marks deducted = 2–3 per error.
> 2. **Sketch without labels** — drawing the SAW schematic but failing to mark the flux bed, arc zone, slag, weld pool, or travel direction. Always label at least **5 components**.
> 3. **Confusing current polarity** — stating "DC" without specifying DC+ (wire +ve) vs DC− (wire −ve). For shipbuilding panel-line SAW on mild/EH-steel, **DC+ on wire** is standard for deeper penetration; **AC** is used to reduce arc blow on large-area panels.
> 4. **Ignoring flux types** — answering about SAW without differentiating **fused vs bonded (basic) fluxes**, missing the **alloying** role of flux. Examiner expects this for a 7-mark question.
> 5. **Dilution formula** — using the wrong denominator (using deposited wire area instead of total bead area).
> 6. **Heat-input unit** — writing kJ/mm but computing in J/mm or vice-versa. Always state the unit explicitly.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **SAW = arc hidden under granular flux**; flux is *not* an inert shield — it actively participates in weld metallurgy.
- **Continuous wire + CV power source + travel carriage + flux recovery** form the four pillars of an SAW station.
- **Heat Input formula:** $HI = (V \cdot I \cdot 60)/S$ in J/mm. Memorise it; it appears in nearly every numerical.
- **Deposition Rate formula:** $DR = (\pi d^{2}/4) \cdot v_{wire} \cdot \rho \cdot 60$ in kg/hr.
- **Dilution** in butt SAW typically **25–35%**; in fillet SAW typically **40–60%**.
- **Flux types:** Fused (acidic), Sintered/Bonded (basic — preferred for EH36), Alloy-bearing (for matching chemistry in a single pass).
- **Polarity:** DC+ on wire for deep penetration (1G butt); AC to reduce **arc blow** on large panels.
- **Position limitations:** SAW is essentially limited to **1G / 2F / 1F / 2F** — never used for 3F/4F (vertical/overhead) in shipyards.
- **WPS for ship plate:** heat input kept between **1.5 and 3.5 kJ/mm** to balance penetration against grain coarsening in EH36/DH36.
- **Typical numbers:** 32 V, 750 A, 450 mm/min → ~3.2 kJ/mm → ~11–15 kg/hr deposition.
- **Advantages:** high productivity, excellent quality, no UV glare, no external shielding gas.
- **Limitations:** flux handling & drying, accurate fit-up required, limited to simple positions, high initial capex.
- **Shipyard application:** longitudinal and transverse hull panel butts, deck seams, bulkhead joints, sub-assemblies of superstructures.
- **Comparison point:** Single-wire vs Twin-wire vs Tandem-arc — productivity rises, distortion and equipment cost also rise.
- **Safety note:** Even though arc is hidden, the surrounding hot flux bed can reach >200°C; PPE and flux dust extraction are mandatory in confined shipyard spaces.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electro Slag Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST831 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Electro Slag Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal KTU 2024 Definition

**Electro Slag Welding (ESW)** is a vertical-position fusion welding process in which coalescence of metals is produced by heating them with the **resistance heat generated by an electric current passing through a molten slag pool** that is sandwiched between the workpieces and a water-cooled copper shoe retainer. Unlike conventional arc welding processes, ESW does **not employ a sustained electric arc** for heat generation — heat is purely the result of **Joule heating (I²R effect)** in the electrically resistive molten flux/slag.
