The process was developed in **1952** at the **E.O. Paton Electric Welding Institute, Kiev, Ukraine (USSR)** and is the dominant heavy-section welding method used in shipbuilding for plate thicknesses ranging from **25 mm to 600 mm** in a single vertical pass.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** ESW is classified as an *Electroslag* process (NOT an *Arc* process). The heat source is **molten slag resistance**, and the filler metal is consumed by **drip melting** from the electrode(s) into the weld pool.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Underground Furnace"

Imagine a tall vertical gap between two thick metal walls (the joint to be welded). You pour a bucket of electrically resistive molten material (slag) into this gap. Now you plunge a long metal wire (electrode) into this hot slag and turn on the electricity. The current struggles to pass through the resistive slag, exactly like electricity struggling to pass through a thin wire in a heater coil. The slag gets so hot (≈ **2000 °C**) that it melts both the incoming wire and the adjacent parent metal walls. As the wire keeps feeding down, the molten metal freezes progressively upward inside the slag "tube," forming a continuous joint.

> [!NOTE]
> The molten slag acts like an **electric furnace liner** — it confines the heat, protects the weld from atmosphere, and continuously melts the filler metal. This is why ESW is sometimes called a "**submerged arc of the vertical world**."

### Key Quantitative Benchmarks (Shipbuilding Standards)

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|-----------|---------------|
| Plate thickness range | **25 mm – 600 mm** |
| Slag pool temperature | **~2000 °C** |
| Weld pool temperature | **~1700 °C – 1900 °C** |
| Deposition rate | **8 – 25 kg/hr** |
| Current type | **AC (preferred) or DC** |
| Wire feed speed | **0.5 – 6 m/min** |
| Current range | **300 – 1000 A** |
| Voltage range | **20 – 50 V** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Stepwise Logic

The ESW process unfolds in five sequential engineering stages:

1. **Initiation (Start-up):** A starting weld pool is established at the bottom of the joint gap (on a copper or steel run-off plate) by either:
   - An *arc strike* (initial arc that is then extinguished by the slag)
   - *Solid slag* (pre-melted conductive flux poured into the gap)
2. **Slag Conduction Stage:** Once the slag is molten (and conductive), the arc is extinguished (or never sustained). Current now flows **through the slag** from the electrode to the parent metal base.
3. **Joule Heating:** The slag has high electrical resistivity (≈ 0.25 – 0.5 Ω·cm). The passage of current heats the slag following the relation $Q = I^{2}Rt$, where $R$ is the slag resistance.
4. **Metal Transfer (Drip Melting):** The wire electrode, immersed in the hot slag, melts at its tip and the molten droplets fall by gravity into the weld pool beneath. No metal transfer arc exists.
5. **Solidification & Travel:** The assembly (electrode + copper shoes) moves upward at a controlled travel speed *v* (typically 12 – 25 mm/min). The molten weld metal solidifies from the bottom upward, while a fresh slag pool is maintained on top.

> [!NOTE]
> The **water-cooled copper dams (shoes)** are critical — they confine the molten slag/weld pool, extract ~70 % of the process heat, and define the weld bead width. Without them, the slag would simply run out.

### 2.2 Why the Process Works — The "Why" Behind the Heat

- **High slag resistance:** ESW flux (e.g., 70 % CaF₂ + 20 % Al₂O₃ + 10 % SiO₂) has a resistivity about **1000× greater** than copper. This forces substantial resistive heating.
- **Self-regulating pool:** The deeper the electrode dips, the lower the resistance, the lower the heat — a negative feedback that stabilizes the pool.
- **Sub-arc-like protection:** The slag completely blankets the weld, eliminating spatter, UV radiation, and atmospheric contamination.

### 2.3 Variations of ESW (Process Variants)

| Variant | Electrode Type | Typical Shipyard Use |
|---------|----------------|----------------------|
| **Single-wire ESW** | 1 × wire (2.4 – 4.0 mm) | Plate thickness 25 – 80 mm |
| **Twin-wire ESW** | 2 × wires | 50 – 150 mm thick panels |
| **Multi-wire ESW** | 3 – 6 × wires | 150 – 400 mm (heavy hull) |
| **Strip ESW** | Wide steel strip electrode | High-quality heavy sections |
| **Consumable Guide ESW** | Wire + guide tube | Small workshops |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NOTE ON TABLE DELIMITERS:** The vertical bar `|` is rendered as `\vert` below to maintain markdown table integrity (per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 anti-collision rule).

| # | Quantity | Formula / Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|----------------------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Joule heat generated in slag | $Q = I^{2} R t$ | Joules (J) | $R$ = slag resistance |
| 2 | Power dissipated in slag | $P = V \cdot I = I^{2} R$ | Watts (W) | $V$ = voltage across slag |
| 3 | Heat input per unit length | $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \cdot 1000}$ | kJ/mm | $\eta$ = arc efficiency (0.85 – 1.0 for ESW) |
| 4 | Deposition rate (mass) | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \cdot v_{f} \cdot \rho$ | kg/min | $d$ = wire dia, $v_f$ = feed speed, $\rho$ = density |
| 5 | Deposition rate (length) | $\dot{L} = v_f \cdot \pi \cdot \dfrac{d^{2}}{4}$ | m³/min × unit | Geometric — used for travel calculations |
| 6 | Slag pool depth | $h_s \approx 30 \text{ to } 60$ | mm | Critical for stable process |
| 7 | Travel speed relation | $v = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{deposit}}{A_{weld} \cdot \rho}$ | m/min | $A_{weld}$ = cross-section of joint |
| 8 | Slag electrical resistivity | $\rho_{slag} \approx 0.25 \text{ to } 0.5$ | Ω·cm | Function of composition & temperature |
| 9 | Heat extraction by shoes | $Q_{shoe} \approx 0.65 \text{ to } 0.75$ of total | — | Water-cooled copper dams |
| 10 | Plate width capacity (single pass) | $t \le 600$ | mm | For ultra-heavy ship plates |

### 2.5 Engineering Utility of ESW in Shipbuilding

- **Longitudinal hull seams** in VLCCs and bulk carriers
- **Transverse bulkhead-to-shell plate** welds (typical 30 – 60 mm)
- **Stern frame and rudder horn** castings welded to hull
- **Decks of large offshore platforms** (Jack-up rigs, FPSOs)
- **Subassembly joining** in modern panel-line construction (where the joint is rotated to vertical position for ESW, then re-oriented)
- **Repair welding** of cracked thick-section ship components

The key shipyard advantage is that ESW eliminates the multi-pass requirement of SAW for thick plate, **reducing welding time by up to 80 %** for a single joint.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation A — Heat Input per Unit Weld Length ($H$)

**Statement:** The heat input per unit length of ESW joint is defined as the ratio of total arc power to travel speed.

**Given:**
- Welding current: $I$ (Amperes)
- Welding voltage: $V$ (Volts)
- Travel speed: $v$ (mm/min)
- Process thermal efficiency: $\eta$ (dimensionless)

**Step 1 — Write the rate of heat generation (power):**
The electrical power consumed at the electrode is given by the product of voltage and current.

$$
P = V \cdot I \quad \text{[Watts]}
$$

**Step 2 — Account for thermal efficiency:**
Only a fraction $\eta$ of this electrical power is delivered as useful heat to the weldment. The remainder is lost to radiation, the copper shoes, and conduction into the base metal.

$$
P_{useful} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \quad \text{[Watts]}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to heat per unit length:**
Heat input $H$ is heat per unit length. We divide $P_{useful}$ (in J/sec) by travel speed $v$ (in mm/min). First convert $v$ to mm/sec:

$$
v_{sec} = \dfrac{v}{60} \quad \text{[mm/sec]}
$$

Then:

$$
H = \dfrac{P_{useful}}{v_{sec}} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{v \cdot 1000} \quad \text{[kJ/mm]}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute for ESW values:**
Take typical ESW values: $V = 38$ V, $I = 600$ A, $v = 15$ mm/min, $\eta = 0.90$.

$$
H = \dfrac{0.90 \times 38 \times 600 \times 60}{15 \times 1000}
$$

$$
H = \dfrac{1,231,200}{15{,}000}
$$

$$
\boxed{H = 82.08 \text{ kJ/mm}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For ESW, the heat input is **enormously high** (often 50 – 100 kJ/mm) — an order of magnitude higher than SMAW (≈ 1 – 2 kJ/mm). This is why ESW joints exhibit **coarse grain HAZ** and require post-weld normalizing in high-strength ship steels.

### 3.2 Derivation B — Deposition Rate (Mass per Unit Time)

**Given:**
- Wire diameter: $d$ (m)
- Wire feed speed: $v_f$ (m/s)
- Wire density: $\rho \approx 7850$ kg/m³ (steel)

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area of wire:**

$$
A = \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \quad \text{[m}^{2}\text{]}
$$

**Step 2 — Volume of wire fed per second:**

$$
\dot{V} = A \cdot v_f \quad \text{[m}^{3}\text{/s]}
$$

**Step 3 — Mass per second (deposition rate):**

$$
\dot{m} = \rho \cdot \dot{V} = \rho \cdot \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \cdot v_f \quad \text{[kg/s]}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to kg/hr:**

$$
\dot{m}_{hr} = \dot{m} \times 3600 = 3600 \cdot \rho \cdot \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \cdot v_f
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical evaluation (Twin-wire, $d = 2.4$ mm, $v_f = 4$ m/min = 0.0667 m/s):**

For a **single wire**:

$$
\dot{m}_{single} = 7850 \times \dfrac{\pi \times (0.0024)^{2}}{4} \times 0.0667
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{single} = 7850 \times 4.524 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.0667
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{single} = 2.37 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s} = 8.53 \text{ kg/hr}
$$

For **twin-wire**, simply double:

$$
\dot{m}_{twin} = 2 \times 8.53 = 17.06 \text{ kg/hr}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matches shipyard quoted values of **15 – 20 kg/hr for twin-wire ESW**, validating the formula.

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Process Parameter Sensitivity

| Parameter | Effect on Slag Temp | Effect on Penetration | Effect on HAZ Width | Risk if Increased |
|-----------|---------------------|------------------------|----------------------|-------------------|
| ↑ Current ($I$) | ↑↑ Strongly | ↑↑ | ↑↑ | Molten pool overflow, slag boil |
| ↑ Voltage ($V$) | ↑ Mildly | ↑ (more sidewall melt) | ↑↑ | Weld undercut |
| ↑ Wire feed speed | ↑ Mildly | ↑ | Slight ↑ | Wire stubbing (short-circuit with pool) |
| ↑ Travel speed | ↓ (less time) | ↓ | ↓ | Lack of fusion, unfilled crater |
| ↑ Slag depth | ↓ (more heat stored) | ↓ | Slight ↓ | Inclusions, sluggish pool |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Trap:** Students often confuse ESW with **Electrogas Welding (EGW)**. The key difference is the **arc vs. slag heat source**:
> - **ESW** → Slag resistance only (no arc after start)
> - **EGW** → External arc + gas shield (arc remains throughout)
> - **SAW** → Arc under granular flux (horizontal/flat position)
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — ESW Process Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power Source - AC Constant Voltage Transformer] --> B[Wire Feeder Unit]
    A --> C[Current Control - Thyristor Regulated]
    B --> D[Contact Tip - Current Conductor to Wire]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Consumable Wire Electrode - 2.4 to 4.0 mm]
    E --> F[Molten Slag Pool - CaF2 + Al2O3 + SiO2]
    F --> G[Joule Heat Generation - I Squared R]
    G --> H[Melting of Electrode Tip - Drip Transfer]
    H --> I[Weld Pool - 1700 to 1900 deg C]
    I --> J[Solidifying Weld Metal - Coarse Columnar Grains]
    F --> K[Water Cooled Copper Shoes - Both Sides]
    K --> L[Heat Extraction - 65 to 75 percent]
    I --> M[Parent Metal - Thick Hull Plate]
    J --> N[ESW Joint - Single Pass Vertical]
    M --> N
    K --> O[Cooling Water Recirculation Tank]
    L --> O
    F --> P[Slag Recycling / Flux Hopper]
    P --> F
```

### 4.2 Process Flow Diagram — Shipyard Panel ESW Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Plate Edge Preparation - Square or Slight Gap] --> S2[Step 2: Plates Mounted in Vertical Position on Turning Roll]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Copper Shoes Attached Both Sides of Joint]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Runoff Plate Welded at Bottom]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Slag Initiated - Arc Start or Pre melted Flux]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Wire Feeds - Arc Extinguished - Slag Conduction Begins]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Oscillator Moves Wire Across Joint - Coverage]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Travel Upward - Continuous Weld Build]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Crater Formation at Top - Arc Re struck to Fill]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10: Slag Removed - Weld Inspected via UT and RT]
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Heat Flow in ESW

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Electrical Power Input - VI Watts] --> Q[Joule Heat in Slag]
    Q --> R[Heat to Melt Electrode - Qm]
    Q --> S[Heat to Melt Parent Metal Edges - Qb]
    Q --> T[Heat to Copper Shoes - Qc]
    Q --> U[Radiation and Convection Losses - Qloss]
    R --> V[Weld Pool Formation]
    S --> V
    V --> W[Solidified Weld Joint]
    T --> X[Cooling Water Outlet]
    U --> Y[Surrounding Atmosphere]
```

### 4.4 Equipment Layout — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HV[High Voltage Side]
        HV1[Mains 3 Phase 415 V AC] --> HV2[Step Down Transformer]
        HV2 --> HV3[Thyristor Rectifier Control]
    end
    HV3 --> L1[Output to Contact Tip]
    L1 --> E[Wire Electrode Moving Downward]
    E --> SLG[Molten Slag Zone]
    SLG --> L2[Return Path to Workpiece]
    L2 --> GND[Grounding Clamp on Plate]
    HV3 --> L2
    WF[Wire Feed Motor Servo] --> E
    OSC[Oscillator Carriage] --> E
    CWS[Water Cooling Pump] --> SHOE[Copper Shoes]
    SHOE --> CWS
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Explain in 3–4 sentences why Electro Slag Welding is preferred for welding thick steel plates in vertical position in shipbuilding.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Electro Slag Welding (ESW) uses the **resistance heat of a molten slag pool** (not an arc) to melt the electrode and parent metal. The slag pool is retained in the joint gap by **water-cooled copper shoes**, which also extract surplus heat. Because the heat source is stationary and the assembly rises vertically, **single-pass welding of plates up to 600 mm thick** is possible with deposition rates of 8–25 kg/hr. This eliminates the multi-pass requirement of conventional arc welding, drastically reducing welding time and distortion in thick hull sections.

*(Examiner award: 1 mark definition + 1 mark copper shoe role + 1 mark thick-plate benefit = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
*List the four essential constituents of a typical ESW flux composition and state the function of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Flux Constituent | Typical % | Function |
|------------------|-----------|----------|
| **CaF₂ (Calcium Fluoride)** | 50 – 70 % | Provides electrical resistivity; lowers melting point of slag |
| **Al₂O₃ (Alumina)** | 15 – 25 % | Increases slag viscosity for stable pool; dissolves inclusions |
| **SiO₂ (Silica)** | 5 – 15 % | Slag fluidity regulator; improves deoxidation |
| **MnO / FeO** | 2 – 10 % | Deoxidizer; improves weld metal Mn/Si content |

*(Examiner award: 0.5 × 4 = 2 marks for components + 1 mark for any correct function = 3 marks)*

---

### PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

---

**Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Module 1 Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *With a neat block diagram, describe the working principle and equipment set-up of Electro Slag Welding. Discuss the role of the water-cooled copper shoes.*

**Model Solution:**

1. **Definition and principle (2 marks):** ESW is a vertical fusion welding process where heat is generated by passing current through a molten slag pool held between the workpieces. The slag, having high electrical resistance, produces Joule heat (I²R) that melts both the consumable electrode and the parent metal edges.

2. **Equipment block diagram (2 marks):** A typical ESW set-up consists of:
   - AC constant-voltage power source (e.g., 600–1000 A capacity)
   - Wire feeder with servo motor
   - Electrode oscillator (for transverse motion of wire)
   - Two water-cooled copper shoes (dams) on either side of the joint
   - Slag hopper and recycling system
   - Travelling carriage that moves the assembly upward
   - Run-off (start) plate at the bottom of the joint

3. **Working sequence (2 marks):**
   - Step 1: Strike arc on run-off plate using a small amount of flux.
   - Step 2: As molten slag forms and accumulates (~30–60 mm deep), the arc extinguishes.
   - Step 3: Current now flows purely through the slag.
   - Step 4: Wire is continuously fed, melts, and droplets fall into the weld pool.
   - Step 5: The assembly moves up at the desired travel speed (12–25 mm/min); weld solidifies progressively.
   - Step 6: At the top, crater is filled by re-striking an arc momentarily.

4. **Role of copper shoes (1 mark):**
   - Confine the molten slag and weld pool to the joint region.
   - Extract ~65–75 % of process heat, preventing overheating of the base metal.
   - Define the bead width and ensure parallel-sided weld profile.
   - Prevent slag overflow that would cause underfill defects.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Compare Electro Slag Welding (ESW) with Submerged Arc Welding (SAW) in terms of position, heat source, plate thickness capability, deposition rate and shipbuilding applications.*

**Model Solution (Tabular Comparison):**

| Parameter | ESW | SAW |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| **Welding position** | Vertical only | Flat / horizontal |
| **Heat source** | Slag resistance (no arc after start) | Arc under granular flux |
| **Typical current** | 300 – 1000 A (AC) | 400 – 1500 A (AC/DC) |
| **Plate thickness (single pass)** | 25 – 600 mm | ≤ 25 mm (multi-pass above) |
| **Deposition rate** | 8 – 25 kg/hr | 5 – 30 kg/hr |
| **Slag form** | Molten pool (retained) | Granular solid flux |
| **Heat input** | Very high (50 – 100 kJ/mm) | Moderate (2 – 6 kJ/mm) |
| **HAZ grain size** | Coarse (needs normalizing) | Finer |
| **Joint preparation** | Square edge; no bevel | Bevel/V-groove required for thick |
| **Shipbuilding use** | Thick hull panels, bulkheads, stern frames | General ship panel fabrication |
| **Solidification cracking risk** | Higher (high heat input) | Lower |
| **Cost of equipment** | High | Moderate |

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** ESW and SAW are complementary — ESW is chosen for very thick vertical joints; SAW for thinner flat panels. Both fall under the broader category of "high-productivity shipyard welding" but differ fundamentally in their heat generation mechanism.

*(Examiner award: 0.5 × 12 rows = 6 marks + 1 mark for the analytical conclusion = 7 marks)*

---

**Question B — 14 Marks [Alternative Internal Choice]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain the metallurgy and microstructural features of an ESW weld joint in ship-grade steel. Discuss the significance of post-weld heat treatment (PWHT) for such joints.*

**Model Solution:**

1. **Weld metal microstructure (2 marks):** Due to the very slow cooling rate and high heat input of ESW, the weld metal solidifies with **coarse columnar grains** growing from the fusion boundary inward. The microstructure in carbon-manganese ship steel (e.g., AH36/DH36) is typically:
   - **Ferrite + Pearlite** in the weld columnar zone
   - **Coarse-grained HAZ** adjacent to the fusion line
   - Possible **Widmanstätten ferrite** (undesirable, lowers toughness)

2. **HAZ characteristics (2 marks):** The Heat Affected Zone experiences peak temperatures near the melting point and very slow cooling. This results in:
   - **Grain coarsening** (especially in the coarse-grained HAZ, CGHAZ)
   - Reduced **Charpy impact toughness** at sub-zero (critical for Arctic/LNG ship service)
   - Possible **tempered martensite** zones if the steel has alloying elements

3. **Significance of PWHT (2 marks):**
   - **Normalizing (920–950 °C, air cool):** Refines the coarse grain, restores toughness.
   - **Stress relieving (580–620 °C):** Reduces residual stresses from non-uniform cooling.
   - **Mandatory** for high-strength ship steel (EH36, EH40) and critical structural joints per IACS / classification society rules (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS).

4. **Defects specific to ESW (1 mark):**
   - Solidification cracking (hot cracking) due to high restraint + coarse grain
   - Lack of fusion at sidewalls if voltage is too low
   - Inclusions from entrapped slag
   - Crater cracking at the top of the joint

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** *A shipyard needs to weld two 80 mm thick mild steel plates in the vertical position in a single pass. Suggest a suitable ESW process variant and estimate the heat input. Assume V = 40 V, I = 700 A, v = 18 mm/min, η = 0.9.*

**Model Solution:**

1. **Process selection (2 marks):** For 80 mm thickness in a single vertical pass, **Twin-Wire Electro Slag Welding** is the most appropriate choice. Rationale:
   - Single-wire ESW is more suited to ≤ 50 mm; beyond this, the heat generation per unit joint length becomes insufficient.
   - Twin-wire provides nearly double the deposition rate (≈ 15 – 20 kg/hr), adequate to fill an 80 mm gap in a single pass.
   - Oscillation of the wires ensures uniform sidewall fusion across the wide joint.

2. **Equipment specification (1 mark):**
   - Power source: 800 A AC thyristor-controlled, constant-voltage type
   - Wire diameter: 3.15 mm (typical)
   - Wire feed: 3.5 – 4.5 m/min (two wires)
   - Copper shoes: 250 – 300 mm wide, water flow 10 L/min
   - Slag: CaF₂–Al₂O₃–SiO₂ ternary, ≈ 5 kg per joint start

3. **Heat input calculation (3 marks):**

$$
H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{v \cdot 1000}
$$

Substitute $V = 40$, $I = 700$, $v = 18$, $\eta = 0.9$:

$$
H = \dfrac{0.9 \times 40 \times 700 \times 60}{18 \times 1000}
$$

$$
H = \dfrac{1{,}512{,}000}{18{,}000}
$$

$$
\boxed{H = 84.0 \text{ kJ/mm}}
$$

4. **Engineering interpretation (1 mark):**
   This heat input is **extremely high** (compare to SMAW ~1 kJ/mm), confirming that PWHT (normalizing at 920 °C) is mandatory before the joint enters service, especially if the ship will operate in cold waters (–40 °C) where Charpy toughness requirements are stringent.

*(Examiner award: 2 + 1 + 3 + 1 = 7 marks for sub-part (b))*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** write "ESW uses an electric arc" — this is the most common error and costs 1–2 marks. Always say "**resistance heat of molten slag**."
> - **Do NOT** confuse the **oscillator** (transverse motion) with the **travel mechanism** (vertical motion) in the diagram.
> - **Do NOT** omit units in numerical heat input problems — write kJ/mm explicitly, not just "84".
> - **Do NOT** say ESW can weld horizontally — it is **strictly vertical** (or near-vertical uphill).
> - In **comparison questions**, marking is split **per row** of the table — a blank row means 0 for that criterion.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ⚓ **ESW = Slag Resistance Welding** — no sustained arc after initiation.
- ⚓ **Process invented (1952)** at E.O. Paton Institute, USSR; ideal for **thick vertical joints** in shipbuilding.
- ⚓ **Single-pass capability:** 25 mm to **600 mm** plate thickness.
- ⚓ **Deposition rate:** **8 – 25 kg/hr** (twin-wire best for 50–150 mm).
- ⚓ **Heat generation:** Joule heating $Q = I^{2}Rt$ in molten CaF₂–Al₂O₃–SiO₂ slag.
- ⚓ **Slag temperature:** ~2000 °C; weld pool ~1700–1900 °C.
- ⚓ **Water-cooled copper shoes** confine the pool and extract 65–75 % of heat.
- ⚓ **Heat input is very high (50–100 kJ/mm)** → coarse grain → **PWHT (normalizing at 920 °C) is mandatory** for ship-grade steels.
- ⚓ **Common defects:** solidification (hot) cracking, lack of fusion, slag inclusions, crater cracks.
- ⚓ **Always AC power source** for multi-wire ESW to avoid arc blow / magnetic interference.
- ⚓ **Cannot weld horizontally** — strictly vertical position only.
- ⚓ **Filler metal consumption** equals wire feed rate: $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot \frac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \cdot v_f$.
- ⚓ **Shipbuilding applications:** hull panels, bulkhead-to-shell joints, stern frames, rudder horns, offshore platform decks.
- ⚓ **Distinguish ESW from EGW** (arc) and SAW (horizontal/granular flux) — these are favorite KTU "trick" comparison questions.
- ⚓ **KTU Module 1 weightage:** ESW + other thick-plate welding processes typically carry **2 marks in Part A + 7–8 marks in Part B** of the ESE paper.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electro Gas Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST831 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Electro Gas Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1 — ELECTRO GAS WELDING (EGW)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Electro Gas Welding (EGW)** is a highly productive, mechanized **arc welding process** classified under **IS0 4063** and **AWS A3.0** as a **gas-shielded, gravity-assisted, vertical-upward welding process**. In EGW, the arc is established between **one or more continuously fed consumable electrode wires** and the workpiece, while the weld pool is mechanically contained between **two water-cooled copper dams (shoes)** that slide along both sides of the joint. The process is performed in the **vertical position (typically 1G / vertical-up)** and uses **external shielding gas** (commonly **CO₂** or an **Ar/CO₂ blend**) to protect the molten weld metal from atmospheric contamination.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Electro Gas Welding is treated as a **high-deposition, single-pass joining process** used specifically for **thick mild steel and low-alloy steel plates** in ship hulls, pressure vessels, and heavy structural fabrications.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of EGW like pouring concrete between two sliding shutters to build a vertical wall:**
> - Imagine you are building a tall concrete wall between two wooden formworks (shutters). Wet concrete is poured from the top.
> - In EGW, the **molten weld metal** plays the role of the wet concrete.
> - The **water-cooled copper dams** act exactly like the sliding shutters — they constrain the liquid metal so it does not spill out.
> - As the weld progresses upward, the **shutters (dams) slide up automatically**, leaving a solid, perfectly shaped weld bead behind — just like the hardened concrete wall.

### 1.3 Process Identification Codes

| Code Authority | Designation |
| :--- | :--- |
| **AWS A3.0** | **EGW** |
| **ISO 4063** | **Process No. 73** |
| **DIN 1910** | **UP-Vertical (UP-V)** variant |

### 1.4 Salient Process Metrics (Industry Standard)

- **Plate thickness range:** **6 mm to 300 mm** (single pass)
- **Current range per wire:** **400 A – 1200 A**
- **Voltage range:** **28 V – 45 V**
- **Wire diameter:** **1.6 mm – 3.2 mm**
- **Travel speed:** **50 mm/min – 150 mm/min (0.05–0.15 m/min)**
- **Wire feed speed:** **2 m/min – 12 m/min**
- **Shielding gas flow:** **15 L/min – 25 L/min**
- **Thermal efficiency (η):** **0.80 – 0.85**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Physical Principle

EGW relies on **three simultaneous physical phenomena**:

1. **Electric Arc Generation:** A constant-voltage DC power source establishes a stable arc between the consumable wire electrode(s) and the base metal.
2. **Gas Shielding:** Shielding gas (typically **CO₂** at **99.8% purity**) is delivered through a nozzle surrounding the wire, displacing atmospheric **O₂** and **N₂** to prevent porosity and embrittlement.
3. **Mechanical Containment:** The molten weld pool, while still fluid, is contained by two **water-cooled copper dams** that travel upward with the welding head. These dams also **extract a portion of the weld heat** (acting as an intensive heat sink), enabling welding of **very thick plates in a single pass** without excessive distortion.

## 2.2 Process Variants

> [!NOTE]
> **Three major variants of EGW exist on the KTU 2024 syllabus:**

| Variant | Electrode Configuration | Typical Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Single-Wire EGW** | One wire, single arc | Plates up to 50 mm |
| **Twin-Wire EGW** | Two parallel wires in one nozzle | Plates 50–100 mm |
| **Multi-Wire EGW (3 or 4 wires)** | Oscillated wire cluster | Plates >100 mm (up to 300 mm) |

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The EGW process proceeds in the following structured sequence:

1. **Joint Preparation**
   - Plates are squared and aligned with a **root gap of 15–20 mm** between them.
   - Edges may be lightly bevelled (15°–30°) or left square for plates <20 mm.

2. **Dam Assembly**
   - Two **water-cooled copper dams** (typically 200–400 mm long) are clamped against the plate faces.
   - Cooling water flow is initiated (flow rate ≈ **4–6 L/min**).

3. **Initial Arc Strike**
   - Wire is fed into the root gap; arc is struck at the bottom.
   - A small **starting pool** is formed against a **starting block / run-off tab** placed at the bottom of the joint.

4. **Steady-State Welding**
   - The welding head travels upward at a controlled rate.
   - Wire feed, current, voltage, gas flow, and travel speed are all **automatically synchronized**.

5. **Weld Termination**
   - At the top of the joint, the arc is extinguished against a **run-off tab**.
   - The dams retract, leaving the solidified weld.

6. **Cooling & Inspection**
   - Weld cools naturally; dams' water cooling prevents HAZ softening in the adjacent base metal.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory formulas for any KTU EGW numerical problem:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Heat Input per unit length (H)} &= \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S \cdot 1000} \;\;\;\; \bigg[\frac{\text{kJ}}{\text{mm}}\bigg]
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Deposition Rate (D)} &= \frac{\pi \cdot d^2 \cdot v_f \cdot \rho}{4 \cdot 10^6} \;\;\;\; \bigg[\frac{\text{kg}}{\text{min}}\bigg]
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Wire Feed Speed (v}_f\text{)} &= \frac{4 \cdot I \cdot 10^3}{\pi \cdot d^2 \cdot \rho \cdot k} \;\;\;\; \bigg[\frac{\text{mm}}{\text{s}}\bigg]
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Melting Rate (M)} &= k \cdot I \;\;\;\; \bigg[\frac{\text{g}}{\text{min}}\bigg]
\end{aligned}
$$

### Symbol Definitions & Boundary Conditions

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value / Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $V$ | Arc Voltage | **28 – 45 V** |
| $I$ | Welding Current | **400 – 1200 A** |
| $S$ | Travel Speed | **50 – 150 mm/min** |
| $\eta$ | Process Thermal Efficiency | **0.80 – 0.85 (dimensionless)** |
| $d$ | Wire Diameter | **1.6 – 3.2 mm** |
| $\rho$ | Wire Density (steel) | **7.85 g/cm³** |
| $v_f$ | Wire Feed Speed | **2 – 12 m/min** |
| $k$ | Melting Coefficient | **0.020 – 0.025 g/(A·min)** for EGW |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> The heat input formula is the **single most critical parameter** shipbuilding quality engineers monitor. Exceeding **2.5 kJ/mm** in ship hull steel causes **HAZ softening** and **toughness loss**, which directly compromises the vessel's classification society certification (e.g., **DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, IRS**).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Heat Input Equation for EGW

The heat input $H$ is defined as the **arc energy delivered per unit length of weld**.

### Step 1 — Electrical Power Delivered

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{arc} &= V \cdot I \;\;\;\; \text{(in Watts)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the instantaneous arc power, where $V$ is the arc voltage (Volts) and $I$ is the welding current (Amperes).

### Step 2 — Energy per Unit Length

When the arc travels a distance $L$ in time $t$, the energy input is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= V \cdot I \cdot t
\end{aligned}
$$

Since travel speed $S = L \, / \, t$, we substitute $t = L/S$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= V \cdot I \cdot \frac{L}{S}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Introduce Thermal Efficiency

Only a fraction $\eta$ of the electrical energy is actually transferred to the workpiece (rest is lost as spatter, radiation, and cooling water extraction):

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{useful} &= \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \frac{L}{S}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Normalize to Per mm Length

Setting $L = 1 \text{ mm} = 10^{-3} \text{ m}$ and converting to kilojoules:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S \cdot 1000} \;\;\;\; \bigg[\frac{\text{kJ}}{\text{mm}}\bigg]
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **canonical EGW heat input formula** expected in all KTU numerical answers.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — Heat Input Calculation

> **Problem Statement:**
> In an EGW operation on a 60 mm thick ship hull plate, the following parameters are recorded:
> - Arc Voltage: $V = 38 \text{ V}$
> - Welding Current (twin wire): $I = 850 \text{ A}$ (each wire)
> - Travel Speed: $S = 80 \text{ mm/min}$
> - Thermal Efficiency: $\eta = 0.82$
>
> **Calculate the heat input per unit length and verify it lies within the safe limit for shipbuilding steel.**

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Substitute into the heat input equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S \cdot 1000}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Insert numerical values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= \frac{0.82 \cdot 38 \cdot 850}{80 \cdot 1000}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply numerator:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.82 \cdot 38 &= 31.16 \\
31.16 \cdot 850 &= 26486
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Divide by denominator:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= \frac{26486}{80000} \\
H &= 0.331 \;\;\;\; \frac{\text{kJ}}{\text{mm}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{calculated} &= 0.331 \text{ kJ/mm} \\
H_{safe\,limit} &= 2.5 \text{ kJ/mm} \\
\therefore H_{calculated} &< H_{safe\,limit} \;\;\;\; \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Heat input of **0.331 kJ/mm** is well below the **2.5 kJ/mm** threshold, ensuring HAZ toughness is preserved — the weld **passes** ship classification requirements.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Multi-Wire EGW Parameter Comparison

The table below maps how each EGW variant scales with current and plate thickness:

| Parameter | Single-Wire | Twin-Wire | Multi-Wire (3–4) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Current Range** | 400–900 A | 700–1600 A | 1500–3000 A |
| **Voltage Range** | 28–35 V | 32–40 V | 38–45 V |
| **Max Plate Thickness** | 50 mm | 100 mm | **300 mm** |
| **Typical Heat Input** | 0.3–0.8 kJ/mm | 0.6–1.4 kJ/mm | 1.0–2.2 kJ/mm |
| **Deposition Rate** | 6–10 kg/h | 12–22 kg/h | **25–40 kg/h** |
| **Wire Oscillation** | None | Optional | **Required** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of an EGW Station

The following Mermaid block-diagram maps the complete functional architecture and inter-component data/energy flow of an Electro Gas Welding station used in modern shipyards.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PWS["DC Constant Voltage Power Source 0 to 1500 A"]
    CTRL["Welding Control Console PLC based"]
    WFS["Wire Feed System Servo Driven Drive Rolls"]
    GAS["Shielding Gas Supply CO2 or ArCO2 Cylinder and Regulator"]
    WATER["Cooling Water Circulator 4 to 6 L per min"]
    HEAD["EGW Welding Head Oscillation Module and Nozzle"]
    DAM_L["Water Cooled Copper Dam Left Side"]
    DAM_R["Water Cooled Copper Dam Right Side"]
    WK["Workpiece Thick Plate Vertical Position 1G"]
    HMI["Operator HMI Touchscreen with Recipe Library"]

    PWS -- "High Current Cable 600 A class" --> HEAD
    CTRL -- "Control Signals 24 V DC" --> PWS
    CTRL -- "Pulse Signal PWM" --> WFS
    CTRL -- "Solenoid Valve Command" --> GAS
    CTRL -- "Pump Relay" --> WATER
    WFS -- "Consumable Wire 1.6 to 3.2 mm" --> HEAD
    GAS -- "Shield Gas Hose" --> HEAD
    WATER -- "Inlet and Outlet hoses" --> DAM_L
    WATER -- "Inlet and Outlet hoses" --> DAM_R
    HEAD -- "Arc and Molten Pool" --> WK
    DAM_L -- "Mechanical Containment" --> WK
    DAM_R -- "Mechanical Containment" --> WK
    HMI -- "Ethernet or Profibus" --> CTRL
```

## 4.2 Process Flow — Vertical-Up Welding Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Step 1 Joint Preparation Gap 15 to 20 mm"] --> B["Step 2 Fix Copper Dams with Clamps"]
    B --> C["Step 3 Start Cooling Water Flow"]
    C --> D["Step 4 Strike Arc at Bottom Tab"]
    D --> E["Step 5 Steady State Travel Upward 50 to 150 mm per min"]
    E --> F["Step 6 Continuous Wire Feed 2 to 12 m per min"]
    F --> G["Step 7 Arc Extinction at Top Run off Tab"]
    G --> H["Step 8 Retract Dams and NDT Inspection"]
```

## 4.3 Heat Flow & Weld Pool Containment Model

```mermaid

    ARC["Electric Arc 6000 to 8000 Degree C"]
    POOL["Molten Weld Pool Contained Region"]
    PLATE["Base Plate HAZ Zone"]
    DAM_C["Copper Dam Heat Sink"]
    AIR["Surrounding Air Radiation Loss"]
    GAS_S["Shielding Gas Atmosphere"]

    ARC -- "Radiant Heat plus Convective" --> POOL
    ARC -- "Gas Shielding Protection" --> GAS_S
    POOL -- "Conduction into Base Metal" --> PLATE
    POOL -- "Lateral Conduction to Dams" --> DAM_C
    ARC -- "Radiation to Environment" --> AIR
```

---
