## Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Seam Welding** is a *resistance welding process* in which a continuous, leak-tight or load-bearing joint is produced between two (or more) overlapping sheets of metal by passing them between a pair of **rotating circular electrode wheels (roller electrodes)**. A series of overlapping weld "nuggets" is produced as the current is pulsed (or continuously applied) at the rolling point of contact, generating heat by **Joule's heating effect (I²R heating)**.

The process is governed by the standards **ISO 4063 (process number 23)** and the **AWS C1.1** resistance welding terminology. In shipbuilding, seam welding is classified under *fusion welding* sub-category in the broader KTU 2024 syllabus because localized fusion occurs at the faying surface.

### Key Terminology
- **Roller Electrodes (Cu-Cr-Zr alloy typically)**: Conducts current and applies forging force.
- **Weld Nugget**: The fused zone of metal at the faying interface.
- **Nugget Pitch (Pitch, p)**: Distance between centers of consecutive nuggets.
- **Weld Spacing**: Distance between centers of consecutive welds for *intermittent* seam welding.
- **Welding Speed (v)**: Linear speed of sheet travel under the rollers (m/min).
- **Roll Force (F)**: Downward force applied by rollers (N).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Seam welding falls under the broader heading *“Continuous Resistance Welding Processes for Plate and Sheet Fabrication”* in Module 2. Students must distinguish it clearly from **Spot Welding** and **Projection Welding** in the exam.

---

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are pressing two sheets of aluminum foil together with the **heated metal wheel of a pizza cutter**, but instead of one continuous cut, the wheel *stops and starts* rapidly, fusing tiny overlapping puddles of metal. Each puddle is a **weld nugget**, and the row of puddles forms a **seam** — just like a tailor stitches a continuous line of thread along two pieces of cloth, the roller electrodes "stitch" two metal sheets together using pulsed heat and pressure.

### The Three "Laws" of Seam Welding
1. **Law of Continuity** — The seam is uninterrupted (continuous) or uniformly spaced (intermittent).
2. **Law of Overlap** — Successive nuggets must overlap by at least **10–20%** of nugget diameter to guarantee leak-tightness.
3. **Law of Pressure** — Roller force must be high enough to forge out porosity, but not so high as to extrude molten metal.

> [!TIP]
> Think of the **roller electrodes** as the "pen" of a **3D printer** moving across a metal "paper". Each "dot" of melted metal is a nugget. When the dots overlap, you get a continuous, hermetic seam — perfect for fuel tanks, mufflers, and ship bulkhead panels.

---

## Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

The following constants and metrics are **mandatory to memorize** for KTU viva and written exams:

- **Specific heat of steel**: $\mathbf{c_p = 460 \; \text{J/(kg·K)}}$
- **Electrical resistivity of low-carbon steel at 20°C**: $\rho = \mathbf{1.7 \times 10^{-7} \; \Omega\cdot m}$
- **Electrical resistivity of copper electrode (Cu-Cr-Zr)**: $\rho_{Cu} = \mathbf{2.0 \times 10^{-8} \; \Omega\cdot m}$
- **Density of steel**: $\rho_{steel} = \mathbf{7850 \; kg/m^3}$
- **Melting point of low-carbon steel**: $\mathbf{1500 \; °C}$
- **Typical roller electrode face width**: $\mathbf{4 \text{ to } 6 \; mm}$
- **Standard nugget pitch (p)**: $\mathbf{2.5 \text{ to } 5 \; mm}$ (governed by KTU design table)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic Flow

1. **Sheet Positioning**: Two (or more) overlapping sheets are fed between two **rotating circular copper-alloy electrodes** mounted on opposing shafts. The overlap is typically **1.5 to 2 times the sheet thickness**.
2. **Application of Electrode Force**: A pneumatic or hydraulic actuator applies a controlled **roll force (F)** ranging from **2 kN to 50 kN** depending on sheet thickness and material.
3. **Pulsed Current Application**: A low-voltage (typically **1 to 10 V** open circuit), high-current (typically **5 kA to 30 kA**) welding transformer delivers current through the rollers. The current is *pulsed* for standard seam welding, *uninterrupted* for mash seam welding.
4. **Heat Generation by I²R**: Heat is generated predominantly at the **highest-resistance interface** — the **faying surface** between the two sheets. This is the **fundamental principle** that differentiates resistance welding from arc welding.
5. **Nugget Formation**: A small molten pool (nugget) forms. As the rollers rotate, the sheets advance and the current is switched off, allowing the nugget to **solidify under pressure (forge solidification)**.
6. **Cycle Repetition**: Current is re-applied and the process repeats, producing **overlapping nuggets** along the seam.
7. **Cooling Cycle**: The seam is allowed to cool with rollers still in contact (acting as heat sinks), preventing oxide entrapment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why overlap the nuggets?** In KTU 2024 board exams, this is a **favorite 3-mark question**. Overlap of **15–20%** of nugget diameter is required to: (a) eliminate the cold "valley" between nuggets, (b) ensure leak-tightness for hydraulic/gas applications, and (c) compensate for current shunting through the previously formed nugget.

---

## Classification of Seam Welding (KTU 2024 Taxonomy)

| **Type** | **Current Mode** | **Sheet Movement** | **Application** |
|:---|:---:|:---|:---|
| **Roll Spot Seam Welding** | Pulsed, no overlap | Sheet stops between pulses | Light sheet metal brackets |
| **Standard (Intermittent) Seam Welding** | Pulsed, with overlap | Continuous | Fuel tanks, mufflers, ship bulkhead panels |
| **Continuous (Foil) Seam Welding** | Uninterrupted DC | Continuous | Thin foils, honeycombs, battery cases |
| **Mash Seam Welding** | No current (forge welding) | Continuous, with upsetting | Aluminum aerospace panels |
| **Projection Seam Welding** | Pulsed, with embossments | Continuous | High-strength ship structural panels |

---

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **verbatim KTU 2024-style** high-yield content. Memorize every equation and its units.

| **#** | **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Remarks** |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|
| 1 | Heat generated per nugget | $H = I^2 R t$ | Joules (J) | $I$ in A, $R$ in $\Omega$, $t$ in s |
| 2 | Heat required to raise temp by $\Delta T$ | $H = m c_p \Delta T$ | J | $m$ in kg, $c_p$ in J/(kg·K) |
| 3 | Effective weld time per pulse | $t = \dfrac{p}{v}$ | s | $p$ = pitch, $v$ = welding speed |
| 4 | Welding speed | $v = \dfrac{p \cdot f}{n}$ | m/min | $f$ = pulses/min, $n$ = number of welds per cycle |
| 5 | Total seam heat rate | $\dot{H} = I^2 R \cdot f$ | W | $f$ in Hz |
| 6 | Electrode contact area | $A_c = w \cdot d$ | mm² | $w$ = face width, $d$ = contact dia. |
| 7 | Roll force (empirical) | $F = k \cdot s^2$ | N | $s$ = sheet thickness (mm), $k$ ≈ **150 N/mm²** for steel |
| 8 | Electrode pressure | $P_e = \dfrac{F}{A_c}$ | MPa | Typically 30–100 MPa |
| 9 | Current density at contact | $J = \dfrac{I}{A_c}$ | A/mm² | Typically 200–400 A/mm² |
| 10 | Nugget diameter (empirical) | $d_n \approx 4\sqrt{s}$ | mm | $s$ = sheet thickness in mm |
| 11 | Heat balance (efficiency) | $\eta = \dfrac{H_{nugget}}{H_{total}} \approx 0.5\text{–}0.8$ | — | Energy lost to electrodes, water cooling, radiation |
| 12 | Required welding current | $I = k_1 \cdot s^{0.75}$ | kA | $k_1 \approx 4$ for low-carbon steel |

> [!CAUTION]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The vertical bar symbol $\vert$ is rendered as `\vert` in LaTeX. Do NOT use plain `|` inside markdown tables — it breaks the column parser.

---

## Real-World Engineering Utility

In **shipbuilding and marine engineering**, seam welding is used for:
- **Double-bottom fuel/oil tanks** (IMO SOLAS compliant, leak-tight seams).
- **Galley ventilation ducts** (stainless steel 304/316).
- **Heat exchanger panels** in **marine boilers** (Cu-Ni, Cu-Al tubes joined to tube sheets).
- **Aluminum superstructure panels** in naval vessels (mash seam welding).
- **Stiffened plate panels** for **ballast tanks** in LNG carriers.

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Linkage:** Modern Hyundai Heavy Industries (HHI) shipyards use **robotic seam welding cells** with **adaptive closed-loop current control** to weld **20+ meter-long bulkhead panels** at speeds of up to **5 m/min**, a typical KTU industrial-visit discussion point.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: Heat Required Per Unit Length of Seam

**Objective:** Derive the heat input per meter of seam as a function of welding current, resistance, and welding speed.

### Step 1 — State the Governing Law (Joule Heating)

Heat generated in the weld zone per unit time is given by **Joule's First Law**:

$$\dot{H} = I^2 R$$

where:
- $\dot{H}$ = rate of heat generation (W)
- $I$ = welding current (A)
- $R$ = dynamic resistance of the faying interface ($\Omega$)

### Step 2 — Express Heat Per Unit Length

Since the seam advances at welding speed $v$ (m/s), the **heat per unit length of seam** is:

$$H_L = \frac{\dot{H}}{v} = \frac{I^2 R}{v}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Welding Current in Terms of Thickness

For a given sheet thickness $s$ (mm), the empirical KTU relation is:

$$I = k_1 \, s^{0.75}$$

Squaring both sides:

$$I^2 = k_1^2 \, s^{1.5}$$

### Step 4 — Express Resistance in Terms of Material Properties

The contact resistance at the faying surface, treating it as a thin resistive layer of resistivity $\rho_c$ and thickness $\delta$ over contact area $A_c$:

$$R = \frac{\rho_c \, \delta}{A_c} = \frac{\rho_c \, \delta}{w \cdot d_n}$$

where $w$ = electrode face width and $d_n$ = nugget diameter.

### Step 5 — Combine All Terms

$$\boxed{\,H_L = \frac{k_1^2 \, s^{1.5} \cdot \rho_c \, \delta}{v \cdot w \cdot d_n}\,}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** This equation shows that heat input per unit length is **directly proportional to $s^{1.5}$** (thickness raised to 3/2 power) and **inversely proportional to welding speed**. Double the speed → halve the heat input.

---

## Derivation 2: Relationship Between Welding Speed, Pitch & Pulse Frequency

**Objective:** Show how welding speed $v$, nugget pitch $p$, and pulses per minute $f$ are interlinked.

### Step 1 — Define Pulse Frequency

Let $f$ = number of current pulses per minute (pulses/min).

### Step 2 — Distance Travelled Per Pulse

In one pulse cycle, the sheet advances by one pitch $p$:

$$p = \frac{v}{f / 60} = \frac{60 \, v}{f}$$

Solving for $v$:

$$\boxed{\,v = \frac{p \cdot f}{60}\,}$$

with units: $v$ in m/min, $p$ in m, $f$ in pulses/min.

### Step 3 — Required Overlap Condition

For leak-tight seams, overlap fraction $\lambda$ must satisfy:

$$\lambda = \frac{d_n - p}{d_n} \geq 0.15$$

Solving for $p$:

$$p \leq 0.85 \, d_n$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

**Given:**
- Sheet thickness $s = 2$ mm
- Welding speed $v = 1.5$ m/min
- Required overlap $\lambda = 0.20$

**Find:** Maximum allowed pitch $p_{max}$ and required pulse frequency $f$.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
d_n &\approx 4\sqrt{s} \\
d_n &\approx 4\sqrt{2} \\
d_n &\approx 5.66 \; \text{mm}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
p_{max} &= (1 - \lambda) \cdot d_n \\
p_{max} &= 0.80 \times 5.66 \\
p_{max} &= 4.53 \; \text{mm}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
f &= \frac{60 \, v}{p_{max}} \\
f &= \frac{60 \times 1.5}{4.53 \times 10^{-3}} \\
f &= 19{,}867 \; \text{pulses/min} \\
f &\approx 331 \; \text{Hz}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **typical KTU 14-mark question** style. Marks are awarded for: (i) formula statement, (ii) unit conversion, (iii) substitution, and (iv) final numerical answer with correct significant figures.

---

## Derivation 3: Force Requirement for Forging the Nugget

### Step 1 — Empirical KTU Relation

For low-carbon steel sheets of thickness $s$ (mm), the roll force $F$ is:

$$F = k \cdot s^2$$

with $k \approx 150$ N/mm² for steel.

### Step 2 — Derive Contact Pressure

$$P_e = \frac{F}{A_c} = \frac{k \cdot s^2}{w \cdot d_n}$$

### Step 3 — Plug In Numerical Values

For $s = 1.5$ mm, $w = 5$ mm, $d_n = 4.9$ mm (using $d_n = 4\sqrt{1.5}$):

$$\begin{aligned}
F &= 150 \times (1.5)^2 \\
F &= 150 \times 2.25 \\
F &= 337.5 \; \text{N (per mm of weld length)}
\end{aligned}$$

This value is multiplied by seam length in practice.

---

## Analytical Comparison Matrix — Seam vs Spot Welding

| **Parameter** | **Spot Welding** | **Seam Welding** | **Engineering Reason** |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|
| Electrode type | Stationary domed tip | Rotating wheel | Continuous feed |
| Current mode | Single pulse | Pulsed/continuous | Multiple nuggets |
| Weld length | Discrete points | Continuous line | Sealability |
| Cooling | Air / water spray | Water-cooled rollers | High duty cycle |
| Typical speed | 30–60 spots/min | 1–5 m/min | Productivity |
| Use in shipbuilding | Brackets, stiffeners | Tanks, ducts, panels | Leak-tight requirement |
| Equipment cost | Lower | 2–3× higher | Continuous operation |
| Current value | 6–12 kA | 8–30 kA | Larger interface |
| Heat input | Concentrated | Distributed | Different metallurgy |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Pattern:** A 14-mark question may ask: *"Compare seam welding with spot welding in the context of shipbuilding. Justify the choice of seam welding for double-bottom fuel tanks."* — Use the above matrix as the backbone of your answer.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram A: Seam Welding Process — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sheet Feeder Roller] --> B[Pre-Heating Station]
    B --> C[Upper Roller Electrode Cu-Cr-Zr]
    C --> D[Faying Interface Contact Zone]
    D --> E[Lower Roller Electrode Cu-Cr-Zr]
    E --> F[Forge Cooling Zone]
    F --> G[Seam Output Take-Up]
    H[AC Welding Transformer 50-60 Hz] --> I[SCR / IGBT Timer Module]
    I --> C
    I --> E
    J[Hydraulic / Pneumatic Cylinder] --> K[Force Transmission Arm]
    K --> C
    L[Cooling Water Recirculation Pump] --> C
    L --> E
    M[PLC Controller with Encoder Feedback] --> I
    M --> J
    N[Operator HMI Panel] --> M
```

**Interpretation of the Architecture:**
- The **Sheet Feeder Roller (A)** supplies the workpiece continuously.
- The **Upper Roller Electrode (C)** is force-loaded by the **Hydraulic Cylinder (J)**.
- The **AC Transformer (H)** feeds current via **SCR/IGBT Timer (I)**, which produces the pulsed waveform characteristic of seam welding.
- The **PLC (M)** monitors **weld current, voltage, and travel speed** via encoders, ensuring **closed-loop adaptive control** as per modern KTU 2024 industrial standards.

---

## Diagram B: Nugget Formation Sequence (Time-Domain Pulse Cycle)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Pulse 1 - Sheet Stationary] --> N1[Nugget 1 Formed]
    N1 --> P2[Sheet Advances by Pitch p]
    P2 --> P3[Pulse 2 - Sheet Stationary]
    P3 --> N2[Nugget 2 Formed - Overlaps with Nugget 1]
    N2 --> P4[Sheet Advances by Pitch p]
    P4 --> P5[Pulse 3 - Sheet Stationary]
    P5 --> N3[Nugget 3 Formed - Continuous Overlap]
    N3 --> Q[Continuous Seam Established]
    R[Off-Time between Pulses allows Solidification] -.-> P1
    R -.-> P3
    R -.-> P5
```

**Engineering Note:** The off-time $t_{off}$ between pulses is critical — it must be long enough for **forged solidification** of the previous nugget but short enough to avoid cold valleys. Typical $t_{off}$ is **2 to 5 cycles of 50 Hz** (i.e., 40–100 ms).

---

## Diagram C: Decision Matrix for Choosing Seam Weld Type (Shipbuilding)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ship Component to be Joined] --> B{Leak-Tight Required?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Single or Both Sides Accessible?}
    B -- No --> D[Use Standard Spot Welding]
    C -- Single Side --> E[Use Projection Seam Welding]
    C -- Both Sides --> F{Sheet Thickness?}
    F -- Less than 1 mm --> G[Use Continuous Foil Seam Welding]
    F -- 1 to 3 mm --> H[Use Standard Pulsed Seam Welding]
    F -- Greater than 3 mm --> I{Alloy Type?}
    I -- Aluminum --> J[Use Mash Seam Welding]
    I -- Steel --> K[Use Heavy-Duty Seam Welding with Water-Cooled Rollers]
```

---

## Diagram D: Electrode Force and Current Waveform — Synchronization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Force F Applied Constantly] --> B[Roller Contact Maintained]
    B --> C{Is Pulse ON?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Current I Flows - Heat Generated]
    C -- No --> E[No Current - Solidification Phase]
    D --> F[Nugget Forms at Faying Surface]
    F --> C
    E --> G[Nugget Solidifies under Force]
    G --> C
    H[Sheet Advances Continuously at Speed v] --> B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Concept:** The roller force is **NOT pulsed** — only the current is pulsed. This is a **very common KTU viva question** that distinguishes an expert student from a surface learner.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set B]`
**Define seam welding. List any two advantages of seam welding over spot welding in shipbuilding applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** Seam welding is a resistance welding process in which a continuous or intermittent weld is made between two overlapping sheets by passing them between two rotating circular roller electrodes, with welding current applied in pulses to produce a series of overlapping weld nuggets along the seam.

> **Advantages (1 mark — any two):**
> 1. Produces **leak-tight, hermetic joints** — essential for fuel tanks and ballast tanks.
> 2. Higher **productivity** for long seams (1–5 m/min) compared to discrete spot welds.
> 3. Uniform **load distribution** along the seam, reducing stress concentration.
> 4. Suitable for **automation** with robotic seam-welding cells.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set A]`
**What is the significance of "nugget pitch" in seam welding? Why must successive nuggets overlap by at least 15%?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Nugget Pitch (1 mark):** Nugget pitch $p$ is the center-to-center distance between two consecutive weld nuggets formed along the seam. It is governed by the welding speed $v$ and the pulse frequency $f$ via $p = 60v/f$.

> **Significance of Overlap (2 marks):** Successive nuggets must overlap by **15–20%** of nugget diameter to:
> 1. **Eliminate cold "valleys"** between nuggets that would otherwise act as leak paths.
> 2. **Compensate for current shunting** — the prior nugget acts as a low-resistance parallel path, which would otherwise starve the next nugget of current.
> 3. Ensure **metallurgical continuity** along the grain structure of the seam.

---

## Part B Question A (14 Marks)

### `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`
**Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of seam welding. Label the major components and describe the role of each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A shipyard uses seam welding to fabricate a **stainless-steel 304 fuel tank panel** of sheet thickness **1.6 mm**. The welding speed is **1.2 m/min**, the required overlap is **20%**, and the nugget diameter is empirically given by $d_n = 4\sqrt{s}$. Determine:
   (i) The nugget diameter.
   (ii) The maximum allowable pitch.
   (iii) The required pulse frequency in Hz.

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Sketch Description (3 marks):** The diagram should show:
- Two overlapping sheets (1.6 mm each) entering from the left.
- **Upper roller electrode** and **lower roller electrode** made of Cu-Cr-Zr alloy.
- **Welding transformer** with SCR/IGBT timer.
- **Force application** via pneumatic cylinder.
- **Cooling water inlet/outlet** to rollers.
- **Output seam** on the right with overlapping nuggets.

**Component Roles (4 marks — 1 each):**
1. **Roller Electrodes**: Conduct current and apply continuous forging force; act as heat sinks.
2. **Welding Transformer**: Steps down mains voltage to 1–10 V and steps up current to 5–30 kA.
3. **SCR/IGBT Timer**: Generates the **pulsed current waveform** (typically 2–4 cycles ON, 1–2 cycles OFF).
4. **PLC / Encoder System**: Synchronizes pulse timing with sheet travel speed for consistent pitch.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- $s = 1.6$ mm
- $v = 1.2$ m/min
- $\lambda = 0.20$ (overlap fraction)
- $d_n = 4\sqrt{s}$

**Step 1 — Nugget diameter (2 marks):**

$$d_n = 4\sqrt{1.6} = 4 \times 1.265 = 5.06 \; \text{mm}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum pitch (2 marks):**

$$p_{max} = (1 - \lambda) \cdot d_n = 0.80 \times 5.06 = 4.05 \; \text{mm}$$

**Step 3 — Pulse frequency (3 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
f_{pulses/min} &= \frac{60 \, v}{p_{max}} \\
f_{pulses/min} &= \frac{60 \times 1.2}{4.05 \times 10^{-3}} \\
f_{pulses/min} &= 17{,}778 \; \text{pulses/min}
\end{aligned}$$

Converting to Hz:

$$f_{Hz} = \frac{17{,}778}{60} = 296.3 \; \text{Hz}$$

**Final Answer:** $d_n = 5.06$ mm, $p_{max} = 4.05$ mm, $f = 296.3$ Hz.

---

## Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

### `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`
**Question B (14 Marks):**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare seam welding with spot welding in the context of shipbuilding. Justify the choice of seam welding for double-bottom fuel tanks in a bulk carrier.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the relationship between welding speed, nugget pitch, and pulse frequency. A seam-welding machine operates at a pitch of **3.5 mm** and pulse frequency of **280 Hz**. Calculate the welding speed in m/min.

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Comparison Table (3 marks):** Use the analytical matrix from Section 3 of these notes.

**Justification (4 marks):** Double-bottom fuel tanks in bulk carriers must comply with **SOLAS Chapter II-2, Regulation 4.2** and **IACS S21** rules, requiring:
1. **Hermetic seal** — even at high hydrostatic pressure (up to 0.3 MPa at full ballast). Spot welds cannot guarantee this; seam welds do.
2. **Vibration resistance** — continuous seam distributes cyclic loading, while discrete spot welds suffer fatigue crack initiation.
3. **Corrosion resistance** — fewer crevices compared to overlapping spot welds where capillary action draws in seawater.
4. **Inspection ease** — modern phased-array UT scanning can detect lack of overlap in seam welds with high reliability.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Derivation (4 marks):**

$$v = \frac{p \cdot f}{60}$$

**Numerical (3 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
v &= \frac{3.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 280 \times 60}{60} \\
v &= 3.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 280 \\
v &= 0.98 \; \text{m/min}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:** Welding speed = **0.98 m/min**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose up to 4 marks each):**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors**: Forgetting to convert mm to m in the pitch calculation. Always write $p$ in **meters** before plugging in.
> 2. **Confusing Rolls vs Sheets**: The "thickness" in $d_n = 4\sqrt{s}$ refers to **one sheet**, not the stack. Examiners deduct 2 marks for this mistake.
> 3. **Ignoring Overlap Requirement**: Students often calculate pitch from speed alone, ignoring the **0.85 $d_n$ limit**. The seam would leak!
> 4. **Mixing Up Frequency Units**: KTU expects **both** Hz and pulses/min. If only one is given, deduct 1 mark.
> 5. **Wrong Heat Formula**: Writing $H = IR^2$ instead of $H = I^2Rt$ — this is a **3-mark killer** in derivations.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Definition**: Seam welding = continuous/intermittent resistance welding using rotating roller electrodes and pulsed current to form overlapping nuggets.
- [x] **Process Number (ISO 4063)**: 23.
- [x] **Electrode Material**: Cu-Cr-Zr alloy (high conductivity, high strength at elevated temperature).
- [x] **Heat Source**: Joule heating ($H = I^2Rt$), localized at the faying interface.
- [x] **Key Overlap Rule**: Successive nuggets must overlap by **15–20%** of $d_n$ for leak-tightness.
- [x] **Nugget Diameter Empirical**: $d_n \approx 4\sqrt{s}$ (mm), $s$ in mm.
- [x] **Speed–Pitch–Frequency Triangle**: $v = p \cdot f / 60$.
- [x] **Roll Force Empirical**: $F = 150 \, s^2$ N per mm weld length (low-carbon steel).
- [x] **Shipbuilding Applications**: Fuel tanks, oil tanks, ballast tanks, ventilation ducts, boiler heat-exchanger panels, double-bottom panels.
- [x] **Classification**: Roll spot, intermittent standard, continuous foil, mash, and projection seam welding.
- [x] **Force is Continuous, Current is Pulsed** — viva-favorite distinction.
- [x] **Higher Cost than Spot Welding** but justified by **leak-tightness and productivity**.
- [x] **Standards**: ISO 4063, AWS C1.1, IACS S21, SOLAS Reg. II-2/4.2.
- [x] **Typical Parameters**: $s$ = 0.5–3 mm, $v$ = 1–5 m/min, $I$ = 5–30 kA, $F$ = 2–50 kN, $f$ = 50–500 Hz, $d_n$ = 3–8 mm.
- [x] **Modern Trend**: Robotic seam-welding cells with **closed-loop adaptive current control** based on real-time resistance feedback.
- [x] **Comparison Mnemonic**: **"Seam is Stream; Spot is Spot"** — continuous stream of nuggets vs discrete spots.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Projection Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST831 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Projection Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Projection Welding** is a specialized variant of **Resistance Welding** (specifically derived from **Spot Welding**) in which the concentration of welding current and the localized generation of heat at the joint interface is achieved through pre-formed geometrical protrusions — called **projections** (also termed *embossments*, *nubs*, or *buttons*) — deliberately designed on one or both of the workpieces to be joined.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Board Definition:**  
> *Projection welding is a resistance welding process in which coalescence is produced by the heat obtained from the resistance of the workpieces to the flow of electric current through them, with the current density and weld nugget location being controlled by the geometry of pre-formed projections on the faying surfaces, while pressure is applied and maintained throughout the heating cycle.*

The projection acts as a **current concentration point**. Because its cross-sectional area is significantly smaller than the parent sheet, the same magnitude of current produces a drastically higher current density at the projection, causing the projection to rapidly heat, soften, and collapse under electrode pressure — forming the weld nugget.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hose Nozzle" Intuition

Imagine you are watering a garden with a hose fitted with a narrow nozzle. The same volume of water passing through a wide pipe section (analogous to the flat sheet) flows calmly, but the instant it enters the narrow constriction (the **projection**), the **velocity and pressure spike dramatically** at that single point. This is the same phenomenon in projection welding — current behaves like water, and the projection is the **nozzle**. Heat, friction, and pressure all concentrate at that tiny zone, melting the metal there before the surrounding parent material is even warm. As the projection collapses, the current path widens, the heat drops, and the molten metal forges into a nugget.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Key Distinguishing Feature:**  
> In **spot welding**, current concentration is achieved by the **electrode tip geometry (dome shape)**. In **projection welding**, the current concentration is achieved by the **workpiece geometry itself** (pre- embossed projections). This is a critical differentiator often asked in KTU exams.

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Projection density on surface:** Typically **2 to 30 projections per cm²** depending on joint design.
- **Current density at projection tip:** Can reach values in the order of **10⁵ to 10⁶ A/mm²** during collapse.
- **Standard projection heights:** Between **0.4 mm to 1.5 mm** for sheet metal work.
- **Specific electrical resistance of mild steel at 20°C:** **ρ ≈ 1.7 × 10⁻⁷ Ω·m**.
- **Melting point of low-carbon steel:** Approximately **1500°C**; weld nugget temperature during projection welding typically **reaches 1200–1500°C**.

> [!TIP]  
> **Why this matters in shipbuilding:** Ship panels often have hundreds of stiffeners and attachment points. Projection welding allows *simultaneous multiple welds* in a single press stroke — making it indispensable for mass production of decks, bulkheads, and hull sub-assemblies.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Sequence — The Six Logical Phases

Projection welding does not occur in a single instant. The KTU board expects students to describe the **staged behaviour** of the projection as heat and force are applied. The process follows a well-defined sequence:

1. **Squeeze Phase (Pre-heat Pressurization):**
   - Electrodes descend and bring workpieces into contact.
   - Light initial pressure establishes electrical contact.
   - No current is yet flowing.

2. **Projection Contact Initiation:**
   - The embossed projection is the *only* point of true metallic contact.
   - Bulk parent sheets are still separated by a thin air/oxide film.
   - **All current is forced through this single small contact.**

3. **Weld Phase (Heating & Collapse):**
   - Current is switched on; **resistive (Joule) heating** occurs at the projection tip: $Q = I^2 R t$.
   - The projection rapidly reaches forging/melting temperature.
   - The projection **softens and collapses** flat under electrode force, displacing molten/ plastic metal outward into a flash.

4. **Forge / Coalescence Phase:**
   - As the projection flattens, the contact area grows, resistance drops, and heating self-limits.
   - A solid forged weld nugget forms at the interface.

5. **Hold Phase:**
   - Current is cut off **while pressure is maintained**.
   - The nugget solidifies under pressure, eliminating voids and porosity.

6. **Off / Release Phase:**
   - Electrodes retract; the welded assembly is removed.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Projection Welding — Engineering Rationale

- **Controlled current path:** Without projections, current would spread across the entire electrode-workpiece interface, producing weak, undefined bonds.
- **Reproducibility:** Pre-formed projections are dimensionally consistent → uniform weld quality across thousands of cycles.
- **Multi-point welding:** A single press can create **dozens of welds simultaneously**, perfect for ship panels and automotive sheet assemblies.
- **Lower current requirement:** Because resistance is concentrated, less total current is needed than for equivalent spot welds.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Physical Meaning** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Heat generated at projection | $Q = I^2 R t$ | Joules (J) | Joule's first law of heating |
| Total interface resistance | $R = 2R_w + 2R_{ew} + R_{proj}$ | Ohms (Ω) | Sum of all resistances in current path |
| Current density at projection | $J = \dfrac{I}{A_{proj}}$ | A/mm² | Crucial parameter for nugget formation |
| Projection collapse force | $F_p = A_{proj} \cdot \sigma_{yield}$ | Newtons (N) | Force required to flatten projection |
| Electrode pressure (typical) | $P = 30$ to $70$ MPa (projected area) | MPa | Must exceed collapse threshold |
| Heat balance equation | $Q = m \cdot c \cdot \Delta T + Q_{losses}$ | Joules | Useful heat + conduction/radiation losses |
| Energy efficiency factor | $\eta = \dfrac{Q_{useful}}{Q_{input}}$ | Dimensionless | Typically $0.5$ to $0.85$ for projection welding |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> In the resistance formula $R = 2R_w + 2R_{ew} + R_{proj}$, the term $R_{proj}$ (projection contact resistance) is **dominant** at the start. As the projection collapses, $R_{proj}$ falls sharply, and the system becomes self-regulating.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **shipbuilding yards**, projection welding is extensively used for:

- Joining **corrugated bulkhead panels** to face plates.
- Welding **stiffener webs** to plating.
- **Brackets, lugs, and cleats** attachment to hull sections.
- Manufacturing **deck panels** with stiffener grids.
- **Stainless steel kitchen sinks, automotive seat frames, and electrical contact assemblies** outside marine use.

Because ship hull sections can be very large (multi-meter panels), projection welding offers a **productivity multiplier** that arc welding cannot match in terms of cycle time.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Heat Concentration Ratio at a Projection

This derivation proves *quantitatively* why a projection heats faster than the surrounding sheet.

**Given:**
- Sheet thickness: $t_s$ (mm)
- Projection diameter: $d_p$ (mm)
- Projection height: $h_p$ (mm)
- Total current: $I$ (A)

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area of the sheet (current path through bulk):**

$$A_s = t_s \cdot w$$

where $w$ is the width of the current conduction zone. For analysis we normalize per unit width, so $A_s \approx t_s$.

**Step 2 — Cross-sectional area of the projection tip:**

$$A_{proj} = \frac{\pi d_p^2}{4}$$

**Step 3 — Current density in the bulk sheet:**

$$J_s = \frac{I}{A_s} = \frac{I}{t_s}$$

**Step 4 — Current density at projection tip (assuming all current funnels through projection):**

$$J_{proj} = \frac{I}{A_{proj}} = \frac{4I}{\pi d_p^2}$$

**Step 5 — Heat concentration ratio (the KTU key result):**

$$\frac{Q_{proj}}{Q_{s}} \propto \frac{J_{proj}^2}{J_s^2} = \left(\frac{A_s}{A_{proj}}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{4 t_s}{\pi d_p^2}\right)^2$$

**Step 6 — Numerical example (typical):**
Take $t_s = 2$ mm, $d_p = 4$ mm.

$$\frac{A_s}{A_{proj}} = \frac{2}{\pi (4)^2 / 4} = \frac{2}{12.57} \approx 0.159$$

$$\frac{Q_{proj}}{Q_s} = (0.159)^2 \approx 0.025$$

This shows that although the projection has only ~16% of the bulk current path area, the *heat generation per unit volume* is concentrated in the projection. The projection heats **~40× faster** than the surrounding bulk for the same mass, because heat generation scales with $J^2$ and $J$ is amplified by the area ratio.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Critical Projection Collapse Force

The projection must be collapsed to a height of **zero** (or a small residual) within the weld time. Therefore the electrode force must exceed a threshold.

**Step 1 — Yield criterion (von Mises approximation for projection at high temperature):**
At projection tip temperature $T \approx 1200°C$, the yield strength $\sigma_y$ of mild steel drops to approximately $\sigma_y \approx 30$ MPa.

**Step 2 — Required forging force:**

$$F_{forge} = \sigma_y(T) \cdot A_{proj}$$

**Step 3 — Substituting $A_{proj} = \pi d_p^2 / 4$:**

$$F_{forge} = \frac{\pi d_p^2 \sigma_y(T)}{4}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example:**
For $d_p = 4$ mm, $\sigma_y = 30$ MPa:

$$F_{forge} = \frac{\pi (0.004)^2 \times 30 \times 10^6}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 1.6 \times 10^{-5} \times 30 \times 10^6}{4}$$

$$F_{forge} = \frac{1.508 \times 10^3}{4} \approx 377 \text{ N}$$

This is the **minimum** force. In practice, a **safety factor of 1.5 to 2.0** is applied, giving actual electrode forces of ~600–800 N per projection.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Projection Geometry vs. Application

| **Projection Type** | **Geometry** | **Typical Application** | **Current Required** | **Weld Strength** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Spherical (Domed)** | Hemisphere embossed on sheet | Most common; sheet-to-sheet joints | Medium | High |
| **Conical (Pointed)** | Sharp cone, 60°–90° apex | Thin sheets, low current applications | Low | Medium |
| **Annular / Ring** | Circular ridge with central recess | Sealed joints (e.g., filter caps) | Medium-High | Very High (sealing + strength) |
| **Embossed (Cross-shaped)** | Cross rib projection | High-strength structural joints | High | High |
| **Multiple button array** | Several small domes in array | Ship panels, automotive sub-assemblies | Distributed (sum of all) | Distributed |
| **Male-Female (interlocking)** | Projection on one sheet fits recess in other | Tube-to-flange welds, precision assembly | Low (intimate contact) | Excellent |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow — Projection Welding Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Workpiece placed between electrodes] --> B[Squeeze Phase: Electrodes descend, light pressure applied]
    B --> C[Initial Contact: Only projection touches mating surface]
    C --> D[Weld Phase: Current ON, Joule heating at projection]
    D --> E[Projection softens and collapses under electrode force]
    E --> F[Forge Phase: Nugget forms, current self-limits as contact area grows]
    F --> G[Hold Phase: Current OFF, pressure maintained for solidification]
    G --> H[Release Phase: Electrodes retract, welded assembly removed]
    H --> I[Inspect: Nugget diameter, shear strength, peel test]
    I --> J{Conforms to WPS?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Accept: Move to next assembly stage]
    J -- No --> L[Reject: Mark and route to rework]
```

## 4.2 Heat Concentration Schematic — Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BulkSheet[Parent Sheet Current Path]
        S1[Sheet Section A] --> S2[Sheet Section B]
        S2 --> S3[Sheet Section C]
    end
    subgraph ProjectionZone[Projection Zone - High Resistance]
        P1[Projection Tip - Maximum Joule Heat]
    end
    subgraph Electrode[Electrode Interface]
        E1[Top Electrode - Copper Alloy]
        E2[Bottom Electrode - Copper Alloy]
    end
    E1 --> S1
    S1 --> P1
    P1 --> S3
    S3 --> E2
    R1[Current Source 50 Hz AC 5 to 20 kA] -.Supplies.-> E1
    R1 -.Returns.-> E2
    P1 -.Generates.-> H1[Heat Q = I squared R t]
    H1 --> M1[Projection Collapses]
    M1 --> N1[Weld Nugget Solidifies]
```

## 4.3 Comparison Architecture — Spot Welding vs Projection Welding

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Resistance Welding Family] --> B[Spot Welding]
    A --> C[Projection Welding]
    A --> D[Seam Welding]
    A --> E[Butt Welding]
    B --> B1[Current concentration via ELECTRODE tip dome shape]
    C --> C1[Current concentration via WORKPIECE projection geometry]
    B --> B2[Single point weld per electrode stroke]
    C --> C2[Multiple simultaneous welds per stroke]
    B --> B3[Limited to small localized joints]
    C --> C3[Ideal for ship panels and mass production]
    B --> B4[Lower equipment cost]
    C --> C4[Higher tooling cost offset by productivity]
```

## 4.4 Equipment Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Three Phase AC Mains Supply] --> B[Step Down Welding Transformer]
    B --> C[Secondary Winding - Low Voltage High Current]
    C --> D[Electrode Arms - Water Cooled]
    D --> E1[Top Electrode]
    D --> E2[Bottom Electrode]
    F1[Pneumatic or Hydraulic Cylinder] --> G[Force Application on Electrode Arms]
    H1[Control Cabinet] --> H2[Welding Timer SCR Contactors]
    H2 --> B
    I1[Cooling Water Circuit] --> D
    J1[Workpiece with Pre-formed Projections] --> E1
    J1 --> E2
    E1 --> K1[Weld Nugget Formation at Projection]
    E2 --> K1
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q1. Define projection welding and state two of its principal advantages over spot welding for shipbuilding applications.** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**
Projection welding is a resistance welding process in which current concentration and localized heating are achieved through pre-formed geometric projections embossed on the faying surface of the workpieces.
**Advantages over spot welding:**
1. **Multiple simultaneous welds** in a single press stroke — critical for ship panel fabrication.
2. **Lower current requirement** per weld point because the projection concentrates the current.
3. *Optional 3rd point:* More uniform weld quality due to consistent projection geometry.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q2. State the Joule's heating equation governing projection welding and explain the role of the term $R_{proj}$ in it.** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**
Joule's heating equation: $Q = I^2 R t$, where $Q$ is heat in joules, $I$ is current in amperes, $R$ is total resistance in ohms, and $t$ is time in seconds.
The term $R_{proj}$ represents the contact resistance at the projection interface. It is the **dominant resistive component** initially because the cross-sectional area at the projection is small, forcing all current through a constricted path. As the projection collapses, $R_{proj}$ decreases, making the process **self-regulating**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Dec 2024 Pattern]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. (a)** With the aid of a neat schematic, explain the working principle of projection welding. Discuss the role of projection geometry, electrode force, and welding current in producing a sound weld. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle:**
Projection welding uses the principle of **Joule resistive heating** concentrated at pre-formed projections. The workpieces to be joined are placed between two water-cooled copper electrodes. One or both workpieces have embossments (projections) at desired weld locations. When current flows, it is forced through the small cross-section of the projection, generating intense localized heat. The projection reaches forging temperature, softens, and collapses under electrode pressure, forming a weld nugget.

**Role of Projection Geometry:**
- The projection must be **dimensionally precise** (typical height 0.4–1.5 mm for sheet metal).
- A smaller cross-sectional area at the tip means **higher current density** ($J = I/A$).
- A dome/spherical shape provides a **gradual collapse** that helps expel surface oxides and contaminants.
- A conical projection offers faster heating but is more prone to burn-through on thin sheets.

**Role of Electrode Force:**
- Must be sufficient to **collapse the projection** completely (typically $F_{forge} = \sigma_y A_{proj}$).
- Force is maintained **throughout the hold phase** to prevent porosity and shrinkage voids.
- Insufficient force → incomplete coalescence; excessive force → metal expulsion and burn-through.

**Role of Welding Current:**
- Provides the energy to raise the projection to welding temperature via $Q = I^2 R t$.
- Typically **5 to 20 kA** for low-carbon steel sheets of 1–3 mm.
- Current is **alternating (50 Hz mains frequency)** from a welding transformer.
- Too low → cold weld; too high → expulsion, burn-through, electrode sticking.

A labelled schematic should show: top electrode, projection on upper sheet, lower sheet, bottom electrode, current flow arrows, and the weld nugget forming zone.

---

**Q.A. (b)** A shipyard uses projection welding to attach a 2 mm thick mild steel stiffener to a 2 mm thick base plate. The projections are spherical with a base diameter of 4 mm. The welding current is 8 kA and the weld time is 0.2 seconds. Given that the resistance at the projection interface is 250 μΩ and the efficiency is 0.7, calculate: (i) the heat generated, (ii) the heat available for nugget formation, and (iii) the average current density at the projection tip. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $I = 8$ kA $= 8000$ A
- $t = 0.2$ s
- $R = 250$ μΩ $= 250 \times 10^{-6}$ Ω
- Efficiency $\eta = 0.7$
- Projection diameter $d_p = 4$ mm

**Step (i) — Heat generated (Joule's law):**

$$Q = I^2 R t = (8000)^2 \times (250 \times 10^{-6}) \times 0.2$$

$$Q = 64 \times 10^6 \times 250 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.2$$

$$Q = 64 \times 250 \times 0.2 = 64 \times 50 = 3200 \text{ J}$$

**Step (ii) — Useful heat for nugget formation:**

$$Q_{useful} = \eta \times Q = 0.7 \times 3200 = 2240 \text{ J}$$

**Step (iii) — Current density at projection tip:**

$$A_{proj} = \frac{\pi d_p^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (4)^2}{4} = 12.57 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$J = \frac{I}{A_{proj}} = \frac{8000}{12.57} = 636.5 \text{ A/mm}^2$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $Q = 3200$ J
- (ii) $Q_{useful} = 2240$ J
- (iii) $J = 636.5$ A/mm²

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**  
> Students frequently **forget to convert μΩ to Ω** (multiply by 10⁻⁶). This single error loses 2 marks. Also, do NOT confuse the projection base diameter with the projection height — for current density, we use the **cross-sectional area of the projection tip**, not the sheet thickness.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B. (a)** Compare and contrast projection welding with spot welding under the following heads: (i) current concentration mechanism, (ii) equipment cost, (iii) production rate, (iv) typical applications in shipbuilding, and (v) weld quality consistency. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **Spot Welding** | **Projection Welding** |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Current concentration | By electrode tip dome geometry | By pre-formed projection on workpiece |
| (ii) Equipment cost | Lower (simpler tooling) | Higher (projection dies + embossing step) |
| (iii) Production rate | One weld per stroke | Multiple welds per stroke (2 to 30+ simultaneously) |
| (iv) Shipbuilding applications | Small brackets, lugs, repair patches | Large panels, bulkheads, stiffener grids, deck plating |
| (v) Weld quality consistency | Depends on electrode wear | More consistent (projection geometry is the controlling factor) |

**Conclusion:** Projection welding offers superior productivity and consistency for high-volume ship panel production, while spot welding is more economical for low-volume, single-point joints.

---

**Q.B. (b)** Explain the different types of projections used in projection welding with suitable diagrams. For each type, mention one specific shipbuilding application. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

1. **Spherical (Domed) Projection:** A hemispherical embossment, most widely used. Provides gradual collapse and good expulsion of oxides. **Application:** Sheet-to-sheet joint in corrugated bulkhead panels.

2. **Conical Projection:** Sharp cone with 60°–90° apex angle. Heats very fast; suitable for thin sheets. **Application:** Stainless steel sink liners and galley equipment in ships.

3. **Annular / Ring Projection:** A circular ridge surrounding a central recess. **Application:** Sealing flanges of marine pumps and valve bodies where both sealing and strength are required.

4. **Cross-shaped Projection:** A cross-rib embossment with high stiffness. **Application:** Structural stiffener-to-plate joints in hull sub-assemblies.

5. **Multiple Button Array Projection:** Many small projections arranged in a defined pattern. **Application:** Large deck panels where distributed load-bearing welds are needed.

6. **Male-Female Interlocking Projection:** A projection on one sheet fits into a recess on the other, ensuring perfect alignment. **Application:** Pipe-to-flange welds in ship piping systems.

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**  
> Many students write only 2–3 types and skip the diagrams. For 7 marks, you must cover **at least 4–5 types** with a labelled sketch of *each*. Even a hand-drawn schematic in the exam earns full marks if dimensions and labels are clear.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]  
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Projection Welding**

- ✅ Projection welding is a **resistance welding** process; current concentration comes from **workpiece geometry**, not electrode shape.
- ✅ Heat generation: $Q = I^2 R t$ (Joule's Law) — the KTU-favourite formula.
- ✅ Total resistance has **five components**: $2R_w + 2R_{ew} + R_{proj}$; **$R_{proj}$ dominates initially**.
- ✅ Process sequence: **Squeeze → Weld → Forge → Hold → Release**.
- ✅ Projection types: Spherical, Conical, Annular, Cross, Button array, Male-Female.
- ✅ Typical projection height: **0.4–1.5 mm**; typical current: **5–20 kA** for mild steel.
- ✅ Electrode force must **exceed** the collapse force $F_{forge} = \sigma_y(T) \cdot A_{proj}$ with a safety factor of 1.5–2.0.
- ✅ Critical shipbuilding applications: **bulkhead panels, stiffener grids, deck plating, brackets**.
- ✅ **Key advantage over spot welding:** Multiple simultaneous welds per press stroke → high productivity.
- ✅ The process is **self-regulating**: as the projection collapses, $R_{proj}$ falls, preventing overheating.
- ✅ Always convert **μΩ → Ω** (multiply by 10⁻⁶) in numerical problems.
- ✅ For current density $J = I/A_{proj}$, use the **projection tip cross-sectional area**, not sheet thickness.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Resistance Upset Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST831 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Resistance Upset Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Resistance Upset Welding (RUW) — Module 2 Study Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Resistance Upset Welding (RUW)** is a variant of **resistance butt welding** in which the heat required for welding is generated by passing a heavy electric current through the abutting faces of the workpieces held under a light initial pressure. Once the interface metal reaches the plastic (forging) temperature, a high **upsetting force** is suddenly applied, causing the softened metal to extrude out as flash and producing a forged, fine-grained weld across the entire cross-section.

The process is governed by **Joule's Law of Heating**, where the heat generated at the joint interface is given by:

$$H = I^{2} \, R \, t$$

where $I$ is the welding current (Amperes), $R$ is the electrical resistance at the interface (Ohms), and $t$ is the time of current flow (seconds).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine holding two ends of a soft toffee stick together and squeezing them while heating the joint with a match. The heat softens the toffee, and the squeeze pushes out a thin "ribbon" of melted/softened material around the joint. The remaining material at the joint is then firmly bonded. **Resistance Upset Welding does exactly this**, but with metals, electricity (instead of a flame), and a precisely controlled hydraulic or pneumatic ram (instead of your hand).

The "resistance" comes from the contact interface between the two pieces, which has the highest electrical resistance in the circuit — so the heat concentrates exactly where we want the weld to form.

### Key Standard Metrics in RUW

> [!NOTE]
> - **Welding current (secondary current)**: typically **5,000 A to 50,000 A** at low voltage (2–10 V).
> - **Upsetting pressure**: **35 MPa to 70 MPa** (significantly higher than the forging pressure).
> - **Forging (initial heating) pressure**: **5 MPa to 15 MPa**.
> - **Time cycle**: usually **1 to 10 seconds** total.
> - **Heat input**: governed by $H = I^{2} R t$ in **Joules**.

### Position in the Family of Welding Processes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Resistance Upset Welding belongs to the **Resistance Welding** family (Class 41 per ISO 4063). It is specifically a **resistance butt welding** process. Its closest cousin is **Flash Butt Welding (FBW)** — the main difference being that in RUW the current is applied *before* the parts touch firmly and upset occurs in one shot, whereas in FBW flashing/vaporization begins first and then upset is applied.

### Typical Shipbuilding Applications

> [!NOTE]
> - Welding of **pipes and tubes** (e.g., longitudinal seams in pipe spools for ship piping systems).
> - Joining of **bars, rods, and reinforcing sections** used in hull stiffeners.
> - Welding of **chain links** and anchor shackles.
> - Production of **rolled rings and discs** for marine bearings.
> - Joining **rail sections** for shipbuilding gantry cranes.
> - **Tailor-welded blanks** for ship hull plates.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — The Physics

Resistance Upset Welding is a three-stage thermomechanical process. Each stage must be tightly controlled, because any deviation produces defects such as lack of fusion, oxide inclusions, or centreline cracks.

### Stage 1 — Squeeze & Initial Contact (Setup)

The two workpieces are clamped in copper alloy (or water-cooled) electrodes/dies and brought into light contact. A light **forging pressure ($P_f$)** of **5–15 MPa** is applied so that only a small number of asperities (microscopic high points) are in true contact.

### Stage 2 — Resistance Heating (Current ON)

Heavy current (low voltage, high amperage) from a welding transformer is passed through the joint. Because:

- The **interface resistance ($R_i$)** is the largest in the circuit (orders of magnitude higher than the bulk material resistance).
- The current path is concentrated at the joint.

…the heat generated by $H = I^{2} R t$ is localized at the interface. Temperature rises rapidly to the **forging range** — typically **0.8 $T_m$ to 0.9 $T_m$** (where $T_m$ is the melting point in Kelvin). At this temperature, the metal becomes plastic but **not molten**, distinguishing RUW from fusion processes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why not melt the metal?** Because molten metal traps oxide films and shrinkage cavities. Keeping the interface in the *plastic* (forging) range allows the subsequent upset to physically *expel* oxides and contaminants sideways as flash, yielding a sound, forged weld.

### Stage 3 — Upsetting (Forging under High Pressure)

The instant the interface reaches forging temperature, the **upsetting force ($P_u$)** is applied — typically **5× to 10× the forging force**. This causes:

1. **Plastic deformation** of the heated zone.
2. **Extrusion of flash** (a thin collar of upset metal) around the joint.
3. **Grain refinement** through dynamic recrystallization.
4. **Forging out** of oxides, lubricants, and surface contaminants.

The flash is normally removed later by machining or grinding.

## 2.2 Heat Generation and Heat Balance (Core Theory)

The total heat generated at the interface:

$$H_{total} = \int_{0}^{t_w} I^{2}(t) \, R(t) \, dt$$

For a constant current approximation:

$$H_{total} = I^{2} \, \bar{R} \, t_w$$

where $\bar{R}$ is the time-averaged interface resistance. A useful **heat balance** for the joint:

$$H_{generated} = H_{interface} + H_{conducted\,away} + H_{radiated} + H_{stored}$$

In practice, **70–90 %** of the generated heat is concentrated within a small zone of width $b$ (heat-affected band) on either side of the interface.

> [!NOTE]
> The **heat-affected zone (HAZ) width** can be approximated for a bar of thermal diffusivity $\alpha$, heated for time $t_w$:

$$b \approx 2 \sqrt{\alpha \, t_w}$$

with $\alpha = \dfrac{k}{\rho \, c_p}$, where $k$ is thermal conductivity, $\rho$ is density, and $c_p$ is specific heat.

## 2.3 Upsetting Mechanics

The upset produces a **reduction in length** $\Delta L$ and a **bulge (flash) of volume $V_{flash}$**. By **conservation of volume**:

$$V_{flash} = A_0 \, \Delta L$$

where $A_0$ is the original cross-sectional area. The **upset ratio** is defined as:

$$\text{Upset Ratio} = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} \times 100\%$$

Typical upset ratios in shipbuilding pipe welds are **10 % to 25 %**.

The **upsetting pressure** required to forge plastic metal at forging temperature is approximated by:

$$P_u = K_f \, \sigma_{y}(T_{forge})$$

where $K_f \approx 2$–$4$ is a constraint factor (due to triaxial stress at the interface) and $\sigma_{y}(T_{forge})$ is the yield stress of the material at forging temperature.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter / Symbol** | **Formula / Expression** | **Typical Value / Unit** | **Engineering Meaning** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Heat generated | $H = I^{2} R t$ | Joules (J) | Joule heating at interface |
| Heat balance | $H_{gen} = H_{int} + H_{cond} + H_{rad} + H_{store}$ | J | Energy distribution at joint |
| HAZ width | $b \approx 2 \sqrt{\alpha t_w}$ | metres (m) | Width of heat-affected band |
| Thermal diffusivity | $\alpha = \dfrac{k}{\rho c_p}$ | m²/s | Rate of heat diffusion |
| Upsetting pressure | $P_u = K_f \sigma_y(T)$ | Pa (MPa) | Pressure needed to forge plastic zone |
| Upset ratio | $\dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0} \times 100\%$ | % | Amount of plastic deformation |
| Flash volume | $V_{flash} = A_0 \Delta L$ | m³ | Volume of extruded flash |
| Power input | $P = I^{2} R$ | Watts (W) | Instantaneous electrical power |
| Current density | $J = \dfrac{I}{A_0}$ | A/m² | Current per unit area |
| Contact resistance | $R_c = \dfrac{\rho_s}{A_{real}}$ | Ω | Real area of contact dominates |
| Forging temperature | $T_f = (0.8 \text{ to } 0.9) \, T_m$ | K | Plastic range, below melting |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the table above, every absolute value, set notation, or magnitude is written using `\vert` instead of the vertical bar `|` to keep the markdown table parser safe.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Marine Engineering

In shipbuilding, RUW is preferred over arc welding for **mass production of longitudinal pipe seams** because:
- It produces a **uniform, full-section forged joint** with no filler metal.
- The weld has **better fatigue and corrosion resistance** than fusion welds.
- Cycle times are short (2–10 s), ideal for **automated production lines** in shipyards.
- No shielding gas or flux is required, reducing consumable costs.
- It is **operator-skill independent** once parameters are set — a key advantage in high-throughput shipyards.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Heat Generated at the Interface (Joule's Law)

**Statement:** Show that the heat generated at the interface of two workpieces during resistance upset welding is given by $H = I^{2} R t$, starting from fundamental electrical and thermal principles.

**Given:**
- Current flowing through the joint: $I$ (Amperes)
- Interface contact resistance: $R$ (Ohms)
- Duration of current flow: $t$ (seconds)

**Step 1 — Electrical Power Dissipated at the Interface**

By the definition of electrical power dissipated in a resistor carrying a steady current $I$ across a potential difference $V$:

$$P = V \, I$$

Using Ohm's Law $V = I R$, we substitute:

$$P = (I R) \, I = I^{2} R$$

This is the **instantaneous power** (in Watts) converted into heat at the joint interface.

**Step 2 — Total Heat Generated Over Time**

Heat is energy, which is the time-integral of power. Assuming $I$ and $R$ are approximately constant during the short heating cycle:

$$H = \int_{0}^{t} P \, dt = \int_{0}^{t} I^{2} R \, dt$$

Pulling the constants outside the integral (since $I$, $R$ do not depend on $t$ in this simplified case):

$$H = I^{2} R \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

Evaluating the integral:

$$H = I^{2} R \, [t]_{0}^{t} = I^{2} R \, t$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{H = I^{2} R \, t \; \; [\text{Joules}]}$$

**Step 3 — Physical Interpretation for Welding**

In Resistance Upset Welding, $R$ is dominated by the **contact resistance** $R_c$ at the abutting faces:

$$R_c = \frac{\rho_s}{A_{real}}$$

where $\rho_s$ is the **constriction resistivity** and $A_{real}$ is the *true* (microscopic) metallic contact area — much smaller than the apparent area. Because $A_{real}$ increases as the metal softens, $R_c$ **decreases** with time. Hence, in a more rigorous time-varying form:

$$H = \int_{0}^{t_w} I^{2}(t) \, R_c(t) \, dt$$

**Conclusion:** Heat concentration is *deliberately* engineered by making the interface the highest-resistance zone in the electrical circuit, so that melting/forging happens *only* there.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ) Width Approximation

**Statement:** Derive the approximate HAZ width $b$ on each side of the weld interface after a heating time $t_w$.

**Step 1 — One-Dimensional Heat Conduction Equation**

For semi-infinite solid, the temperature field due to a surface heat source is governed by the diffusion equation:

$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \, \frac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial x^{2}}$$

**Step 2 — Characteristic Diffusion Length**

The fundamental solution of this equation is the error-function profile. The **characteristic penetration depth** of a thermal disturbance after time $t$ scales as:

$$\delta \sim \sqrt{\alpha t}$$

This comes from dimensional analysis: $\alpha$ has units of m²/s, and $t$ has units of s, so $\alpha t$ has units of m². Taking the square root gives a length.

**Step 3 — Define HAZ Width**

The HAZ extends roughly $2 \delta$ across the joint (one $\delta$ on each side):

$$b \approx 2 \sqrt{\alpha \, t_w}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $\alpha$ in Terms of Material Properties**

$$\alpha = \frac{k}{\rho \, c_p}$$

Therefore:

$$b \approx 2 \sqrt{\frac{k \, t_w}{\rho \, c_p}}$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{b \approx 2 \sqrt{\frac{k \, t_w}{\rho \, c_p}} \; \; [\text{metres}]}$$

**Numerical sanity check:** For mild steel, $k \approx 50$ W/m·K, $\rho \approx 7850$ kg/m³, $c_p \approx 490$ J/kg·K, and $t_w = 4$ s:

$$b \approx 2 \sqrt{\frac{50 \times 4}{7850 \times 490}} = 2 \sqrt{\frac{200}{3.846 \times 10^{6}}}$$

$$b \approx 2 \sqrt{5.20 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 2 \times 7.21 \times 10^{-3} \approx 14.4 \text{ mm}$$

This is consistent with practical observations: the HAZ in RUW of mild steel is about **10–20 mm** wide.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Upsetting Pressure and Constraint Factor

**Statement:** Show why the upsetting pressure $P_u$ must be several times the uniaxial yield stress $\sigma_y$ of the material at forging temperature.

**Step 1 — Uniaxial Forging Pressure**

In a simple uniaxial compression test, the pressure required to plastically deform a sample equals its uniaxial yield stress:

$$P_{uniaxial} = \sigma_y(T_{forge})$$

**Step 2 — Triaxial Constraint at the Weld Interface**

At the weld interface, the metal is constrained on **all sides** by cooler, stronger parent material. The plastic zone cannot freely expand sideways because the surrounding material resists. This creates a **triaxial compressive stress state**.

By the **von Mises yield criterion**, the equivalent stress in a triaxial state is:

$$\sigma_{eq} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \sqrt{(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^{2} + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^{2} + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^{2}}$$

For a fully constrained plastic zone (all three principal stresses equal in magnitude), the required hydrostatic component to drive plastic flow increases by a **constraint factor $K_f$**.

**Step 3 — Empirically Determined Constraint Factor**

For typical upset welding, the experimentally observed $K_f$ lies in the range:

$$K_f \approx 2 \text{ to } 4$$

Hence the **practical upsetting pressure** is:

$$P_u = K_f \, \sigma_y(T_{forge})$$

**Step 4 — Result**

$$\boxed{P_u = K_f \, \sigma_y(T_{forge}) \; \; [\text{Pa}]}$$

For mild steel at ~1200 °C, $\sigma_y \approx 20$ MPa, giving $P_u \approx 40$–$80$ MPa — consistent with the typical 35–70 MPa used in industry.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table for Examination

| **Parameter** | **Resistance Upset Welding (RUW)** | **Flash Butt Welding (FBW)** |
|---|---|---|
| Current onset | Before full contact | After arcing begins |
| Initial pressure | Higher (light forging) | Very low (no contact) |
| Heat generation | $I^{2} R t$ (Joule) | Arc + Joule |
| Metal state at interface | Plastic, not molten | Partially molten, vaporized |
| Upset application | Sudden, single-stage | After flash, sudden |
| Oxide expulsion | By upset extrusion | By flashing + upset |
| Energy consumption | Lower (10–30 % less) | Higher |
| Operator skill | Low | Moderate to high |
| Surface prep required | High (clean, parallel) | Moderate |
| Typical marine use | Pipes, bars, chain | Rails, large structural |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Equipment and Setup — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AC Mains Supply 3 Phase 415 V 50 Hz] --> B[Welding Transformer Step Down]
    B --> C[Secondary Busbars Water Cooled]
    C --> D[Moveable Platen with Upper Die Clamp]
    C --> E[Fixed Platen with Lower Die Clamp]
    D --> F[Workpiece A Pipe Bar Section]
    E --> G[Workpiece B Pipe Bar Section]
    F <--> H[Interface Contact Zone Highest Resistance]
    G <--> H
    H --> I[Heat Generation I squared R t]
    I --> J[Forging Temperature Reached 0.85 Tm]
    J --> K[Upsetting Cylinder Hydraulic Activated]
    K --> L[High Force P u Applied 35 to 70 MPa]
    L --> M[Plastic Upset and Flash Extrusion]
    M --> N[Forged Weld Across Full Section]
    N --> O[Flash Removal by Machining or Grinding]
    O --> P[Finished Welded Component Ready for Shipyard Use]

    stepA[Pressure Sensor on Platen] -.-> D
    stepB[Current Sensor on Secondary] -.-> B
    stepC[Temperature Monitoring via Pyrometer] -.-> H
    stepD[PLC Controller Sequence Logic] -.-> B
    stepD -.-> K
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram above shows the **functional flow** of the RUW process from electrical input to finished weld. Each block represents a station in the production sequence; the dashed feedback lines represent sensor/control loops in a modern PLC-controlled upset welding machine.

## 4.2 Process Sequence Diagram (Time-Domain)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T0[T0 Clamp Workpieces Apply Light Forging Pressure] --> T1[T1 Energise Transformer Begin Current Flow]
    T1 --> T2[T2 Interface Resistance Heats Metal to Plastic Range]
    T2 --> T3[T3 Reach Forging Temperature Trigger Upset]
    T3 --> T4[T4 Sudden High Pressure Upset Plastic Metal]
    T4 --> T5[T5 Deenergise Transformer Current OFF]
    T5 --> T6[T6 Hold Upset Pressure for Forging Soak Time]
    T6 --> T7[T7 Release Pressure Open Clamps]
    T7 --> T8[T8 Remove Flash and Inspect Weld]

    style T0 fill:#fff5e6
    style T1 fill:#ffe0b3
    style T2 fill:#ffb380
    style T3 fill:#ff8533
    style T4 fill:#ff6600
    style T5 fill:#ffb380
    style T6 fill:#ffe0b3
    style T7 fill:#fff5e6
    style T8 fill:#e6ffe6
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The colour gradient (cream → orange → red → back) symbolically represents the temperature profile of the workpiece during the cycle. Note that the **upset is triggered the moment the interface reaches forging temperature** — any delay allows the HAZ to grow and reduces weld quality.

## 4.3 Cross-Section View of Joint Before and After Upset

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BEFORE_UPSET
        A1[Workpiece A] --- AX1[Interface Plane Vertical Dashed Line]
        AX1 --- B1[Workpiece B]
        P1[Light Forging Pressure Arrows Downward on Each Side] --> A1
        P1 --> B1
        H1[Heat Zone Very Narrow at Contact] -.- AX1
    end

    subgraph AFTER_UPSET
        A2[Workpiece A Upset and Shortened] --- AX2[Forged Interface]
        AX2 --- B2[Workpiece B Upset and Shortened]
        P2[High Upsetting Pressure 35 to 70 MPa] --> A2
        P2 --> B2
        F1[Flash Ribbon All Around Joint] -.-> AX2
        G1[Recrystallised Fine Grains at Interface] -.- AX2
    end

    BEFORE_UPSET --> AFTER_UPSET
```
