# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Define **Resistance Upset Welding**. State the governing heat-generation equation and explain the role of contact resistance in localizing the heat at the interface.

**Model Answer:**

Resistance Upset Welding (RUW) is a resistance butt-welding process in which a low-voltage, high-current electric supply is passed through the abutting faces of two workpieces held under an initial light forging pressure. As the contact surfaces heat up to the plastic (forging) range — typically $0.85 \, T_m$ — a sudden high upsetting force is applied to forge the joint and expel oxides as flash.

The heat generated is given by **Joule's Law**:

$$H = I^{2} \, R \, t$$

where $I$ is current, $R$ is electrical resistance, and $t$ is heating time.

**Role of contact resistance:** The interface has the **highest electrical resistance** in the entire current path because true metallic contact occurs only at microscopic asperities. This concentrates $I^{2} R$ heating exactly at the joint, producing localized forging heat without significantly heating the bulk of the workpieces. As the metal softens, true contact area grows, $R$ falls, and heating automatically tapers off — a self-limiting feature.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q:** List **four advantages** of Resistance Upset Welding and state two shipbuilding applications.

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages:**

1. **Forged, full-section weld** — the entire cross-section is bonded; no filler metal, no fusion defects.
2. **Short cycle time** (2–10 s) — ideal for high-throughput shipyard production.
3. **Operator-independent** once parameters are set — improves quality consistency.
4. **No shielding gas or flux** required — reduces consumable cost and simplifies the process.
5. **Good fatigue and corrosion resistance** due to fine recrystallised grains at the interface.

**Shipbuilding applications:**

- Longitudinal seam welding of **steel pipes and tubes** for marine piping systems (sea-water cooling, bilge, ballast lines).
- Welding of **anchor shackles, chain links, and bars** for mooring and anchoring systems.
- Production of **stiffener rods and rolled rings** used in hull construction.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q (a) (7 Marks):** With a neat sketch, describe the **principle and procedure of Resistance Upset Welding**. List the main process parameters and their typical ranges.

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:** Resistance Upset Welding uses the heat generated by electrical resistance at the abutting interface of two workpieces to bring the metal to a plastic (forging) state. A high upsetting force is then applied to forge the joint and produce a sound, full-section weld.

**Procedure (Step-by-Step):**

1. **Preparation:** Workpiece ends are cut square, cleaned, and aligned.
2. **Clamping:** Pieces are gripped in water-cooled copper-alloy dies/clamps connected to the welding transformer's secondary.
3. **Forging Pressure Application:** A light initial pressure of 5–15 MPa is applied to ensure uniform contact.
4. **Heating:** Heavy current (5,000–50,000 A at 2–10 V) is passed for 1–5 s, heating the interface to 0.85 $T_m$.
5. **Upsetting:** Once forging temperature is reached, the upsetting force is suddenly applied (35–70 MPa) to extrude the plastic metal as flash and forge the joint.
6. **Hold:** Upset pressure is held briefly (forging soak) to allow grain refinement.
7. **Release:** Current is switched off, pressure released, and the welded component is removed for flash grinding.

**Main Process Parameters and Typical Ranges:**

| **Parameter** | **Range** |
|---|---|
| Welding current | 5,000 – 50,000 A |
| Open-circuit voltage | 2 – 10 V |
| Forging pressure | 5 – 15 MPa |
| Upsetting pressure | 35 – 70 MPa |
| Heating time | 1 – 5 s |
| Total cycle time | 2 – 10 s |
| Forging temperature | 0.8 – 0.9 $T_m$ |
| Upset ratio | 10 – 25 % |

**Neat Sketch:** A schematic of the RUW setup is shown in the **SECTION 4 architecture diagram** above. The key elements are: welding transformer, secondary busbars, clamping dies, workpieces, interface zone, upsetting cylinder, and the flash that extrudes during upset.

---

**Q (b) (7 Marks):** Derive the **heat generated at the interface** during Resistance Upset Welding from first principles, and discuss why **upsetting pressure must be several times the uniaxial yield stress**.

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation of $H = I^{2} R t$:**

By definition, electrical power dissipated in a resistor is:

$$P = V \cdot I$$

Using Ohm's Law ($V = I R$):

$$P = I^{2} R$$

Total heat is the time-integral of power:

$$H = \int_{0}^{t} I^{2} R \, dt$$

Assuming $I$ and $R$ are approximately constant during the short heating interval:

$$H = I^{2} R \int_{0}^{t} dt = I^{2} R t$$

$$\boxed{H = I^{2} R t \; \; \text{Joules}}$$

**Why Upsetting Pressure is Multiplied by Constraint Factor $K_f$:**

In a uniaxial compression test, plastic flow begins when the applied pressure equals the yield stress $\sigma_y(T)$. At the weld interface, however, the plastic zone is **completely surrounded by cooler, stronger parent metal**, which prevents free lateral expansion. This produces a **triaxial state of stress**.

According to the **von Mises yield criterion**, under such constraint, the equivalent stress required to drive plastic flow is higher than in the uniaxial case. Empirically, this is captured by the **constraint factor** $K_f \approx 2$–$4$, giving:

$$P_u = K_f \cdot \sigma_y(T_{forge})$$

For mild steel at 1200 °C, $\sigma_y \approx 20$ MPa, so:

$$P_u \approx 2 \text{ to } 4 \times 20 = 40 \text{ to } 80 \; \text{MPa}$$

This matches the **35–70 MPa** range used in industry. If $P_u$ is too low, the interface is not properly forged, leaving **oxide inclusions, voids, and a weak bond**. If too high, **excessive flash and thinning** result.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q (a) (7 Marks):** Compare **Resistance Upset Welding (RUW)** and **Flash Butt Welding (FBW)** with respect to principle, heat generation, surface preparation, energy consumption, and typical applications in shipbuilding.

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **Resistance Upset Welding** | **Flash Butt Welding** |
|---|---|---|
| **Principle** | Heat by $I^{2} R t$ at the contact; upset in plastic state | Heat by arcing/vaporization; upset after flashing stops |
| **Heat generation mechanism** | Pure Joule heating at interface | Combination of arcing + Joule heating |
| **Initial state of interface** | Light contact, plastic but not molten | Violent vaporization of asperities |
| **Surface preparation** | Very critical — must be clean, square, parallel | Less critical — flashing burns off contaminants |
| **Material loss** | Minimal (small flash ring) | Significant (5–15 % of cross-section vaporized) |
| **Energy consumption** | Lower (10–30 % less) | Higher |
| **Cycle time** | Shorter (2–10 s) | Longer (flashing takes 5–30 s) |
| **Operator skill needed** | Low | Moderate to high |
| **Weld quality** | Forged, fine-grained, no fusion defects | Good, buttrusion of melt and possibility of inclusions |
| **Shipbuilding applications** | Pipes, tubes, chain links, anchor shackles | Rails, large rolled sections, heavy hull plates |

**Conclusion:** For **small-to-medium cross-sections and mass production** (e.g., ship piping), RUW is preferred for its energy efficiency, simplicity, and repeatability. For **large, irregular cross-sections** (e.g., shipbuilding rails, plates), FBW is preferred because flashing accommodates imperfect surface preparation.

---

**Q (b) (7 Marks):** A shipyard uses **Resistance Upset Welding** to join two mild-steel pipe sections, each of cross-sectional area $A_0 = 2000$ mm². The interface contact resistance is $R = 80 \, \mu\Omega$, the welding current is $I = 12,000$ A, and the heating time is $t = 3$ s. The material has $\sigma_y$ at forging temperature = 22 MPa, and the constraint factor is $K_f = 3$. Calculate:

**(i)** The total heat generated at the interface.

**(ii)** The required upsetting pressure.

**(iii)** The upset length $\Delta L$ if the upset ratio is 15 % and the original gauge length between clamps is $L_0 = 100$ mm.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Heat generated:**

$$H = I^{2} \, R \, t$$

$$H = (12{,}000)^{2} \times 80 \times 10^{-6} \times 3$$

$$H = 1.44 \times 10^{8} \times 80 \times 10^{-6} \times 3$$

$$H = 1.44 \times 10^{8} \times 2.4 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$H = 34{,}560 \; \text{J} = 34.56 \; \text{kJ}$$

$$\boxed{H \approx 34.6 \; \text{kJ}}$$

**(ii) Upsetting pressure:**

$$P_u = K_f \times \sigma_y(T_{forge}) = 3 \times 22 = 66 \; \text{MPa}$$

$$\boxed{P_u = 66 \; \text{MPa}}$$

This lies comfortably inside the typical industrial range of 35–70 MPa.

**(iii) Upset length:**

Upset ratio = 15 % = 0.15.

$$\Delta L = 0.15 \times L_0 = 0.15 \times 100 = 15 \; \text{mm}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta L = 15 \; \text{mm}}$$

The corresponding **flash volume**:

$$V_{flash} = A_0 \times \Delta L = 2000 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.015 = 3.0 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m}^{3} = 30 \; \text{cm}^{3}$$

This flash will be removed by post-weld machining.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in RUW Questions:**
> 1. **Writing $H = IRt$ or $H = IVt$** instead of $H = I^{2} R t$. The square on the current is the most common slip — KTU examiners specifically look for $I^{2}$.
> 2. **Confusing RUW with Flash Butt Welding** in comparison answers. Remember: RUW has *no flashing* stage — current is passed *before* significant vaporization, and upset is applied in a single stroke.
> 3. **Forgetting the constraint factor $K_f$** when calculating upsetting pressure. Writing $P_u = \sigma_y$ is incomplete; the triaxial constraint at the interface raises the required pressure by a factor of 2–4.
> 4. **Stating that the metal melts** in RUW. Wrong — the interface reaches the *plastic forging range* (0.8–0.9 $T_m$), **not** the melting point. This is a defining feature distinguishing it from fusion welding.
> 5. **Mixing up forging and upsetting pressures.** Forging pressure (initial) is 5–15 MPa; upsetting pressure is 35–70 MPa. The two are different stages.
> 6. **Not mentioning flash removal** as a post-weld step. Examiners often award a 1-mark point for "removal of flash by machining/grinding."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Resistance Upset Welding:**

- **Definition:** Resistance butt-welding process; current heats the interface to plastic state; high upset force forges the joint.
- **Heat equation:** $H = I^{2} R t$ (Joule's Law). The square on $I$ is **mandatory**.
- **Interface temperature:** $0.8$ to $0.9 \, T_m$ (plastic, **not molten**).
- **Forging pressure:** $5$–$15$ MPa (light, initial).
- **Upsetting pressure:** $35$–$70$ MPa (heavy, applied suddenly).
- **Current range:** $5{,}000$–$50{,}000$ A at $2$–$10$ V.
- **Cycle time:** $2$–$10$ seconds.
- **Upset ratio:** $10$–$25$ %.
- **HAZ width:** $b \approx 2 \sqrt{\alpha t_w}$, where $\alpha = k / (\rho c_p)$.
- **Constraint factor:** $K_f \approx 2$–$4$, so $P_u = K_f \sigma_y(T_{forge})$.
- **Flash:** Plastic metal extruded sideways during upset; must be removed afterwards.
- **vs Flash Butt Welding:** RUW has no flashing stage; lower energy; better for clean, small cross-sections.
- **Shipbuilding uses:** pipes, tubes, chain links, anchor shackles, stiffener rods.
- **Key advantage:** Forged full-section weld, no filler metal, fine-grained joint, repeatable, fast.
- **Key limitation:** Requires clean, square-cut, parallel surfaces; not suitable for very large or irregular sections.
- **Energy savings:** $10$–$30$ % less than FBW.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Hook — "FUSE before FORCE" is wrong in RUW.** The right sequence is **HEAT (to plastic) → FORCE (upset) → FORGE (soak) → FINISH (flash removal)**. RUW is a *forging-assisted* joining process, not a fusion process.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Module:** Module 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Flash Butt Welding (FBW)**, also referred to as **Flash Welding**, is a variant of the **resistance welding** family wherein two abutting metallic workpieces are joined through a precisely controlled sequence of electrical resistance heating, localized fusion, and **forging pressure application**. The defining characteristic of this process is the deliberate ejection — or *flashing* — of superheated, oxidized, and contaminated molten metal from the joint interface as an electric current is passed across the gap while the parts are progressively advanced toward one another under a controlled upsetting force.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme – OENST831 (Joining Techniques in Shipbuilding)**, Flash Butt Welding is positioned as a critical **mass-production joining method** used extensively for longitudinal and circumferential seam welding of plates, structural sections, and rolled profiles used in **hull fabrication, deck plating, bulkhead stiffeners, and pressure vessel components** of marine vessels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Flash Butt Welding belongs to the broader classification of **Resistance Welding Processes** (per ISO 4063:2009 — process code **45**). It is a *self-contained, mechanized, high-integrity joining process* capable of producing welds across the **full cross-section of a component in a single operation**, which distinguishes it from arc-based or fusion-based processes.

The process is governed by the principles established in standards such as:
- **AWS C1.1M/C1.1:2019** – Recommended Practices for Resistance Welding
- **ISO 15609-1** – Specification and qualification of welding procedures
- **ISO 4063** – Welding and allied processes — Nomenclature

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Explanation

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy for First-Time Learners:**
> Imagine you are holding two ends of a candle under a flame. As the wax melts at the contact line, a small puddle of molten wax forms and drips away. Now, if you press the two softened ends together firmly, they fuse into a single continuous candle. **Flash Butt Welding works on exactly this principle**, except with metals, electricity instead of a candle flame, and mechanical force instead of your hand.

A more practical analogy is the action of **"cutting wire with electricity"** — as current passes through a thin bridge of metal, intense Joule heating causes it to melt and vaporize, while mechanical pressure squeezes the softened ends together to form a homogeneous joint.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics are critical to Flash Butt Welding:

- **Secondary (Welding) Voltage:** Typically **4 V to 12 V** (open-circuit voltage up to ~20 V)
- **Welding Current:** **5 kA to 200 kA** depending on the cross-sectional area being welded
- **Upset (Forging) Force:** **10 MPa to 400 MPa** of projected cross-sectional area
- **Flash Velocity:** **5 mm/s to 25 mm/s** (platen approach velocity during flashing)
- **Upset Distance:** **5 mm to 25 mm**
- **Material Loss (Flash Loss):** Typically **10% to 30%** of the cross-sectional length, depending on material and parameter selection
- **Power Consumption:** Approximately **0.15 kWh to 0.40 kWh per kg** of welded material
- **Joint Efficiency:** Often **95% to 100%** of parent metal strength

## 1.4 Why Flash Butt Welding is Critical in Shipbuilding

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Context Insight:** In modern shipyards (especially those following **Korea/KPI/Japan shipbuilding standards**), Flash Butt Welding lines can produce **plate panels up to 12 m wide × 35 m long × 6 to 80 mm thick** with single-pass welds that rival or exceed the strength of the base plate. This is unmatched by any arc-welding process in terms of productivity per unit length.

The unique advantage in shipbuilding lies in:
1. **Full cross-section welding in a single operation** — no multipass buildup
2. **Minimal heat-affected zone (HAZ)** distortion compared to arc processes
3. **Exceptional throughput** — multiple meters per minute versus 0.3–1.0 m/min for SAW
4. **Ability to weld dissimilar materials** when carefully controlled
5. **Compliance with Class Society rules** (DNV, LR, ABS, BV) for high-strength hull steel (AH36, DH36, EH36, FH36)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle

Flash Butt Welding is governed by three concurrent physical phenomena:

1. **Electrical Resistance Heating (Joule Effect)** — Current $I$ flowing through the interface generates heat $Q$.
2. **Localized Fusion and Flashing** — Molten metal at the contact points vaporizes and is explosively ejected.
3. **Upsetting (Forging) Action** — A high axial force consolidates the softened, but still mostly solid, ends into a sound metallurgical bond.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Other Resistance Welding Processes:**
> - **Spot Welding (RSW):** Uses electrodes to clamp sheets; localized nugget formation
> - **Projection Welding (RPW):** Uses embossed projections to localize current
> - **Seam Welding (RSEW):** Uses roller electrodes for continuous leak-tight joints
> - **Flash Butt Welding (RBW):** No external electrodes; parts themselves carry current

## 2.2 Detailed Sequence of Operations

### Stage 1: Setup and Alignment
The two workpieces (e.g., steel plates) are clamped in **movable (flashing platen)** and **stationary dies** of an FBW machine. The surfaces are aligned with a typical gap of **2 mm to 10 mm** depending on plate thickness. Hydraulic or pneumatic clamping forces of **10 MPa to 30 MPa** ensure electrical contact away from the joint.

### Stage 2: Preheating (Optional)
For heavy sections, a brief **preheat current pulse** (low voltage, low force) warms the parts uniformly to reduce thermal shock and prevent cracking in high-carbon or alloy steels. Preheat duration typically **1 s to 5 s**.

### Stage 3: Flashing Stage
Voltage is applied across the gap. Due to surface roughness (asperities), initial contact occurs at microscopic points carrying enormous current density — instantly melting and vaporizing. This produces the characteristic **"flash"** — a shower of incandescent metal sparks and oxide particles.

As flashing progresses, the platen advances at a controlled rate (**flash velocity**), maintaining a constant arc gap of **1 mm to 3 mm**. The molten bridge maintains a stable electrical resistance, and heating extends inward into the parent material.

### Stage 4: Upsetting Stage
At the **flash-off moment**, the current is abruptly cut, and a high **upset force** is instantly applied. The still-plastic (but mostly solid) metal at the interface is forged together, displacing the remaining molten and oxidized material laterally as **upset metal** (forming the characteristic **fin or burr** around the weld).

### Stage 5: Holding and Release
The upset force is maintained for a brief **hold time** (typically **0.5 s to 2 s**) to allow grain recrystallization and solidification under pressure. The clamps release, and the welded assembly is removed for flash trimming.

## 2.3 Heat Generation Theory

The heat generated at the flashing interface is described by **Joule's Law**:

$$Q = I^2 \cdot R \cdot t$$

Where:
- $Q$ = heat energy (Joules)
- $I$ = welding current (Amperes)
- $R$ = interface resistance (Ohms)
- $t$ = time of current flow (seconds)

In flash butt welding, the resistance $R$ is **not static** — it varies with time as the interface temperature rises and the contact area increases. The total resistance comprises:

$$R_{total} = R_{interface} + R_{bulk}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> During steady flashing, the molten bridge acts as a **constant-voltage arc-like plasma**, with resistance dropping as the temperature rises. This is why flash butt welding is often modeled as a **constant-power process** rather than a constant-resistance one.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Parameter** | **Expression** | **Typical Range / Unit** | **Engineering Significance** |
|------------------------|---------------|--------------------------|------------------------------|
| Joule's Heat Law | $Q = I^2 R t$ | $Q$ in J | Heat generated at interface |
| Power Input | $P = I^2 R = V \cdot I$ | kW | Determines weld penetration |
| Heat Balance | $Q_{gen} = Q_{abs} + Q_{loss}$ | Energy balance | Heat distribution analysis |
| Upset Pressure | $p_u = F_u / A$ | $10$–$400$ MPa | Forging pressure at weld |
| Flash-off Velocity | $v_f = \Delta x / \Delta t$ | $5$–$25$ mm/s | Platen approach rate |
| Material Loss | $\Delta L_{loss} = L_i - L_f$ | $10$–$30$ % of length | Flash and upset material ejected |
| Joint Efficiency | $\eta = \sigma_w / \sigma_b$ | $0.95$–$1.0$ | Strength ratio (weld/base) |
| Specific Energy | $E_s = P \cdot t / V_{weld}$ | $0.15$–$0.40$ kWh/kg | Energy per unit weld mass |
| Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{th} = Q_{joint} / Q_{total}$ | $0.6$–$0.85$ | Fraction of heat in joint zone |
| Power Factor | $PF = \cos \phi$ | $0.7$–$0.9$ lagging | Electrical efficiency indicator |
| Electrode Contact Resistance | $R_c = \rho / (2 a)$ where $a$ = contact radius | $\mu\Omega$ | Microscopic contact theory |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** In numerical problems, students are often asked to calculate **power consumption**, **heat input per unit length**, or **upset force for a given plate cross-section**. Always carry units and state assumptions clearly.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Flash Butt Welding is the backbone of:
- **Shipbuilding** — longitudinal and circumferential plate panel fabrication
- **Railway rolling stock** — rail-end welding (Thermite and FBW hybrids)
- **Automotive industry** — drive shafts, ring gears, wheel rims
- **Aerospace** — landing gear components, ring-rolled parts
- **Pipe and tube manufacturing** — spiral and longitudinal pipe seam welding
- **Pressure vessel fabrication** — shell course joining
- **Structural steelwork** — I-beam and channel fabrication lines
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Upset Force Requirement

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
Determine the minimum upset force $F_u$ required to forge-weld two mild steel plates each of width $w = 1500$ mm and thickness $t_p = 12$ mm, given that the upset pressure for low-carbon steel in flash butt welding is $p_u = 150$ MPa.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the cross-sectional area to be upset:**

$$A = w \times t_p = 1500 \times 12 = 18000 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — State the upset pressure from the design table:**

$$p_u = 150 \text{ MPa} = 150 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Apply the upset force relation:**

$$F_u = p_u \cdot A$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$F_u = 150 \text{ N/mm}^2 \times 18000 \text{ mm}^2 = 2{,}700{,}000 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 — Express the result in engineering units:**

$$F_u = 2.7 \times 10^6 \text{ N} = 2700 \text{ kN} = 2.7 \text{ MN}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** We converted $w$ from mm to itself (no change needed since both $w$ and $t_p$ are in mm), and $p_u$ from MPa to N/mm² using the equivalence $1 \text{ MPa} = 1 \text{ N/mm}^2$. This avoids the need for SI unit conversions.

## 3.2 Derivation of Heat Input and Power Consumption

**Problem Statement:**
For a flash butt welding operation on a 20 mm thick × 2000 mm wide steel plate, the welding current is $I = 80$ kA, the secondary voltage is $V = 9$ V, and the flash duration is $t_f = 12$ s. Calculate the **total heat input**, **power input**, and **specific energy per unit length** of weld.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Calculate the electrical power input:**

$$P = V \cdot I = 9 \text{ V} \times 80{,}000 \text{ A} = 720{,}000 \text{ W} = 720 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the total heat generated:**

$$Q = P \cdot t_f = 720 \text{ kW} \times 12 \text{ s} = 8640 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the welded cross-sectional area:**

$$A = w \cdot t_p = 2000 \times 20 = 40{,}000 \text{ mm}^2 = 0.04 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Assume an effective weld length for heat-per-unit-length:**

For FBW, the **effective heating length** is typically 1.5× the plate thickness due to heat conduction effects:

$$L_{eff} = 1.5 \times t_p = 1.5 \times 20 \text{ mm} = 30 \text{ mm} = 0.03 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate the specific energy per unit length:**

$$E_L = Q / L_{eff} = 8640 \text{ kJ} / 0.03 \text{ m} = 288{,}000 \text{ kJ/m} = 288 \text{ MJ/m}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Although 288 MJ/m appears enormous compared to arc welding (typically 1–5 MJ/m), this is because flash butt welding is a **bulk-resistance process** where the entire cross-section is heated, not just a localized arc puddle. The energy per unit *volume* is actually comparable.

## 3.3 Derivation of Joint Efficiency

**Problem Statement:**
A flash butt welded joint in EH36 shipbuilding steel is tested in tension. The ultimate tensile strength of the weld zone is measured at $\sigma_w = 485$ MPa, while the parent metal strength is $\sigma_b = 510$ MPa. Calculate the joint efficiency.

**Step 1 — State the joint efficiency definition:**

$$\eta = \frac{\sigma_w}{\sigma_b}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$\eta = \frac{485 \text{ MPa}}{510 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate:**

$$\eta = 0.9509 \approx 95.1\%$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

> [!TIP]
> Joint efficiencies above **95%** qualify the joint for **Class Society full-strength classification** (DNV, ABS, LR, BV). A $\eta \geq 1.0$ would indicate that the weld zone is *stronger* than the parent metal — common in flash butt welding of mild steels due to grain refinement at the upset zone.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Process Parameters vs. Weld Quality

| **Parameter** | **Effect if Increased** | **Effect if Decreased** | **Optimum Range (Mild Steel)** |
|--------------|------------------------|--------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Flashing Current | Deeper penetration, faster heating | Insufficient melting, cold joint | $5$–$200$ kA |
| Flash Velocity | Excessive flash loss, undercut | Slow heating, oxide entrapment | $5$–$25$ mm/s |
| Upset Force | Better consolidation, denser weld | Porosity, lack of fusion | $100$–$200$ MPa |
| Upset Distance | Removes more flash, but wastes metal | Residual flash, oxide inclusion | $5$–$15$ mm |
| Clamping Force | Lower contact resistance | Slippage, arcing at clamps | $10$–$30$ MPa |
| Flash-off Time | Higher productivity, more flash loss | Cold joint, poor fusion | $5$–$30$ s |
| Platen Gap | More arc stability | Bridging, premature melting | $2$–$10$ mm |
| Preheat Energy | Reduces thermal gradient | Cracking in HSLA steels | $0$–$10$ kJ |

## 3.5 Validation Check — Energy Audit

For an industrial flash butt welding line producing 1000 m of welded plate per shift:
- Plate: $20$ mm × $2000$ mm × $12000$ mm long
- Energy per weld: $8640$ kJ (from Section 3.2)
- Total daily energy: $1000 \times 8640 = 8.64 \times 10^6$ kJ = $2400$ kWh

Compare to **submerged arc welding (SAW)** of the same length:
- Heat input $\approx 3$ kJ/mm $\Rightarrow 3000$ kJ/m
- Daily energy: $1000 \times 3000 = 3 \times 10^6$ kJ = $833$ kWh

> [!NOTE]
> Flash butt welding consumes **more total energy** but achieves a **full-strength weld in a single operation**, eliminating the need for multiple SAW passes (which would multiply the SAW energy by 3–5× in practice). The net productivity gain is typically **5× to 10×**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define Flash Butt Welding and explain the role of the "flashing stage" in the process.**

**Model Answer:**

Flash Butt Welding (FBW) is a resistance welding process in which two abutting workpieces are joined by passing a high electric current across the joint while advancing them together, causing localized melting and ejection of molten metal called "flash," followed by application of an upset forging force.

The flashing stage plays **three critical roles**:
1. **Heat generation** — establishes the welding temperature across the interface through Joule heating of the molten bridge.
2. **Surface cleaning** — ejects oxides, contaminants, and surface impurities from the joint area.
3. **Material softening** — preheats the bulk material to a plastic state suitable for forging consolidation.

(3 marks — Definition 1, Three roles 2)

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List four advantages and two limitations of Flash Butt Welding as applied in shipbuilding.**

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages:**
1. Single-pass full cross-section weld — high productivity
2. Joint efficiency up to 95–100% of parent metal
3. Minimal distortion due to balanced heating
4. Suitable for dissimilar and large cross-sections (plates, bars, rails)
5. Eliminates filler metal, flux, and shielding gas

**Limitations:**
1. Significant flash material loss (10–30% of cross-section length)
2. Requires precise alignment and heavy-duty machinery
3. Limited to simple butt-joint geometries (no lap or T-joints)

(3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Option 1): [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **working principle of Flash Butt Welding**. Describe the sequence of operations in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A flash butt welding machine is used to weld two mild steel plates of width $w = 1200$ mm and thickness $t_p = 16$ mm. The welding current is $I = 75$ kA, secondary voltage $V = 10$ V, and the flashing duration is $t_f = 14$ s. Determine: **(i)** total heat input, **(ii)** specific energy per unit length of weld (assume effective heated length = $1.5 \times t_p$), and **(iii)** minimum upset force if upset pressure is $p_u = 140$ MPa. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**(a) Working Principle and Sequence (7 marks):**

The principle involves passing a high electric current through two abutting workpieces held under light pressure. Localized melting occurs at contact asperities, and the molten metal is ejected as flash. After sufficient flashing, a high upset force forges the softened ends together.

**Sequence of operations:**
1. **Loading and clamping** — workpieces are placed between stationary and movable platens and clamped hydraulically.
2. **Alignment** — ends are squared and aligned with a controlled gap (2–10 mm).
3. **Preheating (optional)** — low-voltage current pulse for high-carbon steels.
4. **Flashing** — full voltage applied; current flows across arc gap; platen advances at controlled velocity (5–25 mm/s); molten metal ejects as spark shower.
5. **Flash-off detection** — sensors detect endpoint based on platen travel or current drop.
6. **Upsetting** — current cut; high axial force (100–400 MPa) applied within 50–100 ms.
7. **Holding** — upset force held for 0.5–2 s to complete recrystallization.
8. **Release and trimming** — clamps release; external flash burr is trimmed by shear or grinder.

(7 marks — sequence clearly explained with diagram reference)

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Total heat input:**

$$P = V \cdot I = 10 \text{ V} \times 75{,}000 \text{ A} = 750{,}000 \text{ W} = 750 \text{ kW}$$

$$Q = P \cdot t_f = 750 \text{ kW} \times 14 \text{ s} = 10{,}500 \text{ kJ}$$

**(ii) Specific energy per unit length:**

Effective heated length: $L_{eff} = 1.5 \times 16 = 24$ mm $= 0.024$ m

$$E_L = Q / L_{eff} = 10{,}500 \text{ kJ} / 0.024 \text{ m} = 437{,}500 \text{ kJ/m} \approx 437.5 \text{ MJ/m}$$

**(iii) Minimum upset force:**

$$A = w \cdot t_p = 1200 \times 16 = 19{,}200 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$F_u = p_u \cdot A = 140 \text{ N/mm}^2 \times 19{,}200 \text{ mm}^2 = 2{,}688{,}000 \text{ N} = 2688 \text{ kN}$$

(7 marks — 2 + 2 + 3)

---

### Question B (Option 2): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Discuss the **equipment and subsystems of a modern flash butt welding machine** used in shipbuilding plate panel production lines. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Flash Butt Welding with Submerged Arc Welding (SAW)** for ship hull plate joining based on **productivity, joint quality, distortion, energy consumption, and applicability**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**(a) Equipment and Subsystems (7 marks):**

A modern FBW machine for shipbuilding consists of the following subsystems:

1. **Primary power supply** — three-phase AC mains at 415 V/11 kV feeding the welding transformer through thyristor-controlled rectifiers.
2. **Welding transformer** — step-down transformer producing secondary voltage of 4–20 V at currents up to 200 kA.
3. **Secondary circuit** — water-cooled flexible copper bus bars delivering current to the platens.
4. **Movable (flashing) platen** — hydraulically actuated, with copper-alloy jaws for clamping the workpiece; capable of 50–300 mm stroke.
5. **Stationary platen** — fixed-position counterpart with similar clamping arrangement.
6. **Hydraulic system** — supplies upset force up to several MN through high-pressure cylinders with servo-controlled valves.
7. **Flashing control system** — PLC-based, with linear encoders, current sensors, and voltage sensors for closed-loop control of flash velocity.
8. **Cooling system** — recirculated deionized water for transformer, thyristors, and platen jaws.
9. **Operator interface (HMI)** — touchscreen panel for parameter setting, weld logging, and diagnostics.
10. **Flash trimmer** — integrated shear or milling cutter to remove the upset burr.

(7 marks — each subsystem 0.5–1 mark with explanation)

**(b) Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| **Criterion** | **Flash Butt Welding** | **Submerged Arc Welding** |
|---------------|------------------------|---------------------------|
| Productivity | $5$–$15$ m/min | $0.3$–$1.0$ m/min |
| Joint type | Full cross-section butt | Multipass buildup |
| Joint efficiency | $95$–$100\%$ | $90$–$98\%$ |
| Distortion | Low (symmetric heating) | Moderate to high |
| Energy per kg | $0.15$–$0.40$ kWh/kg | $2$–$4$ kWh/kg |
| Material loss | $10$–$30\%$ (flash) | Negligible |
| Filler metal | Not required | Required (wire + flux) |
| Plate thickness range | $3$–$100$ mm | $6$–$50$ mm (multipass) |
| Capital cost | Very high (₹ several crore) | Moderate |
| Labor skill | Low (automated) | High (skilled welder) |
| Joint geometry | Linear butt only | Butt, lap, T, fillet |
| Class society approval | Yes (DNV, LR, ABS, BV) | Yes |
| Best application | Hull plate panels, structural sections | General fabrication, repair |

(7 marks — table 5 marks, inference/discussion 2 marks)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Flash Butt Welding with Spot Welding** — always emphasize that FBW has no external electrodes; current passes directly through the workpieces.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units** — $1 \text{ MPa} = 1 \text{ N/mm}^2$ is critical for upset force calculations.
> 3. **Missing the role of the upset stage** — examiners specifically look for understanding that upsetting happens *after* current cutoff, not during flashing.
> 4. **Ignoring flash material loss** — must be mentioned in any cost/weight analysis of FBW.
> 5. **Neglecting Class Society standards** — shipbuilding context requires reference to DNV, LR, ABS, BV, or IRS rules.
> 6. **Improper sketch** — when asked to "sketch," students must include platen, dies, hydraulic cylinder, transformer, and current direction arrows.
> 7. **Wrong assumption of constant resistance** — the resistance in flash welding is dynamic, not static.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Flash Butt Welding**

**Core Concept:**
- FBW is a **resistance welding** process where current passes across abutting workpieces, melting and ejecting flash, then forging the softened ends.

**Process Stages:**
1. Loading & Clamping
2. Alignment
3. (Optional) Preheat
4. Flashing (controlled platen advance under current)
5. Upsetting (force applied after current cut)
6. Holding
7. Release & Trimming

**Key Parameters (Mild Steel):**
- Voltage: $4$–$12$ V
- Current: $5$–$200$ kA
- Upset pressure: $100$–$200$ MPa
- Flash velocity: $5$–$25$ mm/s

**Key Formulas:**
- Heat: $Q = I^2 R t = V I t$
- Upset force: $F_u = p_u \cdot A$
- Joint efficiency: $\eta = \sigma_w / \sigma_b$

**Shipbuilding Significance:**
- Full cross-section weld in single pass
- Productivity $5$–$15$ m/min
- Used for hull plate panels, structural sections
- Approved by DNV, LR, ABS, BV, IRS

**Critical Differences:**
- vs. Spot Welding: no external electrodes
- vs. Projection Welding: no embossed projections
- vs. Seam Welding: uses rollers, not butt joint
- vs. Arc Welding (SAW): no filler, no arc, no shielding gas

**Typical Material Loss:**
- Flash loss = $10$–$30\%$ of cross-section length
- Accounts for extruded burr material

**Joint Quality:**
- $95$–$100\%$ parent metal strength
- HAZ narrower than arc welds
- Class Society full-strength acceptance

**Equipment Subsystems:**
- Welding transformer
- Thyristor-controlled rectifier
- Secondary bus bars
- Hydraulic platens (movable + stationary)
- PLC-based controller
- HMI panel
- Cooling system
- Flash trimmer

**Standards:**
- ISO 4063 (process code 45)
- AWS C1.1M/C1.1:2019
- ISO 15609-1
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Percussion Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST831 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Percussion Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)**
> **Percussion Welding** is a *resistance welding* process in which coalescence of the faying surfaces is produced by the heat obtained from an **electric arc** that is struck by the rapid percussive (hammer-like) movement of one workpiece against another, immediately followed by the application of **forging pressure** to complete the joint. The arc is sustained only for a fraction of a second (typically **5 to 100 milliseconds**), making it one of the fastest fusion-joining processes used in shipbuilding auxiliary applications.

---

## 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Striking Two Rocks to Make Fire, Then Pressing Them Together**
> Imagine you have two pieces of flint and steel. You strike them together rapidly — the friction and impact briefly generate a hot spark (the **arc**). The moment the spark appears, you slam the two pieces together under pressure so the molten tips fuse and the joint solidifies as one solid mass. That, in essence, is **percussion welding** — but instead of sparks, we use a controlled electric arc discharged from a **capacitor bank**, and instead of rocks, we use carefully aligned metal workpieces mounted in spring-loaded or pneumatically actuated dies.

**What makes it "percussion"?**
- The word *percussion* comes from the Latin *percutere* (to strike).
- The hallmark of the process is the **high-velocity impact** of the moving part against the stationary part, **not** a sustained, slow burn like in conventional arc welding.

---

## 1.2 Position of Percussion Welding in the KTU Classification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hierarchical Classification (KTU Module 2 Reference Frame):**
> 1. **Joining Techniques** (umbrella)
>    - *Mechanical Joining* (riveting, bolting)
>    - *Adhesive Bonding*
>    - *Welding Processes*
>      - **Fusion Welding** → Shielded Metal Arc, Gas Metal Arc, etc.
>      - **Resistance Welding** → Spot, Seam, Projection, **Percussion**, Flash, Upset
>      - **Solid-State Welding** → Friction, Friction Stir, Ultrasonic, Diffusion

Percussion welding sits in the **Resistance Welding** family, alongside **flash butt welding** (its closest relative), but is distinguished by the **speed of arc establishment** and the **extremely short arc duration**.

---

## 1.3 Salient Engineering Metrics (KTU-Highlighted Bold Values)

- **Arc Duration:** **5 – 100 ms** (milliseconds)
- **Current:** **5,000 – 25,000 A** (very high, but ultra-short)
- **Voltage across arc:** **4 – 18 V**
- **Forging Pressure:** **35 – 140 MPa** applied almost instantly after arc extinction
- **Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ):** Practically negligible — typically **< 0.5 mm**
- **Energy Source:** Stored electrical energy, usually **capacitor bank** or **magnetic discharge (inductive)**
- **Distortion:** Among the lowest of all arc-based welding processes

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — The Five Sequential Phases

> [!NOTE]
> **Why five phases?** KTU examiners consistently test the **sequence of events** in percussion welding. Memorizing the order of phases is essential for both 3-mark short questions and 14-mark descriptive questions.

**Phase 1 — Alignment & Clamping**
The two workpieces are mechanically clamped: one in a **fixed holder**, the other in a **movable holder** that is spring-loaded or pneumatically biased toward the fixed piece. The gap between them is preset, typically **0.5 to 3.0 mm**.

**Phase 2 — Stored Energy Charging**
A bank of capacitors (or an inductor in the magnetic variant) is charged from a DC source to a preset voltage. This stores electrical energy $E$ in the capacitor.

**Phase 3 — Arc Initiation (Percussion)**
A trigger signal releases the stored energy and simultaneously releases the moving workpiece. The moving part accelerates toward the stationary part at high velocity (often **> 5 m/s**). As the gap collapses, an **electric arc** is struck at the moment of near-contact. The arc melts a thin film of metal on both faying surfaces.

**Phase 4 — Upset / Forging Stroke**
The momentum of the moving part carries it past the arc zone and brings the molten surfaces into intimate contact. Simultaneously, an **upset force** is applied, expelling molten metal (flash) and forging the joint.

**Phase 5 — Solidification & Cool-Down**
The joint cools under pressure. Because the arc duration is so short, the bulk of each workpiece remains near ambient temperature. The solidified weld is essentially a forged solid-state bond with a thin cast weld nugget at the interface.

---

## 2.2 Why "Percussion" and Not "Flash" Welding? — The Crucial Distinction

| Feature | Percussion Welding | Flash Butt Welding |
|---|---|---|
| Arc duration | **5 – 100 ms** (very short) | 1 – 10 s (long) |
| Energy storage | **Capacitor / Inductor** bank | Continuous transformer supply |
| Burn-off (metal loss) | **Negligible** | Substantial (5 – 25 mm) |
| Surface preparation | Critical — must be flat & clean | Less critical |
| Typical application | **Small parts, dissimilar metals, wires** | Long rails, pipes, structural sections |
| Heat input | Very low | Moderate to high |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Watch-Out:** When asked to "distinguish between percussion and flash welding," many students write the same definitions for both. The **single highest-weightage distinction** is *energy source and arc duration*. Always lead your answer with that.

---

## 2.3 Types of Percussion Welding

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently include a 3-mark or 7-mark question on the **types** of percussion welding. The classification is based on the **energy storage mechanism**.

### 2.3.1 Capacitor-Discharge Percussion Welding (Most Common)
- Energy stored in **electrolytic capacitors**.
- Discharge is via an **ignitron** or **thyristor (SCR)** switch.
- Arc is struck almost instantaneously (sub-millisecond rise time).
- Ideal for **small parts, electronics, thermocouples**.

### 2.3.2 Magnetic-Discharge (Inductive) Percussion Welding
- Energy stored in a **saturable reactor / inductor**.
- The arc is initiated by the rapid collapse of a magnetic field.
- Slightly slower current rise than capacitor type but capable of delivering higher peak currents.
- Used for **larger cross-sections, e.g., tool shanks to carbon-steel bodies**.

### 2.3.3 Electro-Magnetic Percussion Welding (Variety)
- Uses **Lorentz force** between the magnetic field and the discharge current to fling the moving part.
- Common in **battery-tab welding** and **microelectronic packaging**.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All quantities follow SI units unless explicitly stated. Memorize these equations — they appear verbatim in KTU question papers for at least one 7-mark sub-question every examination cycle.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{stored}} &= \tfrac{1}{2}\, C\, V^{2} \quad &\text{[Joules]} \\
E_{\text{stored}} &= \tfrac{1}{2}\, L\, I^{2} \quad &\text{[Joules, magnetic variant]} \\
t_{\text{arc}} &= \dfrac{\pi}{2}\sqrt{L\,C} \quad &\text{[seconds, LC discharge period]} \\
P_{\text{peak}} &= \dfrac{V^{2}}{R_{\text{load}}}\cdot e^{-1} \quad &\text{[Watts, peak power at R = Z]} \\
v_{\text{impact}} &= \sqrt{\dfrac{2\,k\,x}{m}} \quad &\text{[m/s, spring-loaded striker]} \\
F_{\text{forging}} &= k_{\text{spring}}\,x_{\text{compression}} \quad &\text{[Newtons]} \\
\Delta T_{\text{interface}} &= \dfrac{I^{2}R_{\text{c}}\,\Delta t}{m_{\text{interface}}\,c_{p}} \quad &\text{[Kelvin rise at interface]} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $C$ | Capacitance of energy bank | $50 \times 10^{3}$ – $2000 \times 10^{3}$ | $\mu$F |
| $V$ | Charging voltage | $50$ – $400$ | V |
| $L$ | Inductance | $10$ – $500$ | $\mu$H |
| $I$ | Peak discharge current | $5{,}000$ – $25{,}000$ | A |
| $t_{\text{arc}}$ | Arc duration | $5 \times 10^{-3}$ – $100 \times 10^{-3}$ | s |
| $R_{\text{c}}$ | Contact resistance | $50$ – $500$ | $\mu\Omega$ |
| $F_{\text{forging}}$ | Forging upset force | $1$ – $50$ | kN |
| $m$ | Movable part mass | $0.05$ – $5$ | kg |
| $v_{\text{impact}}$ | Impact velocity | $5$ – $20$ | m/s |

> [!WARNING]
> **Vertical Pipe Symbol Ban:** In the row above, *do not* use `|C|` or `|x|` notation inside any KTU-formatted table — use `\vert C \vert` or `\mid x \mid` in LaTeX contexts to avoid breaking table syntax. Examiners have reported rendering errors in submitted answer sheets when students copy-pasted broken tables.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Shipbuilding:** Percussion welding is used in the **electrical grounding strap joints**, **battery-pack assembly** for emergency lighting, **thermocouple junctions** in engine-room instrumentation, and **small-bore instrument-line fittings** where heat-sensitive components surround the joint.
- **Aerospace & Defence:** Joining of dissimilar metals (e.g., **titanium to stainless steel**) for transducer housings.
- **Tool Manufacturing:** Joining **high-speed steel tips to carbon-steel shanks** (drill bits, reamers) — a classic textbook example.
- **Electronics:** Coin-cell battery tab welding, hermetic sealing of microelectronic packages.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Stored Energy and Peak Power

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this derivation matters in KTU 2024 Scheme:**
> A direct "Calculate the energy stored in a 0.1 F capacitor charged to 200 V and the peak discharge current across a 50 $\mu\Omega$ load" question appears in nearly every cycle. Work through this derivation once and you can answer any numerical variant.

### 3.1.1 Energy Stored in a Capacitor Bank

The energy stored in a capacitor of capacitance $C$ charged to a potential difference $V$ is given by the integral of incremental work done in bringing charge $dq$ from one plate to the other across the voltage $v(t)$ that grows linearly with charge.

$$
\begin{aligned}
dW &= v \cdot dq \\
v &= \dfrac{q}{C} \\
W &= \int_{0}^{Q} \dfrac{q}{C}\,dq = \dfrac{1}{C}\left[\dfrac{q^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{Q} = \dfrac{Q^{2}}{2C} \\
\text{Since } Q &= CV, \\
W &= \dfrac{(CV)^{2}}{2C} = \dfrac{C^{2}V^{2}}{2C} \\
\therefore E_{\text{stored}} &= \dfrac{1}{2}CV^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic in plain words:**
1. We start with the differential form of electrical work.
2. We substitute the linear capacitor relation $v = q/C$.
3. We integrate from zero charge to full charge $Q$.
4. We substitute $Q = CV$ from Gauss's law for the capacitor.
5. We simplify to obtain the standard $\tfrac{1}{2}CV^{2}$ formula.

---

### 3.1.2 Peak Discharge Current Through a Resistive Load

When the capacitor discharges through an effective resistance $R$ (mostly the contact + arc resistance + cabling), the current follows an exponentially decaying form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(t) &= V_{0}\,e^{-t/RC} \\
I(t) &= \dfrac{V(t)}{R} = \dfrac{V_{0}}{R}\,e^{-t/RC}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **peak current** occurs at $t = 0$:

$$
\therefore I_{\text{peak}} = \dfrac{V_{0}}{R}
$$

**Numerical example worked out step by step:**

Given: $C = 0.1~\text{F}$, $V_{0} = 200~\text{V}$, $R = 50~\mu\Omega$.

Step 1 — Energy stored:

$$
E_{\text{stored}} = \dfrac{1}{2}\,(0.1)\,(200)^{2} = \dfrac{1}{2}\,(0.1)\,(40{,}000) = 2000~\text{J}
$$

Step 2 — Peak current:

$$
I_{\text{peak}} = \dfrac{200}{50 \times 10^{-6}} = 4 \times 10^{6}~\text{A} = 4~\text{MA}
$$

Step 3 — Realistic interpretation: in practice, lead inductance limits the actual peak to **5 – 25 kA**, not 4 MA. The 50 $\mu\Omega$ figure represents an idealized short-circuit; the **arc resistance** during the brief arc is usually higher ($\sim 1 - 10~\text{m}\Omega$), which is what keeps currents within the practical range shown in §2.4.

---

### 3.1.3 Derivation of Arc Duration for LC Discharge

For an undamped LC discharge (ignoring resistance), the current oscillates sinusoidally with period $T = 2\pi\sqrt{LC}$. The **half-period** corresponds to the time the current flows in one direction — this is what defines the arc duration in the magnetic variant.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= 2\pi\sqrt{LC} \\
t_{\text{arc}} &= \dfrac{T}{2} = \pi\sqrt{LC}
\end{aligned}
$$

Some textbooks (and KTU) use a slightly different factor based on damping; the conservative form used in KTU is:

$$
t_{\text{arc}} = \dfrac{\pi}{2}\sqrt{LC}
$$

**Numerical example:**

Given: $L = 100~\mu\text{H}$, $C = 100{,}000~\mu\text{F} = 0.1~\text{F}$.

$$
t_{\text{arc}} = \dfrac{\pi}{2}\sqrt{(100 \times 10^{-6})(0.1)} = \dfrac{\pi}{2}\sqrt{10 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{\pi}{2}\,(3.16 \times 10^{-3}) \approx 4.97~\text{ms}
$$

This matches the **5 – 100 ms** band from §1.3 — confirming our derivation.

---

### 3.1.4 Derivation of Impact Velocity from Spring Energy

If the movable part is propelled by a compressed spring of stiffness $k$ compressed by an amount $x$, all the spring's elastic energy converts into kinetic energy at the moment of release.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{spring}} &= \tfrac{1}{2}kx^{2} \\
E_{\text{kinetic}} &= \tfrac{1}{2}mv^{2} \\
\tfrac{1}{2}kx^{2} &= \tfrac{1}{2}mv^{2} \\
\therefore v_{\text{impact}} &= \sqrt{\dfrac{kx^{2}}{m}} = x\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:**
- Energy conservation equates stored elastic PE to kinetic energy at release.
- We solve for $v$ and recognise $x$ can be factored out to show velocity scales linearly with spring compression.

---

### 3.1.5 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Process Parameters on Weld Quality

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matrix matters:** KTU Module 2 includes a question almost every year: *"Discuss the effect of process parameters on the quality of percussion welds."* The matrix below is a complete, exam-ready answer.

| Parameter | Too Low | Too High | Optimal Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Charging Voltage $V$ | Incomplete fusion, cold joint | Excessive spatter, burn-through | $50 - 400$ V |
| Spring Compression $x$ | Low impact velocity, weak arc | Violent spatter, electrode damage | Set to give $v = 5-20$ m/s |
| Forging Force $F$ | Porous weld, voids | Excess flash, deformation | $35 - 140$ MPa at interface |
| Gap Before Strike | Weak arc, slow ignition | Arc may not strike uniformly | $0.5 - 3.0$ mm |
| Surface Cleanliness | High resistance, inconsistent arc | — | Degreased, lightly abraded |
| Capacitance $C$ | Insufficient energy | Long arc, deep HAZ | Matched to cross-section |
| Electrode Alignment | Off-center weld, arcing to fixture | — | Within $\pm 0.05$ mm |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — The Percussion Welding Cycle

> [!NOTE]
> This Mermaid **state-transition diagram** maps the exact 5-phase sequence defined in §2.1. Examiners often award 2 bonus marks to students who illustrate their answer with a process flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Load Workpieces] --> B[Phase 1: Clamp and Align]
    B --> C[Phase 2: Charge Capacitor Bank]
    C --> D[Phase 3: Trigger Release]
    D --> E[Phase 4: Arc Ignition at Near Contact]
    E --> F[Phase 5: Forging Upset Stroke]
    F --> G[Phase 6: Hold and Cool]
    G --> H{Quality Check}
    H -- Pass --> I[Unload Welded Assembly]
    H -- Fail --> J[Discard or Rework]
    I --> K[End of Cycle]
    J --> K
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Percussion Welding Machine

> [!NOTE]
> The schematic below is rendered as a **block diagram** rather than a mechanical drawing because Mermaid cannot faithfully depict mechanical CAD details. The block architecture is, however, **exactly** what KTU examiners look for in a 7-mark question.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EnergyBank[Energy Storage Module]
        PS[Power Supply 220 V AC] --> RC[Rectifier]
        RC --> CAP[Capacitor Bank C]
        CAP --> CHK[Voltage Sensor]
    end
    subgraph ControlUnit[Control Module]
        MCU[Microcontroller / PLC] --> TRG[Trigger Circuit]
        MCU --> SAF[Safety Interlocks]
        MCU --> DLY[Delay Timer]
    end
    subgraph MechanicalStack[Mechanical Module]
        FH[Fixed Holder] --> WP1[Stationary Workpiece]
        MH[Movable Holder] --> WP2[Moving Workpiece]
        SPR[Spring or Pneumatic Cylinder] --> MH
        SOL[Solenoid Release] --> SPR
    end
    subgraph DischargePath[Discharge Path]
        CAP --> SCR[Thyristor / Ignitron Switch]
        SCR --> FH
        MH --> GND[Return Busbar]
        GND --> SCR
    end
    TRG --> SCR
    TRG --> SOL
    CHK --> MCU
    SAF --> MCU
    DLY --> TRG
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **Energy Bank** stores $E = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^{2}$.
- The **Control Module** decides *when* to fire and *how much* energy to deliver.
- The **Discharge Path** routes the mega-ampere current through the workpieces.
- The **Mechanical Module** provides the percussion motion and the forging upset.

---

## 4.3 Comparison Tree — Percussion vs. Flash vs. Spot Welding

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[Resistance Welding Family]
    Root --> SpotW[Spot Welding]
    Root --> FlashW[Flash Butt Welding]
    Root --> PercW[Percussion Welding]
    SpotW --> SpotDesc[Sustained low current 3 to 12 kA for 0.1 to 1 s]
    FlashW --> FlashDesc[Continuous arc with progressive burn-off over 1 to 10 s]
    PercW --> PercDesc[Single impulsive discharge over 5 to 100 ms]
    PercDesc --> PercApp1[Crossed wire joints]
    PercDesc --> PercApp2[Tool tip to shank]
    PercDesc --> PercApp3[Battery tabs]
    PercDesc --> PercApp4[Shipboard instrument leads]
```

---
