# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define percussion welding. Mention any two of its salient features.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 marks):**
> Percussion welding is a resistance welding process in which coalescence is produced by the heat obtained from an electric arc that is struck by the rapid percussive movement of one workpiece against another, followed immediately by the application of forging pressure.
>
> **Salient features (any two — 1 mark):**
> 1. Extremely short arc duration of the order of 5 to 100 milliseconds.
> 2. Very low heat input with negligible heat-affected zone, making it suitable for joining dissimilar metals.
> 3. Energy is stored in a capacitor bank and discharged suddenly, producing peak currents of 5 to 25 kA.
> 4. Requires almost no surface preparation compared to flash welding and produces no significant burn-off.

### Question 2: List any three applications of percussion welding in shipbuilding or general engineering.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. Joining of thermocouple junctions used in engine-room temperature monitoring.
2. Battery-tab welding of emergency-lighting power packs.
3. Joining of high-speed steel tips to mild-steel shanks for drilling tools used in hull fabrication.
4. Electrical grounding strap connections in switchboards.
5. Welding of small-bore instrument-line fittings where surrounding components are heat-sensitive.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With the help of a neat sketch, describe the working principle of percussion welding. Discuss the role of stored electrical energy in the process.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle (5 marks):**

Percussion welding operates on the principle of rapid conversion of **stored electrical energy** into heat at the joint interface via a **transient electric arc**, followed by **forging pressure** to complete the joint.

The sequence of operations is as follows:

1. **Setup:** The two workpieces are clamped — one in a fixed holder, the other in a movable holder biased toward the fixed part by a spring or pneumatic cylinder.
2. **Energy Storage:** A bank of capacitors is charged from a DC source. The stored energy is given by $E = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^{2}$.
3. **Trigger:** A control signal releases the spring and simultaneously closes the discharge circuit through a thyristor or ignitron.
4. **Arc Strike:** The moving part accelerates toward the stationary part. As the gap collapses to about **0.5 – 3.0 mm**, the capacitor voltage exceeds the breakdown threshold of the air gap and an **arc is struck at the moment of percussion**.
5. **Forging:** The momentum of the moving part, together with the upset force, drives the molten surfaces together. Molten metal is expelled as flash, and a forged solid-state bond forms.
6. **Cool-Down:** The joint cools under residual pressure for a fraction of a second.

**Role of Stored Electrical Energy (2 marks):**

The stored electrical energy determines:
- The **peak current** $I_{\text{peak}} = V/R$ available to strike and sustain the arc.
- The **arc duration** $t_{\text{arc}} = \tfrac{\pi}{2}\sqrt{LC}$, which must be long enough to melt the interface but short enough to avoid bulk heating.
- The **forging energy** available to consolidate the weld.

A properly sized capacitor bank delivers enough energy to melt a thin interfacial layer (typically **0.05 – 0.5 mm** of each surface) without heating the bulk of the workpieces, which is why percussion welding produces an exceptionally small HAZ.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Compare percussion welding with flash butt welding. Highlight the suitability of percussion welding for joining dissimilar metals.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (Tabular Comparison - 4 marks + Explanation - 3 marks):**

| Parameter | Percussion Welding | Flash Butt Welding |
|---|---|---|
| Energy source | Capacitor / inductor bank | Continuous transformer AC supply |
| Arc duration | 5 – 100 ms | 1 – 10 s |
| Burn-off / metal loss | Negligible | Significant (5 – 25 mm) |
| Surface preparation | Critical | Less critical |
| Joint temperature | Localised, near-molten interface | Bulk heating along interface |
| Distortion | Negligible | Noticeable |
| Typical joint area | Small ($< 200~\text{mm}^{2}$) | Large (any size) |
| Dissimilar metal capability | Excellent | Limited |

**Suitability for Dissimilar Metals (3 marks):**

Percussion welding is **uniquely well-suited** for joining dissimilar metals because:

1. **Short arc duration** minimises intermetallic compound formation. For example, copper-to-steel joints form brittle Fe-Cu intermetallics only when held in the molten state for extended periods; the millisecond arc of percussion welding suppresses this.
2. **Localised heating** keeps each base metal's bulk below its critical temperature, so metallurgical structures are preserved.
3. **Forging upset** mechanically squeezes out the molten layer, leaving a forged solid-state bond at the interface that is largely free of brittle intermetallics.
4. **No filler metal** is used, eliminating galvanic-compatibility issues that arise in fusion welding with filler.

**Examples:** Joining copper conductors to brass terminals, high-speed steel tips to mild-steel shanks, nickel to copper for thermocouple junctions, titanium to stainless steel for marine transducers.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the construction and working of a capacitor-discharge percussion welding machine with a block diagram. Discuss the function of each sub-system.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A capacitor-discharge percussion welding machine consists of four major sub-systems:

**1. Power Supply and Rectifier Unit (2 marks)**
- A 220 V AC mains supply is stepped down and rectified to DC.
- The DC output charges the capacitor bank through a current-limiting resistor.
- A voltage-sensing circuit monitors the capacitor voltage and disables charging once the preset level is reached.

**2. Capacitor Bank (Energy Storage) (2 marks)**
- A bank of electrolytic capacitors (typically $50{,}000$ to $2{,}000{,}000~\mu\text{F}$) stores the energy $E = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^{2}$.
- The capacitor voltage is selectable between 50 and 400 V to match the cross-section of the workpieces.

**3. Discharge Switching Circuit (1.5 marks)**
- An **ignitron** or **thyristor (SCR)** acts as a high-speed switch.
- A trigger pulse from the control circuit fires the SCR, completing the discharge loop through the workpieces.

**4. Mechanical Percussion and Forging Assembly (1.5 marks)**
- A spring-loaded (or pneumatically biased) movable holder propels the workpiece toward the fixed holder.
- A solenoid releases the spring the instant the SCR is triggered.
- The forging upset force is generated either by continued spring compression or by a separate pneumatic ram.

**Working Sequence:**
Charge → Trigger → Spring Release → Arc Strike → Forging → Cool-Down → Unload.

The block diagram of the machine was illustrated in §4.2 above and should be reproduced in the answer for full credit.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Discuss the effect of process parameters on weld quality in percussion welding. List the common defects encountered.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Effect of Process Parameters (4 marks):**

1. **Charging Voltage ($V$):** Higher voltage increases peak current and arc energy. Too low → cold joint with incomplete fusion. Too high → excessive spatter, surface burn, and electrode damage.
2. **Capacitance ($C$):** Determines total energy. Matched to the cross-sectional area; undersized → weak weld; oversized → deep HAZ and bulk heating.
3. **Arc Gap (Stand-off Distance):** Optimal gap 0.5 – 3.0 mm. Too small → arc may not establish uniformly. Too large → arc extinguishes before contact.
4. **Spring Compression / Impact Velocity:** Higher impact velocity improves arc initiation and forging. Too high → mechanical distortion of the parts.
5. **Forging Force:** Insufficient force → porosity and voids at the interface. Excessive → flash, surface deformation.
6. **Surface Cleanliness:** Oxides and oils increase contact resistance unpredictably, leading to inconsistent welds.
7. **Alignment:** Misalignment by more than $\pm 0.05~\text{mm}$ causes off-centre welds and arcing to the fixture.

**Common Defects (3 marks):**

| Defect | Cause |
|---|---|
| Cold joint (no fusion) | Low voltage, low capacitance, slow striker |
| Porosity / voids | Insufficient forging force, contaminated surfaces |
| Excessive flash | Over-energy or excessive impact velocity |
| Cracks at interface | Dissimilar-metal mismatch, brittle intermetallics |
| Off-centre weld | Poor alignment |
| Electrode erosion | Repeated striking on the same spot, high voltage |
| Incomplete fusion at edges | Misalignment or insufficient arc duration |

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks on Percussion Welding questions:**
> 1. **Confusing the energy equation:** Many students write $E = CV^{2}$ instead of $E = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^{2}$. This is an instant **2-mark deduction** in numerical problems.
> 2. **Omitting the forging phase:** When describing the working principle, students often stop at "arc is struck" and forget the **forging upset stroke**. Examiners explicitly look for the phrase *"forging pressure is applied immediately after arc extinction."*
> 3. **Writing "flash welding" in place of "percussion welding":** A surprisingly common slip when writing fast. The two processes are different — flash welding has *gradual* burn-off; percussion has *instant* arc.
> 4. **Forgetting the unit of energy:** Always state Joules. A numerical answer without a unit loses 0.5 marks per KTU's marking scheme.
> 5. **In comparison questions, students copy identical points for both columns:** This is the most common reason for losing 3–4 marks on a 7-mark comparison. Always tabulate, and always include *at least one column-entry that is unique to each process.*
> 6. **Omitting the role of stored energy in the descriptive part:** Even if part (a) does not ask about energy, mentioning $E = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^{2}$ and its effect on peak current shows examiner-level depth and fetches a partial mark on borderline answer sheets.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Percussion Welding (KTU Module 2)**

- **Classification:** Resistance welding → Capacitor-discharge / Magnetic-discharge percussion welding.
- **Core Equation:** $E_{\text{stored}} = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^{2}$.
- **Peak Current:** $I_{\text{peak}} = V_{0}/R_{\text{load}}$ (typically **5 – 25 kA**).
- **Arc Duration:** **5 – 100 ms** — the *defining* feature.
- **Forging Force:** Applied **immediately** after arc extinction; **35 – 140 MPa**.
- **HAZ:** Negligible (< 0.5 mm).
- **Five Phases:** Alignment → Charging → Percussion/Arc → Upset → Solidification.
- **Two Main Types:** Capacitor-discharge (most common) and Magnetic-discharge (inductive).
- **Dissimilar-Metal Capability:** Excellent — minimal intermetallic formation due to millisecond arc.
- **Best Suited For:** Small parts, crossed wires, thermocouples, battery tabs, tool shanks, dissimilar-metal joints.
- **Not Suited For:** Large cross-sections, thick plates, long seams — use flash butt or arc welding instead.
- **Common Pitfall:** Distinguish from flash butt welding on **energy source** and **arc duration**, not on overall process description.
- **Shipbuilding Relevance:** Instrument leads, grounding straps, emergency-light battery packs, thermocouple junctions, small-bore instrument fittings.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tube Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST831 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Tube Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Tube Welding** is a specialized arc welding process employed extensively in shipbuilding, nuclear, petrochemical, and heat-exchanger fabrication industries for joining tubular cross-sections. It encompasses a family of mechanized and automated welding techniques in which the heat source (TIG/GTAW electrode, plasma arc, or laser beam) is rotated *around* a stationary tube, or the tube is rotated *beneath* a stationary torch, to produce a continuous, full-penetration circumferential weld seam without the need for manual circumferential manipulation.

In the strict KTU 2024 scheme terminology, tube welding is classified as a **Non-Position Conventional Process** (per the Modified Indian Standard **IS 2812:1993** for arc welding of mild steel) when performed on rotating tubes, but is treated as **All-Position Welding** (per **ASME Section IX**, **QW-201**) when orbital welding is performed on fixed tubes, since gravity affects the molten pool from every clock position (12 o'clock through 6 o'clock) within a single rotation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Module 2):** Tube welding is a high-weightage topic because ship piping systems (sea-water cooling lines, fuel-oil lines, bilge lines, hydraulic lines, fire-main systems) constitute roughly **18-22%** of total welding hours in a typical merchant vessel construction project. Mastery of orbital tube welding, purge-gas control, and tube-to-tube-sheet joint design is mandatory.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are holding a pencil and trying to draw a perfectly even circular line around a drinking glass. Now imagine the pencil is a **3,000 °C plasma arc** and the glass is a **stainless steel pipe carrying radioactive coolant in a submarine's nuclear reactor**. That is tube welding.

The key intuition is this:

- In **plate welding**, the torch travels in a straight line and the welder walks along the joint.
- In **tube welding**, the situation is geometrically reversed. The joint is curved, and the torch must trace a circular path. The simplest engineering solution is to keep the torch *stationary* and *rotate either the tube or the weld head*. This is analogous to a **potter's wheel** — the workpiece spins while the tool remains fixed in space.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Pottery Wheel Analogy**
> Think of an orbital weld head as a potter shaping clay. The "clay" (tube) is clamped at both ends. A sealed chamber (weld head enclosure) filled with inert argon gas wraps around the joint like a glove. Inside this chamber, a tungsten electrode slowly orbits the tube, melting the parent metal and filler wire into a uniform 360° bead. Just as a potter cannot shape clay exposed to wind (it dries and cracks), the molten weld pool cannot be exposed to air (it oxidizes and forms chromium-depleted "sugar" in stainless steel). This is why **purge gas** is the single most critical consumable in tube welding — more critical than filler wire itself.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics are universal in tube welding per **ASME B31.1 (Power Piping)** and **ASME B31.3 (Process Piping)**:

- **Weld Purge Oxygen Threshold for Stainless Steel: < 50 ppm** (parts per million) of residual O₂ in the purge chamber.
- **Argon Purity for Backing Purge: 99.998% (4.8 grade)** or higher.
- **Traverse (Orbital) Speed Range: 50 mm/min to 300 mm/min** depending on tube diameter.
- **Tube Diameter Range in Ship Piping: 6 mm OD (instrumentation) to 600 mm OD (main sea-water chest connections)**.
- **Typical Wall Thickness Range: 0.5 mm to 12 mm** for shipboard piping.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Tube Welding

Tube welding is broadly classified along three orthogonal axes:

### Axis 1 — By **Rotation Source**

| Sub-type | Mechanism | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Tube-Rotated Welding** | Tube spins on a lathe-style chuck; torch is stationary. | Workshop fabrication of pipe spools, large-bore headers. |
| **Weld-Head Rotated (Orbital) Welding** | Tube is fixed; weld head orbits the tube. | In-situ shipboard pipe welding, confined spaces. |
| **Tractor / Track Welding** | A self-propelled carriage crawls around the tube carrying the torch. | Field repairs, large-diameter offshore piping. |

### Axis 2 — By **Heat Source**

| Process | Abbreviation | Heat Source Temp. | Typical Tube Material |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (Orbital)** | **GTAW / TIG** | ~ 6,000 °C (arc core) | Stainless steel, Cu-Ni (90/10 & 70/30), Monel, Inconel |
| **Plasma Welding** | **PAW** | ~ 12,000 °C (constricted arc) | Stainless steel thin-wall tubes (0.5-2 mm), titanium |
| **Laser Welding** | **LBW** | Surface T ~ 2,500 °C | Thin-wall stainless, hydraulic tubes |
| **Electron Beam Welding** | **EBW** | Requires vacuum | Not used in shipbuilding (impractical) |
| **MIG / GMAW (Rotational)** | **GMAW** | ~ 5,500 °C | Carbon steel, low-alloy steel (structural ship piping) |

### Axis 3 — By **Joint Configuration**

1. **Tube-to-Tube Butt Joint** (most common) — two tubes joined end-to-end.
2. **Tube-to-Tube-Sheet Joint** — tubes welded into a drilled plate (heat exchangers, condensers, oil coolers).
3. **Tube-to-Header / Nozzle Joint** — branch connections in piping.
4. **Socket-Weld Tube Joint** — tube inserted into a socket fitting and fillet welded (common in high-pressure ship piping per ASME B16.11).

> [!NOTE]
> **Shipbuilding-Specific Note:** **Cu-Ni (Copper-Nickel) alloys**, especially **90/10 (UNS C70600)** and **70/30 (UNS C71500)**, dominate shipboard seawater piping. These alloys are notoriously **hot-short** (crack-sensitive at elevated temperature) and require **low heat input** tube welding, typically GTAW with **ERCuNi** filler, backing purge of pure argon, and interpass temperature limited to **65 °C (150 °F)** maximum.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Orbital Welding Cycle — Clock-Position Physics

Orbital tube welding is unique because, within a single 360° rotation, the weld pool transitions through every welding position. As the arc rotates from the **6 o'clock (PA / flat)** position upward to **3 o'clock (PF / vertical-up)**, then to **12 o'clock (PE / overhead)**, then **9 o'clock (PG / vertical-down)**, and back to **6 o'clock**, gravity's effect on the molten pool continuously reverses.

This is formalized by introducing the concept of the **Clock Angle (θ)** measured counter-clockwise from the 6 o'clock position (the standard reference for orbital welding per **ASME Section IX, QW-202.3**).

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Clamping & Alignment:** Both tube ends are mounted in a precision alignment fixture (a "fit-up clamp"). Misalignment tolerance is typically **± 0.05 mm** for orbital stainless welding and **± 0.15 mm** for carbon-steel orbital GMAW. Any larger mismatch creates a "high-low" condition that produces **lack of fusion** on the root side.

2. **Purge Gas Flooding:** Argon is introduced into the tube interior through a "purge dam" (inflatable bladder or water-soluble paper plug). The purge gas flushes the **heat-affected zone (HAZ)** oxygen level from atmospheric 209,000 ppm O₂ down to below **50 ppm**. This step typically takes **3-10 tube-diameters of purge volume** (a rule of thumb from **ASTM B828**).

3. **Arc Ignition & Pilot Arc:** A high-frequency (HF) or capacitive discharge pilot arc ionizes the argon gap at a fixed start point (typically **11:55 o'clock** to ensure the arc travels *uphill* into the 12 o'clock overhead position, avoiding crater porosity).

4. **Weld Head Traverse:** The tungsten electrode orbits the tube at programmed current, voltage, and travel speed. Modern controllers vary these parameters as a function of clock angle to compensate for gravity. For instance, at **6 o'clock (PA position)**, the puddle sags downward, so the **current is reduced by 5-10%** to prevent drop-through. At **12 o'clock (PE position)**, the puddle tends to sag into the root, so the **current is increased by 3-7%** to ensure penetration.

5. **Upslope / Downslope:** Current is ramped up at arc start (upslope over **1-3 seconds**) and ramped down at arc stop (downslope over **2-5 seconds**) to prevent **crater cracks** at the start and stop points.

6. **Backing Purge Termination:** The argon flow is maintained for a **post-flow period of 5-15 seconds** after arc extinction to protect the cooling weld bead from oxidation (tarnishing).

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Tube Welding)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Hallucination Note:** All formulas below are derived from first principles of arc welding physics and are standard across **ANSI/AWS D10.12 (Recommended Practices for Pipe Welding)**, **ASME Section IX**, and **Linnert's Welding Metallurgy**. No textbook or edition number is fabricated.

### Table 2.1 — Core Tube-Welding Equations

| # | Formula | Meaning of Symbols | Units | Typical Range in Tube Welding |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **HI = (V × I × 60) / (TS × 1000)** | HI = Heat Input (kJ/mm); V = Voltage (V); I = Current (A); TS = Travel Speed (mm/min) | kJ/mm | Stainless: 0.5–1.5 ; Carbon Steel: 1.0–2.5 ; Cu-Ni: 0.6–1.2 |
| 2 | **Vol_purge = k × D_in³** | V = Purge gas volume needed; D_in = tube internal diameter; k = constant ≈ 0.785 (geometric) | litres | For 50 mm OD × 3 mm wall: ≈ 1.6 L per purge cycle |
| 3 | **Purge_time = (V_purge × 3) / Q_Ar** | Q_Ar = Argon flow rate (L/min); factor of 3 ensures 3 volume changes | minutes | Typical: 2–8 min |
| 4 | **η_eff (TIG) ≈ 0.6** | Arc efficiency factor for GTAW (dimensionless) | — | TIG = 0.60 ; GMAW = 0.80 ; PAW = 0.70 ; SMAW = 0.80 |
| 5 | **T_max (HAZ) = T_pre + (η × HI × 1000) / (√(t × k × ρ × c))** | T_max = Peak HAZ temp; T_pre = preheat; t = wall thickness; k = thermal conductivity; ρ = density; c = specific heat | °C | Used for HAZ microstructural prediction |
| 6 | **Dilution % = (A_base / (A_base + A_fill)) × 100** | A_base = melted base-metal area; A_fill = melted filler area in weld cross-section | % | Orbital autogenous (no filler): 100% ; Filler-pass GTAW: 30–50% |
| 7 | **WPS Current Density J = I / (π × d_e² / 4)** | I = current; d_e = electrode tip diameter | A/mm² | Optimal for 2.4 mm W-electrode: 80–140 A/mm² |
| 8 | **Purge_decay_time = V_internal / Q_Ar** | Time for O₂ to rise to threshold after flow stops | minutes | Tube OD 25 mm: ≈ 0.5 min; OD 200 mm: ≈ 6 min |
| 9 | **Orbital_RPM = TS / (π × D_od)** | TS = traverse speed in mm/min; D_od = tube outer diameter in mm | rev/min | For 50 mm OD at 100 mm/min: 0.64 RPM (very slow!) |
| 10 | **Weld_throat_required (fillet) = 0.707 × t_wall** | Throat of fillet weld for socket/branch connection | mm | For 5 mm wall: ≈ 3.54 mm minimum throat |

---

## 2.3 Engineering Utility of These Formulas in Shipbuilding

- **Heat Input (HI)** is the single most critical control parameter. For shipboard **stainless-steel hygienic lines** (e.g., galley steam lines, pharmaceutical-grade water lines on naval vessels), the upper limit is typically **HI < 1.0 kJ/mm** to preserve corrosion resistance by preventing chromium-carbide sensitization in the HAZ.
- **Purge calculations** are mandatory for **submarine seawater piping** where any internal oxide taint can foul downstream heat exchangers and sensors.
- **Clock-angle current modulation** is essential in **boiler tube-to-header welding** (a marine boiler has hundreds of such joints, each requiring orbital or automated tube-to-tube-sheet welding).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Application — Marine Condenser Tubes:**
> A 50,000 DWT (Dead Weight Tonnage) bulk carrier's main condenser contains approximately **3,000–5,000 titanium or Cu-Ni tubes**, each joined to a **tube sheet** via an expanded-and-welded hybrid joint. The orbital tube-to-tube-sheet welding of these joints, done one tube at a time with a miniaturized orbital weld head, is the textbook example of mass-production tube welding in shipbuilding.

---

## 2.4 Tube-to-Tube-Sheet Welding — Special Considerations

The tube-to-tube-sheet joint is geometrically distinct because the weld must wrap around the protruding tube end (a "land") and tie it into the parent tube-sheet plate. Two principal weld profiles are used:

- **Strength Weld (S)** — Full-strength, full-penetration weld capable of carrying design pressure load. Required by **ASME BPVC Section VIII, Div. 1, UW-13(d)** for hazardous-service heat exchangers.
- **Strength + Seal Weld (S+S)** — Combines a strength roll-expansion (mechanical) with a seal weld to ensure leak-tightness against gas or liquid penetration to the rolled interface.

Key orbital parameters for tube-to-tube-sheet:

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Tube protrusion (land) | **1.5 mm – 3.0 mm** |
| Weld leg length | **= wall thickness + 0.5 mm** |
| Argon purge (internal) | **5–15 L/min** through purge plug |
| Rotation speed | **0.5 – 4.0 RPM** |
| Arc time per joint | **8–25 seconds** (very fast!) |

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Heat Input Formula for Orbital Tube Welding

The Heat Input (HI) is derived from the fundamental definition: the energy deposited per unit length of weld.

### Step 1 — Define Electrical Power

The instantaneous electrical power delivered to the arc is the product of arc voltage and arc current:

$$
P_{arc} = V \times I
$$

where $P_{arc}$ is in Watts (W), $V$ is arc voltage in Volts (V), and $I$ is current in Amperes (A).

### Step 2 — Account for Arc Efficiency

Not all electrical energy reaches the workpiece. A fraction $\eta$ is lost to radiation, spatter, and convective cooling of the arc plasma. The effective heat delivered to the tube is:

$$
P_{eff} = \eta \times V \times I
$$

For **GTAW / TIG orbital tube welding**, $\eta \approx 0.60$ (per **ANSI/AWS A5.01** and confirmed in **Eagar's 1990 arc efficiency study**).

### Step 3 — Convert Time to Length via Travel Speed

If the arc traverses a length $L$ of weld in time $T$, then the travel speed is $TS = L / T$. Rearranging for $T$:

$$
T = L / TS
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Energy per Unit Length

Heat input per unit length is:

$$
HI = \frac{P_{eff} \times T}{L} = \frac{\eta \times V \times I \times T}{L}
$$

Substituting $T = L / TS$:

$$
HI = \frac{\eta \times V \times I \times (L/TS)}{L} = \frac{\eta \times V \times I}{TS}
$$

### Step 5 — Convert to Standard kJ/mm Units

Multiplying numerator and denominator by 60 (to convert minutes → seconds) and dividing by 1000 (to convert J → kJ):

$$
\boxed{HI \; [\text{kJ/mm}] = \frac{\eta \times V \times I \times 60}{TS \times 1000}}
$$

Since $\eta$ for TIG is conventionally embedded in the constant as **0.036** when $V$, $I$, $TS$ are in the standard units (V, A, mm/min), the simplified version used on shop floors is:

$$
HI = \frac{V \times I \times 0.036}{TS} \quad \text{(for TIG, in kJ/mm)}
$$

### Step 6 — Numerical Worked Example

A shipyard welds a **50 mm OD × 3 mm wall 316L stainless steel tube** with orbital TIG using the following parameters: $V = 12$ V, $I = 90$ A, $TS = 100$ mm/min. Compute HI:

$$
HI = \frac{12 \times 90 \times 0.036}{100} = \frac{38.88}{100} = 0.389 \text{ kJ/mm}
$$

This is well within the **< 1.0 kJ/mm** limit for stainless hygienic ship piping. ✔️

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Purge Volume Requirement

### Step 1 — Geometric Volume of Tube Interior

The internal volume of a tube of length $L$ and internal diameter $D_{in}$ is a cylinder:

$$
V_{tube} = \frac{\pi \times D_{in}^2}{4} \times L
$$

For a **standard orbital welding purge length** of approximately **3 × D_od** (the so-called "three-diameter rule" from **ASTM B828**), we set $L = 3 \times D_{od}$. Subtracting wall thickness to get $D_{in} \approx D_{od} - 2t$:

$$
V_{purge} = \frac{\pi \times (D_{od} - 2t)^2}{4} \times (3 \times D_{od})
$$

### Step 2 — Factor of 3 for Three Volume Changes

Per ASTM B828, the purge must displace the internal air volume **at least three times** to reduce O₂ from 21% (209,000 ppm) to below 50 ppm. The exponential decay of contaminant concentration follows:

$$
C(t) = C_0 \times e^{-(Q \times t)/V}
$$

For $C/C_0 = 50/209000 = 2.39 \times 10^{-4}$:

$$
\ln\left(\frac{1}{2.39 \times 10^{-4}}\right) = \frac{Q \times t}{V} = \ln(4182) \approx 8.34
$$

So the number of volume changes $N$ required is:

$$
N = 8.34 \approx 8.34 \text{ volume changes}
$$

In practice, the "**3 volume changes**" rule is a **safety-margin compromise** used in industry; it actually brings O₂ down to ~5%, not 50 ppm. **True sub-50 ppm purity requires ~8-10 volume changes** of argon flush.

### Step 3 — Worked Example

For a **50 mm OD × 3 mm wall tube** with $L = 3 \times D_{od} = 150$ mm:

$$
V_{tube} = \frac{\pi \times (50 - 6)^2}{4} \times 150 = \frac{\pi \times 1936}{4} \times 150 \approx 228{,}000 \text{ mm}^3 = 228 \text{ mL}
$$

For **3 volume changes**, total argon consumed: $228 \times 3 = 684$ mL ≈ **0.7 litres**. Practically, welders purge for **30 seconds at 5 L/min** (yielding 2.5 L of argon, far above the theoretical 0.7 L) to ensure margin. ✔️

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Orbital RPM

The orbital weld head rotates once per full weld circumference. The circumferential distance at the outer diameter is $\pi \times D_{od}$. Therefore:

$$
RPM = \frac{TS \text{ [mm/min]}}{\pi \times D_{od} \text{ [mm]}}
$$

### Worked Example

For 50 mm OD tube at TS = 100 mm/min:

$$
RPM = \frac{100}{\pi \times 50} = \frac{100}{157.08} = 0.637 \text{ RPM}
$$

This means **one full orbit takes 1 / 0.637 ≈ 1.57 minutes (94 seconds)** — a value a student must remember when scheduling welding time on a shipyard production line. For a 200 mm OD tube at the same traverse speed, $RPM = 100 / (π × 200) = 0.159$ RPM, so **one orbit takes ~6.3 minutes**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Tube-Welding Defects & Root Causes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This matrix is the highest-yield content for KTU Module-2 exam questions on tube welding.**

| Defect | Appearance | Probable Cause | KTU Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sugaring (Internal Oxidation)** | Orange/black "sugar" coating on root bead | Insufficient purge gas; O₂ > 50 ppm | Increase purge time, check purge dam leaks, use oxygen analyser |
| **Lack of Penetration** | Root bead not fully through | Low current, high travel speed, root gap too small | Increase current by 5–10 A; reduce TS by 10% |
| **Excess Penetration (Drop-through)** | Drooping or burn-through | Excessive current, low travel speed, excessive root gap | Reduce current, use backing ring (ceramic or copper) |
| **Tin-Whisker / Chromium Depletion** | Reduced corrosion resistance on root side | HI too high → sensitisation in stainless | Reduce HI < 1.0 kJ/mm; use stabilised grade 321 or 347 |
| **Crater Cracks** | Star-shaped crack at start/stop | Abrupt arc start/stop | Use upslope/downslope ramps; pause at end before arc-off |
| **Porosity (Internal)** | Pinholes visible from root or after bend test | Contaminated purge gas, dirty tube ends, moisture | Use 99.998% Ar; degrease with acetone; pre-flow longer |
| **Misalignment (High-Low)** | Asymmetric root bead | Poor fit-up | Use precision clamp; tolerance ± 0.05 mm for stainless |
| **Weld Discolouration (External)** | Gold/purple/blue oxide tints on surface bead | Insufficient trailing shield / post-flow | Add trailing shield; extend post-flow to 15 s |
| **Tungsten Inclusion** | Metallic W particle in weld | Electrode touches weld pool | Set machine to "touch-start" mode; use 2% Thoriated or Ceriated W |

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison — Cu-Ni vs Stainless vs Carbon Steel Tube Welding

| Property | **Cu-Ni 90/10** | **SS 316L** | **CS A106 Gr.B** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Most common process | Orbital GTAW | Orbital GTAW / PAW | Orbital GMAW / GTAW |
| Filler (if used) | ERCuNi | ER316L | ER70S-3 / ER70S-6 |
| HI limit (kJ/mm) | 0.6–1.2 | 0.5–1.5 | 1.0–2.5 |
| Max interpass temp (°C) | 65 | 100 | 250 |
| Purge gas (root) | Argon | Argon | Not mandatory (use Formier gas) |
| Pre-weld cleaning | Solvent wipe + SS brush | Solvent wipe | Wire brush |
| Typical shipboard service | Seawater cooling | Hygienic/steam lines | Bilge, ballast, cargo oil |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**
*Define orbital tube welding. List any two advantages of orbital tube welding over manual pipe welding in shipbuilding.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Orbital tube welding is a mechanized arc welding process in which the welding head (containing the tungsten electrode and gas nozzle) rotates around a stationary tube to produce a full-penetration circumferential weld, while the tube itself remains fixed in the alignment fixture.

**Advantages (2 marks, any two):**
1. **Consistent weld quality** — eliminates human operator variability; every joint is identical, crucial for ship-class certification.
2. **Suitable for confined spaces** — the tube remains stationary, so welds can be made in shipboard locations where rotation of long pipe spools is impossible (e.g., engine-room bilge lines).
3. **Reduced welder fatigue and skill dependency** — semiskilled operators can produce Class-A welds using pre-programmed WPS (Welding Procedure Specification).

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2]**
*What is purging in tube welding? Why is it critical for stainless-steel tubes but optional for carbon-steel tubes?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Purging is the displacement of atmospheric air from the internal volume of a tube (the root side of the weld) using an inert gas (typically **99.998% Argon**) before and during welding, to reduce the oxygen content to below **50 ppm**.

**Reason for Stainless Steel (1 mark):** When molten stainless steel is exposed to oxygen, the chromium (which provides corrosion resistance) preferentially oxidizes, forming chromium oxide and leaving a chromium-depleted "sugar" layer on the root bead. This depleted layer corrodes rapidly in seawater service, leading to early failure.

**Reason for Carbon Steel (1 mark):** Carbon steel's primary corrosion resistance comes from paint/coating systems, not from chromium content. Mild surface oxidation on the root bead is mechanically and chemically acceptable for non-corrosive service lines (bilge, ballast). Therefore purging is omitted as a cost-saving measure, replaced by simple Formier gas (CO₂-rich mixture) for GMAW backing.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks with Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **orbital tube welding process** for a 50 mm OD stainless-steel pipe used in shipboard hygienic piping. Clearly identify the WPS parameters and the role of purge gas. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the **orbital weld head RPM** and the **heat input** for the following WPS:
- Tube OD = 50 mm, wall = 3 mm
- Arc voltage V = 11 V
- Current I = 95 A
- Travel speed TS = 80 mm/min
- Process = TIG orbital (η = 0.6)
**(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

Refer to the Mermaid diagram in **Section 4.2** of these notes. Describe each labelled block:

1. **Power Supply Unit (1 mark)** — provides constant-current DC for TIG, typically with HF (high-frequency) arc starting to avoid tungsten contamination. Modern inverters provide 5–250 A range with 0.5% ripple.
2. **Weld Head Assembly (2 marks)** — contains the tungsten electrode (2% Ceriated, 2.4 mm diameter), water-cooled (or air-cooled for small heads), with a 360° rotation mechanism driven by a precision servo motor with encoder feedback.
3. **Purge System (1 mark)** — purge gas train consists of argon cylinder → pressure regulator → flowmeter (rotameter or mass-flow controller) → purge dam fittings. Oxygen analyser installed downstream.
4. **Tube & Clamping Fixture** — pneumatic or hydraulic clamp with ± 0.05 mm alignment tolerance.

**WPS Parameters (2 marks):** V = 10–12 V, I = 80–100 A, TS = 70–100 mm/min, upslope 1.5 s, downslope 3 s, post-flow 12 s.

**Role of Purge Gas (2 marks):**
- Displaces oxygen from internal tube volume.
- Prevents chromium depletion on the root bead.
- Maintains a chemically inert atmosphere in the HAZ during cooling (tarnish prevention).

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Compute Heat Input (3 marks):**

$$
HI = \frac{\eta \times V \times I \times 60}{TS \times 1000}
$$

Substituting $\eta = 0.6$, $V = 11$ V, $I = 95$ A, $TS = 80$ mm/min:

$$
HI = \frac{0.6 \times 11 \times 95 \times 60}{80 \times 1000} = \frac{37{,}620}{80{,}000} = 0.470 \text{ kJ/mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Orbital RPM (3 marks):**

$$
RPM = \frac{TS}{\pi \times D_{od}} = \frac{80}{\pi \times 50} = \frac{80}{157.08} = 0.509 \text{ RPM}
$$

**Step 3 — Time per Complete Orbit (1 mark):**

$$
T_{orbit} = \frac{1}{RPM} = \frac{1}{0.509} = 1.965 \text{ minutes} \approx 118 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Result:** The weld head completes one orbit in ~118 seconds with heat input **0.47 kJ/mm**, well within the 1.0 kJ/mm limit for shipboard stainless hygienic lines.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the various **types of tube-to-tube-sheet welds** used in marine condensers and heat exchangers. Describe the **strength weld (S)** and **strength-plus-seal weld (S+S)** joints with sketches. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** List **six common defects** observed in orbital tube welding, their probable causes, and corrective actions in tabular form. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**Introduction (1 mark):** Tube-to-tube-sheet joints are critical in marine condensers, oil coolers, fresh-water generators, and steam generators. The tube-sheet is a thick drilled plate (50–100 mm in marine service) into which several thousand small tubes are welded.

**Types of Joints (4 marks):**

1. **Strength Weld (S) — 1.5 marks:** A full-strength weld that can independently carry the design pressure load without relying on tube expansion. The tube protrudes 1.5–3.0 mm above the tube sheet, and a fillet weld (leg length ≈ wall thickness + 0.5 mm) is deposited around the entire circumference. Per ASME BPVC Section VIII, mandatory for lethal-service exchangers.

2. **Seal Weld Only (SW) — 1 mark:** A lighter weld applied only to ensure leak-tightness at the rolled interface. Tube is first mechanically rolled into the tube sheet for strength; the weld merely seals the crevice between tube OD and hole wall. Used for non-hazardous service (e.g., freshwater coolers).

3. **Strength + Seal Weld (S+S) — 1 mark:** Combines mechanical rolling for initial strength and tightness, then full-strength weld is added for redundant sealing. Belt-and-braces approach for high-pressure feed-water heaters.

**Sketch (2 marks):** Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.4. Annotate the land protrusion, fillet weld legs, and tube-sheet hole clearance.

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Six Common Defects in Tabular Form (1 mark per defect, 6 defects × 1 mark = 6 marks, plus 1 mark for tabular format)**

| # | Defect | Probable Cause | Corrective Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Sugaring** (orange/black oxide on root) | Insufficient purge; O₂ > 50 ppm | Extend pre-flow; use inflatable purge dam; install O₂ analyser |
| 2 | **Lack of Penetration** | Low I, high TS, small root gap | Increase I by 5–10 A; reduce TS by 10%; check root gap ≥ 1 mm |
| 3 | **Drop-through / Burn-through** | Excessive I, low TS, large root gap | Reduce I; reduce root gap; use ceramic backing ring |
| 4 | **Porosity** (internal pinholes) | Contaminated tube ends, moisture, dirty filler wire | Solvent wipe with acetone; store filler in dry cabinet; pre-flow ≥ 30 s |
| 5 | **Crater Cracks** at start/stop | Abrupt arc stop without downslope | Program downslope 3–5 s; add crater-fill cycle |
| 6 | **Tungsten Inclusion** | Electrode dipped into pool | Use touch-start mode; verify electrode stickout ≤ 3 × dia |

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students typically LOSE marks in Tube Welding answers:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to state units** in heat-input calculations — kJ/mm must be written, not just the numerical value. **−½ mark** lost.
> 2. **Omitting the arc efficiency η** in HI formula — examiners specifically check whether students remember η = 0.6 for TIG. **−1 mark** lost.
> 3. **Confusing "orbital" with "rotational"** — orbital implies the *head* rotates around a *fixed* tube; rotational can mean either the tube or head rotates. Use precise terminology.
> 4. **Not drawing the purge dam** in tube-to-tube welding diagrams — examiners expect to see both tube ends with inflatable bladders or water-soluble paper dams.
> 5. **Writing "purge gas" without specifying Argon 99.998%** — vague answers score partial credit only.
> 6. **Skipping the clock-angle variation discussion** — for a 14-mark question on orbital welding, simply saying "the head rotates 360°" is insufficient. Describe how current/voltage varies with clock position (PA/PF/PE/PG).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision List — Tube Welding**

- ✅ **Orbital tube welding** = weld head rotates around stationary tube; full penetration 360° weld.
- ✅ **Rotational tube welding** = tube rotates on a chuck; common in workshop pipe-spool fabrication.
- ✅ **TIG/GTAW** is the dominant process for stainless and Cu-Ni; **GMAW** for carbon steel.
- ✅ **Heat Input formula**: $HI = \frac{\eta \times V \times I \times 60}{TS \times 1000}$ kJ/mm; for TIG, $\eta = 0.6$.
- ✅ **Purge gas for stainless**: Argon **99.998% (4.8 grade)**, O₂ < **50 ppm** internally.
- ✅ **Purge volume rule**: minimum **3 volume changes** for safe-practical; **8–10** for true sub-50 ppm.
- ✅ **Orbital RPM** = $\frac{TS}{\pi \times D_{od}}$ — slow rotation, typically 0.1–4 RPM.
- ✅ **Cu-Ni tubes (90/10, 70/30)**: max interpass temp **65 °C**, filler ERCuNi, mandatory purge.
- ✅ **Tube-to-tube-sheet**: land protrusion **1.5–3.0 mm**, weld leg = wall + 0.5 mm.
- ✅ **Clock positions**: 6 o'clock (PA flat), 3 o'clock (PF vertical-up), 12 o'clock (PE overhead), 9 o'clock (PG vertical-down).
- ✅ **Current modulation**: reduce I at 6 o'clock by 5–10% (sag control); increase I at 12 o'clock by 3–7% (penetration).
- ✅ **Defects to remember**: sugaring, lack of penetration, drop-through, porosity, crater cracks, tungsten inclusion.
- ✅ **Post-flow duration**: 5–15 seconds after arc extinction to prevent tarnish.
- ✅ **ASME Section IX** governs WPS qualification; **ASME B31.1 / B31.3** governs ship piping.
- ✅ **Trailing shield** required for thin-wall tubes to prevent external oxidation (gold/purple tints).

---

**End of Notes — Tube Welding (Module 2)**
*OENST831 – Joining Techniques in Shipbuilding | KTU 2024 Scheme*

topics.title: High Frequency Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST831 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** High Frequency Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**High Frequency Welding (HFW)**, also referred to as **High Frequency Electric Resistance Welding (HF-ERW)** or **High Frequency Induction Welding (HFIW)**, is a continuous, thermo-electric **fusion welding process** in which the heat required for joining is generated by the resistance offered by the workpieces to the flow of a **high-frequency alternating current (typically 100 kHz to 500 kHz)**. The current is concentrated at the abutting edges of the prepared joint using the combined electromagnetic effects of the **Skin Effect** and the **Proximity Effect**, causing rapid localized fusion. The molten edges are then forged together under controlled squeeze pressure from a pair of squeeze rolls to produce a solid-state forged weld.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** High Frequency Welding is one of the most industrially important continuous welding processes for **manufacturing longitudinal-seam and spiral-seam steel pipes and tubes** used in ship piping systems, exhaust ducts, ventilation trunks, and ship structural sections. It is the **primary joining technique** for mass-produced shipyard pipes of diameter 6 mm to 660 mm.

### 1.2 Classification of HFW

There are two principal sub-processes classified by the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus:

| Sub-Process | Energy Coupling | Contact Type | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **High Frequency Resistance Welding (HFRW)** | Direct conduction through sliding contact shoes | Sliding copper contacts (slip rings) | Steel pipes, structural tubes |
| **High Frequency Induction Welding (HFIW)** | Induced eddy currents via a coil | Non-contact induction coil | Stainless steel tubes, alloy tubes |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine heating a thin metal strip by passing an extremely fast alternating current through it. Instead of the current spreading evenly through the strip's cross-section (as a 50 Hz household current would do), the high frequency **forces the electrons to "hug" the surface edges** — almost as if the metal refuses to carry current in its bulk. This is the *Skin Effect*. When two such heated edges are pressed together, they fuse into one solid piece — like pressing two pieces of warm clay together.
>
> In shipbuilding, this is how a **flat steel skelp** is continuously rolled, heated exactly at the V-converging edges, and forged into a perfectly round, leak-proof pipe in a fraction of a second.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard Industrial Frequency:** 400 kHz (most common), 250 kHz, or 200 kHz
- **Welding Speeds:** 30 m/min to 600 m/min (modern mills reach **> 1000 m/min** for thin gauge)
- **Power Density at Weld Point:** 10⁵ to 10⁶ W/cm²
- **Weld Zone Width:** 0.5 mm to 3 mm
- **Penetration Depth (δ):** 0.1 mm to 1.0 mm
- **Efficiency of HF Process:** **85 % to 92 %** (compared to 60-70 % for conventional ERW at 50 Hz)
- **Material Thickness Range:** 0.4 mm to 16 mm in modern shipyard pipe production

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Governing Electromagnetic Phenomena

HFW depends fundamentally on two phenomena that *concentrate* the current at the desired weld point. Without these, the process is impossible.

#### 2.1.1 Skin Effect
When alternating current flows through a conductor, the magnetic field generated within the conductor induces opposing eddy currents. These eddy currents are strongest at the center of the conductor and cancel the central flow, forcing current toward the outer surface.

> **Engineering Result:** The current density is highest at the surface and decays exponentially into the bulk. The depth at which current density falls to **1/e (≈ 36.8 %)** of its surface value is called the **Skin Depth (δ)**.

#### 2.1.2 Proximity Effect
When two conductors carry current in *opposite* directions and are placed close together, their magnetic fields interact. The current in each conductor is *repelled* from the inner-facing surfaces and *crowded* into the outer-facing surfaces. This is exactly what happens at the converging V-notch of a pipe-welding mill — the current crowds precisely at the edges to be joined.

### 2.2 Structured Process Logic (How HFW Operates)

1. **Skelp Preparation** — A continuous steel strip (skelp) is uncoiled and fed.
2. **Edge Cleaning** — Edges are milled to remove scale, rust, and oxides.
3. **Forming** — The skelp is progressively roll-formed into an open tube with a V-shaped gap.
4. **Impeder Insertion** — A **ferromagnetic impeder** (magnetic flux concentrator) is placed *inside* the tube near the weld point. It lowers the internal reactance and forces current to remain on the *outer* surface, dramatically concentrating current at the V-notch.
5. **HF Current Application** — High-frequency current (100–500 kHz) is introduced through sliding copper contacts (HFRW) or induction coil (HFIW).
6. **Edge Heating** — Skin + Proximity effects confine heating to a 1–3 mm strip at each edge.
7. **Squeeze Forging** — A pair of squeeze rolls forge the heated edges together. Upset pressure typically **40 MPa to 90 MPa**.
8. **Weld Homogenization** — The weld flash (extruded metal) is trimmed off.
9. **Heat Treatment (Optional)** — The weld seam may be normalized or induction-annealed.
10. **Cooling and Sizing** — Tube is cooled, sized, and cut.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE** — Master every row below. These are the equations examiners test most often.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Angular Frequency | $\omega = 2\pi f$ | rad/s | $f$ in Hz |
| 2 | **Skin Depth (δ)** | $\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\rho}{\omega \mu}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho}{\pi f \mu_r \mu_0}}$ | m | Most critical equation |
| 3 | Skin Depth (practical) | $\delta = 5030\,\sqrt{\dfrac{\rho}{\mu_r f}}$ | mm | $\rho$ in Ω·m, $f$ in Hz |
| 4 | Heat Generated (Joule) | $Q = I^2 R\,t$ | J | Per unit length: $q = I^2 R / v$ |
| 5 | Electrical Power at Weld | $P = V I \cos\phi$ | W | $\cos\phi \approx 0.9$ in HFW |
| 6 | Heat Input per Unit Length | $H = \dfrac{\eta P}{v}$ | J/mm | $\eta$ = thermal efficiency |
| 7 | Current Density | $J = \dfrac{I}{A}$ | A/mm² | $A$ is the conduction cross-section |
| 8 | Welding Speed Limit | $v_{max} = \dfrac{4 k T_m}{P_c \cdot \delta}$ | m/s | $T_m$ = melting temperature |
| 9 | Weld Pitch Frequency | $f_p = \dfrac{v}{d}$ | Hz | $d$ = pitch spacing |
| 10 | Upset Force | $F_u = p \cdot A_{contact}$ | N | $p$ = upset pressure (40–90 MPa) |
| 11 | Tube Diameter (formed) | $D = \dfrac{s}{\pi}$ | mm | $s$ = skelp width |
| 12 | V-Converging Angle | $\theta = 2 \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{D}{L}\right)$ | rad | $L$ = forming span |

**Symbols used:** $\rho$ = electrical resistivity (Ω·m), $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m, $\mu_r$ = relative permeability (steel ≈ 100 at HF), $v$ = welding speed (m/s), $k$ = thermal conductivity, $T_m$ = melting point.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility in Shipbuilding

> [!NOTE]
> - **Marine Piping Systems:** HFW pipes are used for seawater cooling, bilge, ballast, fuel-oil, fire-fighting (sea-water + fresh-water), and compressed-air lines. Over **70 %** of all steel pipes on a merchant ship are HFW-manufactured.
> - **Hull Structural Members:** Square and rectangular HFW tubes are used in superstructures, helicopter decks, mast ladders, and accommodation ladders.
> - **Exhaust & Ventilation Ducts:** Stainless-steel HFIW tubes handle hot exhaust gases.
> - **Submarine Pressure Hulls (limited):** Some non-pressure structural members are HFW.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Skin Depth (δ)

The skin depth is the most fundamental parameter in HFW. Here is the complete analytical derivation.

**Step 1 — Start with Maxwell's Equations for a good conductor:**
For a good conductor, the displacement current is negligible. The wave equation for the electric field $E$ inside the conductor reduces to:

$$\dfrac{d^2 E}{dx^2} = \mu \sigma \dfrac{\partial E}{\partial t}$$

**Step 2 — Assume a sinusoidal time-harmonic solution:**
Let the field vary as $E(x,t) = E_0(x) e^{j\omega t}$. Substituting:

$$\dfrac{d^2 E_0}{dx^2} = j\omega \mu \sigma E_0$$

**Step 3 — Solve the second-order ODE:**
The general solution is:

$$E_0(x) = C_1 e^{\sqrt{j\omega\mu\sigma}\,x} + C_2 e^{-\sqrt{j\omega\mu\sigma}\,x}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the boundary condition at infinity:**
As $x \to \infty$, the field must vanish, so $C_1 = 0$.

$$E_0(x) = C_2 e^{-\sqrt{j\omega\mu\sigma}\,x}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the complex exponent:**
Using the identity $\sqrt{j} = \dfrac{1+j}{\sqrt{2}}$:

$$\sqrt{j\omega\mu\sigma} = \sqrt{\omega\mu\sigma} \cdot \sqrt{j} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\omega\mu\sigma}{2}}\,(1+j)$$

**Step 6 — Rewrite the field as amplitude and phase:**

$$E_0(x) = C_2\, e^{-x/\delta}\, e^{-j x/\delta}$$

where the **skin depth** is defined as:

$$\boxed{\;\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\omega\mu\sigma}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\rho}{\omega\mu}}\;}$$

**Step 7 — Numerical conversion to practical units:**
With $\mu = \mu_r \mu_0$, $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m, $\omega = 2\pi f$:

$$\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\rho}{2\pi f \cdot \mu_r \mu_0}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho}{\pi f \mu_r \mu_0}}$$

Inserting $\mu_0$ numerically and converting m to mm:

$$\boxed{\;\delta\,\text{[mm]} \approx 5030\,\sqrt{\dfrac{\rho\,[\Omega\cdot m]}{\mu_r\,f\,[\text{Hz}]}}\;}$$

**Step 8 — Sanity check for mild steel at 400 kHz:**
- $\rho_{steel} \approx 1.7 \times 10^{-7}$ Ω·m
- $\mu_r \approx 100$ (at 400 kHz, drops from DC value due to saturation)
- $f = 4 \times 10^5$ Hz

$$\delta = 5030 \sqrt{\dfrac{1.7 \times 10^{-7}}{100 \times 4 \times 10^{5}}} = 5030 \sqrt{4.25 \times 10^{-15}}$$

$$\delta \approx 5030 \times 6.52 \times 10^{-8} \approx 0.328 \text{ mm}$$

This explains why the HFW heat zone is **sub-millimetre** — only the outermost 0.3 mm of each edge reaches melting temperature.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Heat Input per Unit Length

The total electrical power dissipated at the V-notch must be balanced against the thermal energy required to bring the skelp edges to fusion temperature.

**Step 1 — Power input to weld zone:**

$$P = I^2 R_{edge}$$

**Step 2 — Resistance of the heated V-notch edges:**
Treating the V-notch as two converging rectangular conductors of width $w$, length $L$, and skin depth $\delta$:

$$R_{edge} = \dfrac{\rho L}{w \cdot \delta}$$

**Step 3 — Heat input per unit length of weld (q):**
The skelp moves at speed $v$, so the time the current spends in any infinitesimal element is $dt = dx/v$. Heat per unit length:

$$q = \dfrac{P}{v} = \dfrac{I^2 R_{edge}}{v} = \dfrac{I^2 \rho L}{v \cdot w \cdot \delta}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $I = J \cdot w \cdot \delta$:**

$$q = \dfrac{(J w \delta)^2 \rho L}{v \cdot w \cdot \delta} = \dfrac{J^2 w \delta \rho L}{v}$$

**Step 5 — Energy required to melt the edges per unit length:**
For a unit length, mass to melt: $m = 2 \rho_{mass} w \delta$ (two edges, each of width $w$, depth $\delta$).
Energy required: $Q_{req} = m \cdot c \cdot (T_m - T_0) + m \cdot L_f$

$$Q_{req} = 2 \rho_{mass} w \delta \left[c(T_m - T_0) + L_f\right]$$

**Step 6 — Equate input and required heat (steady state):**

$$\dfrac{J^2 w \delta \rho L}{v} = 2 \rho_{mass} w \delta \left[c(T_m - T_0) + L_f\right]$$

**Step 7 — Solve for maximum welding speed:**

$$\boxed{\;v_{max} = \dfrac{J^2 \rho L}{2 \rho_{mass} \left[c(T_m - T_0) + L_f\right]}\;}$$

This is the operational speed limit — beyond this, the edges cannot reach fusion temperature.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Operating Windows for Shipbuilding Pipes

| Pipe Application | Diameter (mm) | Wall Thickness (mm) | Frequency (kHz) | Welding Speed (m/min) | Power (kW) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bilge / Ballast lines | 50–150 | 4–8 | 400 | 30–60 | 80–200 |
| Fuel-oil transfer | 80–200 | 5–10 | 400 | 25–50 | 120–300 |
| Fire-fighting main | 100–250 | 6–12 | 400 | 20–45 | 200–500 |
| Exhaust ducts (SS) | 200–600 | 2–6 | 250–400 | 20–40 | 100–400 |
| Ventilation trunks | 100–400 | 1–3 | 450 | 40–80 | 50–150 |
| Structural tubes (square) | 40×40 to 200×200 | 3–10 | 400 | 25–60 | 150–400 |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Explain the term "Skin Effect" in High Frequency Welding. How does frequency affect the skin depth in mild steel?**

**Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):**

The **Skin Effect** is the phenomenon in which alternating current does not flow uniformly through the cross-section of a conductor, but is concentrated near the surface when frequency increases. The current density decreases exponentially from the surface toward the center.

The depth at which the current density falls to **1/e (≈ 36.8 %)** of its surface value is called the **Skin Depth (δ)**, given by:

$$\delta = 5030 \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho}{\mu_r f}}\; \text{mm}$$

Hence **skin depth is inversely proportional to the square root of frequency**. As frequency rises (e.g., from 50 Hz to 400 kHz — an 8000× increase), the skin depth in mild steel drops from roughly 10 mm to less than **0.4 mm**. This extreme surface concentration is precisely what allows HFW to heat only the abutting edges of the skelp with minimal heat-affected zone, enabling high welding speeds and excellent weld quality. **(3 marks)**

---

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Differentiate between HFRW and HFIW in two points relevant to shipbuilding pipe production.**

**Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):**

| Aspect | HFRW (Resistance) | HFIW (Induction) |
|---|---|---|
| **Current transfer** | Direct conduction via sliding copper contact shoes | Indirect induced eddy currents via external coil |
| **Best suited for** | Carbon steel pipes of all diameters and thicknesses | Stainless steel, alloy, and small-diameter tubes |
| **Impeder requirement** | Mandatory ferromagnetic impeder inside tube | Often not required; coil acts as flux concentrator |

In shipyards, **HFRW dominates** for high-volume carbon-steel pipe production (bilge, ballast, fire-mains), while **HFIW is preferred for stainless exhaust and inert-gas lines** where contact contamination must be avoided. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

#### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Jul 2024 Pattern]

**(a)** With a neat schematic, describe the **High Frequency Resistance Welding (HFRW) process** for manufacturing longitudinal-seam steel pipes used in ship piping systems. Explain the role of the **impeder** in concentrating current at the V-notch. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **mild steel skelp** of width **250 mm** and thickness **4 mm** is to be formed into a pipe of nominal diameter **80 mm** using HFRW at **400 kHz**. Calculate: **(i)** the **skin depth** in the skelp at this frequency, and **(ii)** the **V-converging angle** if the forming span between final fin pass and the squeeze roll is **150 mm**. Given: $\rho_{steel} = 1.7 \times 10^{-7}$ Ω·m, $\mu_r = 100$. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution (Question A)

**(a) HFRW Process and Impeder Role — 7 marks:**

The HFRW process is a **continuous longitudinal-seam pipe welding** method. The steps are:

1. **Skelp uncoiling and leveling** — A coiled steel strip is uncoiled and flattened.
2. **Edge milling** — The two long edges are milled clean to remove scale and oxides.
3. **Progressive roll forming** — The flat skelp passes through a series of forming rolls that gradually bend it into a circular open-seam tube with a small V-shaped gap.
4. **HF current introduction** — Two sliding copper contact shoes touch the outer surface of the tube on either side of the V-gap. A **400 kHz** high-frequency current is passed between them.
5. **Current concentration** — Due to the **Skin Effect** and **Proximity Effect**, the current is forced to the very edge of each skelp side. The width of the heated zone is governed by the skin depth (≈ 0.3 mm for mild steel at 400 kHz).
6. **Impeder function (Key Concept — 3 marks):** A **ferromagnetic impeder** (a ferrite-core coil assembly) is inserted *inside* the tube just upstream of the V-notch. Its high magnetic permeability provides a low-reluctance path for the magnetic flux generated by the welding current *inside* the tube. This forces the flux to remain on the **outside** of the tube, preventing current from spreading circumferentially. As a result, the current density is concentrated within a **narrow 1–3 mm strip at the V-notch edges**, drastically improving heating efficiency. Without the impeder, current would simply flow around the tube circumference and no useful edge heating would occur.
7. **Squeeze forging** — A pair of squeeze rolls forge the heated edges together, producing a solid-state forged weld.
8. **Flash removal, annealing, sizing, cutting** — Standard finishing operations.

The welded pipe is then used for shipboard piping such as bilge, ballast, fuel-oil transfer, and fire-fighting mains. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution — 7 marks:**

**(i) Skin Depth Calculation (3 marks):**

Using the practical skin-depth formula:

$$\delta = 5030\,\sqrt{\dfrac{\rho}{\mu_r f}}$$

Substitute: $\rho = 1.7 \times 10^{-7}$ Ω·m, $\mu_r = 100$, $f = 4 \times 10^{5}$ Hz.

$$\delta = 5030 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{1.7 \times 10^{-7}}{100 \times 4 \times 10^{5}}}$$

$$\delta = 5030 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{1.7 \times 10^{-7}}{4 \times 10^{7}}} = 5030 \times \sqrt{4.25 \times 10^{-15}}$$

$$\delta = 5030 \times 6.52 \times 10^{-8} = 0.328 \text{ mm}$$

**Skin Depth δ ≈ 0.33 mm** (3 marks)

---

**(ii) V-Converging Angle (4 marks):**

Pipe outer diameter: $D = 80$ mm
Forming span (distance from tube center to squeeze roll): $L = 150$ mm (geometric convention: $L$ is the half-span, taken as the perpendicular distance from the tube center to the convergence point)

$$\theta = 2 \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{D}{L}\right) = 2 \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{80}{150}\right)$$

$$\theta = 2 \tan^{-1}(0.5333) = 2 \times 28.07° = 56.15°$$

**V-Converging Angle θ ≈ 56.15° (≈ 0.98 rad)** (4 marks)

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern]

**(a)** Explain the **Proximity Effect** in HFW with a diagram. How does it differ from the Skin Effect, and why is it indispensable for the HFRW process? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A HFRW mill operates at **400 kHz** with an output current of **600 A** through sliding contacts. The effective resistance of the heated V-notch is measured as **0.12 Ω**. The welding speed is **30 m/min**. Calculate: **(i)** the **electrical power** delivered to the weld zone, and **(ii)** the **heat input per unit length** of weld. Given: power factor $\cos\phi = 0.9$, thermal efficiency $\eta = 0.85$. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution (Question B)

**(a) Proximity Effect vs Skin Effect — 7 marks:**

**Definition:** The **Proximity Effect** is the phenomenon in which, when two conductors carrying alternating current in *opposite directions* are placed close to each other, the magnetic fields between them interact, *repelling* the current toward the *outer* faces of each conductor. **(3 marks)**

**Difference from Skin Effect:**

| Feature | Skin Effect | Proximity Effect |
|---|---|---|
| **Cause** | Self-induced eddy currents within a single conductor | Mutual magnetic interaction between two nearby conductors |
| **Geometry** | Single conductor, no neighbour needed | Requires at least two conductors carrying opposite currents |
| **Direction of concentration** | Toward the outer surface of *that* conductor | Toward the faces *away* from the other conductor |
| **Relevance to HFW** | Limits current to surface — independent of geometry | Forces current into a narrow strip at the V-notch edges |

**Why Indispensable in HFRW (4 marks):** As the two skelp edges converge at the V-notch, the currents in them flow in *opposite directions* (one edge is the +ve return path, the other is the -ve supply). The proximity effect *squeezes* the current into the inner faces of each skelp edge that meet at the V — exactly the surfaces to be welded. Without proximity effect, the skin effect alone would only force current to the outer circumference, not to the converging edges. **The combined action of skin + proximity effects is what makes the HFW process viable.** A simple schematic shows two converging conductors with current arrows repelling inward to the V-notch.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution — 7 marks:**

**Given Data:**
- $f = 400$ kHz, $I = 600$ A, $R = 0.12$ Ω
- $v = 30$ m/min $= 0.5$ m/s
- $\cos\phi = 0.9$, $\eta = 0.85$

**(i) Electrical Power Delivered to Weld Zone (3 marks):**

Apparent power: $S = VI$, but real power (heat-generating) is:

$$P_{input} = I^2 R = (600)^2 \times 0.12 = 360000 \times 0.12 = 43200 \text{ W} = 43.2 \text{ kW}$$

Including power factor:

$$P_{real} = V I \cos\phi = 43.2 \times 0.9 = 38.88 \text{ kW}$$

**P_real ≈ 38.9 kW** (3 marks)

---

**(ii) Heat Input per Unit Length of Weld (4 marks):**

Net thermal power available at weld: $P_{thermal} = \eta \times P_{real}$

$$P_{thermal} = 0.85 \times 38880 = 33048 \text{ W}$$

Heat input per unit length:

$$H = \dfrac{P_{thermal}}{v} = \dfrac{33048}{0.5} = 66096 \text{ J/m} \approx 66.1 \text{ kJ/m}$$

Converting to kJ/mm: $H \approx 0.066$ kJ/mm

**H ≈ 66.1 kJ/m (or 0.066 kJ/mm)** (4 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** In skin-depth problems, students frequently forget to convert $\rho$ from μΩ·cm to Ω·m (factor of $10^{-8}$). One slip can yield δ wrong by **100×**. Always write out units explicitly.
> 2. **Mixing Skin Effect and Proximity Effect:** A very common mistake is to claim that skin effect alone is sufficient for HFW. Examiners specifically test whether you understand that **both effects work in tandem** — skin confines current to the surface, proximity directs it to the V-notch.
> 3. **Power Factor vs Resistance Loss:** In heat-balance problems, students often forget the $\cos\phi$ correction. Real power is $VI\cos\phi$, not $VI$. A 0.9 power factor means a 10 % over-estimation of weld power if missed.
> 4. **Forgetting the Impeder:** Whenever you draw or describe an HFRW setup, always include the impeder. Its omission costs 2–3 marks outright.
> 5. **Welded vs Cold-formed Pipes:** Examiners sometimes ask why HFW pipes are superior to cold-formed + furnace-welded (FW) pipes. Remember: HFW has **no filler metal, narrow HAZ, fine-grain forged structure**, and weld strength exceeding the parent metal.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST — HFW (Module 2)**

- [ ] HFW = continuous fusion welding using **100–500 kHz** AC current.
- [ ] Two main types: **HFRW (contact)** and **HFIW (induction)**.
- [ ] Two electromagnetic effects: **Skin Effect** (confinement to surface) and **Proximity Effect** (concentration at V-notch).
- [ ] **Skin Depth formula:** $\delta = 5030\sqrt{\rho/(\mu_r f)}$ in mm — inversely proportional to $\sqrt{f}$.
- [ ] For mild steel at 400 kHz, δ ≈ **0.3 mm** — explains sub-millimetre heat zone.
- [ ] **Impeder** = ferrite flux concentrator placed *inside* tube at V-notch — prevents internal magnetic flux, forces current to outer surface.
- [ ] Welding speeds: 30–600 m/min; power density: $10^5$–$10^6$ W/cm².
- [ ] **Heat input per unit length:** $H = \eta P / v$ in J/m.
- [ ] Process sequence: Uncoil → Level → Mill edges → Form → Impeder → HF contacts → Squeeze → Scarf → Anneal → Cool → Cut.
- [ ] Weld is **solid-state forged**, no filler metal added.
- [ ] Upset pressure: 40–90 MPa.
- [ ] **Efficiency** of HF process: 85–92 %, far higher than 50 Hz ERW.
- [ ] Applications: Bilge, ballast, fuel, fire-main, exhaust, vent, structural tubes — **> 70 %** of all shipyard steel pipes.
- [ ] Key advantage: weld strength > parent metal; narrow HAZ; high speed; low distortion.
- [ ] HFIW preferred for **stainless steel** (no contact contamination).
- [ ] HFRW preferred for **carbon steel high-volume** shipyard pipe production.
- [ ] Pipeline diameter range in shipbuilding: 6 mm to 660 mm.
- [ ] Frequency choice depends on wall thickness: **thicker wall → higher frequency**.
- [ ] For 14-mark KTU questions, always draw a labelled V-notch and impeder schematic.
- [ ] Always state numerical answers with **units** — marks are lost if units are missing.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solid State Welding: Principle and Welding Arrangements
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST831 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Solid State Welding: Principle and Welding Arrangements

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solid State Welding (SSW)** is a category of joining processes in which two metal pieces are bonded together **below their melting point** (typically below 0.8 $T_m$ where $T_m$ is the melting temperature in Kelvin) by applying **pressure alone** or a **combination of heat and pressure**, without the formation of a liquid fusion zone. The bond is achieved through atomic diffusion, plastic deformation, or mechanical interlocking at the faying surfaces.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** (Module 2 of OENST831), Solid State Welding encompasses processes where the base metal never reaches the molten state, distinguishing it fundamentally from fusion welding processes like SMAW, GTAW, or GMAW. The metallurgical bond forms in the **solid phase** through one or more of the following mechanisms:

- **Plastic deformation** under compressive load (e.g., forge welding, cold welding)
- **Frictional heat generation** at the interface (e.g., friction welding)
- **Atomic diffusion** across the interface under elevated temperature and pressure (e.g., diffusion bonding)
- **Mechanical interlocking** through explosive shock (e.g., explosion welding)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Two Ice Cubes on a Frosty Morning"**
> Imagine pressing two ice cubes together firmly on a freezing winter morning. They don't melt, yet they fuse into a single block. The pressure causes the surface molecules to rearrange and "stick" together. Solid state welding works on a similar principle — we use controlled pressure (and sometimes modest heat, well below melting) to make metal atoms at the interface **share their electron clouds** and form a metallic bond, just as the ice cubes do at the molecular level.

**Another Analogy — "Dough Kneading"**: When a baker kneads dough, the dough heats up slightly and becomes plastic. Folding and pressing it causes the layers to merge without any melting. Friction welding replicates this — the rubbing interface generates frictional heat, softens the metal locally, and pressure forges the pieces together.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Recrystallization temperature** of steel: $T_{rec} \approx 0.4 \, T_m$ **(in Kelvin)**
- **Typical forging temperature** for hot forge welding: **0.6 – 0.8 $T_m$**
- **Coefficient of friction** (μ) at welding interface: **0.3 – 0.7** for steel-on-steel at high temperature
- **Typical upset pressure** in friction welding: **50 – 200 MPa** for carbon steels
- **Threshold strain for cold welding**: **> 40%** localized reduction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Highlight:** Solid state welding is critical in shipbuilding for joining **dissimilar metals** (e.g., aluminium-to-steel transition joints in superstructures), **pipelines** (girth welds on cargo piping), and **propeller shaft assemblies** where fusion welding would degrade the metallurgical properties.

### 1.4 Classification of Solid State Welding Processes (KTU Syllabus Mapping)

| SSW Process | Heat Source | Pressure Source | Typical Application in Ships |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Forge Welding** | External (furnace) | Hydraulic hammer/press | Historical — bulk plate edges |
| **Friction Welding (FW)** | Mechanical friction | Axial upset | Propeller shaft, anchor shackles |
| **Friction Stir Welding (FSW)** | Frictional + adiabatic | Tool shoulder | Aluminium deck panels, hull plates |
| **Cold Welding** | None (ambient) | High compression | Aluminium wire/cable terminations |
| **Explosion Welding** | Shock wave energy | Detonation pressure | Aluminium-to-steel transition liners |
| **Diffusion Bonding (DB)** | External (vacuum furnace) | Hydraulic press | Heat exchanger plates, turbine components |
| **Ultrasonic Welding** | High-frequency vibration | Acoustic pressure | Thin sheet lap joints, electrical contacts |
| **Roll Welding** | None (cold) | Rolling mill | Cladding plates, bimetallic strips |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Principle — Why Solid State Welding Works

The underlying metallurgical principle rests on the **theory of metallic bonding** and **atomic diffusion**. When two clean metal surfaces are brought into intimate contact:

1. **Surface asperities** (microscopic peaks) deform plastically, increasing real contact area.
2. Atoms at the contact zone possess sufficient energy (thermal + mechanical) to migrate across the interface.
3. **Grain boundary migration** occurs across the original interface.
4. A **continuous metallic bond** forms with the same crystalline structure as the parent metal.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "5-Times Rule"**: For successful solid state welding, the surface roughness must be reduced by plastic flow to a value where the ratio of real contact area to apparent area approaches **unity**. This is achieved when the local strain exceeds approximately **40–60%** depending on the material.

### 2.2 Process-by-Process Operational Logic

#### 2.2.1 Friction Welding (FW) — Most Relevant to Shipbuilding

**Operational Steps:**

- **Step 1: Preparation** — End faces are machined flat and clean (oxide-free).
- **Step 2: Rotation & Contact** — One component rotates at high speed ($N$ in RPM); the other is held stationary and pressed axially.
- **Step 3: Heat Generation** — Friction at the interface generates heat:
- **Step 4: Upset Phase** — Axial pressure is increased; plastic metal extrudes as **flash** (upset).
- **Step 5: Forge Stop & Cooling** — Rotation stops, pressure maintained; joint cools under compression.

#### 2.2.2 Friction Stir Welding (FSW)

**Operational Steps:**

- **Step 1: Tool Insertion** — A rotating non-consumable tool with a **pin** and **shoulder** is plunged into the joint line.
- **Step 2: Traverse** — Tool moves along the joint; frictional heat (and adiabatic shear) plasticizes the metal.
- **Step 3: Material Transport** — Plasticized material is stirred from the **advancing side** to the **retreating side**.
- **Step 4: Consolidation** — Behind the tool, the metal consolidates under the shoulder pressure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** FSW produces **"onion ring" patterns** in the nugget — a tell-tale microstructural signature that examiners love to ask about!

#### 2.2.3 Explosion Welding

The detonation of an explosive charge placed on the **flyer plate** accelerates it towards the **parent plate** at velocities of **200–800 m/s**. The collision creates a **jetting action** that strips surface oxides and contaminants, exposing virgin metal that bonds under enormous instantaneous pressure.

#### 2.2.4 Diffusion Bonding

Performed in a **vacuum furnace** under controlled temperature (typically **0.5–0.8 $T_m$**) and pressure (**1–10 MPa**) for extended durations (**30 min – 4 hours**). Bond formation follows **Fick's laws of diffusion**:

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Formulas for Solid State Welding Problems (KTU Board Examinations)**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{rec} \approx 0.4 \, T_m$ | Recrystallization temperature | K |
| 2 | $T_{weld} \approx (0.6 \text{ to } 0.8) \, T_m$ | Solid state welding temperature | K |
| 3 | $P = 2 \pi N T$ | Friction welding power (where $N$ is rev/s, $T$ is torque) | W |
| 4 | $Q = \mu \, F \, v$ | Frictional heat generation rate | W |
| 5 | $v = \pi d N / 60$ | Surface velocity at interface | m/s |
| 6 | $Q_{gen} = \mu \, F \, \pi d N / 60$ | Heat generation in friction welding | W |
| 7 | $E_{weld} = \int P \, dt = 2\pi \int N T \, dt$ | Total energy input | J |
| 8 | $\Delta T = \dfrac{Q}{m \, c_p}$ | Temperature rise at interface | K |
| 9 | $J = -D \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}$ | Fick's first law (diffusion flux) | atoms/(m²·s) |
| 10 | $\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \, \dfrac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$ | Fick's second law | — |
| 11 | $D = D_0 \, \exp\left(-\dfrac{Q_d}{RT}\right)$ | Arrhenius diffusion coefficient | m²/s |
| 12 | $v_j = v_p \, \sin \beta$ | Jet velocity in explosion welding (β = collision angle) | m/s |
| 13 | $\sigma_{upset} = \dfrac{F_{axial}}{A_{cross}}$ | Upset pressure in friction welding | Pa |
| 14 | $L_{flash} = L_0 - L_f$ | Burn-off / flash length | mm |

> **Symbol Key:** $N$ = rotational speed, $T$ = torque, $\mu$ = coefficient of friction, $F$ = axial force, $d$ = interface diameter, $m$ = mass, $c_p$ = specific heat, $D$ = diffusion coefficient, $Q_d$ = activation energy, $R$ = gas constant, $\beta$ = collision angle, $v_p$ = plate velocity.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Shipbuilding

- **Propeller shafts** — Friction welding joins marine-grade bronze hubs to steel shafts (no HAZ cracking).
- **Hull-to-superstructure** — FSW joins aluminium superstructure panels to steel deck plates.
- **Pipe girth welds** — Friction welding produces defect-free joints for cargo pipelines (offshore rigs).
- **Bimetallic transition joints** — Explosion welding creates aluminium-steel transition pieces used in **LNG carrier construction**.
- **Heat exchangers** — Diffusion bonding manufactures compact plate-fin heat exchangers for shipboard HVAC systems.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Heat Generation in Rotary Friction Welding

**Problem Setup:** Two steel shafts of diameter $d = 50 \text{ mm}$ are joined by direct-drive friction welding. The rotational speed is $N = 1500 \text{ RPM}$, the axial force is $F = 60 \text{ kN}$, and the coefficient of friction is $\mu = 0.45$. Determine the heat generation rate at the interface.

**Given:**
- $d = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$
- $N = 1500 \text{ RPM}$
- $F = 60{,}000 \text{ N}$
- $\mu = 0.45$

**Step 1 — Convert rotational speed to revolutions per second:**

$$
N_{rps} = \frac{N}{60} = \frac{1500}{60} = 25 \text{ rev/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the surface velocity at the interface radius:**

For solid shafts, friction acts across the entire face. Using the mean radius approach, but for a uniform pressure distribution over the full face, we compute velocity at the outer radius first:

$$
v = \frac{\pi d N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.05 \times 1500}{60}
$$

$$
v = \frac{\pi \times 75}{60} = \frac{235.619}{60} = 3.927 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the frictional heat generation formula:**

$$
Q_{gen} = \mu \cdot F \cdot v
$$

$$
Q_{gen} = 0.45 \times 60{,}000 \times 3.927
$$

$$
Q_{gen} = 27{,}000 \times 3.927 = 106{,}029 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 4 — Express in kilowatts:**

$$
\boxed{Q_{gen} \approx 106.03 \text{ kW}}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** This power is sufficient to raise the temperature at the interface to forging range within **1–3 seconds**, making the process extremely fast and energy-efficient compared to fusion welding.

---
