### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Temperature Rise at Friction Interface

**Problem Setup:** Using the result above, calculate the temperature rise in 2 seconds if the heated mass (considering both workpieces) is $m = 0.5 \text{ kg}$ and $c_p = 500 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$ for steel.

**Step 1 — Total heat input in 2 seconds:**

$$
Q_{total} = Q_{gen} \times t = 106{,}029 \times 2 = 212{,}058 \text{ J}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the sensible heat equation:**

$$
\Delta T = \frac{Q_{total}}{m \cdot c_p}
$$

$$
\Delta T = \frac{212{,}058}{0.5 \times 500}
$$

$$
\Delta T = \frac{212{,}058}{250} = 848.23 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 3 — Add ambient (293 K) to get interface temperature:**

$$
T_{interface} = 293 + 848.23 \approx 1141 \text{ K} \approx 868 \text{ °C}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** For mild steel ($T_m \approx 1500 \text{ °C}$), this temperature represents $\approx 0.74 \, T_m$ — squarely in the hot forging range, confirming the validity of the solid-state mechanism.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Upset Pressure and Flash Geometry

**Problem Setup:** During friction welding, the axial upset force is $F_{upset} = 100 \text{ kN}$ applied over the cross-section $A = 1963.5 \text{ mm}^2$ (for $d = 50 \text{ mm}$). Compute the upset pressure.

**Step 1 — Area calculation:**

$$
A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.05)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.0025}{4}
$$

$$
A = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 = 1963.5 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Compute upset pressure:**

$$
\sigma_{upset} = \frac{F_{upset}}{A} = \frac{100{,}000}{1.9635 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
\sigma_{upset} = 50{,}929{,}366 \text{ Pa} \approx 50.93 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** An upset pressure of **~51 MPa** is typical for carbon steel friction welding, sufficient to extrude flash and forge-bond the interface.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Process Comparison Table for Exam Answers

| Criterion | Forge Welding | Friction Welding | FSW | Explosion Welding | Diffusion Bonding |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Heat source** | Furnace | Friction | Friction + adiabatic | Detonation | Furnace (vacuum) |
| **Temperature** | 0.7 $T_m$ | 0.7–0.9 $T_m$ | 0.6–0.8 $T_m$ | Localized adiabatic | 0.5–0.8 $T_m$ |
| **Time** | Minutes | Seconds | Minutes | Microseconds | Hours |
| **Joint strength** | Parent metal | Parent metal | Parent metal (fine grain) | Parent metal | Parent metal |
| **Dissimilar metals** | Limited | Yes | Limited | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Shipbuilding use** | Historical | Propeller shafts | Hull panels | Transition joints | Heat exchangers |
| **Equipment cost** | Low | Medium | High | High (specialized) | Very high |

---

### 3.5 Derivation 4 — Diffusion Bonding Time Estimate

**Problem Setup:** Two titanium plates are diffusion-bonded at $T = 850 \text{ °C} = 1123 \text{ K}$ with diffusion coefficient $D = 10^{-14} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$. Estimate the time required for surface asperities (height $h = 10 \text{ μm}$) to be eliminated by diffusion.

**Step 1 — Relate diffusion distance to time using Einstein's relation:**

$$
h \approx \sqrt{D \cdot t}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for time:**

$$
t = \frac{h^2}{D} = \frac{(10 \times 10^{-6})^2}{10^{-14}}
$$

$$
t = \frac{100 \times 10^{-12}}{10^{-14}} = \frac{10^{-10}}{10^{-14}} = 10^4 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to hours:**

$$
\boxed{t = 10{,}000 \text{ s} \approx 2.78 \text{ hours}}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** This is why diffusion bonding is reserved for **high-value components** where 2–4 hours of furnace time is economically justified.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Friction Welding Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Workpiece 1 - Stationary] --> C[Clean Machined Faying Surfaces]
    B[Workpiece 2 - Rotating] --> C
    C --> D[Axial Contact - Friction Phase]
    D --> E[Heat Generation at Interface]
    E --> F[Interface Plasticizes]
    F --> G[Upset Force Applied]
    G --> H[Flash Extrudes as Upset Metal]
    H --> I[Rotation Stops - Forge Phase]
    I --> J[Cool Under Pressure]
    J --> K[Forged Solid State Joint]

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style F fill:#FF4500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style G fill:#DC143C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style H fill:#8B0000,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style I fill:#4682B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style J fill:#5F9EA0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style K fill:#228B22,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

### 4.2 Friction Stir Welding — Tool Geometry and Material Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Pin Tool - Rotating] --> TS[Shoulder Contact]
    TS --> AS[Advancing Side]
    TS --> RS[Retreating Side]
    AS --> ST[Stirred Plasticized Material]
    ST --> RS
    RS --> CZ[Consolidated Nugget Zone]
    CZ --> OR[Onion Ring Microstructure]
    OR --> FW[Final Solid State Weld]

    style P fill:#4682B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style TS fill:#FF8C00,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style AS fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style RS fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style ST fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style CZ fill:#228B22,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style OR fill:#8A2BE2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style FW fill:#006400,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

### 4.3 Explosion Welding — Jetting Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    EXP[Explosive Charge Layer] --> FLY[Flyer Plate Accelerated]
    FLY --> JET[Reentrant Jet Formation]
    JET --> OX[Oxide Stripped from Surfaces]
    OX --> VAC[Virgin Metal Contact]
    VAC --> WAVE[Sinusoidal Wavy Interface]
    WAVE --> BOND[Metallurgical Bond at Interface]

    style EXP fill:#8B0000,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style FLY fill:#FF4500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style JET fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style OX fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style VAC fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style WAVE fill:#4682B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style BOND fill:#006400,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

### 4.4 Shipbuilding Application Map — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph HULL[Hull Structure]
        H1[Steel Hull Plates] --> FSW1[Friction Stir Welded Seams]
        FSW1 --> H2[Watertight Hull Assembly]
    end

    subgraph SUPR[Superstructure]
        S1[Aluminium Panels] --> FSW2[FSW to Steel Deck]
        FSW2 --> S2[Lightweight Superstructure]
    end

    subgraph PROP[Propulsion System]
        P1[Steel Shaft] --> FW1[Friction Welded Bronze Hub]
        FW1 --> P2[Propeller Assembly]
    end

    subgraph PIPE[Piping Systems]
        PI1[Cargo Pipe Sections] --> FW2[Friction Welded Girth Joints]
        FW2 --> PI2[Cargo Pipeline Network]
    end

    subgraph LNG[LNG Carrier Special]
        L1[Aluminium Liner] --> EW1[Explosion Welded Transition]
        EW1 --> L2[Steel Outer Hull]
    end

    style HULL fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style SUPR fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style PROP fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style PIPE fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style LNG fill:#F0FFF0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.5 Solid State Welding Process Selection Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q{Material Thickness?} -->|Thin Sheet| R{Clean Surface Required?}
    Q -->|Medium Shaft| S[Dissimilar Metals?]
    Q -->|Thick Plate| T[Long Welding Length?]

    R -->|Yes Electrical| UW[Ultrasonic Welding]
    R -->|No Cladding| RW[Roll Welding]

    S -->|Yes| FW[Friction Welding]
    S -->|No| FW2[Friction Welding]

    T -->|Yes Aluminium| FSW3[Friction Stir Welding]
    T -->|Yes Al-Steel| EW2[Explosion Welding]
    T -->|No Small Parts| DB[Diffusion Bonding]

    style Q fill:#4682B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style R fill:#4682B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style S fill:#4682B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style T fill:#4682B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style UW fill:#228B22,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style RW fill:#228B22,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style FW fill:#228B22,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style FW2 fill:#228B22,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style FSW3 fill:#228B22,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style EW2 fill:#228B22,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style DB fill:#228B22,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
**"Define solid state welding. List any four solid state welding processes with one shipbuilding application each."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Solid state welding is a joining process in which two metals are bonded below their melting point by the simultaneous application of pressure and (sometimes) heat, without any fusion (liquid phase). The bond forms through plastic deformation, diffusion, or mechanical interlocking at the faying surfaces.

**Four processes and shipbuilding applications:**

| Process | Shipbuilding Application |
|---|---|
| Friction Welding | Propeller shaft to bronze hub joint |
| Friction Stir Welding (FSW) | Aluminium deck-to-superstructure panels |
| Explosion Welding | Aluminium-to-steel transition joints (LNG carriers) |
| Diffusion Bonding | Plate-fin heat exchangers for shipboard HVAC |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]**
**"Explain why friction welding is preferred over arc welding for joining propeller shafts in ships."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

Friction welding is preferred for propeller shafts because:

1. **No HAZ defects** — Being a solid-state process, it avoids Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ) cracking, which is critical for fatigue-loaded shafts.
2. **Dissimilar metal joining** — Easily joins steel shafts to bronze/cast iron hubs that fusion welding struggles with.
3. **Superior fatigue strength** — The fine-grained forged microstructure has higher endurance limit than fusion welds.
4. **Automation & repeatability** — Process is fully mechanized, ideal for shipyards producing multiple shafts.
5. **No filler material** — Eliminates filler metal incompatibility issues at dissimilar metal interfaces.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **principle, working, and process parameters of rotary friction welding**. Discuss its **advantages and limitations** for shipbuilding applications. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Two mild steel tubes of outer diameter 40 mm are joined by friction welding at $N = 1200 \text{ RPM}$, axial force $F = 40 \text{ kN}$, and $\mu = 0.5$. The friction phase lasts 3 seconds. Compute:
- (i) The heat generation rate in watts.
- (ii) The total energy supplied to the joint.
- (iii) The temperature rise if the mass involved is 0.3 kg and $c_p = 480 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.A (a):**

**Principle:** Rotary friction welding joins two workpieces by converting mechanical friction into heat at the interface. When one component is rotated against a stationary component under axial pressure, frictional heat raises the interface temperature into the plastic range without melting. The subsequent application of higher upset pressure forges the parts together.

**Working Sequence:**
1. **Preparation** — End faces are machined square and degreased.
2. **Friction phase** — Rotating component is brought against the stationary part at a controlled pressure (friction pressure); flash begins to form.
3. **Upset (forge) phase** — Rotation stops; higher forge pressure consolidates the joint and extrudes more flash.
4. **Cooling** — Joint cools under held pressure to ambient.

**Process Parameters:**
- Rotational speed $N$ (RPM)
- Axial pressure $P_1$ (friction), $P_2$ (upset)
- Friction time $t_f$
- Burn-off length $L_{flash}$

**Advantages:**
- Excellent for dissimilar metals (steel-bronze, aluminium-copper).
- No filler, no shielding gas.
- Short cycle time.
- Consistent, defect-free joints.
- No HAZ — superior fatigue performance.

**Limitations:**
- Limited to **circular cross-sections** (or specially designed interfaces).
- High initial equipment cost.
- Requires precise surface preparation.
- One workpiece must be rotatable.

**Shipbuilding applications:** Propeller shafts, valve stems, anchor shackles, pipe girth welds.

---

**Model Solution to Q.A (b):**

**Given:** $d = 40 \text{ mm} = 0.04 \text{ m}$, $N = 1200 \text{ RPM}$, $F = 40{,}000 \text{ N}$, $\mu = 0.5$, $t = 3 \text{ s}$, $m = 0.3 \text{ kg}$, $c_p = 480 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$.

**(i) Heat generation rate:**

Surface velocity:
$$
v = \frac{\pi d N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.04 \times 1200}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 48}{60} = \frac{150.796}{60}
$$

$$
v = 2.513 \text{ m/s}
$$

Heat generation rate:
$$
Q_{gen} = \mu \cdot F \cdot v = 0.5 \times 40{,}000 \times 2.513
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_{gen} = 50{,}265 \text{ W} \approx 50.27 \text{ kW}}
$$

**(ii) Total energy supplied:**

$$
E_{total} = Q_{gen} \times t = 50{,}265 \times 3
$$

$$
\boxed{E_{total} = 150{,}796 \text{ J} \approx 150.8 \text{ kJ}}
$$

**(iii) Temperature rise:**

$$
\Delta T = \frac{E_{total}}{m \cdot c_p} = \frac{150{,}796}{0.3 \times 480}
$$

$$
\Delta T = \frac{150{,}796}{144}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta T = 1047.2 \text{ K} \approx 1047 \text{ °C}}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The interface reaches $\approx 1340 \text{ K}$ (with ambient 293 K), well within the forging range for mild steel ($T_m \approx 1500 \text{ °C}$). This confirms the process operates strictly in the **solid state**.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain **Friction Stir Welding (FSW)** in detail. With a sketch, describe the **tool geometry** and the **microstructural zones** formed in the weld nugget. Mention **two shipbuilding applications**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the relationship between **flash length, upset pressure, and axial force** during the upset phase of friction welding. A friction weld is made on a 30 mm diameter steel bar with a final upset force of 80 kN and a measured flash length of 8 mm. Compute the **upset pressure** and comment on its adequacy. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.B (a):**

**FSW Principle:** Friction Stir Welding uses a non-consumable rotating tool consisting of a **shoulder** and a **pin (probe)**. The tool is plunged into the joint line and traversed along it. Frictional heat (from shoulder) and adiabatic shear (from pin) plasticize the metal without melting. The pin mechanically stirs the plasticized material from the **advancing side** to the **retreating side**, where it consolidates to form a fine-grained solid-state joint.

**Tool Geometry:**
- **Shoulder** — Larger diameter disc that contacts the top surface; generates most of the heat and confines the plasticized zone.
- **Pin (Probe)** — Extends below the shoulder into the joint line; provides stirring action and ensures through-thickness deformation.
- Pin shapes include **cylindrical, threaded, conical, and triflute**.

**Microstructural Zones:**
1. **Nugget (Stir Zone, SZ)** — Fine, equiaxed, recrystallized grains; exhibits the characteristic **onion-ring pattern**.
2. **Thermo-Mechanically Affected Zone (TMAZ)** — Deformed but not recrystallized grains.
3. **Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ)** — No deformation, only thermal exposure.
4. **Base Metal (BM)** — Unaffected parent material.

**Shipbuilding applications:**
- Welding **aluminium alloy deck panels** for naval vessels.
- Joining **aluminium superstructure sections** to reduce topside weight.

---

**Model Solution to Q.B (b):**

**Derivation:**

The upset pressure during the forging phase is the axial force distributed over the cross-sectional area at the interface:

$$
\sigma_{upset} = \frac{F_{axial}}{A}
$$

For a solid round bar:
$$
A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4}
$$

Therefore:
$$
\sigma_{upset} = \frac{F_{axial}}{\frac{\pi d^2}{4}} = \frac{4 F_{axial}}{\pi d^2}
$$

The flash length $L_{flash}$ is related to the volumetric upset as:
$$
L_{flash} = L_0 - L_f = \frac{\text{Volume of upset material}}{A_{flash}}
$$

For a quality joint, the flash length is typically **3–8 mm** for carbon steel.

**Numerical Computation:**

Given: $d = 30 \text{ mm} = 0.03 \text{ m}$, $F_{upset} = 80{,}000 \text{ N}$, $L_{flash} = 8 \text{ mm}$.

**Cross-sectional area:**
$$
A = \frac{\pi (0.03)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 9 \times 10^{-4}}{4} = 7.0686 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Upset pressure:**
$$
\sigma_{upset} = \frac{80{,}000}{7.0686 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
\sigma_{upset} = 1.1317 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa} \approx 113.17 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Comment on Adequacy:**
For carbon steel friction welding, the recommended upset pressure range is **80–200 MPa**. The computed value of **113.17 MPa** falls comfortably within this range, indicating an **adequate forging pressure** for producing a defect-free, fully consolidated weld. The flash length of 8 mm also indicates good plastic flow and oxide expulsion, confirming the weld is sound.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks (KTU Board Examiner Observations):**
>
> 1. **Confusing fusion welding with solid state welding** — Do NOT mention arc, filler metal, or shielding gas in SSW answers. Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** if you do.
> 2. **Wrong temperature regime** — Always quote temperatures as a **fraction of $T_m$** (e.g., 0.7 $T_m$) and clearly state "below melting point." Vague answers like "high temperature" lose marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the flash mechanism** — In friction welding derivations, forgetting to compute **surface velocity $v$** first leads to cascading errors. Always show $v = \pi d N / 60$ as Step 1.
> 4. **Mixing up onion ring vs. dendrites** — FSW produces **onion rings (recrystallized grains)**, NOT dendritic solidification structures (that's fusion welding).
> 5. **Not mentioning dissimilar metal capability** — Friction welding's biggest advantage is **dissimilar metal joining**. Examiners reward this insight in shipbuilding applications.
> 6. **Units inconsistency** — Keep SI units throughout (N, m, s, J, K, Pa). Mixing mm and m is a common, costly error.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Solid State Welding (Module 2)**

**Core Concepts (Must Know):**

- ☐ Solid state welding = bonding **below $T_m$** with **pressure ± heat**, **no fusion**.
- ☐ Operating temperature regime: **0.4 – 0.8 $T_m$** (in Kelvin).
- ☐ Four bonding mechanisms: **plastic deformation, friction, diffusion, mechanical interlocking**.
- ☐ Friction welding produces **flash (upset metal)** — visible sign of quality weld.

**Key Formulas (Memorize):**

- ☐ $v = \pi d N / 60$ — surface velocity
- ☐ $Q_{gen} = \mu F v$ — friction heat rate
- ☐ $E_{total} = Q_{gen} \times t$ — total energy
- ☐ $\Delta T = E / (m c_p)$ — interface temperature rise
- ☐ $\sigma_{upset} = 4F / (\pi d^2)$ — upset pressure
- ☐ $h \approx \sqrt{Dt}$ — diffusion bonding depth-time relation

**Processes (Know at least 4 in detail):**

- ☐ **Forge Welding** — old, furnace-heated, hammer-forged.
- ☐ **Friction Welding** — rotary, axial upset, propeller shafts.
- ☐ **Friction Stir Welding (FSW)** — tool with pin + shoulder, "onion ring" nugget.
- ☐ **Cold Welding** — room temperature, high pressure, used for aluminium wires.
- ☐ **Explosion Welding** — flyer plate + detonation, dissimilar metal transition joints.
- ☐ **Diffusion Bonding** — vacuum furnace, hours, heat exchangers.
- ☐ **Ultrasonic Welding** — high-frequency vibration, thin sheets.
- ☐ **Roll Welding** — rolling mill pressure, cladding.

**Shipbuilding Applications (Always link to syllabus!):**

- ☐ Propeller shafts → Friction Welding
- ☐ Aluminium deck/superstructure → FSW
- ☐ Cargo pipe girth welds → Friction Welding
- ☐ LNG transition liners → Explosion Welding
- ☐ Heat exchangers → Diffusion Bonding

**Exam-Ready Phrases (Use in answers for bonus marks):**

- ☐ "Solid state welding operates in the **0.6–0.8 $T_m$** regime, below the melting point..."
- ☐ "The bond forms through **grain boundary migration** across the interface..."
- ☐ "Friction welding produces a **flash** which is a metallurgical signature of a sound joint..."
- ☐ "FSW yields a **fine-grained, recrystallized nugget** with onion-ring morphology..."
- ☐ "Explosion welding exploits the **jetting phenomenon** to strip surface oxides..."

**Last-Minute Mnemonics:**

- **"P-F-D-M"** → **P**lastic deformation, **F**riction, **D**iffusion, **M**echanical interlocking (4 bonding mechanisms).
- **"FUSE-DAC"** → **F**orge, **U**ltrasonic, **S**olid-state (**F**riction, **FSW**, **E**xplosion), **D**iffusion, **A**luminium-relevant, **C**old — to recall processes.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advantages and Disadvantages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OENST831 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Advantages and Disadvantages # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **shipbuilding engineering** (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 2), the term **"Joining Techniques"** refers to the comprehensive set of metallurgical, mechanical, thermal, and adhesive processes employed to permanently or semi-permanently unite two or more metallic (and occasionally non-metallic) components of a vessel's hull, superstructure, machinery foundations, piping, and outfitting elements into a single, structurally continuous assembly. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** Joining techniques in shipbuilding are broadly classified into **Fusion Welding Processes**, **Solid-State Welding Processes**, **Mechanical Fastening**, and **Adhesive/Soldering-Brazing** families — each possessing a unique signature of advantages and limitations that govern their selection aboard the building berth. The comparative analysis of **advantages and disadvantages** forms the backbone of **process selection in shipyards**, where engineers must weigh factors such as positional capability (welding overhead, vertically, or horizontally), productivity rates (kg of weld metal deposited per hour), metallurgical compatibility (matching filler metal to base metal), distortion control, weld quality (radiographic/UT acceptance per IACS standards), operator skill requirements, capital investment, and operating cost. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Shipyard as a Tailor's Workshop Imagine you are a **master tailor stitching a giant steel garment** — the ship. Different fabrics (mild steel plates, high-tensile steel, stainless steel, aluminium superstructures, copper-nickel piping) require different needles, threads, and stitching techniques: - A **cotton shirt** (thin plate) needs a fine, precise stitch → **TIG/GTAW** (Gas Tungsten Arc Welding). - A **denim jacket** (thick plate) needs a strong, fast stitch → **SAW** (Submerged Arc Welding). - A **leather belt** (high-strength hull) needs rivets punched through → **Riveting** (traditional method). - A **waterproof raincoat** (stainless piping) needs careful sealing → **Brazing** or **Soldering**. Just as a tailor picks the right method based on the fabric, shipyard planners select joining techniques based on **material thickness, position, strength, environment, and cost**. The **advantages** are the reasons to pick a method; the **disadvantages** are the warnings that tell you when NOT to use it. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics in Joining Technology The following engineering parameters are universally used to quantify the merit of any joining process in shipbuilding: | Metric | Symbol | Typical Unit | |--------|--------|--------------| | Heat input | $H$ | **kJ/mm** | | Deposition rate | $R_d$ | **kg/h** | | Welding speed | $v$ | **mm/min** | | Distortion | $\delta$ | **mm** | | Penetration depth | $p$ | **mm** | | Joint efficiency | $\eta_j$ | **%** | > [!NOTE] > **Standard Reference:** Heat input $H$ is conventionally limited to **$\le 3.5 \text{ kJ/mm}$** for higher-strength shipbuilding steels (per IACS UR W11 / W13) to preserve Charpy impact toughness in the HAZ (Heat Affected Zone). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Five Major Joining Process Families in Shipbuilding The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies shipbuilding joining techniques into **five primary families**. The advantages and disadvantages of each are discussed below. --- ### 2.1.1 Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW) — "Stick Electrode Welding" **Process Logic:** An electric arc is struck between a consumable flux-coated electrode and the base metal. The flux coating decomposes to generate a shielding gas and a molten slag that protects the weld pool from atmospheric contamination. **Advantages:** - Extremely **portable** and rugged equipment — ideal for shipyard outdoor conditions. - **Lowest capital cost** of all arc welding processes. - Operates effectively in **all positions** (1G, 2F, 3G, 4F, 5G, 6G as per ASME Section IX). - Tolerant to **dirty, rusty, or painted base metal** (in low-hydrogen applications). - Wide range of electrode types available (cellulosic, basic, rutile, iron powder). **Disadvantages:** - **Low deposition rate** ($\approx 1$ to $3 \text{ kg/h}$) — slow for thick plate work. - Frequent **electrode changing** reduces arc time efficiency. - Generates **substantial smoke and fume** — poor ergonomics. - Slag must be **chipped off** between passes (additional labour). - **Limited to short weld lengths**; not suited for long, automated seams. - Hydrogen cracking risk with cellulosic electrodes on thicker steels. --- ### 2.1.2 Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW / MIG-MAG) **Process Logic:** A continuous solid wire electrode is fed through a gun while an externally supplied shielding gas (Ar, CO₂, or Ar/CO₂ mix) protects the weld pool. **Advantages:** - **High deposition rate** ($3$ to $8 \text{ kg/h}$). - **Continuous wire feed** eliminates stop-start interruptions. - Produces **slag-free welds** (in pure gas shielding) — no chipping needed. - Excellent for **semi-automatic and robotic** shipyard automation. - Good **bead appearance** and smooth profile. - Suitable for **aluminium and stainless steel** superstructures. **Disadvantages:** - Equipment is **more expensive** than SMAW (wire feeder + gas cylinder). - **Wind-sensitive** — outdoor shipyard drafts can disperse shielding gas (gust limit typically $< 6 \text{ km/h}$). - Requires **clean base metal** (oil, rust, mill scale reduce quality). - More complex **parameter tuning** (voltage, current, wire feed speed, gas flow). - Spatter generation in CO₂ shielding mode. --- ### 2.1.3 Flux-Cored Arc Welding (FCAW) **Process Logic:** A continuous tubular electrode filled with flux replaces the solid wire of GMAW. The flux may be self-shielded (no external gas) or gas-shielded (external CO₂/Ar). **Advantages:** - **Highest deposition rate** among semi-automatic processes ($4$ to $12 \text{ kg/h}$). - **Self-shielded variant** works in windy outdoor conditions — perfect for shipbuilding. - Excellent **mechanical properties** in weld metal (high toughness). - Deep **penetration** — fewer passes for thick plate. - Good productivity for **longitudinal and circumferential** hull seams. **Disadvantages:** - Produces **slag** that must be removed. - **Smoke and fume** generation higher than solid wire GMAW. - Filler wire is **more expensive** than solid wire. - **Limited positional capability** of self-shielded variants (mostly 1G, 2G). - Flux must be kept dry (hydrogen cracking risk). --- ### 2.1.4 Submerged Arc Welding (SAW) **Process Logic:** The arc is submerged under a granular flux bed. The flux completely covers the arc, preventing spatter, fume, and radiation losses. **Advantages:** - **Highest deposition rate** ($10$ to $30 \text{ kg/h}$ with multi-wire setups). - **Excellent weld quality** with low defect rates. - **Minimal fume, spatter, and UV radiation** — best operator comfort. - Very **deep penetration** — single-pass welding of plates up to **25 mm** thick. - Highly amenable to **mechanisation and automation** (panel line production). - Low hydrogen content in weld metal → **crack-resistant**. **Disadvantages:** - **Limited to flat (1G) and horizontal (2G) positions** only. - Requires **substantial flux handling** systems (recovery, recycling). - High **capital investment** in mechanised heads and flux systems. - **Plate preparation** (square groove, backing) is critical. - Not suitable for thin sheets or field assembly (no portability). --- ### 2.1.5 Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW / TIG) **Process Logic:** A non-consumable tungsten electrode produces the arc; filler metal is added separately by hand. Inert gas (Argon/Helium) shields the weld. **Advantages:** - **Superior weld quality** — cleanest, most precise process. - **Excellent for root passes** in critical pipe and pressure-vessel welds. - Welds **stainless steel, aluminium, copper-nickel, titanium** with precision. - **No spatter, no slag**, minimal distortion. - Full positional capability (1G to 6G). **Disadvantages:** - **Lowest deposition rate** ($0.5$ to $2 \text{ kg/h}$) — very slow. - **Highest skill requirement** — operator must coordinate two hands (torch + filler). - Expensive **inert gas** consumption. - Not economical for thick plate joining. - Sensitive to **electrode contamination** (touching tungsten to pool ruins weld). --- ### 2.1.6 Resistance Welding (Spot, Seam, Projection) **Process Logic:** Heat is generated by passing high current (typically $5000$ to $20000 \text{ A}$) through the joint under mechanical pressure. No filler metal is used. **Advantages:** - **Extremely fast** — each spot weld in $< 0.5 \text{ s}$. - Easily **automated** for thin-sheet fabrication (decks, bulkheads, panelling). - **No filler metal, no shielding gas** required. - Reproducible quality via precise electronic control. **Disadvantages:** - **Limited to thin sheets** (typically $\le 3 \text{ mm}$ per sheet). - Requires **expensive heavy machinery** (transformers, electrode cooling). - Not suitable for **structural primary hull** welds. - Electrode wear and maintenance costs. --- ### 2.1.7 Friction Stir Welding (FSW) — Modern Solid-State Process **Process Logic:** A non-consumable rotating tool with a profiled pin is plunged into the joint line. Frictional heat softens (but does not melt) the material, and the tool's shoulder consolidates the joint. **Advantages:** - **Solid-state** — no melting → no porosity, no solidification cracking. - **Excellent for aluminium alloys** (marine superstructures, decks). - **Low distortion** compared to fusion welding. - Superior mechanical properties (high joint efficiency, fine grain). - Environmentally clean (no fume, spatter, shielding gas). **Disadvantages:** - Requires **heavy clamping** and rigid backing. - **Keyhole** at tool exit must be managed. - Limited to **butt and lap joint** geometries. - Tool wear — expensive pin materials (tool steel, WC, PCBN). - Slow traverse speeds for thick sections. --- ### 2.1.8 Mechanical Fastening — Riveting **Process Logic:** Holes are drilled (or punched hot) through overlapping plates, and a heated or cold rivet is inserted and deformed to lock the joint. **Advantages:** - **No heat input** → no HAZ, no distortion, no metallurgical changes. - Excellent **fatigue resistance** in mild steel. - Easily inspectable (visual + tap-test). - Long service history (pre-1950s shipbuilding). **Disadvantages:** - **Labour-intensive** and slow. - **Adds weight** to the structure. - Requires **skilled riveting crews**. - Pre-drilled holes are **stress concentrators**. - Largely **obsolete** for primary hulls in modern shipyards. --- ### 2.1.9 Adhesive Bonding & Brazing/Soldering **Adhesive Bonding Advantages:** Uniform stress distribution, joins dissimilar materials, no heat. **Adhesive Bonding Disadvantages:** Surface prep critical, temperature limits, non-destructive inspection difficult. **Brazing Advantages:** Lower temperature than welding, joins dissimilar metals, clean joint. **Brazing Disadvantages:** Lower joint strength, filler metal cost, flux residue. --- ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Selection Matrix | # | Process | Heat Input $H$ (kJ/mm) | Deposition Rate $R_d$ (kg/h) | Position Capability | Best Use in Ship | |---|---------|-----------------------|------------------------------|---------------------|------------------| | 1 | SMAW | 0.8 – 2.5 | 1 – 3 | All positions | Field erection, repairs | | 2 | GMAW | 0.6 – 2.0 | 3 – 8 | All positions | Aluminium superstructure | | 3 | FCAW | 1.0 – 3.0 | 4 – 12 | All (gas), Flat (self) | Hull panel seams | | 4 | SAW | 1.5 – 5.0 | 10 – 30 | Flat/Horizontal | Panel line fabrication | | 5 | GTAW | 0.4 – 1.5 | 0.5 – 2 | All positions | Root pass, pipe, stainless | | 6 | FSW | n/a (solid-state) | 1 – 5 | Butt only | Aluminium decks, bulkheads | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** The heat input formula is $\displaystyle H = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \times 1000}$ where $\eta$ is process efficiency ($\approx 0.8$ for arc welding), $V$ is voltage in volts, $I$ is current in amperes, and $v$ is travel speed in **mm/s**. Mastering this formula is essential for any numerical question. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility In a modern Korean or Japanese shipyard (e.g., Hyundai Heavy Industries, Samsung Heavy Industries), the joining method selection follows a clear workflow: 1. **Hull panel fabrication (berth-side):** **SAW + FCAW** (high productivity in flat position). 2. **Block assembly (erection):** **FCAW + SMAW** (positional flexibility). 3. **Pipe spool welding:** **GTAW root + SMAW/FCAW fill + cap**. 4. **Stainless/ Aluminium outfitting:** **GTAW + GMAW**. 5. **Aluminium superstructures:** **FSW or GMAW**. > [!IMPORTANT] > The advantages-disadvantages analysis is therefore NOT merely academic — it directly drives the **production planning, cost estimation, and quality assurance** strategy of every shipyard worldwide. # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Comparative Decision Matrix ## 3.1 Heat Input Calculation — Worked Analytical Example A shipyard is welding a **20 mm thick EH36 grade hull plate** using **FCAW** in the flat (1G) position. The welding parameters recorded are: - Voltage: $V = 28 \text{ V}$ - Current: $I = 320 \text{ A}$ - Travel speed: $v = 6 \text{ mm/s}$ - Process thermal efficiency: $\eta = 0.85$ **Step 1 — Identify the governing equation for heat input:** $$ H = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \times 1000} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values into the formula:** $$ H = \frac{0.85 \times 28 \times 320}{6 \times 1000} $$ **Step 3 — Compute the numerator first (efficiency × voltage × current):** $$ 0.85 \times 28 = 23.8 $$ $$ 23.8 \times 320 = 7616 $$ **Step 4 — Compute the denominator (travel speed × 1000):** $$ 6 \times 1000 = 6000 $$ **Step 5 — Perform the final division to obtain heat input:** $$ H = \frac{7616}{6000} = 1.269 \text{ kJ/mm} $$ **Step 6 — Check against IACS UR W11 limit for EH36:** $$ H_{calculated} = 1.269 \text{ kJ/mm} \quad \le \quad H_{limit} = 3.5 \text{ kJ/mm} $$ **Step 7 — Conclusion:** Since $1.269 < 3.5$, the procedure is **WITHIN CODE LIMITS** for Charpy impact toughness preservation in the HAZ. The FCAW parameters are **acceptable** for this hull application. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Insight:** Students frequently lose marks by forgetting to convert travel speed units from **mm/s to mm/min** when the answer is required in kJ/mm. Always check units first. --- ## 3.2 Step-by-Step Selection Logic — Which Process to Choose? When a shipyard production engineer must decide the joining method, the following logical chain (decision tree) is applied: **Step 1 — Material Identification:** - Mild steel (Grade A/B/D/E) → almost all processes applicable. - High-tensile (AH36/DH36/EH36) → avoid high heat input. - Stainless steel → prefer GTAW/GMAW. - Aluminium → prefer GMAW/FSW/GTAW. **Step 2 — Thickness Check:** - $\le 3 \text{ mm}$ → consider Resistance spot welding or GTAW. - $3$ to $10 \text{ mm}$ → GMAW, FCAW, SMAW. - $10$ to $25 \text{ mm}$ → SAW (flat), FCAW (positional). - $> 25 \text{ mm}$ → multi-pass SAW or Electroslag. **Step 3 — Position Constraint:** - Flat only → SAW is most economical. - All positions → FCAW (gas-shielded) or SMAW. **Step 4 — Production Volume:** - Mass production → mechanised SAW. - Job-shop / repair → SMAW or GTAW. **Step 5 — Cost & Skill:** - Lowest cost equipment → SMAW. - Highest productivity → SAW. - Highest quality → GTAW. --- ## 3.3 Exhaustive Comparative Matrix (Advantages vs Disadvantages) | Process | Top 3 Advantages | Top 3 Disadvantages | Shipbuilding Application | |---------|------------------|---------------------|--------------------------| | SMAW | Portable, low cost, all-position | Slow, slag, frequent stops | Field repair, block joints | | GMAW | Continuous wire, slag-free, automatable | Wind-sensitive, needs cleanliness | Aluminium superstructure | | FCAW | High deposition, self-shield option, deep penetration | Slag, fume, expensive wire | Panel-line hull welding | | SAW | Highest rate, deep penetration, clean operation | Flat only, flux handling, high capital | Panel fabrication line | | GTAW | Best quality, no slag, all metals | Slowest, expensive gas, high skill | Root pass, pipe, stainless | | FSW | Solid-state, low distortion, no fume | Rigid clamping, keyhole, slow for thick | Aluminium deck panels | | Riveting | No heat, inspectable, fatigue-resistant | Labour-intensive, adds weight | Historical hulls, repairs | | Brazing | Low temp, dissimilar metals | Low strength, flux residue | Pipe joints, non-structural | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Insight:** When asked to "compare" two processes in a 14-mark question, always structure your answer with **at least three advantages and three disadvantages of EACH process**, then conclude with **justification of selection** based on the given shipbuilding scenario. --- ## 3.4 Process Efficiency Reference Values For completeness, the thermal efficiency $\eta$ values used in heat input calculations are: $$ \eta_{SMAW} \approx 0.80 $$ $$ \eta_{GMAW} \approx 0.85 $$ $$ \eta_{FCAW} \approx 0.85 $$ $$ \eta_{SAW} \approx 0.95 $$ $$ \eta_{GTAW} \approx 0.60 $$ $$ \eta_{FSW} \approx 0.95 \text{ (mechanical-to-thermal)} $$ > [!NOTE] > SAW has the highest thermal efficiency because the granular flux blanket minimises radiative heat losses. GTAW has the lowest because the open arc loses substantial heat via convection and radiation. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Shipbuilding Joining Techniques ```mermaid flowchart TD A["JOINING TECHNIQUES
IN SHIPBUILDING"] A --> B["FUSION WELDING"] A --> C["SOLID-STATE WELDING"] A --> D["MECHANICAL FASTENING"] A --> E["ADHESIVE / BRAZING"] B --> B1["SMAW
Stick Electrode"] B --> B2["GMAW / MIG-MAG
Solid Wire + Gas"] B --> B3["FCAW
Tubular Wire"] B --> B4["SAW
Submerged Arc"] B --> B5["GTAW / TIG
Tungsten + Inert Gas"] C --> C1["FRICTION STIR WELDING
FSW"] C --> C2["RESISTANCE WELDING
Spot / Seam"] C --> C3["ULTRASONIC WELDING"] D --> D1["RIVETING"] D --> D2["BOLTING"] D --> D3["STITCH WELDING"] E --> E1["ADHESIVE BONDING"] E --> E2["BRAZING"] E --> E3["SOLDERING"] ``` ## 4.2 Process Selection Decision Flow (KTU Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD P1["START
Identify Base Material"] P1 --> P2{"Material
Thickness?"} P2 -->|"<= 3 mm"| P3["Choose
GTAW or RSW"] P2 -->|"3 to 10 mm"| P4["Choose
GMAW or SMAW"] P2 -->|"10 to 25 mm"| P5["Choose
SAW flat position"] P2 -->|"> 25 mm"| P6["Choose
Multi-pass SAW"] P4 --> Q1{"Welding
Position?"} Q1 -->|"Flat / Horizontal"| R1["Use FCAW or SAW"] Q1 -->|"All positions"| R2["Use FCAW gas-shielded"] P3 --> Q2{"Quality
Requirement?"} Q2 -->|"Critical root pass"| S1["Use GTAW"] Q2 -->|"General thin plate"| S2["Use GMAW"] P5 --> T1["Production Line?"] T1 -->|"Yes, mechanised"| U1["Use SAW + FCAW combo"] T1 -->|"No, field repair"| U2["Use SMAW"] R1 --> END["END
Procedure Selected"] R2 --> END S1 --> END S2 --> END U1 --> END U2 --> END ``` ## 4.3 Advantages vs Disadvantages Block Diagram (Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph ADV["ADVANTAGES BLOCK"] A1["High Productivity"] A2["All-Position"] A3["Low Cost"] A4["Clean Quality"] A5["Low Distortion"] end subgraph DIS["DISADVANTAGES BLOCK"] D1["Equipment Cost"] D2["Position Limitation"] D3["Operator Skill"] D4["Slag or Fume"] D5["Wind Sensitivity"] end PRO["SELECTED
JOINING PROCESS"] --> ADV PRO --> DIS ADV --> DEC["SHIPYARD
DECISION"] DIS --> DEC ``` ## 4.4 Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ) Comparison Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR H1["SMAW HAZ
Narrow 1-2 mm"] H2["GMAW HAZ
Narrow 1.5 mm"] H3["SAW HAZ
Wider 3-5 mm
High heat input"] H4["FSW HAZ
Minimal, thermo-mechanically
affected zone only"] H1 --> COMP["HAZ WIDTH
COMPARISON"] H2 --> COMP H3 --> COMP H4 --> COMP ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: List any three advantages of Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW) over Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW) in shipbuilding applications.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** 1. **Higher deposition rate** — GMAW delivers $3$ to $8 \text{ kg/h}$ versus SMAW's $1$ to $3 \text{ kg/h}$, increasing productivity on long hull seams. 2. **Continuous wire feed** eliminates frequent electrode changes, increasing arc-time efficiency (up to $70\%$ vs $30\%$ for SMAW). 3. **Slag-free weld** in pure gas-shielded mode — eliminates chipping between passes, reducing labour cost on aluminium superstructure fabrication. > [!TIP] > **Marking Pattern:** 1 mark per valid advantage with brief shipbuilding justification. Maximum 3 advantages = 3 marks. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: State any three disadvantages of Submerged Arc Welding (SAW) limiting its use in ship erection.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** 1. **Limited positional capability** — SAW is restricted to flat (1G) and horizontal fillet (2F) positions, hence unsuitable for vertical or overhead welds during block erection. 2. **High capital investment** in mechanised heads, flux recovery systems, and panel line machinery — unaffordable for small shipyards. 3. **Flux handling** requirement — flux must be dried, recovered, recycled, and disposed of, adding logistical burden and cost. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) --- ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a) Compare the advantages and disadvantages of SMAW and FCAW processes in shipbuilding. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **SMAW — Advantages:** 1. **Lowest equipment cost** and highest portability — ideal for shipyard outdoor and field conditions. 2. **All-position capability** (1G, 2F, 3G, 4F, 5G, 6G per ASME Section IX). 3. **Tolerant to surface contamination** (rust, primer, mill scale) — useful in repair and erection where cleaning is impractical. 4. **Wide electrode range** (cellulosic, basic, rutile, low-hydrogen) enables matching to various strength grades. **SMAW — Disadvantages:** 1. **Low deposition rate** ($1$ to $3 \text{ kg/h}$) increases project duration. 2. **Frequent electrode changes** reduce arc-time efficiency. 3. **Slag must be chipped** between passes — additional labour. 4. **Smoke and fume generation** reduces operator comfort. **FCAW — Advantages:** 1. **High deposition rate** ($4$ to $12 \text{ kg/h}$) — nearly $3$ to $4$ times SMAW. 2. **Continuous tubular wire** eliminates stop-starts. 3. **Self-shielded variant** tolerates windy outdoor conditions — perfect for hull panel welding. 4. **Deep penetration** reduces number of passes for thick plate. **FCAW — Disadvantages:** 1. Produces **slag requiring removal**. 2. **Higher fume emission** than solid wire GMAW. 3. **Tubular wire cost** is higher than SMAW electrodes. 4. **Hydrogen cracking risk** if flux is not properly stored/dried. **(b) For a 22 mm thick EH36 hull plate to be welded in the flat position at a shipyard panel line, justify whether SAW or FCAW would be the preferred process. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Decision: SAW is preferred.** **Justification:** 1. **Position compatibility:** Flat position is fully compatible with SAW's optimal operating orientation. 2. **Highest deposition rate** ($10$ to $30 \text{ kg/h}$) far exceeds FCAW's $4$ to $12 \text{ kg/h}$, essential for mass-production panel lines. 3. **Heat input management:** With $\eta = 0.95$, SAW delivers controlled heat input suitable for EH36 grade (must be $\le 3.5 \text{ kJ/mm}$ per IACS). 4. **Weld quality:** Low hydrogen content and slag protection produce defect-free welds meeting radiographic acceptance (Class 1 per AWS D1.1). 5. **Operator comfort:** No UV radiation, minimal fume, and no spatter during SAW. 6. **Mechanisation potential:** SAW integrates with carriage / tractor systems for fully automated panel-line fabrication. 7. **Cost efficiency at scale:** Despite higher capital cost, the per-metre weld cost is lowest when producing thousands of metres of panel seam. **Conclusion:** For a panel-line production environment welding EH36 in flat position, **SAW is decisively preferred** over FCAW on the basis of productivity, weld quality, and long-run cost economics. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students frequently confuse **panel-line fabrication** (flat, SAW) with **block erection** (positional, FCAW/SMAW). The scenario wording determines the correct process choice. Read carefully! --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a) Discuss the advantages and limitations of Friction Stir Welding (FSW) for marine aluminium superstructure fabrication. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **FSW — Advantages:** 1. **Solid-state process** — material does not melt, eliminating porosity, solidification cracking, and hydrogen-induced defects. 2. **Superior mechanical properties** — fine-grained microstructure produces joint efficiencies up to $90$ to $100\%$ of parent metal (vs $80\%$ for GMAW aluminium). 3. **Low distortion** — lower heat input than fusion welding maintains dimensional accuracy in thin aluminium panels. 4. **Environmentally clean** — no shielding gas, no fume, no spatter, no UV radiation. 5. **Welds difficult alloys** like AA5083, AA6082 commonly used in marine superstructures. 6. **No filler metal required** — material is conserved and chemistry is unchanged. **FSW — Limitations:** 1. **Rigid clamping** required to resist forging forces; heavy backing fixtures add cost. 2. **Exit keyhole** at end of weld must be trimmed or managed. 3. **Limited joint geometry** — primarily butt and lap; not suitable for T-joints or complex 3D paths without special tooling. 4. **Tool wear** — pin and shoulder erode, requiring replacement and adding cost. 5. **Slower traverse** than SAW for thick sections. 6. **High capital investment** in CNC FSW machines. **(b) Explain why GTAW remains indispensable for stainless steel pipe welding in shipboard piping systems despite its low productivity. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** GTAW (TIG) is the **gold standard** for root-pass welding of austenitic stainless steel pipes used in shipboard systems (seawater cooling, fuel, lube oil, fire-fighting). The justification is as follows: 1. **Root pass quality:** The root bead controls the entire weld's corrosion resistance. GTAW produces oxide-free, defect-free roots with full penetration. 2. **No spatter, no slag, no fume** — eliminates inclusions that would initiate pitting or crevice corrosion in stainless steel. 3. **Precise heat control** — critical to avoid sensitisation (chromium carbide precipitation at $450$ to $850 ^\circ \text{C}$) which causes intergranular corrosion. 4. **Backing gas capability** — argon purging on the root side prevents oxidation (sugaring) of the internal bore. 5. **Dissimilar metal joining** — GTAW can weld stainless to carbon steel transition joints with appropriate filler. 6. **Regulatory acceptance** — classification societies (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS, BV) accept GTAW roots for high-pressure Class I piping. The **productivity loss is justified** because: - Pipe root failure in service is catastrophic (flooding, fire, pollution). - Cost of rework / downtime far exceeds cost of slow GTAW root. - Subsequent fill and cap passes use faster FCAW, so total time remains acceptable. **Conclusion:** GTAW's superior metallurgical control outweighs its slow deposition rate in critical shipboard piping applications. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** When answering "advantages of GTAW," students often write generic points like "good quality." This earns only partial credit. Always link the advantage to the **specific shipbuilding application** (e.g., stainless pipe root, aluminium superstructure, etc.) to earn full marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Advantages and Disadvantages" of Joining Techniques (Module 2)** - **SMAW** — Cheap, portable, all-position, but slow and slag-prone. Use: field erection, repair. - **GMAW** — Continuous wire, slag-free, automatable, but wind-sensitive. Use: aluminium superstructure. - **FCAW** — High deposition, self-shield option, but produces slag and fume. Use: hull panel-line welding. - **SAW** — Highest productivity, deepest penetration, but flat-only and capital-intensive. Use: mechanised panel lines. - **GTAW** — Best quality, all metals, but slowest and most expensive. Use: root pass, stainless, aluminium. - **FSW** — Solid-state, low distortion, but rigid clamping and keyhole. Use: aluminium marine structures. - **Riveting** — No heat, inspectable, but labour-heavy. Use: legacy hulls and historical vessels. - **Heat input formula** — $\displaystyle H = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \times 1000}$ — must convert $v$ to **mm/s** if given in mm/min. - **IACS UR W11 limit** — $H \le 3.5 \text{ kJ/mm}$ for higher-tensile hull steels. - **Always justify process selection** using the three pillars: **material, position, productivity**. - **In Part B questions**, structure your answer as **Process A advantages → Process A disadvantages → Process B advantages → Process B disadvantages → Comparative conclusion with scenario-specific justification.** - **Self-shielded FCAW** is uniquely suited to outdoor windy shipyard environments where gas shielding fails. - **SAW's thermal efficiency** ($\eta \approx 0.95$) is the highest among arc welding processes due to the flux blanket. - **GTAW** is the only practical process for **backing-gas purged** root passes in stainless pipe welding. - **Friction stir welding** is a **solid-state** process — no melting, no HAZ in the classical sense.

topics.title: Different Types of Solid State Welding: Cold Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST831 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Different Types of Solid State Welding: Cold Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cold Welding (CW)** is a solid-state welding process in which metallurgical bonding between two faying surfaces is achieved at **ambient room temperature (≈ 20–25 °C)** through the application of high mechanical pressure alone, without the deliberate use of external heat, filler metal, or shielding gas. The bond is established once the applied pressure drives sufficient **plastic deformation** that surface contaminants, oxides, and absorbed gas layers are fractured and displaced, allowing clean, virgin metal surfaces to converge within atomic interaction distances (typically **< 0.5 nm**), where short-range metallic bonding forces become active.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):** Cold Welding is classified under *Solid-State Welding* (Module 2) and is defined as a pressure-only bonding process in which joint integrity depends entirely on **plastic deformation ratio (η)** and **surface cleanliness** — not on thermal activation.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Explanation)

Imagine pressing two pieces of **wet, soft clay together** between your palms. The clay is dirty on the outside, but when you press hard enough, the **dirty outer skin cracks open**, and the clean, wet clay underneath squeezes out and touches the clean clay of the other piece. They **stick as one mass** because the molecules of clean clay are now in direct contact. That is essentially cold welding — the "dirt" is the oxide layer, the "clay" is the ductile metal, and the "palms" are the welding dies applying enormous force.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the "Wet Clay" Analogy Works:** Metals like aluminum and copper behave similarly at room temperature when squeezed — they are ductile, their oxide shells fracture, and pure metal "bursts out" to form a permanent bond.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Welding Temperature | **20 – 25 °C** (ambient, no external heat) | °C |
| Required Plastic Deformation (η) | **60 – 90 %** | % |
| Bonding Pressure | **700 – 2500 MPa** | MPa |
| Critical Oxide-Layer Disruption Threshold | ≈ **0.5 µm penetration depth** | µm |
| Atomic Bonding Distance | **< 0.5 nm** | nm |
| Deformation Ratio (η) | (t₁ − t₂) ⁄ t₁ × 100 | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** Cold welding is the *only* standard welding process that operates at **room temperature** with **no fusion zone (FZ)** and **no heat-affected zone (HAZ)**.

---

### 1.4 Classification of Cold Welding Variants

Cold welding is broadly classified into the following sub-types based on joint geometry:

1. **Butt Cold Welding** — Two workpieces are aligned end-to-end and pressed axially (e.g., wire-to-wire splicing).
2. **Lap Cold Welding** — Overlapping sheets/wires are compressed between dies (most common in wire manufacturing).
3. **Cross-Wire (X) Cold Welding** — Two wires cross at 90° and are pressed at the intersection.
4. **Roll Bonding (Cold Pressure Welding)** — Continuous sheets passed through rollers under high load (used for cladding, bimetallic strips).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shipbuilding Context (KTU Module 2):** Cold welding finds niche but important applications in marine **electrical busbar joints**, **aluminum cable terminations**, and **non-structural sheet-metal assemblies** where heat input must be avoided (e.g., near already-installed electronics or heat-sensitive coatings).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Bonding Mechanism — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

Cold welding, although appearing simple externally, involves a sequence of **micro-mechanical and metallurgical events** that must occur in order for a sound joint to form. The KTU 2024 Scheme requires students to understand *why* the bond forms, not just *that* it forms.

#### Stage 1: Surface Preparation (Pre-Bond)
- Workpiece surfaces are **degreased** (acetone, alkaline cleaners) to remove oils and greases.
- The brittle **oxide layer** is removed mechanically using a **stainless-steel wire brush** or chemically etched.
- The exposed "virgin" metal must be **protected from re-oxidation** — the time between cleaning and pressing is critical (typically **< 5 minutes** for aluminum).

#### Stage 2: Initial Contact (Elastic Deformation Phase)
- The welding dies (usually tool steel, hardened to **HRC 58–62**) apply a preliminary load.
- Asperities (microscopic peaks) on each surface first touch, creating localized contact points.
- Stress concentration at these asperities initiates the next stage.

#### Stage 3: Plastic Deformation (Core Bonding Phase)
- Pressure exceeds the **material yield strength (σ_y)** and continues to rise until asperities flatten and the **oxide shell fractures**.
- The extrusion of clean metal through the cracks in the oxide film brings two virgin-metal surfaces into atomic contact.
- This is the **bonding window** — the joint is forming.

#### Stage 4: Diffusion & Metallurgical Continuity
- Once surfaces are within **< 0.5 nm**, short-range metallic forces (electron-shared bonds) lock the surfaces.
- A thin interfacial layer of **disordered, intermixed atoms** forms. No grain growth, no phase change.

#### Stage 5: Consolidation
- The load is held for a brief dwell time (typically **0.5 – 2.0 s**) to ensure full interfacial consolidation.
- Pressure is released; elastic spring-back of the surrounding bulk metal clamps the bond, often increasing contact area further.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Each Step:** Stage 1 ensures no chemical barrier; Stage 3 provides the *mechanical* driving force; Stage 4 is the *physical* bond formation; Stage 5 stabilizes the joint. Skip any one → the bond fails.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Unit | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Deformation Ratio (η)** | $\eta = \dfrac{t_{1} - t_{2}}{t_{1}} \times 100$ | % | How much the material is squeezed; **the single most important CW parameter** |
| 2 | **Upset Pressure (p)** | $p = \dfrac{F}{A_{0}}$ | MPa (N/mm²) | The bearing load at the die face |
| 3 | **Theoretical Bonding Pressure (Lubkin model)** | $p_{b} = \dfrac{2 \sigma_{y}}{1 - \rho}$ | MPa | Pressure needed to bring virgin metal into contact (ρ = surface roughness ratio) |
| 4 | **Required Upset Force (Butt CW)** | $F = p \cdot A_{0} = p \cdot \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4}$ | N | Axial force for round bar/wire of diameter d |
| 5 | **Joint Efficiency (η_eff)** | $\eta_{eff} = \dfrac{\sigma_{joint}}{\sigma_{parent}} \times 100$ | % | Strength of welded joint vs. parent metal — typically **70 – 90 %** for CW |
| 6 | **Critical Interfacial Distance** | $d_{c} < 0.5 \times 10^{-9}$ | m | Atomic spacing for metallic bond |
| 7 | **Bulk Plastic Strain (true strain)** | $\varepsilon = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{t_{1}}{t_{2}}\right)$ | dimensionless | Logarithmic strain used in CW analysis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** In numerical problems, examiners love testing the **deformation ratio (η)**. Memorize the formula — most 3-mark Part A questions are framed around it.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Wire & Cable Industry:** Joining copper and aluminum conductors without heat (saves cost, avoids brittle intermetallics like CuAl₂ that form at elevated temperatures).
- **Electrical Switchgear & Busbars:** Cold-welded joints have **near-parent-metal conductivity** — critical in ship switchboards and DC distribution.
- **Shipboard HVAC & Plumbing:** Joining thin aluminum ducts and pipes when arc welding would distort the thin walls.
- **Aerospace Cladding:** Bimetallic aluminum sheets for corrosion-resistant panels.
- **Nuclear & Cryogenic Industries:** No HAZ means no residual stress or sensitization — valuable where embrittlement must be avoided.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why CW Survives in Modern Industry Despite Being "Old":** It is the only process that **avoids the entire metallurgy of the HAZ**, making it indispensable for **dissimilar metal joints** and **high-conductivity electrical assemblies** — both of which appear regularly in shipboard systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Bonding Pressure (Lubkin's Geometric Model)

This derivation is based on the classical **Lubkin (1957)** model of cold pressure welding, in which the faying surface is treated as a series of conical asperities (peaks) of height *h* and base radius *r* that must be flattened to bring clean metal into contact.

**Given:**
- Asperity height = h
- Asperity base radius = r
- Material yield strength in shear = k ≈ σ_y / √3 (von Mises)

**Step 1: Volume of a single asperity (cone)**

$$V_{asp} = \frac{1}{3} \pi r^{2} h$$

**Step 2: The asperity is flattened to a circular disc of radius R_f such that volume is conserved**

$$\frac{1}{3} \pi r^{2} h = \pi R_{f}^{2} h_{f}$$

where h_f is the final flattened height (≈ 0 for ideal flattening).

**Step 3: The plastic work done to flatten one asperity is approximated as**

$$W_{asp} = k \cdot V_{asp} = k \cdot \frac{1}{3} \pi r^{2} h$$

**Step 4: The apparent contact area of the asperity is A = π r². The pressure required is**

$$p = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{W_{asp} / h}{A} = \frac{k \cdot \frac{1}{3} \pi r^{2} h / h}{\pi r^{2}} = \frac{k}{3}$$

But accounting for the **roughness ratio ρ** (ratio of actual contact area to nominal area) and strain hardening, Lubkin obtained:

$$p_{b} = \frac{2 \sigma_{y}}{1 - \rho}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic — Step-by-Step Explanation:**
> - **Step 1** treats each surface peak as a perfect cone (geometric idealization).
> - **Step 2** enforces volume conservation: the cone is squashed into a disc, no metal is lost.
> - **Step 3** uses the principle of *plastic work = shear-yield-stress × sheared volume* — a standard plasticity approximation.
> - **Step 4** divides total work by the original height to recover a force, then divides by area to get pressure.
> - **Final form:** includes the roughness factor ρ; as ρ → 1 (perfectly smooth), p_b → ∞, which is physically consistent (you cannot bond without breaking the oxide).

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

**Problem:** Two aluminum sheets each of initial thickness t₁ = 3.0 mm are lap-cold-welded. After the weld, the combined thickness at the bond zone is measured as t₂_total = 2.4 mm. Calculate the **deformation ratio (η)** and the **true plastic strain (ε)** at the joint.

> *This is the exact format KTU examiners use for 3–5 mark numerical questions.*

**Given:**
- Initial single-sheet thickness t₁ = 3.0 mm
- Final combined thickness t₂_total = 2.4 mm
- Therefore final single-sheet thickness t₂ = t₂_total / 2 = 1.2 mm

**Step 1: Deformation Ratio**

$$\eta = \frac{t_{1} - t_{2}}{t_{1}} \times 100 = \frac{3.0 - 1.2}{3.0} \times 100$$

$$\eta = \frac{1.8}{3.0} \times 100 = 60\%$$

**Step 2: True Plastic Strain**

$$\varepsilon = \ln\!\left(\frac{t_{1}}{t_{2}}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{3.0}{1.2}\right) = \ln(2.5)$$

$$\varepsilon = 0.916 \text{ (dimensionless)}$$

**Step 3: Interpretation**

Since η = 60% lies within the recommended bonding range (60–90%) for aluminum, the joint is **predicted to be sound**. Joint efficiency η_eff ≈ 75–80% of parent aluminum strength can be expected.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineer's Sanity Check:** For pure aluminum CW, η must exceed ~60% — below this, the oxide film is not fully disrupted and the bond is unreliable. Our result of 60% is at the lower acceptable limit; in practice, η ≈ 70–80% is targeted.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When To Use Cold Welding

| Engineering Criterion | Cold Welding Suitability | Comments |
|---|---|---|
| Dissimilar metal joining (e.g., Cu–Al) | ★★★★★ Excellent | Avoids brittle intermetallics that form in fusion welding |
| Heat-sensitive assemblies | ★★★★★ Excellent | No HAZ, no thermal distortion |
| Thick-section structural joints | ★☆☆☆☆ Poor | Forces become impractical above ~6 mm |
| Galvanized / heavily oxidized steel | ★☆☆☆☆ Poor | Oxide disruption extremely difficult |
| Electrical conductivity retention | ★★★★★ Excellent | No alloying at interface |
| Production rate (units/hour) | ★★★☆☆ Moderate | Stroke-limited mechanical presses |
| Marine aluminum ducting | ★★★★☆ Good | Standard shipboard application |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Takeaway:** Cold welding is a **specialist process** — not a replacement for arc welding in shipbuilding, but a **complementary technique** for specific non-structural and electrical systems.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block represents the functional architecture of a **lap-type cold welding cell** as used in shipyard cable shops and busbar fabrication bays.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Feedstock Wire or Sheet] --> B[Mechanical Degreasing Station]
    B --> C[Stainless Steel Wire Brushing Unit]
    C --> D[Surface Quality Check - Visual]
    D --> E[Alignment and Clamping Dies]
    E --> F[High-Tonnage Hydraulic Press]
    F --> G[Plastic Deformation Stage]
    G --> H[Hold and Consolidate 0.5 to 2.0 s]
    H --> I[Eject Welded Coupon]
    I --> J[Non-Destructive Testing]
    J --> K{Acceptance Criteria Met}
    K --> L[Marking and Storage]
    K --> M[Scrap or Rework Loop]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Each block is a station in the cold-welding cell. The decision diamond (block K) routes acceptable welds to packaging and rejects them back for surface prep rework — a standard quality-loop embedded in shipyard production.

---

### 4.2 Schematic of Lap Cold Welding Tooling

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Upper Die - Hardened Tool Steel] --> B[Workpiece Sheet A]
    B --> C[Workpiece Sheet B]
    C --> D[Lower Die - Hardened Tool Steel]
    D --> E[Hydraulic Ram]
    E --> F[Reaction Frame and Bed]
    F --> G[Force Sensor and Pressure Gauge]
    G --> H[PLC Controller]
    H --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Functional Mapping:** The press exerts a vertical force F through the upper die. Workpieces A and B are squeezed between A and D. The force sensor (G) feeds back to the PLC (H), which modulates ram pressure to maintain target bonding pressure p within ±5% tolerance.

---

### 4.3 Comparative Process Map — Cold Welding vs. Other Solid-State Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SSW[Solid State Welding Family] --> CW[Cold Welding]
    SSW --> FW[Friction Welding]
    SSW --> UW[Ultrasonic Welding]
    SSW --> EXW[Explosion Welding]
    SSW --> DW[Diffusion Welding]
    CW --> C1[Butt CW]
    CW --> C2[Lap CW]
    CW --> C3[Cross Wire CW]
    CW --> C4[Roll Bonding CW]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 2 Context:** The full Module 2 syllabus covers the entire *Solid State Welding* family tree shown above. Cold welding is the **simplest node** in this tree — no heat, no filler, no shielding, no consumables. It is the *baseline reference* against which all other solid-state methods are compared.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define cold welding. List any two advantages and two limitations of the process.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Cold welding is a solid-state welding process in which two metallic surfaces are joined at room temperature by applying high mechanical pressure alone, without the use of external heat, filler metal, or shielding gas. Bonding is achieved when the pressure causes sufficient plastic deformation to fracture the surface oxide layer and bring virgin metal surfaces within atomic interaction distances.

**Advantages (1 Mark — any two):**
1. No heat-affected zone (HAZ); no thermal distortion or residual stresses.
2. No filler metal or shielding gas required; environmentally clean and cost-effective.
3. Excellent electrical conductivity at the joint — near parent-metal levels.
4. Highly suitable for joining dissimilar ductile metals (e.g., Al–Cu) without forming brittle intermetallics.

**Limitations (1 Mark — any two):**
1. Restricted to ductile, non-heavily-oxidized metals (Al, Cu, Pb, Zn, Ni).
2. Requires very high forming forces; not practical for thick sections (>6 mm).
3. Surface preparation is critical — any oxide or grease residue drastically reduces joint strength.
4. Joint strength is generally lower than the parent metal (efficiency ~70–90%).

---

#### **Question 2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is the role of plastic deformation in cold welding? Why is a deformation ratio of 60–90% required?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Role of Plastic Deformation (2 Marks):**
Plastic deformation is the **fundamental driving mechanism** in cold welding. Its roles are:
1. **Breaks the brittle oxide layer** that naturally forms on metal surfaces, exposing the clean virgin metal beneath.
2. **Extrudes the clean metal** through the fractured oxide shell so that two clean surfaces converge within atomic bonding distance (< 0.5 nm).
3. **Increases the true contact area** between the faying surfaces, ensuring a high proportion of the joint consists of metallic bonds rather than voids.

**Why 60–90% is Required (1 Mark):**
- Below **60%**: the oxide layer is not fully disrupted and exposed metal area is insufficient; bond is weak and unreliable.
- Above **90%**: the workpiece may crack or buckle, surface finish degrades, and the die-workpiece friction may damage tooling.
- The **60–90% window** represents the optimum range that ensures complete oxide disruption without material failure.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **working principle of lap-type cold welding**. Discuss the function of each stage. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **deformation ratio (η)** formula. An aluminum sheet of initial thickness **3.5 mm** is lap-welded to another identical sheet, and the final thickness at the joint is **1.4 mm** per sheet. Calculate η and comment on joint quality. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Working Principle (4 Marks):**
In lap cold welding, two metal sheets (or wires) are overlapped and placed between a pair of hardened tool-steel dies. A hydraulic or mechanical press applies a high axial load. The stages are:

| Stage | Function |
|---|---|
| **Cleaning** | Removes grease, oil, and loose debris using solvents/alkaline baths. |
| **Brushing** | Mechanically disrupts the brittle oxide film to expose virgin metal. |
| **Positioning** | Aligns the lap region centrally between the dies to ensure uniform pressure. |
| **Pressing** | Applies 700–2500 MPa pressure, causing the bulk material to upset laterally. |
| **Bonding** | The oxide shell fractures; clean metal extrudes through cracks; atomic bonds form. |
| **Holding & Ejection** | Pressure is held for 0.5–2.0 s; the die retracts and the joint is ejected for inspection. |

**Sketch (3 Marks):** A simple labelled diagram showing two overlapping sheets between upper and lower dies with arrows indicating force direction, the deformation bulge, and the bonded zone.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Derivation (3 Marks):**
The deformation ratio is defined as the fractional reduction in thickness caused by the upsetting process.

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Initial Thickness} - \text{Final Thickness}}{\text{Initial Thickness}} \times 100$$

**Calculation (3 Marks):**

$$\eta = \frac{3.5 - 1.4}{3.5} \times 100 = \frac{2.1}{3.5} \times 100 = 60\%$$

**Comment on Joint Quality (1 Mark):**
η = 60% lies exactly at the lower bound of the recommended bonding window (60–90%) for aluminum. The joint is **acceptable but at the margin**; in production, η ≈ 70–80% is targeted for higher reliability and joint efficiency (≈ 80–90% of parent strength).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **mechanism of bond formation** in cold welding. Discuss the role of surface preparation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare cold welding with **fusion welding** and **friction welding** in terms of (i) heat input, (ii) HAZ, (iii) joint efficiency, (iv) suitability for dissimilar metals, and (v) typical applications in shipbuilding. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Mechanism of Bond Formation (5 Marks):**
The cold welding bond forms through the following sequence:
1. **Surface contact:** Microscopic asperities on the two faying surfaces make initial point contact.
2. **Asperity deformation:** As pressure rises above the material yield strength, asperities flatten plastically, increasing the true contact area.
3. **Oxide fracture:** The brittle, pre-existing oxide layer (Al₂O₃, Cu₂O, etc.) is unable to deform plastically and fractures under the bulk-metal flow.
4. **Virgin metal extrusion:** Clean, unoxidized parent metal extrudes through the cracks in the oxide film, emerging on both sides.
5. **Atomic bonding:** Where two clean extrusions meet, atoms are within < 0.5 nm, allowing short-range metallic forces to lock the joint. No fusion, no diffusion bonding in the thermal sense — pure **mechanical-metallurgical interlocking**.

**Role of Surface Preparation (2 Marks):**
- **Degreasing** removes hydrocarbon films that would block metal-to-metal contact.
- **Wire brushing** disrupts and partially removes the oxide layer; the freshly exposed metal must be welded within ~5 minutes to prevent re-oxidation.
- Without proper preparation, the joint is weak because oxides act as a **continuous barrier film** that prevents virgin-metal contact.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

| Parameter | Cold Welding (CW) | Fusion Welding (FW) | Friction Welding (FRW) |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Heat Input | **None** (room temperature) | High (10³–10⁴ °C) | Moderate (below melting point) |
| (ii) HAZ | **None** | Pronounced | Present but narrow |
| (iii) Joint Efficiency | 70–90% | 90–100% | 90–100% |
| (iv) Dissimilar Metals | **Excellent** (Al–Cu) | Poor (brittle intermetallics) | Good (with care) |
| (v) Shipbuilding Application | Electrical busbars, cable terminations, aluminum ducting | Hull plates, structural stiffeners, pipelines | Propeller shafts, rudder posts, anchor chains |

**Concluding Statement (1 Mark):**
Cold welding complements — but does not replace — fusion and friction welding in shipbuilding. Its niche is **heat-sensitive, dissimilar-metal, and high-conductivity joints** that other processes cannot perform reliably.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Cold Welding with Cold Pressure Welding** — they are the *same* process; do not invent a distinction in your answer.
> 2. **Writing "no heat is used" without explaining *why* heat is unnecessary** — examiners want the link to **atomic bonding at room temperature** and **plastic deformation as the driving force**.
> 3. **Forgetting to state the deformation ratio range (60–90%)** — this is a high-frequency marking keyword.
> 4. **Drawing an unrealistic sketch** in Part B — the dies must be shown as **hardened tool steel**, the deformation bulge must be visible, and the force arrow must be labelled.
> 5. **Numerical errors in deformation ratio** — always divide by the *initial* thickness, not the final. Re-check the formula before writing.
> 6. **Ignoring surface preparation** in mechanism questions — this is worth at least 2 marks and is a frequent omission.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things To Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Cold welding = **solid-state, room-temperature, pressure-only** bonding.
- ✅ No fusion zone (FZ), no heat-affected zone (HAZ), no filler metal, no shielding gas.
- ✅ **Driving force:** plastic deformation (η = 60–90%) that breaks the oxide shell.
- ✅ **Bonding condition:** clean metal-to-metal contact at atomic distance < 0.5 nm.
- ✅ **Best materials:** ductile, soft metals — Al, Cu, Pb, Zn, Ni, low-carbon steel (limited).
- ✅ **Best applications in shipbuilding:** electrical busbars, cable terminations, aluminum ducting, dissimilar Cu–Al joints.
- ✅ **Key formula:** $\eta = \dfrac{t_1 - t_2}{t_1} \times 100$.
- ✅ **Bonding pressure range:** 700–2500 MPa depending on material yield strength.
- ✅ **Joint efficiency:** 70–90% of parent metal.
- ✅ **Critical step:** surface preparation (degrease + wire brush) within ~5 minutes of pressing.
- ✅ **Not suitable for:** thick sections, heavily oxidized metals (e.g., stainless steel), high-strength alloys.
- ✅ **Theoretical foundation:** Lubkin (1957) — bonding pressure $p_b = \dfrac{2\sigma_y}{1 - \rho}$.
- ✅ **Advantage over fusion welding:** avoids brittle intermetallics in Cu–Al joints.
- ✅ **Examiner's favourite keywords:** "oxide disruption", "virgin metal extrusion", "atomic bonding distance", "deformation ratio", "no HAZ".
- ✅ **Module 2 sibling processes to remember:** Friction Welding, Ultrasonic Welding, Explosion Welding, Diffusion Welding, Roll Bonding — cold welding is the *simplest* in this family.

> **End of Module 2 Topic Notes — Cold Welding**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Diffusion Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OENST831 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Diffusion Welding ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] Core Definition > **Diffusion Welding (DW)**, also known as **Diffusion Bonding (DB)**, is a solid-state joining process in which two carefully prepared surfaces are coalesced at an elevated temperature (typically **0.5 to 0.8 of the absolute melting temperature, $T_m$**) under a controlled applied pressure, held for a defined dwell time, in a protective atmosphere (vacuum or inert gas), such that metallurgical bonding is achieved primarily through **atomic diffusion** across the interface — *without macroscopic melting, casting structure, or fusion zone formation*. In the strict KTU 2024 definition, diffusion welding is classified as a **Solid-State Welding Process** under the AWS/CWS family, alongside forge welding, friction welding, and ultrasonic welding. It is governed by the principles of **physical metallurgy and kinetic diffusion theory**, rather than by fusion metallurgy. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview Imagine pressing two slices of soft butter against each other for a long time inside a warm room. Even though neither slice melts completely, the molecules at the contact face slowly cross over and intermingle. After several hours, the slices become one inseparable piece. That is essentially what diffusion welding does — but at sub-melting temperatures, with metals, under vacuum, and driven by atomic-scale diffusion rather than flow. > [!NOTE] Why this matters in Shipbuilding > A modern naval vessel often combines **steel hulls with aluminium superstructures**, **titanium propellers**, and **composite radar domes**. Conventional fusion welding between, say, aluminium and steel produces brittle intermetallics (e.g., FeAl, FeAl$_2$, Fe$_3$Al) that crack under sea shock loads.
