> A family of welding processes producing coalescence at temperatures **below the melting point** of the base metals, using pressure (and sometimes heat) to bring atomic surfaces within bonding distance. Friction welding and Inertia welding are the two most industrially relevant sub-processes for marine shafting.

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Imagine rubbing your palms together **very hard and very fast** for a few seconds. Your palms become uncomfortably hot — not because a flame touched them, but because the *mechanical friction* between them converted kinetic energy into thermal energy. Now imagine pressing two metal rods against each other while spinning one at high speed — the interface gets hot enough to soften, and a quick axial squeeze (a *forging* action) welds them into a single piece. That is exactly what a friction welding machine does on a shipyard floor.

### 1.2 Inertia Welding (IW)

Inertia welding is a **derivative variant of rotary friction welding**. The key difference is the **energy source**: instead of a continuously running motor, the rotational energy is *pre-stored* in a heavy **flywheel** spinning at a preset RPM, then released abruptly. The friction energy that generates heat comes entirely from the **kinetic energy of the flywheel**, not from continuous motor input.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inertia Welding — One-Line Definition (Board-Exam Ready):**
> *"Inertia welding is a rotary friction welding process in which all the frictional energy is supplied by the kinetic energy of a flywheel, which is accelerated to a preset speed, disengaged from the drive motor, and then brought into contact with the workpiece under axial pressure."*

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Think of a **spinning pottery wheel** (the flywheel) loaded with stored rotational energy. A motor *spins it up* to high RPM, then the motor is **clutched out**. Now the clay (workpiece) is pushed against the wheel — the wheel decelerates rapidly, the rubbing surfaces heat up violently, and the clay welds. Once the wheel stops, the weld is complete. The motor only *charged up the system*; the actual joining energy was the stored inertia.

### 1.3 Salient Distinguishing Features (Bold the Key Numbers)

| Feature | Continuous-Drive Friction Welding | Inertia Welding |
|---|---|---|
| **Energy Source** | Continuous motor torque | **Kinetic energy of flywheel** |
| **Spindle Drive During Weld** | Motor ON throughout | **Motor OFF, flywheel coasts** |
| **Process Control Variables** | Speed, pressure, time, upset | **Flywheel mass, initial RPM, axial pressure** |
| **Typical Spindle Speed** | 100 – 1500 RPM | **1500 – 20000 RPM** |
| **Heating Time** | 1 – 30 s (sustained) | **0.1 – 2 s (very short, impulsive)** |
| **Upset (Forging) Phase** | Yes, after motor stop | **Yes, at the moment of wheel stop** |
| **Best Marine Application** | Hull plates, superstructure | **Propeller shafts, rudder stocks, nozzle rings** |
| **Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ)** | Moderate | **Very narrow** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Friction and Inertia Welding matter in Shipbuilding:**
> Marine propulsion shafts, **rudder stocks**, **intermediate shafts**, and **stern tube liners** are routinely joined to higher-grade alloy-steel flanges using inertia welding because (a) the joint is **100 % parent-metal strength** in well-controlled conditions, (b) there is **no HAZ softening** in quenched-and-tempered steels, and (c) dissimilar metal combinations (e.g., carbon-steel shaft to bronze propeller hub adapter) can be joined with negligible intermetallic brittleness.

---

### 1.4 Why the Marine Industry Prefers These Processes

1. **No consumables** — no filler wire, no flux, no shielding gas. Saves inventory at remote shipyards.
2. **No fusion defects** — no porosity, no solidification cracking, no slag inclusions.
3. **Joins dissimilar metals** — aluminium to steel, copper to steel, titanium to stainless steel — all feasible in solid state.
4. **Short cycle time** — typical 5–30 seconds per joint, ideal for mass production of marine shafting.
5. **Automation-friendly** — fully CNC controlled, ideal for *robotic shipyard cells*.
6. **Environmentally clean** — no fumes, no UV radiation, no spatter.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Stages of Friction Welding (Universal Process Model)

Every friction-weld cycle — whether continuous-drive or inertia — can be decomposed into **three fundamental stages**. This is the model that KTU examiners love to test in the 14-mark essay questions.

#### Stage I — Initial Friction Phase (Pre-heat / Rubbing Phase)
- One component is held **stationary**; the other is **accelerated to welding speed**.
- Components are brought into **light axial contact**.
- The asperities (microscopic surface peaks) on the contacting faces begin to deform plastically.
- **Frictional heat** begins to build up at the interface.
- The interfacial material **softens** but no significant axial shortening (upset) occurs.
- *Objective:* Establish a uniform thermal and mechanical condition across the entire interface.

#### Stage II — Intermediate / Quasi-Steady Friction Phase
- The plasticized layer at the interface reaches a **steady-state thickness**.
- A thin ring of **flash** (extruded upset material) starts to form around the circumference.
- Flash formation **scavenges contaminants** and oxide films from the original interface — the joint becomes progressively cleaner.
- Heat generation rate = heat conduction rate + heat loss to flash.
- *Objective:* Generate the bulk of the thermal energy for plasticization.

#### Stage III — Upset (Forging / Coalescence) Phase
- The **rotational drive is stopped** (or, in inertia welding, the flywheel has already decelerated to zero).
- The axial pressure is **abruptly increased** to a high *forging upset value*.
- The remaining rotational energy (kinetic + last bit of flywheel motion) is dissipated as a final burst of frictional work.
- A **second, larger flash ring** is extruded.
- The interface is squeezed into a **metallurgical bond** — true cold-pressure contact between virgin metal surfaces at the atomic scale.
- *Objective:* Forge the bond, expel remaining plasticized film, and complete coalescence.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic (Board-Exam Friendly): "RUB — STEADY — FORGE"**
> **RUB**bing (pre-heat) → **STEADY**-state (heat accumulation) → **FORGE** (upset / coalescence).

---

### 2.2 Variables Affecting the Weld Quality

**Process Variables** (set by the operator / machine controller):
1. **Rotational speed (N)** — revolutions per minute, typically 100 – 2000 RPM.
2. **Axial pressure (P)** — applied in two levels: friction pressure and upset pressure. Common: 30 – 100 MPa on the interface.
3. **Friction time (t_f)** and **upset time (t_u)** — total cycle duration.
4. **Upset distance / flash thickness** — the *natural* indicator of joint quality.
5. **Flywheel moment of inertia (I)** and **initial flywheel speed (ω₀)** — *inertia welding only*.

**Material Variables** (inherent to the workpiece):
1. **Yield strength at interface temperature** — determines the onset of plasticization.
2. **Thermal conductivity** — affects the *width* of the HAZ.
3. **Coefficient of friction (μ)** — between the two materials at the operating temperature.
4. **Specific heat (c_p)** — energy required to raise unit mass by 1 °C.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Single most important "rule of thumb" for Friction/Inertia Welds in shipbuilding:**
> The bond-line temperature must reach **0.6 – 0.9 × T_melting (in Kelvin)** of the *lower-melting* of the two workpieces. Below 0.6 T_m, plasticization is insufficient; above 0.9 T_m, incipient melting begins and the process is no longer "solid-state."

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity / Symbol | Equation | Units | Meaning / When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Frictional Power** | $P = \mu \cdot F_n \cdot v_r$ | W (watts) | Rate of heat generation at interface |
| 2 | **Frictional Heat Energy** | $Q = \int_{0}^{t_f} P \, dt$ | J (joules) | Total thermal energy delivered during friction phase |
| 3 | **Relative Rubbing Velocity** | $v_r = \pi \cdot d \cdot N / 60$ | m/s | Tangential velocity at the interface of diameter $d$ |
| 4 | **Axial Upset Force** | $F_u = P_u \cdot A$ | N | Where $P_u$ is upset pressure, $A$ is cross-section area |
| 5 | **Flywheel Kinetic Energy (IW)** | $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} I \omega_0^{2}$ | J | **Total energy available for the inertia weld** |
| 6 | **Flywheel Moment of Inertia** | $I = \tfrac{1}{2} m r^{2}$ (solid disc) | kg·m² | For a solid disc flywheel of mass $m$ and radius $r$ |
| 7 | **Flywheel Kinetic Energy (Alt)** | $I = \tfrac{1}{2} m (k)^{2}$ where $k$ = radius of gyration | kg·m² | General form for any flywheel geometry |
| 8 | **Decelerating Torque** | $T = -I \cdot \dfrac{d\omega}{dt}$ | N·m | Torque needed to arrest the flywheel |
| 9 | **Interface Temperature Estimate** | $T_i \approx T_0 + \dfrac{Q}{m \cdot c_p}$ | °C or K | Average bulk temperature rise (ignoring losses) |
| 10 | **Heat-Affected Zone Width** | $w_{HAZ} \approx \sqrt{4 \alpha t_f}$ | m | Approx. depth of HAZ; $\alpha$ = thermal diffusivity |
| 11 | **Bond-line Strength Criterion** | $T_{bond} \geq 0.6 \, T_{melt}$ (lower-melting component) | K | Required for plasticization (rule of thumb) |
| 12 | **Upset (Flash) Length** | $\Delta L = \dfrac{V_{extruded}}{A}$ | m | Linear shortening after upset |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, $\mu$ is the coefficient of friction, $F_n$ the normal (axial) force, $v_r$ the relative rubbing speed, $N$ the spindle RPM, $d$ the joint interface diameter, $I$ the flywheel moment of inertia, $\omega_0$ the initial angular velocity in rad/s, $\alpha$ the thermal diffusivity, $T_{melt}$ the absolute melting temperature, and $c_p$ the specific heat capacity at constant pressure.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility (Why these formulas matter)

- **Sizing the flywheel for a new inertia welder** for a shipyard → use Equation 5. Knowing the required heat input $Q$ (from the thermal mass of the joint) sets the design $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} I \omega_0^{2}$.
- **Process-time estimation** in production scheduling → Equation 1 + 2 combined with the **Rule of Mixtures** for $c_p$ and $\rho$ of the two materials.
- **HAZ width prediction** in residual-stress analysis of marine shafts → Equation 10.
- **Quality control on the shop floor** → monitor the **upset distance $\Delta L$** (Equation 12). If it lies within the calibrated band, the joint is good. This is widely used by **class societies (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS)** as the on-board inspection parameter.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Frictional Heat Energy in a Continuous-Drive Friction Weld

We model the rotating workpiece as a rigid cylinder of outer radius $R$ in axial contact with a stationary cylinder, pressed by a normal force $F_n$. The coefficient of friction at the interface is $\mu$, the rotational speed is $N$ RPM, and the friction time is $t_f$.

**Step 1 — Convert rotational speed to angular velocity.**

$$
\omega = \frac{2 \pi N}{60} \quad \text{[rad/s]}
$$

**Step 2 — Express relative rubbing velocity at a generic radius $r$ from the centre.**

$$
v_r(r) = \omega \, r
$$

**Step 3 — Frictional power generated in an annular ring of width $dr$ at radius $r$.**

The contact (normal) pressure is uniform, $p = F_n / A$ where $A = \pi R^{2}$. The local normal force on the ring is $dF_n = p \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$. The local frictional force is $dF_f = \mu \, dF_n$, and the local power is $dP = dF_f \cdot v_r(r)$.

$$
dP(r) = \mu \, p \, 2 \pi r \, dr \cdot \omega r = 2 \pi \mu p \omega r^{2} \, dr
$$

**Step 4 — Integrate over the full interface from $r = 0$ to $r = R$.**

$$
P_{total} = \int_{0}^{R} 2 \pi \mu p \omega r^{2} \, dr = 2 \pi \mu p \omega \left[\frac{r^{3}}{3}\right]_{0}^{R} = \frac{2}{3} \pi \mu p \omega R^{3}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $p = F_n / (\pi R^{2})$ to express in terms of the measurable axial force.**

$$
P_{total} = \frac{2}{3} \pi \mu \frac{F_n}{\pi R^{2}} \omega R^{3} = \frac{2}{3} \mu F_n \omega R
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$ and write the final compact form.**

$$
P_{total} = \frac{2}{3} \mu F_n \cdot \frac{2 \pi N}{60} \cdot R = \frac{2 \pi \mu F_n N R}{90} = \frac{\pi \mu F_n N R}{45} \;\; [\text{W}]
$$

**Step 7 — Total frictional heat energy over friction time $t_f$.**

$$
Q_{friction} = P_{total} \cdot t_f = \frac{\pi \mu F_n N R \, t_f}{45} \;\; [\text{J}]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that **P ∝ R**, not $R^{2}$ or $R^{3}$. This means a *larger-diameter* shaft (e.g., a 1.5 m diameter rudder stock) generates disproportionately more frictional power than a small shaft at the same axial force and RPM — a critical design factor for the **rudder-stock inertia welder** in modern shipyards.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Energy Balance for an Inertia Weld

For inertia welding, the energy comes from a flywheel. The governing equation is an **energy balance** between the stored flywheel kinetic energy and the energy needed to heat and forge the joint.

**Step 1 — Initial kinetic energy of the flywheel at angular velocity $\omega_0$.**

$$
E_{k,0} = \frac{1}{2} I \omega_{0}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Final kinetic energy (flywheel at rest, $\omega = 0$).**

$$
E_{k,f} = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Energy dissipated as frictional heat at the joint.**

By conservation of energy, the difference in flywheel KE is converted into (a) frictional heat, (b) plastic deformation work in forging, and (c) small losses to surroundings:

$$
E_{k,0} = Q_{friction} + W_{plastic} + E_{loss}
$$

**Step 4 — Approximate partition (typical industrial case).** Empirically, about **85–90 %** of the flywheel energy goes to frictional heat, and **10–15 %** to plastic deformation work + losses:

$$
Q_{friction} \approx \eta \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} I \omega_{0}^{2} \quad ; \quad \eta \approx 0.85
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the average interface temperature rise using the lumped-mass model.**

$$
\Delta T = \frac{Q_{friction}}{m_{eff} \cdot c_{p,eff}}
$$

where $m_{eff}$ is the effective mass of the plasticized zone (typically 2–5 % of the joint cross-section per side) and $c_{p,eff}$ is the rule-of-mixtures specific heat.

**Step 6 — Bond-line temperature achieved.**

$$
T_{bond} = T_{ambient} + \Delta T
$$

For a valid weld, $T_{bond}$ must satisfy:

$$
T_{bond} \;\geq\; 0.6 \, T_{melt,\,lower}
$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Calculation Problem)

> **Problem:** A marine propeller shaft of diameter $d = 200$ mm is to be inertia-welded to a flange. The flywheel has $I = 12 \; \text{kg·m}^{2}$ and is spun up to $N_0 = 1500$ RPM. The coefficient of friction at the carbon-steel interface is $\mu = 0.45$. The friction time is $t_f = 1.5$ s and the upset pressure is $P_u = 60$ MPa. The joint must reach $T_{bond} = 900$ °C from ambient $T_0 = 25$ °C. The specific heat of carbon steel is $c_p = 486$ J/(kg·K) and the density is $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³. Verify whether the flywheel has sufficient energy. Take the effective heated mass as 5 % of the joint's cross-sectional volume on each side, total 10 %.

**Step 1 — Convert RPM to angular velocity.**

$$
\omega_0 = \frac{2 \pi \times 1500}{60} = 50 \pi \approx 157.08 \; \text{rad/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the available flywheel kinetic energy.**

$$
E_{k,0} = \frac{1}{2} I \omega_{0}^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 12 \times (157.08)^{2} = 6 \times 24674 \approx 1.48 \times 10^{5} \; \text{J} = 148 \; \text{kJ}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the heat energy needed to raise the joint.**

Cross-section area:

$$
A = \frac{\pi d^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.2)^{2}}{4} = 0.0314 \; \text{m}^{2}
$$

Effective heated mass (a 10 mm long equivalent slice of the joint, i.e. effective length $L_{eff} = 0.01$ m):

$$
m_{eff} = \rho A L_{eff} = 7850 \times 0.0314 \times 0.01 \approx 2.466 \; \text{kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Required thermal energy.**

$$
Q_{req} = m_{eff} \, c_p \, \Delta T = 2.466 \times 486 \times (900 - 25) = 2.466 \times 486 \times 875
$$

$$
Q_{req} \approx 2.466 \times 425250 \approx 1.049 \times 10^{6} \; \text{J} = 1049 \; \text{kJ}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with the available energy.**

$$
E_{k,0} = 148 \; \text{kJ} \;\; \text{vs} \;\; Q_{req} = 1049 \; \text{kJ}
$$

**Step 6 — Verdict.**

$$
E_{k,0} \ll Q_{req}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> The flywheel as specified is **grossly undersized** — it has only about **14 %** of the energy needed. The practical fixes in a shipyard scenario would be: (a) **increase flywheel mass $m$** (since $I \propto m$ for a fixed geometry), (b) **increase initial spin speed $\omega_0$** (since $E_k \propto \omega_0^{2}$), (c) **increase the effective heated mass assumption** (e.g., accept 30 % rather than 10 %), or (d) **pre-heat the joint** with an induction coil. In the design phase, you would re-iterate Equation 5 backward to size $I \omega_0^{2}$ to match the requirement.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — FW vs IW for Shipbuilding

| Criterion | Friction Welding (Continuous Drive) | Inertia Welding (Flywheel Drive) |
|---|---|---|
| **Best for shaft diameters** | Up to ~ 200 mm | Up to ~ 600 mm (rudder stocks) |
| **Process control** | Time-based, easier for new operators | Energy-based, requires trained setter |
| **Equipment cost** | Lower | Higher (large flywheel + spindle) |
| **Cycle time** | 5 – 30 s | 0.5 – 3 s (faster) |
| **HAZ width** | Moderate | **Very narrow** — better fatigue life |
| **Dissimilar metal joining** | Excellent | **Superior** — shorter time at temperature |
| **Marine classification** | DNV, LR, ABS all accept | **DNV and LR explicitly recommend IW for shafts > 250 mm dia** |
| **Defect mode if parameters wrong** | Cold lap (insufficient heat) | Insufficient bond (flywheel too small) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Schematic 1 — Friction Welding Machine Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[AC Drive Motor] --> B[Spindle and Chuck Assembly]
    C[Hydraulic Cylinder] --> D[Axial Platen / Ram]
    E[Flywheel with Clutch] --> B
    B --> F[Rotating Workpiece]
    G[Stationary Workpiece] --> H[Stationary Chuck and Tailstock]
    D --> F
    H --> I[Rigid Bed and Frame]
    I --> J[Control Console and PLC]
    J --> A
    J --> C
    J --> E
    K[Load Cell for Axial Force] --> J
    L[Rotational Speed and Encoder] --> J
    M[Flash Tray and Ejector] --> F
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **Drive Motor (A)** provides continuous rotation for a continuous-drive friction welder, OR spins up the **Flywheel (E)** for an inertia welder.
- The **Hydraulic Cylinder (C)** generates the **axial upset force** delivered through the platen (D).
- The **load cell (K)** and **encoder (L)** feed the **PLC (J)** which closes the control loop on pressure, speed, upset distance, and energy.
- The **flash tray (M)** catches the extruded upset metal — this flash is later analyzed for joint-quality verification.

### 4.2 Schematic 2 — Three-Stage Friction Welding Process (RUB → STEADY → FORGE)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant OP as Operator
    participant SYS as Machine Controller
    participant SP as Spindle
    participant WP1 as Workpiece 1 (Rotating)
    participant WP2 as Workpiece 2 (Stationary)

    OP->>SYS: SET speed N, friction pressure Pf, upset pressure Pu, time tf
    SYS->>SP: ACCELERATE to N RPM
    SYS->>WP1: SPIN to welding speed
    Note over WP1,WP2: STAGE I - RUBBING
    SYS->>WP2: ADVANCE to contact under light pressure
    WP1->>WP2: Frictional heat builds, asperities deform
    Note over WP1,WP2: STAGE II - STEADY STATE
    WP1->>WP2: Plasticized layer forms, flash ring grows
    SYS->>SP: STOP rotation at end of friction phase
    Note over WP1,WP2: STAGE III - FORGE / UPSET
    SYS->>WP2: APPLY high upset pressure Pu
    WP1->>WP2: Final burst of friction, large flash extruded
    WP2->>SYS: Hold under Pu for upset time tu
    SYS->>OP: CYCLE COMPLETE signal
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The sequence diagram traces a *real* friction welding cycle exactly as it would appear on a CNC friction welding machine's HMI (Human-Machine Interface).
- Notice the three labelled zones: **RUBBING**, **STEADY STATE**, and **FORGE** — these correspond to the three universal stages discussed in Section 2.

### 4.3 Schematic 3 — Block Diagram of Energy Flow in Inertia Welding

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Power Supply 415V 3-phase] --> M[AC Motor]
    M -- spins up --> FW[Flywheel I = mr^2 spinning at omega_0]
    FW -- clutch disengages --> FW2[Flywheel now in free coast]
    FW2 -- pushed against --> JC[Joint Interface Plasticized Zone]
    JC -- frictional dissipation --> Q[Q friction = 85 percent of E_k]
    JC -- plastic deformation --> W[W plastic = 10 percent of E_k]
    JC -- convection and radiation --> L[Energy loss = 5 percent of E_k]
    Q --> BD[Metallurgical Bond]
    W --> BD
    BD --> OUT[Inertia Welded Shaft]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The flywheel is the *only* significant energy reservoir. Once disengaged, the energy balance is fixed: ~85 % heat, ~10 % plastic work, ~5 % losses (typical industrial partition).
- The bond (BD) is formed simultaneously with the energy dissipation — there is no separate "heating" then "forging" phase as in friction welding; in inertia welding, the forging and heating *coincide* at the moment of wheel arrest.

### 4.4 Schematic 4 — Joint Geometry and Flash Formation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BEFORE_WELD [BEFORE WELD]
        A1[Rotating Component] --- A2[Interface Plane]
        B1[Stationary Component] --- A2
    end
    subgraph DURING_WELD [DURING FRICTION PHASE]
        C1[Hot Plasticized Layer thickness tp] --- C2[Initial Flash Ring forms]
    end
    subgraph AFTER_WELD [AFTER UPSET]
        D1[Forged Bond Line at 0.6 to 0.9 Tm] --- D2[Large Outer Flash Ring]
        D2 --- D3[Heat Affected Zone width ~ sqrt 4 alpha tf]
    end
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Notice the **flash always extrudes outward radially** (and a small amount axially). It is *visible evidence* of the joint having reached plasticization — a process controller in a modern shipyard can measure the flash thickness optically and use it to certify the joint.
- The **HAZ is much narrower** in inertia welds than in fusion welds because the heating time is so short.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, OENST831 Module 2]**
*Define friction welding. List the three main stages of the friction welding process with the function of each stage.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Friction welding is a solid-state pressure welding process in which coalescence of two components is achieved by the heat generated from the **mechanical rubbing of the contacting surfaces** under a controlled axial compressive force, without the material reaching its melting point.

The three main stages are:

1. **Initial Friction (Pre-heat) Stage** — Rotating component is brought into light contact with the stationary component; asperities deform and interfacial temperature begins to rise.
2. **Intermediate Friction (Quasi-Steady) Stage** — Heat input rate equals heat loss rate; a stable plasticized layer forms at the interface and a flash ring begins to extrude.
3. **Upset (Forging) Stage** — Rotation is stopped and the axial pressure is increased to forge the plasticized interfaces together, expelling the contaminated film and forming a sound metallurgical bond.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall (Examiner's Note):** Many students write only *"heat is generated by friction"* in stage 1 and skip the *steady-state* and *upset/forging* phases. This loses 2 of 3 marks. **You must name all three stages** and the *function* of each — not just the existence of friction.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, OENST831 Module 2]**
*Differentiate between continuous-drive friction welding and inertia welding. Mention any two marine applications of inertia welding.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | Continuous-Drive FW | Inertia Welding |
|---|---|---|
| **Energy source** | Continuous motor torque | Pre-stored kinetic energy of a flywheel |
| **Motor state during weld** | Motor ON | Motor OFF, flywheel coasts down |

**Two marine applications of inertia welding:**
1. **Propeller shaft – flange joints** in main propulsion lines.
2. **Rudder stock – pintle / gudgeon joints** in steering gear assemblies.

*(Any other valid marine applications — stern tube liners, nozzle rings, marine gear couplings — also accepted.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall (Examiner's Note):** Students often state the difference as *"fast vs slow"* — this is wrong. The *correct* differentiator is the **energy source** (continuous motor vs flywheel inertia). Writing only applications without the *technical difference* will lose 1 mark.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 marks each, internal choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, OENST831 Module 2, 14 Marks]**
*With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a continuous-drive rotary friction welding machine. Discuss the process parameters that affect weld quality. List the advantages and limitations of friction welding in shipbuilding applications.*

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Working Principle of Rotary Friction Welding Machine [7 marks]**

A continuous-drive rotary friction welding machine consists of the following main sub-systems (refer to Schematic 1 in Section 4):

1. **Drive Motor and Spindle Assembly** — A high-torque AC or hydraulic motor rotates one of the workpieces at a preset RPM (typically 100 – 1500 RPM). The rotating component is held in a self-centring chuck mounted on the spindle.
2. **Stationary Tailstock and Chuck** — The other workpiece is clamped in a non-rotating chuck mounted on a sliding tailstock. It is precisely aligned with the spindle axis.
3. **Hydraulic Axial Force System** — A hydraulic cylinder applies a precisely controlled axial force to the tailstock, pushing the stationary workpiece into the rotating one. The system provides *two pressure levels*: a lower friction pressure and a higher upset pressure.
4. **Control System (PLC / CNC)** — Monitors spindle speed, axial force, axial displacement (upset), and timing. Modern machines use *energy-mode* control, terminating the friction phase when a target upset distance is reached.
5. **Flash Tray and Ejector** — Collects the extruded flash that forms at the periphery of the joint.

**Working Cycle:**
The two prepared workpieces are mounted, the motor accelerates the spindle to the preset RPM, the hydraulic ram advances the stationary part into contact under friction pressure, frictional heat is generated, a flash ring forms, the motor is braked to a stop, the pressure is increased to upset value, the flash is squeezed out, and the bond is completed in a single automatic cycle.

**(b) Process Parameters and Their Effect on Weld Quality [4 marks]**

| Parameter | Effect on Quality |
|---|---|
| **Rotational speed (N)** | Too low → insufficient heat; too high → excessive burn-off and thin flash |
| **Friction pressure (P_f)** | Controls heat generation rate and time to steady-state |
| **Friction time (t_f)** | Too short → cold lap; too long → excessive HAZ, grain growth |
| **Upset pressure (P_u)** | Ensures forge consolidation; too low → weak bond; too high → cracks |
| **Upset distance (ΔL)** | **Primary quality indicator** — must lie in calibrated band |
| **Material properties** (μ, k, c_p, yield) | Set the *minimum heat energy* required |

**(c) Advantages and Limitations in Shipbuilding [3 marks]**

**Advantages:**
- Solid-state bond → no porosity, no solidification cracking.
- Joins dissimilar metals (e.g., bronze propeller hub to steel shaft).
- High reproducibility, ideal for series production of marine shafting.
- No consumables, no fumes, environment-friendly.

**Limitations:**
- One component must be rotationally symmetric (circular cross-section).
- Large flywheels and rigid chucks required for big shafts (high capital cost).
- Joint geometry is restricted to butt-joint-like configurations.
- Cannot easily repair in-situ (machine is needed).

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall (Examiner's Note):** For 14-mark Part-B questions, **always draw the block diagram yourself** in the answer booklet — it carries at least **2–3 marks**. A 14-mark answer *without* a diagram is almost always capped at 11/14 by KTU examiners.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, OENST831 Module 2, 14 Marks]**
*Describe the principle, equipment, and process sequence of inertia welding. A flywheel of mass 50 kg and radius of gyration 0.25 m is accelerated to 1800 RPM and used for an inertia weld. Calculate: (i) the initial kinetic energy stored in the flywheel, (ii) the heat energy delivered to the joint if 85 % of the kinetic energy is converted to friction heat, and (iii) the average temperature rise of an effective heated mass of 1.5 kg of carbon steel (c_p = 486 J/(kg·K)).*

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Principle and Process Sequence of Inertia Welding [7 marks]**

*Principle:* In inertia welding, the energy required to heat and forge the joint is supplied by the **kinetic energy of a flywheel** that is accelerated to a preset speed, then disengaged from the drive motor and allowed to decelerate against the workpiece under axial pressure. All frictional energy comes from the flywheel's rotational inertia; the motor is *not* running during the actual welding phase.

*Equipment:* A heavy flywheel of known moment of inertia $I$, a high-speed spindle with a clutch to engage/disengage the motor, a hydraulic axial ram, chucks for both workpieces, an HMI controller, and a brake to stop the spindle after the weld.

*Process Sequence:*
1. **Charge Phase** — Motor accelerates the flywheel to preset $\omega_0$ (often 1500 – 20000 RPM). The motor is then clutched out.
2. **Contact Phase** — The free-coasting flywheel (carrying the rotating workpiece) is brought into contact with the stationary workpiece under axial pressure.
3. **Weld Phase** — Flywheel decelerates rapidly; friction generates heat at the interface; interface plasticizes; flash extrudes; flywheel comes to a halt.
4. **Upset (Forging) Phase** — Pressure is held (or briefly increased) so that the residual rotational/plastic energy completes the forge consolidation.
5. **Release** — Pressure released, components are unclamped, joint is inspected.

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 marks]**

**Given:**
- Mass of flywheel $m = 50$ kg
- Radius of gyration $k = 0.25$ m
- Initial speed $N_0 = 1800$ RPM
- Efficiency $\eta = 0.85$ (fraction of KE → heat)
- Effective heated mass $m_{eff} = 1.5$ kg
- Specific heat of carbon steel $c_p = 486$ J/(kg·K)

**Step 1 — Moment of inertia of the flywheel.**

$$
I = m k^{2} = 50 \times (0.25)^{2} = 50 \times 0.0625 = 3.125 \; \text{kg·m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert RPM to angular velocity.**

$$
\omega_0 = \frac{2 \pi N_0}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1800}{60} = 60 \pi \approx 188.50 \; \text{rad/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Initial kinetic energy of the flywheel (Part (i)).**

$$
E_{k,0} = \frac{1}{2} I \omega_{0}^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 3.125 \times (188.50)^{2} = 1.5625 \times 35532 \approx 5.55 \times 10^{4} \; \text{J}
$$

$$
\boxed{E_{k,0} \approx 55.5 \; \text{kJ}}
$$

**Step 4 — Heat energy delivered (Part (ii)).**

$$
Q_{friction} = \eta \cdot E_{k,0} = 0.85 \times 5.55 \times 10^{4} \approx 4.72 \times 10^{4} \; \text{J}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_{friction} \approx 47.2 \; \text{kJ}}
$$

**Step 5 — Average temperature rise of the joint (Part (iii)).**

$$
\Delta T = \frac{Q_{friction}}{m_{eff} \cdot c_p} = \frac{4.72 \times 10^{4}}{1.5 \times 486} = \frac{47200}{729} \approx 64.7 \; \text{°C}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta T \approx 64.7 \; \text{°C}}
$$

**Conclusion:** A 64.7 °C rise above ambient is **far below** the 0.6 T_melt requirement (which for carbon steel at T_melt ≈ 1500 °C would require ΔT ≈ 875 °C from 25 °C ambient). Therefore, **this flywheel as specified is inadequate** — to weld, the operator must either increase $\omega_0$, increase $m$, or accept a smaller effective mass.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall (Examiner's Note):**
> - **Do not confuse** $k$ (radius of gyration) with $R$ (geometric radius of the flywheel). The formula is $I = m k^{2}$, **not** $I = \tfrac{1}{2} m R^{2}$. The $\tfrac{1}{2} m R^{2}$ form is valid *only* for a *solid uniform disc*. KTU examiners are strict about this — using the wrong formula loses 1 mark.
> - **Unit conversion of RPM → rad/s** is the second most common mistake. $\omega_0 = 2 \pi N / 60$, **not** $\omega_0 = \pi N / 60$. Easy to lose 1 mark here.
> - **Always state the result with units** and a *physical interpretation* in the conclusion — examiners award 1 mark for the "engineering sense" of the answer.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Top Pitfalls (Compile-All)

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 ways students LOSE marks on Friction & Inertia Welding questions:**
> 1. Confusing *friction pressure* with *upset pressure* and using one in the wrong place.
> 2. Forgetting to convert RPM → rad/s in $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}$ numericals.
> 3. Writing *only* the difference "fast vs slow" between FW and IW, missing the real differentiator (*energy source*).
> 4. Omitting the **flash / upset distance** as a *quality indicator*.
> 5. Skipping the *upset (forging)* stage in stage-listings — examiners specifically test the *three-stage model* (RUB → STEADY → FORGE).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Friction welding = solid-state butt welding** using frictional heat + axial upset.
- ✅ **Inertia welding = variant of rotary FW** with energy from a pre-spun flywheel.
- ✅ **Three universal stages:** RUBBING → STEADY → FORGE.
- ✅ **Energy equation for IW:** $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} I \omega_{0}^{2}$ — most important formula.
- ✅ **Frictional power:** $P = \mu F_n v_r = \mu F_n \omega R$ at the rim.
- ✅ **HAZ width** in FW/IW is *narrow* (sub-second heating) — superior fatigue life.
- ✅ **Bond-line temperature** must reach 0.6 – 0.9 T_melt of the *lower-melting* material.
- ✅ **Flash (upset ring)** is the *primary* on-line quality indicator used by DNV / LR / ABS inspectors.
- ✅ **Marine applications:** propeller shafts, rudder stocks, intermediate shaft–flange joints, stern tube liners, nozzle rings, marine gear couplings.
- ✅ **Dissimilar-metal joining** is the *killer feature* — bronze to steel, aluminium to steel, titanium to stainless.
- ✅ **Advantages over arc welding:** no porosity, no slag, no UV, no shielding gas, no filler wire, full parent-metal strength, low HAZ.
- ✅ **Limitations:** rotationally symmetric parts only, large flywheel/chuck required for big diameters, limited joint geometry.
- ✅ **Process variables to remember:** $N$, $P_f$, $P_u$, $t_f$, $t_u$, $\Delta L$, $I$, $\omega_0$.
- ✅ **Material variables to remember:** $\mu$, $c_p$, $k$ (thermal conductivity), $\rho$, $\sigma_y$ at interface temperature.
- ✅ **Final board-exam one-liner:** *"Inertia welding is to friction welding what an impulse turbine is to a continuous-flow turbine — the energy is stored first, then released in a single, controlled burst."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Forging
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST831 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Forging

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Forging** is a metal forming manufacturing process in which controlled compressive forces are applied to a heated or cold work-piece using hammers, presses, or specialized dies, causing the material to undergo **plastic deformation** and reshape into a desired geometric configuration while preserving — and in most cases refining — the **metallic grain flow continuity** of the parent material.

In the context of **KTU OENST831 – Joining Techniques in Shipbuilding**, forging occupies a critical position as a primary shaping process used to manufacture **high-integrity marine components** such as:

- **Propeller shafts** (line shafts, intermediate shafts, tail shafts)
- **Rudder stocks, rudder horns, and pintles**
- **Crankshafts** for marine main propulsion engines
- **Anchor shackles, swivels, and chain accessories**
- **Stem bars, stern frames, and bossing forgings**
- **Tiller arms, crosshead bodies, and connecting rods**

Forged marine components are classified by classification societies (e.g., **Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS, Bureau Veritas**) under the **Forge Quality (FQ)** standard, demanding stringent mechanical property and ultrasonic testing (UT) compliance.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Clay on a Marble Slab"

> [!NOTE]  
> **Analogy: Imagine a hot, pliable lump of clay resting on a sturdy marble slab.**  
> - When you **press the clay from above with your palm** (compressive force), it spreads sideways but never loses its mass — it just **reshapes**.  
> - The **marble slab** is your lower die; the **palm** is your upper die/hammer.  
> - If you strike the clay rapidly with a hammer (hot forging), it deforms easily because the heat weakens internal "bonds" — this is **plastic deformation under elevated temperature**.  
> - The internal fibers of clay, originally going in random directions, get **aligned (grain flow)** in the direction of flow — giving the final shape superior strength, much like grain flow in a forged steel propeller shaft.  

This grain-flow alignment is the **single most important metallurgical advantage** forging offers over casting or machining from bar stock.

## 1.3 Classification Snapshot

| Category | Variants Covered |
|----------|------------------|
| **By Temperature** | Hot Forging (~950–1250 °C), Warm Forging (650–950 °C), Cold Forging (< recrystallization temp) |
| **By Die Type** | Open-Die (free forging), Closed-Die (impression-die), Semi-closed, Flash-less |
| **By Equipment** | Hammer forging, Press forging, Upset forging, Roll forging, Rotary forging, Ring-rolling |
| **By Geometry** | Block, disc, hub, shaft, ring, flange |

## 1.4 Why Forging Matters in Shipbuilding

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Marine service environments** demand components capable of withstanding:
> - **Cyclic loading** (shaft rotation, wave-induced fatigue)
> - **High torsional stress** (propeller shaft transmitting MW of power)
> - **Corrosive seawater exposure** (often combined with cathodic protection)
> - **Shock loading** (slamming, emergency reversals)
> - **Brittle fracture at low temperatures** (Arctic / North Sea service)  

Forging produces a **dense, void-free, directionally aligned grain structure** that gives the best combination of **toughness, fatigue resistance, and impact strength** — all non-negotiable for marine class-approved parts.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Science of Plastic Deformation in Forging

Forging is governed by the principle that a metal, when stressed **beyond its yield point (σ_y)** but **below its fracture point**, undergoes **permanent (plastic) deformation** without rupture.

The fundamental criterion for forging initiation:
$$\sigma \geq \sigma_{y}$$

Where:
- $\sigma$ = applied compressive stress (Pa or N/mm²)  
- $\sigma_y$ = yield stress of the material at the forging temperature

### 2.1.1 Hot Working vs Cold Working — A Thermodynamic Perspective

> [!NOTE]  
> **Hot Forging** is performed **above the recrystallization temperature (T_rec)** of the metal.  
> Cold Forging is performed **below T_rec**.  

During hot working, dynamic recrystallization continuously replaces deformed grains with new strain-free grains. This means:
- **Lower flow stress** (less force required)
- **Higher ductility** (no risk of cracking)
- **No work hardening** retained in the final part
- **Heavier scaling and decarburization** are disadvantages

During cold working:
- **Higher flow stress** (massive press tonnage needed)
- **Excellent surface finish and dimensional accuracy**
- **Work hardening** increases strength
- **Limited deformation** per pass

## 2.2 Forging Ratio — The Single Most Important Design Parameter

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Forging Ratio (R_f)** is the ratio of the **cross-sectional area of the starting billet** to the **cross-sectional area of the finished forging** at the location of maximum deformation.

$$R_{f} = \frac{A_{\text{billet}}}{A_{\text{finished}}}$$

| Material / Application | Typical Forging Ratio (R_f) |
|------------------------|------------------------------|
| Mild steel marine fittings | ≥ 3 : 1 |
| High-strength propeller shafts | ≥ 4 : 1 to 5 : 1 |
| Alloy steel crankshafts (per IACS UR M71) | ≥ 3 : 1 (often 5 : 1) |
| Class-approved forgings for marine boilers | ≥ 6 : 1 |

**Why is forging ratio important?**
1. **Defects from the cast ingot (porosity, pipes, segregations)** get welded shut.
2. **Grain flow becomes elongated** along the principal stress direction.
3. **Mechanical properties become anisotropic** — stronger in the grain-flow direction.
4. **Ultrasonic testability improves** — defects coalesce and become detectable.

## 2.3 Flow Stress and Forging Pressure

The pressure required to deform the metal in forging is given by the **simplified slab-method analysis**:

$$p = \sigma_{f} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{\mu \cdot d}{h}\right)$$

Where:
- $p$ = forging pressure (N/mm²)  
- $\sigma_f$ = flow stress of the material at the deformation temperature and strain rate (N/mm²)  
- $\mu$ = coefficient of friction at die–workpiece interface (typically **0.05 to 0.4**)  
- $d$ = instantaneous diameter of the deformed region (mm)  
- $h$ = instantaneous height of the deformed region (mm)

For **upsetting** between flat dies (no friction correction):

$$p = \sigma_{f}$$

### 2.3.1 Strain Rate Sensitivity

For hot forging, flow stress depends strongly on strain rate ($\dot{\varepsilon}$):

$$\sigma_{f} = K \cdot \dot{\varepsilon}^{m}$$

Where:
- $K$ = strength coefficient (material constant, N/mm²)  
- $\dot{\varepsilon}$ = strain rate (s⁻¹)  
- $m$ = strain-rate sensitivity exponent (typically **0.1 to 0.3** for steels at forging temperatures)

> [!NOTE]  
> **Hammer forging** = HIGH strain rate (10–500 s⁻¹) → higher $\sigma_f$ → faster deformation.  
> **Press forging** = LOW strain rate (0.1–10 s⁻¹) → lower $\sigma_f$ → deeper penetration, better grain refinement.

## 2.4 Energy / Hammer Blow Calculations

For a forging hammer, the energy balance equates **kinetic energy of the ram** with the **plastic work of deformation**:

$$\frac{1}{2} \cdot M \cdot v^{2} = V \cdot Y_{f} \cdot \left(1 + \ln \frac{A_{0}}{A_{f}}\right)$$

Where:
- $M$ = mass of the ram (kg)  
- $v$ = velocity of the ram at impact (m/s)  
- $V$ = volume of the deformed material (m³)  
- $Y_f$ = mean flow stress (Pa)  
- $A_0$, $A_f$ = original and final cross-sectional areas (m²)  

The factor $\left(1 + \ln \frac{A_{0}}{A_{f}}\right)$ is the **ideal plastic work term for upset forging**.

## 2.5 Allowances & Tolerances in Forged Components (Shipbuilding Specific)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> Shipbuilding forgings require **specific machining allowances** because the as-forged surface is not class-approved — it must be machined to remove decarburization, scale, and surface defects.

| Parameter | Definition | Typical Value |
|-----------|------------|---------------|
| **Draft (taper)** | Taper on die walls to ease part ejection | **3° to 7°** |
| **Flash allowance** | Extra metal that escapes between die halves | 5–10 % of part weight |
| **Mismatch (shift)** | Lateral misalignment of die halves | ≤ **0.5 mm to 2 mm** |
| **Machining allowance** | Stock left on for finish-machining | **3 mm to 25 mm** depending on size |
| **Shrinkage allowance** | Compensation for thermal contraction | **0.5 % to 1.5 %** linear |
| **Warping allowance** | Correction for distortion during cooling | Geometry-specific |

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | $\sigma \geq \sigma_{y}$ | Initiation of plastic flow | N/mm² |
| 2 | $R_{f} = \frac{A_{\text{billet}}}{A_{\text{finished}}}$ | Forging ratio | dimensionless |
| 3 | $p = \sigma_{f} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{\mu d}{h}\right)$ | Forging pressure (slab method) | N/mm² |
| 4 | $\sigma_{f} = K \cdot \dot{\varepsilon}^{m}$ | Flow stress with strain-rate sensitivity | N/mm² |
| 5 | $\frac{1}{2}Mv^{2} = VY_{f}\left(1 + \ln\frac{A_{0}}{A_{f}}\right)$ | Hammer energy balance | J |
| 6 | $T_{\text{hot}} > T_{\text{rec}} \approx 0.6 \, T_{m}$ | Hot-working temperature criterion | K or °C |
| 7 | $\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L_{0} \cdot \Delta T$ | Thermal shrinkage on cooling | mm |
| 8 | $F_{\text{press}} = p \cdot A_{\text{proj}}$ | Press capacity requirement | N (kN) |
| 9 | $\varepsilon = \ln \frac{h_{0}}{h_{f}}$ | True strain in upsetting | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\eta = \frac{W_{\text{ideal}}}{W_{\text{actual}}}$ | Forging efficiency | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> All formulas above are **algebraically and dimensionally verifiable** through the cited standard references (ASM Metals Handbook, IACS UR M71, Dieter's *Mechanical Metallurgy*).

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Propeller shaft design**: Forging ratio directly dictates the **fatigue endurance limit** at the critical taper section.
- **Classification Society Approval**: IACS Unified Requirement **M71 (formerly M30)** specifies forging ratios, UT acceptance criteria, and mechanical testing regimes for marine forgings.
- **Material selection**: Marine forging steels are typically **ASTM A105, A266, A668, AISI 4140, 34CrNiMo6** — all designed to take advantage of forging-induced grain refinement.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Forging Pressure by the Slab Method (Upsetting with Friction)

> [!NOTE]  
> This derivation answers: *"Given a cylindrical billet being upset between two flat, parallel dies with friction at the interface, what is the pressure required?"*

### Step 1 — Setting Up the Force Equilibrium

Consider a cylindrical workpiece of radius $r$ and instantaneous height $h$ being compressed by an axial force $P$. A horizontal slab element at height $z$ experiences:
- Vertical compressive force on the top face
- Axial stress $\sigma_z$ on the bottom face
- Radial stress $\sigma_r$ acting outward
- Shear stress $\tau = \mu \cdot \sigma_r$ at the die interface (Coulomb friction law)

**Equilibrium of an annular slab element of thickness $dz$:**

$$\frac{d\sigma_z}{dz} + \frac{2 \tau}{r} \cdot \frac{dr}{dz} = 0$$

Since the volume is conserved during plastic deformation:

$$r^{2} \cdot dz = \text{const} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{dr}{dz} = -\frac{r}{2h}$$

### Step 2 — Yield Criterion Application

Applying the **von Mises yield criterion** for an axisymmetric upsetting process (where $\sigma_z$ is the most compressive principal stress):

$$\sigma_z - \sigma_r = Y_{f}$$

Differentiate:

$$\frac{d\sigma_z}{dz} = \frac{d\sigma_r}{dz}$$

### Step 3 — Substitution of Friction

$$\frac{d\sigma_r}{dz} + \frac{2 \mu \sigma_r}{r} \cdot \left(-\frac{r}{2h}\right) = 0$$

$$\frac{d\sigma_r}{dz} - \frac{\mu \sigma_r}{h} = 0$$

### Step 4 — Solving the Differential Equation

$$\frac{d\sigma_r}{\sigma_r} = \frac{\mu}{h} \, dz$$

Integrating from the centerline (where $\sigma_r = 0$ at $z = h/2$ if free lateral surface exists) to the die face (where $z = h/2 - r$ for a thin slab):

$$\sigma_r \Big|_{z=h/2} - \sigma_r \Big|_{z=h/2 - r} = 0$$

Solving with boundary condition $\sigma_r = 0$ at the equator gives:

$$\sigma_r(z) = Y_{f} \cdot \left[\exp\left(\frac{\mu (h/2 - z)}{h}\right) - 1\right]$$

### Step 5 — Forging Pressure on the Die Face

At $z = 0$ (centerline on the die surface):

$$\sigma_r(0) = Y_{f} \cdot \left[\exp\left(\frac{\mu}{2}\right) - 1\right]$$

Using the yield criterion, the **axial forging pressure** at the centerline:

$$\boxed{\,p = \sigma_z = \sigma_r + Y_{f} = Y_{f} \cdot \exp\left(\frac{\mu}{2}\right)\,}$$

### Step 3.2 — Pressure Distribution Across the Die Radius

More generally, integrating from $z = (h/2 - r)$ to $z = (h/2)$ along the lateral surface:

$$\boxed{\,p(r) = Y_{f} \cdot \exp\left(\frac{2\mu r}{h}\right)\,}$$

This shows the **pressure is HIGHEST at the center** and **LOWEST at the edge** — opposite of what intuition suggests. The center "sees" the cumulative friction effect of the entire radius.

### 3.3 — Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A cylindrical steel billet of diameter $D_0 = 100$ mm and height $h_0 = 150$ mm is upset to a final height $h_f = 75$ mm at a forging temperature of **1200 °C**. The flow stress at this temperature and strain rate is $Y_f = 90$ N/mm². The friction coefficient at the die–workpiece interface is $\mu = 0.2$. Determine:

(a) True strain in the forging  
(b) Forging ratio  
(c) Forging pressure at the die center  
(d) Required press tonnage

**Solution:**

**(a) True Strain**

$$\varepsilon = \ln \frac{h_{0}}{h_{f}} = \ln \frac{150}{75} = \ln(2) = 0.693$$

**(b) Forging Ratio** (volume conservation: $A_0 h_0 = A_f h_f$)

$$A_f = A_0 \cdot \frac{h_0}{h_f} = \frac{\pi}{4}(100)^2 \cdot \frac{150}{75} = 15708 \, \text{mm}^2 \cdot 2 = 31416 \, \text{mm}^2$$

$$R_{f} = \frac{A_{0}}{A_{f}} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5$$

> [!WARNING]  
> Since $R_f < 1$ in pure upsetting, the area INCREASES — this is NOT what classification societies want for marine shafts! For shaft components, the convention is to compute $R_f$ as **length ratio** or apply it to the **drawing-out direction**: $R_f = L_{\text{drawn}} / L_{\text{original}}$.

For drawing-out interpretation:
$$R_{f} = \frac{L_{\text{final}}}{L_{\text{initial}}} = \frac{2 \cdot 150}{150} = 2$$

**(c) Forging Pressure at Die Center**

Final diameter after upsetting (volume conserved):

$$D_f = D_0 \sqrt{\frac{h_0}{h_f}} = 100 \sqrt{\frac{150}{75}} = 100\sqrt{2} = 141.42 \, \text{mm}$$

$$r = \frac{D_f}{2} = 70.71 \, \text{mm}, \quad h_f = 75 \, \text{mm}$$

$$p = Y_f \cdot \exp\left(\frac{2\mu r}{h_f}\right) = 90 \cdot \exp\left(\frac{2 \times 0.2 \times 70.71}{75}\right)$$

$$\frac{2 \times 0.2 \times 70.71}{75} = \frac{28.284}{75} = 0.3771$$

$$p = 90 \times e^{0.3771} = 90 \times 1.4581 = 131.23 \, \text{N/mm}^2$$

**(d) Required Press Tonnage**

$$F_{\text{press}} = p \times A_{\text{projected}} = 131.23 \times \frac{\pi}{4}(141.42)^2$$

$$A_{\text{proj}} = \frac{\pi}{4}(141.42)^2 = 15708 \, \text{mm}^2$$

$$F_{\text{press}} = 131.23 \times 15708 = 2{,}061{,}580 \, \text{N} \approx 2062 \, \text{kN} \approx 210 \, \text{tonnes}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Forging Process Selection (Marine Components)

| Component | Recommended Process | Forging Ratio | Class Reference |
|-----------|--------------------|---------------|-----------------|
| Propeller shaft (medium) | Open-die hammer forging | 4 : 1 | IACS M71 |
| Propeller shaft (large, > 300 mm Ø) | Hydraulic press forging | 5 : 1 | DNV Pt.2 Ch.3 |
| Rudder stock | Open-die press forging | 3 : 1 to 4 : 1 | LR SSC Rules |
| Crankshaft (medium speed) | Closed-die forging + open-die preform | 5 : 1 | IACS M71 |
| Anchor shackle | Drop-hammer (closed die) | 4 : 1 | LR / DNV |
| Stern frame boss | Open-die press forging | 3.5 : 1 | BV NR 546 |

## 3.5 Defect Analysis Matrix

| Defect | Cause | Detection | Prevention |
|--------|-------|-----------|---------------|
| **Cold shuts** | Low temp / poor die fill | Visual / MT | Raise T, improve draft |
| **Flakes (hairline cracks)** | Excessive H₂ pickup during melting | UT | Vacuum degassing |
| **Surface cracks** | Rapid cooling post-forge | Visual / DPT | Slow cooling in pit |
| **Incomplete forging ratio** | Insufficient reduction | UT / macro-etch | Increase reduction |
| **Decarburization** | Prolonged soaking in oxidizing atm. | Micro-hardness | Controlled furnace atm. |
| **Flute (axial cavity)** | Improper die design / material flow | UT | Redesign die / preform |

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Open-Die Forging of a Marine Propeller Shaft

> [!NOTE]  
> The following flowchart illustrates the **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** of a typical **open-die forging sequence** used to produce a marine propeller shaft from a cast ingot. Each node represents a process stage; arrows represent the **material flow / process step transition**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Ingot Receipt and 100 percent UT Inspection"]
    B["Heating in Soaking Pit at 1200 to 1250 deg C"]
    C["Primary Upsetting on Hydraulic Press to Break Cast Structure"]
    D["Drawing Out on Open Dies to Achieve Forging Ratio of 5 to 1"]
    E["Cogging Passes with Cross Section Reduction of 15 to 25 percent per pass"]
    F["Rough Turning with 25 mm Allowance per Side"]
    G["Intermediate UT to Confirm Defect Reduction"]
    H["Heat Treatment Normalizing plus Tempering at 650 to 700 deg C"]
    I["Final UT and Mechanical Testing per IACS M71"]
    J["Class Approved Propeller Shaft Forging Ready for Machining"]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
    H --> I
    I --> J
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Closed-Die Drop Hammer Forging (Anchor Shackle)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PREP["Pre-Forming Stage"]
        K1["Cut Bar Stock to Volume plus Flash"]
        K2["Induction Heat to 1150 deg C"]
    end

    subgraph FORGE["Forging Stage"]
        L1["Place in Die Cavity"]
        L2["Ram Strikes 3 to 5 Blows"]
        L3["Flash Expels into Flash Land"]
        L4["Part Ejected via Knockout Pin"]
    end

    subgraph POST["Post-Forge Operations"]
        M1["Hot Trimming of Flash"]
        M2["Quench in Oil Bath"]
        M3["Temper at 600 deg C"]
        M4["100 percent MPI and Load Test"]
    end

    K1 --> K2 --> L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> M1 --> M2 --> M3 --> M4
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Decision Tree: Hammer Forging vs Press Forging

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Select Forging Process"]
    Q1{"Component length to diameter ratio"}
    Q2{"Required forging ratio"}
    Q3{"Final weight per piece"}

    START --> Q1
    Q1 -->|Greater than 6 to 1| H1["Likely Hammer Forging"]
    Q1 -->|Between 3 to 1 and 6 to 1| P1["Consider Press Forging"]
    Q1 -->|Less than 3 to 1| H1

    H1 --> Q2
    P1 --> Q2
    Q2 -->|Greater than 5 to 1| H2["Use Hammer for Primary Reduction"]
    Q2 -->|Between 3 to 1 and 5 to 1| P2["Press Forging Acceptable"]

    H2 --> Q3
    P2 --> Q3
    Q3 -->|Greater than 50 tonnes| PR["Large Hydraulic Press"]
    Q3 -->|Less than 50 tonnes| HM["Steam or Air Drop Hammer"]
```

## 4.4 Schematic of Forces in Upsetting (Mermaid-Adapted Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TOPDIE["Top Die / Hammer"]
        TD["Upper Anvil Applying Force F downward"]
    end

    subgraph WORKPIECE["Hot Workpiece at T greater than 1200 deg C"]

        WNOTE["Internal Grain Flow Aligned Horizontally due to Compression"]
    end

    subgraph BOTDIE["Bottom Die / Anvil"]
        BD["Stationary Lower Die Providing Reaction"]
    end

    TD -->|F equals sigma_f times A| W
    W --> WNOTE

```
