# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define forging. List any four marine components that are manufactured by forging.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Forging is a metal forming process in which controlled compressive forces are applied to a heated or cold work-piece using dies, hammers, or presses to cause plastic deformation and reshape the material into a desired geometry while preserving the metallic grain flow.

Four marine components manufactured by forging:
1. **Propeller shafts** (line, intermediate, tail shafts) — *1 mark*
2. **Rudder stocks and pintles** — *0.5 mark*
3. **Crankshafts** of marine propulsion engines — *0.5 mark*
4. **Anchor shackles and swivels** — *0.5 mark*

*(Total: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for examples)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define forging ratio. State its significance in marine component manufacturing.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Forging ratio** is defined as the ratio of the cross-sectional area of the original billet to that of the finished forging:

$$R_{f} = \frac{A_{\text{billet}}}{A_{\text{finished}}}$$

**Significance in marine components:** *(2 marks)*
1. **Defect consolidation**: Internal defects in the cast ingot (porosity, pipes) get welded shut.
2. **Grain flow alignment**: Improves fatigue resistance along the principal stress axis of shafts.
3. **Class compliance**: IACS UR M71 mandates minimum forging ratios (3:1 to 5:1) for marine shaft forgings.
4. **Improved UT inspectability**: Refined structure allows detection of sub-surface flaws.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — Alternative 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]
**Explain in detail the different types of forging processes with neat sketches. Highlight which types are most commonly used in shipbuilding.**

**Model Answer:**

Forging processes can be classified based on **die geometry**, **equipment**, and **temperature**.

**1. Open-Die Forging (Free Forging)** *(2 marks)*  
- Material is deformed between flat or simple contoured dies that do not enclose the work-piece completely.
- Used for **large marine components**: propeller shafts, rudder stocks, stern frames.
- Forging ratios of 3:1 to 6:1 are achievable.
- Operated on **hydraulic presses** (up to 15,000 tonnes) or **forging hammers**.

**2. Closed-Die Forging (Impression-Die Forging)** *(2 marks)*  
- Work-piece is fully enclosed within the die cavity; excess metal escapes as **flash**.
- Used for **anchor shackles, connecting rods, crosshead bodies**.
- Provides superior dimensional accuracy.
- Operated on **drop hammers** or **mechanical presses**.

**3. Upset Forging** *(1 mark)*  
- Increasing the cross-section at one end of a bar by axial compression.
- Used to pre-form **bolt heads, rudder pin ends, propeller shaft flanges**.

**4. Roll Forging** *(1 mark)*  
- Work-piece passes through contoured rolls that progressively reduce section.
- Used for **preforms of long shafts and tapered marine bars**.

**5. Ring-Rolling** *(1 mark)*  
- A thick ring is rolled between a main roll and a mandrel roll to increase diameter and reduce wall.
- Used for **propeller shaft sleeves, bearing rings, large marine flanges**.

> Most commonly used in shipbuilding: **Open-die forging** (for shafts) and **closed-die forging** (for fittings).

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]
**A cylindrical steel billet of diameter 150 mm and height 200 mm is upset isothermally at 1200 °C. The flow stress at the deformation temperature is 100 N/mm² and the coefficient of friction at the die-workpiece interface is 0.15. Determine the forging pressure at the centre of the die when the billet has been upset to a height of 100 mm. Also calculate the press tonnage required.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Find the new diameter after upsetting** (volume conservation):

$$D_f = D_0 \sqrt{\frac{h_0}{h_f}} = 150 \sqrt{\frac{200}{100}} = 150\sqrt{2} = 212.13 \, \text{mm}$$

**Step 2 — Forging pressure at die center** (using slab-method formula):

$$p = Y_f \cdot \exp\left(\frac{2\mu r}{h_f}\right)$$

$$r = \frac{D_f}{2} = 106.07 \, \text{mm}, \quad h_f = 100 \, \text{mm}, \quad \mu = 0.15, \quad Y_f = 100 \, \text{N/mm}^2$$

$$\frac{2\mu r}{h_f} = \frac{2 \times 0.15 \times 106.07}{100} = \frac{31.82}{100} = 0.3182$$

$$p = 100 \times e^{0.3182} = 100 \times 1.3747 = 137.47 \, \text{N/mm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Press tonnage**:

$$F_{\text{press}} = p \times A_{\text{proj}} = 137.47 \times \frac{\pi}{4}(212.13)^2$$

$$A_{\text{proj}} = \frac{\pi}{4}(212.13)^2 = 35{,}345 \, \text{mm}^2$$

$$F_{\text{press}} = 137.47 \times 35{,}345 = 4{,}859{,}374 \, \text{N} \approx 4859 \, \text{kN} \approx 495 \, \text{tonnes}$$

> [!WARNING]  
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting volume conservation**: Many students assume the diameter stays the same. Always compute $D_f$ first.
> 2. **Using the wrong exponent**: The formula uses $\frac{2\mu r}{h_f}$, not $\frac{\mu d}{h}$. Mixing these leads to a factor-of-2 error.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert kN to tonnes** (÷9.81) — KTU expects the answer in **tonnes (metric)** for press sizing.
> 4. **Neglecting friction when given μ**: Even small μ significantly amplifies pressure.

---

### Question B — Alternative 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]
**Discuss the forging of marine propeller shafts. Include details on material selection, forging sequence, and post-forge inspection as per IACS Unified Requirements.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Material Selection for Marine Propeller Shafts** *(2 marks)*  
Common grades (per LR / DNV / ABS):
- **ASTM A668 Class E/F** (carbon-steel forgings)
- **ASTM A266 Grade 2/3** (carbon and C-Mo steel)
- **34CrNiMo6 / 40CrNiMo** (alloy steel for high-strength applications)
- **F22V** (Cr-Mo-V for high-temperature service)

Selection criteria: yield strength ≥ 250 MPa, Charpy V-notch ≥ 27 J at 0 °C, good weldability, class-approved source.

**2. Forging Sequence** *(3 marks)*  
- **Step 1 — Ingot preparation**: UT inspection; cropping top/bottom (≥ 20% each).
- **Step 2 — Heating**: 1200–1250 °C in gas-fired soaking pit; soak time ≥ 1 hour per 25 mm section.
- **Step 3 — Upsetting**: Hydraulic press upset to break cast structure.
- **Step 4 — Drawing-out (cogging)**: Multiple passes reducing area by 15–20% per blow; aim for forging ratio ≥ 4:1 (typically 5:1 for IACS M71 class).
- **Step 5 — Rough turning**: 25 mm allowance per side.
- **Step 6 — Heat treatment**: Normalize + temper (or quench + temper for alloy grades).
- **Step 7 — Final UT + mechanical tests**.

**3. Post-Forge Inspection per IACS M71** *(2 marks)*  
- **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)**: 100% volumetric scan per ASTM A388.
- **Mechanical tests**: Tensile, Charpy V-notch (3 specimens at specified temp).
- **Chemical analysis**: Ladle + product analysis.
- **Macro-etch**: For forging ratio verification on a transverse disc.
- **Surface inspection**: Magnetic Particle (MT) or Dye Penetrant (DPT).
- **Class surveyor's witnessed testing** for **FQ (Forge Quality)** certification.

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]
**A marine crankshaft preform is to be upset from a cylindrical billet of 250 mm diameter and 400 mm height to a height of 200 mm at 1150 °C. The flow stress at this temperature is 120 N/mm² and μ = 0.2. Calculate: (i) the new diameter, (ii) the forging ratio (in upsetting sense), (iii) the maximum forging pressure, and (iv) the press tonnage.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) New diameter** (volume conservation):

$$D_f = D_0 \sqrt{\frac{h_0}{h_f}} = 250 \sqrt{\frac{400}{200}} = 250\sqrt{2} = 353.55 \, \text{mm}$$

**(ii) Forging ratio** (area ratio):

$$R_f = \frac{A_0}{A_f} = \frac{h_f}{h_0} = \frac{200}{400} = 0.5$$

> For upsetting, since the cross-section grows, $R_f < 1$. In the **elongation sense** (length/drawing-out): $R_f = 400/200 = 2$. **For marine crankshafts, the forging ratio is reported in the elongation direction achieved during subsequent cogging passes** — typical target is ≥ 5:1 overall.

**(iii) Forging pressure** (slab method):

$$p = Y_f \cdot \exp\left(\frac{2\mu r}{h_f}\right)$$

$$r = \frac{D_f}{2} = 176.78 \, \text{mm}, \quad h_f = 200 \, \text{mm}, \quad \mu = 0.2, \quad Y_f = 120 \, \text{N/mm}^2$$

$$\frac{2\mu r}{h_f} = \frac{2 \times 0.2 \times 176.78}{200} = \frac{70.71}{200} = 0.3536$$

$$p = 120 \times e^{0.3536} = 120 \times 1.4242 = 170.90 \, \text{N/mm}^2$$

**(iv) Press tonnage**:

$$F_{\text{press}} = p \times A_{\text{proj}} = 170.90 \times \frac{\pi}{4}(353.55)^2$$

$$A_{\text{proj}} = \frac{\pi}{4}(353.55)^2 = 98{,}175 \, \text{mm}^2$$

$$F_{\text{press}} = 170.90 \times 98{,}175 = 16{,}775{,}408 \, \text{N} \approx 16{,}775 \, \text{kN} \approx 1710 \, \text{tonnes}$$

> [!WARNING]  
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Points:**
> 1. **Mixing up forging ratio interpretation** — must specify whether it is in upsetting or drawing-out sense.
> 2. **Press tonnage calculation** — never forget to multiply by the projected area after deformation, not the original area.
> 3. **Units**: KTU accepts answer in **kN** or **tonnes (metric)**; both should be stated for clarity.
> 4. **Flow stress assumption**: The problem assumes $Y_f$ is given — if not stated, students must look up the value in ASM Metals Handbook or recall from the syllabus data tables.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST — FORGING (MODULE 2)**

- [x] **Definition**: Plastic deformation under compressive forces to shape metal while preserving grain flow.
- [x] **Types**: Open-die, closed-die, upset, roll, ring-roll — match process to component size & geometry.
- [x] **Marine applications**: Propeller shafts, rudder stocks, crankshafts, anchor shackles, stern frames, tiller arms.
- [x] **Forging ratio**: $R_f = A_{\text{billet}} / A_{\text{finished}}$; target ≥ 3:1 to 6:1 for class-approved marine parts.
- [x] **Slab-method pressure**: $p = Y_f \cdot \exp(2\mu r / h_f)$ — pressure peaks at die center.
- [x] **Strain rate sensitivity**: $\sigma_f = K \cdot \dot{\varepsilon}^{m}$ — hammers give higher $p$ than presses.
- [x] **Hammer energy balance**: $\frac{1}{2}Mv^2 = VY_f(1 + \ln(A_0/A_f))$.
- [x] **Forging temperature**: Hot forging $T > 0.6 T_m$; for steel ≈ 950–1250 °C.
- [x] **Allowances**: Draft (3°–7°), flash (5–10% weight), machining (3–25 mm), shrinkage (0.5–1.5%).
- [x] **IACS UR M71**: Defines forging ratio, UT acceptance, mechanical test requirements for marine forgings.
- [x] **Defects to remember**: Cold shuts, flakes, surface cracks, incomplete forging ratio, decarburization, flutes.
- [x] **Heat treatment**: Normalize + temper (carbon steel) or quench + temper (alloy steel) — required post-forge.
- [x] **Inspection stack**: 100% UT (ASTM A388) + tensile + Charpy V-notch + macro-etch + MT/DPT.
- [x] **Volume conservation**: $A_0 h_0 = A_f h_f$ — used to find dimensions after each forging step.
- [x] **Press sizing**: $F = p \times A_{\text{proj}}$ — convert N to kN (÷1000) and to tonnes (÷9.81).
- [x] **Key materials for marine forging**: ASTM A668, A266, 34CrNiMo6, F22V.
- [x] **Friction influence**: Even a small μ (0.1–0.2) can increase pressure by **30–50%** — always include it in calculations.
- [x] **Grain flow**: Continuous along principal stress axis → superior fatigue and impact performance vs. cast.
- [x] **Class approval**: Forgings for marine use must be certified by classification societies (LR, DNV, ABS, BV).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principle and Welding Arrangements: Advantages and Disadvantages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OENST831 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Principle and Welding Arrangements: Advantages and Disadvantages ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Welding** is a **fusion joining process** in which two or more pieces of metal (or thermoplastic) are united by heating the surfaces to a suitable temperature, with or without the application of pressure and with or without the use of a filler material, to produce a **permanent metallurgical bond** across the joint interface. In the context of **shipbuilding**, welding is the *primary* fabrication technique used to assemble the hull, superstructure, decks, bulkheads, and internal stiffeners. It has largely replaced riveting because it offers lighter structures, superior watertightness, and higher production rates. ### 1.2 Principle of Welding — The Three Essentials The principle of welding rests upon three indispensable physical conditions that must be simultaneously satisfied: 1. **Generation of sufficient heat** to bring the base metal (and filler metal) to a plastic or molten state. 2. **Application of pressure (mechanical force)** — either externally applied or generated internally by thermal expansion/contraction — to bring the atoms of the two surfaces within atomic-range interaction distance (≈ 3 × 10⁻¹⁰ m). 3. **Atomic diffusion and metallurgical bonding** across the interface, forming either a *solid-state bond* (no melting) or a *fusion bond* (with melting and re-solidification). > [!NOTE] > **Classification based on the above principle:** > - **Fusion Welding** — Both pressure and filler may be used; the base metal melts. (e.g., SMAW, GMAW, GTAW, SAW, FCAW — all used extensively in shipyards). > - **Pressure Welding** — Heat is supplied below the melting point; pressure does the bonding. (e.g., Resistance Spot Welding, Forge Welding, Friction Welding). > - **Brazing & Soldering** — Only the filler melts (below 450 °C for soldering; above 450 °C for brazing). ### 1.3 Welding Arrangements — Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > A **welding arrangement** refers to the **geometric configuration** in which two or more parts are positioned relative to each other and relative to the welder (or welding torch) before and during the welding operation. It is described by two orthogonal concepts: > 1. **Joint Type** — the *geometry of the parts* at the interface (Butt, Lap, Tee, Corner, Edge). > 2. **Welding Position** — the *orientation of the joint* in space (Flat, Horizontal, Vertical, Overhead). ### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — "Gluing Two Papers with Controlled Heat"** 📄🔥 > Imagine you have two sheets of metal and you want to fuse them forever. Simply pressing them together at room temperature does nothing — the atoms are too far apart. If you press them *and* heat them strongly enough, the metal at the surface becomes soft/molten (think of butter becoming tacky when warm), and the atoms can now "shake hands" across the boundary. When they cool, they are locked together as one continuous piece — this is exactly what welding does. > > **Welding arrangement** is analogous to *how* you hold the two papers: > - **Edge-to-edge (Butt joint)** = most efficient, strongest. > - **Overlapping (Lap joint)** = easier to hold, but weaker. > - **Standing up vertical (Vertical position)** = harder, gravity pulls the molten metal down. > - **Lying flat (Flat position)** = easiest, gravity helps. > > This is why shipbuilders *prefer* flat-position butt welds whenever possible — they are faster, stronger, and cheaper. ### 1.5 Standardized Coding for Welding Arrangements (ISO 6947 / AWS A3.0) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU students must memorize the **standard position codes**: > - **PA** = Flat (Position 1) > - **PB** = Horizontal (Position 2) > - **PC** = Vertical (Position 3) > - **PD** = Overhead (Position 4) > - **PE** = Horizontal (fixed pipe) → used in pipe welding onboard ships. > - **PF** = Vertical-up (fixed pipe, restricted access) **Joint-type codes (AWS A2.4):** - **BW** = Butt Weld - **FW** = Fillet Weld - **PW** = Plug/Pocket Weld - **SW** = Slot Weld --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Principle of Welding — Step-by-Step Breakdown The welding process, irrespective of the heat source (electric arc, gas flame, laser, electron beam, friction), obeys the following sequential logic: **Step 1 — Heat Generation at the Interface** The heat source (arc, flame, or friction) deposits energy into the joint zone. The **heat input** (per unit length of weld) is governed by: $$Q = \eta \cdot \frac{V \cdot I}{v}$$ where: - $Q$ = Heat input in **Joules per millimetre (J/mm)** - $V$ = Arc voltage in **Volts (V)** - $I$ = Welding current in **Amperes (A)** - $v$ = Travel speed in **mm/s** - $\eta$ = Process thermal efficiency (dimensionless) > [!NOTE] > **Typical η values (must memorize for shipbuilding context):** > - **SMAW (Stick)** → η ≈ 0.70 – 0.85 > - **GMAW (MIG/MAG)** → η ≈ 0.65 – 0.85 > - **GTAW (TIG)** → η ≈ 0.30 – 0.50 > - **SAW (Submerged Arc)** → η ≈ 0.85 – 0.95 > - **FCAW** → η ≈ 0.70 – 0.85 > - **PAW (Plasma Arc)** → η ≈ 0.55 – 0.75 > - **Electroslag (ESW)** → η ≈ 0.85 – 0.90 *(heavily used in shipbuilding for thick plate butt welds in the hull)* **Step 2 — Melting and Formation of Weld Pool** The local temperature rises above the **melting point** of the base metal (≈ 1500 °C for mild steel). A molten pool, called the **weld pool** or **fusion zone**, forms. The depth to which the base metal melts is the **penetration depth**. **Step 3 — Filler Metal Deposition (for fusion welding with filler)** Electrode (consumable) or filler wire melts and transfers droplets into the weld pool, mixing with the molten base metal. The mixture solidifies to form the **weld bead**. **Step 4 — Solidification and Metallurgical Bonding** As the heat source moves away, the weld pool cools. Columnar grains grow inward from the fusion boundary (epitaxial solidification). The result is a continuous metallic lattice across what was originally two separate pieces. **Step 5 — Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ) Formation** Adjacent to the fusion zone, the base metal does not melt but undergoes microstructural changes due to elevated temperatures. This region is called the **Heat-Affected Zone** and is often the *weakest* part of the welded joint, especially in higher-strength shipbuilding steels (e.g., AH36, DH36, EH36 per IACS UR S31). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters in shipbuilding:** Classification societies (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, Bureau Veritas) impose **strict limits on heat input** for high-strength marine steels to preserve HAZ toughness. Exceeding Q = 3.5 kJ/mm for thick DH36 plates, for instance, embrittles the HAZ. ### 2.2 Detailed Taxonomy of Welding Arrangements #### A. By Joint Type (Five Standard Types per AWS / ISO 9692) | Joint Type | Symbol | Description | Typical Shipbuilding Use | |---|---|---|---| | **Butt Joint** | BW | Two members lie in the same plane, joined at their edges | Hull plating seams, deck plating seams, longitudinal welds | | **Lap Joint** | FW (fillet) | Two members overlap each other | Stiffener-to-plate attachments (light work) | | **Tee Joint** | FW (fillet) | One member is perpendicular to the other, forming a "T" | Frames-to-shell, bulkhead-to-deck | | **Corner Joint** | FW | Two members meet at an angle (often 90°) | Hatch corners, coaming-to-deck | | **Edge Joint** | — | Two parallel edges are brought together | Sheet-metal ducting, vent pipes | #### B. By Welding Position (per ISO 6947) | Position | Code | Orientation | Difficulty | Shipyard Preference | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Flat** | PA (1G/1F) | Weld face points up | Easiest | **Most preferred** — robotic/automated lines | | **Horizontal** | PB (2F) | Weld runs horizontally, axis vertical | Moderate | Common for vertical shell plating | | **Vertical** | PC (3F/3G) | Weld axis vertical (up or down) | Hard | Vertical seams of hull, derrick booms | | **Overhead** | PD (4F/4G) | Weld face points down | Hardest | Restricted use, mostly manual | ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!NOTE] > **Formula Table — All Key Equations for Welding Calculations** | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Application | |---|---|---|---| | Heat Input | $Q = \eta \cdot \frac{V \cdot I}{v}$ | Q [J/mm], V [V], I [A], v [mm/s] | Selecting process parameters | | Deposition Rate | $R = k \cdot I$ (linear approx.) | R [kg/h], I [A], k = process constant | Estimating welding time | | Theoretical Weld Metal Weight | $W = \rho \cdot A_{cs} \cdot L$ | ρ ≈ 7.85 g/cm³ (steel), A_cs [cm²], L [cm] | Consumable costing | | Cooling Time (t₈/₅) | Empirical from 2D/3D heat-flow models | [s] | HAZ hardness control | | Welding Cost | $C_t = C_e + C_g + C_l + C_o$ | C_e=electrode, C_g=gas, C_l=labor, C_o=overhead | Shipyard estimation | | Heat-Affected Zone Hardness (Empirical) | $HV \approx f(\text{CE},\, t_{8/5})$ | CE = Carbon Equivalent | Critical for DH/EH grade acceptance | | Carbon Equivalent (IIW formula) | $CE_{IIW} = C + \frac{Mn}{6} + \frac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \frac{Ni+Cu}{15}$ | All wt.% | Preheating determination | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Shipbuilding Threshold:** If $CE_{IIW} > 0.45$, **preheating to ≥ 100 °C is mandatory** before welding, per IACS UR W11/W31 requirements. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Hull Erection:** Modern shipyards (e.g., Korean, Japanese mega-yards) use **panel-line welding** in flat position (PA) using **Submerged Arc Welding (SAW)** with multiple heads — productivity reaches **> 1 metre of butt weld per minute**. - **Block Assembly:** Heavy **Electroslag (ESW)** and **Electrogas (EGW)** vertical-up welds join thick plates in the *vertical position* on stiffeners and bulkheads. - **Pipe Welding Onboard:** All six pipe positions (1G–6G) must be qualified by welders — **PE and PF** are essential for engine-room piping. - **Cost Control:** Heat-input miscalculation can waste tonnes of electrodes. A correct Q-value selection reduces HAZ cracking, repair costs, and downtime. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Heat Input Formula The **heat input** is the total energy deposited per unit length of weld. It is *not* an empirical law but a direct energy-balance identity. **Start from the definition:** Power delivered by the arc: $$P = V \cdot I \quad [\text{Watts}]$$ Time spent on a unit length of weld: $$t = \frac{1}{v} \quad [\text{seconds per mm}]$$ Energy per unit length (gross): $$Q_{gross} = \frac{P}{v} = \frac{V \cdot I}{v}$$ However, **not all electrical energy** reaches the weld pool — some is radiated, convected, and conducted into the surroundings. The **process efficiency** η accounts for this loss: $$\eta = \frac{Q_{useful}}{Q_{gross}}$$ Therefore the **effective heat input** delivered to the joint is: $$Q = \eta \cdot \frac{V \cdot I}{v}$$ **Result:** $$\boxed{Q = \eta \cdot \frac{V \cdot I}{v}}$$ ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Heat Input Calculation **Problem (KTU-style):** A shipyard welds **AH36 grade steel** hull plates using **Submerged Arc Welding (SAW)**. The parameters are: - Arc voltage: $V = 32 \text{ V}$ - Current: $I = 600 \text{ A}$ - Travel speed: $v = 30 \text{ mm/s}$ (= 1.8 m/min) - Process efficiency for SAW: $\eta = 0.90$ **Find:** Heat input $Q$ in J/mm, and verify it does **not exceed** the IACS UR W31 limit of **3.5 kJ/mm** for this plate thickness. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Substitute the values into the formula.** $$ \begin{aligned} Q &= \eta \cdot \frac{V \cdot I}{v} \\ &= 0.90 \cdot \frac{32 \cdot 600}{30} \\ &= 0.90 \cdot \frac{19200}{30} \\ &= 0.90 \cdot 640 \\ &= 576 \text{ J/mm} \\ &= 0.576 \text{ kJ/mm} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Comparison with the regulatory limit.** $$ 0.576 \text{ kJ/mm} \; < \; 3.5 \text{ kJ/mm} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \textbf{Complies.} $$ **Step 3 — Engineering interpretation.** The chosen parameters yield a heat input **six times lower** than the upper limit. This is desirable for AH36 plates: a *lower* heat input produces a finer HAZ microstructure with superior toughness — exactly what classification societies demand. > [!NOTE] > **If the question asked the inverse** — *"Find the maximum allowable travel speed for Q = 3.5 kJ/mm"* — we simply rearrange: > $$v_{max} = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{Q_{max}} = \frac{0.90 \cdot 32 \cdot 600}{3500} = 4.94 \text{ mm/s} \approx 0.296 \text{ m/min}$$ ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Advantages vs. Disadvantages of Welding Arrangements > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mandatory KTU Comparison Table — memorize this for the 14-mark question.** #### A. By JOINT TYPE | Joint Type | Advantages | Disadvantages | |---|---|---| | **Butt Joint** | • Highest strength (stress aligned with weld axis)
• Uniform stress distribution
• Most efficient use of plate
• Ideal for radiographic inspection
• Preferred for pressure hulls and tanks | • Requires precise edge preparation (groove angle)
• Higher skill required for accurate alignment
• More filler metal consumed
• Sensitive to fit-up gap | | **Lap Joint** | • Simplest edge preparation
• Tolerant of poor fit-up
• Faster welding (single-pass fillet possible)
• No edge beveling needed | • Stress concentration at the overlap tip
• Trapped slag/corrosion in crevices (corrosion-fatigue risk)
• Less efficient use of plate (overlap area is redundant)
• Lower fatigue strength — *forbidden in critical hull structural lines* | | **Tee Joint** | • Easy to fit up for stiffeners
• Suitable for fillet welds on both sides
• Efficient in block assembly
• High productivity with automatic fillet heads | • Stress concentration at the toe of the weld
• Lamellar tearing risk in thick plates (rolling direction)
• Single-sided fillet welds leave an unwelded root | | **Corner Joint** | • Useful where two plates meet at 90° (hatch coamings, etc.)
• Can be welded from inside (one-side access) | • Difficult to achieve full penetration
• Tendency for lack-of-fusion at the corner root | | **Edge Joint** | • Simple, minimal edge prep | • Limited strength
• Rarely used in primary ship structures | #### B. By WELDING POSITION | Position | Advantages | Disadvantages | |---|---|---| | **Flat (PA)** | • **Easiest** — gravity keeps weld pool in place
• Highest deposition rate possible
• Best bead appearance
• Ideal for mechanization (carriage, robot)
• Lowest welder fatigue | • Not always geometrically possible (overhead structures, internal compartments) | | **Horizontal (PB)** | • Moderate ease; good for vertical shell plating
• Fair deposition rate
• Reasonable bead control | • Tendency for undercut on the upper plate
• Slag inclusion risk in fillet welds | | **Vertical (PC)** | • Only option for vertical seams
• Allows welding of erected blocks | • Gravity sags the molten pool → requires low current / weave technique
• Slower travel speeds
• Higher welder skill demanded
• Higher spatter loss | | **Overhead (PD)** | • Sometimes the only physically possible orientation (e.g., bottom of deck, ceiling plating) | • **Most difficult** — molten metal wants to fall
• Highest spatter & porosity risk
• Lowest productivity
• Severe welder fatigue and safety hazard (falling spatter) | #### C. OVERALL Welding (versus Riveting) — Shipbuilding Perspective > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **classic 14-mark question** in OENST831. **Advantages of Welded Ship Construction:** 1. **Lighter hull** — eliminates rivet overlaps; weight saving ~10–15% compared to riveted ships. 2. **Watertight / airtight joints** — critical for tankers, submarines, LNG carriers. 3. **Higher strength-to-weight ratio** — butt welds in modern mild/HTS steel can equal base-metal strength. 4. **Lower fabrication cost** — faster production; fewer parts; less labour. 5. **Smoother hull surface** — improved hydrodynamic efficiency (reduced frictional resistance by ~5–8%). 6. **Easier repair in service** — gouging and re-welding is straightforward; riveting repair requires cutting and re-drilling. 7. **Better use of high-tensile steel** — riveting HT steel is impractical due to cracking risk. 8. **Reduced noise & vibration transmission** — continuous joints dampen structure-borne noise. 9. **Modern automation-friendly** — GMAW/SAW/FCAW robots dominate new shipyards. 10. **Larger block sizes possible** — welded mega-blocks can reach 400+ tonnes vs. riveted limits. **Disadvantages of Welded Ship Construction:** 1. **Residual stresses & distortion** — uneven heating causes shrinkage and warpage; requires correction (flame straightening, pre-setting). 2. **Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ) embrittlement** — loss of toughness in critical steels; requires heat-input control. 3. **Welding defects** — porosity, slag inclusion, lack of fusion, undercuts, cracks → **NDT mandatory** (UT, RT, MPI, DPI). 4. **High initial capital investment** — automatic lines, positioners, robots are expensive (favours large yards). 5. **Highly skilled workforce required** — welders must hold classification-society certificates per **ISO 9606-1**. 6. **Brittle fracture risk** — poor weld quality + low temperature + thick plate = catastrophic crack propagation (e.g., **Liberty ship failures** during WWII). 7. **Difficult to inspect and repair underwater** — whereas rivets can be visually checked. 8. **Health & safety hazards** — fumes (especially manganese, hexavalent Cr from SS), UV radiation, electric shock. 9. **Susceptible to lamellar tearing** in T-joints with thick plates. 10. **Post-weld treatment (PWHT)** sometimes required for thick sections → time and cost overhead. --- ## 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** > *Define the principle of welding. List any **two** classification methods for welding processes.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Principle:** Welding is a permanent joining process in which two similar or dissimilar metals are united by **heating** them to a suitable temperature (with or without pressure) so that atomic bonding occurs across the interface, producing a continuous metallic lattice. (2 marks) **Two classifications (any two):** 1. Based on heat source — Arc, Gas, Resistance, Friction, Laser, Electron beam. 2. Based on state of metal during joining — Fusion welding, Pressure (solid-state) welding, Brazing & Soldering. (1 mark for listing two valid classifications) --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** > *What is meant by "welding position"? Name the **four** standard welding positions with their ISO codes.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Welding position** is the orientation of the welded joint in space relative to the earth (gravity) and the welder. It determines weld-pool behaviour, deposition rate, and required skill. (1 mark for definition) | Position | ISO Code | AWS Code | |---|---|---| | Flat | PA | 1G / 1F | | Horizontal | PB | 2F | | Vertical | PC | 3G / 3F | | Overhead | PD | 4G / 4F | (2 marks — ½ mark per correctly named position with code) --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice) > **Q3. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]** > > **(A)** *(7 marks)* Explain the **principle of welding** in detail. Derive the formula for **heat input** and discuss the role of process efficiency (η) with examples for **SMAW, GMAW, GTAW and SAW**. > > **(B)** *(7 marks)* Discuss the **advantages and disadvantages of welded ship construction** compared to the older riveted construction. Highlight at least **six** advantages and **six** disadvantages relevant to a modern shipyard. ### Solution — Question 3(A) — Principle of Welding & Heat Input **(a) Definition and stages (3 marks):** Welding joins metals by heating the interface to the plastic or molten state, applying pressure (external or internal), and allowing atomic diffusion to create a metallurgical bond. The five sequential stages are: 1. Heat generation 2. Local melting → weld pool 3. Filler-metal transfer 4. Solidification → fusion bond 5. HAZ formation in adjacent base metal **(b) Heat-input derivation (2.5 marks):** As derived in Section 3.1, $Q = \eta \cdot \frac{V \cdot I}{v}$, where η is the thermal efficiency. **(c) Process efficiency table (1.5 marks):** | Process | η | |---|---| | SAW | 0.85 – 0.95 | | SMAW | 0.70 – 0.85 | | FCAW | 0.70 – 0.85 | | GMAW | 0.65 – 0.85 | | GTAW | 0.30 – 0.50 | Interpretation: SAW is preferred for thick hull seams (highest η → highest productivity). GTAW gives the lowest η but the **best quality**, hence used for stainless-steel pipe welding and critical root passes. (✓ complete answer) --- ### Solution — Question 3(B) — Welded vs. Riveted Ship Construction **Six advantages of welded construction (3 marks):** 1. **Lighter hull** – no rivet overlap zone; weight saving ~10–15%. 2. **Watertight joints** – essential for tankers, submarines, bulk carriers. 3. **Higher strength-to-weight ratio** – butt welds can match base-metal strength. 4. **Faster production** – lower labour hours per tonne of steel. 5. **Smoother hull surface** – reduced frictional resistance → better fuel efficiency. 6. **Easier in-service repair** – weld removal & re-welding is straightforward. **Six disadvantages of welded construction (3 marks):** 1. **Residual stresses & distortion** – from uneven heating/cooling. 2. **HAZ embrittlement** – loss of toughness in higher-strength shipbuilding steels. 3. **Welding defects** – porosity, slag, lack of fusion, cracks; **NDT mandatory**. 4. **Brittle fracture risk** – historical lesson: Liberty ship failures of WWII. 5. **Highly skilled welders required** – ISO 9606-1 certification needed. 6. **Health hazards** – fumes (Mn, Cr⁶⁺ from stainless), UV radiation, electric shock. **Conclusion (1 mark):** Despite disadvantages, welded construction dominates modern shipbuilding because its advantages (weight, speed, watertightness) outweigh the limitations — provided **qualified welders, controlled procedures (WPS/PQR per ISO 15614), and rigorous NDT** are enforced. --- > **Alternative Question B (Internal Choice — KTU pattern):** > > **Q3B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** > > **(A)** *(7 marks)* With neat sketches, explain the **five standard joint types** used in shipbuilding. Indicate which joint is preferred for primary hull seams and justify why. > > **(B)** *(7 marks)* Compare the **four welding positions** (flat, horizontal, vertical, overhead) on the basis of difficulty, productivity, and weld quality. Recommend the most suitable positions for (i) panel-line welding in the workshop, (ii) ship-side butt welds during block erection. ### Solution — Question 3B(A) — Five Joint Types | Joint | Description | Shipbuilding Application | |---|---|---| | Butt | Two plates in same plane, joined at edges | **Primary hull seams** ✓ | | Lap | Plates overlap | Light stiffeners (rarely primary) | | Tee | One plate perpendicular to other | Frames-to-shell, bulkhead stiffeners | | Corner | Two plates at angle (often 90°) | Hatch coamings | | Edge | Parallel edges | Ventilation ducts | **Preferred for primary hull seams = BUTT joint** because: - Stress aligned with weld axis → no eccentricity. - Full-penetration possible with radiographic inspection. - Efficient use of plate (no overlap area wasted). - Highest fatigue strength (Class society requirement). (7 marks: 1 per joint description, 2 marks for justification of butt joint as preferred.) --- ### Solution — Question 3B(B) — Four Welding Positions | Position | Difficulty | Productivity | Quality | |---|---|---|---| | Flat (PA) | Easiest | Highest | Excellent | | Horizontal (PB) | Moderate | Good | Good (undercut risk) | | Vertical (PC) | Hard | Low | Requires weave technique | | Overhead (PD) | Hardest | Lowest | Worst (porosity, spatter) | **(i) Panel-line welding in workshop → Flat (PA)** — parts are rolled into positioners so the joint lies horizontally; gravity assists the weld pool; SAW/FCAW multi-head lines can achieve > 1 m/min. **(ii) Ship-side butt welds during block erection → Vertical (PC)** — the seam runs vertically up the ship's side; the only practical position. Welder uses weave technique or vertical-down stringer beads. (7 marks: 3 marks for comparison table, 4 marks for justified recommendations with shipyard logic.) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout** > ❌ **Common mistakes students make (each costs 1–2 marks):** > 1. Confusing **welding position** with **joint type** — they are *two orthogonal classifications*. Examiners deduct marks if you interchange them. > 2. Writing *"welding is melting two metals and joining them"* — too vague. You must mention **atomic diffusion, metallurgical bonding, heat + pressure**. > 3. Forgetting the units of **heat input** (J/mm or kJ/mm) — a number without units is incomplete. > 4. In the *advantages question*, listing fewer than six points → lose balance of marks. > 5. Writing "**welding is better than riveting in every way**" — examiners want a *balanced* answer. Always include both advantages AND disadvantages. > 6. Not mentioning **NDT (Non-Destructive Testing)** when discussing welding defects — mandatory per IACS UR Z17. > 7. Confusing **fusion zone (FZ)** with **heat-affected zone (HAZ)** in sketches. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌 > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day** ✅ Welding = **heat + pressure + atomic diffusion** → permanent metallurgical bond. ✅ **Three categories:** Fusion welding, Pressure welding, Brazing/Soldering. ✅ **Heat-input formula:** $Q = \eta \cdot \frac{V \cdot I}{v}$ **[J/mm]** — must know η values for SMAW, GMAW, GTAW, SAW, FCAW. ✅ **Five joint types:** Butt (BW), Lap, Tee, Corner, Edge. → **Butt = primary hull joints.** ✅ **Four welding positions:** Flat (PA, easiest, preferred), Horizontal (PB), Vertical (PC), Overhead (PD, hardest). Plus pipe positions **PE, PF**. ✅ **IACS heat-input limit** for HTS shipbuilding steel: typically **≤ 3.5 kJ/mm**. ✅ **CE (Carbon Equivalent)** $> 0.45 \Rightarrow$ **preheating mandatory**. ✅ **Welded vs. Riveted — must memorize** at least 6 advantages and 6 disadvantages each. ✅ Welded construction is **lighter, watertight, faster, smoother** — but has **distortion, HAZ embrittlement, defect risk, brittle-fracture history**. ✅ Mandatory **NDT methods:** UT (Ultrasonic), RT (Radiographic), MPI (Magnetic Particle), DPI (Dye Penetrant) — per **IACS UR Z17 / Z23**. ✅ **WPS (Welding Procedure Specification)** and **PQR (Procedure Qualification Record)** per **ISO 15614-1** are required for any production weld. ✅ **Welder qualification:** **ISO 9606-1** — exam-ready welders are certified per position (1G–4G). ✅ **Liberty ships** — historical reference for brittle-fracture disasters due to poor weld quality → led to modern fracture-mechanics rules. ✅ **Shipyard preference order** of welding positions: **PA > PB > PC > PD** (always design for flat-position welding if possible — it's faster, cheaper, and stronger). ✅ **Modern mega-shipyards** use **panel-line welding (PA)** with **SAW/FCAW multi-head carriages** for hull seams — productivity metric **> 1 m/min** of butt weld. ✅ Always state **units** in heat-input problems. Always **justify** joint-type and position choices in 14-mark questions. ---

topics.title: Different Types of Thermochemical Welding: Thermit Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST831 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Different Types of Thermochemical Welding: Thermit Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Thermochemical Welding** refers to a class of fusion welding processes in which the necessary heat for melting and joining the parent metal is generated entirely (or predominantly) by an **exothermic chemical reaction** between a metallic oxide and a reducing metal (typically aluminum). No external electric arc, gas flame, or resistance heating is the primary heat source. The process relies purely on the **thermodynamic enthalpy release** of a chemical reduction reaction.

**Thermit Welding** (also spelled **Thermite Welding**) is the most industrially significant member of the thermochemical welding family. It is formally defined by **ISO 857** and **AWS A5.31** as:

> *"A fusion welding process wherein coalescence is produced by heating the workpieces with superheated molten metal and slag resulting from the exothermic reaction between a metallic oxide (usually iron oxide, Fe₂O₃) and a finely divided reducing metal (usually aluminum, Al). Filler metal, when used, is obtained from the molten metal produced by the reaction."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Thermit welding is a critical joining technique in **heavy engineering and shipbuilding** because it can generate temperatures exceeding **2500 °C (≈ 4500 °F)** without requiring any external power source — making it ideal for **field repairs at sea, in dry-docks, and at remote shipyard locations**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to weld two large steel rails together in the middle of a remote railway track where there is no electricity, no oxy-acetylene cylinder, and no welding machine. How would you melt the steel to fuse the joint?

The answer is **chemistry itself**.

Think of it like this:
- Mix **rust powder (iron oxide, Fe₂O₃)** with **aluminum powder (Al)** in a refractory crucible (the **thermite pot**).
- Ignite the mixture with a magnesium ribbon or sparkler.
- An incredibly violent, bright-white reaction occurs, releasing so much heat that the products — **molten iron** and **alumina slag** — are produced at temperatures **hotter than the surface of lava**.
- Pour this superheated molten iron into a prepared gap between the two rails. The molten metal melts the rail ends and fuses them into one continuous piece.

In essence, **Thermit Welding is "welding by an internal chemical fire"** — the chemical energy stored in the bonds of iron oxide is converted directly into thermal energy sufficient to perform fusion welding.

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| **Adiabatic Flame Temperature of Thermite Reaction** | **≈ 2500 °C – 3000 °C** |
| **Heat of Reaction (ΔH)** | **≈ −850 kJ/mol of Fe₂O₃** |
| **Melting Point of Pure Iron** | **1538 °C** |
| **Melting Point of Alumina (Al₂O₃) Slag** | **≈ 2072 °C** |
| **Density of Liquid Steel from Reaction** | **≈ 7.0 g/cm³** |
| **Density of Liquid Alumina Slag** | **≈ 3.0 g/cm³** |
| **Typical Thermite Charge for Rail Welding** | **8 – 15 kg** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why such a high temperature matters in shipbuilding:** Low-carbon shipbuilding steel (Grade A, B, D, E — per IACS standards) has a melting point around **1490 – 1520 °C**. The thermite reaction produces molten iron at nearly **double** this temperature, providing a massive superheat that ensures complete fusion and good fluidity for pouring into large weld gaps in thick hull plates (often **25 mm – 100 mm** thick).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Thermite Reaction — The Heart of the Process

The basic chemical reaction is the **Goldschmidt reaction** (discovered by **Hans Goldschmidt in 1895**):

$$\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 + 2\,\text{Al} \longrightarrow 2\,\text{Fe} + \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 + \text{Heat}$$

This is an **oxidation-reduction (redox)** reaction:
- **Iron oxide (Fe₂O₃)** is **reduced** (oxygen is removed).
- **Aluminum (Al)** is **oxidized** (combines with the oxygen).

The enthalpy of reaction (per mole of Fe₂O₃):

$$\Delta H \approx -850\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

The adiabatic flame temperature (highest temperature attainable if no heat is lost) is calculated from:

$$T_{ad} = \frac{-\Delta H}{\sum n_i \, C_{p,i}}$$

where:
- $n_i$ = moles of product $i$
- $C_{p,i}$ = mean specific heat capacity of product $i$

The theoretical adiabatic temperature is approximately **3000 °C**, but in practice due to radiation and conduction losses, the pouring temperature is closer to **2200 – 2400 °C**.

---

### 2.2 Thermit Charge Composition

A commercial thermit mixture is not pure Fe₂O₃ + Al. It is a carefully engineered blend:

| Component | Function | Typical Wt % |
|---|---|---|
| **Iron Oxide (Fe₂O₃)** — mill scale or synthetic | Oxidizer / source of iron | 70 – 75 % |
| **Aluminum Powder (Al)** | Reducing agent | 18 – 22 % |
| **Ferro-alloys** (Fe-Mn, Fe-Si, Fe-Ti) | Alloying elements to improve weld metal properties | 2 – 8 % |
| **Steel Punchings / Shot** | Add volume, modify carbon content | 5 – 15 % |
| **Magnesium Ribbon / Powder** | Ignition aid | Trace |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **steel punchings** are added because pure thermite produces **low-carbon, soft, coarse-grained** weld metal. By adding carbon-rich steel punchings, the weld metal carbon content can be raised to **0.2 – 0.5 % C**, giving the desired strength and hardness for shipbuilding applications.

---

### 2.3 Process Variants of Thermit Welding

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the **classification of types of thermit welding**. There are **three principal variants**:

#### A. Pressure Thermit Welding (PTW)
- After the molten thermite metal is poured into the joint gap, **external mechanical pressure** is applied to forge the two ends together.
- Used primarily for **rail welding** (rail ends are butted together and upset under 30 – 50 tonnes of force).
- The pressure expels slag inclusions and produces a fine-grained, ductile weld.

#### B. Non-Pressure Thermit Welding (Fusion Thermit Welding)
- The superheated molten metal is simply poured into a prepared gap and **allowed to solidify without forging pressure**.
- The joint relies entirely on the **fusion bond** between the molten thermite metal and the parent metal.
- Used for **repair welding of large castings, ship propeller shafts, stern frames, rudder fittings, and rudder horns**.

#### C. Composite (Augmented) Thermit Welding
- The thermite reaction is **augmented by an electric arc** or **gas flame** to preheat the joint.
- Used when the parent metal section is very thick (> 100 mm) or when preheating is mandatory to avoid HAZ cracking.
- Common in **submarine hull construction** and **ice-breaker bow plate repairs**.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula / Relation | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Heat released by thermite | $Q = n \cdot \Delta H$ | kJ | $n$ = moles of Fe₂O₃ reacted |
| 2 | Adiabatic flame temperature | $T_{ad} = \dfrac{-\Delta H}{\sum n_i C_{p,i}}$ | K or °C | Assumes zero heat loss |
| 3 | Mass of thermite required (approx.) | $m_t = \dfrac{m_w \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T}{\eta \cdot H_s}$ | kg | $\eta$ = thermal efficiency ≈ 0.5 |
| 4 | Specific heat of liquid iron (mean) | $C_p \approx 0.82\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$ | kJ/kg·K | Between 1500–2500 °C |
| 5 | Latent heat of fusion of iron | $L_f \approx 247\ \text{kJ/kg}$ | kJ/kg | At 1538 °C |
| 6 | Slag-to-metal ratio (mass) | $r_{sm} = \dfrac{M_{\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3}}{M_{\text{Fe}}} = \dfrac{102}{112} \approx 0.91$ | — | Roughly 1:1 by mass |
| 7 | Weld dilution (typical) | $D = \dfrac{A_{\text{parent}}}{A_{\text{parent}} + A_{\text{filler}}} \times 100$ | % | 30 – 60 % for non-pressure |

---

### 2.5 Engineering Utility of the Process

In shipbuilding, thermit welding has niche but irreplaceable applications:

1. **Repair of large marine castings** — rudder horns, stern frames, propeller shaft brackets — where removal and replacement would require dry-docking for weeks.
2. **Joining of heavy ship plates** that are too thick for efficient arc welding without multiple passes.
3. **Field repairs at remote shipyards** where the power supply for arc welding is unavailable or unreliable.
4. **Welding of dissimilar grade steels** in transition joints (e.g., mild steel hull plate to high-tensile steel bracket).
5. **On-site joining of large pipe spools** in marine piping systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Reality:** Although thermit welding is being steadily replaced by **SAW (Submerged Arc Welding)** and **ESW (Electroslag Welding)** in modern shipyards due to superior mechanical properties and repeatability, it is still **mandatory in some classification society rules (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS)** for specific emergency repair scenarios and for joining thick cast components.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Estimation of Thermite Charge Required to Weld a 25 mm Thick Hull Plate

Let us solve a **typical KTU numerical problem** based on the thermite reaction.

#### **Problem Statement:**
> A thermit welding operation is to be carried out on a **low-carbon shipbuilding steel plate** of thickness **t = 25 mm** and width **w = 200 mm**. The joint gap to be filled (after melting the plate edges) is approximately **g = 20 mm**. The plate must be heated from room temperature (assume **25 °C**) to a pouring temperature of approximately **2400 °C**, and then brought to the melting point of **1500 °C**. Using the thermite reaction, calculate the **minimum mass of thermite charge** required. Assume thermal efficiency of the process, **η = 50 %**, and the heat of reaction **ΔH = 850 kJ per mole of Fe₂O₃ (M = 160 g/mol)**.

---

#### **Step 1: Calculate the Volume of Weld Metal Required**

The molten thermite metal must fill a rectangular cavity of dimensions:
- Width = $w = 200\ \text{mm} = 0.20\ \text{m}$
- Length (gap) = $g = 20\ \text{mm} = 0.020\ \text{m}$
- Depth (plate thickness) = $t = 25\ \text{mm} = 0.025\ \text{m}$

$$V_{weld} = w \times g \times t$$

$$V_{weld} = 0.20 \times 0.020 \times 0.025$$

$$V_{weld} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^3 = 100\ \text{cm}^3$$

---

#### **Step 2: Calculate the Mass of Steel Required to Fill the Gap**

Using density of liquid/weld steel: $\rho_{steel} \approx 7850\ \text{kg/m}^3$

$$m_{steel} = \rho_{steel} \times V_{weld}$$

$$m_{steel} = 7850 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$m_{steel} = 0.785\ \text{kg}$$

So approximately **785 grams of molten steel** is required to fill the joint.

---

#### **Step 3: Calculate the Total Heat Required**

The heat needed has two components:

**(a) Sensible heat** to raise steel from 25 °C → 1500 °C (melting point):

$$Q_{sense} = m_{steel} \cdot C_{p,steel} \cdot \Delta T_1$$

Using $C_{p,steel} = 0.68\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$ (mean value from 25 °C to 1500 °C):

$$Q_{sense} = 0.785 \times 0.68 \times (1500 - 25)$$

$$Q_{sense} = 0.785 \times 0.68 \times 1475$$

$$Q_{sense} = 787.5\ \text{kJ}$$

---

**(b) Latent heat of fusion** at 1500 °C:

$$Q_{latent} = m_{steel} \cdot L_f$$

Using $L_f = 247\ \text{kJ/kg}$:

$$Q_{latent} = 0.785 \times 247$$

$$Q_{latent} = 193.9\ \text{kJ}$$

---

**(c) Total useful heat required:**

$$Q_{total} = Q_{sense} + Q_{latent}$$

$$Q_{total} = 787.5 + 193.9$$

$$Q_{total} = 981.4\ \text{kJ}$$

---

#### **Step 4: Account for Process Inefficiency**

With thermal efficiency $\eta = 0.50$:

$$Q_{supply} = \frac{Q_{total}}{\eta}$$

$$Q_{supply} = \frac{981.4}{0.50}$$

$$Q_{supply} = 1962.8\ \text{kJ}$$

---

#### **Step 5: Calculate the Mass of Fe₂O₃ Required**

Heat released per mole of Fe₂O₃ = 850 kJ
Heat released per gram = $\dfrac{850}{160} = 5.3125\ \text{kJ/g} = 5312.5\ \text{kJ/kg}$

$$m_{\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3} = \frac{Q_{supply}}{5312.5}$$

$$m_{\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3} = \frac{1962.8}{5312.5}$$

$$m_{\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3} = 0.369\ \text{kg} = 369\ \text{g}$$

---

#### **Step 6: Calculate the Total Thermite Charge (Including Al Powder)**

The stoichiometric ratio in the reaction $\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 + 2\,\text{Al} \rightarrow 2\,\text{Fe} + \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$:
- Molar mass of Fe₂O₃ = **160 g/mol**
- Molar mass of 2 Al = **54 g/mol**
- Total mass of stoichiometric mixture = $160 + 54 = 214\ \text{g/mol}$

Mass fraction of Fe₂O₃ in pure thermite = $\dfrac{160}{214} = 0.7477$

Therefore, total pure thermite charge required:

$$m_{thermite} = \frac{m_{\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3}}{0.7477}$$

$$m_{thermite} = \frac{0.369}{0.7477}$$

$$\boxed{m_{thermite} \approx 0.494\ \text{kg} \approx 500\ \text{g}}$$

---

#### **Step 7: Add Steel Punchings and Alloy Additions**

In practice, additional 10 – 15 % is added to account for steel punchings and alloying elements, giving a **commercial charge of approximately 550 – 600 g** for this small joint.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Process Parameters vs. Shipbuilding Application

| Parameter | Pressure Thermit | Non-Pressure (Fusion) Thermit | Composite Thermit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Typical Application** | Rail joining | Marine casting repair | Thick plate joining (>50 mm) |
| **Weld Metal Carbon** | 0.3 – 0.5 % | 0.15 – 0.30 % | 0.20 – 0.40 % |
| **Ultimate Tensile Strength** | 550 – 700 MPa | 400 – 550 MPa | 500 – 650 MPa |
| **Elongation %** | 8 – 15 % | 5 – 12 % | 8 – 14 % |
| **Joint Preparation** | Square butt, no gap | V-groove, 45° included angle | Double-V or U-groove |
| **Preheat Required** | Yes (200 – 300 °C) | Yes (300 – 400 °C) | Yes (300 – 500 °C) |
| **Post-Weld Heat Treatment** | Optional | Mandatory (Stress Relief) | Mandatory |
| **Typical Section Thickness** | Up to 50 mm | 25 – 150 mm | 50 – 300 mm |
| **Power Source Needed** | None | None | Arc + Thermite |
| **Slag Removal Method** | Mechanical (after upset) | Naturally floats (lower density) | Manual chipping |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Thermit Welding Process — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Raw Materials: Fe2O3 Mill Scale] --> B1[Weighing and Blending Station]
    A2[Aluminum Powder] --> B1
    A3[Ferro-Alloys Fe-Mn, Fe-Si] --> B1
    A4[Steel Punchings] --> B1
    B1 --> C1[Thermite Charge in Refractory Crucible]
    C1 --> D1[Ignition with Mg Ribbon / Sparkler]
    D1 --> E1[Exothermic Reaction Chamber]
    E1 --> F1[Slag Al2O3 floats on top]
    E1 --> F2[Superheated Liquid Steel at approx 2400 C]
    F1 --> G1[Slag Collection Tundish]
    F2 --> G2[Pouring Gate into Mould]
    G2 --> H1[Pre-Heated Joint Gap]
    H1 --> I1[Fusion with Parent Metal]
    I1 --> J1{Process Variant}
    J1 --> K1[Option A: Pressure Upset Forging]
    J1 --> K2[Option B: Free Solidification Fusion]
    J1 --> K3[Option C: Arc Augmentation]
    K1 --> L1[Finished Welded Joint]
    K2 --> L1
    K3 --> L1
    L1 --> M1[NDT Inspection UT, RT, MPI]
    M1 --> N1[Acceptance as per IACS Rules]
    M1 --> O1[Rework / Repair]
```

### 4.2 Detailed Joint Preparation Diagram (Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SG1 [Joint Preparation Stage]
        direction TB
        J1[Parent Plate 1] --> JG[Joint Gap 15 to 25 mm]
        JG --> J2[Parent Plate 2]
        J2 --> RP[Refractory Mould Box clamped around joint]
        RP --> SP[Sand Packing and Drying]
    end
    subgraph SG2 [Welding Stage]
        direction TB
        CR[Refractory Crucible Thermite Pot] --> IG[Ignition Source Mg Ribbon]
        IG --> RX[Exothermic Reaction approx 30 to 45 sec]
        RX --> T1[Reaction Temperature approx 2500 C]
        T1 --> PG[Pouring through Tap Hole]
    end
    subgraph SG3 [Solidification and Finishing]
        direction TB
        PG --> SL[Solidification approx 5 to 15 minutes]
        SL --> MS[Mould Stripping]
        MS --> GR[Grinding Flush with Plate Surface]
        GR --> IN[NDT Inspection]
    end
```

### 4.3 Process Variant Selection Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Shipbuilding Application Identified] --> Q2{Section Thickness}
    Q2 -->|< 30 mm| Q3[Rail Welding or Thin Plate]
    Q2 -->|30 to 100 mm| Q4[Marine Casting Repair]
    Q2 -->|> 100 mm| Q5[Thick Hull Plate or Stern Frame]
    Q3 --> R1[Pressure Thermit Welding]
    Q4 --> R2[Non-Pressure Fusion Thermit]
    Q5 --> R3[Composite Thermit with Arc Preheat]
    R1 --> S1[Upset Force 30 to 50 tonnes]
    R2 --> S2[Allow Free Solidification with PWHT]
    R3 --> S3[SMAW or SAW Preheat 300 to 500 C]
    S1 --> T1[Final Weld]
    S2 --> T1
    S3 --> T1
```

### 4.4 Equipment Layout in a Shipyard Thermit Welding Bay

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EQ1 [Storage Zone]
        direction LR
        ST1[Oxide Storage Silo]
        ST2[Aluminum Powder Cabinet Class A Fire Risk]
        ST3[Ferro-Alloy Bins]
    end
    subgraph EQ2 [Preparation Zone]
        direction LR
        PR1[Weighing Scale]
        PR2[Mixing Drum]
        PR3[Crucible Pre-Heating Furnace]
    end
    subgraph EQ3 [Welding Bay]
        direction LR
        WB1[Work-Piece on Skid with Mould]
        WB2[Refractory Crucible on Trolley]
        WB3[Ignition Control Panel Remote]
        WB4[Fume Extraction Hood]
    end
    subgraph EQ4 [Inspection Zone]
        direction LR
        IN1[UT Probe Station]
        IN2[RT Bunker if applicable]
        IN3[PWHT Furnace 600 to 650 C]
    end
    ST1 --> PR1
    ST2 --> PR1
    ST3 --> PR1
    PR1 --> PR2
    PR2 --> PR3
    PR3 --> WB2
    WB1 --> WB3
    WB2 --> WB3
    WB3 --> WB4
    WB4 --> IN1
    IN1 --> IN3
    IN2 --> IN3
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**"Define Thermit Welding. State the basic chemical reaction involved and name the inventor of the process."**

**Model Answer:**

> **Thermit Welding** is a thermochemical fusion welding process in which the heat necessary for melting and joining the parent metal is obtained from the **exothermic oxidation-reduction reaction** between a metallic oxide (usually iron oxide, Fe₂O₃) and a finely divided reducing metal (usually aluminum).
>
> **Basic Reaction:**
>
> $$\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 + 2\,\text{Al} \longrightarrow 2\,\text{Fe} + \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 + \text{Heat (≈ 850 kJ/mol)}$$
>
> The process was invented by the German chemist **Dr. Hans Goldschmidt** in **1895**, and is therefore also called the **Goldschmidt Process**.

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark reaction + 1 mark inventor)*

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**"List any six applications of thermit welding in shipbuilding industry."**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Repair welding of large marine castings** such as stern frames, rudder horns, and propeller struts.
2. **Joining of thick ship hull plates** where multiple-pass arc welding is uneconomical.
3. **Field emergency repairs** at remote shipyards or at sea where arc-welding power is unavailable.
4. **Welding of dissimilar grade steels** in transition joints (e.g., mild steel hull to high-tensile steel bracket).
5. **Joining of marine propeller shafts** to coupling flanges.
6. **Repair of rudder fittings, stem fittings, and shaft brackets** while afloat or in dry-dock.
7. **Welding of crane pedestal bases** and heavy deck machinery foundations.

*(3 marks: ½ mark per correct application, any 6)*

---

### 📕 PART B — Full ESE Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Modified]`

**"Explain in detail the process of Thermit Welding with neat sketches. Discuss (a) the procedure of pressure thermit welding used for joining shipbuilding steel plates, and (b) the metallurgical aspects of the weld metal produced."**

##### **Part (a) — Procedure of Pressure Thermit Welding (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Joint Preparation**
- The two steel plates to be joined are aligned with a **gap of 15 – 25 mm**.
- The plate ends are **square-cut or slightly bevelled** (max 5° included angle).
- A **refractory mould box** (silica-based or magnesia-based) is clamped around the joint to contain the molten metal.

**Step 2 — Mould Drying and Pre-Heating**
- The mould is dried with a **gas torch or low-current arc** to remove moisture and preheated to **200 – 300 °C** to prevent thermal shock cracking.

**Step 3 — Crucible Loading**
- A tapered **refractory crucible (thermite pot)** is mounted above the mould.
- The thermite mixture (Fe₂O₃ + Al + ferro-alloys + steel punchings) is loaded into the crucible.
- A small quantity of **magnesium ribbon and barium peroxide** is placed on top as an ignition aid.

**Step 4 — Ignition**
- The mixture is ignited using a **magnesium sparkler or electric heating element**.
- The exothermic reaction initiates within **5 – 10 seconds** and proceeds violently for **30 – 45 seconds**.

**Step 5 — Reaction and Tapping**
- The reaction temperature reaches **2500 – 3000 °C**.
- The slag (Al₂O₃, density ~3.0 g/cm³) **floats on top** of the molten iron (density ~7.0 g/cm³).
- The slag is **tapped first** into a slag basin; then the **molten steel** is poured into the joint gap through the tap hole.

**Step 6 — Pressure Application**
- Within **3 – 5 seconds** of the metal entering the mould, **hydraulic or mechanical upset force (30 – 50 tonnes)** is applied.
- The pressure:
  - Expels slag inclusions upward.
  - Forges the grains into a fine equiaxed structure.
  - Reduces porosity and produces a high-integrity weld.

**Step 7 — Cooling and Finishing**
- The weld is allowed to cool under pressure for **5 – 15 minutes**.
- The mould is stripped, and the **upset metal (fin)** is removed by **grinding or hot-chipping**.
- The joint is then subjected to **NDT (Ultrasonic Testing and Magnetic Particle Inspection)**.

*(7 marks distributed: 1 mark each step + 1 mark for neat sketch description)*

---

##### **Part (b) — Metallurgical Aspects of Weld Metal (7 Marks)**

**1. Chemistry of Weld Metal:**
- Pure thermite yields **very low-carbon iron (< 0.05 % C)** because aluminum strips oxygen and carbon from the bath.
- To increase carbon content to a usable **0.2 – 0.5 %**, **steel punchings or cast iron chips** are added to the charge.
- **Ferro-manganese and ferro-silicon** are added as deoxidizers and to introduce **Mn and Si** for strength and grain refinement.

**2. Macrostructure:**
- The weld metal shows a **coarse columnar grain structure** in the as-welded condition, due to the slow cooling rate (large thermal mass).
- Pressure welding refines this to **fine equiaxed grains** at the bond line.

**3. Microstructure:**
- The weld metal microstructure is predominantly **ferrite + pearlite** for plain carbon steel compositions.
- With high carbon addition (0.4 % C), the structure may contain **proeutectoid ferrite networks** along prior austenite grain boundaries.

**4. Inclusions:**
- **Alumina (Al₂O₃)** inclusions are common if slag separation is incomplete. These act as **stress raisers** and reduce fatigue life.
- Pressure thermit welding significantly reduces inclusion content.

**5. Mechanical Properties:**
- Typical **UTS = 400 – 700 MPa**, depending on the alloying additions.
- **Elongation = 8 – 15 %**, **Impact toughness = 20 – 60 J at 0 °C**.
- Hardness of weld metal ≈ **150 – 220 BHN**.

**6. Heat Affected Zone (HAZ):**
- The HAZ in the parent plate experiences **grain coarsening** due to the high heat input.
- **Post-weld heat treatment (PWHT)** at **600 – 650 °C** is mandatory to relieve residual stresses and temper the HAZ.

**7. Defects:**
- Common defects include **porosity, slag inclusions, lack of fusion at the parent-metal interface, and HAZ cracking**.
- Mitigation is achieved by proper joint preparation, controlled pour rate, and PWHT.

*(7 marks distributed across the 7 sub-points above)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Modified]`

**"With the help of a neat process flow diagram, describe the steps involved in non-pressure thermit welding. Also discuss (a) the role of steel punchings and ferro-alloys in the thermite charge, and (b) the advantages and limitations of thermit welding compared to arc welding in shipbuilding."**

##### **Part (a) — Role of Steel Punchings and Ferro-Alloys (7 Marks)**

**1. Steel Punchings:**
- **Function:** Add **carbon** and **volume** to the weld metal.
- Pure thermite reaction yields only **~ 55 % iron by mass**, with the balance being Al₂O₃ slag. This is insufficient in volume and too low in carbon for structural use.
- Adding **5 – 15 % by mass of low-carbon steel punchings** raises:
  - **Carbon content** from ~ 0.05 % to **0.20 – 0.50 %**.
  - **Yield of metallic iron** for the joint volume.
- The punchings also act as a **coolant**, slightly lowering the peak reaction temperature to a safer level (~ 2400 °C instead of 3000 °C), which protects the refractory lining of the crucible.

**2. Ferro-Manganese (Fe-Mn):**
- **Function:** Deoxidizer + Alloying element.
- Mn combines with dissolved oxygen and sulfur to form **MnO and MnS**, which float into the slag.
- Adds **0.5 – 1.2 % Mn** to the weld metal, improving **strength, hardness, and hot-shortness resistance**.

**3. Ferro-Silicon (Fe-Si):**
- **Function:** Strong deoxidizer.
- Si reduces any remaining FeO in the weld pool, producing SiO₂ that enters the slag.
- Adds **0.2 – 0.5 % Si**, which **increases fluidity** of the molten weld pool.

**4. Ferro-Titanium (Fe-Ti):**
- **Function:** Grain refiner.
- Ti forms **TiC and TiN** particles that act as nucleation sites, producing a **finer grain size** in the weld metal.
- Improves **toughness and fatigue resistance**.

**5. Other Additives:**
- Small additions of **Ni, Cr, or Mo** may be made for matching the mechanical properties of **higher-strength hull steels** (e.g., AH36, DH36).

*(7 marks: ~1.4 marks per sub-point)*

---

##### **Part (b) — Advantages and Limitations vs. Arc Welding (7 Marks)**

| Aspect | Thermit Welding | Arc Welding (SMAW/SAW) |
|---|---|---|
| **Power source** | **Not required** (chemical energy only) | **Required** (electric generator) |
| **Portability** | Excellent for remote sites | Limited by cable length / generator |
| **Heat input control** | Poor (single large pour) | Excellent (controlled current/voltage) |
| **Weld metal quality** | Coarse grain, inclusions possible | Fine grain, superior toughness |
| **Weld speed for thick plates** | Very fast (single pour) | Slow (multi-pass) |
| **Repeatability** | Low (chemistry-sensitive) | High (parameter-controlled) |
| **Operator skill** | Moderate (charge prep critical) | High (multi-parameter control) |
| **PWHT requirement** | Mandatory | Often not required for thin sections |
| **NDT defect rate** | Higher (porosity, inclusions) | Lower |
| **Cost for thin sections** | High (was of thermite charge) | Low |
| **Cost for very thick sections** | Moderate | High (many passes, time) |
| **Safety hazards** | Molten metal splash, Mg ignition, Al dust explosion | Electric shock, arc flash, fumes |

**Advantages of Thermit Welding:**
1. No electricity required — ideal for **field/sea repairs**.
2. Single-operation joining of **very thick sections**.
3. High **deposition rate** in one pour.
4. Suitable for joining **large castings** without dismantling.
5. Lower equipment cost (no welding machine required).

**Limitations of Thermit Welding:**
1. Poor **metallurgical quality** — coarse grain, inclusions.
2. **Limited to ferrous metals** (cannot be used for aluminium, copper, etc.).
3. **High heat input** causes severe **HAZ softening and distortion**.
4. Mandatory **PWHT** adds cost and time.
5. **Hazardous chemicals** (aluminum powder explosion risk; molten metal splash).
6. Cannot be used for **thin sections** (< 8 mm) due to burn-through.

*(7 marks: ~1 mark per row in the comparison + summary)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Do NOT confuse Thermit Welding with Gas Welding.** Many students incorrectly describe oxy-acetylene flame as the heat source. The hallmark of thermit welding is that **the heat comes purely from a chemical reaction**, NOT from an external flame or arc.
> 2. **Always write the balanced equation** with proper state symbols (s, l, g). Partial equations lose marks.
> 3. **Do not forget to mention slag removal.** Slag floats because **ρ(Al₂O₃) ≈ 3.0 g/cm³ < ρ(Fe) ≈ 7.0 g/cm³**.
> 4. **Mention PWHT** — Examiners specifically look for it as it is a common KTU board question.
> 5. **Steel punchings** are added **not for cooling alone** but primarily for **carbon addition**. Many students lose 1 mark by missing this.
> 6. **Unit consistency:** Always use SI units (kg, m, K) and clearly state the units in numerical problems.
> 7. **Process diagram:** A neat labelled diagram is worth at least **2 marks** by itself — never skip it.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Thermit Welding = Chemical reduction welding** — heat comes from the **Goldschmidt reaction**: Fe₂O₃ + 2Al → 2Fe + Al₂O₃ + Heat.
- ✅ The reaction releases **~ 850 kJ/mol** of Fe₂O₃ and reaches **~ 2500 – 3000 °C**.
- ✅ Invented by **Hans Goldschmidt (1895)**.
- ✅ **Three variants**: Pressure, Non-Pressure (Fusion), and Composite (Arc-Augmented).
- ✅ **Charge composition:** Iron oxide (70–75 %) + Aluminum (18–22 %) + Ferro-alloys (2–8 %) + Steel punchings (5–15 %).
- ✅ **Steel punchings** are added to **increase carbon content and volume** of the weld metal.
- ✅ **Slag (Al₂O₃)** floats because its density (~3.0 g/cm³) is lower than that of molten iron (~7.0 g/cm³).
- ✅ **Preheat** of mould: 200 – 300 °C; **PWHT**: 600 – 650 °C (mandatory).
- ✅ **Shipbuilding applications:** Stern frames, rudder fittings, propeller shafts, thick hull plates, casting repairs, dissimilar steel joints.
- ✅ **Advantages:** No power source, high deposition rate, ideal for thick sections and field repairs.
- ✅ **Limitations:** Coarse grain, inclusions, high heat input, mandatory PWHT, limited to ferrous metals.
- ✅ **Efficiency η ≈ 50 %** — roughly half the chemical energy reaches the joint.
- ✅ **Mass of thermite charge ≈ 1.5× mass of weld metal required** (rule of thumb).
- ✅ **NDT methods used:** UT, RT, MPI — all as per IACS UR Z17/Z23 rules.
- ✅ **Classification society rules:** DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS — all have specific clauses for thermit welding acceptance criteria.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Atomic Hydrogen Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST831 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Atomic Hydrogen Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Atomic Hydrogen Welding (AHW)** is a non-pressure, fusion-type gas welding process in which a stream of **molecular hydrogen (H₂)** is passed through an **electric arc struck between two non-consumable tungsten electrodes**. The intense thermal energy of the arc **dissociates** the diatomic hydrogen molecules into monatomic hydrogen (H) atoms. These highly energetic atoms, being thermodynamically unstable, immediately **recombine** into H₂ molecules upon striking the relatively cooler surface of the workpiece. This exothermic recombination releases a tremendous amount of latent heat energy, which melts the parent metal to form the weld joint.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board-Exam Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> *"Atomic Hydrogen Welding is an arc welding process in which an electric arc is maintained between two tungsten electrodes in an atmosphere of hydrogen. The hydrogen molecules are dissociated into atoms in the arc, and recombination of these atoms on the work surface produces the welding heat."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a crowd of people (H₂ molecules) walking calmly on a road. Suddenly, a powerful explosion (the electric arc at **~4000 °C**) blasts through the crowd, throwing each pair of people apart into **two individual, agitated, "angry" individuals (H atoms)**. These scattered individuals run around in a frenzy, carrying huge amounts of energy. When they eventually bump into a cool, solid wall (the workpiece), they instantly grab onto a partner and "hug" tightly to form a pair again, **screaming out** all the extra energy they absorbed from the explosion. This scream of energy — released as a flame reaching **~6000 °C** — is what melts the metal.

In one line: **Energy borrowed from the arc is banked into the atoms and "cashed out" on the metal surface.**

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Dissociation Energy of H₂ | **436 kJ/mol (≈ 104 kcal/mol)** | Energy required to split one mole of H₂ |
| Bond Length (H–H) | **0.074 nm** | Internuclear distance in H₂ |
| Arc Temperature | **~4000 °C (≈ 4500 K)** | Where dissociation occurs |
| Recombination Flame Temperature | **~6000 °C (≈ 6300 K)** | Hottest of all commercial gas flames |
| Electrode Material | **Tungsten (W)**, melting point **3422 °C** | Non-consumable, water-cooled |
| Shielding/Process Gas | **Pure Hydrogen (H₂)** | Acts as both heat carrier and shielding medium |
| Heat Source | **Electric arc + Chemical recombination** | Hybrid (electrical + chemical) |

> [!NOTE]
> AHW is the **only commercial welding process** in which the heat for fusion is supplied *indirectly* by an **exothermic chemical reaction** (recombination) rather than directly by the arc itself. The arc acts only as the dissociation agent, not the fusion agent.

## 1.4 Historical & Industrial Context

The process was invented in **1912 by Irving Langmuir** (Nobel Laureate in Chemistry, 1932) of the General Electric Company, USA. It is one of the **oldest arc welding processes** ever developed, predating shielded metal arc welding. In modern shipyards, its use is niche but persistent — primarily reserved for **specialty alloys, thin gauge sheet work, and non-ferrous metals** where minimal contamination is critical.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-Stage Thermodynamic Principle

AHW operates on a beautifully clean two-step thermochemical cycle:

**Stage 1 — Endothermic Dissociation (in the arc):**
Hydrogen enters the arc in molecular form. The arc's collision energy exceeds the bond dissociation energy, breaking the covalent bond:

$$H_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Arc at } \sim 4000^\circ C} 2H \quad (\text{absorbs } 436 \text{ kJ/mol})$$

**Stage 2 — Exothermic Recombination (at the workpiece):**
The atomic hydrogen is metastable. On contact with the cooler metal surface, it catalytically recombines:

$$2H \xrightarrow{\text{Workpiece surface}} H_2 \quad (\text{releases } 436 \text{ kJ/mol})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The energy released in Stage 2 is **identical in magnitude** to the energy absorbed in Stage 1 (conservation of energy). AHW is essentially a **"heat pump"** that transports thermal energy from a region of low thermal demand (the arc column) to a region of high thermal demand (the weld pool), using hydrogen atoms as the energy carrier.

## 2.2 Equipment and Circuit Description

The AHW setup consists of:

1. **Two water-cooled copper-jacketed tungsten electrodes** held in a special **welding torch** (also called the *atomic hydrogen torch*).
2. **An AC power source** (typically a step-down welding transformer). AC is preferred because:
   - Equal heating of both electrodes
   - No arc blow due to magnetic fields
   - Stable arc maintenance
3. **Hydrogen gas supply** with pressure regulator, flow meter, and flashback arrestor.
4. **Water cooling system** for the torch body and electrodes.
5. **No flux** is required (hydrogen is a reducing atmosphere that prevents oxidation).
6. **Filler rod** is *optional* — the process can be run **autogenously** (without filler).

## 2.3 Operating Procedure (Sequence of Steps)

1. **Hydrogen purge:** Hydrogen is allowed to flow through the torch for ~30 seconds to expel air (prevents explosive oxyhydrogen mixtures).
2. **Pilot arc:** A low-current pilot arc is struck between the two tungsten electrodes using a high-frequency (HF) starter or by briefly touching them apart.
3. **Hydrogen injection:** H₂ is directed to flow *across* (perpendicular to) the arc column so molecules pass through the hottest zone.
4. **Dissociation:** H₂ molecules are torn apart into atoms in the arc plasma.
5. **Arc deflection:** The atomic hydrogen flame is deflected toward the workpiece either by the **jet velocity of H₂** or by an auxiliary magnetic field (in some torch designs).
6. **Recombination heating:** Atoms strike the workpiece, recombine, and the released heat fuses the metal.
7. **Cooling & solidification:** The molten pool solidifies, with hydrogen providing a reducing shield against atmospheric oxygen and nitrogen.

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Engineering Choice

| Design Choice | Underlying Reason |
|---|---|
| Two tungsten electrodes (not one + workpiece) | Keeps the arc *off* the work → independent control of arc and weld pool; prevents tungsten contamination of weld metal |
| AC supply | DC would overheat one electrode and cause arc deflection; AC balances electrode wear |
| Pure H₂, not oxy-hydrogen | Oxygen would burn the tungsten; pure H₂ acts as both process gas and shield |
| Water-cooled electrode holders | Tungsten at 4000 °C would melt copper holders; cooling permits sustained operation |
| Recombination on workpiece, not in arc | H atoms are unstable in plasma (would just re-dissociate); they need a *cool, catalytically active* metal surface to recombine efficiently |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula / Expression | Units | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dissociation energy per mole of H₂ | $E_d = 436 \text{ kJ/mol}$ | kJ/mol | Energy bookkeeping |
| Energy released per kg of H₂ recombined | $E = \dfrac{E_d \times 1000}{M_w} = \dfrac{436{,}000}{2.016} \approx 216{,}270 \text{ kJ/kg}$ | kJ/kg | Total heat available |
| Heat input rate to workpiece | $Q = \dot{m}_{H_2} \times L_d$ | W (J/s) | Power delivered |
| Equivalent flame temperature | $T_{flame} \approx 6000 \,^\circ C$ | K or °C | Comparison with oxy-fuel flames |
| Mass flow of H₂ for desired Q | $\dot{m}_{H_2} = \dfrac{Q}{L_d}$ | kg/s | Process design |
| Theoretical arc energy balance | $P_{arc} \approx P_{recombination} + P_{losses}$ | W | Energy audit |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the table above, $L_d$ = latent heat of dissociation = **216,270 kJ/kg** of H₂ (this is the figure to write in derivations). Avoid using the pipe symbol `\|` in equations — use `\vert` or `\mid` instead.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

* **Aerospace & shipbuilding:** Joining thin gauge **stainless steel sheets** for exhaust manifolds and ducting where TIG might over-heat-sink.
* **Tool & die making:** Hard-facing delicate tool tips.
* **Non-ferrous joining:** Aluminum, **Monel, Inconel, brass, nickel, copper** — materials highly sensitive to oxygen pickup.
* **Historically:** Used to weld thin sheets before TIG (GTAW) became dominant post-1940s.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Energy Released per kg of Hydrogen

**Problem:** Calculate the total heat released when 1 kg of monoatomic hydrogen (already dissociated) recombines to form molecular hydrogen on the workpiece surface.

**Given:**
- Dissociation energy of H₂: $E_d = 436 \text{ kJ/mol}$
- Molar mass of H₂: $M_{H_2} = 2.016 \text{ g/mol}$

**Step 1 — Convert molar mass to kg per kmol:**

$$M_{H_2} = 2.016 \text{ g/mol} = 2.016 \text{ kg/kmol}$$

**Step 2 — Relate energy per mole to energy per kg using unit cancellation:**

$$\text{Energy per kg} = \frac{E_d \text{ [kJ/mol]}}{M_{H_2} \text{ [kg/kmol]}} \times 1000 \text{ mol/kmol}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values:**
