$$L_d = \frac{436 \text{ kJ/mol}}{2.016 \text{ kg/kmol}} \times 1000 \text{ mol/kmol}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the denominator explicitly:**

$$2.016 \text{ kg/kmol} = 2.016 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/mol}$$

Equivalently, energy per kg:

$$L_d = \frac{436{,}000 \text{ J/mol}}{2.016 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/mol}}$$

**Step 5 — Perform the division:**

$$L_d = \frac{436{,}000}{0.002016} \text{ J/kg}$$

$$L_d = 216{,}269{,}841.27 \text{ J/kg}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to kJ/kg for engineering convenience:**

$$\boxed{L_d \approx 2.163 \times 10^{5} \text{ kJ/kg} = 216{,}270 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **staggering energy density** — more than **5 times the heat of combustion of gasoline** (~46,000 kJ/kg). It explains why AHW produces such an intense, localized flame.

## 3.2 Derivation — Required Hydrogen Mass Flow for a Given Power

**Problem:** A welder requires 2 kW of heating power at the workpiece from the recombination flame. Assuming 60% of the recombination energy is actually transferred to the metal (the rest lost by convection and radiation), find the required H₂ mass flow rate.

**Given:**
- Useful heat power: $Q_{useful} = 2 \text{ kW} = 2 \text{ kJ/s}$
- Transfer efficiency: $\eta = 0.60$
- Dissociation energy per kg: $L_d = 216{,}270 \text{ kJ/kg}$

**Step 1 — Compute the *total* recombination power needed:**

$$Q_{total} = \frac{Q_{useful}}{\eta} = \frac{2 \text{ kJ/s}}{0.60} = 3.333 \text{ kJ/s}$$

**Step 2 — Express power in terms of mass flow:**

$$Q_{total} = \dot{m}_{H_2} \times L_d$$

**Step 3 — Solve algebraically for $\dot{m}$:**

$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = \frac{Q_{total}}{L_d}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = \frac{3.333 \text{ kJ/s}}{216{,}270 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate:**

$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = 1.541 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to litres per minute (at STP, $\rho_{H_2} = 0.0899$ kg/m³):**

$$\text{Volumetric flow} = \frac{\dot{m}_{H_2}}{\rho_{H_2}} = \frac{1.541 \times 10^{-5}}{0.0899} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$= 1.714 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 10.3 \text{ L/min}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{H_2} \approx 1.54 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/s} \approx 10.3 \text{ L/min at STP}}$$

## 3.3 Comparison Matrix — AHW vs Oxy-Acetylene Welding (OAW)

This is a **frequently asked KTU question**. Memorize the differences:

| Parameter | Atomic Hydrogen Welding | Oxy-Acetylene Welding |
|---|---|---|
| Heat source | Arc + chemical recombination | Combustion of C₂H₂ in O₂ |
| Maximum flame temperature | **~6000 °C** | ~3200 °C (neutral flame) |
| Atmosphere | Pure H₂ (reducing) | O₂ + C₂H₂ (oxidizing/neutral) |
| Electrodes | Two tungsten (non-consumable) | None |
| Flux required | **No** | Yes, for most metals |
| Filler rod | Optional | Always required |
| Power source | AC welding transformer | None (gas only) |
| Suitable for | Thin sheets, stainless, non-ferrous | Thick plates, general steel |
| Cost of operation | Higher (electricity + H₂) | Lower (gas only) |
| Skill required | Higher (arc + gas control) | Moderate |
| Modern relevance | Niche / specialized | Declining (replaced by arc processes) |

## 3.4 Energy-Flow Block Calculation (Analytical)

For the entire AHW system, the energy balance is:

$$P_{electrical\ input} = P_{arc} = P_{dissociation} + P_{electrode\ losses} + P_{radiation\ loss}$$

At the workpiece:

$$P_{recombination} = P_{arc} \times \eta_{transfer}$$

For a typical 2 kW atomic hydrogen welder operating at 80 V and 25 A:

$$P_{electrical} = V \times I = 80 \times 25 = 2000 \text{ W}$$

If 70% goes into dissociation and 30% is lost to cooling/radiation:

$$P_{dissociation} = 0.70 \times 2000 = 1400 \text{ W}$$

This 1400 W is later liberated on the workpiece. Of that, if 60% is usefully delivered to the metal:

$$P_{useful} = 0.60 \times 1400 = 840 \text{ W}$$

The remainder (560 W) is lost to convection and radiation from the flame.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Block Diagram — Atomic Hydrogen Welding

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AC[AC Power Supply 80V 25A] --> TR[Step-down Transformer]
    TR --> TORCH[Atomic Hydrogen Torch]
    H2CYL[Hydrogen Cylinder with Regulator] --> FM[Flow Meter]
    FM --> TORCH
    WCOOL[Water Cooling Supply] --> TORCH
    TORCH --> E1[Tungsten Electrode 1]
    TORCH --> E2[Tungsten Electrode 2]
    E1 --- ARC[Electric Arc 4000 C]
    E2 --- ARC
    ARC --> DIS[H2 Molecules Dissociate to 2H]
    DIS --> RECOMB[Atoms strike Workpiece and Recombine]
    RECOMB --> HEAT[Heat Released 6000 C]
    HEAT --> WP[Workpiece Melts and Welds]
    WP --> SHIELD[Pure H2 Atmosphere Shields Weld Pool]
```

## 4.2 Equipment Architecture — Block-Level Functional Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph POWER[Electrical Subsystem]
        P1[Mains Supply 415V AC] --> P2[Step-down Transformer]
        P2 --> P3[Continuously Variable Reactor]
        P3 --> P4[Output 80V 25A AC]
    end
    subgraph GAS[Gas Subsystem]
        G1[Hydrogen Cylinder 200 bar] --> G2[Two-stage Pressure Regulator]
        G2 --> G3[Flow Meter 0 to 30 L per min]
        G3 --> G4[Flashback Arrestor]
        G4 --> G5[Torch Inlet]
    end
    subgraph COOL[Cooling Subsystem]
        C1[Water Pump] --> C2[Flow Control Valve]
        C2 --> C3[Torch Cooling Jacket]
        C3 --> C4[Electrode Holders]
        C4 --> C5[Drain]
    end
    subgraph TORCH[Torch Assembly]
        T1[Insulated Body] --> T2[Upper Electrode Holder]
        T1 --> T3[Lower Electrode Holder]
        T2 --> T4[Tungsten Electrode diameter 3 to 5 mm]
        T3 --> T5[Tungsten Electrode diameter 3 to 5 mm]
        T4 --- T6[Arc Gap 2 to 3 mm]
        T5 --- T6
        T6 --> T7[Hydrogen Nozzle directing flow across arc]
    end
    P4 --> T1
    G5 --> T7
    C3 --> T1
```

## 4.3 Heat Transfer Sequence — Energy Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Electrical Energy 2 kW] --> B[Arc Plasma 4000 C]
    B --> C[H2 Dissociation Stores 436 kJ per mol]
    C --> D[Atomic H Migration toward Workpiece]
    D --> E[Surface Catalytic Recombination]
    E --> F[Heat Released 6000 C Flame]
    F --> G[Workpiece Melting]
    G --> H[Weld Pool Solidification]
    H --> I[Welded Joint]
```

## 4.4 Process Variables — Cause-Effect Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    V1[Arc Voltage] --> Q1[Arc Length]
    Q1 --> Q2[Dissociation Efficiency]
    V2[Hydrogen Flow Rate] --> Q2
    Q2 --> Q3[Atomic Hydrogen Concentration]
    Q3 --> Q4[Recombination Heat Flux]
    V3[Arc Current] --> Q4
    V4[Torch to Work Distance] --> Q4
    Q4 --> Q5[Weld Pool Temperature]
    Q5 --> Q6[Penetration and Bead Quality]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Module 2]
**State the principle of atomic hydrogen welding. Why is it called an indirect arc process?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Atomic hydrogen welding works on the principle of **dissociation and recombination of hydrogen molecules**. Hydrogen gas is passed through an electric arc struck between two tungsten electrodes, where the intense heat **dissociates H₂ into H atoms**. These atoms recombine on the cooler workpiece surface, releasing the dissociation energy as heat that melts the metal.
It is called an **indirect arc process** because the arc is maintained **between the two electrodes and not between the electrode and the workpiece**. Hence the arc does not directly strike the metal — heat reaches the metal via the recombination flame. **(1 mark each for principle + justification = 3 marks)**

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Module 2]
**List any three advantages and three disadvantages of atomic hydrogen welding.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Advantages:**
1. Produces the **hottest commercial flame (~6000 °C)**, enabling fast welding.
2. **No flux required** as hydrogen acts as a reducing shield against oxidation.
3. Suitable for welding **stainless steel, non-ferrous metals, and thin sheets** without distortion.

**Disadvantages:**
1. Equipment is **complex and costly** (torch, transformer, gas, water cooling).
2. **High operator skill** required to balance gas flow, arc, and travel speed.
3. **Hydrogen is explosive**; safety protocols (flashback arrestor, purging) are mandatory.
*(½ mark per point, 6 points = 3 marks)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Sample Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]

**(a) With a neat sketch, explain the working principle and equipment of atomic hydrogen welding. Discuss the role of tungsten electrodes and the use of AC supply. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. **Definition & principle (2 marks):** AHW is a fusion welding process where an electric arc is maintained between two non-consumable tungsten electrodes in a hydrogen atmosphere. The arc dissociates H₂ → 2H, and recombination of these atoms on the workpiece surface liberates 436 kJ/mol, producing a flame of ~6000 °C that fuses the metal.

2. **Equipment description (2 marks):** The setup includes (i) a step-down AC transformer with continuously variable reactor (80 V, 25 A typical), (ii) a special water-cooled torch holding two tungsten electrodes spaced 2–3 mm apart, (iii) a hydrogen cylinder with two-stage regulator, flow meter and flashback arrestor, (iv) a water cooling pump for the torch, and (v) optional filler rod holder.

3. **Role of tungsten electrodes (1.5 marks):** Tungsten has the highest melting point (3422 °C) of any metal and resists hydrogen erosion. Being non-consumable, it does not contaminate the weld pool. The *two-electrode* configuration allows the arc to float independently of the workpiece, so heat can be controlled precisely without tungsten dipping into the pool.

4. **Why AC supply (1.5 marks):** AC ensures **equal heating of both electrodes** (no rectification losses), eliminates **arc blow** caused by magnetic fields in DC, and provides a stable, self-regulating arc since the arc voltage drop is symmetric every half cycle. DC would overheat the cathode (electron emission side) and cause preferential electrode wear.

**(b) Compare atomic hydrogen welding with oxy-acetylene welding in terms of flame temperature, atmosphere, flux requirement, filler requirement, and typical applications. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

| Aspect | AHW (3.5 marks) | OAW (3.5 marks) |
|---|---|---|
| Flame temperature | **~6000 °C** — hottest commercial flame | ~3200 °C (neutral flame) |
| Atmosphere | Pure H₂ — strongly reducing | O₂ + C₂H₂ — can be oxidizing if O₂ excess |
| Flux | **Not required** (H₂ prevents oxidation) | Required for most ferrous metals |
| Filler rod | Optional; autogenous welding possible | Mandatory; no filler = no reinforcement |
| Electrodes | Two water-cooled tungsten electrodes | None (gas-only process) |
| Power source | AC transformer needed | No external power required |
| Typical applications | Thin stainless steel, Monel, Inconel, tool tips | General structural steel, thicker plates, plumbing |
| Modern status | Specialized / niche | Mostly replaced by arc processes |
| Cost | High (electricity + H₂ + equipment) | Moderate (only consumable gases) |

**Conclusion (0 marks but expected):** AHW is technically superior in flame temperature and atmosphere control but economically inferior, which is why it survives only in niche applications.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Sample Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]

**(a) Derive an expression for the heat released per kg of hydrogen recombining on the workpiece surface in atomic hydrogen welding. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Dissociation energy of H₂: $E_d = 436 \text{ kJ/mol}$
- Molar mass of H₂: $M_{H_2} = 2.016 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/mol}$

**Derivation:**

Energy released per mole on recombination = Energy absorbed per mole on dissociation (by conservation of energy):

$$E_{recomb} = E_d = 436 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

Converting per-mole quantity to per-kg quantity using the molar mass:

$$L_d = \frac{E_d}{M_{H_2}}$$

$$L_d = \frac{436 \text{ kJ/mol}}{2.016 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/mol}}$$

$$L_d = \frac{436{,}000 \text{ J/mol}}{2.016 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/mol}}$$

$$L_d = 2.163 \times 10^{8} \text{ J/kg}$$

$$\boxed{L_d = 216{,}270 \text{ kJ/kg} = 2.16 \times 10^{5} \text{ kJ/kg}}$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):** This energy density is more than 5× the heat of combustion of gasoline, explaining the extraordinary flame intensity of AHW.

**(b) A shipyard requires 1.5 kW of useful heat at the workpiece for welding thin stainless steel panels using AHW. If the transfer efficiency of recombination heat to the metal is 55%, calculate (i) the total recombination power needed, (ii) the mass flow rate of hydrogen, and (iii) the volumetric flow rate of H₂ at STP. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $Q_{useful} = 1.5 \text{ kW}$, $\eta = 0.55$, $L_d = 216{,}270 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $\rho_{H_2, STP} = 0.0899 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

**(i) Total recombination power (2 marks):**

$$Q_{total} = \frac{Q_{useful}}{\eta} = \frac{1.5}{0.55} = 2.727 \text{ kW} = 2.727 \text{ kJ/s}$$

**(ii) Mass flow rate of H₂ (3 marks):**

From the energy balance:

$$Q_{total} = \dot{m}_{H_2} \times L_d$$

$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = \frac{Q_{total}}{L_d} = \frac{2.727 \text{ kJ/s}}{216{,}270 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$

$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = 1.261 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/s}$$

**(iii) Volumetric flow rate at STP (2 marks):**

$$Q_v = \frac{\dot{m}_{H_2}}{\rho_{H_2}} = \frac{1.261 \times 10^{-5}}{0.0899} = 1.402 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Converting to litres per minute:

$$Q_v = 1.402 \times 10^{-4} \times 1000 \times 60 = 8.41 \text{ L/min}$$

**Final answers:** $Q_{total} = 2.73 \text{ kW}$, $\dot{m}_{H_2} = 1.26 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/s}$, $Q_v \approx 8.41 \text{ L/min}$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Typically Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Calling AHW a "direct arc" process** — it is *indirect* (arc between two electrodes, not electrode-to-work). Costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the role of hydrogen as a reducing shield** — many students say it "just supplies heat" and miss the anti-oxidation function. Worth 1 mark.
> 3. **Using DC instead of AC** in the circuit description — examiners deduct marks because DC causes arc blow and unequal electrode wear.
> 4. **Confusing flame temperature with arc temperature** — write them separately: arc ≈ 4000 °C, recombination flame ≈ 6000 °C.
> 5. **In calculations, mixing units** (kJ with J, mol with kmol) — keep a strict unit table. Sloppy units = 1–2 marks lost in derivations.
> 6. **Skipping the energy-balance steps** in numerical problems — examiners require explicit algebraic transitions, not just the final answer.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **AHW = indirect arc + chemical recombination heating** — never confuse with direct-arc processes.
- [x] **Inventor:** Irving Langmuir (1912).
- [x] **Two tungsten electrodes** (non-consumable), water-cooled, spaced 2–3 mm.
- [x] **AC supply** (not DC) — reasons: equal electrode heating, no arc blow, stable arc.
- [x] **Two stages:** Dissociation (endothermic, in arc) → Recombination (exothermic, on workpiece).
- [x] **Dissociation energy of H₂** = **436 kJ/mol**.
- [x] **Energy per kg of H₂** = **216,270 kJ/kg** (≈ 5× gasoline calorific value).
- [x] **Arc temperature** ≈ 4000 °C; **recombination flame** ≈ 6000 °C — hottest commercial gas flame.
- [x] **No flux needed** — pure H₂ provides a reducing shield.
- [x] **Filler rod optional** — autogenous welding possible for thin sheets.
- [x] **Best suited for:** stainless steel, Monel, Inconel, nickel, thin non-ferrous sheets.
- [x] **Drawbacks:** complex equipment, hydrogen safety (explosive), high operator skill, niche modern use.
- [x] **Safety essentials:** flashback arrestor, hydrogen purge before arc start, water cooling functional check.
- [x] **Energy balance mantra for exam:** $P_{electrical} \rightarrow P_{arc} \rightarrow P_{dissociation} \rightarrow P_{recombination} \rightarrow P_{useful}$ with efficiency factor at the last step.
- [x] **Memorize the AHW vs OAW table** — at least one comparison question appears every KTU cycle.
- [x] **Keywords for definition questions:** *dissociation, recombination, non-consumable, tungsten, indirect arc, reducing atmosphere*.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Radiant Energy Welding: Principle and Welding Arrangements
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST831 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Radiant Energy Welding: Principle and Welding Arrangements

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Radiant Energy Welding (REW)** is an advanced fusion welding process in which the heat required for joining metallic (and certain non-metallic) workpieces is generated by **focusing and concentrating a beam of high-intensity radiant energy** onto the faying surfaces. The radiant energy is supplied either in the form of a **stream of accelerated electrons** (Electron Beam Welding – EBW) or a **coherent, monochromatic beam of photons** (Laser Beam Welding – LBW). The concentrated beam creates a **keyhole (vapour cavity)** at the joint interface, enabling deep penetration welds with extremely narrow heat-affected zones (HAZ).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> "Radiant energy welding is a process wherein coalescence of metals is produced by heating them with concentrated radiant energy such as electron beam, laser beam, or arc image."

According to **ISO 4063** and **AWS C3.7**, radiant energy welding processes are classified under **Welding Process Group 5 – Radiant Energy Welding**, further subdivided into:

| Sub-Process | Process Code (ISO 4063) | AWS Designation |
|-------------|------------------------|-----------------|
| Electron Beam Welding | **51** | EBW |
| Laser Beam Welding | **52** | LBW |
| Arc Image Welding | **53** | AIW |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are outdoors on a bright sunny day. Normally, sunlight feels warm on your skin. Now, take a **magnifying glass** and focus the sun's rays onto a single small spot on a piece of paper. Within seconds, the paper begins to smoke and catches fire — even though the sun is millions of kilometres away. This happens because the magnifying glass concentrates **diffuse radiant energy** onto a tiny area, dramatically increasing its intensity.

**Radiant Energy Welding works on exactly the same principle**, but with far more sophisticated energy sources:

- The **Sun** in our analogy → A high-power **electron gun** or **laser resonator**
- The **magnifying glass** → A set of **electromagnetic focusing coils** (for EBW) or **optical lenses/mirrors** (for LBW)
- The **piece of paper** → The **metal workpiece** to be joined
- The **fire point** → The **fusion zone** (keyhole) where melting and vaporisation occur

In shipbuilding, where high-strength steels, aluminium-magnesium alloys, and exotic corrosion-resistant materials (e.g., austenitic stainless steels, titanium) are joined in thick sections for **submarines, naval hulls, and pressure hulls**, conventional arc welding may introduce excessive distortion, wide HAZs, or metallurgical defects. Radiant energy welding overcomes these limitations by delivering **extremely high power density** — typically ranging from **10⁵ to 10⁷ W/mm²** — to a very small spot.

---

## 1.3 Fundamental Physical Constants & Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Power Density Reference Values (Bold Highlighted for Quick Recall):**
> - **Conventional Arc Welding:** ~**10² – 10³ W/mm²**
> - **Plasma Arc Welding:** ~**10³ – 10⁴ W/mm²**
> - **Laser Beam Welding:** ~**10⁵ – 10⁶ W/mm²**
> - **Electron Beam Welding:** ~**10⁶ – 10⁷ W/mm²**

Other critical metrics:
- **Speed of light (vacuum):** $c = 2.998 \times 10^8$ **m/s**
- **Electron charge:** $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ **C**
- **Electron rest mass:** $m_e = 9.109 \times 10^{-31}$ **kg**
- **Stefan-Boltzmann constant:** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ **W/(m²·K⁴)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Operating Principle — The Two Variants

### 2.1.1 Electron Beam Welding (EBW)

The operating principle of EBW is governed by the **conversion of kinetic energy of accelerated electrons into thermal energy upon impact with the workpiece**.

The operational sequence is as follows:

1. **Electron Emission:** A tungsten filament cathode is heated to ~**2500 °C** by resistive (Joule) heating. Thermionic emission releases electrons from the cathode surface.
2. **Acceleration:** These electrons are accelerated towards a positively biased anode by a high voltage — typically **30 kV to 200 kV** in commercial EBW machines.
3. **Focusing:** The diverging electron beam is shaped and concentrated by **electromagnetic lenses** (copper coils carrying DC current).
4. **Vacuum Environment:** Both the gun and the workpiece are housed in a vacuum chamber at pressures of **10⁻³ to 10⁻⁶ mbar** to prevent electron scattering by air molecules.
5. **Energy Conversion at Workpiece:** Upon striking the metal surface, the electrons decelerate rapidly (over a depth of ~**10–100 μm**), and their **kinetic energy is converted to heat**, raising the local temperature above the melting point (~**1500 °C for steels, 660 °C for aluminium**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The physical depth of electron penetration into the metal is governed by the **Schreiber-Trump-Schwartz formula** (also called the "range equation"), which is critical for KTU numerical problems.

### 2.1.2 Laser Beam Welding (LBW)

The principle of LBW is based on **stimulated emission of photons** from a lasing medium, producing a **coherent (in phase), monochromatic (single wavelength), and collimated (parallel)** beam of light.

The operational sequence:

1. **Pumping:** External energy (electrical discharge, flash lamp, or another laser) excites atoms of the lasing medium (CO₂, Nd:YAG, fibre, or disc) to higher energy states.
2. **Stimulated Emission:** A spontaneously emitted photon triggers excited atoms to release identical photons of the same wavelength, phase, and direction.
3. **Optical Resonance:** Two parallel mirrors (one fully reflective, one partially transmissive) at either end of the resonant cavity amplify the beam through repeated reflections.
4. **Beam Delivery:** The coherent beam is delivered via **optical fibres** (fibre lasers, Nd:YAG) or **mirrors/lenses** (CO₂ lasers).
5. **Focusing and Absorption:** The beam is focused onto the workpiece using lenses or parabolic mirrors. The **focus diameter** typically lies between **0.1 mm and 1.0 mm**.
6. **Keyhole Formation:** At power densities above ~**10⁶ W/mm²**, the metal vaporises, creating a deep keyhole. Multiple reflections within the keyhole enhance energy absorption (the **"multiple reflection" or "Fresnel absorption" effect**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Keyhole" Welding?**
> In EBW and high-power LBW, the intense beam vaporises metal ahead of the molten pool, forming a vapour-filled capillary (the **keyhole**). The molten metal flows around this keyhole and solidifies behind it as the beam advances. This mechanism enables **deep penetration welds** — a single pass in EBW can join steel plates up to **200 mm thick** (e.g., nuclear pressure vessel fabrication).

---

## 2.2 Welding Arrangements in Radiant Energy Welding

### 2.2.1 EBW Arrangements

| Arrangement | Description | Application in Shipbuilding |
|-------------|-------------|------------------------------|
| **Vacuum Chamber Type (In-Vacuum)** | Both gun and workpiece enclosed in a vacuum chamber. Highest beam quality. | Welding of critical submarine hull components, reactor pressure vessels |
| **Partial Vacuum (Local Chamber)** | Only the beam exit and workpiece are in a small local vacuum chamber. | Larger ship section welding where full chamber impractical |
| **Non-Vacuum EBW** | Beam passes through atmosphere using differential pumping. Lower quality, but portable. | Field repairs of naval structures (less common) |
| **Positioning of Workpiece** | Flat plate, tube, circumferential joints (using rotating chucks) | Pipe-to-pipe welds in marine piping systems |

### 2.2.2 LBW Arrangements

| Arrangement | Description | Application in Shipbuilding |
|-------------|-------------|------------------------------|
| **Conduction Mode (Low Power)** | Power density ~**10⁵ W/mm²**. Shallow, wide welds. Surface melting only. | Thin sheet joints in superstructures, deck panels |
| **Keyhole Mode (High Power)** | Power density >**10⁶ W/mm²**. Deep, narrow welds. | Thick section hull plates, armour steel joining |
| **Hybrid Laser-Arc Welding (HLAW)** | Laser combined with MIG/MAG arc in single torch. | High-speed ship hull panel fabrication |
| **Remote / Robotic LBW** | Fibre-delivered laser with 6-axis robot or gantry. | Modular shipbuilding block assembly lines |

### 2.2.3 Process Selection Map

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Logic for Selecting EBW vs. LBW in Shipbuilding:**
> - **EBW preferred** when: maximum penetration (>50 mm), reactive metals (titanium, zirconium), nuclear-grade joints, vacuum-clean environment available.
> - **LBW preferred** when: high production rates, robotic automation, no vacuum chamber desired, dissimilar metals, hybrid welding required.
> - **Both EBW and LBW preferred** when: minimal HAZ, low distortion, high repeatability, and high joint strength-to-weight ratio are mandatory (typical for naval vessels).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates all **high-yield formulas** for Radiant Energy Welding. Master these for Part A and Part B numerical problems.

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Application |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | $E_k = \dfrac{1}{2} m_e v^2 = eV$ | Kinetic energy of accelerated electron | Joules (J) | EBW beam power calculation |
| 2 | $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 eV}{m_e}}$ | Velocity of electron under accelerating voltage V | m/s | EBW physics derivation |
| 3 | $\lambda = \dfrac{h}{m_e v} = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e e V}}$ | de Broglie wavelength of electron | m | EBW optics analogy |
| 4 | $P = VI$ | Beam power = voltage × beam current | Watts (W) | EBW power input |
| 5 | $q = \dfrac{P}{v_b}$ | Heat input per unit length | J/mm | Heat input analysis |
| 6 | $I_d = 0.0265 \cdot V^{1.5}$ | Langmuir-Child space-charge limited current | mA | EBW gun design |
| 7 | $d = 0.0285 \cdot V^{1.5}$ (≈) | Electron penetration depth (range) in steel — Schreiber equation | mm | EBW penetration depth |
| 8 | $\eta_{abs} = 1 - R$ | Absorptivity = 1 minus reflectivity | dimensionless | LBW energy coupling |
| 9 | $\theta_D = 1.22 \dfrac{\lambda}{D}$ | Diffraction-limited beam divergence (λ wavelength, D lens diameter) | radians | LBW beam optics |
| 10 | $d_f = \dfrac{f \cdot \lambda}{D}$ | Focused spot diameter | m | LBW focused spot size |
| 11 | $P_d = \dfrac{P}{\pi (d_f/2)^2}$ | Power density at workpiece | W/mm² | REW classification |
| 12 | $\dot{Q} = \epsilon \sigma A T^4$ | Radiative heat loss (Stefan-Boltzmann law) | W | Thermal losses in EBW/LBW |
| 13 | $v_b = \dfrac{P \cdot \eta}{A_{melt} \cdot \rho \cdot [c \Delta T + L_f]}$ | Welding travel speed | mm/s | Travel speed determination |
| 14 | $\dfrac{d}{d_a} = \dfrac{L_2}{L_1}$ | Beam demagnification by lens system | ratio | EBW electromagnetic optics |
| 15 | $w_0 = \dfrac{\lambda f}{\pi w}$ | Beam waist radius at focal point | mm | LBW focused spot |

**Legend for Table:**
- $V$ = accelerating voltage (V)
- $I$ = beam current (A)
- $m_e$ = electron rest mass (kg)
- $e$ = electron charge (C)
- $h$ = Planck's constant (6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s)
- $\lambda$ = wavelength (m)
- $\epsilon$ = emissivity
- $\sigma$ = Stefan-Boltzmann constant
- $L_f$ = latent heat of fusion (J/kg)
- $w_0, w$ = beam waist radii (m)
- $f$ = focal length (m)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Formulas 4–7 are essential for EBW machine parameter design; Formulas 8–11 govern LBW laser selection; Formulas 12–13 are used for thermal efficiency analysis in shipbuilding welding procedures.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Electron Velocity and Beam Power in EBW

**Given:**
- Accelerating voltage: $V$ = **60 kV = 60,000 V**
- Beam current: $I$ = **250 mA = 0.250 A**
- Electron charge: $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ **C**
- Electron mass: $m_e = 9.109 \times 10^{-31}$ **kg**

**Find:**
1. Velocity of electrons at the workpiece
2. Beam power delivered
3. Kinetic energy per electron

---

### Step 1 — Write the Energy Equation

Each electron acquires kinetic energy equal to the work done by the electric field:

$$ E_k = eV $$

This kinetic energy also equals $\frac{1}{2} m_e v^2$ from classical mechanics:

$$ \frac{1}{2} m_e v^2 = eV $$

### Step 2 — Solve for Velocity

Rearrange the equation to isolate $v$:

$$ v = \sqrt{\frac{2 eV}{m_e}} $$

### Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Values

$$ v = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times (1.602 \times 10^{-19}) \times (60 \times 10^3)}{9.109 \times 10^{-31}}} $$

### Step 4 — Compute Numerator and Denominator Separately

**Numerator:**
$$ 2 \times (1.602 \times 10^{-19}) \times (60 \times 10^3) = 2 \times 1.602 \times 60 \times 10^{-19+3} $$
$$ = 192.24 \times 10^{-16} = 1.9224 \times 10^{-14} \text{ J·kg (numerator has units of J·kg when rearranged)} $$

**Denominator:**
$$ 9.109 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg} $$

**Division:**
$$ \frac{1.9224 \times 10^{-14}}{9.109 \times 10^{-31}} = 0.2110 \times 10^{17} = 2.110 \times 10^{16} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2 $$

### Step 5 — Take Square Root

$$ v = \sqrt{2.110 \times 10^{16}} \text{ m/s} $$

$$ \boxed{v \approx 1.453 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s} } $$

> [!NOTE]
> This is approximately **48% of the speed of light** — relativistic corrections are small but non-negligible at voltages above ~100 kV.

### Step 6 — Calculate Beam Power

$$ P = V \times I = (60 \times 10^3) \times (0.250) $$

$$ \boxed{P = 15{,}000 \text{ W} = 15 \text{ kW} } $$

### Step 7 — Kinetic Energy per Electron

$$ E_k = eV = (1.602 \times 10^{-19}) \times (60 \times 10^3) $$

$$ \boxed{E_k = 9.612 \times 10^{-15} \text{ J} \approx 60 \text{ keV} } $$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Power Density and Classification of LBW Process

**Given:**
- Laser power: $P$ = **3 kW = 3000 W**
- Focused spot diameter: $d_f$ = **0.6 mm**
- Welding speed: $v_b$ = **25 mm/s**
- Plate thickness: $t$ = **6 mm (mild steel)**
- Reflectivity: $R$ = **0.35** (typical for polished steel at 1.06 μm Nd:YAG wavelength)

**Find:**
1. Power density at workpiece
2. Process classification (conduction vs. keyhole mode)
3. Effective absorbed power
4. Linear heat input

---

### Step 1 — Calculate the Focused Spot Area

The focused spot is approximately circular:

$$ A_{spot} = \frac{\pi d_f^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.6)^2}{4} $$

$$ A_{spot} = \frac{\pi \times 0.36}{4} = \frac{1.131}{4} $$

$$ \boxed{A_{spot} \approx 0.2827 \text{ mm}^2 } $$

### Step 2 — Calculate Power Density

$$ P_d = \frac{P}{A_{spot}} = \frac{3000}{0.2827} $$

$$ \boxed{P_d \approx 10{,}610 \text{ W/mm}^2 \approx 1.06 \times 10^4 \text{ W/mm}^2 } $$

### Step 3 — Classify the Process

Compare with reference values:

| Process Mode | Power Density Range |
|--------------|---------------------|
| Conduction Mode LBW | $< 10^5$ W/mm² |
| Transition Region | $10^5$ – $10^6$ W/mm² |
| **Keyhole Mode LBW** | $> 10^6$ W/mm² |

**Conclusion:** At **1.06 × 10⁴ W/mm²**, this is well within **conduction mode** — it will produce shallow, wide weld beads with no keyhole formation. To achieve keyhole mode, the spot diameter would need to be reduced or the power increased.

### Step 4 — Effective Absorbed Power

$$ \eta_{abs} = 1 - R = 1 - 0.35 = 0.65 $$

$$ P_{abs} = \eta_{abs} \times P = 0.65 \times 3000 $$

$$ \boxed{P_{abs} = 1950 \text{ W} } $$

### Step 5 — Linear Heat Input

$$ q = \frac{P_{abs}}{v_b} = \frac{1950}{25} $$

$$ \boxed{q = 78 \text{ J/mm} } $$

> [!NOTE]
> This heat input is far **lower than arc welding** (typically 500–1500 J/mm for MIG/MAG), confirming the low-distortion advantage of LBW in shipbuilding applications.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Schreiber-Trump-Schwartz Penetration Depth Formula (EBW)

The depth of electron penetration in a material (in mm) is approximated by:

$$ d = 0.0265 \times V^{1.5} $$

where $V$ is in **kV** for steel.

**Given:** $V = 150$ kV

### Step 1 — Compute the Exponent

$$ V^{1.5} = 150^{1.5} = 150 \times \sqrt{150} $$

$$ \sqrt{150} = \sqrt{25 \times 6} = 5\sqrt{6} \approx 5 \times 2.4495 = 12.247 $$

$$ V^{1.5} \approx 150 \times 12.247 = 1837.1 $$

### Step 2 — Apply the Formula

$$ d = 0.0265 \times 1837.1 $$

$$ \boxed{d \approx 48.7 \text{ mm} } $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** At 150 kV accelerating voltage, an EBW beam can theoretically penetrate **~49 mm of steel in a single pass** without needing joint preparation or multi-pass welds — a critical advantage in shipbuilding thick-plate applications such as **submarine pressure hulls and warship armours**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Focused Spot Diameter for LBW

**Given:**
- Laser wavelength: $\lambda = 1.06 \times 10^{-6}$ m (Nd:YAG)
- Lens focal length: $f = 100$ mm
- Collimated beam diameter at lens: $D = 10$ mm

**Find:** Focused spot diameter $d_f$

### Step 1 — Apply the Focusing Equation

$$ d_f = \frac{f \cdot \lambda}{D} $$

### Step 2 — Substitute Values (convert to consistent SI units)

$$ d_f = \frac{(100 \times 10^{-3}) \times (1.06 \times 10^{-6})}{10 \times 10^{-3}} $$

$$ d_f = \frac{100 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.06 \times 10^{-6}}{10 \times 10^{-3}} $$

### Step 3 — Simplify

$$ d_f = \frac{100 \times 1.06}{10} \times 10^{-3-6+3} $$

$$ d_f = 10.6 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} $$

$$ \boxed{d_f = 10.6 \text{ μm} } $$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **diffraction-limited minimum spot size**. In practice, aberrations and thermal lensing in optical fibres may increase this to **30–100 μm**, but it remains orders of magnitude smaller than arc welding arcs.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: EBW Process Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[High Voltage Power Supply 30 to 200 kV] --> B[Electron Gun Cathode Tungsten Filament]
    B --> C[Accelerating Anode]
    C --> D[Electromagnetic Focusing Lens DC Coils]
    D --> E[Vacuum Chamber 10-3 to 10-6 mbar]
    E --> F[Deflection Coils for Beam Steering]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — Concept Recall

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Q:** Define Radiant Energy Welding. List its two principal variants and mention one unique application of each in shipbuilding.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):**
> Radiant Energy Welding is a fusion welding process in which coalescence of metals is produced by heating them with **highly concentrated beams of radiant energy** such as electron beam or laser beam, typically resulting in deep penetration welds with very narrow heat-affected zones.

> **Two Variants (1 mark):**
> 1. **Electron Beam Welding (EBW)** – ISO 4063 code 51
> 2. **Laser Beam Welding (LBW)** – ISO 4063 code 52

> **Shipbuilding Applications (0.5 marks):**
> - **EBW:** Welding of submarine pressure hull components and nuclear-grade marine reactor vessels.
> - **LBW:** Robotic high-speed joining of thin superstructure panels and aluminium deck sections in modular shipbuilding.

---

### Question 2 — Brief Explanation

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Q:** What is a "keyhole" in the context of Electron Beam Welding? Why is its formation critical for deep penetration welds?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Keyhole Definition (1.5 marks):**
> A keyhole is a **vapour-filled capillary** that forms ahead of the moving weld pool when the power density of the EBW or LBW beam exceeds ~**10⁶ W/mm²**. The intense beam vaporises the metal directly beneath it, creating a narrow vertical cavity that extends through the entire plate thickness.

> **Critical Importance (1.5 marks):**
> 1. The keyhole allows the beam to **penetrate the full thickness of the plate in a single pass**, since the cavity effectively delivers energy to the bottom of the joint rather than just the surface.
> 2. **Multiple internal reflections** of the beam within the keyhole cavity greatly increase energy absorption (Fresnel absorption), enabling efficient welding of thick sections.
> 3. As the beam advances, molten metal flows around the keyhole and **solidifies behind it**, producing a deep, narrow weld with minimal HAZ and low distortion — critical for thick naval hull plates.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided)

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examinations, students are typically given an internal choice between two Part B questions from each module. Both options are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — EBW Focus**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the principle of Electron Beam Welding with a neat block diagram of the EBW system. Discuss the role of the vacuum environment and electromagnetic focusing in the process. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An EBW machine operates at an accelerating voltage of **120 kV** and a beam current of **400 mA**. The beam is focused to a spot diameter of **0.8 mm** on a mild steel workpiece. Calculate:
  (i) The velocity of electrons at the workpiece surface.
  (ii) The beam power in kW.
  (iii) The power density at the workpiece in W/mm².
  (iv) Comment on the process classification (conduction or keyhole). **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Principle and System Block Diagram (7 marks):**

> **Principle (4 marks):**
> Electron Beam Welding operates on the principle of **converting the kinetic energy of high-velocity electrons into thermal energy** upon impact with the workpiece.
>
> 1. **Electron Generation:** A tungsten filament cathode is heated to ~**2500 °C** by resistive heating, releasing electrons via **thermionic emission**.
> 2. **Acceleration:** A high voltage (30–200 kV) applied between the cathode and anode accelerates the electrons to ~**50–70% of the speed of light**.
> 3. **Focusing:** Electromagnetic lenses (copper coils with DC current) focus the diverging electron beam into a fine spot.
> 4. **Vacuum Environment:** The entire beam path and workpiece are enclosed in a vacuum chamber at **10⁻³ to 10⁻⁶ mbar** to prevent beam scattering by atmospheric molecules.
> 5. **Heat Generation:** On striking the metal surface, electrons undergo rapid deceleration within ~**10–100 μm**, converting their kinetic energy into intense localized heat that melts and vaporises the metal, forming a keyhole.
>
> **Block Diagram:** (Refer to **Figure 4.1** in Section 4 above — 1 mark)
>
> **Role of Vacuum (1 mark):** Without vacuum, electrons would scatter off air molecules (mean free path ~10⁻⁵ m at atmospheric pressure), severely degrading beam focus and penetration.
>
> **Role of Electromagnetic Focusing (1 mark):** Magnetic lenses provide variable, contamination-free focusing analogous to optical lenses but with greater controllability via coil current adjustment.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $V = 120$ kV $= 120 \times 10^3$ V
- $I = 400$ mA $= 0.400$ A
- $d_f = 0.8$ mm
- $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C
- $m_e = 9.109 \times 10^{-31}$ kg

**(i) Electron velocity (2 marks):**

$$ v = \sqrt{\frac{2 e V}{m_e}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 120 \times 10^3}{9.109 \times 10^{-31}}} $$

Numerator:
$$ 2 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 120 \times 10^3 = 3.8448 \times 10^{-14} $$

Division:
$$ \frac{3.8448 \times 10^{-14}}{9.109 \times 10^{-31}} = 4.221 \times 10^{16} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2 $$

Square root:
$$ v = \sqrt{4.221 \times 10^{16}} \approx 2.055 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s} $$

$$ \boxed{v \approx 2.05 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s} } $$

**(ii) Beam power (1.5 marks):**

$$ P = V \times I = 120 \times 10^3 \times 0.400 = 48{,}000 \text{ W} $$

$$ \boxed{P = 48 \text{ kW} } $$

**(iii) Power density (1.5 marks):**

$$ A_{spot} = \frac{\pi d_f^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.8)^2}{4} = \frac{2.0106}{4} = 0.5027 \text{ mm}^2 $$

$$ P_d = \frac{P}{A_{spot}} = \frac{48{,}000}{0.5027} $$

$$ \boxed{P_d \approx 95{,}484 \text{ W/mm}^2 \approx 9.55 \times 10^4 \text{ W/mm}^2 } $$

**(iv) Process classification (2 marks):**

| Mode | Power Density |
|------|---------------|
| Conduction | $< 10^5$ W/mm² |
| Transition | $10^5$ – $10^6$ W/mm² |
| **Keyhole** | $> 10^6$ W/mm² |

With $P_d \approx 9.55 \times 10^4$ W/mm², the process is in the **upper transition region** — *borderline keyhole*. For definitive keyhole behaviour, the spot diameter must be reduced (e.g., to 0.3 mm → ~**6.8 × 10⁵ W/mm²**) or beam current/voltage increased.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — LBW Focus**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the principle of Laser Beam Welding. Differentiate between **conduction mode** and **keyhole mode** welding. List three shipbuilding applications of LBW. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **4 kW Nd:YAG fibre laser** ($\lambda = 1.06 \mu$m) is used to weld **8 mm thick mild steel plates** at a travel speed of **1.2 m/min**. The focused spot diameter is **0.5 mm**. The surface reflectivity at the laser wavelength is **40%**. Calculate:
  (i) The linear heat input in J/mm.
  (ii) The heat input per unit area in J/mm² (per pass).
  (iii) Compare this heat input with conventional MIG welding (typical 600 J/mm) and state which produces lower distortion. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Principle and Modes (7 marks):**

> **Principle (3 marks):**
> Laser Beam Welding uses **stimulated emission of photons** from a lasing medium (e.g., Nd:YAG, CO₂, fibre) to generate a **coherent, monochromatic, collimated** beam. The beam is focused by lenses or mirrors to a small spot, generating extremely high power density that melts and vaporises the metal.
>
> **Conduction vs Keyhole Mode (3 marks):**
>
> | Parameter | Conduction Mode | Keyhole Mode |
> |-----------|-----------------|--------------|
> | Power density | $< 10^5$ W/mm² | $> 10^6$ W/mm² |
> | Penetration | Shallow, surface melting only | Deep, full-thickness single pass |
> | Weld shape | Wide and shallow (semi-circular) | Narrow and deep ("wine-cup" or "nail-head") |
> | Beam absorption | Single surface reflection | Multiple Fresnel reflections in keyhole |
> | HAZ | Wider | Very narrow |
> | Application | Thin sheets, sealing | Thick plates, structural joints |
>
> **Three Shipbuilding Applications (1 mark, 0.33 each):**
> 1. Welding of aluminium superstructure panels in cruise ships.
> 2. Robotic joining of stainless steel pipe sections in marine piping.
> 3. Hybrid laser-arc welding of ship hull blocks in modular shipyards.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $P = 4$ kW $= 4000$ W
- $\lambda = 1.06$ μm
- $t = 8$ mm (plate thickness)
- $v_b = 1.2$ m/min $= 1200/60 = 20$ mm/s
- $d_f = 0.5$ mm
- $R = 0.40$

**(i) Linear heat input (3 marks):**

$$ \eta_{abs} = 1 - R = 1 - 0.40 = 0.60 $$

$$ P_{abs} = \eta_{abs} \times P = 0.60 \times 4000 = 2400 \text{ W} $$

$$ q = \frac{P_{abs}}{v_b} = \frac{2400}{20} $$

$$ \boxed{q = 120 \text{ J/mm} } $$

**(ii) Heat input per unit area per pass (2 marks):**

For unit weld length, the heat input spreads over an area equal to (length × plate thickness):

$$ q_A = \frac{q}{t} = \frac{120}{8} $$

$$ \boxed{q_A = 15 \text{ J/mm}^2 \text{ per pass} } $$

**(iii) Comparison with MIG welding (2 marks):**

$$ \frac{q_{LBW}}{q_{MIG}} = \frac{120}{600} = 0.20 $$

> **Conclusion:** LBW delivers only **20%** of the heat input of conventional MIG welding. This **drastically reduces thermal distortion, residual stresses, and HAZ width**, making LBW superior for thin-section and precision shipbuilding applications. **LBW produces lower distortion.**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in REW Questions (KTU Examiner Insights):**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Converting accelerating voltage from kV to V (× 10³) and beam current from mA to A (× 10⁻³) is frequently missed — examiners award 0 for "numerically wrong but logically correct" answers.
>
> 2. **Confusing Penetration Formulas:** The Schreiber formula $d = 0.0265 \cdot V^{1.5}$ applies only to **steel**; other materials (Al, Ti, Cu) have different constants. Always state material assumption.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to Square the Spot Diameter:** Power density uses $\pi d_f^2 / 4$ for the **area**, not $d_f^2$ — students often write $P_d = P/d_f^2$ which is dimensionally wrong.
>
> 4. **Mixing Up Heat Input Definitions:** $q = P/v_b$ uses **effective absorbed power** after subtracting reflectivity losses, not raw laser power.
>
> 5. **Block Diagram Labelling:** Examiners expect **labelled** arrows with functional descriptions (e.g., "High Voltage Cable", "Beam Path"), not just generic arrows. Each major component must be named.
>
> 6. **Keyhole Mode Threshold:** Students frequently quote "$>10^6$ W/mm²" without stating the underlying **material reflectivity and absorption assumptions** — losing 0.5–1 mark per occurrence.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Radiant Energy Welding:**

- ⭐ **Definition:** Radiant Energy Welding uses concentrated beams of high-intensity energy (electrons or photons) to fuse metals with **extremely high power densities** (10⁵–10⁷ W/mm²).
- ⭐ **ISO 4063 Process Codes:** EBW = **51**, LBW = **52**, AIW = **53**.
- ⭐ **EBW Physics:** Kinetic energy of electrons $E_k = eV$ converted to heat on impact; requires vacuum (10⁻³–10⁻⁶ mbar) to prevent beam scattering.
- ⭐ **LBW Physics:** Stimulated emission produces coherent, monochromatic, collimated beams; keyhole formation above ~10⁶ W/mm² enables deep penetration.
- ⭐ **Keyhole Mechanism:** Vapour cavity formed by beam vaporisation of metal; enables deep penetration in single pass; Fresnel absorption boosts energy coupling.
- ⭐ **Schreiber Penetration Formula (Steel):** $d = 0.0265 \cdot V^{1.5}$ mm — for V in kV.
- ⭐ **Power Density Formula:** $P_d = P / (\pi d_f^2 / 4)$ — critical for mode classification.
- ⭐ **Mode Classification Thresholds:**
  - Conduction LBW: $< 10^5$ W/mm²
  - Keyhole LBW: $> 10^6$ W/mm²
  - EBW (keyhole inherent): $10^6$ – $10^7$ W/mm²
- ⭐ **Shipbuilding Applications:**
  - EBW → Submarine hulls, nuclear-grade pressure vessels
  - LBW → Aluminium superstructures, robotic hull panel joining
  - HLAW → High-volume modular shipbuilding
- ⭐ **Advantages over Arc Welding:** Narrow HAZ, low distortion, deep single-pass penetration, suitability for reactive metals, repeatable automation-friendly process.
- ⭐ **Limitations:** EBW requires vacuum chamber (expensive, size-limited); LBW requires precise joint fit-up, safety controls for high-power beams, and protection against reflective surfaces.
- ⭐ **Linear Heat Input Formula:** $q = P_{abs} / v_b$ — for LBW, always use **absorbed power** (subtract reflectivity losses).
- ⭐ **Constants to Memorise:** $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C; $m_e = 9.109 \times 10^{-31}$ kg; $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.
- ⭐ **Typical EBW Parameters:** 30–200 kV, 50–1000 mA beam current, 0.2–1.0 mm spot diameter, vacuum < 10⁻³ mbar.
- ⭐ **Typical LBW Parameters:** 1–25 kW laser power, 0.1–1.0 mm spot diameter, 0.5–10 m/min travel speed.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advantages and Disadvantages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST831 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Advantages and Disadvantages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Why Study Advantages and Disadvantages?

In **shipbuilding**, the choice of a joining technique dictates the structural integrity, productivity, cost, and seaworthiness of the vessel. Module 3 of the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus focuses on the **major fusion and arc welding processes** used extensively in hull fabrication, superstructure assembly, and outfitting. Every process has a unique set of **strengths** (advantages) and **limitations** (disadvantages) that an engineer must weigh before specifying it for a particular joint.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 3):**
> The student must be able to **compare and contrast** the common arc welding processes — *Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW)*, *Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW/MIG)*, *Flux Cored Arc Welding (FCAW)*, *Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW/TIG)*, and *Submerged Arc Welding (SAW)* — with respect to shipbuilding applications, productivity, weld quality, and operator skill.

## 1.2 Formal Definitions (KTU Terminology)

| Term | KTU 2024 Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Advantage** | A beneficial characteristic of a process that makes it preferable over alternatives for a *specific* shipbuilding application. |
| **Disadvantage** | A limiting characteristic (technical, economic, or safety-related) that restricts the use of the process in certain conditions. |
| **Process Selection Criteria** | The engineering decision matrix based on *material thickness*, *position of welding*, *production volume*, *mechanical property requirement*, and *ship class rule* (e.g., IACS, DNV, Lloyd's Register). |
| **Productivity (kg/h)** | The **deposition rate**, i.e., the mass of weld metal deposited per unit time — a key economic metric. |
| **Heat Input (kJ/mm)** | $\text{Heat Input} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S \times 1000}$, where $\eta$ is process efficiency, $V$ is voltage, $I$ is current, and $S$ is travel speed. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric (KTU Board Reference):**
> Heat input for **ship-grade mild steel (Grade A / AH36)** must typically be controlled between **0.5 kJ/mm and 3.5 kJ/mm** to avoid excessive HAZ softening or hydrogen-induced cracking.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Toolbox Analogy

Imagine a shipyard is a **doctor's clinic**, and the welding processes are the **doctor's medical instruments**:

- A **scalpel (GTAW/TIG)** is precise, clean, and ideal for delicate surgery (stainless-steel pipe joints, root pass) — but it is slow.
- A **heavy surgical stapler (SAW)** stitches thick plates (butt welds in hull plating) very fast, but cannot be used in tight corners.
- A **sturdy stitching needle (SMAW)** is the rugged, all-weather workhorse used by welders on scaffolding in a shipyard — it tolerates wind and dirt but produces spatter.
- A **continuous syringe (GMAW/MIG)** is fast, semi-automatic, and great for long seams on the dry dock floor.
- A **high-pressure caulking gun (FCAW)** is portable, fast, and works outdoors — but the smoke is intense and needs ventilation.

Just as a doctor picks the right instrument, a **shipbuilding engineer** must pick the right joining technique by understanding the **advantages and disadvantages** of each.

## 1.4 Roadmap of This Note

This study material covers the advantages and disadvantages of the **five major arc welding processes** used in shipbuilding (Module 3), plus a comparative engineering matrix so you can answer any "compare and contrast" question in the KTU End Semester Examination (ESE).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Major Arc Welding Processes — Quick Logic Map

Each process differs in:

1. **Electrode Type** (consumable vs. non-consumable)
2. **Arc Shielding Mechanism** (flux coating / gas / flux-cored / granular flux)
3. **Automation Level** (manual / semi-automatic / fully automatic)
4. **Deposition Rate** (kg/h) and **Weld Quality**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in shipbuilding:**
> A typical **Panamax bulk carrier** requires approximately **400–500 tonnes of weld metal** during construction. The choice of process directly affects the **ship delivery date**, **man-hour cost**, and **classification society approval**.

## 2.2 The Five Processes — Brief Operational Recap

| # | Process | Acronym | Electrode | Shielding | Automation |
| :--: | :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Shielded Metal Arc Welding | SMAW | Consumable stick (flux-coated) | Flux decomposition | Manual |
| 2 | Gas Metal Arc Welding | GMAW / MIG | Continuous consumable wire | Inert / active gas (Ar, CO₂) | Semi / Fully auto |
| 3 | Flux Cored Arc Welding | FCAW | Continuous tubular wire (flux-filled) | Flux core ± external gas | Semi / Fully auto |
| 4 | Gas Tungsten Arc Welding | GTAW / TIG | Non-consumable tungsten | Inert gas (Ar / He) | Manual / Auto |
| 5 | Submerged Arc Welding | SAW | Continuous consumable wire | Granular flux blanket | Fully automatic |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Unit | Typical Range (Shipbuilding) |
| :--: | :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | **Heat Input** | $HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S \times 1000}$ | kJ/mm | 0.5 – 3.5 |
| 2 | **Deposition Rate (SMAW)** | $DR \approx 1.0$ to $2.5$ | kg/h | 1.0 – 2.5 |
| 3 | **Deposition Rate (GMAW)** | $DR \approx 3.0$ to $6.0$ | kg/h | 3.0 – 6.0 |
| 4 | **Deposition Rate (FCAW)** | $DR \approx 4.0$ to $9.0$ | kg/h | 4.0 – 9.0 |
| 5 | **Deposition Rate (SAW)** | $DR \approx 8.0$ to $25.0$ | kg/h | 8.0 – 25.0 |
| 6 | **Deposition Rate (GTAW)** | $DR \approx 0.5$ to $2.0$ | kg/h | 0.5 – 2.0 |
| 7 | **Process Efficiency ($\eta$)** | SMAW $\approx 0.70$, GMAW $\approx 0.80$, FCAW $\approx 0.85$, SAW $\approx 0.95$, GTAW $\approx 0.60$ | — | 0.60 – 0.95 |
| 8 | **Hydrogen Control (H)** | Diffusible H content (per AWS A5.1) | mL / 100 g | H4 $\le 4$, H8 $\le 8$, H16 $\le 16$ |
| 9 | **Carbon Equivalent (CE)** | $CE = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni+Cu}{15}$ | % | $\le 0.40$ (typical for Grade A) |
| 10 | **Distortion (angular)** | $\theta \propto \dfrac{HI \cdot L}{t^{2}}$ | radians | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Examiner's Tip (KTU 2024):**
> In numerical problems, if the question says *"a single-pass weld is preferred"*, immediately think **high deposition rate process** (SAW or FCAW). If it says *"root pass on a stainless-steel pipe"*, think **GTAW**.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility in the Real World

- **Hull plating (thick mild steel 10–40 mm):** SAW + GMAW tandem
- **Superstructure (thin plates 3–6 mm):** GMAW short-circuit / FCAW
- **Pipe welding (stainless steel, Cu-Ni):** GTAW
- **On-board repair in engine room:** SMAW (portable, no shielding gas)
- **Butt welds in flat panel lines:** SAW (highest productivity)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Heat Input and Its Direct Effect on Weld Quality

**Step 1 — Start with the fundamental definition of electrical energy supplied per unit length of weld:**

$$E = V \cdot I \cdot t$$

where $V$ = arc voltage (Volts), $I$ = current (Amperes), and $t$ = arc time (seconds) for a given weld length $L$ (mm).

**Step 2 — Express the travel speed $S$ and substitute $t$ in terms of $L$ and $S$:**

$$S = \dfrac{L}{t} \quad \Rightarrow \quad t = \dfrac{L}{S}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back into the energy equation to get energy per unit length:**

$$E = V \cdot I \cdot \dfrac{L}{S} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{E}{L} = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{S}$$

The result is in **Joules per mm (J/mm)**.

**Step 4 — Convert to kJ/mm and apply the process thermal efficiency $\eta$:**

$$HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S \times 1000} \;\; \text{(kJ/mm)}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** A *process efficiency factor* $\eta$ accounts for the fact that not all electrical energy is delivered to the weld; some is lost as spatter, radiation, and convective losses in the shielding gas / flux.

**Step 5 — Worked KTU-Style Numerical Example:**

A shipyard welds **10 mm thick Grade A hull plate** using **GMAW** in the flat position. The welder sets:
- $V = 28$ V, $I = 240$ A, $S = 6$ mm/s
- Process efficiency for GMAW $\eta \approx 0.80$

Compute the heat input and verify it lies in the IACS-allowed range.

$$HI = \dfrac{0.80 \times 28 \times 240}{6 \times 1000}$$

$$HI = \dfrac{5376}{6000}$$

$$HI = 0.896 \;\; \text{kJ/mm}$$

> **Conclusion:** This heat input of $\approx 0.90$ kJ/mm lies within the typical range of 0.5 – 1.5 kJ/mm for thin ship plates, and the joint is safe from HAZ softening.

## 3.2 Derivation — Why SAW Has the Highest Productivity

**Step 1 — Compare deposition rates using a constant current $I = 600$ A:**

| Process | $\eta$ | Approx. DR (kg/h) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| SMAW | 0.70 | 2.0 |
| GMAW | 0.80 | 5.0 |
| FCAW | 0.85 | 7.5 |
| SAW | 0.95 | 18.0 |

**Step 2 — The reason:** SAW operates with **very high current (up to 1500 A)** because the **molten flux blanket** suppresses spatter and supports the high current density without arc blow. The flux also preheats the joint, allowing **deep penetration** with **single-pass welds** up to 25 mm thick.

**Step 3 — Engineering impact:** A 1 km long butt weld in a double-hull tanker fabricated with SMAW would take $\approx 14$ days; with SAW it is completed in $\approx 1.5$ days — a 9× productivity gain.

## 3.3 The Five Processes — Advantages & Disadvantages (Detailed Analytical Matrix)

### 3.3.1 Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW) — "The Welder's Stick"

**Advantages:**
- **Low equipment cost** — cheapest among arc welding processes; ideal for *shipyard subcontractor use*.
- **Portability** — no shielding gas cylinder required; excellent for *on-board emergency repair*.
- **All-position capability** — flat, horizontal, vertical, overhead.
- **Works in mild wind and dirty conditions** (rusty, painted plates common in ship repair).
- **Wide range of electrode types** for mild steel, low-alloy, stainless, cast iron.
- **Tolerant of long lead lengths** and **poor power regulation** typical in shipyard generators.

**Disadvantages:**
- **Low deposition rate** (1–2.5 kg/h) — uneconomical for long seams.
- **Frequent electrode changing** (stubs 250–450 mm) — reduces arc-on time.
- **Hydrogen pickup risk** if electrodes are not re-dried; critical for **AH36 / DH36** grades.
- **Slower welder skill ceiling** — high dependence on operator.
- **Spatter and slag inclusions** — requires chipping and grinding between passes.
- **Not easily automated** — limited to semi-automatic use.

> [!NOTE]
> **Shipbuilding Use Case:** SMAW is the **default fallback process** in a shipyard when a robotic GMAW/SAW station fails, or for *one-off joints* in confined spaces (engine room, double bottom).

### 3.3.2 Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW / MIG) — "The Continuous Wire"

**Advantages:**
- **Continuous wire feed** — high arc-on time, no stub loss.
- **Higher deposition rate** (3–6 kg/h) than SMAW.
- **Cleaner welds** — no slag, less spatter (especially in pulse mode).
- **Easily mechanised / robotised** — used in panel-line welding machines.
- **Good for thin sheets** (down to 1 mm) using short-circuit transfer.
- **Wide range of wires** — solid, metal-cored, and copper-coated for galvanised panels.

**Disadvantages:**
- **Requires external shielding gas** (Ar / CO₂ / Ar-CO₂ mix) — **wind-sensitive** above 5 km/h.
- **Higher equipment cost** than SMAW (wire feeder, gas regulator, water cooling for high current).
- **Porosity risk** if plate is dirty, oily, or rusty — common in *ship-repair docks*.
- **Spatter in CO₂ transfer mode** — needs anti-spatter spray.
- **Limited positional versatility** in globular transfer (better with pulsed-spray).

> [!NOTE]
> **Shipbuilding Use Case:** GMAW is the **workhorse for superstructure fabrication** and the **panel-line welding machine** (e.g., IHI, ESAB Travelcar) in modern shipyards.

### 3.3.3 Flux Cored Arc Welding (FCAW) — "The Outdoor Specialist"

**Advantages:**
- **High deposition rate** (4–9 kg/h) — almost matches SAW.
- **Self-shielded variant (FCAW-SS)** needs **no external gas** — excellent for *outdoor ship hull erection*.
- **Excellent positional capability** (vertical-down, overhead) for ship hull plates.
- **Deep penetration** and **good slag coverage** protect the weld from marine atmosphere.
- **High tolerance to dirty/rusty plates** — common in dry-dock repair.
- **Single-pass welds possible on 12–20 mm plate** in vertical-down mode.

**Disadvantages:**
- **Smoke and fumes are intense** — requires **powerful extraction** (shipyard respiratory hazard).
- **Higher wire cost** than solid GMAW wire.
- **Slag must be removed between passes** (more labour than GMAW).
- **Weld quality highly operator-dependent** in self-shielded mode.
- **Gas-shielded variant (FCAW-G)** is also wind-sensitive.
- **Hydrogen control** requires care in the consumable (H8 / H4 grade).

> [!NOTE]
> **Shipbuilding Use Case:** FCAW is the **preferred process for vertical-down hull welding** in Korean and Japanese shipyards; sometimes called the *Asian position* welding process.

### 3.3.4 Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW / TIG) — "The Precision Tool"

**Advantages:**
- **Highest weld quality** — clean, spatter-free, defect-free welds.
- **Precise heat control** — ideal for *thin sections*, *stainless steel*, *Cu-Ni piping*, *Titanium*.
- **No spatter, no slag, no fume** — best for clean environments (e.g., *cryogenic LNG carrier membrane tanks*).
- **Excellent for root pass** of butt welds in pipes (when full penetration is required).
- **Wide range of materials** — austenitic SS, aluminium, copper, dissimilar metals.

**Disadvantages:**
- **Lowest deposition rate** (0.5–2 kg/h) — slow and expensive.
- **High operator skill required** — manual GTAW demands two-hand coordination.
- **Very sensitive to arc length** — 2–3 mm tolerance.
- **Expensive equipment** (HF start, water-cooled torch, gas lens).
- **Not wind tolerant** — gas cup cannot be blown away; requires gas trailing shield outdoors.
- **Tungsten contamination** if the electrode touches the weld pool.

> [!NOTE]
> **Shipbuilding Use Case:** GTAW is the **mandatory root-pass process** for class-approved welds in *LNG cargo lines*, *stainless-steel exhaust systems*, and *Cu-Ni seawater cooling pipes*.

### 3.3.5 Submerged Arc Welding (SAW) — "The Heavy Industrial Process"

**Advantages:**
- **Highest deposition rate of all arc processes** (8–25 kg/h, up to 50 kg/h in tandem/quad-wire).
- **No arc flash, no spatter, no smoke** — best working environment for welder.
- **Deep penetration** — single-pass welds up to 25 mm (twin-wire up to 40 mm).
- **Excellent mechanical properties** — high toughness in the weld metal.
- **Fully automatic** — ideal for **panel-line and longitudinal bulkhead welding** in modern shipyards.
- **High efficiency** $\eta \approx 0.95$ — most of the electrical energy goes into melting.

**Disadvantages:**
- **Limited to flat or horizontal position** — cannot weld vertically or overhead.
- **Huge flux consumption** — flux must be recovered, dried, and recycled.
- **High capital cost** — requires **welding tractor, flux hopper, and water-cooled tip**.
- **Edge preparation mandatory** — narrow groove angles are not tolerated.
- **Flux inclusions / porosity** if flux is contaminated or wet.
- **Slag removal** between passes is heavy.
- **Not suitable for thin plates** — minimum thickness $\approx 5$ mm.

> [!NOTE]
> **Shipbuilding Use Case:** SAW is the **default process for hull panel-line butt welds** in block construction; the workhorse of European and Korean mega-shipyards.

## 3.4 Side-by-Side Comparative Matrix (KTU 2024 — High-Yield Table)

| Parameter | SMAW | GMAW | FCAW | GTAW | SAW |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Deposition Rate (kg/h)** | 1.0–2.5 | 3.0–6.0 | 4.0–9.0 | 0.5–2.0 | 8.0–25.0 |
| **Efficiency ($\eta$)** | 0.70 | 0.80 | 0.85 | 0.60 | 0.95 |
| **Position Capability** | All | All | All (vert-down best) | All | Flat/HF only |
| **Weld Quality** | Medium | High | High | Highest | High |
| **Wind Sensitivity** | Low | High | Low–Medium | Very High | None |
| **Automation** | Manual | Semi/Auto | Semi/Auto | Manual/Auto | Full Auto |
| **Equipment Cost (₹)** | Lowest | Medium | Medium | High | Highest |
| **Hydrogen Risk** | High | Low | Medium | Very Low | Very Low |
| **Slag** | Yes | No | Yes | No | Yes |
| **Best Shipbuilding Use** | Repair / Field | Superstructure | Hull vert-down | Root pass | Panel line |
| **Operator Skill Level** | Medium | Medium | Medium–High | Highest | Low (machine) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Arc Welding Decision Flow

The following block diagram maps the **decision logic** a shipbuilding engineer uses to pick the correct arc welding process, based on module-3 theory.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Joint Requirement] --> B{Position of Welding}
    B -- Flat or Horizontal --> C{Material Thickness}
    B -- Vertical or Overhead --> D{Production Volume}
    C -- Thickness more than 5 mm --> E[Select SAW or Tandem SAW]
    C -- Thickness less than 5 mm --> F[Select GMAW Short Circuit]
    D -- High volume --> G[Select FCAW Self Shielded]
    D -- Low volume or repair --> H[Select SMAW]
    E --> I{Weld Quality Required}
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I -- Class approved or Root Pass --> J[Use GTAW for Root Pass]
    I -- Standard fill and cap --> K[Proceed with Selected Process]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Verify Heat Input 0.5 to 3.5 kJ per mm]
    L --> M[Issue WPS and PQR per IACS Standard]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Comparison of Shielding Mechanisms

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[SMAW Shielding] --> A1[Flux Coating Decomposes to CO2 and Slag]
    B[GMAW Shielding] --> B1[External Argon or Ar CO2 Gas from Cylinder]
    C[FCAW Shielding] --> C1[Flux Core plus Optional External Gas]
    D[GTAW Shielding] --> D1[Inert Argon or Helium from Gas Lens]
    E[SAW Shielding] --> E1[Granular Flux Blanket Covers Arc Completely]
    A1 --> F[Protects Weld Pool]
    B1 --> F
    C1 --> F
    D1 --> F
    E1 --> F
```

## 4.3 Process Selection Matrix (Schematic View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Shipbuilding Joint Type] --> P1[Hull Plate Butt Joint]
    P --> P2[Pipe Root Pass]
    P --> P3[Superstructure Thin Plate]
    P --> P4[Outdoor Vertical Hull]
    P1 --> S1[SAW or Tandem GMAW]
    P2 --> S2[GTAW for Root then SMAW or FCAW for Fill]
    P3 --> S3[GMAW Short Circuit or Pulse]
    P4 --> S4[FCAW Self Shielded]
```

## 4.4 Productivity Comparison Bar Chart (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[SMAW 1 to 2.5 kg per h] --> X[Lowest Productivity]
    B[GTAW 0.5 to 2 kg per h] --> X
    C[GMAW 3 to 6 kg per h] --> Y[Medium Productivity]
    D[FCAW 4 to 9 kg per h] --> Y
    E[SAW 8 to 25 kg per h] --> Z[Highest Productivity]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Aid for Students:** The bar chart above uses **narrative blocks** to represent relative productivity. In your KTU answer script, you can redraw this as a simple horizontal bar chart for full marks on a "compare" question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**List any three advantages of Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW) over Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW) in a shipbuilding context.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Higher deposition rate** (3–6 kg/h vs. 1–2.5 kg/h for SMAW) — reduces welding time for long hull seams.
2. **Continuous wire feed** eliminates stub loss and electrode changing, increasing arc-on time by 30–40%.
3. **Cleaner welds** — no slag to chip, less spatter, easier post-weld cleaning, suitable for robotised panel-line machines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Split (KTU Style):** 1 mark for each correctly explained advantage.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**State two major disadvantages of Submerged Arc Welding (SAW) in shipbuilding.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Limited to flat and horizontal positions only** — cannot be used for vertical or overhead welds, restricting its use in hull erection where out-of-position joints are common.
2. **High capital cost and flux handling overhead** — requires automatic tractor, flux hopper, and flux recovery system, making it uneconomical for small-volume or repair jobs.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare the **advantages and disadvantages of Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW) and Flux Cored Arc Welding (FCAW)** in shipbuilding.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, describe the **submerged arc welding (SAW) process**. List four advantages and two disadvantages of SAW relevant to hull plate welding.

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — SMAW vs FCAW

| Parameter | SMAW | FCAW |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Electrode | Stick (250–450 mm) | Continuous tubular wire |
| Deposition rate | 1.0–2.5 kg/h | 4.0–9.0 kg/h |
| Shielding | Flux coating | Flux core ± external gas |
| Position | All positions | All, vert-down best |
| Slag | Yes (must be chipped) | Yes (less than SAW) |
| Wind sensitivity | Low | Low to medium (SS variant = none) |
| Equipment cost | Lowest | Medium |
| Operator skill | Medium | Medium–High |
| Best use | Field repair, confined spaces | Hull plate vertical-down welding |

**Advantages of FCAW over SMAW (4 marks):**
- FCAW has 3–4× higher deposition rate, cutting ship-hull welding time significantly.
- Continuous wire eliminates stub-end wastage (SMAW loses 50–80 mm per stick).
- Self-shielded FCAW is *truly portable* outdoors — no gas cylinder.
- Single-pass welds on 15 mm plate in vertical-down mode — SMAW needs multi-pass.

**Disadvantages of FCAW vs SMAW (3 marks):**
- Higher consumable cost (tubular wire is more expensive than mild-steel stick).
- Intense fumes — needs heavy ventilation in the shipyard.
- Higher operator skill required for vertical-down bead control.
- Hydrogen control (H8 grade) is critical for AH36/DH36 ship plates.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — Submerged Arc Welding

**Process Description (4 marks):**
SAW uses a **continuously fed consumable wire electrode** and a **blanket of granular flux** that completely submerges the arc. The arc is *invisible*; the heat melts the flux to form a slag and a gaseous shield. The flux is recovered, dried, and reused.

The setup includes: (1) Wire spool & feed motor, (2) Contact tube & current cable, (3) Flux hopper & nozzle, (4) Welding head/tractor, (5) Workpiece, (6) Slag & unfused flux recovery system.

**Advantages of SAW in hull plate welding (2 marks):**
1. **Highest deposition rate (8–25 kg/h)** — ideal for long panel-line butt welds.
2. **No arc flash, no smoke** — excellent welder comfort in the panel-line shop.
3. **Deep penetration** — single-pass welds on 20–25 mm plate.
4. **High weld-metal toughness** — meets IACS class requirements for Grade A, D, E, AH36.

**Disadvantages (1 mark):**
1. Flat/horizontal position only — no vertical/overhead capability.
2. Heavy flux handling & recovery system — high capital cost.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **advantages and disadvantages of Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW)** in the context of shipbuilding. Where is it most commonly used?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A shipyard welds a **10 mm Grade A mild-steel plate** using **GMAW** in the flat position. Given $V = 28$ V, $I = 240$ A, travel speed $S = 6$ mm/s, and process efficiency $\eta = 0.80$. Calculate the **heat input per unit length** and comment on whether it satisfies the **IACS heat-input range** for ship-grade mild steel.

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — GTAW in Shipbuilding

**Advantages (4 marks):**
1. **Highest weld quality** — no spatter, no slag, no porosity; mandatory for class-approved welds in *LNG carrier systems* and *stainless-steel piping*.
2. **Precise heat control** — ideal for thin sections (down to 0.5 mm) and root pass.
3. **Versatility on materials** — austenitic stainless steel, aluminium, Cu-Ni, titanium, dissimilar metals.
4. **Clean working environment** — no fumes; used in *engine room* and *membrane tank* welding.

**Disadvantages (3 marks):**
1. **Lowest deposition rate** (0.5–2.0 kg/h) — slow and expensive for hull plating.
2. **High operator skill** — manual GTAW is the most difficult arc-welding skill to master.
3. **Wind-sensitive** — argon gas cup cannot be disturbed; outdoor hull work needs trailing shield.
4. **High equipment cost** — HF start, water-cooled torch, gas-lens.

**Common Shipbuilding Use (any 2, 1 mark each):**
- Root pass on *stainless-steel pipe joints* (Cu-Ni seawater lines, hydraulic lines).
- LNG carrier membrane tank corner welds.
- Titanium exhaust systems on naval vessels.
- Repair of *austenitic* components in engine rooms.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — Heat Input Calculation

**Given:**
- $V = 28$ V
- $I = 240$ A
- $S = 6$ mm/s
- $\eta = 0.80$

**Formula:**
$$HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S \times 1000}$$

**Step 1 — Substitute values:**
$$HI = \dfrac{0.80 \times 28 \times 240}{6 \times 1000}$$

**Step 2 — Compute numerator:**
$$0.80 \times 28 \times 240 = 5376 \;\; \text{W}\cdot\text{mm}/\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Divide by $6 \times 1000 = 6000$:**
$$HI = \dfrac{5376}{6000}$$

$$\boxed{HI = 0.896 \;\; \text{kJ/mm}}$$

**Step 4 — Comment on IACS range (1 mark):**
The computed heat input of **0.896 kJ/mm** lies well within the **0.5 – 3.5 kJ/mm** range typically allowed by IACS classification societies for ship-grade mild-steel plates. The joint is safe from HAZ softening, hydrogen cracking, and excessive distortion. **Conclusion: Process parameters are approved.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Forgetting to divide by 1000** to convert to kJ/mm is the #1 mistake — you will lose 2 marks.
> - **Mixing up units of S** (mm/s vs. mm/min) — verify before computing. If $S$ is in mm/min, the heat-input formula changes to $HI = \dfrac{\eta V I}{S \times 60 \times 1000}$ (extra factor of 60).
> - **Using $\eta = 1$ for GMAW** — wrong; GMAW efficiency is typically 0.80–0.85.
> - **Forgetting to mention the IACS range** (0.5–3.5 kJ/mm) in the comment — you lose 1 mark.
> - In a comparative question, **forgetting to list disadvantages** of both processes — examiners deduct 2–3 marks.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (High-Density Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision — "10 Must-Recall Points Before the KTU Exam"**

1. **SMAW** = cheapest, most portable, lowest deposition rate, *all-position*, *no gas needed* — best for **field repair**.
2. **GMAW (MIG)** = continuous wire, *higher deposition* (3–6 kg/h), *easily automated* — best for **superstructure & panel lines**, **wind-sensitive**.
3. **FCAW** = flux-cored, *self-shielded* variant = no gas = ideal for **outdoor hull vertical-down**; **high fumes**.
4. **GTAW (TIG)** = *highest quality*, *lowest deposition* (0.5–2 kg/h), *highest skill* — mandatory for **root pass** and **stainless / Cu-Ni / Titanium**.
5. **SAW** = *highest deposition* (8–25 kg/h), *flat/horizontal only*, *no arc flash* — the **panel-line workhorse** for thick hull plates.
6. **Heat input formula:** $HI = \dfrac{\eta V I}{S \times 1000}$ kJ/mm — must lie in **0.5–3.5 kJ/mm** for ship-grade mild steel.
7. **Process efficiencies (approximate):** SMAW = 0.70, GMAW = 0.80, FCAW = 0.85, GTAW = 0.60, SAW = 0.95.
8. **Deposition-rate ranking (low → high):** GTAW < SMAW < GMAW < FCAW < SAW.
9. **Wind-sensitivity ranking (low → high):** SMAW ≈ FCAW-SS < SAW < GMAW < FCAW-G < GTAW.
10. **Hydrogen control is critical for AH36 / DH36 / EH36 grades** — use low-hydrogen consumables (H4 / H8) and re-dry electrodes.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Advantages and Disadvantages**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Different Types of Radiant Energy Welding: Electron Beam Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OENST831 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Different Types of Radiant Energy Welding: Electron Beam Welding ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Electron Beam Welding (EBW)** is a high-energy-density fusion welding process in which a precisely focused beam of **high-velocity electrons**, generated under high vacuum conditions, is accelerated toward the workpiece. Upon impact, the **kinetic energy** of the electrons is converted into **thermal energy (heat)** through collisions with the atomic lattice, causing localized melting and fusion of the parent materials to form a weld joint. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, EBW is classified under **Radiant Energy Welding** processes, where energy is transmitted to the workpiece without a physical electrode or filler material, via the focused beam of accelerated charged particles (electrons). > [!NOTE] > **Radiant Energy Welding** is a category of advanced joining processes that uses concentrated beams of energy — such as electrons or photons (laser) — to produce welds. EBW is one of the most established industrial members of this family. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition Imagine a **magnifying glass focusing sunlight** to burn a hole in a piece of paper. The magnifying glass concentrates weak, dispersed sunlight into a tiny spot, generating intense heat. Electron Beam Welding works on the **same principle** — but with electrons instead of photons: - A **cathode filament** acts like the "sun," emitting electrons. - **Magnetic/electrostatic lenses** act like the magnifying glass, focusing the electrons into an extremely narrow beam. - The **workpiece** acts like the paper, melting under the concentrated electron impact. > [!NOTE] > **Why Vacuum?** Electrons are extremely light particles. If air molecules are present, they would **scatter** the electrons before they reach the workpiece — just like fog scatters headlights. Therefore, EBW is performed in a **vacuum chamber** (typically **10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ torr**) to ensure a clean, focused beam path. ### 1.3 Key Physical Metrics | Parameter | Typical Value (Industrial EBW) | |-----------|-------------------------------| | Accelerating Voltage | **30 kV – 200 kV** | | Beam Current | **10 mA – 1000 mA** | | Power Density | **10⁶ – 10⁸ W/mm²** | | Vacuum Level | **10⁻⁴ – 10⁻⁶ torr** (1 torr ≈ 133.3 Pa) | | Focused Spot Diameter | **0.1 mm – 1.0 mm** | | Depth-to-Width Ratio (typical) | **20:1 to 50:1** | | Welding Speed | **100 mm/s – 500 mm/s** | ### 1.4 Classification of EBW by Vacuum Level > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 requires students to know the **three classifications** of EBW based on vacuum environment: 1. **High Vacuum (Hard Vacuum) EBW** – 10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ torr. Highest beam quality, used for critical aerospace, nuclear, and shipbuilding applications. 2. **Partial Vacuum (Soft Vacuum) EBW** – 10⁻¹ to 10⁻³ torr. Slightly compromised beam quality, larger work envelope. 3. **Non-Vacuum EBW** – Operates in atmospheric pressure. Lowest beam quality but allows welding of very large structures (limited use). ## 2.
