Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic > [!IMPORTANT] > The **principle of EBW** is governed by the conversion of **electrical energy → kinetic energy of electrons → heat upon impact**. 1. **Electron Emission (Cathode Stage):** - A **tungsten filament** (cathode) is heated to approximately **2500 °C – 2800 °C**. - At this temperature, electrons overcome the **work function** barrier of tungsten (≈ 4.5 eV) and escape from the surface — this is **thermionic emission**. - Alternative: **field emission** uses a high electric field to extract electrons from a cold cathode. 2. **Electron Acceleration (Anode Stage):** - A high negative potential (–V) is applied to the cathode. - The anode (at ground potential) attracts the electrons. - Each electron gains kinetic energy equal to: $$ KE = e \cdot V $$ - Where **e** is the electron charge (**1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**) and **V** is the accelerating voltage in volts. 3. **Electron Focusing (Magnetic Lens Stage):** - A **magnetic lens** (electromagnetic coil carrying DC current) focuses the diverging electron beam to a fine spot of diameter **0.1 mm – 1.0 mm**. - The focusing action obeys a relationship analogous to the **thin-lens equation in optics**: $$ \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{u} + \frac{1}{v} $$ - Where **f** is the focal length, **u** is the object distance (cathode-to-lens), and **v** is the image distance (lens-to-workpiece). 4. **Electron Deflection & Beam Steering:** - **Deflection coils** (electromagnetic coils with controlled current) allow the beam to be steered in X and Y directions, enabling **weld seam tracking** and **pattern welding** (e.g., circular or oscillating patterns). 5. **Energy Conversion at Workpiece:** - When high-velocity electrons strike the workpiece, they undergo **inelastic collisions** with atoms. - Their kinetic energy is converted to heat within a **shallow interaction volume**, causing rapid localized melting. - The depth of penetration is **directly proportional** to the accelerating voltage and inversely related to the material density. 6. **Solidification & Weld Formation:** - The molten pool solidifies rapidly due to the **steep thermal gradient** created by the small heat-affected zone (HAZ). - Result: **Narrow weld, deep penetration, minimal distortion**. ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table consolidates **all critical formulas** required for KTU 2024 numerical and analytical questions on EBW. | # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | Application | |---|---------|----------------|-------|-------------| | 1 | $KE = e \cdot V$ | Kinetic energy per electron | Joules (J) | Electron acceleration | | 2 | $P_{beam} = V \cdot I$ | Beam power (input electrical) | Watts (W) | Total power delivered | | 3 | $P_{density} = \dfrac{P_{beam}}{A_{spot}} = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{\pi r^{2}}$ | Power density (intensity) | W/mm² | Welding penetration capability | | 4 | $Q = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v}$ | Heat input per unit length | J/mm | Heat-affected zone analysis | | 5 | $d_{min} = \dfrac{K}{\sqrt{V}}$ | Minimum spot diameter | mm | Beam optics | | 6 | $f = \dfrac{e \cdot V \cdot r}{2 \cdot m_{e} \cdot v_{r}^{2}}$ | Focal length (electron optics) | mm | Lens design | | 7 | $v_{electron} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot e \cdot V}{m_{e}}}$ | Electron velocity | m/s | Speed of electrons | | 8 | $\eta_{thermal} = 0.65$ to $0.85$ | Energy conversion efficiency | dimensionless | Heat generated / Beam power | > **Where:** > - $e$ = electron charge = **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C** > - $m_e$ = electron rest mass = **9.109 × 10⁻³¹ kg** > - $\eta$ = process efficiency > - $v$ = welding travel speed (mm/s) > - $r$ = beam spot radius (mm) > - $K$ = constant (machine-dependent) ### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields | Industry | Application Example | |----------|---------------------| | **Shipbuilding** | Joining thick plates for **hull sections, propeller shafts, and pressure vessel liners** in submarines | | **Aerospace** | Welding of **titanium alloy compressor blades**, rocket motor casings | | **Nuclear** | Joining of **zirconium alloy fuel cladding**, stainless steel reactor vessels | | **Automotive** | Precision joining of **gear assemblies, transmission components** | | **Electronics** | Hermetic sealing of **vacuum tubes, sensors, MEMS devices** | > [!IMPORTANT] > In **shipbuilding**, EBW is preferred for joining **thick steel plates (>25 mm)** in a single pass without beveling — this dramatically reduces welding time and filler metal consumption. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Electron Velocity from Accelerating Voltage > **Given:** An electron is accelerated through a potential difference **V** volts, starting from rest. > **Find:** Final velocity **v_e** of the electron. **Step 1 — Energy Conservation:** The work done by the electric field on the electron equals the gain in kinetic energy: $$ W = q \cdot V = KE_{final} - KE_{initial} $$ **Step 2 — Apply boundary conditions:** Since the electron starts from rest, $KE_{initial} = 0$. Substituting $q = e$ (electron charge): $$ e \cdot V = \frac{1}{2} m_{e} v_{e}^{2} - 0 $$ **Step 3 — Solve algebraically for $v_e$:** $$ v_{e}^{2} = \frac{2 \cdot e \cdot V}{m_{e}} $$ $$ v_{e} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot e \cdot V}{m_{e}}} $$ **Step 4 — Numerical Verification (for V = 60 kV):** $$ v_{e} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times (1.602 \times 10^{-19}) \times (60 \times 10^{3})}{9.109 \times 10^{-31}}} $$ $$ v_{e} = \sqrt{\frac{1.9224 \times 10^{-14}}{9.109 \times 10^{-31}}} $$ $$ v_{e} = \sqrt{2.111 \times 10^{16}} \approx 1.453 \times 10^{8} \ \text{m/s} $$ > [!NOTE] > This velocity is approximately **48% of the speed of light** — which is why relativistic corrections begin to matter at voltages above 100 kV. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Power Density in the Focused Beam > **Given:** Beam voltage V, beam current I, and spot radius r at the workpiece. > **Find:** Power density $P_d$ at the focal point. **Step 1 — Total Beam Power:** The electrical power delivered to the electron beam is: $$ P_{beam} = V \cdot I $$ **Step 2 — Spot Area:** Assuming a circular focal spot of radius **r**: $$ A_{spot} = \pi \cdot r^{2} $$ **Step 3 — Power Density Definition:** Power density is power per unit area: $$ P_{density} = \frac{P_{beam}}{A_{spot}} $$ **Step 4 — Substitute:** $$ P_{density} = \frac{V \cdot I}{\pi \cdot r^{2}} $$ **Step 5 — Numerical Example (for V = 100 kV, I = 200 mA, r = 0.5 mm):** $$ P_{beam} = (100 \times 10^{3}) \times (200 \times 10^{-3}) = 20{,}000 \ \text{W} = 20 \ \text{kW} $$ $$ A_{spot} = \pi \times (0.5)^{2} = 0.7854 \ \text{mm}^{2} $$ $$ P_{density} = \frac{20{,}000}{0.7854} \approx 25{,}464 \ \text{W/mm}^{2} = 2.55 \times 10^{4} \ \text{W/mm}^{2} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > Compare this to **arc welding** (≈ 10³ W/mm²) and **laser welding** (≈ 10⁵–10⁶ W/mm²). EBW sits comfortably in the **high-energy-density** regime. --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Heat Input per Unit Length > **Given:** Process efficiency η, accelerating voltage V, beam current I, welding speed s. > **Find:** Heat input Q (J/mm). **Step 1 — Effective Heat Generated:** Not all beam power converts to useful heat at the weld. A fraction **η** is absorbed: $$ P_{heat} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I $$ **Step 2 — Heat per Unit Length:** Dividing by welding speed s (mm/s): $$ Q = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{s} $$ **Step 3 — Units Check:** $$ Q = \frac{[\text{dimensionless}] \cdot [\text{V}] \cdot [\text{A}]}{[\text{mm/s}]} = \frac{\text{W}}{\text{mm/s}} = \frac{\text{J}}{\text{mm}} $$ **Step 4 — Numerical Example (η = 0.75, V = 80 kV, I = 150 mA, s = 10 mm/s):** $$ Q = \frac{0.75 \times 80{,}000 \times 0.150}{10} = \frac{9{,}000}{10} = 900 \ \text{J/mm} $$ > [!NOTE] > The very **low heat input per unit length** of EBW (compared to arc welding at 1500–3000 J/mm) explains the **minimal HAZ and low distortion** — a critical advantage in shipbuilding where large welded assemblies must retain dimensional stability. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: EBW vs Other Radiant Energy Welding > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 frequently tests comparative understanding. The matrix below is exam-ready. | Parameter | Electron Beam Welding (EBW) | Laser Beam Welding (LBW) | |-----------|----------------------------|--------------------------| | Energy Source | Accelerated electrons | Photons (coherent light) | | Power Density | 10⁶–10⁸ W/mm² | 10⁵–10⁷ W/mm² | | Atmosphere | **High vacuum required** | Shielding gas or air | | Joint Preparation | **Minimal / none** | Minimal | | Penetration Depth | **Very high (up to 300 mm steel)** | Up to 25 mm typical | | Filler Metal | **Not required** | Sometimes used | | Heat-Affected Zone | **Very narrow** | Narrow | | Vacuum Chamber Cost | **High** | None | | Shipbuilding Use | **Hull plates, pressure vessels** | Thin sheet panels | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of an EBW System > [!NOTE] > The Mermaid diagram below maps the **functional architecture** and signal/power flow of a complete EBW installation — a standard KTU 2024 visual requirement. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[HV Power Supply
30 kV to 200 kV] --> B[Electron Gun
Tungsten Filament Cathode] B --> C[Accelerating Anode
Electron Beam Formation] C --> D[Magnetic Focusing Lens
Beam Collimation] D --> E[Beam Deflection Coils
X-Y Steering] E --> F[Vacuum Chamber
10^-4 to 10^-6 torr] F --> G[Workpiece Table
CNC XY Motion] G --> H[Workpiece
Melting and Fusion] H --> I[Solidified Weld Joint] I --> J[Vacuum Pump System] J --> F A --> K[Control Console
PLC / CNC] K --> A K --> E K --> G L[Cooling System
Water Circulation] --> B L --> D L --> E ``` ### 4.2 Process Flow of EBW Operation ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Step 1: Load Workpiece] --> B[Step 2: Seal and Evacuate Chamber] B --> C[Step 3: Achieve Target Vacuum] C --> D[Step 4: Heat Cathode Filament] D --> E[Step 5: Apply HV Bias] E --> F[Step 6: Generate Electron Beam] F --> G[Step 7: Focus and Steer Beam] G --> H[Step 8: Initiate Welding Travel] H --> I[Step 9: Monitor Weld Pool] I --> J[Step 10: Cool and Vent Chamber] J --> K[Step 11: Unload Welded Assembly] ``` ### 4.3 Weld Cross-Section Schematic (Visual Description for Notes) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 expects students to identify the **narrow, deep, parallel-sided weld profile** characteristic of EBW. ``` Top Surface of Plate ┌─────────────────────────────┐ │ ║ │ <- Plate top │ ║ │ │ ║ │ Weld Width: ~1–3 mm │ ║ │ Weld Depth: 25–300 mm │ ║ │ Aspect Ratio: 20:1 to 50:1 │ ║ │ │ ║ │ │ ║ │ │ HAZ │ <- Narrow HAZ on both sides └─────────────────────────────┘ (Schematic — not drawn to scale) ``` **Key observations:** - **Narrow fusion zone**: As wide as the focused spot (typically 1–3 mm). - **Deep penetration**: Often through the full plate thickness in a **single pass**. - **Parallel-sided weld**: Unlike arc welds which have a "V" or "U" profile. - **Narrow HAZ**: Typically 0.5–2 mm wide — minimizes metallurgical degradation. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]** *Define Electron Beam Welding. Why is it performed in a vacuum?* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** Electron Beam Welding (EBW) is a high-energy-density fusion welding process in which a focused beam of high-velocity electrons, accelerated by a high voltage (30–200 kV), is directed at the workpiece. The kinetic energy of the electrons is converted into heat upon impact, causing localized melting and fusion of the base materials. **Why Vacuum? (1.5 marks):** 1. To prevent **scattering of electrons** by air molecules, which would defocus the beam. 2. To prevent **oxidation** of the molten weld pool, ensuring high weld quality. 3. To allow **stable electron emission** from the cathode and prevent electrical breakdown at high voltages. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]** *List any four advantages of Electron Beam Welding over conventional arc welding processes.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** 1. **Very high power density** (10⁶–10⁸ W/mm²) enables deep penetration in a single pass without beveling. 2. **Minimal heat-affected zone (HAZ)** reduces distortion and metallurgical degradation. 3. **No filler metal required**, reducing material costs. 4. **High welding speeds** and ability to join dissimilar metals and refractory materials. 5. *(Bonus)* Produces **narrow, parallel-sided welds** with excellent mechanical properties. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 ESE Part B carries **14 marks**, typically divided as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Two alternative questions are provided as required. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]** **(a)** Explain the **principle of operation of Electron Beam Welding** with a neat functional block diagram. Discuss the role of each major component. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Derive the expression for the **power density of the electron beam** at the focal point. Given an EBW machine operating at 80 kV and 250 mA with a focal spot diameter of 0.6 mm, calculate the power density. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution — Part (a):** **Principle (2 marks):** EBW works on the principle of **conversion of kinetic energy of accelerated electrons into heat** at the workpiece. Electrons emitted by a heated tungsten cathode are accelerated to high velocities (≈ 10⁸ m/s) by a high-voltage field, then focused by a magnetic lens onto the workpiece. Upon impact, the kinetic energy is absorbed by the lattice, causing rapid localized melting. **Components (5 marks):** 1. **Electron Gun (Cathode + Anode):** Generates and accelerates the electron beam via thermionic emission and a high-potential field. 2. **Magnetic Focusing Lens:** A current-carrying electromagnetic coil that focuses the diverging electron beam onto a fine spot on the workpiece. 3. **Deflection Coils:** Electromagnetic coils that steer the beam in X and Y for weld tracking and pattern welding. 4. **Vacuum Chamber:** Maintains 10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ torr to prevent electron scattering and oxidation. 5. **High-Voltage Power Supply:** Provides 30–200 kV accelerating voltage. 6. **Workpiece Manipulator (CNC Table):** Moves the workpiece under the stationary beam. 7. **Vacuum Pumping System:** Maintains the required vacuum level. 8. **Control Console:** PLC/CNC-based system for parameter monitoring and automation. 9. **Cooling System:** Water circulation to cool the cathode, lens coils, and anode. --- **Model Solution — Part (b):** **Derivation (4 marks):** Starting from the definition of power density: $$ P_{d} = \frac{P_{beam}}{A_{spot}} = \frac{V \cdot I}{\pi r^{2}} $$ **Numerical Calculation (3 marks):** Given: V = 80 kV = 80,000 V, I = 250 mA = 0.250 A, d = 0.6 mm → r = 0.3 mm. **Step 1 — Beam Power:** $$ P_{beam} = V \cdot I = 80{,}000 \times 0.250 = 20{,}000 \ \text{W} = 20 \ \text{kW} $$ **Step 2 — Spot Area:** $$ A_{spot} = \pi \cdot r^{2} = \pi \times (0.3)^{2} = 0.2827 \ \text{mm}^{2} $$ **Step 3 — Power Density:** $$ P_{d} = \frac{20{,}000}{0.2827} \approx 70{,}735 \ \text{W/mm}^{2} = 7.07 \times 10^{4} \ \text{W/mm}^{2} $$ **Answer:** $P_d \approx 7.07 \times 10^{4}$ W/mm² --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]** **Q4. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]** **(a)** Compare **Electron Beam Welding (EBW)** and **Laser Beam Welding (LBW)** with respect to energy source, power density, atmosphere requirement, penetration, and typical applications. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A shipbuilding company needs to join two **30 mm thick low-carbon steel plates** for a hull section. As a production engineer, justify why **EBW would be preferred** over conventional shielded metal arc welding (SMAW). List at least **four process parameters** that must be controlled. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution — Part (a):** **Comparison Table (5 marks):** | Parameter | EBW | LBW | |-----------|-----|-----| | Energy source | Accelerated electrons | Coherent photons (CO₂, Nd:YAG, fiber laser) | | Power density | 10⁶–10⁸ W/mm² | 10⁵–10⁷ W/mm² | | Atmosphere | High vacuum required | Shielding gas (Ar, He, N₂) or air | | Penetration | Up to 300 mm in single pass | Up to 25 mm typically | | Joint preparation | None/minimal | Minimal | | Equipment cost | Very high (vacuum) | Moderate to high | | Application | Thick plates, aerospace, nuclear | Auto body, thin sheet, electronics | **Key Insight (2 marks):** EBW excels in **thick-section joining in vacuum**, while LBW is more versatile for **atmospheric shop-floor use** and is preferred for non-ferrous and thin-section applications. --- **Model Solution — Part (b):** **Justification (4 marks):** 1. **Single-pass full penetration:** EBW can penetrate 30 mm steel in a single pass without edge beveling, while SMAW requires multi-pass V-groove preparation (consuming filler metal and time). 2. **Minimal distortion:** The narrow HAZ and high power density reduce the heat input, minimizing plate warping — critical for **dimensional accuracy of hull sections**. 3. **Higher productivity:** Welding speed of 100–500 mm/s vs. 2–5 mm/s for SMAW, drastically reducing production time. 4. **Superior weld quality:** Vacuum environment eliminates porosity, slag inclusions, and oxidation defects common in SMAW. 5. **No filler metal cost:** Saves consumable expenses. **Process Parameters to Control (3 marks):** 1. **Accelerating voltage (kV)** — controls penetration depth. 2. **Beam current (mA)** — controls weld bead width and deposition. 3. **Welding speed (mm/s)** — controls heat input and bead profile. 4. **Focus current (A)** — controls spot size and power density. 5. **Vacuum level (torr)** — affects beam stability and weld integrity. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit errors:** Students frequently forget to convert kV → V and mA → A before power calculations. Always show unit conversions explicitly. > 2. **Confusing beam power with power density:** Beam power is in **watts (W)**, while power density is in **W/mm²**. They differ by a factor of the spot area. > 3. **Forgetting vacuum justification:** "EBW needs vacuum" alone is **incomplete**. Always cite the **three reasons**: prevent scattering, prevent oxidation, prevent high-voltage breakdown. > 4. **Mixing up EBW with LBW:** Both are radiant energy welding, but their **energy carriers, atmospheres, and penetration capabilities** differ fundamentally. > 5. **Skipping derivation steps:** KTU awards **method marks** — never jump from the given data straight to the answer. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final high-density summary for last-minute revision before the KTU 2024 exam.** - ✅ **EBW = Radiant Energy Welding process** using accelerated electrons. - ✅ **Principle:** Electrical energy → Kinetic energy of electrons → Heat at workpiece. - ✅ **Accelerating voltage range:** **30 kV – 200 kV** (high-voltage DC). - ✅ **Power density range:** **10⁶ – 10⁸ W/mm²** (very high). - ✅ **Vacuum level required:** **10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ torr** (high vacuum). - ✅ **Three types of EBW:** High vacuum, Partial vacuum, Non-vacuum. - ✅ **Key components:** Electron gun, Magnetic lens, Deflection coils, Vacuum chamber, HV power supply, Workpiece manipulator, Vacuum pump, Cooling system. - ✅ **Spot diameter:** **0.1 – 1.0 mm** (extremely fine). - ✅ **Weld aspect ratio:** **20:1 to 50:1** (deep and narrow). - ✅ **Filler metal:** Not required. - ✅ **HAZ:** Very narrow (0.5 – 2 mm). - ✅ **Power density formula:** $P_d = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{\pi r^{2}}$ - ✅ **Heat input formula:** $Q = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v}$ - ✅ **Electron velocity formula:** $v_e = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 e V}{m_e}}$ - ✅ **Major shipbuilding application:** Thick plate joining for hulls, propeller shafts, submarine pressure hulls. - ✅ **Key advantage:** Single-pass full penetration without beveling. - ✅ **Key limitation:** Requires vacuum chamber → high equipment cost, limited workpiece size. - ✅ **EBW vs LBW:** EBW uses electrons + vacuum; LBW uses photons + shielding gas. EBW has higher penetration; LBW is more versatile for thin sections. - ✅ **Energy conversion efficiency:** **65% – 85%** (very high compared to arc welding).

topics.title: Laser Beam Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST831 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Laser Beam Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Laser Beam Welding (LBW)** is a non-contact, fusion joining process that uses a highly coherent, monochromatic, and collimated beam of light generated by a laser source to melt and fuse the parent material, producing a weld joint. The energy concentration at the focal point produces extremely high power densities (typically in the range of **10⁶ – 10⁷ W/mm²**), enabling deep penetration welds with extremely narrow fusion zones.

In shipbuilding, LBW is officially classified under **High Energy Density (HED) joining processes** alongside Electron Beam Welding (EBW) and Plasma Arc Welding (PAW). It is recognized by KTU and classification societies (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, IRS) as a qualified process for joining mild steel, high-strength low-alloy (HSLA) steels, austenitic stainless steel, and aluminium alloys used in hull structures, deck panels, bulkheads, and superstructures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Laser Beam Welding (LBW) is a **fusion welding process** where the heat source is a focused laser beam, NOT an electric arc. This distinction is frequently tested in KTU examinations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine using a **magnifying glass** to focus sunlight onto a single point on a piece of paper — the paper burns instantly at that spot, even though sunlight is normally harmless. A laser works on a similar principle but is **millions of times more concentrated**.

In shipbuilding terms: instead of the traditional arc that spreads heat across a wide zone (like a campfire heating a tent), a laser acts like a **surgical laser scalpel** — it cuts a deep, narrow, clean seam through steel plate with surgical precision. The result is a weld that looks almost like a seam drawn by a fine pen.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Callout:**
> - **Coherent** = all light waves in phase
> - **Monochromatic** = single wavelength
> - **Collimated** = parallel rays (do not spread)
> - Together these three properties allow the laser beam to be focused to a microscopic spot, achieving astronomical power densities.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Power density of LBW | **10⁶ – 10⁷ W/mm²** |
| Weld pool width | **0.5 – 2.0 mm** |
| Depth-to-width ratio (keyhole) | **up to 10:1** |
| Welding speed | **1 – 10 m/min** (typical for shipbuilding) |
| Wavelength (CO₂ laser) | **10.6 μm** |
| Wavelength (Nd:YAG) | **1.06 μm** |
| Wavelength (Fiber laser) | **1.07 μm** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — The "Why and How" Behind LBW

The LBW process can be broken down into **four sequential physical stages**:

### Step 1 — Beam Generation
Electrical energy is pumped into a lasing medium (e.g., CO₂ gas, Nd:YAG crystal, or fiber optic doped with Ytterbium). This excites the atoms/molecules to a higher energy state.

### Step 2 — Stimulated Emission & Amplification
Excited atoms return to a lower energy state by emitting photons. These photons trigger other excited atoms to emit identical photons (**stimulated emission**), creating an amplifying cascade inside an optical resonator cavity (two mirrors).

### Step 3 — Beam Delivery & Focusing
The coherent beam is delivered via mirrors, fiber optics, or articulated arms and then focused through a lens (focal length typically 50–300 mm) onto the workpiece.

### Step 4 — Keyhole Formation & Metal Fusion
- The **intense power density vaporizes the metal**, forming a cavity called the **keyhole**.
- Molten metal flows around the keyhole walls.
- As the beam moves, the keyhole collapses and solidifies, forming the weld.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Concept — Keyhole vs Conduction Mode:**
> - **Conduction mode** (low power density, < 10⁵ W/mm²): shallow, wide weld (cosmetic welds).
> - **Keyhole mode** (high power density, > 10⁶ W/mm²): deep, narrow weld (shipbuilding structural welds).

## 2.2 Types of Lasers Used in Shipbuilding

| Laser Type | Wavelength | Beam Quality | Shipbuilding Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CO₂ laser** | 10.6 μm | Excellent | Thick plate butt welds, T-joints |
| **Nd:YAG** | 1.06 μm | Good | Stainless steel pipes, dissimilar joints |
| **Fiber laser** | 1.07 μm | Excellent | Modern shipyards (most common today) |
| **Disc laser** | 1.03 μm | Excellent | High-speed longitudinal welds |

## 2.3 Process Parameters

| Parameter | Typical Range | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| Laser power (P) | 1 – 25 kW | Increases penetration depth |
| Welding speed (v) | 1 – 10 m/min | Higher speed → narrower weld |
| Focal point position | On surface / below / above | Affects penetration |
| Shielding gas | Argon, Helium, N₂ | Prevents oxidation |
| Joint gap tolerance | ≤ 0.2 mm | Critical for quality |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following equations are KTU high-yield. Memorize all of them.**

$$
P_d = \frac{P}{A_f}
$$

Where $P_d$ = power density (W/mm²), $P$ = laser power (W), $A_f$ = focused spot area (mm²).

$$
Q = \eta \cdot \frac{P}{v} \cdot 60
$$

Where $Q$ = heat input per unit length (J/mm), $\eta$ = process efficiency factor (typically **0.7 – 0.85** for LBW), $P$ = power (W), $v$ = travel speed (mm/min).

$$
A_f = \pi \cdot \left(\frac{d_f}{2}\right)^2
$$

Where $d_f$ = focused beam diameter at the workpiece (mm).

$$
\text{Aspect Ratio} = \frac{\text{Penetration Depth}}{\text{Weld Width}}
$$

For LBW keyhole mode, aspect ratio can reach **6:1 to 10:1**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern shipyards such as Meyer Werft, Fincantieri, and Cochin Shipyard, **fiber laser welding robots** are deployed for:
- Welding **corrugated bulkheads** at speeds up to 5 m/min.
- Joining **stainless steel LNG tank membranes** (9% Ni steel + austenitic SS).
- Producing **duplex stainless steel** pipe joints in engine rooms.
- Reducing weld distortion by **70–80%** compared to traditional GMAW.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Power Density Calculation for LBW

### Problem Setup
A 4 kW CO₂ laser is focused to a spot diameter of 0.6 mm on a shipbuilding steel plate. Calculate the power density at the focal point.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the given data**
The laser power is the source energy output.
$$
P = 4 \text{ kW} = 4000 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the beam spot diameter**
The focused spot diameter is given as the width at the focal plane.
$$
d_f = 0.6 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the focal spot area (circular)**
The focused spot is treated as a perfect circle for analytical purposes.
$$
A_f = \pi \cdot \left(\frac{d_f}{2}\right)^2
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values into the area equation**
We square the radius term and multiply by pi to obtain the circular cross-section.
$$
A_f = \pi \cdot \left(\frac{0.6}{2}\right)^2 = \pi \cdot (0.3)^2 = \pi \cdot 0.09
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the numerical value**
We multiply by the constant pi.
$$
A_f = 0.2827 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute into the power density equation**
Power density equals total power divided by focal area.
$$
P_d = \frac{P}{A_f} = \frac{4000}{0.2827}
$$

**Step 7 — Final calculation**
Performing the division gives the concentrated power density.
$$
P_d \approx 1.414 \times 10^{4} \text{ W/mm}^2
$$

**Step 8 — Engineering interpretation**
The computed value is well above the keyhole threshold required for deep penetration welding.
$$
P_d = 1.414 \times 10^{4} \text{ W/mm}^2 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{Keyhole mode confirmed (} > 10^6 \text{ W/mm}^2\text{)}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Heat Input per Unit Length for LBW

### Problem Setup
A laser welding system operates at 6 kW power with a welding speed of 3 m/min on a ship hull plate. Given the LBW efficiency factor $\eta = 0.80$, determine the heat input per unit length.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify known parameters**
Power is converted to watts and travel speed is converted to mm/min.
$$
P = 6 \text{ kW} = 6000 \text{ W}
$$
$$
v = 3 \text{ m/min} = 3000 \text{ mm/min}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the heat input formula**
Heat input equals efficiency times power divided by speed, scaled by 60.
$$
Q = \eta \cdot \frac{P}{v} \cdot 60
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values**
Insert the values into the equation.
$$
Q = 0.80 \cdot \frac{6000}{3000} \cdot 60
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the division term**
Reducing 6000 divided by 3000 yields a clean value.
$$
Q = 0.80 \cdot 2 \cdot 60
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply sequentially**
Perform the multiplication in two stages for clarity.
$$
Q = 1.6 \cdot 60
$$

**Step 6 — Final heat input value**
The resulting heat input per unit length is the final answer.
$$
Q = 96 \text{ J/mm}
$$

**Step 7 — Engineering comparison**
The result is significantly lower than conventional arc welding heat input ranges.
$$
Q_{LBW} = 96 \text{ J/mm} \;\; < \;\; Q_{GMAW} \approx 600 \text{–} 1500 \text{ J/mm}
$$

**Step 8 — Conclude on distortion impact**
Lower heat input directly implies reduced residual stress in welded plates.
$$
\therefore \text{Minimal distortion and HAZ shrinkage — ideal for shipbuilding plate panels.}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Penetration Depth Estimation

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — State the empirical relation for LBW keyhole penetration**
For deep penetration laser welding, an empirical approximation relates power and speed.
$$
D \approx k \cdot \frac{P}{v^{0.5}}
$$

**Step 2 — Define constant k**
For typical shipbuilding steels under CO₂ laser keyhole welding.
$$
k \approx 0.10 \text{ mm}^{1.5} \cdot \text{s}^{0.5}/\text{W}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values**
Insert the power and speed values into the equation.
$$
D \approx 0.10 \cdot \frac{6000}{(3000/60)^{0.5}}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the velocity in mm/s**
The speed is converted to mm/s for dimensional consistency.
$$
v = \frac{3000}{60} = 50 \text{ mm/s}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the square root term**
Taking the square root of 50 gives the denominator.
$$
D \approx 0.10 \cdot \frac{6000}{\sqrt{50}}
$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate square root**
Square root of 50 is approximately equal to 7.071.
$$
D \approx 0.10 \cdot \frac{6000}{7.071}
$$

**Step 7 — Final division**
The division yields the final penetration depth estimate.
$$
D \approx 0.10 \cdot 848.5
$$

**Step 8 — Final penetration depth**
The estimated penetration depth is the final numerical answer.
$$
D \approx 8.5 \text{ mm in single pass}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Conclusion:** This penetration is sufficient for typical shipbuilding plate thicknesses (6–10 mm) in a **single pass**, eliminating the need for multi-pass welds required in SMAW/GMAW.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Summary

| Property | LBW | GMAW | SMAW |
|---|---|---|---|
| Heat input (J/mm) | 50 – 200 | 600 – 1500 | 800 – 2000 |
| Welding speed (m/min) | 1 – 10 | 0.3 – 1.5 | 0.1 – 0.5 |
| Distortion | Very low | Medium | High |
| Automation ease | Excellent | Good | Poor |
| Capital cost | Very high | Medium | Low |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Laser Beam Welding System

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the functional architecture and signal/energy flow of a complete LBW workstation as deployed in a modern shipyard.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PWR[Power Supply Unit] --> CON[Control Console CNC]
    LAS[Laser Source CO2 NdYAG Fiber] --> OPT[Optical Beam Delivery System]
    OPT --> FOC[Focusing Lens Assembly]
    FOC --> WP[Workpiece Ship Steel Plate]
    CON --> DRV[Servo Driven XY Table]
    DRV --> WP
    WPS[Wire Feeder Optional Filler] --> WP
    SHG[Shielding Gas Unit Argon Helium] --> WP
    WP --> SENS[Sensors Temperature Plasma]
    SENS --> CON
    CON --> MON[Monitoring HMI Display]
```

## 4.2 Process Flow Diagram — LBW Operation Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1 Power on Laser Source] --> B[Step 2 Set Process Parameters Power Speed Gas]
    B --> C[Step 3 Align Beam Focal Point]
    C --> D[Step 4 Initiate Shielding Gas Flow]
    D --> E[Step 5 Fire Laser Beam]
    E --> F[Step 6 Keyhole Forms and Melting Begins]
    F --> G[Step 7 Travel Along Joint Line]
    G --> H[Step 8 Keyhole Collapse Solidification]
    H --> I[Step 9 Inspect Weld NDT]
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Keyhole vs Conduction Mode Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph KEY[Keyhole Mode Power density greater than 10 to the 6 W per mm squared]
        K1[Focused Laser Beam] --> K2[Narrow Deep Cavity]
        K3[High Aspect Ratio Up to 10 to 1]
    end
    subgraph CON[Conduction Mode Power density less than 10 to the 5 W per mm squared]
        C1[Defocused Laser Beam] --> C2[Wide Shallow Pool]
        C3[Low Aspect Ratio about 1 to 1]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Note for Students:** The diagrams above use uppercase alphanumeric text only inside the node labels (no bold, italics, or HTML tags) to comply with Mermaid v10 syntax safety rules.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`
**Define Laser Beam Welding. State any two advantages of LBW in shipbuilding.**

**Model Answer:**
Laser Beam Welding is a fusion welding process in which a highly focused, coherent beam of light is used as the heat source to melt and join metallic parts. The beam is concentrated to a very small spot, producing power densities in the order of **10⁶ to 10⁷ W/mm²**, leading to deep, narrow welds.

**Advantages:**
1. Very high welding speed — enables automated mass production of ship panels.
2. Narrow heat-affected zone (HAZ) — minimal distortion in thin hull plates.
3. Capable of joining dissimilar metals (e.g., stainless steel to mild steel).

*(Any two well-explained points = 3 marks)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`
**Distinguish between conduction mode and keyhole mode in laser welding.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Conduction Mode | Keyhole Mode |
|---|---|---|
| Power density | < 10⁵ W/mm² | > 10⁶ W/mm² |
| Penetration | Shallow (surface melting) | Deep (vaporization occurs) |
| Weld shape | Wide, hemispherical | Narrow, deep |
| Aspect ratio | ~1:1 | Up to 10:1 |
| Application | Thin sheets, cosmetic welds | Structural shipbuilding welds |

*(3 marks for correct tabular comparison with one-line explanation)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

### `[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, Module 3]`

**Q. A (a)** Explain the working principle of Laser Beam Welding with a neat block diagram. Discuss the different types of lasers used in shipbuilding applications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle:**
LBW uses a focused beam of monochromatic, coherent, and collimated light. The beam is generated in a laser resonator cavity, delivered through an optical system, and focused onto the workpiece through a lens. At the focal point, the power density becomes extremely high, causing the metal to **vaporize and form a keyhole**. Molten metal surrounds the keyhole and solidifies behind the moving beam, forming a weld.

**Block Diagram (Refer Section 4.1 of these notes).**

**Types of Lasers Used in Shipbuilding:**

1. **CO₂ Laser** — Wavelength 10.6 μm, high power (up to 25 kW), excellent for thick steel plate welding.
2. **Nd:YAG Laser** — Wavelength 1.06 μm, transmitted via fiber optics, suitable for 3D robotic welding.
3. **Fiber Laser** — Wavelength 1.07 μm, high efficiency (>30%), currently the most preferred in modern shipyards.
4. **Disc Laser** — Wavelength 1.03 μm, very high beam quality for precision joints.

**Conclusion:** Among these, **fiber lasers dominate modern shipbuilding** due to high wall-plug efficiency, fiber delivery, and lower maintenance compared to CO₂ lasers.

*(Mark distribution: Principle – 2, Block diagram – 2, Laser types – 3 = 7 marks)*

---

**Q. A (b)** A laser welding system is used to weld 8 mm thick shipbuilding steel plate. The laser power is 5 kW, welding speed is 2.5 m/min, and process efficiency is 0.85. Calculate: **(i)** Heat input per unit length, **(ii)** power density if the focused spot diameter is 0.5 mm, and **(iii)** comment on whether keyhole mode will form. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
$$
P = 5000 \text{ W}, \quad v = 2.5 \text{ m/min} = 2500 \text{ mm/min}, \quad \eta = 0.85, \quad d_f = 0.5 \text{ mm}
$$

**(i) Heat input per unit length:**
We use the standard LBW heat input equation.
$$
Q = \eta \cdot \frac{P}{v} \cdot 60
$$
Substitute the given numerical values into the formula.
$$
Q = 0.85 \cdot \frac{5000}{2500} \cdot 60
$$
Reduce the fraction to simplify the arithmetic.
$$
Q = 0.85 \cdot 2 \cdot 60
$$
Perform the multiplication in two steps for clarity.
$$
Q = 1.7 \cdot 60 = 102 \text{ J/mm}
$$

**(ii) Power density:**
Compute the focal spot area using the circle formula.
$$
A_f = \pi \cdot \left(\frac{0.5}{2}\right)^2 = \pi \cdot (0.25)^2 = 0.1963 \text{ mm}^2
$$
Power density equals power divided by focal area.
$$
P_d = \frac{5000}{0.1963} = 2.546 \times 10^4 \text{ W/mm}^2
$$

**(iii) Keyhole mode confirmation:**
Compare the calculated power density against the threshold.
$$
P_d = 2.546 \times 10^4 \text{ W/mm}^2 \;\; > \;\; 10^6 \text{ W/mm}^2 \text{ (keyhole threshold)}
$$
Therefore the answer confirms keyhole formation.
$$
\therefore \text{Keyhole mode WILL form — deep penetration welding is achieved.}
$$

*(Mark distribution: Q calculation – 2, P_d calculation – 2, Interpretation – 3 = 7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q. B (a)** Compare Laser Beam Welding (LBW) with conventional arc welding processes. List the merits and demerits of LBW in shipbuilding. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | LBW | GMAW / SMAW |
|---|---|---|
| Heat source | Coherent light beam | Electric arc |
| Power density | 10⁶ – 10⁷ W/mm² | 10³ – 10⁴ W/mm² |
| HAZ size | Very narrow | Wide |
| Distortion | Negligible | Significant |
| Speed | Very high (1–10 m/min) | Low (0.1–1.5 m/min) |
| Joint preparation | Precise (≤0.2 mm gap) | Tolerant |
| Capital cost | Very high | Low – Medium |

**Merits of LBW in Shipbuilding:**
1. Single-pass deep penetration on 6–10 mm plates.
2. Suitable for automation and robotic production lines.
3. Excellent for austenitic stainless steel (LNG carrier membrane tanks).
4. Low distortion preserves hull plate flatness.

**Demerits of LBW:**
1. Extremely high capital investment (₹ Crores for fiber laser systems).
2. Strict joint fit-up tolerances required.
3. Safety hazards from high-power laser radiation (Class 4).
4. Difficult to weld highly reflective materials without special absorptive coatings.

*(7 marks: Comparison – 3, Merits – 2, Demerits – 2)*

---

**Q. B (b)** Explain the safety precautions and quality control measures required in laser welding operations inside a shipyard. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Safety Precautions:**
1. **Laser Safety Eyewear (LSE):** All personnel must wear OD 6+ goggles matching the laser wavelength.
2. **Enclosed Workstation:** Class 4 laser beams must be enclosed in interlocked cabinets.
3. **Beam Alignment Tools:** Use low-power alignment lasers only — never look directly into a Class 4 beam.
4. **Fume Extraction:** LBW produces metal vapor fumes — local exhaust ventilation is mandatory.
5. **Fire Safety:** High-power beams can ignite reflected surfaces — non-flammable shielding curtains required.

**Quality Control Measures:**
1. **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT):** Ultrasonic testing (UT), radiographic testing (RT), and dye penetrant testing (DPT) post-weld.
2. **In-process Monitoring:** Plasma photodiode sensors and thermal cameras detect weld pool anomalies in real time.
3. **Procedure Qualification:** All LBW procedures must comply with **IACS UR W17** and **AWS D1.1/D1.6**.
4. **Welder Qualification:** Operators must hold a Class 4 laser safety certificate plus a certified LBW welder qualification.
5. **Parameter Logging:** Every joint's power, speed, and gas flow are digitally logged for traceability.

*(7 marks: Safety – 3, QC – 4)*

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing units:** Students frequently forget to convert **m/min to mm/min** in the heat input formula. KTU examiners *strictly deduct 1 mark* for missing unit conversion.
>
> 2. **Wrong efficiency factor:** Many students use $\eta = 0.6$ (arc welding value) for LBW. Correct LBW $\eta$ is **0.7 – 0.85**. Wrong η = loss of 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Missing the "why" of keyhole mode:** Simply stating "keyhole forms" without explaining **vaporization → cavity formation → deep penetration** will earn only partial marks (2 out of 4 in 7-mark questions).
>
> 4. **No mention of safety:** In any 14-mark question on LBW, *always* include at least 2 safety points. Examiners consider safety as a **mandatory KTU 2024 outcome** per NEP 2020 alignment.
>
> 5. **Skipping classification society standards:** Naming **IACS UR W17** or **AWS D1.6** in your answer instantly boosts marks because KTU examiners reward industry-relevant terminology.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- LBW is a **HED fusion process** using a focused coherent light beam.
- **Power density threshold:** $\geq 10^6 \text{ W/mm}^2$ for keyhole mode.
- **Heat input formula:** $Q = \eta \cdot \dfrac{P}{v} \cdot 60$ with $\eta \approx 0.75 \text{ – } 0.85$.
- **Most common laser in modern shipyards:** **Fiber laser** (1.07 μm, >30% efficiency).
- **Aspect ratio:** Up to **10:1** in keyhole mode.
- **Key advantage in shipbuilding:** Minimal distortion → critical for thin hull plates.
- **Critical safety:** Class 4 laser — requires LSE goggles and enclosed workstation.
- **Applicable standards:** IACS UR W17, AWS D1.1, AWS D1.6, ISO 15614-11.
- **Joint fit-up:** Must be $\leq 0.2 \text{ mm}$ gap tolerance.
- **Shielding gases:** Argon, Helium, or Nitrogen (depending on material).
- **Disadvantages:** High capital cost, strict fit-up, safety hazards.
- **Application areas:** Bulkheads, deck panels, LNG membrane tanks, stainless steel pipe joints.
- **Always convert units** before substituting into the heat input equation.
- **Always state the efficiency factor $\eta$** explicitly in your solutions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Underwater Welding: Principle, Welding Arrangements, Advantages and Disadvantages, Specific Aspects, Precautions, and Wet Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST831 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Underwater Welding: Principle, Welding Arrangements, Advantages and Disadvantages, Specific Aspects, Precautions, and Wet Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Underwater Welding** is a specialized non-conventional welding process in which coalescence of metals is achieved by heating them to the required fusion temperature with or without the application of pressure, while the welding operation is being carried out in a submerged aqueous environment. It is governed by international standards such as **AWS D3.6M (Underwater Welding Code)** and is broadly classified into two principal categories:

1. **Wet Welding** — Welding performed directly in the surrounding water (ambient pressure environment).
2. **Dry Welding** — Welding performed inside a sealed chamber (habitat) where the water is displaced, allowing a dry, atmospheric or hyperbaric environment around the weld pool.

The fundamental principle relies on sustaining a stable **electric arc** between a consumable/non-consumable electrode and the base metal, with sufficient thermal energy to locally vaporize the surrounding water, creating a transient gaseous cavity (steam envelope) around the arc and weld pool.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Underwater welding is a mandatory topic under Module 3 of OENST831 because ship hull repairs, offshore platform maintenance, and submarine pipeline restoration are impossible without it. The ability to weld *in situ* (without dry-docking) is a strategic naval and commercial capability.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it like this:** Imagine trying to light a campfire inside a waterfall. The water keeps trying to douse your flame. To succeed, you need:
> 1. A **super-charged flame** (high-energy electric arc),
> 2. A **shield** that briefly pushes water away (vaporized steam envelope),
> 3. A **fuel that resists water damage** (waterproof coated electrode).
>
> Underwater welding works on exactly this principle — the welder-diver becomes the "fire-keeper" of an industrial arc, while the surrounding ocean is the "waterfall."

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| **Arc Temperature** | **6,000 °C – 20,000 °C** | Sufficient to dissociate water locally |
| **Water Pressure @ 10 m depth** | **≈ 1 atm (101.325 kPa)** | Hydrostatic rule: +1 atm per 10 m |
| **Typical Operating Depth (Wet)** | **0 – 50 m** | Practical diving limit for comfort |
| **Typical Operating Depth (Dry/Hyperbaric)** | **Up to 300 m +** | Used in deep-sea pipelaying |
| **Preferred Polarity** | **DCEP (Direct Current Electrode Positive)** | Provides deeper penetration, ~25% more heat in workpiece |
| **Standard Reference** | **AWS D3.6M:2010** | Class A (best) – Class C (lowest) weld quality |

### 1.4 Why Underwater Welding Matters in Shipbuilding

Ships in service develop cracks in the hull, propeller blades, rudders, and sea-water intake pipes. **Dry-docking a 200,000 DWT tanker can cost ₹2–5 crore and immobilize the vessel for 2–4 weeks.** Underwater welding allows emergency repairs to be carried out at berth or anchorage, saving both time and money — a critical capability for naval fleets and commercial shipping.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The wet underwater welding arc behaves fundamentally differently from an in-air arc. The sequence of events is:

1. **Electrode Initiation (Arc Striking):** The diver-electrode is brought into contact with the base metal. Contact resistance heating occurs, vaporizing the thin water film locally.
2. **Arc Formation & Vapor Cavity:** As the electrode lifts off, an arc is established. The extreme arc temperature (≈ 8,000 K) instantly vaporizes surrounding water, creating a transient **steam/gas envelope** (also called the *bubble* or *vapor cavity*) that displaces water within a few millimeters of the arc.
3. **Metal Transfer:** Metal droplets transfer from the consumable electrode to the weld pool. In wet welding, this transfer is predominantly **short-circuit or globular** because the shielding gas envelope is unstable.
4. **Pool Solidification:** The weld pool cools extremely rapidly due to the high thermal conductivity of surrounding water (≈ 0.6 W/m·K vs air at 0.025 W/m·K — water conducts heat **24× faster**).
5. **Microstructural Consequences:** Rapid cooling produces **hard, brittle martensite** in carbon steels (Hv 400+), reducing toughness and increasing hydrogen-cracking susceptibility.
6. **Collapse of Vapor Cavity:** As the arc moves away or stops, the steam envelope collapses with an audible *pop*, and water rushes back into the weld crater.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why wet welds are brittle:** The **cooling rate** in wet welding is approximately **10× faster** than in air. This is governed by Newton's law of cooling and the high convective heat-transfer coefficient of water.

### 2.2 Classification of Underwater Welding

```
UNDERWATER WELDING
        |
        +-- 1. WET WELDING (Direct in water)
        |       - Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW) – most common
        |       - Flux-Cored Arc Welding (FCAW)
        |
        +-- 2. DRY WELDING (Chamber-based)
                |
                +-- 2a. Habitat Welding (large dry chamber, 1 atm)
                +-- 2b. Hyperbaric Welding (pressurized chamber, >1 atm)
                +-- 2c. Localized Dry Chamber (small cofferdam-style)
```

### 2.3 Welding Arrangements — Detailed Description

#### A. Wet Welding (Most Common Arrangement)

- **Setup:** Diver holds a waterproof coated electrode in a specially sealed holder. Power cable runs from surface welding generator via waterproof umbilical.
- **Environment:** Direct contact with seawater.
- **Position:** Almost always **overhead or vertical-up** because the welder works from above the joint.
- **Electrodes:** Specially formulated with waterproof coatings (e.g., cellulose-based with paraffin or varnish seal).

#### B. Habitat / Chamber Welding (Dry)

- A **hyperbaric habitat** is lowered over the repair site and bolted to the hull. Water is pumped out; the chamber is pressurized to ambient sea pressure at that depth.
- Welder operates inside in a "shirt-sleeve" environment (1 atm relative pressure inside the chamber, but absolute pressure = depth pressure).
- Allows use of conventional GMAW, GTAW, SMAW processes.

#### C. Localized Dry Chamber

- A small transparent enclosure (often acrylic dome) is mounted directly on the workpiece.
- Gas (often argon or air) displaces water locally.
- Limited to small repair patches.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hydrostatic Pressure | $P = P_{atm} + \rho g h$ | Pa | Depth calculation |
| 2 | Pressure @ 10 m depth | $P \approx 2$ atm | atm | Quick rule of thumb |
| 3 | Cooling Rate (Newton's Law) | $\dfrac{dT}{dt} = -\dfrac{hA}{mc_p}(T - T_{water})$ | °C/s | Weld HAZ cooling |
| 4 | Heat Transfer Coefficient | $h_{water} \approx 24 \times h_{air}$ | W/m²·K | Why wet welds cool fast |
| 5 | Arc Energy (Heat Input) | $E = \dfrac{V \times I \times 60}{v \times 1000}$ | kJ/mm | Penetration & HAZ |
| 6 | Hardness (Martensite) | $H_v \approx 802 + 290 \cdot \text{C}_{eq}$ | HV | HAZ prediction |
| 7 | Carbon Equivalent (IIW) | $C_{eq} = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni+Cu}{15}$ | wt% | Crack sensitivity |

### 2.5 Engineering Utility and Real-World Applications

- **Naval Repairs:** Emergency hull patches on warships and submarines.
- **Offshore Oil & Gas:** Repair of platform legs, risers, subsea pipelines.
- **Commercial Shipping:** Propeller blade restoration, rudder repair, sea-chest welding.
- **Hydropower:** Underwater turbine blade repair without dewatering.
- **Bridge Piers:** Repair of submerged concrete-rebar pilings.

> [!NOTE]
> The North Sea oil platforms and the Trans-Alaska Pipeline system have historically depended on hyperbaric welding for routine subsea maintenance.

### 2.6 Advantages of Underwater Welding

1. **Eliminates dry-docking** — major cost and time saving.
2. **In-situ repair** — vessel/platform need not be taken out of service.
3. **Rapid emergency response** — critical for naval combat damage repair.
4. **Lower setup cost** than dry-dock (especially wet welding).
5. **Suitable for deep, inaccessible structures** where dry-docking is impossible.

### 2.7 Disadvantages of Underwater Welding

1. **Poor visibility** for the diver-welder.
2. **Rapid cooling** → brittle martensitic microstructure → cracking risk.
3. **Hydrogen embrittlement** due to dissociation of water into H⁺ and OH⁻ ions in the arc.
4. **Operator safety hazards** — electrical shock, drowning, decompression sickness.
5. **Lower weld quality** (Class B/C per AWS D3.6) vs in-air welding.
6. **Limited weldable positions** — primarily overhead/vertical.
7. **Porosity and slag inclusions** more prevalent than dry welding.

### 2.8 Specific Aspects Unique to Underwater Welding

| Aspect | Description |
|---|---|
| **Arc Stability** | Reduced due to vapor cavity oscillations (~100 Hz frequency) |
| **Spatter** | Excessive — water violently quenches molten droplets |
| **Slag Removal** | Difficult — slag tends to be granular, water-washed |
| **Bead Appearance** | Irregular, rippled, often oxidized surface |
| **Penetration** | Slightly higher due to rapid solidification shrinkage |
| **Distortion** | Lower because heat is extracted quickly |
| **Fume Disposal** | Bubbles rise to surface — can be toxic (Cr, Ni, Mn oxides) |

### 2.9 Precautions in Underwater Welding

> [!WARNING]
> **Diver safety is non-negotiable.** The combination of electricity, water, and a working diver demands rigorous safety protocols.

1. **Electrical Safety:**
   - Use **DCEP** (DC electrode positive) — reduces shock hazard to diver.
   - Equipotential bonding of all metal in the dive zone.
   - Use **GFCI (Ground Fault Circuit Interrupter)** on surface supply.
   - Insulated electrode holder with "Dead-Man Switch" (current only flows when switch is pressed).

2. **Electrode Selection:**
   - Use only **waterproof-coated electrodes** (e.g., AWS E6013 specially modified, or proprietary brands like *Phelps Dodge Ferro-Nickel*).
   - Store electrodes in airtight containers; moisture pickup destroys low-hydrogen coatings.

3. **Surface Preparation:**
   - Clean marine growth, rust, paint, oil by **hydraulic brushing or grinding**.
   - Remove salt deposits (cause chloride-induced cracking).

4. **Operational:**
   - Maintain **communication** between diver and surface tender.
   - Monitor **decompression limits** — no hot-work in saturation diving without hyperbaric chamber standby.
   - Ensure **adequate ventilation** of fumes rising to surface.

5. **Environmental:**
   - Avoid welding in contaminated water (oil spills → toxic fumes).
   - Stop work during poor visibility or strong currents.

### 2.10 Wet Welding — The Detailed View

Wet welding is the **most widely used** underwater welding method (~80% of all subsea welding jobs) because it is portable, economical, and rapid.

#### Characteristics of Wet Welding:

- **Process Used:** Primarily **Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW)** with specially designed waterproof electrodes.
- **Electrode Types:**
  - *Rutile-coated* (TiO₂ base) — general purpose, AC/DC compatible.
  - *Cellulose-coated* — for deep penetration.
  - *Ferro-nickel alloy* — for welding dissimilar steels or cast iron underwater; produces austenitic weld with low HRC.
- **Welding Current:** Typically **20–40% higher** than in-air equivalent to compensate for rapid heat loss.
- **Voltage:** Slightly **higher arc voltage** (by 2–4 V) due to longer arc length needed to maintain vapor cavity.
- **Travel Speed:** Slower than in-air because operator must compensate for poor visibility.

#### Welding Procedure (Step-by-Step) for Wet SMAW:

1. Surface preparation (cleaning).
2. Position diver at joint; establish ground (work clamp) close to weld area.
3. Strike arc with scratch technique; maintain short arc length (~3 mm).
4. Weld using **weave or stringer beads**; avoid excessive weave (causes slag entrapment).
5. After completion, allow bead to cool before next pass (avoid stacking hot beads).
6. Inspect visually — UT/RT inspection possible but difficult.

#### Wet Welding Quality per AWS D3.6M:

| Class | Application | Quality Level |
|---|---|---|
| **Class A** | Critical structural (pressure vessels, primary hull) | Highest — comparable to in-air |
| **Class B** | Secondary structural | Moderate — non-critical |
| **Class C** | Non-structural, temporary, or above-water-line patches | Lowest |

> Wet welds generally qualify only for **Class B or C** unless exceptional procedure qualification is performed.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Hydrostatic Pressure at Welding Depth

We start with the fundamental pressure equation for a fluid column:

$$P = P_{atm} + \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$

Where:
- $P_{atm}$ = atmospheric pressure at sea level = **101,325 Pa**
- $\rho$ = density of seawater = **1,025 kg/m³**
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration = **9.81 m/s²**
- $h$ = depth below water surface (m)

**Step 1:** Substitute values for a typical wet-welding depth of $h = 20$ m:

$$P = 101{,}325 + (1{,}025)(9.81)(20)$$

**Step 2:** Compute the hydrostatic term:

$$\rho g h = 1{,}025 \times 9.81 \times 20 = 201{,}105 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 3:** Add atmospheric pressure:

$$P = 101{,}325 + 201{,}105 = 302{,}430 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to atmospheres:

$$P = \dfrac{302{,}430}{101{,}325} \approx 2.985 \text{ atm} \approx 3 \text{ atm}$$

**Conclusion:** At 20 m depth, a hyperbaric habitat must withstand ~3 atm absolute pressure. This affects equipment design and diver decompression.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Why Wet Welds Cool Faster (Heat Transfer Analysis)

Using **Newton's Law of Cooling** for the weld pool:

$$\dfrac{dT}{dt} = -\dfrac{h \cdot A}{m \cdot c_p} (T - T_\infty)$$

Where:
- $h$ = convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $A$ = surface area of weld pool (m²)
- $m$ = mass of weld pool (kg)
- $c_p$ = specific heat of steel ≈ **450 J/kg·K**
- $T_\infty$ = surrounding temperature (water ≈ 4–25 °C)

**Step 1:** Compare heat transfer coefficients:
- In **air**: $h_{air} \approx 10\text{–}50$ W/m²·K
- In **water**: $h_{water} \approx 500\text{–}1{,}200$ W/m²·K

**Step 2:** Establish the ratio:

$$\dfrac{h_{water}}{h_{air}} \approx \dfrac{600}{25} \approx 24$$

**Step 3:** Since cooling rate is proportional to $h$, the cooling rate in water is ~**24× faster** than in air.

**Step 4:** Calculate cooling time constant $\tau$:

$$\tau = \dfrac{m \cdot c_p}{h \cdot A}$$

For a typical weld pool ($m = 0.005$ kg, $A = 2 \times 10^{-4}$ m²):

In air:

$$\tau_{air} = \dfrac{(0.005)(450)}{(25)(2 \times 10^{-4})} = 450 \text{ s}$$

In water:

$$\tau_{water} = \dfrac{(0.005)(450)}{(600)(2 \times 10^{-4})} = 18.75 \text{ s}$$

**Conclusion:** The weld pool in water cools in ~19 seconds vs ~7.5 minutes in air — explaining the formation of brittle martensite in the HAZ of underwater welds.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Carbon Equivalent and Crack Susceptibility

The **IIW Carbon Equivalent Formula** predicts cold-cracking tendency:

$$C_{eq} = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr + Mo + V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni + Cu}{15}$$

**Worked Example** — Typical shipbuilding steel ASTM A36 with composition: C = 0.20%, Mn = 1.0%, Cr = 0.05%, Ni = 0.05%, Cu = 0.10%, Mo = 0.02%, V = 0.01%.

**Step 1:** Substitute each term:

$$C_{eq} = 0.20 + \dfrac{1.0}{6} + \dfrac{0.05 + 0.02 + 0.01}{5} + \dfrac{0.05 + 0.10}{15}$$

**Step 2:** Compute each fraction:

$$C_{eq} = 0.20 + 0.1667 + \dfrac{0.08}{5} + \dfrac{0.15}{15}$$

**Step 3:** Add:

$$C_{eq} = 0.20 + 0.1667 + 0.0160 + 0.0100$$

**Step 4:** Final result:

$$C_{eq} = 0.3927 \approx 0.39$$

**Interpretation:** $C_{eq} > 0.40$ → **high cracking risk**. A36 is borderline; for underwater wet welding, **preheating is impractical**, so a low-C_eq electrode or austenitic filler (ferro-nickel) is preferred.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Heat Input Calculation

The **heat input** $E$ governs weld penetration and HAZ width:

$$E = \dfrac{V \times I \times 60}{v \times 1000} \quad \text{[kJ/mm]}$$

Where:
- $V$ = arc voltage (V)
- $I$ = welding current (A)
- $v$ = travel speed (mm/min)

**Example:** Wet SMAW at $V = 25$ V, $I = 180$ A, $v = 150$ mm/min.

**Step 1:** Numerator:

$$V \times I \times 60 = 25 \times 180 \times 60 = 270{,}000$$

**Step 2:** Denominator:

$$v \times 1000 = 150 \times 1000 = 150{,}000$$

**Step 3:** Divide:

$$E = \dfrac{270{,}000}{150{,}000} = 1.80 \text{ kJ/mm}$$

**Conclusion:** For wet welding, heat input must be increased by 20–40% over in-air (typically 1.5–2.5 kJ/mm) to compensate for rapid heat extraction by water.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Process Selection in Underwater Welding

| Scenario | Recommended Process | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Emergency hull patch at port | Wet SMAW | Fast, cheap, no chamber needed |
| Critical pressure vessel repair | Hyperbaric GTAW/GMAW | Class A weld quality required |
| Submarine pipeline tie-in | Hyperbaric GMAW (orbital) | Deep water, high integrity |
| Above-water-line crack | Wet SMAW (splash zone) | Diver in surface-supplied air |
| Cast iron propeller blade | Wet SMAW with Ni-Fe electrode | Austenitic weld, no cracking |
| Offshore platform leg | Habitat welding (dry) | Critical fatigue-loaded joint |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Underwater Welding Process Classification

```mermaid

    C1 --> E3[In Air Quality]

    C2 --> F3[Class A Welds]

```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Cooling Sequence and Microstructure Formation

```mermaid

    POOL --> WATER[Surrounding Water at 4 to 25C]
    WATER --> CONVECT[Forced Convection Cooling]
    CONVECT --> RATE[Cooling Rate 100 to 500 C per second]

    RATE --> TTT[TTT Diagram Crossing]
    TTT --> MART[Martensite Formation Below Ms]
    MART --> HV[Hardness 400 plus HV]
    HV --> CRACK[Hydrogen Induced Cracking Risk]

    H[Water Dissociation in Arc] --> H2[Atomic Hydrogen]
    H2 --> DIF[Diffuses into Weld]
    DIF --> TRAP[Trapped at Dislocations]
    TRAP --> CRACK

    CRACK --> SOL[Solution Austenitic Filler]
    CRACK --> SOL2[Preheat in Habitat Only]
    SOL --> SAFE[Safer Weld]
    SOL2 --> SAFE
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU University Exam Pattern:** Part A carries 3 marks; answer in 4–5 sentences with a labeled diagram wherever possible.

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] Define underwater welding. List any four advantages.**

**Model Answer:**

Underwater welding is a non-conventional welding process performed in a submerged aqueous environment to achieve coalescence of metals, primarily used for marine hull repairs and offshore structure maintenance. It is classified into **wet welding** (direct in water) and **dry welding** (inside a habitat chamber).

**Four Advantages:**
1. Eliminates the need for dry-docking — major cost saving.
2. Repairs can be carried out without taking the vessel out of service.
3. Rapid emergency response capability (especially for naval ships).
4. Lower setup cost compared to dry-dock, especially for wet welding.

(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for four advantages @ 0.5 each)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] List six precautions to be observed during underwater wet welding.**

**Model Answer:**

1. Use **DCEP polarity** and equipotential bonding to prevent electric shock to diver.
2. Employ **GFCI** and **dead-man switch** on welding power source.
3. Use only **waterproof-coated electrodes** stored in airtight containers.
4. Clean the joint of marine growth, rust, and chloride deposits before welding.
5. Maintain continuous **surface-to-diver communication**.
6. Ensure fume management and observe **decompression limits** of the diver.

(3 marks: 6 points × 0.5 marks each)

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Internal choice is provided between **Question A** and **Question B**.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *Wet Welding in Detail*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**

#### (a) Explain the principle of wet underwater welding with a neat sketch. Discuss the formation of the vapor cavity and its role in sustaining the arc. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:**
Wet underwater welding is performed with the arc and weld pool in direct contact with the surrounding water. The arc is struck between a consumable waterproof-coated electrode and the base metal. The arc temperature (≈ 8,000 K) instantly vaporizes the water in a small zone around the arc, producing a **transient steam envelope** (vapor cavity) that locally displaces water and allows the arc to be sustained for a brief interval.

**Vapor Cavity Formation:**
- The intense arc heat dissociates water molecules into H₂ and O₂ gases (and ionized plasma).
- These gases form a bubble that envelops the arc and weld pool.
- The cavity oscillates in size (~100 Hz) due to fluctuations in arc pressure.
- When the arc moves away or stops, the cavity **collapses abruptly**, producing a characteristic *pop* sound.

**Role:**
- It acts as a **temporary gaseous shield** allowing metal transfer.
- Without it, water would quench the arc immediately.

*(Sketch: simple diagram showing electrode, arc, vapor cavity, weld pool, base metal, surrounding water.)*

**Mark split:**
- Principle (3 marks)
- Vapor cavity formation (2 marks)
- Role + sketch (2 marks)

---

#### (b) Discuss the specific aspects of wet welding — bead shape, microstructure, slag, and spatter — and explain why the HAZ becomes hard and brittle. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Bead Shape:** Irregular, rippled surface; slightly convex due to rapid solidification. Width varies because of unstable arc length maintained by the diver.

**Microstructure:** The HAZ of carbon steel welds contains **hard, brittle martensite** (Hv 400+) because:
- Cooling rate is ~24× faster in water than in air.
- Austenite → martensite transformation occurs without sufficient tempering.
- Hydrogen from dissociated water diffuses into the HAZ → **hydrogen-induced cracking**.

**Slag:** Difficult to remove; tends to be granular and water-diluted. Slag inclusions reduce weld quality.

**Spatter:** Excessive because water violently quenches molten droplets ejected from the arc.

**Hardness control:** Use **ferro-nickel austenitic electrodes** to produce a tough, non-martensitic weld metal; or use hyperbaric (dry) welding for critical joints.

**Mark split:**
- Bead, slag, spatter (3 marks)
- HAZ microstructure & martensite explanation (3 marks)
- Hydrogen cracking + remedial measure (1 mark)

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *Dry Welding & Hyperbaric Systems*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3, Alternative Choice]**

#### (a) Describe with sketches the different arrangements for underwater welding: (i) wet welding, (ii) habitat welding, (iii) hyperbaric welding. Compare their quality and depth capability. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Wet Welding:** Diver holds electrode directly in water; no enclosure. Quality: AWS Class B/C. Depth: 0–50 m typical.

**(ii) Habitat Welding:** A large dry chamber is sealed to the hull; water is pumped out. Welder works in shirt-sleeve conditions at 1 atm relative pressure. Quality: AWS Class A achievable. Depth: up to ~50 m practical.

**(iii) Hyperbaric Welding:** Chamber is pressurized to ambient sea pressure at depth. Allows in-chamber welding at full sea pressure. Quality: Class A; used for deep pipelines. Depth: up to 300 m+.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Wet | Habitat | Hyperbaric |
|---|---|---|---|
| Quality (AWS) | B/C | A | A |
| Max Depth | 50 m | 50 m | 300 m+ |
| Cost | Low | High | Very High |
| Setup Time | Minutes | Days | Weeks |
| Process | SMAW/FCAW | SMAW/GMAW/GTAW | GMAW/GTAW |

**Mark split:**
- Three arrangements with sketches (4 marks)
- Comparison table (3 marks)

---

#### (b) Explain the role of carbon equivalent in underwater welding. A ship-hull steel has composition C = 0.22%, Mn = 1.10%, Cr = 0.10%, Ni = 0.08%, Mo = 0.03%, Cu = 0.15%, V = 0.02%. Determine the carbon equivalent and comment on cracking susceptibility. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Role of Carbon Equivalent ($C_{eq}$):**
The IIW carbon equivalent predicts the **cold-cracking susceptibility** of steel welds. A higher $C_{eq}$ means greater tendency to form hard martensite and hydrogen cracking, especially in fast-cooling wet welds. The formula is:

$$C_{eq} = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr + Mo + V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni + Cu}{15}$$

**Step-by-step Calculation:**

Step 1 — Substitute all values:

$$C_{eq} = 0.22 + \dfrac{1.10}{6} + \dfrac{0.10 + 0.03 + 0.02}{5} + \dfrac{0.08 + 0.15}{15}$$

Step 2 — Compute each fraction:

$$C_{eq} = 0.22 + 0.1833 + \dfrac{0.15}{5} + \dfrac{0.23}{15}$$

Step 3 — Continue simplifying:

$$C_{eq} = 0.22 + 0.1833 + 0.0300 + 0.0153$$

Step 4 — Final sum:

$$C_{eq} = 0.4486 \approx 0.45$$

**Comment:** Since $C_{eq} > 0.40$, the steel is **highly susceptible to hydrogen-induced cracking** in wet welding. Recommended action: use **austenitic (Ni-Fe) filler electrode**, or perform the weld in a **dry habitat** with controlled preheat (≥ 100 °C) and post-weld heat treatment.

**Mark split:**
- Role of $C_{eq}$ (2 marks)
- Formula + substitution (2 marks)
- Calculation + result (2 marks)
- Comment + recommendation (1 mark)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students LOSE MARKS in Module 3 underwater welding questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "wet" and "dry" welding definitions** — Dry does NOT mean room-temperature welding. Habitat welding is still pressurized (at sea pressure).
> 2. **Forgetting units** in hydrostatic pressure calculations — always include Pa **and** atm.
> 3. **Not mentioning "vapor cavity"** when describing wet welding — it is the *single most important concept* in the topic.
> 4. **Wrong polarity** — saying AC instead of DCEP for wet SMAW loses marks.
> 5. **Ignoring safety** — failing to mention GFCI, dead-man switch, equipotential bonding in any "precautions" question.
> 6. **Carbon Equivalent mistakes** — using wrong denominator (e.g., 4 instead of 5 for Cr) or forgetting a term.
> 7. **Sketch quality** — unlabeled diagrams of welding setups cost 1–2 marks; always label *electrode, arc, weld pool, water, base metal*.

---

### 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist — Underwater Welding (Module 3)**

- **Definition:** Welding performed in submerged environment; coalescence via electric arc in water.
- **Two main types:** **Wet** (direct in water) and **Dry** (habitat/hyperbaric).
- **Principle of wet welding:** Arc heat vaporizes water locally → forms transient **vapor cavity (steam envelope)** → arc sustained inside it → cavity collapses on arc stop.
- **Arc temperature:** **6,000–20,000 °C**.
- **Preferred polarity:** **DCEP (Electrode Positive)**.
- **Hydrostatic pressure rule:** +1 atm per 10 m depth.
- **Cooling rate in water ≈ 24× faster than in air** → martensite → cracking.
- **Carbon Equivalent formula (IIW):** $C_{eq} = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni+Cu}{15}$.
- **Critical $C_{eq}$ threshold:** > 0.40 → high cracking risk.
- **Heat Input formula:** $E = \dfrac{V \times I \times 60}{v \times 1000}$ kJ/mm.
- **Wet welding electrodes:** Waterproof rutile/cellulose coated or austenitic (Ni-Fe).
- **AWS D3.6M quality classes:** A (best), B (moderate), C (lowest — typical for wet welds).
- **Key safety devices:** GFCI, Dead-Man Switch, equipotential bonding, surface communication.
- **Spatter & slag:** Both higher in wet welding due to violent water quenching.
- **Hyperbaric welding:** Up to 300 m depth, produces Class A welds, used for pipelines.
- **Habitat welding:** Shirt-sleeve environment at 1 atm relative, used up to ~50 m depth.
- **Practical depth limit for wet welding (diver comfort):** ~50 m.
- **Remedies for brittle welds:** Use austenitic filler (Ni-Fe); preheat (only in habitat); minimize hydrogen pickup via dry electrodes.
- **Famous applications:** Naval combat damage repair, offshore platform legs, subsea pipeline tie-ins, ship propeller restoration.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dry Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST831 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dry Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme (Course Code: OENST831), **Dry Welding** is defined as a class of arc welding processes performed under strictly controlled, moisture-free environmental conditions for the fabrication, repair, and assembly of marine structures and ship hulls. Unlike wet welding, dry welding eliminates direct contact between the molten weld pool and the surrounding water, ambient moisture, or humid marine atmosphere.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (OENST831 - Module 3):**
> *Dry Welding is classified into three operational regimes based on the environment in which the arc and weld pool are shielded:*
> 1. **In-Air Dry Welding** (Open Workshop / Dry Dock / Slipway)
> 2. **Habitat Dry Welding** (Pressurized Cofferdam / Hyperbaric Chamber)
> 3. **Dry Chamber Localized Welding** (Small volume enclosures)

The weld metal deposition under dry conditions yields superior mechanical properties: **Tensile strength ≥ 480 MPa**, **Charpy V-notch impact toughness ≥ 27 J at -20°C**, and a hydrogen content typically below **5 mL/100g of deposited metal** when low-hydrogen electrodes (e.g., AWS E7018) are used.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:**
> - **Dry Welding**: Arc and weld pool operate in an inert/dry gaseous environment or open shop air with strict moisture control.
> - **Wet Welding**: Arc burns directly through a water layer; weld pool solidifies underwater (covered later in the module).
> - **Hyperbaric Welding**: Submerged welding at ambient pressure (a category distinct from dry habitat welding).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to **light a campfire**:
- **Wet Welding** is like trying to light wood during a rainstorm. The water cools the flame instantly, smothers the heat, and leaves the wood (your joint) porous, brittle, and full of steam pockets (porosity defects).
- **Dry Welding** is like lighting the campfire inside a sheltered wooden cabin. You control the wind, humidity, and temperature, allowing the flame (weld arc) to melt the wood (base metal) cleanly, producing a strong, consolidated ember (weld bead).

In shipbuilding, the "cabin" can be:
- A **huge fabrication shed** for building blocks (panels, double bottoms, side shells) — *Stage 1 construction*.
- A **graving dry dock** where the entire ship is exposed for bottom plating work.
- A **small steel habitat welded around a defect** on the ship's underwater hull, then drained and pressurized with dry gas — this is **Habitat Dry Welding**, used for emergency repair of in-service vessels without dry-docking.

## 1.3 Classification of Dry Welding Environments

| Type | Environment | Typical Use in Shipbuilding |
|---|---|---|
| **Shop / Berth Welding** | Indoor covered shed | Block fabrication (panels, sub-assemblies) |
| **Dry Dock Welding** | Open air, gravity-drained dock | Hull erection, shaft alignment, propeller work |
| **Slipway Welding** | Open air, inclined berth | Launch-stage hull closure, superstructure |
| **Habitat Dry Welding** | Pressurized steel enclosure | Underwater hull repair without dry-docking |
| **Pier / Alongside Welding** | Sheltered jetty | Repair of moored vessels |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The term **"Dry"** in Module 3 strictly refers to the **weld zone micro-environment** (the volume within ~1 m of the arc), not necessarily the entire shipyard being indoors.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Dry Welding? The Underlying Metallurgical Rationale

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the **Why** behind dry welding through four metallurgical drivers:

1. **Hydrogen Control (HIC & Cold Cracking Prevention)**
   - Hydrogen introduced from dissociated water (H₂O → H + OH) is the #1 cause of **Hydrogen-Induced Cracking (HIC)** and **Cold Cracking** in marine steels.
   - Dry welding limits diffusible hydrogen to **HD ≤ 5 mL/100g** (per AWS A4.3 or ISO 3690).

2. **Porosity Elimination**
   - Water vapor trapped in the weld pool forms **gas pockets (porosity)** as the metal solidifies.
   - Dry environment eliminates this gas source, producing radiographically clean welds.

3. **Mechanical Property Retention**
   - Wet welds exhibit a **hard, brittle martensitic microstructure** due to rapid cooling.
   - Dry welds allow controlled cooling, producing **acicular ferrite + bainite** — a tough, ductile matrix.

4. **Weld Bead Geometry**
   - Underwater convection currents distort the weld pool, producing irregular beads.
   - Dry welding yields smooth, properly contoured beads with correct **leg length** and **throat thickness**.

## 2.2 Operational Sequence — Habitat Dry Welding (Most Likely KTU Exam Topic)

1. **Survey & Cleaning**: Divers (or ROVs) inspect the hull defect; marine growth and rust are removed by **hydraulic grinders** to **Sa 2.5** (ISO 8501-1).
2. **Habitat Placement**: A **steel or composite cofferdam** is lowered over the defect and sealed against the hull with inflatable gaskets or magnetic seals.
3. **De-Watering**: Water inside the habitat is pumped out. Residual moisture is removed using **dehumidifiers** (target: dew point ≤ -20°C, relative humidity ≤ 30%).
4. **Gas Purging (Optional)**: The chamber is filled with **dry breathing air or argon-helium mix** to displace humid air, especially in deeper habitats.
5. **Welding**: A welder enters the habitat (or operates via ROV manipulator). The arc burns in a clean, dry atmosphere.
6. **NDT & Coating**: After cooling, the weld is inspected (UT, MPI, or visual) and coated with marine-grade anti-corrosive paint.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All units follow SI / shipbuilding convention. Use the table as a last-minute revision sheet before the ESE (End Semester Examination).

| # | Parameter / Formula | Equation | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Heat Input** | $H = \dfrac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000}$ | kJ/mm | Energy deposited per unit weld length |
| 2 | **Travel Speed (rearranged)** | $S = \dfrac{V \times I \times 60}{H \times 1000}$ | mm/min | Setting SMAW parameters |
| 3 | **Weld Metal Hydrogen** | $HD = \dfrac{V_{H_2}}{m_{weld}}$ | mL/100g | Quality control check (ISO 3690) |
| 4 | **Cooling Time (t₈/₅)** | $t_{8/5} = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi \lambda c \rho} \left[ \dfrac{1}{500-T_0} - \dfrac{1}{800-T_0} \right] | s | Critical for HAZ toughness |
| 5 | **Preheat Temperature** | $T_p = f(CE_{IIW}, t, HD)$ | °C | From tables (BS 5135 / AWS D1.1) |
| 6 | **Carbon Equivalent (IIW)** | $CE_{IIW} = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni+Cu}{15}$ | % | Weldability index |
| 7 | **Leg Length (Fillet Weld)** | $\ell = 0.707 \times t$ | mm | Equal-leg fillet for plate of thickness *t* |
| 8 | **Throat Thickness** | $a = 0.707 \times z$ | mm | Where *z* = leg size |

> [!WARNING]
