<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
**List any four mechanisms of adhesion and state which one is the dominant contributor in epoxy-bonded aluminium marine joints.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The four mechanisms of adhesion are:
> 1. **Mechanical Interlocking** — adhesive flows into substrate surface roughness and hardens.
> 2. **Adsorption** — secondary van der Waals and hydrogen bonds form on intimate molecular contact.
> 3. **Chemical Bonding** — primary covalent/ionic bonds form across the interface through reactive functional groups.
> 4. **Diffusion** — polymer chains interdiffuse across the interface (polymer-to-polymer only).
>
> **Dominant mechanism in epoxy-bonded aluminium:** **Chemical bonding** — the epoxy's oxirane ring opens and reacts covalently with the hydroxyl (-OH) groups on the Boehmite (AlOOH) layer produced by the FPL etch on aluminium. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State the ISO 8501-1 surface preparation standard required for adhesive bonding of marine steel and explain why surface preparation is called the "first 80 % of bond strength."**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **ISO 8501-1 Sa 2.5 ("near-white metal")** is the required standard — defined as **≥ 95 % of the surface free from visible mill scale, rust, paint, and contaminants**, with only slight shadows or streaks permitted.
>
> **Why 80 % of bond strength:** A contaminated surface (oils, salt, mill scale, oxide films) prevents the adhesive from achieving **molecular contact** with the substrate. A **1 µm oil film** can reduce bond strength by **40–70 %**, while grit-blasting to Sa 2.5 increases both the **wetted area** (roughness) and the **surface energy** of the substrate, activating all four adhesion mechanisms. Class societies (DNV, Lloyd's) require Sa 2.5 as the **minimum substrate condition for primary structural bonding**. **(2 marks)**

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Descriptive Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Set A]**

> **(a)** [7 marks] Explain the four mechanisms of adhesion in detail. Describe the role of **surface preparation** in achieving a high-strength marine adhesive bond, listing the standard preparation steps for **mild-steel hull plates** and **aluminium superstructure plates**.
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] With a neat sketch, derive the **average shear-stress equation** for a single-lap adhesive joint. State its limitations and explain how **Volkersen's shear-lag theory** improves upon it. A single-lap joint of bond length $L = 60 \text{ mm}$, width $b = 30 \text{ mm}$, carries an axial load of $F = 6 \text{ kN}$. Compute the **average shear stress** and the **peak-to-average stress ratio** (use $\omega L / 2 = 9$ for this joint).

---

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The four mechanisms are:
- **Mechanical interlocking** — physical keying of hardened adhesive into substrate micro-roughness; enhanced by **grit-blasting** (steel) or **FPL etching** (aluminium).
- **Adsorption** — secondary bonding; depends on **wetting** governed by Young-Dupré: $W_a = \gamma_{LV}(1+\cos\theta)$; contact angle $\theta \to 0$ for complete wetting.
- **Chemical bonding** — primary covalent/ionic bonds; requires reactive groups (epoxy + -OH on AlOOH for aluminium).
- **Diffusion** — polymer chain interdiffusion; applies to **FRP-FRP** bonds (e.g., vinylester-to-vinylester).

**Surface preparation for mild steel (3 marks for steel steps):**
1. **Degrease** with solvent/alkaline cleaner (ISO 8502).
2. **Grit-blast** to **Sa 2.5**; roughness **Ra ≈ 50–100 µm**.
3. **Apply zinc-rich epoxy primer** within **4 hours** to prevent re-oxidation.
4. Apply structural adhesive within primer's open time.

**Surface preparation for aluminium (3 marks for aluminium steps):**
1. Degrease with acetone or MEK.
2. Alkaline clean (sodium hydroxide, 60 °C, 10 min).
3. **FPL etch**: sodium dichromate + sulphuric acid, 65 °C, 30 min → produces Boehmite (AlOOH) layer.
4. Rinse, dry, and bond within **8 hours**.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Derivation of average shear stress** (already shown in Section 3.1, write the four steps concisely):

Step 1: FBD — adhesive transmits load $F$ in shear.

Step 2: $\tau = F / A_{bond}$.

Step 3: $A_{bond} = b \cdot L$.

Step 4: $\boxed{\tau_{avg} = F / (b L)}$.

**Limitations (1 mark):** Assumes uniform shear stress along the bond length. In reality, shear stress **peaks at the overlap ends** due to adherend elastic stretching, leading to **premature failure at the ends** if not properly accounted for.

**Volkersen improvement (1.5 marks):** Volkersen introduced the shear-lag parameter $\omega = \sqrt{G_a b / (E t t_a)}$ and derived the true stress distribution $\tau(x) = (F \omega / 2b) \cdot \cosh(\omega x) / \sinh(\omega L/2)$, which correctly predicts **end peaks** and the asymptotic **long-joint limit**.

**Numerical computation (2.5 marks):**

$$\tau_{avg} = \frac{F}{b \cdot L} = \frac{6000}{30 \times 60} = \frac{6000}{1800} = 3.33 \text{ MPa}$$

At $\omega L / 2 = 9$, $\tanh(9) \approx 0.9999 \approx 1.0$, so:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{F \omega}{2 b \tanh(\omega L/2)} \approx \frac{F \omega}{2 b} = \frac{6000 \times \omega}{2 \times 30} = 100 \omega \text{ MPa}$$

**Peak-to-average ratio:**

$$\frac{\tau_{max}}{\tau_{avg}} = \frac{F \omega / (2b)}{F / (bL)} = \frac{\omega L}{2} = 9$$

The peak stress is **9 times the average** — a dramatic non-uniformity that explains why single-lap joints fail at the ends. The remedy: **lengthen the overlap** until $\omega L/2 \geq 20$.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Set B, Module Choice]**

> **(a)** [7 marks] Classify **marine structural adhesives** with at least four families. Compare **epoxy and polyurethane** adhesives in terms of chemical structure, mechanical behaviour, water resistance, cure conditions, and typical marine applications.
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] A **double-strap (symmetric lap) joint** is proposed for a **6 mm-thick steel bulkhead-to-shell connection**. The joint width is $b = 100 \text{ mm}$, total bond length on each side $L = 50 \text{ mm}$, and the adhesive layer is $t_a = 0.25 \text{ mm}$ thick with shear modulus $G_a = 0.8 \text{ GPa}$. The steel adherend has $E = 200 \text{ GPa}$. A tensile load of $F = 40 \text{ kN}$ is transmitted.
>
> Compute:
> (i) the **Volkersen shear-lag parameter** $\omega$,
> (ii) the **maximum shear stress** in the adhesive,
> (iii) the **factor of safety** if the ultimate shear strength is $\tau_{ult} = 18 \text{ MPa}$,
> (iv) comment on whether a **single-lap** of the same total length $2L = 100 \text{ mm}$ would be acceptable, using the peak-to-average ratio.

---

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Classification table (3 marks):**

| Family | Chemistry | Curing | Marine Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Epoxy** | Diglycidyl ether of bisphenol-A + amine hardener | RTV or heat | Hull plate splicing |
| **Polyurethane** | Isocyanate + polyol | RTV (moisture) | Flexible sealants, deck joints |
| **Phenolic / Resorcinol** | Phenol-formaldehyde | Heat + pressure | Marine plywood, wood hulls |
| **Acrylic / Methacrylate** | Methyl methacrylate + peroxide initiator | RTV (10–60 min) | Field repairs, FRP bonding |
| **Silicone** | Polydimethylsiloxane | RTV (moisture) | Gasket sealing, non-structural |
| **Anaerobic** | Dimethacrylate ester | Metal contact, no air | Thread-locking fasteners |

**Epoxy vs Polyurethane comparison (4 marks — one short paragraph per criterion):**

- **Chemical structure:** Epoxy has a **3-membered oxirane (epoxide) ring** that opens to crosslink with amines, forming a **rigid 3D network**. Polyurethane forms **-NH-COO- urethane linkages** between isocyanate and polyol, producing a **flexible segmented polymer** (hard + soft blocks).
- **Mechanical behaviour:** Epoxy is **rigid and brittle** ($E \approx 3 \text{ GPa}$, elongation at break 2–5 %); polyurethane is **flexible and tough** ($E \approx 0.05 \text{ GPa}$, elongation 100–600 %).
- **Water resistance:** Epoxy is **excellent** — minimal water absorption (<1 %), stable bondline in seawater. Polyurethane is **moderate** — ester linkages are **hydrolysable** in hot water; not preferred for permanently immersed joints.
- **Cure conditions:** Epoxy cures by **stoichiometric mixing** of resin + hardener (no atmospheric dependence). Polyurethane cures by **moisture reaction** with atmospheric water — cure rate depends on **humidity**.
- **Applications:** Epoxy → **structural hull splicing, FRP-to-steel transitions, sandwich-core bonding**. Polyurethane → **flexible sealants, vibration mounts, deck caulking, plumbing joints**.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** $b = 100 \text{ mm}$, $L = 50 \text{ mm}$, $t_a = 0.25 \text{ mm}$, $G_a = 0.8 \text{ GPa} = 800 \text{ MPa}$, $E = 200 \text{ GPa} = 200000 \text{ MPa}$, adherend $t = 6 \text{ mm}$, $F = 40 \text{ kN} = 40000 \text{ N}$.

**Step (i) — Volkersen parameter (1.5 marks):**

$$\omega = \sqrt{\frac{2 G_a b}{E t t_a}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 800 \times 100}{200000 \times 6 \times 0.25}}$$

Calculate numerator and denominator:

$$\omega = \sqrt{\frac{160000}{300000}} = \sqrt{0.5333} = 0.7303 \text{ mm}^{-1}$$

**Step (ii) — Maximum shear stress (2 marks):**

The dimensionless parameter:

$$\frac{\omega L}{2} = \frac{0.7303 \times 50}{2} = 18.26$$

Since $\omega L / 2 = 18.26 \gg 1$, $\tanh(18.26) \approx 1.0000$. Therefore:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{F \omega}{2 b \tanh(\omega L/2)} \approx \frac{40000 \times 0.7303}{2 \times 100 \times 1.0} = \frac{29212}{200} = 146.1 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step (iii) — Factor of safety (1 mark):**

$$n = \frac{\tau_{ult}}{\tau_{max}} = \frac{18}{146.1} = 0.123$$

> [!WARNING]
> **The double-strap joint FAILS.** The factor of safety is **0.123**, far below 1.0. The joint must be **lengthened to at least $L = 200 \text{ mm}$ on each side** to enter the long-joint plateau where $\tau_{max}$ falls to a safe ~10 MPa.

**Step (iv) — Single-lap comparison (2.5 marks):**

For a single-lap joint of the **same total length** $L_{total} = 100 \text{ mm}$, with $L = 100 \text{ mm}$ and $b = 100 \text{ mm}$:

$$\tau_{avg} = \frac{F}{b L} = \frac{40000}{100 \times 100} = 4.0 \text{ MPa}$$

Compute the Volkersen parameter for the single lap (using the same $G_a, E, t, t_a$):

$$\omega_{SL} = \sqrt{\frac{G_a b}{E t t_a}} = \sqrt{\frac{800 \times 100}{200000 \times 6 \times 0.25}} = \sqrt{\frac{80000}{300000}} = \sqrt{0.2667} = 0.5164 \text{ mm}^{-1}$$

Compute the peak-to-average ratio:

$$\frac{\omega_{SL} L}{2} = \frac{0.5164 \times 100}{2} = 25.8$$

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{F \omega_{SL}}{2 b \tanh(\omega_{SL} L/2)} \approx \frac{40000 \times 0.5164}{2 \times 100 \times 1.0} = \frac{20656}{200} = 103.3 \text{ MPa}$$

**Peak-to-average ratio** for the single-lap: $\tau_{max}/\tau_{avg} = 103.3/4.0 = 25.8$, which is **2× worse** than the double-strap (ratio 9). The single lap also introduces a **peel stress** absent in the double strap. Therefore: **The single lap is NOT acceptable** — the double-strap is the correct geometry despite both joints failing numerically; the double-strap's symmetry halves the load per bond line and eliminates peel.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **The five classic mistakes that cost students 2–3 marks per question on adhesive bonding topics:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units.** The most common error: mixing GPa and N/mm². Remember: **1 GPa = 1000 MPa = 1000 N/mm²**.
> 2. **Confusing adhesive and cohesive failure.** A question asking "why did the joint fail?" expects you to distinguish between **interfacial (adhesive) failure** and **internal (cohesive) failure** — a 1-mark discriminator.
> 3. **Omitting the surface preparation step** in process questions. A "list the steps to bond two steel plates" answer that skips grit-blasting loses 2 marks immediately.
> 4. **Using the average-stress formula for short joints.** If $\omega L / 2 < 5$, you MUST use the Volkersen formula; the average formula under-predicts peak stress by **up to 9×**.
> 5. **Neglecting the factor of safety.** KTU 2024 follows the DNV convention: **FoS = 4 on ultimate shear strength**. A "safe" joint calculation that doesn't end with $\tau_{max} \leq \tau_{ult}/4$ is incomplete.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 3: Adhesives and Bonding Methods**

- ✅ **Adhesive bonding** is a non-mechanical joining process transferring load through an interfacial polymer layer — complementary to welding and riveting in shipbuilding.
- ✅ **Four adhesion mechanisms:** mechanical interlocking, adsorption, chemical bonding, diffusion — **chemical bonding dominates** in epoxy-aluminium marine joints.
- ✅ **Epoxy** is the marine workhorse (high strength, water-resistant); **polyurethane** is the flexible alternative (sealants, vibration mounts).
- ✅ **Surface preparation = 80 % of bond strength.** Steel: Sa 2.5 grit-blast. Aluminium: FPL etch producing Boehmite (AlOOH) layer.
- ✅ **ISO 8501-1 Sa 2.5** is the universal marine steel preparation standard.
- ✅ **Single-lap joints** develop **shear + peel**; **double-strap** = pure balanced shear; **scarf (1:10 to 1:20 taper)** = shear-dominated, lowest peel.
- ✅ **Average shear stress:** $\tau = F / (bL)$ — only valid for **long joints** where $\omega L / 2 \gg 1$.
- ✅ **Volkersen shear-lag parameter:** $\omega = \sqrt{G_a b / (E t t_a)}$ — quantifies the joint's "long-vs-short" regime.
- ✅ **Peak-to-average stress ratio** in single-lap joints can reach **25×** for very short, stiff overlaps; the **safe design target** is $\omega L / 2 \geq 20$.
- ✅ **Optimum adhesive thickness:** $t_a = 0.15$ to $0.30 \text{ mm}$ — balances shear transfer (thicker = better) against peel-stress reduction.
- ✅ **Factor of Safety:** **FoS = 4 on ultimate shear** (DNV / Lloyd's Register / KTU 2024 convention).
- ✅ **Three failure modes:** **adhesive** (interfacial), **cohesive** (within adhesive), **substrate** (within adherend — ideal outcome).
- ✅ **Peel stress** in single-lap joints scales as $1/t_a^{2}$ — thicker bondlines **dramatically reduce** peel and are preferred for peel-critical applications.
- ✅ **Young-Dupré equation** $W_a = \gamma_{LV}(1+\cos\theta)$ governs wettability — low contact angle = strong adsorption.
- ✅ **Underwater-curing epoxies** (amine-cured) are used for emergency hull patch repairs by divers without dry-docking.
- ✅ **Sandwich-core superstructures** (Nomex honeycomb + CFRP face sheets) in stealth corvettes rely entirely on adhesive film bonding — no fasteners permitted in the radar-transparent zone.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Weld Defects and Measures for Their Control: Distortion Control and Destructive/Non-Destructive Tests
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST831 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Weld Defects and Measures for Their Control: Distortion Control and Destructive/Non-Destructive Tests

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Weld Defects — Formal Definition

A **weld defect** is any irregularity or imperfection in a weld that fails to meet the specified acceptance criteria defined by classification societies (e.g., **ABS**, **DNV**, **LR**, **IRS**) or international standards (e.g., **ISO 6520-1**, **AWS A3.0**). In shipbuilding, where welds form the structural backbone of hulls, decks, bulkheads, and pressure vessels, defects directly compromise the **fatigue life**, **corrosion resistance**, and **load-bearing capacity** of the entire marine structure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The classification of defects follows the **ISO 6520-1** numerical coding system, where every defect is assigned a unique reference number (e.g., 100-series for cracks, 200-series for cavities, 400-series for lack of fusion, etc.).

## 1.2 Distortion — Formal Definition

**Weld distortion** is the **unwanted change in shape or dimensions** of a welded component caused by the non-uniform heating and cooling cycles inherent to fusion welding processes. In shipbuilding, distortion manifests as hull plate warping, angular twisting of stiffeners, and longitudinal shrinkage of long welded seams — all of which disrupt **fair line alignment** and complicate downstream outfitting.

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification of Weld Defects (ISO 6520-1 Reference):**
> - **Group 1 (100s):** Cracks (longitudinal, transverse, crater, etc.)
> - **Group 2 (200s):** Cavities (porosity, blowholes, wormholes, shrinkage cavities)
> - **Group 3 (300s):** Solid inclusions (slag, tungsten, oxide, metallic)
> - **Group 4 (400s):** Lack of fusion and incomplete penetration
> - **Group 5 (500s):** Imperfect shape (undercut, overlap, excessive convexity/reinforcement)
> - **Group 6 (600s):** Miscellaneous defects (spatter, arc strikes, surface discontinuities)

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — Welding Is Like Baking a Cake

Imagine you are baking a layered cake. You pour hot batter into a pan and then put it in a cold oven. The center cooks and shrinks **unevenly** compared to the edges, and the top crust forms before the inside sets. The result? The cake **warps, cracks on top, and shrinks in diameter**. 

Welding behaves identically:

| Baking Analogy | Welding Equivalent |
|----------------|--------------------|
| Hot batter poured in pan | Molten weld pool deposited between plates |
| Uneven cooling (edges first) | Steep thermal gradient across heat-affected zone (HAZ) |
| Cake shrinks in diameter | Transverse and longitudinal shrinkage of weld |
| Cake top cracks | Solidification cracking in weld bead |
| Air bubbles trapped inside | Porosity and blowholes in weld metal |
| Dry, crumbly interior | Lack of fusion / incomplete penetration |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Big Picture:** Welding defects arise from **thermal cycle mismanagement**, **operator skill**, **process parameters**, and **material incompatibility**. Quality assurance in shipbuilding requires both **prevention** (distortion control + correct procedure) and **verification** (DT + NDT).

## 1.4 The Quality Assurance Triad in Shipbuilding Welding

For every critical ship hull weld, three control pillars operate simultaneously:

1. **Prevention** → Distortion control techniques (jigging, backstep sequence, peening, pre-setting)
2. **Destructive Testing (DT)** → Mechanical & metallurgical verification on coupon samples
3. **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)** → In-service inspection of actual welds using VT, RT, UT, MPI, DPI

The acceptance thresholds are governed by bodies such as **IACS (International Association of Classification Societies)** and standards like **AWS D1.1**, **ISO 5817**, and **EN 10160**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Weld Defects — Detailed Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Cracks (Group 1 — ISO 6520-1)

Cracks are the **most severe** weld defect because they act as **stress concentrators** and can propagate under cyclic marine loading (wave-induced fatigue).

| Crack Type | Code | Cause | Shipbuilding Risk |
|-----------|------|-------|-------------------|
| Longitudinal crack | 101 | High longitudinal restraint, high heat input | Hull plate seams |
| Transverse crack | 102 | High transverse restraint, hydrogen embrittlement | Butt welds in stiffeners |
| Crater crack | 104 | Arc termination without crater fill | Manual welder stops |
| Underbead crack | 106 | Hydrogen + martensite in HAZ | Thick T-joints on deck plating |
| HAZ crack | 107 | Hardened microstructure in HAZ | High-carbon steel weldments |
| Lamellar tearing | 105 | Through-thickness strain in rolled plates | Tee-butt connections in hull nodes |

### 2.1.2 Porosity and Cavities (Group 2)

- **Porosity (201):** Uniformly distributed gas pockets from contaminated base metal, moisture, or inadequate shielding gas coverage.
- **Blowholes (202):** Larger surface-breaking gas pockets, often elongated.
- **Wormhole porosity (205):** Elongated tunnel-like cavities, particularly harmful in pressure-retaining hull compartments.
- **Shrinkage cavity (209):** Interdendritic voids formed during solidification.

### 2.1.3 Solid Inclusions (Group 3)

- **Slag inclusions (301):** Entrapped flux from previous passes in multi-pass welds.
- **Tungsten inclusions (303):** From GTAW process with contaminated electrode or poor technique.
- **Oxide inclusions (304):** Result of inadequate gas shielding.

### 2.1.4 Lack of Fusion and Penetration (Group 4)

- **Lack of fusion (401):** Faying surfaces not melted together — caused by low heat input, dirty surfaces, incorrect torch angle.
- **Incomplete penetration (402):** Weld metal does not extend through the joint root — catastrophic in full-strength butt welds in hull plating.

### 2.1.5 Imperfect Shape (Group 5)

- **Undercut (501):** Groove melted into base metal at weld toe, reducing cross-section.
- **Overlap (506):** Weld metal rolls over base metal without fusion.
- **Excess weld metal (502/503):** Over-reinforcement causing stress concentration at the toe.

## 2.2 Weld Distortion — Types and Root Causes

### 2.2.1 Fundamental Mechanism

When an arc deposits a weld bead, the local temperature can exceed **1500°C** while the surrounding parent metal remains near ambient. The molten zone tries to expand but is restrained by the surrounding cold metal → **compressive plastic strain** forms. On cooling, the weld metal wants to contract but is now restrained by the surrounding structure → **tensile residual stress** + **net shrinkage** = distortion.

The governing thermal-metallurgical relationship is:

$$ \varepsilon_{thermal} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T $$

where:
- $\varepsilon_{thermal}$ = free thermal strain
- $\alpha$ = coefficient of thermal expansion (≈ **12 × 10⁻⁶ /°C** for mild steel)
- $\Delta T$ = temperature difference

### 2.2.2 Types of Distortion

| Type | Schematic | Cause | Shipbuilding Impact |
|------|-----------|-------|---------------------|
| **Transverse shrinkage** | Weld contracts perpendicular to its axis | Volume reduction on solidification | Misalignment of butt seams in plate panels |
| **Longitudinal shrinkage** | Weld shortens along its axis | Same as above, axial direction | "Shortening" of long hull seams by several mm/m |
| **Angular distortion** | Plate edges rotate out-of-plane | Non-symmetric thermal gradient across V-groove | V-shaped opening of butt joints |
| **Bowing distortion** | Long thin plates curve longitudinally | Eccentric weld causing bending moment | Hull plate camber deviation |
| **Buckling distortion** | Thin panels ripple/wrinkle | Compressive restraint of thin plating | Critical for thin bulkhead panels (<6 mm) |
| **Rotational distortion** | Joint rotates about its length | Asymmetric weld in T-joint | Stiffener foot-of-fillet misalignment |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Rule:** For a single-pass butt weld in a thin plate, the **angular distortion** $\Delta\theta$ can be approximated by:

$$ \Delta\theta \approx \frac{\alpha \cdot \Delta T_{peak} \cdot t_{w}}{2 \cdot t_{p}^{2}} $$

where $t_w$ = weld throat thickness, $t_p$ = plate thickness. This is a high-yield KTU formula.

## 2.3 Distortion Control Measures — The Engineer's Toolkit

### 2.3.1 Design Stage Controls

- **Symmetric joint design:** Use **double-V** or **double-U** grooves instead of single-V to balance shrinkage.
- **Minimum weld size:** Specify only the throat size that meets strength needs (oversized welds → more heat input → more distortion).
- **Low-alloy filler selection:** Match filler CTE to base metal.

### 2.3.2 Process Stage Controls

- **Pre-set / Pre-bend (Counter-distortion):** Tilt the plates *toward* the expected distortion direction before welding so that after welding the structure straightens out.
- **Backstep welding sequence:** Weld short segments in the *opposite* direction of overall progression to distribute heat evenly.
- **Skip welding / Staggered sequence:** Alternate between separated segments to avoid heat buildup.
- **Reduced heat input:** Lower current, higher travel speed, use **narrow-gap GMAW** or **FCAW** for thick sections.
- **Symmetric multi-arc welding:** Use **twin-arc** or **tandem-arc** systems to deposit balanced heat from both sides simultaneously.
- **Clamping and strongbacks:** Rigid fixtures resist shrinkage forces during cooling.
- **Peening:** Mechanical hammering of each weld pass to induce counter-stress (controversial — may introduce cracks if overdone).

### 2.3.3 Post-Weld Correction Methods

- **Flame straightening (Line heating):** Controlled oxy-fuel heating of plate surface — common in **Japanese and Korean shipyards** for hull plate fairing. Uses **1:4:1 line heating** (water distance : plate width : shrinkage force).
- **Mechanical straightening:** Press or roller straightening of welded subassemblies.
- **Stress-relief annealing:** Reduces residual stress (less effective for distortion correction).

> [!NOTE]
> **Shipbuilding Specific Note:** **Line heating (火工矯正)** is a uniquely developed Japanese technique using the **Thermal Elasto-Plastic Transformation (TEPT)** principle. The heated zone contracts upon cooling, pulling the plate toward the heated line. Multiple parallel lines can induce complex 3D curvature changes.

## 2.4 Destructive Testing (DT) — Full Reference

Destructive testing is performed on **test coupons** welded using the **same WPS (Welding Procedure Specification)** as the production weld, but the coupon itself is sacrificed to extract mechanical property data.

| DT Method | Standard | What It Measures | Shipbuilding Use |
|-----------|----------|------------------|------------------|
| **Tensile test** | ISO 4136, AWS B4.0 | Ultimate tensile strength (UTS), yield strength (YS), % elongation | Verify weld metal matches base metal strength |
| **Bend test (Root/Face/Side)** | ISO 5173, ASTM E190 | Ductility, fusion integrity | Detect lack of fusion, slag, porosity in HAZ |
| **Charpy V-notch impact test** | ISO 148-1, ASTM E23 | Toughness at service temperature (e.g., 0°C, -20°C, -40°C) | Critical for **Arctic-class** and **LNG carrier** hulls |
| **Vickers / Brinell hardness test** | ISO 6507 / 6506 | Hardness in weld, HAZ, base metal | Detect brittle martensitic HAZ (max **380 HV** typically allowed for ship steels) |
| **Macro-examination** | ISO 13277 | Penetration profile, fusion zone, lamination | Visual cross-section verification |
| **Micro-examination** | ISO 17639 | Grain structure, inclusions, phase identification | Metallurgical research, failure analysis |
| **Chemical analysis** | ISO 14284 | Weld metal composition (C, Mn, S, P, alloying) | Verify WPS filler compliance |
| **Nick-break test** | (Historical) | Internal soundness of single-pass welds | Quick shop-floor defect screening |

## 2.5 Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) — Full Reference

NDT is performed on the **actual production weld** without damaging the component. It is the cornerstone of shipbuilding quality assurance during construction and in-service inspection.

| NDT Method | Code | Principle | Detects | Shipbuilding Application |
|-----------|------|-----------|---------|--------------------------|
| **Visual Testing (VT)** | VT | Direct eye / borescope / magnifying lens | Surface cracks, undercut, spatter, misalignment | **100%** of welds — first-line inspection |
| **Radiographic Testing (RT)** | RT | X-ray or γ-ray (Ir-192, Co-60) penetrates weld; film/digital detector | Internal porosity, slag, lack of fusion, root defects | Critical butt welds in hull, pressure vessels |
| **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)** | UT | High-freq sound waves (1–25 MHz) reflected from defects | Internal cracks, laminations, lack of fusion, root defects | **Class A & B** welds; thick plate >12 mm |
| **Magnetic Particle Testing (MPI/MPI)** | MT | Magnetic field + ferrous particles reveal surface/near-surface flaws | Surface & sub-surface cracks in **ferromagnetic** steels | Hull fillet welds, stiffener intersections |
| **Dye Penetrant Testing (DPI/LPI)** | PT | Capillary action draws penetrant into surface defects | Surface-breaking cracks, porosity in **non-magnetic** materials | Stainless steel exhaust systems, aluminum superstructures |
| **Eddy Current Testing (ET)** | ET | Induced eddy currents detect conductivity variations | Surface cracks, corrosion, material variations | Aluminum alloy marine structures, heat exchanger tubes |
| **Acoustic Emission (AE)** | AE | Active flaws emit stress waves when loaded | Growing cracks under load | Pressure vessel fitness-for-service |
| **Phased Array UT (PAUT)** | UT-PA | Multi-element transducers with electronic beam steering | Volumetric defect characterization, sizing | Modern replacement for radiography in thick welds |
| **Time-of-Flight Diffraction (TOFD)** | TOFD | Diffracted wave timing measures defect height | Precise through-thickness defect sizing | Critical for offshore platform welds |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point — Acceptance Standards:**
> - **ISO 5817:2014** — Quality levels B, C, D for fusion welds.
> - **Level B** = highest quality (e.g., pressure vessels, submarine hulls).
> - **Level D** = lowest acceptable quality (general ship structures).
> - **IACS UR W17** — Unified Requirements for weld joint classification in ship hulls.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\varepsilon_{th} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | Free thermal strain | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\Delta\theta \approx \dfrac{\alpha \cdot \Delta T_{peak} \cdot t_w}{2 \cdot t_p^{2}}$ | Angular distortion estimate | radians |
| 3 | $\sigma_{residual} = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot f(\text{restraint})$ | Residual stress estimate | MPa |
| 4 | $R_m = \dfrac{F_{max}}{A_0}$ | Ultimate tensile strength from DT | MPa |
| 5 | $KV = \text{absorbed energy at notch}$ | Charpy impact toughness | Joules (J) |
| 6 | $HRC \approx 0.8 \cdot HV + 10$ | Hardness conversion (approx) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $Heat\ Input\ (J/mm) = \dfrac{60 \cdot V \cdot I}{1000 \cdot S}$ | Heat input = $\eta \cdot V \cdot I / S$ | kJ/mm |
| 8 | $\dot{Q} = k \cdot A \cdot \dfrac{\Delta T}{\Delta x}$ | Heat conduction (Fourier's law) | W |

Where: $E$ = Young's modulus (~210 GPa for steel), $V$ = arc voltage, $I$ = current, $S$ = travel speed (mm/min), $\eta$ = arc efficiency (0.6 GMAW, 0.8 GTAW, 1.0 SAW).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Does a Butt Weld Distort Angularly?

### Step 1 — Establish the initial geometry
Consider two plates of thickness $t_p$ joined by a single-V groove butt weld. The V-groove has a **depth asymmetry** — the heated zone is concentrated on the top side, leaving the bottom side relatively cool.

### Step 2 — Apply the thermal expansion principle
On heating, the top fiber of the weld tries to expand by $\varepsilon_{th} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ but is restrained by the cooler bulk.

### Step 3 — Compute free thermal strain
For mild steel heated to peak temperature $T_{peak} = 1500^\circ C$ from ambient $T_0 = 20^\circ C$:

$$
\Delta T = T_{peak} - T_0 = 1500 - 20 = 1480^\circ C
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{th} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T = (12 \times 10^{-6}) \times 1480
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{th} = 1.776 \times 10^{-2} = 1.78\%
$$

This is **plastic** strain, far beyond elastic limits (~0.1%).

### Step 4 — Formulate angular distortion using curvature analogy
The plate bends into a circular arc of radius $R$ on cooling. The angular change is:

$$
\Delta\theta = \frac{L_{arc} - L_{chord}}{R} \approx \frac{L^2}{8R}
$$

where $L$ is the chord length. Relating the curvature to thermal strain across the plate thickness:

$$
\frac{1}{R} = \frac{\alpha \cdot \Delta T_{effective}}{2 \cdot t_p}
$$

Substituting (with $L = t_w$ for a unit-width assumption):

$$
\Delta\theta = \frac{\alpha \cdot \Delta T_{effective} \cdot t_w}{2 \cdot t_p^2}
$$

### Step 5 — Numerical example (KTU exam style)
Given: mild steel plate, $t_p = 12$ mm, weld throat $t_w = 6$ mm, $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6} /^\circ C$, $\Delta T_{effective} = 800^\circ C$ (effective mean, not peak).

$$
\Delta\theta = \frac{(12 \times 10^{-6}) \times 800 \times 6}{2 \times (12)^2}
$$

$$
\Delta\theta = \frac{0.0576}{288}
$$

$$
\Delta\theta = 2.0 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{rad} = 0.0115^\circ
$$

For a 1-meter plate length, this corresponds to a tip displacement:

$$
\delta = \frac{L^2}{2R} = \frac{1000^2 \times 1.776 \times 10^{-2}}{2 \times 24000} \approx 0.37\ \text{mm/m}
$$

This matches the typical rule of thumb for shipbuilding: **plan for ~0.3–0.5 mm/m shrinkage** in single-pass butt welds.

## 3.2 Derivation: Heat Input Effect on HAZ Hardness

The cooling time between 800°C and 500°C ($t_{8/5}$) governs HAZ microstructure and hardness. A simplified form is:

$$
t_{8/5} = \frac{(Q/V)^2}{4\pi \lambda \rho c_p} \cdot \left[\frac{1}{(T_1 - T_0)^2} - \frac{1}{(T_2 - T_0)^2}\right]
$$

For shipbuilding QA, the practical limit is that **t₈/₅ should be ≥ 8 s** to avoid martensite formation in C-Mn ship steel. Substitute $T_1 = 800°C$, $T_2 = 500°C$, $T_0 = 20°C$:

$$
t_{8/5} = \frac{(Q/V)^2}{4\pi \lambda \rho c_p} \cdot \left[\frac{1}{(800-20)^2} - \frac{1}{(500-20)^2}\right]
$$

$$
= \frac{(Q/V)^2}{4\pi \lambda \rho c_p} \cdot \left[\frac{1}{780^2} - \frac{1}{480^2}\right]
$$

$$
= \frac{(Q/V)^2}{4\pi \lambda \rho c_p} \cdot [1.64 \times 10^{-6} - 4.34 \times 10^{-6}]
$$

$$
= \frac{(Q/V)^2}{4\pi \lambda \rho c_p} \cdot (-2.70 \times 10^{-6})
$$

The **magnitude** is what matters:

$$
t_{8/5} \propto \frac{(Q/V)^2}{\text{thermal properties}}
$$

This shows that **doubling the heat input Q quadruples the cooling time**, dramatically altering HAZ hardness.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Distortion vs. Mitigation Effectiveness

| Distortion Type | Primary Mitigation | Mitigation Effectiveness | KTU Mark Weightage |
|----------------|--------------------|--------------------------|---------------------|
| Transverse shrinkage | Strongback clamping | **High (80–95%)** | 3-mark question favorite |
| Longitudinal shrinkage | Pre-setting plates | **High (70–90%)** | Frequently tested |
| Angular distortion | Double-sided weld / pre-bend | **Very High (90%+)** | 14-mark derivations |
| Bowing distortion | Symmetric multi-arc, line heating correction | **Moderate (60–80%)** | Concept question |
| Buckling (thin plate) | Stiffener spacing, weld sequence | **High (85%+)** | NDT-related question |
| Rotational distortion | Alternate-side welding sequence | **High (80–90%)** | Often paired with stiffener welding |

## 3.4 Decision Matrix — Selecting NDT Method for a Hull Weld

| Weld Type | Material | Thickness | Recommended NDT | Backup NDT |
|-----------|----------|-----------|------------------|------------|
| Hull plate butt | Mild steel | 8–15 mm | RT or PAUT | MPI for surface |
| Stiffener fillet | Mild steel | 4–6 mm | MPI + VT | UT for critical |
| Stem/stern cast steel | Cast steel | >30 mm | RT + UT | TOFD for sizing |
| Aluminum superstructure | 5083/5086 Al | 4–10 mm | DPI + UT | Eddy current |
| Stainless exhaust | 316L SS | 3–6 mm | DPI | Eddy current |
| Submarine pressure hull | HY-80/100 | 20–50 mm | RT + UT + PAUT | TOFD + AE monitoring |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**
*List and briefly explain any **three** types of weld distortions encountered in ship hull construction.*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Transverse Shrinkage** — The weld contracts perpendicular to its axis, pulling the two plates forming the joint toward each other. Caused by volumetric reduction of the molten weld pool during solidification.
2. **Angular Distortion** — The plates rotate out-of-plane (forming a V-shape) due to non-symmetric heat input across the joint thickness. Common in single-V butt welds of hull plating.
3. **Bowing Distortion** — A long thin plate curves longitudinally when an eccentric weld induces a bending moment. Frequently seen in wide hull panels welded only along one edge.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Differentiate between **Radiographic Testing (RT)** and **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)** for weld inspection.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | RT | UT |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| Principle | Electromagnetic radiation passes through weld | High-frequency sound waves reflect from defects |
| Detects best | Volumetric defects (porosity, slag) | Planar defects (cracks, lack of fusion) |
| Output | Permanent film/digital radiograph | A-scan, B-scan, C-scan displays |
| Safety | Radiation hazard, requires controlled zone | Safe, requires couplant gel |
| Best for | Thin-medium thickness welds, butt joints | Thick welds, all orientations |
| Cost | Higher (film/equipment/radiation safety) | Lower, faster |

The welding parameters are: Voltage = 24 V, Current = 220 A, Travel speed = 300 mm/min, Arc efficiency $\eta$ = 0.8, and the thermal expansion coefficient of the steel is $12 \times 10^{-6} /°C$. Calculate the **heat input (kJ/mm)** and estimate the **expected angular distortion** in degrees per meter of weld length. Assume an effective temperature difference of 700°C and effective weld throat equal to plate thickness.

   2. Dye Penetrant Inspection (DPI)
   3. Phased Array Ultrasonic Testing (PAUT)

**(b)** *(7 Marks)* A shipyard is constructing an offshore patrol vessel's hull. Discuss the **distortion control measures** that should be adopted:
   1. At the design stage
   2. During the welding process

Include the principle of **line heating (Thermal Elasto-Plastic Transformation)** used in Japanese/Korean shipyards for hull plate fairing.

## 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

### Solution to Question A(a)

**1. Lack of Fusion (ISO 6520-1 Code 401)**

- **Definition:** Failure of the weld metal to fuse completely with the parent metal or with a previous weld pass.
- **Causes:**
  - Insufficient heat input (low current / high travel speed)
  - Contaminated faying surface (rust, oil, paint, mill scale)
  - Incorrect electrode angle (electrode too steep or too flat)
  - Arc blow (deflection due to magnetic fields in DC welding)
- **Prevention:**
  - Clean surfaces to bright metal before welding
  - Maintain proper heat input per WPS
  - Use correct electrode manipulation (slight weaving, 70–80° drag angle)
- **Acceptance Criteria:** Not permitted at any location in **Class A welds** (per IACS UR W17). For Class B, isolated lack of fusion ≤ 10% of joint thickness may be acceptable per **ISO 5817 Level B**.

**2. Incomplete Penetration (ISO 6520-1 Code 402)**

- **Definition:** Weld metal does not extend through the entire joint thickness; root remains unfilled.
- **Causes:**
  - Root gap too small
  - Excessive root face (land) not removed
  - Low heat input
  - Incorrect root pass technique
- **Prevention:**
  - Proper root gap and root face control
  - Back-gouging from the reverse side for full-penetration joints
  - Use of backing strips (ceramic or copper) for single-sided welds
- **Acceptance Criteria:** **Zero tolerance** for incomplete penetration in full-strength butt welds of ship hulls.

**3. Undercut (ISO 6520-1 Code 501)**

- **Definition:** A groove melted into the base metal at the weld toe, not filled by weld metal.
- **Causes:**
  - Excessive welding current
  - Incorrect electrode angle
  - Fast travel speed
  - Excessive weaving
- **Prevention:**
  - Adjust current and travel speed per WPS
  - Use proper electrode angle (5–15° drag)
  - Reduce weave amplitude
- **Acceptance Criteria:** Depth ≤ 0.5 mm for **Level B**, ≤ 1.0 mm for **Level C** (per ISO 5817).

---

### Solution to Question A(b) — Numerical Problem

**Given:**
- Plate thickness $t_p$ = 12 mm
- Voltage $V$ = 24 V
- Current $I$ = 220 A
- Travel speed $S$ = 300 mm/min
- Arc efficiency $\eta$ = 0.8
- $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6} /^\circ C$
- $\Delta T_{effective}$ = 700°C
- Effective weld throat $t_w = t_p$ = 12 mm

**Step 1 — Heat Input Calculation**

$$
Heat\ Input = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \times 1000}
$$

$$
Heat\ Input = \frac{0.8 \times 24 \times 220 \times 60}{300 \times 1000}
$$

$$
Heat\ Input = \frac{253440}{300000} = 0.8448\ \text{kJ/mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Angular Distortion per Unit Length**

$$
\Delta\theta = \frac{\alpha \cdot \Delta T_{effective} \cdot t_w}{2 \cdot t_p^2}
$$

$$
\Delta\theta = \frac{(12 \times 10^{-6}) \times 700 \times 12}{2 \times (12)^2}
$$

$$
\Delta\theta = \frac{0.1008}{288} = 3.5 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{rad}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to degrees per meter**

$$
\Delta\theta_{deg/m} = \Delta\theta \times \frac{180}{\pi} \times 1000
$$

$$
\Delta\theta_{deg/m} = 3.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 57.2958 \times 1000
$$

$$
\Delta\theta_{deg/m} \approx 20.05^\circ/\text{m}
$$

This is unrealistically high — indicating that a 12 mm single-V butt weld on thin plate would be severely distorted. **This justifies why double-sided welding or pre-bending is mandatory for ship hulls.**

**Step 4 — Verification of the heat input**
A heat input of 0.84 kJ/mm is in the typical range for GMAW in shipbuilding (0.5–1.5 kJ/mm). However, for 12 mm plate, **pre-setting angle** of about 1–2° opposite to the expected distortion is recommended.

---

### Solution to Question B(a) — NDT Methods

**1. Magnetic Particle Inspection (MPI / MT)**

- **Principle:** When a ferromagnetic component is magnetized, flux lines emerge at the surface at defect locations. Fine ferrous particles (dry powder or wet suspension) applied to the surface are attracted to these flux leakages, forming visible indications of defects.
- **Procedure:**
  1. Pre-clean weld surface
  2. Magnetize using yoke, prod, or wrap technique
  3. Apply magnetic particles (dry or wet)
  4. Inspect under UV-A light (for fluorescent particles) or white light
  5. Demagnetize
- **Detects:** Surface and near-surface cracks, lack of fusion, porosity
- **Limitations:** Works only on ferromagnetic materials (carbon steel, low-alloy steel). Not for austenitic stainless steel or aluminum.
- **Shipbuilding Use:** Hull fillet welds, stiffener intersections, T-joints in ferromagnetic steel.

**2. Dye Penetrant Inspection (DPI / PT)**

- **Principle:** A low-surface-tension penetrant liquid is drawn into surface-breaking defects by capillary action. Excess is removed, a developer is applied which reverses the capillary action and draws out the penetrant, forming a visible indication.
- **Procedure:**
  1. Pre-clean and dry
  2. Apply penetrant (5–30 min dwell)
  3. Remove excess penetrant
  4. Apply developer
  5. Inspect under white or UV-A light
- **Detects:** Surface-breaking cracks, porosity, laps, cold shuts
- **Limitations:** Surface-breaking defects only; not for sub-surface
- **Shipbuilding Use:** Stainless steel exhaust systems, aluminum superstructure welds, non-magnetic castings.

**3. Phased Array Ultrasonic Testing (PAUT)**

- **Principle:** Uses a multi-element transducer with electronically controlled time delays (phasing) to steer, focus, and scan ultrasonic beams through the weld. The phased beam can be electronically swept through different angles, generating B-scan, C-scan, or S-scan images.
- **Procedure:**
  1. Apply couplant
  2. Calibrate using reference block with known reflectors
  3. Scan with phased array probe
  4. Real-time imaging on display
- **Detects:** Volumetric and planar defects with accurate sizing and position
- **Advantages over conventional UT:** No manual scanning; faster; full coverage; permanent records; replaces radiography in many applications.
- **Shipbuilding Use:** Class A butt welds, thick plate hulls (>20 mm), offshore structures, critical pressure boundaries.

---

### Solution to Question B(b) — Distortion Control Measures

**1. Design Stage**
- Use **symmetric joint designs** (double-V, double-U, double-J) for butt welds in thick plate.
- Specify the **minimum weld size** that meets strength requirements (oversized welds cause excess heat input).
- Position welds near the **neutral axis** of the section to minimize bending moments.
- Use **balanced weld groups** around the centroid of stiffener connections.
- Select filler metals with **matching CTE** to the base metal.

**2. Welding Process Stage**
- **Pre-setting (counter-distortion):** Tilt the plates or set the joint opening wider so that after welding-induced shrinkage, the component reaches the desired final geometry.
- **Backstep / Skip / Cascade sequences:** Distribute heat uniformly along the joint.
- **Heat input control:** Use low-heat-input processes like **FCAW** or **narrow-gap GMAW** for thick plate.
- **Symmetric multi-arc / Tandem-arc welding:** For double-sided welds, deposit from both sides simultaneously to balance the heat input.
- **Clamping and strongbacks:** Mechanical restraint against shrinkage.
- **Peening:** Light hammering of each pass (controversial; not allowed on final passes or in high-toughness applications).

**3. Post-Weld Stage (Post-Correction)**
- **Flame Straightening / Line Heating (火工矯正):**
  - **Principle (TEPT — Thermal Elasto-Plastic Transformation):** Localized oxy-fuel heating causes plastic expansion on the heated side. On cooling, the heated zone contracts, pulling the surrounding material toward the line. By controlling line spacing, heating intensity, and water-cooling, complex 3D curvature can be induced.
  - **The 1:4:1 Rule:** Line distance : Plate width : Effective shrinkage ratio for typical shipbuilding applications.
  - **Common in Japanese, Korean, and Chinese shipyards** for hull plate fairing.
- **Mechanical Straightening:** Press or roller straightening for local corrections.
- **Stress-Relief Heat Treatment (SR/PWHT):** Primarily reduces residual stress, not distortion geometry.
- **Controlled heating + cooling cycles** for shape correction of large panels.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit errors in heat input:** Many students forget to convert travel speed from mm/min to mm/s, leading to a factor of 60 error. Always show units.
> 2. **Mixing up distortion types:** Bowing vs. buckling is often confused. Bowing = longitudinal curvature of a long member; Buckling = wave-like rippling of a thin plate under compression.
> 3. **Confusing RT and UT principles:** RT uses **electromagnetic radiation**; UT uses **mechanical sound waves**. Many students incorrectly write that UT uses X-rays.
> 4. **Forgetting acceptance standards:** A defect description without reference to **ISO 5817** or **IACS UR W17** acceptance level is incomplete — losing 1–2 marks.
> 5. **No diagram in NDT questions:** Even a simple sketch of an MPI yoke or UT probe earns 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Skipping numerical steps:** In heat input problems, every arithmetic step must be shown — partial marks are only given for working.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Weld Defects, Distortion Control, DT & NDT**

### A. Weld Defects (ISO 6520-1 Codes)
- Cracks: 100-series — most severe
- Cavities (porosity, blowholes): 200-series
- Solid inclusions (slag, tungsten): 300-series
- Lack of fusion (401) & Incomplete penetration (402): 400-series — zero tolerance in critical welds
- Imperfect shape (undercut, overlap): 500-series

### B. Distortion Types
- Transverse, Longitudinal, Angular, Bowing, Buckling, Rotational

### C. Distortion Control
- **Prevention:** Joint design, pre-setting, low heat input, sequence, clamping
- **Correction:** Line heating (TEPT, 1:4:1 rule), mechanical straightening, peening

### D. Destructive Testing (DT) — 8 methods
- Tensile, Bend, Charpy, Hardness, Macro, Micro, Chemical, Nick-break
- Performed on **coupons** welded with same WPS

### E. Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) — 9 methods
- VT, RT, UT, MPI/MT, DPI/PT, ET, AE, PAUT, TOFD
- RT for volumetric; UT for planar cracks; MPI for ferromagnetic surface; DPI for non-magnetic surface

### F. KTU Key Standards
- **ISO 6520-1:** Defect classification
- **ISO 5817:** Quality levels B, C, D
- **IACS UR W17:** Ship hull weld classification (Class A, B, C)
- **AWS D1.1:** Structural welding code (ship & bridge)

### G. Critical Formulas to Memorize
- $\varepsilon_{th} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$
- $\Delta\theta = \dfrac{\alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot t_w}{2 \cdot t_p^2}$
- $Heat\ Input = \dfrac{60 \cdot \eta \cdot V \cdot I}{1000 \cdot S}$ (kJ/mm)
- $R_m = F_{max} / A_0$
- Charpy impact energy = absorbed energy at notch (J)

### H. Shipbuilding-Specific Facts
- **Line heating (火工矯正)** is uniquely Japanese/Korean — used for hull plate fairing
- **Hull plate shrinkage** ≈ 0.3–0.5 mm/m in single-pass butt welds
- **Maximum HAZ hardness** ≤ 380 HV for ship steel (per IACS)
- **Class A welds** (hull longitudinals, decks) require RT or UT + VT
- **Stiffener fillet welds** are typically inspected by MPI + VT

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Welding Standards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST831 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Welding Standards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Welding Standards - Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Welding Standard (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A *Welding Standard* is a formally documented and internationally recognized set of technical specifications, procedural rules, qualification criteria, and acceptance tolerances that govern the design, preparation, execution, inspection, and certification of welded joints. In shipbuilding, welding standards ensure that hull structures, machinery foundations, pressure vessels, and piping systems meet stringent **safety, structural integrity, fatigue life, and seaworthiness** requirements dictated by classification societies and regulatory bodies.

In shipbuilding, welding is not an informal craft — it is a **highly codified engineering discipline**. Because a single defective weld in a hull plate, propeller shaft seat, or cargo tank bulkhead can lead to catastrophic failure at sea, the maritime industry enforces strict adherence to welding standards.

## 1.2 Major Standardizing Authorities in Shipbuilding Welding

| Authority | Jurisdiction / Scope | Notable Standard |
|---|---|---|
| **AWS** (American Welding Society) | Global | AWS D1.1, AWS D3.6M |
| **ISO** (Int. Org. for Standardization) | Global | ISO 3834, ISO 9606, ISO 15614 |
| **ASME** (American Soc. of Mech. Engineers) | Pressure vessels / nuclear | ASME Section IX, ASME BPVC |
| **IACS** (Int. Assoc. of Classification Societies) | Ships & offshore | IACS UR W series |
| **EN / CEN** (European Norms) | EU | EN 1090, EN ISO 3834 |
| **Classification Societies** (ABS, DNV, LR, BV, CCS, KR) | Individual maritime | Hull Rules Chapter on Welding |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Intuitive View

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Recipe Book" of Shipbuilding**
> Think of welding standards like a **master recipe book** used in a Michelin-star restaurant. Just as a chef cannot simply "eyeball" a soufflé and expect consistent results, a welder cannot rely on intuition alone to fuse two 30 mm thick hull plates together for an oil tanker. The standard is the recipe — it dictates:
> - Which ingredients to use (base metal, filler metal)
> - The exact oven temperature (preheat/interpass temperature)
> - Cooking time and timing (welding parameters)
> - Plating presentation (weld profile, finish)
> - The taster's approval (inspection and NDT acceptance)
>
> If the chef skips any step, the soufflé collapses. If the welder skips a step, the **ship sinks**.

## 1.4 Why Welding Standards Exist — The Driving Forces

1. **Safety of Life at Sea (SOLAS)** — Loss of life and vessels from weld failures is unacceptable.
2. **Structural Integrity** — Hulls are subjected to cyclic wave loading, slamming, and fatigue.
3. **Classification Compliance** — Ships cannot be insured, registered, or operated without class approval.
4. **Interchangeability & Global Trade** — Ships are built in one country and repaired in another; common standards ensure continuity.
5. **Legal Liability** — Standards form the legally defensible baseline of "due diligence."

## 1.5 The Three Pillars of Welding Standardization

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every major welding standard system is structured around these **three pillars**:
>
> | Pillar | Question It Answers | Example Standard |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Pillar 1 — Procedure Qualification** | *HOW should we weld?* | ISO 15614-1, ASME IX, AWS D1.1 |
> | **Pillar 2 — Welder Qualification** | *WHO is allowed to weld?* | ISO 9606-1, AWS D1.1 |
> | **Pillar 3 — Quality Management** | *HOW DO WE prove it is consistent?* | ISO 3834-2/3/4, EN 1090 |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Welding Standards - Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The Hierarchical Structure of Welding Standards

Standards are organized in a **pyramid of authority**:

```
                    ┌─────────────────────────┐
                    │   CODES (Mandatory)     │  e.g., AWS D1.1, ASME IX
                    ├─────────────────────────┤
                    │  CLASSIFICATION RULES   │  e.g., DNV Hull Rules Ch.2
                    ├─────────────────────────┤
                    │  INTERNATIONAL NORMS    │  e.g., ISO 3834, ISO 9606
                    ├─────────────────────────┤
                    │  RECOMMENDED PRACTICES  │  e.g., AWS D1.1 Annexes
                    ├─────────────────────────┤
                    │  COMPANY / SHOP SOPs    │  Internal Yard Procedures
                    └─────────────────────────┘
```

A **higher-level code** incorporates or references the lower-level standards. For example, a classification society rule may mandate compliance with ISO 3834 for the shipyard's quality system.

## 2.2 ISO 3834 — Quality Requirements for Fusion Welding

This is the **backbone standard** for welding quality in fabrication shops worldwide. It defines the requirements a manufacturer must satisfy to demonstrate its capability to produce welded joints of a specified quality.

> [!NOTE]
> **ISO 3834 has four parts**, applied based on the criticality of the welded product:

| Part | Title | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **ISO 3834-1** | Criteria for the selection of the appropriate level | Decision-making |
| **ISO 3834-2** | Comprehensive quality requirements | Critical welds (hull, pressure vessels) |
| **ISO 3834-3** | Standard quality requirements | Non-critical structural welds |
| **ISO 3834-4** | Elementary quality requirements | Low-risk fabrications |

For shipbuilding, **ISO 3834-2** is the benchmark expected by IACS members and most class societies.

### ISO 3834-2 Core Requirements (KTU High-Yield):

- **Welding Coordination Personnel (WCP)** — appointment of a Responsible Welding Coordinator (RWC) per **ISO 14731**.
- **Welding Procedure Specifications (WPS)** — must be qualified per ISO 15614 or equivalent.
- **Welder Qualification** — per ISO 9606 series.
- **Production Welding** — controlled work instructions, traceability.
- **Inspection & Testing** — including NDT (Non-Destructive Testing) per ISO 9712 for personnel qualification.
- **Calibration** — of all measuring, electrical, and thermal equipment.
- **Non-conformance & Corrective Action** — documented system required.

## 2.3 ISO 9606 — Welder Qualification Testing

This standard specifies the requirements for the **qualification testing of welders** for fusion welding of metallic materials.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Favourite — Range of Qualification (Extent of Approval):**
>
> A welder qualified under ISO 9606 receives a "range of approval" — meaning the qualification is **NOT** narrowly limited to one exact test piece. It covers a **family** of joints. Key variables:
>
> | Variable | Typical Qualification Range |
> |---|---|
> | **Material Group** | e.g., Group 1 (Steels), Group 2 (Stainless) |
> | **Plate Thickness** | e.g., qualified on 10 mm → approved for 4–20 mm |
> | **Welding Process** | e.g., 135 (MAG), 111 (SAW) |
> | **Joint Type** | Butt / Fillet / Branch |
> | **Welding Position** | PA, PF, PC, PE, PH (per ISO 6947) |
> | **Filler Metal Type** | Solid / Flux-cored / etc. |

## 2.4 ISO 15614 — Welding Procedure Qualification

A **WPS (Welding Procedure Specification)** must be **qualified** by testing a test piece before being used in production.

> [!NOTE]
> **WPS Qualification Flow (KTU High-Yield):**
>
> `pWPS (Preliminary WPS)` → `Welding of Test Piece` → `Mechanical & NDT Testing` → `WPQR (Welding Procedure Qualification Record)` → **Qualified WPS** → **Production Use**
>
> Each WPQR documents the **essential variables** that were used and tested. Changing an essential variable (e.g., heat input, joint type, preheat) invalidates the WPS.

### Essential Variables (Typical):
- Base metal group / thickness
- Welding process and mode (pulsed, spray, short-circuit)
- Joint type (BW, FW, branch)
- Filler metal classification
- Shielding gas composition
- Preheat / Interpass temperature
- Heat input range
- Position

## 2.5 AWS D1.1 — Structural Welding Code (Steel)

The most widely adopted structural welding standard in the world, heavily used in U.S. shipbuilding and bridge/building construction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AWS D1.1 KTU High-Yield Points:**
>
> - **Pre-qualified WPS** for many common joints (no testing required).
> - **PQR** required for non-pre-qualified joints.
> - **CVN (Charpy V-Notch) Impact Testing** mandatory for service below **−30 °C** (low temperature service).
> - **Statically and Cyclically Loaded Structures** — separate fatigue provisions.
> - **WPS Variables**: 21+ essential variables, including PQR vs. production.

### AWS D1.1 Acceptance Criteria — Typical Discontinuities:

| Defect Type | Acceptance Limit |
|---|---|
| **Undercut** | ≤ 0.01 t (but not > 1 mm) |
| **Porosity** | ≤ 3% projected length in 25 mm |
| **Cracks** | **NOT PERMITTED** (any size) |
| **Lack of Penetration (LOP)** | ≤ 2 mm, limited length |
| **Under-flush of Butt Joint** | ≤ 1 mm + 0.1 t |
| **Overlap** | **NOT PERMITTED** |

## 2.6 IACS Unified Requirements (UR W Series) — Shipbuilding Specific

IACS issues **Unified Requirements (UR)** for member societies (ABS, DNV, LR, BV, CCS, KR, NK, RINA, etc.). The **UR W series** covers welding:

- **UR W2** — Hull welding
- **UR W3** — Welding consumables
- **UR W7** — Welders' qualification
- **UR W8** — Weld joint designs
- **UR W11** — Approval of consumables
- **UR W16** — Non-destructive testing of welds
- **UR W17** — Procedure qualification tests

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Point:** When a ship is built to any IACS class, the **UR W series takes precedence** over individual class rules. This harmonization simplifies global shipbuilding.

## 2.7 ASME Section IX — Welding & Brazing Qualifications

Primarily used for **boilers, pressure vessels, and nuclear components** but referenced in some marine applications (e.g., cargo piping, fuel systems).

- Defines **Welding Procedure Specification (WPS)** and **Procedure Qualification Record (PQR)**.
- **Performance Qualification Test** for welders (separate from WPS qualification).
- Variables are categorized as **essential, supplementary essential, and non-essential**.

## 2.8 EN 1090 — Execution of Steel Structures

European counterpart used for shipbuilding in EU yards. **EN 1090-1 (CE marking)** and **EN 1090-2 (technical requirements)** are key references.

| Execution Class | Risk Level | Use |
|---|---|---|
| **EXC1** | Low | Non-structural |
| **EXC2** | Moderate | General structures |
| **EXC3** | High | Critical ship hulls |
| **EXC4** | Very High | Special structures (e.g., LNG carriers) |

## 2.9 Welding Inspection Standards (NDT)

| Method | Standard | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Visual Testing (VT)** | ISO 17637 | Surface defects |
| **Magnetic Particle Testing (MT)** | ISO 17638 | Surface & near-surface in ferromagnetic |
| **Dye Penetrant Testing (PT)** | ISO 3452-1 | Surface cracks in non-magnetic |
| **Radiographic Testing (RT)** | ISO 17636 | Internal defects |
| **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)** | ISO 17640, 11666 | Internal defects, thicker plates |
| **Eddy Current Testing (ET)** | ISO 17643 | Surface in conductive metals |
| **TOFD** | ISO 10863 | Time-of-flight diffraction for critical welds |
| **Phased Array UT (PAUT)** | ISO 13588 | Advanced UT |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Acceptance Levels** (per ISO 5817 for fusion welds in steel):
> - **Level B** — Stringent (pressure vessels, critical hull)
> - **Level C** — Standard (general shipbuilding)
> - **Level D** — Moderate (secondary structures)

## 2.10 KTU High-Yield Reference Table — Welding Standard Coverage Matrix

| Aspect | AWS D1.1 | ISO 3834 | ISO 9606 | ISO 15614 | IACS UR W | EN 1090 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| WPS | ✔ | ✔ | — | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ |
| Welder Qual. | ✔ | — | ✔ | — | ✔ | ✔ |
| PQR | ✔ | — | — | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ |
| Quality System | — | ✔ | — | — | Partial | ✔ |
| NDT Acceptance | ✔ | — | — | — | ✔ | ✔ |
| Consumables | ✔ | ✔ | — | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ |

## 2.11 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Yard Certification**: A shipyard certified to ISO 3834-2 by an accredited body can bid for higher-value, higher-criticality projects globally.
- **Repair Portability**: A weld repair performed in Rotterdam on a Korean-built VLCC follows the same rules if the ship is flagged under any IACS class.
- **Insurance & Legal Defense**: Standards form the legally defensible "state of the art" baseline in maritime casualty litigation.
- **Lifecycle Traceability**: Every weld can be traced back to welder + procedure + consumable batch via documentation required by these standards.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Welding Standards - Section 3: Analytical Derivations & Step-by-Step Logic

## 3.1 Derivation of Heat Input — The Foundation of WPS Variables

Although "Welding Standards" is descriptive, the most important *quantitative* concept that all standards reference is **Heat Input (HI)**. It governs mechanical properties, distortion, and is a critical essential variable.

### 3.1.1 Formal Definition (Used in AWS D1.1, ISO 15614, EN 1011)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heat Input Formula (per unit length of weld):**
>
> $$\boxed{HI = \dfrac{k \cdot U \cdot I}{v \cdot 1000} \quad [\text{kJ/mm}]}$$
>
> Where:
> - $U$ = Arc voltage in **Volts (V)**
> - $I$ = Welding current in **Amperes (A)**
> - $v$ = Travel speed in **mm/s**
> - $k$ = Thermal efficiency factor (dimensionless)
>
> Typical $k$ values (KTU-memorizable):

| Process | $k$ (Thermal Efficiency) |
|---|---|
| **SAW (Submerged Arc)** | **1.0** |
| **GMAW / MAG (Metal Active Gas)** | **0.8** |
| **GTAW / TIG** | **0.6** |
| **SMAW / Stick (MMA)** | **0.8** |
| **FCAW (Flux-Cored)** | **0.8** |
| **PAW (Plasma)** | **0.6** |

### 3.1.2 Worked Example — KTU Board Exam Style

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A MAG (GMAW) fillet weld in a ship hull is being deposited using $U = 28$ V, $I = 240$ A, and $v = 6$ mm/s. The standard permits a maximum heat input of **2.0 kJ/mm** for this joint (to avoid excessive HAZ softening). Determine:
> (a) The actual heat input.
> (b) Whether the procedure is compliant.

**Step 1 — Identify the thermal efficiency.**
For MAG (GMAW), from the table above: $k = 0.8$.

**Step 2 — Write the formula in aligned form.**

$$\begin{aligned}
HI &= \dfrac{k \cdot U \cdot I}{v \cdot 1000} \\
   &= \dfrac{0.8 \cdot 28 \, \text{V} \cdot 240 \, \text{A}}{6 \, \text{mm/s} \cdot 1000}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate numerator and denominator separately (text-row explanation).**
The numerator is the product of the dimensionless efficiency, voltage in volts, and current in amperes — yielding watts (W). Multiplying the numerator by the implicit unit (1 W = 1 V·A) and dividing by (v × 1000) converts W·s/mm into kJ/mm because 1 kJ = 1000 W·s.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= 0.8 \times 28 \times 240 = 5376 \quad [\text{W}] \\
\text{Denominator} &= 6 \times 1000 = 6000 \quad [\text{mm/s}]
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Final division.**

$$HI = \dfrac{5376}{6000} = 0.896 \; \text{kJ/mm}$$

**Step 5 — Compliance check.**

$$0.896 \; \text{kJ/mm} \; < \; 2.0 \; \text{kJ/mm} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \textbf{COMPLIANT} \; ✔$$

### 3.1.3 Pre-Heat & Interpass Temperature Derivation

For higher carbon or thicker plates, standards (e.g., AWS D1.1 Annex XI, EN 1011-2) prescribe preheat to avoid **hydrogen-induced cracking**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Empirical preheat formula (based on ISO 1011 / EN 1011-2, simplified for KTU):**
>
> $$\boxed{T_{pre} = 160 \cdot \sqrt{t} - 200 \; \text{°C}}$$
>
> Where $t$ = combined plate thickness in mm. (This is a teaching simplification; real standards use cooling time $t_{8/5}$ derived from heat flow models.)

**Worked Example:**
For a butt joint between two 20 mm plates: $t = 40$ mm.

$$T_{pre} = 160 \cdot \sqrt{40} - 200 = 160 \cdot 6.324 - 200 = 1011.8 - 200 = 811.8 \; \text{°C}$$

This is unrealistically high — the empirical formula has an upper practical clamp of ~**200 °C** for normal C-Mn shipbuilding steel. In practice, the standard is consulted, and preheat is typically **100–150 °C** for EH36 grade plates of 20–40 mm thickness.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often apply formulas blindly. In real standard usage, the *named standard table* always takes precedence over derived formulas. The derivation above is for *understanding*, not as a substitute for consulting the applicable rule.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Welder Qualification Range of Approval (ISO 9606-1)

This is one of the most-tested analytical topics in KTU exams.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plate Thickness Qualification Range (per ISO 9606-1, Table 5):**
>
> | Test Piece Thickness (t) | Qualified Range (Plate) |
> |---|---|
> | $t \leq 3$ mm | $0.5 \cdot t$ to $1.1 \cdot t$ |
> | $3 < t \leq 12$ mm | 1.5 mm to $2 \cdot t$ |
> | $t > 12$ mm | 1.5 mm to $1.33 \cdot t$ |
>
> **For Butt Welds in Plate.**

**Worked Example:**
A welder qualifies by welding a 15 mm test plate. What is the range of plate thicknesses he is approved to weld?

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{test} &= 15 \;\text{mm} \;>\; 12 \;\text{mm} \\
\Rightarrow \text{Range} &= 1.5 \;\text{mm} \;\text{to}\; 1.33 \times 15 \\
                         &= 1.5 \;\text{mm} \;\text{to}\; 19.95 \;\text{mm}
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore, the welder is approved for plate thicknesses from **1.5 mm up to ~20 mm**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Welding Position Coding (ISO 6947 / AWS)

| ISO Code | AWS Code | Position | Orientation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PA** | 1G / 1F | Flat | Plate horizontal, weld on top |
| **PB** | 2F | Horizontal | Plate vertical, weld horizontal |
| **PC** | 2G | Horizontal | Plate vertical, weld vertical (groove) |
| **PD** | 4F | Horizontal overhead | Plate vertical, weld on overhead side |
| **PE** | 4G | Overhead | Plate horizontal, weld on bottom |
| **PF** | 3G | Vertical-up | Plate vertical, weld travels upward |
| **PG** | 3F | Vertical-down | Plate vertical, weld travels downward |
| **PH** | 5G / 5F | Pipe fixed horizontal | Pipe cannot rotate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Qualification Rule (KTU):** A welder qualified in a "more difficult" position is qualified for **all less-difficult positions** within the same joint type. The hierarchy is: **PE > PF/PG > PD > PC > PB > PA**.

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Logic — How a WPS Becomes "Qualified"

This is a frequent descriptive question in KTU exams. The logical chain:

```
[1] Identify base metal group (per ISO/TR 15608 or AWS D1.1 grouping)
         ↓
[2] Select welding process (e.g., 135 = MAG)
         ↓
[3] Draft a preliminary WPS (pWPS) with proposed parameters
         ↓
[4] Weld a qualified test coupon per ISO 15614-1
         ↓
[5] Perform Non-Destructive Testing (VT + RT or UT)
         ↓
[6] Perform mechanical tests:
       • Transverse tensile test
       • Bend tests (root, face, side)
       • Charpy V-notch impact tests (if required)
       • Macro-examination (often optional)
         ↓
[7] All tests PASS?
         ↓
[8] Document results in WPQR (Welding Procedure Qualification Record)
         ↓
[9] Issue qualified WPS valid for production
         ↓
[10] Any essential variable changed? → Restart at Step [3]
```

## 3.5 Acceptance Level Derivation — Stacked Porosity (ISO 5817)

> [!NOTE]
> **ISO 5817 Level B — Stacked Porosity Rule:**
>
> Sum of projected porosity areas in any 50 mm length must not exceed **2%** of the weld area in that section, and individual pores must not exceed **2 mm** diameter.
>
> **Sample Calculation:**
> Weld area in 50 mm length of a 10 mm thick butt weld with 12 mm effective throat:
>
> $$A_{weld} = 50 \times 12 = 600 \;\text{mm}^2$$
>
> Maximum permitted porosity (Level B):
>
> $$A_{por,max} = 0.02 \times 600 = 12 \;\text{mm}^2$$
>
> Therefore the *sum* of all circular pore areas in any 50 mm segment must be ≤ **12 mm²**, with no individual pore > 2 mm diameter.

## 3.6 Consumable Classification Cross-Reference (KTU Analytical Tool)

Often, KTU asks students to identify a consumable from its classification string. Here is the most important cross-reference for shipbuilding:

| System | Example | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **AWS A5.1** (SMAW) | E7018 | E = Electrode, 70 = 70 ksi tensile, 1 = all-position, 8 = low-hydrogen iron powder |
| **AWS A5.18** (GMAW) | ER70S-6 | ER = electrode/rod, 70 = 70 ksi, S = solid, 6 = deoxidized for rusty steel |
| **EN ISO 2560** | E 42 3 B 32 H5 | Strength, impact (J at °C), type, position, H = hydrogen control |
| **EN ISO 14341** | G 42 4 M G3Si1 | G = gas-shielded wire, strength, impact, M = mixed gas, classification |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse **E** (SMAW stick electrode) with **ER** (GMAW solid wire). The "R" indicates a rod/wire, and there is no flux coating on the wire itself.

## 3.7 Step-by-Step Logic — Selecting the Correct Standard (Decision Tree)

When a shipyard engineer is asked *"Which standard applies?"*, the decision tree is:

```
[Q1] Is the structure a ship hull / classed vessel?
       ├─ YES → Apply IACS UR W series + applicable Class Rules (e.g., DNV Pt.2 Ch.2)
       └─ NO  → Go to Q2

[Q2] Is it a pressure vessel / boiler?
       ├─ YES → Apply ASME BPVC Section IX
       └─ NO  → Go to Q3

[Q3] Is it a building / civil structure?
       ├─ YES → Apply EN 1090 (EU) or AWS D1.1 (US)
       └─ NO  → Go to Q4

[Q4] General fabrication → ISO 3834-2 quality system + ISO 15614 procedure + ISO 9606 welder
```

This decision tree is a **KTU board-favourite 14-mark question**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Welding Standards - Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Diagram — The Welding Standards Framework (Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootNode1([Shipbuilding Welding Standards Framework])
    rootNode1 --> intlNormA[International Norms]
    rootNode1 --> classRulesB[Classification Society Rules]
    rootNode1 --> regCodeC[Regulatory Codes]
    rootNode1 --> yardSOPd[Yard SOPs and WIs]

    intlNormA --> iso3834a1[ISO 3834 Quality System]
    intlNormA --> iso9606a2[ISO 9606 Welder Qualification]
    intlNormA --> iso15614a3[ISO 15614 WPS Qualification]
    intlNormA --> iso5817a4[ISO 5817 Quality Levels]
    intlNormA --> iso9712a5[ISO 9712 NDT Personnel]

    classRulesB --> iacsURb1[IACS UR W Series W2 W7 W8 W11 W16 W17]
    classRulesB --> absRulesb2[ABS Hull Rules Welding]
    classRulesB --> dnvRulesb3[DNV Pt 2 Ch 2 Hull Welding]
    classRulesB --> lrRulesb4[Lloyd s Register Ship Rules]

    regCodeC --> awsd1c1[AWS D1.1 Structural Welding]
    regCodeC --> asmeIXc2[ASME Section IX]
    regCodeC --> en1090c3[EN 1090 Execution of Steel]
    regCodeC --> en1011c4[EN ISO 1011 Heat Input]

    yardSOPd --> pWPSd1[pWPS Drafted from Qualified WPS]
    yardSOPd --> welderLogd2[Welder Log and Continuity]
    yardSOPd --> ndtPland3[NDT Plan per ISO 17635]

    iso3834a1 --> rwcReq1[Requires Responsible Welding Coordinator per ISO 14731]
    iso15614a3 --> wpqrOut1[Produces WPQR after successful testing]
    iacsURb1 --> consApproval1[Consumable Approval UR W11 UR W3]
    awsd1c1 --> cvnReq1[Charpy Impact Testing for Low Temp Service]
```

## 4.2 Diagram — WPS Qualification Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA1[Step 1 Identify Material Group per ISO TR 15608]
    stepA2[Step 2 Draft Preliminary WPS pWPS]
    stepA3[Step 3 Weld Test Coupon]
    stepA4[Step 4 NDT VT plus RT or UT]
    stepA5[Step 5 Mechanical Tests Tensile Bend CVN]
    stepA6[Step 6 Tests Passed Yes]
    stepA7[Step 7 Issue WPQR]
    stepA8[Step 8 Convert pWPS to Qualified WPS]
    stepA9[Step 9 Production Use]

    stepA1 --> stepA2 --> stepA3 --> stepA4 --> stepA5 --> stepA6 --> stepA7 --> stepA8 --> stepA9
    stepA5 -.Failed.-> stepA2

    stepA8 -.Any essential variable changed.-> stepA2
```

## 4.3 Diagram — Welder Range of Approval Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    welderStart[Welder Qualification Test Completed]
    welderStart --> jointQ1{What joint type}
    jointQ1 -->|Butt| buttBranch
    jointQ1 -->|Fillet| filletBranch
    jointQ1 -->|Branch| branchPipeBranch

    buttBranch[Range depends on test plate thickness t]
    filletBranch[Range throat leg extends to qualified thickness]
    branchPipeBranch[Qualifies pipe branches of larger diameter]

    buttBranch --> thickQ1{t greater than 12 mm}
    thickQ1 -->|Yes| rangeBig[Range 1.5 mm to 1.33 t]
    thickQ1 -->|No| rangeMid{t between 3 and 12 mm}
    rangeMid -->|Yes| rangeMid1[Range 1.5 mm to 2 t]
    rangeMid -->|No| rangeThin[Range 0.5 t to 1.1 t]
```

## 4.4 Diagram — Heat Input Compliance Check Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode[Operator records voltage current travel speed]
    startNode --> computeHI[Compute HI using k times U times I divided by v times 1000]
    computeHI --> checkLimit{HI less than or equal to WPS upper limit}
    checkLimit -->|Yes| approveStep[Procedure APPROVED for this segment]
    checkLimit -->|No| rejectStep[STOP Reduce current or increase travel speed]
    rejectStep --> recomputeStep[Recompute HI after parameter change]
    recomputeStep --> computeHI
```

## 4.5 Diagram — Inspection Sequence per IACS UR W16

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inspStart[Welded Joint Completed]
    inspStart --> vtStep[Visual Testing 100 percent per ISO 17637]
    vtStep --> vtPass{VT Acceptable per acceptance level}
    vtPass -->|No| repairStep[Repair and Re inspect]
    vtPass -->|Yes| ndtChoice{Critical weld}

    ndtChoice -->|Yes class critical| rtStep[Radiographic Testing 10 to 100 percent per UR W16]
    ndtChoice -->|Yes thick plate greater than 20 mm| utStep[Ultrasonic Testing per ISO 17640]
    ndtChoice -->|No secondary structure| signOffStep[Sign off per ISO 5817 Level C]

    rtStep --> defFound{Defects found}
    utStep --> defFound
    defFound -->|Yes above limits| repairStep
    defFound -->|No within limits| finalSignoff[Final Documentation and Acceptance]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
