# Welding Standards - Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Define a Welding Procedure Specification (WPS). What are its essential variables?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Welding Procedure Specification (WPS)** is a documented procedure that provides all the necessary parameters and conditions for producing a weld in accordance with the relevant code or standard. It includes details such as base metal group, thickness range, joint type, welding process, filler metal, shielding gas, electrical parameters, preheat/interpass temperature, and position.

**Essential variables** (per ISO 15614-1, AWS D1.1, ASME IX) are those parameters whose change invalidates the qualification. Examples: **base metal group, joint type, filler metal classification, shielding gas composition, preheat temperature, heat input, position, and process mode** (e.g., pulsed vs. spray for GMAW).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**List the four parts of ISO 3834 and state which part applies to critical ship hull welding.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Part | Title | Use |
|---|---|---|
| ISO 3834-1 | Criteria for selection of level | Decision-making |
| ISO 3834-2 | Comprehensive quality requirements | **Critical ship hull welding** |
| ISO 3834-3 | Standard quality requirements | Non-critical structural |
| ISO 3834-4 | Elementary quality requirements | Low-risk fabrications |

For critical ship hull welds (e.g., strength deck plating, sheer strake, bilge strake), **ISO 3834-2** is the applicable level, requiring a Responsible Welding Coordinator (RWC) per ISO 14731, fully documented WPS/WPQR, full traceability of consumables and welders, and 100 % NDT coverage of critical joints.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Rule:** *Each Part B question carries 14 marks and offers internal choice. Below are two alternative 14-mark questions for full coverage.*

---

### **QUESTION A — (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 4 | 14 Marks]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the three pillars of welding standardization. Describe in detail the **Welder Qualification Procedure as per ISO 9606-1**, including the range of approval for plate thickness in butt welds.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A MAG (GMAW) butt weld is being performed in a shipyard using:
- Voltage $U = 30$ V
- Current $I = 280$ A
- Travel speed $v = 5$ mm/s
- Thermal efficiency $k = 0.8$ for MAG

The applicable WPS specifies a **maximum allowable heat input of 1.6 kJ/mm**.
Calculate the actual heat input and comment on compliance. If non-compliant, suggest TWO corrective actions.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Model Answer (7 marks):**

The three pillars of welding standardization are:

1. **Pillar 1 — Welding Procedure Qualification:** This answers *HOW should we weld?* It is governed by standards such as ISO 15614 (procedure qualification), ASME IX, or AWS D1.1. A preliminary WPS (pWPS) is drafted, a test coupon is welded, NDT and mechanical testing are performed, and upon success a **WPQR (Welding Procedure Qualification Record)** is issued, which validates the WPS for production use.

2. **Pillar 2 — Welder Qualification:** This answers *WHO is allowed to weld?* Standards such as ISO 9606-1 (welders), ISO 14732 (operators of mechanized welding), and AWS D1.1 (welder performance qualification) require a welder to weld a test piece, which is then subjected to visual, radiographic/ultrasonic, and mechanical tests.

3. **Pillar 3 — Quality Management System:** This answers *HOW DO WE prove it is consistent?* Standards such as ISO 3834-2 (comprehensive) require a documented quality system, appointment of a Responsible Welding Coordinator per ISO 14731, calibration of equipment, traceability, and corrective action procedures.

**Welder Qualification Procedure as per ISO 9606-1:**
- Welder submits proof of identity and prior experience.
- Welder welds a test coupon of a specific material group, joint type, position, and plate thickness.
- Test coupon undergoes VT, RT/UT, and mechanical tests (tensile, bend, macro).
- If passed, a **welder qualification certificate** is issued with a defined **range of approval**.

**Range of approval for plate thickness in butt welds (ISO 9606-1, Table 5):**

| Test Thickness $t$ | Qualified Plate Thickness Range |
|---|---|
| $t \leq 3$ mm | $0.5t$ to $1.1t$ |
| $3 < t \leq 12$ mm | 1.5 mm to $2t$ |
| $t > 12$ mm | 1.5 mm to $1.33t$ |

For example, qualification on a 15 mm plate → approval for 1.5 mm to 19.95 mm plates.

**(b) Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Recall the heat input formula.**

$$HI = \dfrac{k \cdot U \cdot I}{v \cdot 1000} \quad [\text{kJ/mm}]$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values in aligned form.**

$$\begin{aligned}
HI &= \dfrac{0.8 \times 30 \, \text{V} \times 280 \, \text{A}}{5 \, \text{mm/s} \times 1000} \\
   &= \dfrac{0.8 \times 8400}{5000} \\
   &= \dfrac{6720}{5000} \\
   &= 1.344 \; \text{kJ/mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compliance check.**

$$1.344 \; \text{kJ/mm} \;\leq\; 1.6 \; \text{kJ/mm} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \textbf{COMPLIANT} \; ✔$$

The actual heat input is **below the WPS maximum**, so the procedure is acceptable.

**Step 4 — Hypothetical corrective actions** (only required if non-compliant):
1. **Reduce the welding current** $I$ (e.g., from 280 A to 250 A), which proportionally reduces HI.
2. **Increase the travel speed** $v$ (e.g., from 5 mm/s to 6 mm/s), which inversely reduces HI.
3. Alternatively, **change the welding process** to one with lower thermal efficiency (e.g., from SAW to GMAW).

---

### **QUESTION B — (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 4 | 14 Marks]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a neat flowchart, explain the **WPS Qualification Workflow as per ISO 15614-1**. Why is this qualification critical for shipbuilding?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail **IACS Unified Requirements (UR W Series)** for shipbuilding welding. List at least **six (6)** UR W documents with their scope.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Importance for Shipbuilding:**
Ships operate in hostile marine environments — cyclic wave loading, fatigue, corrosion, and temperature variation. A hull weld failure can cause catastrophic loss of life, cargo, and the vessel. WPS qualification ensures that the *specific* combination of base metal, filler metal, process, and parameters used in production has been **proven to produce acceptable mechanical properties** before being applied to the actual hull. It also provides a **legal and contractual defense**, since most classification societies (DNV, ABS, LR, BV) require ISO 15614 or equivalent qualification as a precondition for class approval.

**WPS Qualification Workflow (ISO 15614-1):**

```
Step 1 → Identify material group (per ISO/TR 15608)
Step 2 → Select welding process (e.g., 135 = MAG)
Step 3 → Draft preliminary WPS (pWPS)
Step 4 → Weld qualified test coupon
Step 5 → NDT: 100 % VT + RT or UT
Step 6 → Mechanical tests: tensile, bend, CVN (if required)
Step 7 → All tests pass? → Issue WPQR
Step 8 → Promote pWPS → qualified WPS
Step 9 → Production use
Step 10 → Essential variable changed? → Re-qualify (back to Step 3)
```

**Critical points:**
- The pWPS is the *recipe*; the WPQR is the *proof*.
- A WPQR is valid only for the exact ranges of essential variables tested.
- The qualified WPS is the document actually used by welders on the shop floor.

**(b) Model Answer (7 marks):**

The **International Association of Classification Societies (IACS)** is a body of leading marine classification societies (ABS, DNV, Lloyd's Register, Bureau Veritas, ClassNK, RINA, KR, CCS, etc.). IACS issues **Unified Requirements (UR)** that harmonize rules across all member societies to ensure consistent shipbuilding standards globally.

**The UR W Series covers welding-related matters:**

| UR | Scope |
|---|---|
| **UR W2** | Hull welding — general requirements, joint categories, and acceptance levels |
| **UR W3** | Welding consumables — approval and classification |
| **UR W7** | Welder qualification testing for hull welding |
| **UR W8** | Weld joint design — preparation, geometry, fit-up tolerances |
| **UR W11** | Approval of welding consumables (Filler metals and fluxes) |
| **UR W16** | Non-destructive testing of welds — methods, extent, acceptance |
| **UR W17** | Welding procedure qualification tests (PQT) |

**Importance in Shipbuilding:**
- Any IACS-classed ship (which represents > 90 % of world tonnage) must comply with UR W.
- These UR are **adopted identically** by all member societies, simplifying global ship construction and repair.
- A weld repair performed in Singapore on a Greek-flagged, Korean-built, DNV-classed VLCC follows the **same UR W rules** as original construction.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing WPS, WPQR, and pWPS:** Many students use these interchangeably. WPS = the *instruction*; pWPS = *preliminary version*; WPQR = *evidence record* proving qualification.
> 2. **Forgetting the thermal efficiency factor $k$:** Heat input calculations without $k$ lose 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Not stating the units of heat input:** Always write **kJ/mm** (or kJ/in in AWS). KTU examiners are strict on units.
> 4. **Mixing ISO and AWS terminology:** ISO uses *positions PA, PB, PC...* while AWS uses *1G, 2F, 3G...*. Pick one and stick with it.
> 5. **Omitting traceability:** Standards like ISO 3834 require traceability — always mention it.
> 6. **Wrong classification of ISO 3834 parts:** Many students confuse ISO 3834-3 and -4. Memorize: **2 = strictest, 4 = loosest**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Revision Block — Welding Standards**

- **Welding Standard** = codified rules for the design, qualification, execution, and inspection of welds.
- **Three pillars** = WPS Qualification (ISO 15614) + Welder Qualification (ISO 9606) + Quality System (ISO 3834).
- **ISO 3834-2** is the comprehensive quality standard for ship hull welding.
- **Responsible Welding Coordinator (RWC)** is mandated under ISO 14731 for ISO 3834-2 shops.
- **WPQR** is the *evidence* document; **WPS** is the *instruction* document.
- **Heat input formula**: $HI = (k \cdot U \cdot I) / (v \cdot 1000)$, in **kJ/mm**.
- Thermal efficiency $k$: SAW = 1.0, GMAW/SMAW/FCAW = 0.8, GTAW/PAW = 0.6.
- **Range of approval** for welder plate thickness (ISO 9606-1):
  - $t \leq 3$ mm → $0.5t$ to $1.1t$
  - $3 < t \leq 12$ mm → 1.5 mm to $2t$
  - $t > 12$ mm → 1.5 mm to $1.33t$
- **Position hierarchy** (most → least difficult): PE > PF/PG > PD > PC > PB > PA.
- **IACS UR W series** governs shipbuilding welding globally — URs W2, W3, W7, W8, W11, W16, W17 are key.
- **AWS D1.1** pre-qualified WPS for many joints; cracks are NEVER permitted.
- **Charpy CVN impact testing** is mandatory below **−30 °C** in AWS D1.1.
- **NDT methods** for weld inspection: VT, MT, PT, RT, UT, TOFD, PAUT (per ISO 17637-17643).
- **Acceptance levels** per ISO 5817: **B** (stringent), **C** (standard), **D** (moderate).
- **EN 1090** Execution Classes: EXC1 → EXC4 (lowest to highest risk).
- **EN 1011 / ISO 1011** governs heat input and preheat determination for arc welding.
- **Consumable classification examples**:
  - AWS E7018 = SMAW low-hydrogen electrode, 70 ksi tensile.
  - AWS ER70S-6 = GMAW solid wire.
  - EN ISO 2560 E 42 3 B 32 H5 = SMAW European equivalent.
- **All critical welds require** 100 % VT + appropriate NDT (RT or UT) before sign-off.
- **Hull weld acceptance** in IACS rules is typically ISO 5817 **Level C** for primary structure, **Level B** for highly stressed regions.
- **Documentation is law** — every weld must be traceable to welder, WPS, consumable batch, and inspection record.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Welding Standards | OENST831 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Welding Performance and Procedure Qualification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST831 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Welding Performance and Procedure Qualification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Welding Procedure Qualification (WPQ)** is the formal, documented process used to demonstrate that a proposed **Welding Procedure Specification (WPS)** is capable of producing welds possessing the mechanical properties, soundness, and metallurgical integrity required by the applicable fabrication standard (e.g., **AWS D1.1**, **ASME Section IX**, **ISO 15614-1**, **IACS UR W17**).

**Welding Performance Qualification (Welder/Operator Qualification)** is the separate but linked process used to verify that an *individual welder or welding operator* possesses the manual skill, dexterity, and technique necessary to execute a weld in accordance with a qualified WPS, producing sound welds within the defined qualification ranges.

Together, these two qualifications form the dual-pillar quality assurance system mandated by every classification society and shipbuilding standard worldwide.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction (Board Favourite):** A *Procedure* qualifies the **document** (WPS). A *Performance* test qualifies the **person** (welder). One WPS can be used by many welders, but every welder must be individually qualified against the WPS they will use.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are opening a commercial bakery that must produce a *Vanilla Sponge Cake* to a government food-safety standard:

- **WPS = The Master Recipe Card.** It lists *every* ingredient (filler metal, base metal, shielding gas), *every* oven setting (voltage, current, travel speed, preheat/interpass temperature), and *every* step in the exact sequence. Before you can sell the cake to the public, the recipe itself must be **laboratory-tested** — a sample cake is baked and chemically/sensorily tested. If it passes, the recipe is "**Procedure Qualified**" and stamped on a **Procedure Qualification Record (PQR)**.

- **Performance Qualification = The Cook's Cooking Test.** The recipe is now proven, but you must still prove that *Chef Ravi* can follow it. Chef Ravi is asked to bake a test cake using *only* the approved recipe and approved oven. If the cake passes the same lab tests, Chef Ravi receives a personal **Welder Qualification Certificate** valid for a specific material group, thickness range, and welding process.

In a shipyard, the *cake* is a **test coupon plate** that gets machined into mechanical test specimens, the *lab tests* are **tensile, bend, impact, hardness, and macroetch tests**, and the *stamps* are the **PQR** (for the recipe) and the **Welder's Certificate** (for the cook).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Shipbuilding Insight:** Classification societies (Lloyd's Register, DNV, Bureau Veritas, ABS, ClassNK, KR) treat WPS/PQR/Welder qualification as a *legal pre-condition* before a single production fillet weld is permitted on a hull, pressure vessel, or offshore structure. Failure to qualify is a contract-termination event.

### 1.3 Standard Bodies & Metrics Used in This Topic

- **AWS** — American Welding Society
- **ASME** — American Society of Mechanical Engineers (Section IX)
- **ISO** — International Organization for Standardization (ISO 9606, ISO 15614)
- **IACS** — International Association of Classification Societies (Unified Requirements)
- **Base units used throughout:** **millimetres (mm)** for thickness, **Joules (J)** for impact energy, **MegaPascals (MPa)** for tensile strength, **degrees Celsius (°C)** for temperature.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Core Documents — The "Triad of Qualification"

A shipyard's welding quality system rests on **three inseparable documents**:

| # | Document | Full Name | Purpose | Issuer | Validity |
|---|----------|-----------|---------|--------|----------|
| 1 | **WPS** | Welding Procedure Specification | Written instruction defining *all* welding parameters (process, materials, joint type, electrical parameters, preheat, position) for a specific application. | Shipyard (preparer) | Indefinite, but tied to the supporting PQR |
| 2 | **PQR** | Procedure Qualification Record | The *test report* and *test results* proving that a WPS, when followed, yields acceptable welds. | Testing laboratory / classification surveyor | Permanent record |
| 3 | **WPQ Certificate** | Welder/Operator Performance Qualification | Personal certificate proving an individual welder can deposit sound welds per a qualified WPS. | Classification society / shipyard QA | Typically **2 to 3 years**, renewable by re-test |

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Workflow

The complete qualification workflow in a shipyard follows a strictly defined **7-stage sequence**:

1. **Identify the Application & Base Material** — e.g., welding **EH36 shipbuilding steel** (yield strength ≥ 355 MPa) using **GTAW** (Tungsten Inert Gas / TIG) process.
2. **Draft a Preliminary WPS** — Specify filler metal (e.g., ER70S), joint geometry (butt, fillet, T-joint), thickness range, position (1G, 2G, 3G, 4G, 5G, 6G), shielding gas (e.g., Argon 99.99%), preheat (e.g., 100 °C).
3. **Prepare a Test Coupon** — A plate of the *exact* base material and thickness, welded under strict supervision by a *highly skilled* welder, following the draft WPS precisely. The coupon must be welded in the most demanding qualification position (typically **3G or 5G**).
4. **Conduct Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)** — Visual (VT) + Radiographic (RT) or Ultrasonic (UT) examination to confirm the weld is *free of defects* before mechanical testing. Cracks, lack of fusion, or porosity cause **immediate rejection** of the PQR.
5. **Machine Mechanical Test Specimens** — Standardised tensile, bend, impact (Charpy V-notch), hardness, and macroetch specimens are extracted from the coupon at locations defined by the standard.
6. **Conduct Destructive Testing** — All specimens are pulled, bent, impacted, and etched. The results must equal or exceed the **minimum acceptance values** specified in the standard.
7. **Issue the PQR & Approve the WPS** — On passing, the PQR is signed, the WPS is officially approved, and the procedure is now *qualified* for production. Production welders must then be individually performance-qualified against it.

### 2.3 The Hierarchy of Variables — "Essential, Supplementary, Non-Essential"

A change in welding parameters can either **invalidate** a qualified WPS, **require re-qualification**, or **require only a new WPS without re-testing**. Standards categorise variables into three tiers:

| Tier | Variable Type | Effect of a Change | KTU Significance |
|------|---------------|--------------------|------------------|
| **Tier 1** | **Essential Variables** | *Invalidates* the PQR → Full re-qualification mandatory | Highest exam weightage |
| **Tier 2** | **Supplementary Essential Variables** | Affects impact toughness only → Re-impact test | Important for shipbuilding |
| **Tier 3** | **Non-Essential Variables** | Does not affect qualification → Only a new WPS is needed | Rarely examined |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Essential Variable Examples (Memorise for KTU):** Base metal group change, filler metal classification change, joint type change (butt ↔ fillet), thickness change *outside* qualified range, position change (e.g., 1G → 2G), preheat temperature drop below minimum, welding process change (SMAW → FCAW), heat input change > 25 %.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The formulas and numerical rules used in welding qualification are limited but precise. The table below is the **complete mathematical toolkit** for this topic.

| # | Formula / Rule | Expression | Variables Explained | Engineering Use |
|---|----------------|------------|---------------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Heat Input (HI)** | $HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S \cdot 1000}$ | $V$ = Voltage (V), $I$ = Current (A), $S$ = Travel speed (mm/s), $\eta$ = Process thermal efficiency (dimensionless) | Determines mechanical properties and HAZ width |
| 2 | **Process Efficiency $\eta$** | SMAW = 0.80, GMAW = 0.85, FCAW = 0.85, GTAW = 0.60, SAW = 1.00 (sub-arc) | $\eta$ varies per process | Used inside Heat Input formula |
| 3 | **Qualified Thickness Range (Butt Joints)** | $t_{qual} = t_{test} \pm \text{range}$ (typically $0.5\,t$ to $2\,t$) | $t_{test}$ = test coupon thickness | Defines the thicknesses a single PQR covers |
| 4 | **Qualified Fillet Leg Range** | $L_{qual} = 0.5\,L_{test}$ to $1.5\,L_{test}$ | $L_{test}$ = test fillet leg size | Range of fillet weld sizes a welder is approved for |
| 5 | **Tensile Strength Acceptance** | $\sigma_{uts,weld} \geq \sigma_{uts,base}$ (minimum) | $\sigma_{uts}$ = Ultimate Tensile Strength | Weld metal must reach base metal strength |
| 6 | **Charpy Impact Acceptance (Ship Hull)** | $KV \geq 27\,\text{J}$ (longitudinal), $KV \geq 20\,\text{J}$ (transverse) at **0 °C** or **-20 °C** per class | $KV$ = Charpy V-notch energy in Joules | Mandatory for Grade A, D, E, AH, DH, EH, FH shipbuilding steels |
| 7 | **Bend Test Acceptance** | No crack or defect $> 3\,\text{mm}$ in any direction after bending to **180°** over a mandrel of diameter **4t** | $t$ = specimen thickness | Proves weld ductility and fusion integrity |
| 8 | **Hardness Acceptance (Class Critical)** | $HV_{10} \leq 350$ (typical IACS UR W13 limit) | $HV_{10}$ = Vickers hardness at 10 kgf load | Prevents HAZ embrittlement and cold cracking |
| 9 | **Welding Position Code** | 1 = Flat, 2 = Horizontal, 3 = Vertical-up, 4 = Overhead, 5 = Horizontal fixed pipe, 6 = All positions inclined | Position number 6 = universal qualification | Universally used shorthand in WPS |
| 10 | **Welder Re-qualification Interval** | Typically **6 months** (active service) or **2 years** (max expiry) | Time-based | A lapsed welder must re-qualify |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pipeline / Class Cross-Reference:** *Class NK* and *DNV* use slightly different hardness limits (e.g., 380 HV max in specific HAZ zones). Always cross-check the applicable class rule book for the project before quoting hardness acceptance criteria in an exam answer.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Shipbuilding

- **Hull Construction (Plate ≤ 20 mm):** A single WPS qualified on a **15 mm** plate typically covers production from **7.5 mm to 30 mm** — enabling flexibility in yard scheduling.
- **Offshore & Cryogenic LNG Carriers:** Impact testing at **-196 °C** (LNG service) and **-40 °C** is essential; the supplementary essential variable rules ensure the PQR is never used outside the tested toughness window.
- **Repair Welding at Sea:** Aboard ship, performance-qualified welders are limited to the position and process they were tested in — preventing accidental over-qualification claims.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Qualified Thickness Range Rule

The standard thickness qualification rule is not arbitrary — it is derived from the **thermal mass principle** of welding metallurgy. Below is the full logical derivation that examiners expect to see when marks are allocated to a "derive/explain" question.

> **Statement of the Rule (ASME Section IX QW-202.2 / AWS D1.1 Clause 6.4):**
> A butt-joint PQR welded on a test coupon of thickness **$t$** qualifies production welds in the thickness range **$\mathbf{0.5\,t}$ to $\mathbf{2.0\,t}$** (for materials of equal or lower strength).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Upper Limit Reasoning:} \quad & \text{Heat dissipation rate} \propto \sqrt{\text{plate thickness}} \\
& \text{Thicker plate} = \text{higher heat sink} = \text{slower cooling} = \text{coarser grain} \\
& \text{To allow coarser-grain welds, the test plate is the THINNEST expected production plate} \\
& \Rightarrow t_{max,\,qual} = 2\,t_{test}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Lower Limit Reasoning:} \quad & \text{Thinner plate} = \text{lower heat sink} = \text{faster cooling} = \text{harder HAZ} \\
& \text{To validate against the hardest, most crack-sensitive condition, the test is on the THICKEST expected production plate} \\
& \Rightarrow t_{min,\,qual} = 0.5\,t_{test}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,0.5\,t_{test} \;\leq\; t_{production} \;\leq\; 2\,t_{test}\,}
$$

> **Worked Numerical Example (Essential for KTU Numerical Problems):**
> A shipyard qualifies a butt-joint WPS on a **20 mm** EH36 plate.
> Determine the range of production plate thicknesses the WPS covers.

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{test} &= 20\;\text{mm} \\[4pt]
t_{min,\,qual} &= 0.5 \times 20 = 10\;\text{mm} \\[4pt]
t_{max,\,qual} &= 2.0 \times 20 = 40\;\text{mm} \\[4pt]
\therefore \text{Qualified range} &= 10\;\text{mm} \;\text{to}\; 40\;\text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation of transition logic:** The 10 mm lower bound covers light hull plating; the 40 mm upper bound covers heavy sheer strakes and bilge brackets. The yard can therefore schedule this single WPS across the bulk of hull fabrication.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — The Five Mandatory Destructive Tests

The complete *analytical matrix* of mechanical tests performed on a qualified procedure test coupon is given below. This is the most frequently tested sub-topic in the KTU ESE.

| # | Test Name | Specimen Geometry | Standard Reference | Acceptance Criterion | What It Verifies |
|---|-----------|-------------------|--------------------|----------------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Transverse Tensile Test** | Rectangular, full weld cross-section, reduced gauge | ASTM E8 / ISO 4136 | $\sigma_{uts} \geq$ minimum base metal UTS, fracture location recorded | Weld strength |
| 2 | **Transverse Side Bend** (or Root/Face Bend) | 10 mm × 38 mm × L, bent over 4t mandrel to 180° | ASTM E190 / ISO 5173 | No crack $\geq$ 3 mm in any direction | Ductility & fusion |
| 3 | **Charpy V-Notch Impact Test** | 10 mm × 10 mm × 55 mm, notch in Weld Metal, Fusion Line, HAZ | ASTM E23 / ISO 148-1 | $KV \geq 27\,\text{J}$ (long) / 20 J (trans) at design T | Toughness |
| 4 | **Macroetch Examination** | Polished & etched cross-section, viewed at 10× | ASTM E340 / ISO 1321 | No cracks, complete fusion, no lack of penetration | Internal soundness |
| 5 | **Hardness Survey (Vickers HV10)** | Polished cross-section, indents at Weld, HAZ, Base | ASTM E92 / ISO 6507 | $HV_{10} \leq 350$ (IACS) or per class | Resistance to HAZ cracking |

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Welder Performance Qualification Range (AWS D1.1 Clause 6.2)

This matrix is the most exam-likely **tabular question** and is presented here in full derivable form:

| Position Tested in WPQ | Positions Qualified for Production | Plate Thickness Qualified | Fillet Leg Size Qualified | Process Qualified |
|------------------------|------------------------------------|--------------------------|---------------------------|-------------------|
| 1G (Flat) | 1G only | $t_{test}$ only, ± 25 % | As tested | As tested |
| 2G (Horizontal) | 1G, 2G | $t_{test}$ only, ± 25 % | As tested | As tested |
| 3G (Vertical-up) | 1G, 2G, 3G | $t_{test}$ only, ± 25 % | As tested | As tested |
| 4G (Overhead) | 1G, 2G, 4G | $t_{test}$ only, ± 25 % | As tested | As tested |
| **5G / 6G (Pipe All-position)** | **ALL positions (1G, 2G, 3G, 4G, 5G, 6G)** | $t_{test}$ only, ± 25 % | As tested | As tested |
| 6GR (Pipe with Restriction Ring) | All positions, but **restricted welds only** | $t_{test}$ only, ± 25 % | As tested | As tested |

> **Logical Derivation of the Position Hierarchy:** Welding position difficulty increases as the molten weld pool becomes less supported by gravity. The 1G (flat) position is the easiest because gravity holds the pool in the groove. Therefore, qualifying in the hardest position (5G/6G — pipe inclined, weld pool fights gravity in all axes) automatically qualifies the welder for every easier position. This is the *cascading qualification principle*.

### 3.4 Worked Heat Input Calculation (Mandatory Numerical Skill)

> **Problem:** A shipyard welds a **12 mm EH36** plate using **FCAW (flux-cored arc welding)** with the following parameters: $V = 28\,\text{V}$, $I = 240\,\text{A}$, $S = 6\,\text{mm/s}$. Given the thermal efficiency $\eta = 0.85$, calculate the heat input and assess its suitability for a maximum permitted HI of **2.5 kJ/mm** per the WPS.

$$
\begin{aligned}
HI &= \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S \cdot 1000} \quad \text{(kJ/mm, with S in mm/s)} \\[6pt]
HI &= \frac{0.85 \times 28 \times 240}{6 \times 1000} \\[6pt]
HI &= \frac{5712}{6000} \\[6pt]
HI &= 0.952\;\text{kJ/mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\therefore HI = 0.952\;\text{kJ/mm} \;<\; 2.5\;\text{kJ/mm}
$$

**Interpretation of result:** The calculated heat input is **0.952 kJ/mm**, which is well within the WPS maximum of 2.5 kJ/mm. The procedure is operating with a **healthy 62 % safety margin** below the upper limit — typical of shipyards aiming for fine-grained HAZ microstructure in EH36 steel.

> **If the result were borderline (e.g., 2.45 kJ/mm):** The welder would be instructed to *increase travel speed by 0.4 mm/s* to drop HI by ~0.05 kJ/mm and restore safety margin. This is the standard **production-floor corrective action**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

The Mermaid diagrams below use **purely alphanumeric node IDs** (no reserved keywords as node names), **no markdown formatting inside labels**, and **no embedded tables**. All labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text.

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Welding Qualification Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[STEP 1: IDENTIFY APPLICATION AND BASE METAL] --> A2[STEP 2: DRAFT PRELIMINARY WPS]
    A2 --> A3[STEP 3: WELD TEST COUPON PLATE]
    A3 --> A4[STEP 4: NON DESTRUCTIVE TESTING VT PLUS RT OR UT]
    A4 --> A5[STEP 5: MACHINE MECHANICAL TEST SPECIMENS]
    A5 --> A6[STEP 6: DESTRUCTIVE TESTING TENSILE BEND IMPACT HARDNESS MACRO]
    A6 --> A7{STEP 7: ALL TESTS PASSED}
    A7 -->|YES| A8[ISSUE PQR SIGNED BY SURVEYOR]
    A8 --> A9[APPROVE WPS FOR PRODUCTION USE]
    A9 --> A10[PERFORMANCE QUALIFY EACH WELDER AGAINST WPS]
    A10 --> A11[ISSUE WELDER WPQ CERTIFICATE]
    A7 -->|NO| A12[REJECT PQR REVISE WPS AND START AGAIN FROM STEP 2]
    A12 --> A2
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — The Triad of Qualification Documents

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[WELDING PROCEDURE SPECIFICATION WPS] --> B2[PROCEDURE QUALIFICATION RECORD PQR]
    B2 --> B3[PQR VERIFIES WPS]
    B1 --> B4[USED BY PRODUCTION WELDER]
    B4 --> B5[WELDER PERFORMANCE QUALIFICATION TEST]
    B5 --> B6[WPQ CERTIFICATE ISSUED]
    B6 --> B4
    B1 --> B7[STANDARD REFERENCE AWS D1 1 ASME IX ISO 15614 IACS UR W17]
    B2 --> B7
    B6 --> B7
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Essential Variable Cascade and Re-qualification Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[CHANGE IN WELDING PARAMETER DETECTED] --> C2{WHICH VARIABLE TIER}
    C2 -->|TIER 1 ESSENTIAL| C3[FULL PQR RE QUALIFICATION MANDATORY]
    C2 -->|TIER 2 SUPPLEMENTARY ESSENTIAL| C4[RE IMPACT TEST ONLY]
    C2 -->|TIER 3 NON ESSENTIAL| C5[ISSUE REVISED WPS NO RE TEST]
    C3 --> C6[RE WELD TEST COUPON]
    C6 --> C7[RE RUN DESTRUCTIVE TESTS]
    C7 --> C8[NEW PQR ISSUED]
    C4 --> C9[CHARPY TEST AT NEW CONDITION]
    C9 --> C10[PQR SUPPLEMENT ISSUED]
    C5 --> C11[WPS REVISION DOCUMENTED]
```

```mermaid
    D1 --> D5[MACROETCH SPECIMEN BOTTOM]
    D1 --> D6[HARDNESS TRAVERSE SPECIMEN BOTTOM RIGHT]
    D2 --> D7[TENSILE TEST]
    D3 --> D8[BEND TEST 180 DEGREE]
    D4 --> D9[IMPACT TEST CHARPY V NOTCH]
    D5 --> D10[MACRO ETCH AT 10X]
    D6 --> D11[HV10 HARDNESS SURVEY]
```
