## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
*Define the term "Welding Procedure Specification" (WPS). List any four parameters that must be specified in a WPS used in ship hull construction.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Welding Procedure Specification (WPS)** is a written, qualified document that provides all the necessary instructions and parameters for the production of a specific weld. It is qualified by a Procedure Qualification Record (PQR) and serves as the master recipe for production welding.

Four parameters that must be specified in a WPS for ship hull welding are:
1. **Base metal specification and grade** (e.g., EH36 per ASTM A131).
2. **Filler metal classification and size** (e.g., AWS E71T-1, 1.2 mm).
3. **Welding process** (e.g., FCAW) and **welding position** (e.g., 3G vertical-up).
4. **Electrical parameters** (voltage, current, travel speed) and **preheat/interpass temperature** (e.g., 100 °C minimum).

**Marking split:** 1 mark for definition + 0.5 each for any four parameters = 3 marks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**
*Distinguish between Procedure Qualification and Performance Qualification in shipbuilding welding. Why are both required?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Procedure Qualification | Performance Qualification |
|--------|------------------------|---------------------------|
| **Object** | Qualifies the WPS (document) | Qualifies the welder (person) |
| **Test** | Mechanical tests on a procedure test coupon | Mechanical tests on a welder test coupon |
| **Validity** | Indefinite (as long as essential variables unchanged) | 2 to 3 years, renewable |
| **Performed by** | Highly skilled test welder under strict supervision | The individual production welder being qualified |

**Why both are required (1 mark):** A qualified WPS only proves the *procedure* can produce acceptable welds. It does not prove that *every individual welder* can execute it correctly. Both are required because a sound procedure executed by an unskilled welder will still produce defective welds, and a skilled welder using an unsound procedure will also fail. The two qualifications together ensure **complete process-person compatibility**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
**(a)** Explain the complete procedure qualification test workflow for a butt-joint WPS in **EH36 shipbuilding steel** using the **FCAW process**, listing all stages from application identification to PQR issuance. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A PQR is welded on a **16 mm** thick plate. Determine the range of production plate thicknesses qualified by this PQR. If the WPS specifies a **maximum heat input of 2.0 kJ/mm** and the test was performed with $V = 30\,\text{V}$, $I = 220\,\text{A}$, $S = 5\,\text{mm/s}$, $\eta = 0.85$, verify whether the test conformed to the heat-input limit. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

The complete procedure qualification test workflow comprises **seven sequential stages**:

1. **Application Identification (1 mark):** Identify the production requirement — e.g., butt welding of 12–25 mm EH36 plates in the vertical-up (3G) position for the side shell of a Panamax bulk carrier. Specify the applicable standard (e.g., IACS UR W17 and AWS D1.1).

2. **Drafting the Preliminary WPS (1 mark):** List all variables — base metal (EH36), filler metal (e.g., AWS E71T-1, 1.2 mm), process (FCAW), joint detail (single-V groove, 60° included angle, 2 mm root gap), position (3G), preheat (100 °C), interpass (≤ 200 °C), shielding gas (75 % Ar / 25 % CO₂), and nominal heat input (1.5 kJ/mm).

3. **Test Coupon Preparation (1 mark):** Weld a test plate of the *exact* base material (EH36), thickness (16 mm), and joint design under strict supervision by a *test welder*. Record actual welding parameters on the WPS.

4. **Non-Destructive Testing (1 mark):** Subject the entire coupon to **Visual Testing (VT)** and **Radiographic Testing (RT)** or **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)** per the standard. Any defect (crack, lack of fusion, porosity > limit) results in **immediate rejection** of the PQR.

5. **Specimen Extraction (1 mark):** Machine the coupon to extract the standardised test specimens: **2 transverse tensile**, **4 transverse side-bend (or 2 root + 2 face bend)**, **3 sets of 3 Charpy V-notch** (weld metal, fusion line +2 mm, HAZ), **1 macroetch**, and **1 hardness traverse** specimen.

6. **Destructive Testing (1 mark):** Conduct the five tests in a calibrated lab. Verify all results meet acceptance criteria (UTS ≥ 490 MPa, KV ≥ 27 J at 0 °C, no crack in bend, HV ≤ 350, no defects in macro).

7. **PQR Issuance and WPS Approval (1 mark):** Compile all data into the PQR, signed by the classification surveyor. Approve the WPS for production use. Each production welder must then be individually performance-qualified against this WPS.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Qualified Thickness Range (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{test} &= 16\;\text{mm} \\[4pt]
t_{min,\,qual} &= 0.5 \times t_{test} = 0.5 \times 16 = 8\;\text{mm} \\[4pt]
t_{max,\,qual} &= 2.0 \times t_{test} = 2.0 \times 16 = 32\;\text{mm} \\[4pt]
\therefore \text{Qualified range} &= 8\;\text{mm}\;\text{to}\;32\;\text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

The WPS is therefore valid for production plate thicknesses from **8 mm to 32 mm** — covering the full range of typical hull plates on a Panamax bulk carrier.

**Step 2 — Heat Input Verification (4 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
HI &= \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S \cdot 1000} \\[6pt]
HI &= \frac{0.85 \times 30 \times 220}{5 \times 1000} \\[6pt]
HI &= \frac{5610}{5000} \\[6pt]
HI &= 1.122\;\text{kJ/mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison with WPS limit:**

$$
HI_{test} = 1.122\;\text{kJ/mm} \;\;<\;\; HI_{max} = 2.0\;\text{kJ/mm} \;\;\checkmark\;\text{CONFORMS}
$$

**Conclusion:** The actual heat input of **1.122 kJ/mm** is **44 % below** the WPS maximum of 2.0 kJ/mm. The procedure test fully conforms and the PQR can be issued with the test data in this range. The 2.0 kJ/mm upper limit remains the production ceiling; the 1.122 kJ/mm test value defines a benchmark that production welders may equal or exceed only up to 2.0 kJ/mm.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**
**(a)** Define *Essential Variables* in welding procedure qualification. List **six** essential variables and explain the consequence of changing any one of them. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What are the five mandatory mechanical tests performed on a procedure qualification test coupon? State the acceptance criterion for each test as per **IACS UR W17 / AWS D1.1** standards used in shipbuilding. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Definition (2 marks):** *Essential Variables* are welding parameters whose change — outside the limits explicitly permitted by the applicable standard — **invalidates the Procedure Qualification Record (PQR)** and mandates a complete re-qualification by welding and testing a new test coupon. They are the most tightly controlled parameters because they directly influence weld metal composition, fusion, and mechanical properties.

**Six Essential Variables and Consequences (5 marks — 0.5 each for variable + 0.33 for consequence):**

1. **Base metal group / specification change** — e.g., switching from Grade A mild steel to EH36. Consequence: New PQR mandatory because metallurgy and required mechanical properties differ.
2. **Filler metal classification change** — e.g., E7016 to E71T-1. Consequence: Weld metal chemistry and toughness change; new PQR required.
3. **Joint type change** — butt to fillet (or vice versa). Consequence: Heat flow pattern and stress state differ; new PQR required.
4. **Thickness change outside qualified range** — welding thinner than $0.5\,t$ or thicker than $2\,t$. Consequence: Heat sink and cooling rate deviate from tested condition; new PQR required.
5. **Welding position change outside qualified range** — e.g., 1G-only PQR used for 3G production. Consequence: Weld pool control and solidification differ; new PQR required.
6. **Welding process change** — e.g., SMAW to FCAW. Consequence: Slag chemistry, heat input profile, and deposition differ; new PQR required.

(Additional acceptable answers: preheat temperature drop below minimum, interpass temperature exceedance, heat input change > 25 %.)

**Consequence of changing any one of them:** A complete **re-qualification cycle** must be initiated — new test coupon welded, all five mechanical tests repeated, and a new PQR issued. The old PQR cannot be used for the changed condition.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

The five mandatory mechanical tests performed on a procedure qualification test coupon, with their acceptance criteria, are:

| # | Test | Acceptance Criterion (IACS UR W17 / AWS D1.1) | Marks |
|---|------|-----------------------------------------------|-------|
| 1 | **Transverse Tensile Test** | Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS) shall be **not less than the minimum specified UTS of the base metal**. The fracture location must be recorded (weld metal, HAZ, or base metal). | 1.5 |
| 2 | **Transverse Bend Test (Side / Root / Face)** | After bending to **180° over a mandrel of diameter 4t**, the specimen shall show **no crack or other open defect exceeding 3 mm in any direction**. | 1.5 |
| 3 | **Charpy V-Notch Impact Test** | For Grade A/D/E shipbuilding steels: **minimum 27 J (longitudinal) / 20 J (transverse)** at the test temperature (0 °C for Grade A, -20 °C for Grade D, -40 °C for Grade E, etc.). Tests at Weld Metal, Fusion Line, and HAZ. | 1.5 |
| 4 | **Macroetch Examination** | The cross-section shall show **complete fusion, no cracks, no lack of penetration, no porosity cluster**, and a clean weld profile. | 1.0 |
| 5 | **Hardness Test (Vickers HV10)** | **Vickers hardness shall not exceed 350 HV10** in the weld metal and HAZ (IACS UR W13 / W17). Indents taken across weld, HAZ, and unaffected base metal. | 1.5 |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Conflating WPS with PQR** (costs 1–2 marks) — A WPS is the *recipe*; a PQR is the *test report*. Never write "WPS is tested" — write "PQR is the test record that supports the WPS."
> 2. **Forgetting units in numerical problems** — Always quote heat input in **kJ/mm**, not J/mm or J/s.
> 3. **Applying the wrong thickness rule to fillet welds** — Butt joints use $0.5t$ to $2t$ rule; fillet welds use the **fillet leg size rule** ($0.5L$ to $1.5L$). Mixing these is a common 2-mark loss.
> 4. **Omitting acceptance values** in test descriptions — Merely naming the test (e.g., "impact test") without giving the **27 J at 0 °C** acceptance value is incomplete. Always quote the number.
> 5. **Confusing 1G/2G/3G/4G/5G/6G positions** — Remember: G refers to plate (Grove); 6G is the universal pipe position. Writing "5G means vertical" is a 1-mark error.
> 6. **Not stating surveyor/class involvement** — For shipbuilding, the PQR must be **witnessed by a classification society surveyor** (LR, DNV, ABS, BV, ClassNK, KR). Omitting this loses a mark on "industry relevance" sub-criteria.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4 Topic**

- **WPS** = the master welding recipe; a *document*.  
- **PQR** = the *test record* that proves the WPS works; permanent record.  
- **WPQ Certificate** = the *personal* certificate proving a welder can use the WPS.  
- **WPQ validates the person; PQR validates the procedure.** Never confuse.  
- **Essential Variable** change ⇒ full re-qualification (re-weld coupon, re-test).  
- **Supplementary Essential Variable** change ⇒ re-impact test only.  
- **Non-Essential Variable** change ⇒ new WPS revision, no re-test.  
- **Thickness Rule (Butt):** $0.5\,t_{test} \;\leq\; t_{prod} \;\leq\; 2\,t_{test}$.  
- **Fillet Leg Rule:** $0.5\,L_{test} \;\leq\; L_{prod} \;\leq\; 1.5\,L_{test}$.  
- **Position Hierarchy:** 6G/5G qualifies ALL positions; 4G excludes 3G & 5G/6G; 3G covers 1G, 2G, 3G.  
- **Heat Input Formula:** $HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S \cdot 1000}$ kJ/mm (with $S$ in mm/s).  
- **Process Efficiencies:** SAW = 1.00, GMAW/FCAW = 0.85, SMAW = 0.80, GTAW = 0.60.  
- **Five Mandatory Tests:** Tensile, Bend (180°/4t mandrel, no crack > 3 mm), Charpy (27 J long / 20 J trans at design T), Macroetch (no defects), Hardness (HV10 ≤ 350 per IACS).  
- **Standards:** AWS D1.1, ASME Section IX, ISO 15614-1, ISO 9606, **IACS UR W17** (the shipbuilding-specific rule).  
- **Welder Certificate Validity:** 2 to 3 years, renewable by re-test; max 6 months inactive service without re-test.  
- **Shipbuilding Impact Test Temperatures:** Grade A = 0 °C, D = -20 °C, E = -40 °C, AH = 0 °C, DH = -20 °C, EH = -40 °C, FH = -60 °C.  
- **Surveyor Witness:** Every PQR for a classed vessel must be witnessed and signed by the **classification society surveyor** (LR / DNV / ABS / BV / ClassNK / KR / RINA).  
- **Higher HI** = coarser HAZ grain, lower toughness, higher risk of softening in TMCP steels.  
- **Lower HI** = finer grain, higher hardness, higher risk of HAZ cold cracking in thick sections.  
- **WPS is "what to do"; PQR is "proof it works"; WPQ is "proof the person can do it."** — Memorise this sentence for the 3-mark definition questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Qualification of Welders and Operators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST831 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Performance Qualification of Welders and Operators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Welder Performance Qualification (WPQ)** is the procedure by which a welder's or welding operator's ability to produce welds that meet prescribed quality standards is verified through standardized test pieces, destructive and/or non-destructive examination, and formal certification, in accordance with codes such as **ASME Section IX**, **ISO 9606-1**, **AWS D1.1**, and **IACS Classification Society Rules** (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, Bureau Veritas).

In shipbuilding — a high-reliability fabrication industry — every welder joining **hull structural members**, **piping systems**, and **critical components** must hold a valid qualification certificate covering the specific **welding process**, **joint type**, **material group**, **thickness range**, and **welding position** applicable to the production work.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The Driver's License Analogy**
> Think of Welder Performance Qualification exactly like a driver's license. A license is not issued for driving "a car in general" — it specifies *what class* of vehicle (car/bus/HCV) you can drive, *where* (city/highway/hill), and *under what conditions*. Similarly, a WPQ certificate is not a generic "welder certificate" — it specifies the *process* (SMAW/GMAW/FCAW), *position* (flat/vertical/overhead), *material* (mild steel/HY-80/stainless), and *thickness range* the welder is approved to work on.

### 1.3 Critical Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **WPQ** | Welder Performance Qualification — the record confirming a welder's skill. |
| **WPS** | Welding Procedure Specification — the "recipe" (parameters, preheat, etc.) to be followed. |
| **PQR** | Procedure Qualification Record — the test record proving the WPS works. |
| **Essential Variable** | A change in welding condition that requires re-qualification. |
| **Range of Qualification** | The set of production welding conditions a single test coupon authorizes. |
| **Test Coupon** | The standardized piece welded by the welder for examination. |

### 1.4 Why Qualification is Non-Negotiable in Shipbuilding

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A ship is a **floating welded structure** subjected to cyclic wave loads, fatigue, and (in naval vessels) potentially hostile threats. Weld defects in **hull plating**, **transverse bulkheads**, **foundations**, or **piping** can cause catastrophic failure. Hence, every individual welder is qualified — not just the company or the WPS.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-Tier Qualification Pyramid

Welding quality assurance in shipbuilding is structured in three interlinked layers:

1. **WPS Qualification (Procedure Level)** — Proves the *procedure* produces a sound weld.
2. **PQR (Test Record)** — The documented test result supporting the WPS.
3. **WPQ (Welder/Operator Level)** — Proves the *individual human* can execute the WPS competently.

> The WPS says "what to do"; the PQR proves "it works"; the WPQ proves "**this person** can actually do it."

### 2.2 Essential Variables Affecting Welder Qualification

A welder is qualified for a *range* of production work. The boundaries of that range are governed by **essential variables**. Changing any of them outside the qualified range **invalidates** the certificate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Essential Variables for Welder Qualification (per ISO 9606-1 / ASME IX):**
> - Welding process (e.g., SMAW ↔ GMAW are NOT interchangeable without separate qualification).
> - Type of joint (butt vs. fillet).
> - Material group (e.g., Group 1 mild steel ↔ Group 8 stainless require new qualification).
> - Plate thickness range.
> - Pipe outside diameter and thickness.
> - Welding position (qualified position progression is restricted).
> - Type of backing (with/without).
> - Fillet weld leg length.
> - For operator qualification: also wire feed speed, travel speed, and machine settings.

### 2.3 Position Designation System

The position code is structured as **digit + letter**:

| Code | Position | Plate / Pipe |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1G / 1F | Flat | Plate butt (G) / Fillet (F) |
| 2G / 2F | Horizontal | Plate |
| 3G / 3F | Vertical up | Plate |
| 4G / 4F | Overhead | Plate |
| 5G / 5F | Fixed pipe horizontal | Pipe (multiple) |
| 6G / 6F | Fixed pipe inclined 45° | Pipe (all positions) |

> **Position Qualification Rule (Plate):** Qualified on **3G** → also qualified for **1G, 2G, 4G**. Qualified on **6G** → qualified for **all pipe positions** including plate equivalents.

### 2.4 Range of Qualification — Thickness Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **For Plate Thickness (per ASME IX QW-202.2 and ISO 9606-1):**
> - Qualified thickness **T** → authorizes production on thicknesses from **0.5 T to 2 T** (ASME IX) or per ISO ranges.
> - **Minimum thickness tested = 6 mm**; below this, the welder is restricted to thin-plate work.
> - Qualified on a butt joint → also qualifies for fillet welds of the same material/process.

### 2.5 KTU Formula / Data Sheet

| Quantity | Symbol / Rule | Unit | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Qualified thickness lower limit | $t_{min} = 0.5\,t_{test}$ | mm | ASME IX rule |
| Qualified thickness upper limit | $t_{max} = 2\,t_{test}$ | mm | ASME IX rule |
| Qualified fillet leg max | $L_{max} = 1.5\,L_{test}$ | mm | Approximate for ISO |
| Pipe diameter qualification | $D_{min} \geq 25$ mm qualifies all larger | mm | ISO 9606-1 |
| Position progression (plate) | $3G \to \lbrace 1G, 2G, 4G \rbrace$ | — | Most restrictive wins |
| Position progression (pipe) | $6G \to$ all | — | Highest qualification |
| Test piece minimum length | $\ell_{min} = 150$ mm (plate) | mm | For bend specimens |
| Bend test mandrel radius | $R = 2\,t$ (root) or $4\,t$ (face) | mm | Per AWS D1.1 |

### 2.6 Examination Methods Applied to Test Coupons

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Test Sequence for Welder Qualification Test Piece:**
> 1. **Visual Examination (VT)** — always first; per **AWS QC1** or **ISO 17637**.
> 2. **Radiographic (RT) per ISO 17636** *or* **Ultrasonic (UT) per ISO 17640** — for butt welds.
> 3. **Bend Tests (per ISO 5173 / AWS B4.0)** — root bend + face bend, transverse.
> 4. **Macro Examination (per ISO 17639)** — optional but used to verify penetration and fusion.
> 5. **Fillet Weld Break Test (per AWS QC1)** — for fillet qualification.
> 6. **Impact (Charpy V-notch per ISO 148)** — only when specified by the contract.

### 2.7 Operator Qualification vs. Welder Qualification

> [!NOTE]
> A **Welding Operator** controls **mechanized or automatic** equipment (e.g., SAW, orbital TIG). A **Welder** performs **manual** welding. Their qualification requirements differ in essential variables because the operator's skill is *equipment control* rather than *torch manipulation*. **ASME IX QW-301** addresses operators separately under **QW-301.2**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Range of Qualification for Plate Thickness

**Problem:** A welder passes a 12 mm thick plate butt-weld qualification test in the 3G (vertical) position using SMAW. Determine the range of production work the welder is authorized to perform.

**Step 1 — Identify governing rule:**

Per **ASME Section IX QW-202.2**, the qualified thickness range for a butt-weld test coupon of thickness $t_{test}$ is:

$$t_{min} = 0.5 \cdot t_{test} \qquad t_{max} = 2 \cdot t_{test}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the test thickness $t_{test} = 12$ mm:**

$$t_{min} = 0.5 \cdot 12 = 6 \text{ mm}$$

$$t_{max} = 2 \cdot 12 = 24 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the position progression rule (plate butt welds):**

A welder qualified in **3G (vertical up)** is automatically qualified for **1G, 2G, and 4G** positions, per **ASME IX QW-202.1**.

**Step 4 — Apply the joint-type extension rule:**

A butt-weld qualification also covers **fillet welds** of the same material group, process, and within the same thickness range (per ASME IX QW-202.3).

**Step 5 — Final authorized production range:**

$$\boxed{\;t \in [6 \text{ mm},\ 24 \text{ mm}],\ \text{positions } 1G, 2G, 3G, 4G,\ \text{butt and fillet joints}\;}$$

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Pipe Qualification Range

**Problem:** A welder qualifies on a 100 mm OD × 8 mm WT pipe in the 6G position. Determine the production range.

**Step 1 — Pipe thickness range per ASME IX QW-202.2:**

$$t_{min} = 0.5 \cdot 8 = 4 \text{ mm}$$

$$t_{max} = 2 \cdot 8 = 16 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Pipe diameter range per ASME IX QW-202.4:**

A welder qualified on pipe of **D ≥ 25 mm** is qualified for **all pipe diameters ≥ 25 mm**. Therefore:

$$D_{min,\ qualified} = 25 \text{ mm},\quad D_{max} = \text{unlimited}$$

**Step 3 — Position rule for pipe 6G:**

The **6G** test (pipe fixed at 45° angle, all positions combined) qualifies the welder for **all pipe positions (1G through 6G)** and also for all plate positions (1G–4G), per **ASME IX QW-202.1**.

**Step 4 — Final range:**

$$\boxed{\;t \in [4,\ 16]\ \text{mm},\ D \in [25,\ \infty)\ \text{mm},\ \text{all pipe + plate positions}\;}$$

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Bend Test Acceptance Criterion

**Problem:** A root-bend specimen of thickness $t = 10$ mm is bent over a mandrel of radius $R$. Per **AWS D1.1 Clause 4.10**, the required mandrel radius for a root bend on a mild-steel test plate is $R = 2t$. Compute $R$ and check acceptance if the bent specimen shows no crack > 3 mm.

**Step 1 — Compute mandrel radius:**

$$R = 2 \cdot t = 2 \cdot 10 = 20 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Compute total bend angle required:**

$$\theta_{bend} = 180°\ \text{(full flattening)}$$

**Step 3 — Surface crack acceptance (per AWS D1.1 4.10.3.2):**

$$\text{Crack}_{\max} = 3 \text{ mm (any direction)}$$

If **observed crack** < 3 mm → **ACCEPT**.
If **observed crack** ≥ 3 mm → **REJECT** → welder fails qualification.

**Step 4 — Decision rule:**

$$\text{Result} = \begin{cases} \text{ACCEPT} & \text{if } c_{obs} < 3 \text{ mm} \\ \text{REJECT} & \text{if } c_{obs} \geq 3 \text{ mm} \end{cases}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When Re-Qualification is Required

| Change in Production Condition | Re-Qualification Required? | Code Reference |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Different welding process (e.g., SMAW → GMAW) | **YES** | ASME IX QW-200.1 |
| Different material group (e.g., carbon → stainless) | **YES** | ASME IX QW-200.2 |
| Plate thickness outside [0.5 T, 2 T] | **YES** | ASME IX QW-202.2 |
| Position outside qualified set | **YES** | ASME IX QW-202.1 |
| Pipe diameter reduction below 25 mm (from large pipe) | **YES** | ASME IX QW-202.4 |
| Change from mechanized to fully manual | **YES (operator level)** | ASME IX QW-301 |
| Same WPS, same welder, repeat production | **NO** | Within validity period |
| Long break in welding (> 6 months per IACS UR W) | **YES (re-test)** | IACS UR W22 |

### 3.5 Validity Period and Renewal

> [!NOTE]
> Per **IACS Unified Requirements UR W22** and most classification societies:
> - A welder's qualification is valid for **6 months of continuous active work** on the same process/material, OR
> - Must be **re-tested within 6 months** of last witnessed production weld if no work performed.
> - A **continuity record** (logbook) must be maintained by the company.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Welder Qualification Workflow (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Welder Training Completed] --> B{Process and Material Identified}
    B --> C[Prepare Test Coupon per WPS]
    C --> D[Welder Welds Coupon Under Examiner Witness]
    D --> E[Visual Examination VT]
    E --> F{Visual Pass?}
    F -- No --> G[REJECT Welder]
    F -- Yes --> H[Radiographic RT or Ultrasonic UT]
    H --> I{NDT Pass?}
    I -- No --> G
    I -- Yes --> J[Mechanical Tests - Bend and Tensile]
    J --> K{Mechanical Pass?}
    K -- No --> G
    K -- Yes --> L[Macro Examination Optional]
    L --> M[Compile WPQ Certificate]
    M --> N[Issue Range of Qualification]
    N --> O[Authorize for Production Welding]
    O --> P[Maintain 6-Month Continuity Log]
    P --> Q{Continuous Work in 6 Months?}
    Q -- Yes --> O
    Q -- No --> R[Re-qualification Test Required]
    R --> C
```

### 4.2 Position Qualification Progression (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[1G Flat Plate] --> P3[3G Vertical Up Plate]
    P2[2G Horizontal Plate] --> P3
    P4[4G Overhead Plate] --> P3
    P3 --> P5[5G Pipe Fixed Horizontal]
    P5 --> P6[6G Pipe Fixed Inclined 45 degrees]
    P6 --> PALL[ALL Positions Authorized]
    P6 --> PLATE[All Plate Positions Also Authorized]
```

### 4.3 Range of Qualification Logic Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T[Test Coupon Thickness T] --> T1[Lower Limit 0.5 T]
    T --> T2[Upper Limit 2 T]
    D[Test Pipe Diameter D] --> D1[If D greater than 25 mm - Qualifies All D]
    D --> D2[If D less than 25 mm - Limited Range]
    L[Test Fillet Leg L] --> L1[Authorizes L up to 1.5 L]
    POS[Test Position] --> POS1[Plate 3G Qualifies 1G 2G 4G]
    POS --> POS2[Pipe 6G Qualifies All Pipe and Plate Positions]
```

### 4.4 Examination Test Hierarchy Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    EX[Test Coupon Examinations] --> EX1[Visual VT per ISO 17637]
    EX1 --> EX2[NDT Selection]
    EX2 --> EX2A[Radiographic RT ISO 17636]
    EX2 --> EX2B[Ultrasonic UT ISO 17640]
    EX2 --> EX2C[Magnetic MT ISO 17638]
    EX2 --> EX2D[Penetrant PT ISO 3452-1]
    EX2A --> EX3[Mechanical Tests]
    EX2B --> EX3
    EX2C --> EX3
    EX2D --> EX3
    EX3 --> EX3A[Transverse Tensile]
    EX3 --> EX3B[Root and Face Bend]
    EX3 --> EX3C[Charpy Impact when specified]
    EX3 --> EX3D[Macro Examination ISO 17639]
    EX3A --> FINAL[Pass or Fail Decision]
    EX3B --> FINAL
    EX3C --> FINAL
    EX3D --> FINAL
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define Welder Performance Qualification (WPQ). Why is it mandatory in shipbuilding rather than relying solely on a WPS?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Welder Performance Qualification is the formal process of testing and certifying an individual welder's ability to produce sound welds on a standard test coupon, in accordance with codes like ASME Section IX or ISO 9606-1. (1 mark)

In shipbuilding, hull welds are subjected to **cyclic wave loading**, **fatigue**, and **dynamic stresses** throughout the vessel's life. (1 mark)

A WPS only proves the *procedure* is sound, not that the *human operator* can consistently execute it. Hence, a defective weld by an unskilled welder — even under a perfect WPS — could cause **catastrophic hull failure**. Therefore, individual welder qualification is mandatory. (1 mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*State the difference between a Welding Operator and a Welder in the context of qualification under ASME Section IX.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Welder** performs *manual* welding using hand-held torches and depends on hand-eye coordination, torch angle, and travel speed control. Their qualification is governed by **ASME IX QW-301.1**. (1.5 marks)

A **Welding Operator** operates *mechanized, automatic, or semi-automatic* equipment (e.g., SAW, orbital TIG, robotic welders). Their skill is equipment control, and qualification is governed by **ASME IX QW-301.2**, which lists different essential variables such as wire feed speed and machine settings. (1.5 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

**QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Style]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **essential variables** that govern the validity of a welder's qualification under ASME Section IX. How does a change in each variable affect the range of qualification? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Essential variables are those welding-condition changes that invalidate an existing welder qualification. Under **ASME Section IX QW-200** and **QW-202**, the following essential variables apply:

1. **Welding Process (QW-200.1):** A welder qualified for SMAW is *not* automatically qualified for GMAW, FCAW, or GTAW. Each process requires its own test coupon. Sub-processes within a family (e.g., pulse variants) may be grouped per the code. *(1 mark)*

2. **Base Metal Group (QW-200.2 / QW-403):** Material groups are classified (Group 1 carbon steel, Group 2 low alloy, etc.). A change in material group requires re-qualification. A welder qualified on mild steel cannot weld stainless without a new test. *(1 mark)*

3. **Joint Type (QW-200.3 / QW-202.3):** A *butt-weld* qualification covers *fillet welds* of the same process/material. However, the converse is *not* true — a fillet-only qualification does not cover butt welds. *(1 mark)*

4. **Plate Thickness (QW-202.2):** If the test coupon is thickness T, the welder is qualified for production thickness in the range $[0.5T,\ 2T]$. Welds outside this range require re-test. *(1 mark)*

5. **Pipe Diameter (QW-202.4):** A test on pipe of D ≥ 25 mm qualifies for all pipe diameters ≥ 25 mm. Tests on smaller pipes have restricted ranges. *(1 mark)*

6. **Welding Position (QW-202.1):** Position qualification is *progressive* — a stricter test authorizes less-strict positions but not vice versa. 6G qualifies all; 3G qualifies 1G, 2G, 4G only. *(1 mark)*

7. **Backing, Fillet Leg Length, and Others:** Use of backing vs. no-backing, and tested fillet leg length determine fillet weld authority. *(1 mark)*

**Effect on Range of Qualification:** Any change in an essential variable *outside* the qualified envelope **invalidates** the existing WPQ and mandates a fresh test coupon, examination, and certificate before the welder resumes production on the changed condition.

---

**(b)** A welder qualifies on a **16 mm thick** plate butt-weld test coupon in the **3G position** using **SMAW**. Determine the **range of authorized production work** in terms of thickness, position, and joint type. Apply ASME Section IX QW-202 rules. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Thickness Range (QW-202.2):**

$$t_{min} = 0.5 \times 16 = 8 \text{ mm}$$

$$t_{max} = 2 \times 16 = 32 \text{ mm}$$

$$\text{Authorized thickness: } t \in [8,\ 32]\ \text{mm} \quad (2 \text{ marks})$$

**Step 2 — Position Range (QW-202.1):**

A **3G (vertical up)** plate butt-weld qualification automatically authorizes:

- 1G (flat)
- 2G (horizontal)
- 3G (vertical up)
- 4G (overhead)

*(2 marks)*

**Step 3 — Joint Type (QW-202.3):**

A butt-weld qualification also covers **fillet welds** of the same process, material, and within the same thickness range. So the welder is authorized for **both butt and fillet joints** on plate.

*(1 mark)*

**Step 4 — Material and Process Scope:**

Valid only for **SMAW** (process unchanged) and **Group 1 carbon steel** (material unchanged) — a change to GMAW or to stainless would require a *new* qualification.

*(1 mark)*

**Step 5 — Validity Period (IACS UR W22):**

The certificate is valid provided the welder performs **continuous work** on the same process and material group, with a **6-month continuity log**. Otherwise, re-test is required.

*(1 mark)*

**Final Summary:**

$$\boxed{\;\text{SMAW},\ t \in [8, 32]\ \text{mm},\ \text{plate positions 1G, 2G, 3G, 4G},\ \text{butt and fillet joints},\ \text{Group 1 CS}\;}$$

---

**QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - May 2023 Style]**

**(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the **various welding positions** used in plate and pipe welding under **ASME Section IX / ISO 9606** classification. Explain the **position progression rule** (i.e., which test positions qualify which production positions). **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Welding Position Designation (ASME IX / ISO 9606-1):**

Positions are coded as **digit + G (groove/butt) or F (fillet)**.

**Plate Positions:**

| Code | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| 1G / 1F | Flat — weld face up, plate horizontal |
| 2G / 2F | Horizontal — plate vertical, weld horizontal |
| 3G / 3F | Vertical — plate vertical, weld vertical (up or down) |
| 4G / 4F | Overhead — weld face down |

*(2 marks)*

**Pipe Positions:**

| Code | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| 1G / 1F | Pipe rolled, weld flat (rotated) |
| 2G / 2F | Pipe fixed, weld horizontal |
| 5G / 5F | Pipe fixed horizontal axis — all positions around the pipe |
| 6G / 6F | Pipe fixed at 45° inclination — all positions (most stringent) |

*(2 marks)*

**Position Progression Rule (QW-202.1 / ISO 9606):**

For **plate:**
- 3G → authorizes 1G, 2G, 3G, 4G
- 4G → authorizes 1G, 2G, 3G, 4G
- 2G → authorizes 1G, 2G only
- 1G → authorizes 1G only

For **pipe:**
- 6G → authorizes **all pipe positions** and **all plate positions**
- 5G → authorizes 5G and 6G
- 2G (pipe) → authorizes 1G and 2G pipe only

*(2 marks)*

**Key Rule:** A welder qualified on a *more difficult* position is automatically qualified for *less difficult* positions, but **not vice versa**. Thus, the 6G pipe test is the most comprehensive single qualification.

*(1 mark)*

---

**(b)** A welder has qualified on a **6G pipe test** with **OD = 200 mm** and **WT = 10 mm** using **GMAW** on **stainless steel (Group 8)**. He applies for a job involving **GMAW on stainless plate, 12 mm thick, in vertical position**. Is he qualified? Justify your answer by applying the relevant range-of-qualification rules. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Material Group Check:**

Tested on **Group 8 (stainless steel)** → Production is also **stainless steel Group 8**. ✅ **Material group OK.** *(1 mark)*

**Step 2 — Process Check:**

Tested on **GMAW** → Production is **GMAW**. ✅ **Process OK.** *(1 mark)*

**Step 3 — Position Check (QW-202.1):**

Tested on **6G (pipe, all positions, fixed 45°)** → 6G is the most comprehensive test. Per ASME IX QW-202.1, 6G qualifies the welder for **all pipe positions AND all plate positions** (1G, 2G, 3G, 4G). The production is **3G plate (vertical)**. ✅ **Position OK.** *(2 marks)*

**Step 4 — Thickness Check (QW-202.2):**

For pipe tests, ASME IX QW-202.2 authorizes production on plate within:

$$t \in [0.5 t_{test},\ 2 t_{test}] = [0.5 \times 10,\ 2 \times 10] = [5 \text{ mm},\ 20 \text{ mm}]$$

Production plate thickness = **12 mm** ∈ [5, 20]. ✅ **Thickness OK.** *(2 marks)*

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**

$$\boxed{\;\text{The welder IS qualified for the job — all essential variables are within the authorized range.}\;}$$

*(1 mark for conclusion)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing WPQ with PQR/WPS** — students often merge the three. WPQ = welder's skill; PQR = procedure's soundness; WPS = the procedure document.
> 2. **Reversing the position progression rule** — claiming 1G qualifies 6G. It does NOT.
> 3. **Forgetting process change requires re-qualification** — a common 2-mark loss in Part A.
> 4. **Ignoring the 6-month continuity rule** — examiners specifically test IACS UR W22 in 14-mark questions.
> 5. **Not citing the exact code clause** (e.g., QW-202.2) — KTU examiners award partial marks for precise references.
> 6. **Calculating thickness range incorrectly** — using T or 3T instead of [0.5T, 2T].

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **WPQ certifies the human; PQR certifies the procedure; WPS is the recipe.** Never confuse them.
- **Essential variables** include: process, material group, joint type, thickness, position, backing, and (for operators) machine settings.
- **Thickness rule:** $t \in [0.5\,T_{test},\ 2\,T_{test}]$.
- **Position progression (plate):** 3G → 1G, 2G, 3G, 4G. (4G also qualifies all four plate positions.)
- **Position progression (pipe):** 6G → all pipe + all plate positions.
- **Pipe diameter rule:** Test on D ≥ 25 mm → qualifies all pipe diameters ≥ 25 mm.
- **Test coupon is always welded by the candidate welder** (not by a sample welder) under examiner witness.
- **Visual + NDT + Mechanical tests** are all mandatory; passing one does not waive the others.
- **Bend test mandrel radius:** $R = 2t$ (root/face) for mild steel per AWS D1.1.
- **Validity period (IACS UR W22):** 6 months of continuous work; otherwise re-test.
- **Operator vs. Welder** distinction is critical — different essential variables.
- **IACS members** (ABS, DNV, LR, BV, RINA, KR, CCS, IRS) accept each other's qualifications under **IACS Unified Requirements**.
- **Documentation trail:** Welder Logbook → Continuity Record → WPQ Certificate → Range of Qualification attached to each welder's ID badge.
- **Shipbuilding-specific:** Hull structural welds require the *highest* qualification level (typically 6G for pipe and 3G/4G for plate).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Test Reports
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST831 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Test Reports

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Test Report** is a formally documented, traceable, and authenticated engineering record that consolidates the **qualitative and quantitative results** of mechanical, chemical, non-destructive, and metallurgical tests performed on welded joints, base metals, filler metals, and weld procedure qualification test (PQT) coupons. It serves as the legally and contractually binding evidence of compliance with classification society rules (IACS members: ABS, DNV, LR, BV, KR), ISO 3834 quality requirements, and shipbuilding standards (AWS D1.1, ASME Section IX, ISO 15614-1).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, a *Test Report* is the **final output artefact** of the entire welding quality assurance chain — it transforms raw testing data (load values, deflection, hardness numbers, radiograph densities) into a controlled, sign-off document used by surveyors, classification society auditors, and ship-owner representatives.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Weld's Medical Report":**
> Imagine a human being asked to undergo a complete health check-up before being cleared for a critical job (say, a deep-sea diver). The diver must produce a *medical fitness report* signed by a certified doctor, containing blood test values, ECG readings, X-ray plates, and a final verdict. If any one of those is missing or abnormal, the diver is rejected.
>
> A **weld test report** plays the *exact same role* for a welded joint in a ship hull, pressure vessel, or propeller bracket. The **welded joint = the diver**, the **testing laboratory = the hospital**, the **classification surveyor = the doctor**, and the **test report = the signed fitness certificate**. Without this certificate, the welded structure **cannot legally enter service**.

## 1.3 Why Test Reports Are Critical in Shipbuilding

| Function of Test Report | Engineering Consequence if Missing |
|---|---|
| Records weld procedure qualification (WPQR) | No authorisation to use the welding procedure onboard |
| Documents mechanical properties (UTS, YS, %E) | Cannot prove joint strength exceeds design stress |
| Captures impact toughness (Charpy/J) | Brittle fracture risk in Arctic service unverified |
| Logs NDT acceptance (RT/UT/MT/PT) | Hidden defects may propagate → catastrophic hull failure |
| Certifies chemical composition of weld deposit | Corrosion resistance, weldability, and grade traceability lost |
| Records hardness survey (PWHT compliance) | Hydrogen cracking / stress corrosion cracking risk unassessed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bold Standard Reference:** Under **IACS UR Z17 (Procedural Requirements for Service Suppliers)** and **ISO 17025**, all mechanical and chemical test reports issued for marine welded structures **must** be endorsed by an ISO 17025-accredited laboratory and bear the **ILAC-MRA** traceability mark for international recognition.

## 1.4 Categories of Test Reports Encountered in Ship Welding

1. **Weld Procedure Qualification Test (PQT / WPQR) Report**
2. **Welder Performance Qualification (WPQ) Test Report**
3. **Production Weld Test Report (Mechanical + Chemical)**
4. **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) Evaluation Report**
5. **Weld Repair Test Report**
6. **First-Article / Prototype Weld Inspection Report**
7. **Production Performance Test (PPT) Report**
8. **Filler Metal Batch Test / Certificate of Conformance Report**
9. **PWHT (Post-Weld Heat Treatment) Verification Report**
10. **Hull Block / Erection Stage Test Report**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Standard Weld Test Report — Hierarchical Logic

A KTU/IACS-grade test report follows a strict 10-tier logical structure. The **Why** and **How** of each tier is given below:

- **Tier 1 — Project & Reference Metadata**
  * *Why:* Anchors the report to a specific ship hull number, block, and contract.
  * *How:* Records shipyard name, project ID, hull number, drawing reference, WPS number, and applicable code (e.g., IACS UR S21, S22).

- **Tier 2 — Base Metal & Filler Metal Identification**
  * *Why:* Traceability from heat number to mechanical result.
  * *How:* Includes base metal grade (e.g., AH36, DH36, EH36), plate thickness, batch/lot number, and filler metal classification (e.g., AWS E7018, ER70S-6).

- **Tier 3 — Welding Process & Procedure Parameters**
  * *Why:* Allows reconstruction of *how* the test weld was made.
  * *How:* Lists welding process (SMAW, GMAW, FCAW, SAW), current type, voltage, travel speed, heat input, interpass temperature, preheat, and PWHT cycle.

- **Tier 4 — Specimen Extraction Plan & Geometry**
  * *Why:* Mechanical properties depend on specimen location (top, mid, root).
  * *How:* Diagram of coupon with extract locations, dimensions per ISO 4136 / ISO 5173 / AWS B4.0.

- **Tier 5 — Test Method & Standard Reference**
  * *Why:* Audits the *how* of testing.
  * *How:* E.g., "Tensile testing per **ISO 6892-1:2019**", "Charpy V-notch per **ISO 148-1:2016**".

- **Tier 6 — Raw Test Data Tabulation**
  * *Why:* Provides auditable, re-checkable numerical evidence.
  * *How:* Load vs extension tables, dial gauge readings, absorbed energy values in joules.

- **Tier 7 — Calculated Property Values**
  * *Why:* Converts raw data into design-usable parameters.
  * *How:* UTS, YS, %Elongation, %RA (Reduction of Area), Charpy energy, hardness HV/HRC.

- **Tier 8 — Acceptance Verdict**
  * *Why:* Single-line contractual YES/NO decision.
  * *How:* "PASS / FAIL" with reference to acceptance criteria clause number.

- **Tier 9 — Non-Conformance & Deviation Notes**
  * *Why:* Honest engineering documentation of anomalies.
  * *How:* Any deviation from WPS, retest requirement, or failed specimen with corrective action.

- **Tier 10 — Authorisation Chain (Sign-off Block)**
  * *Why:* Establishes legal accountability.
  * *How:* Signatures of Lab Technician → QC Engineer → Welding Engineer → Classification Surveyor (third-party verification).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **every formula a KTU student must memorise** for solving numerical/analytical questions on Test Reports in Module 4. All symbols are mathematically standard.

| # | Property | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Standard | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS) | $\sigma_u = \dfrac{F_{max}}{A_0}$ | $F_{max}$ = max load, $A_0$ = original cross-section | ISO 6892-1 | MPa (N/mm²) |
| 2 | Yield Strength (Upper / Lower) | $\sigma_y = \dfrac{F_y}{A_0}$ | $F_y$ = load at yield (offset method) | ISO 6892-1 | MPa |
| 3 | % Elongation | $\%E = \dfrac{L_u - L_0}{L_0} \times 100$ | $L_u$ = final gauge length, $L_0$ = original gauge | ISO 6892-1 | % |
| 4 | % Reduction in Area | $\%RA = \dfrac{A_0 - A_u}{A_0} \times 100$ | $A_u$ = final cross-section at fracture | ISO 6892-1 | % |
| 5 | Charpy Impact Energy | $KV_2$ (direct) | Energy absorbed at $V$-notch | ISO 148-1 | Joules (J) |
| 6 | Lateral Expansion | $LE = t_f - t_i$ | Thickness at fracture − original | ASTM E23 | mm |
| 7 | Vickers Hardness (HV) | $HV = 0.1891 \cdot \dfrac{F}{d^2}$ | $F$ in N, $d$ = diagonal mean in mm | ISO 6507-1 | dimensionless |
| 8 | Brinell Hardness (HB) | $HB = 0.012 \cdot \dfrac{F}{\pi D h}$ | $F$ in N, $D$ = ball dia, $h$ = depth | ISO 6506-1 | dimensionless |
| 9 | Heat Input (per unit length) | $E = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \times 1000}$ | $\eta$ = arc efficiency, $V$ = voltage, $I$ = current, $v$ = travel speed (mm/s) | AWS / EN 1011 | kJ/mm |
| 10 | Cooling Time $t_{8/5}$ | $t_{8/5} = \dfrac{E}{c \cdot \rho} \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{500-T_0} - \dfrac{1}{800-T_0}\right)$ | $c$ = specific heat, $\rho$ = density, $T_0$ = preheat | EN 1011-2 | seconds |
| 11 | Bend Test Acceptance | $D \leq 4t$ (transverse) or $D \leq 2t$ (longitudinal root) | $D$ = mandrel dia, $t$ = specimen thickness | ISO 5173 | mm |
| 12 | Macro-etch Acceptance | No defect > 3 mm or 10% t | Per IACS UR S21 | IACS | mm / % |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility of the Test Report System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Test Report is **not just paperwork** — it is the legal artefact that:
> (i) Transfers liability from shipyard to classification society upon survey release,
> (ii) Enables **forensic root-cause analysis** if a structural failure occurs 5–20 years post-delivery,
> (iii) Provides the **chain of custody** required by marine insurance underwriters (e.g., Lloyd's, Gard, UK P&I),
> (iv) Acts as the **legal evidence** in maritime arbitration under Lloyd's Standard Salvage and Repair Clauses.

In modern **Industry 4.0 shipyards** (e.g., HD Hyundai Heavy Industries, Samsung Heavy Industries, Mazagon Dock), test reports are now generated digitally in **MES (Manufacturing Execution Systems)** with cryptographic hash signatures for tamper-proof archival.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Ultimate Tensile Strength From Test Report Raw Data

**Problem Setup (typical KTU-style numerical):**
A transverse tensile specimen extracted from a butt-weld test coupon of a Grade-A hull plate has:
- Original diameter: $d_0 = 12.5 \text{ mm}$
- Original cross-section area: $A_0 = \pi \cdot d_0^2 / 4$
- Maximum load recorded: $F_{max} = 92.4 \text{ kN}$
- Final diameter at fracture: $d_u = 8.20 \text{ mm}$
- Original gauge length: $L_0 = 50 \text{ mm}$
- Final gauge length after fracture: $L_u = 63.5 \text{ mm}$

Required: Calculate UTS, %Elongation, %RA, and verdict against ABS Rule 2: Part 2 (min UTS = 400 MPa for Grade A).

### Step 1 — Compute Original Cross-Sectional Area

$$
A_0 = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d_0^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot (12.5)^2}{4}
$$

$$
A_0 = \dfrac{\pi \cdot 156.25}{4} = \dfrac{490.8739}{4} = 122.7185 \text{ mm}^2
$$

*Logic:* This is the *engineering* cross-section (not true stress), used universally in code-based UTS reporting.

### Step 2 — Compute Ultimate Tensile Strength

$$
\sigma_u = \dfrac{F_{max}}{A_0} = \dfrac{92{,}400 \text{ N}}{122.7185 \text{ mm}^2}
$$

$$
\sigma_u = 752.98 \text{ N/mm}^2 \equiv 752.98 \text{ MPa}
$$

*Logic:* UTS = maximum load divided by **original** (not fracture) area. This is *engineering stress*, per ISO 6892-1 convention.

### Step 3 — Compute Percentage Elongation

$$
\%E = \dfrac{L_u - L_0}{L_0} \times 100 = \dfrac{63.5 - 50}{50} \times 100
$$

$$
\%E = \dfrac{13.5}{50} \times 100 = 27.0\%
$$

*Logic:* Ductility indicator. Higher = more ductile weld. ABS Grade A requires minimum **22%** for plates ≤ 50 mm thick → **PASS**.

### Step 4 — Compute Final Fracture Area

$$
A_u = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d_u^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot (8.20)^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot 67.24}{4} = 52.81 \text{ mm}^2
$$

### Step 5 — Compute Percentage Reduction in Area

$$
\%RA = \dfrac{A_0 - A_u}{A_0} \times 100 = \dfrac{122.72 - 52.81}{122.72} \times 100
$$

$$
\%RA = \dfrac{69.91}{122.72} \times 100 = 56.97\%
$$

*Logic:* %RA is a sensitive indicator of weld metal soundness; a low value with high UTS may indicate brittle failure or porosity clusters.

### Step 6 — Verdict Entry in Test Report

$$
\text{Verdict} = \begin{cases} \text{PASS}, & \sigma_u \geq 400 \text{ MPa} \quad \text{AND} \quad \%E \geq 22\% \\ \text{FAIL}, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

Since $752.98 \text{ MPa} > 400 \text{ MPa}$ and $27.0\% > 22\%$, the report is stamped **PASS — ACCEPTED**.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Charpy Impact Energy Verdict for a 27J Class Requirement

**Problem Setup:**
Three Charpy V-notch specimens taken from a weld heat-affected zone (HAZ) at 0 °C gave absorbed energies of 38 J, 52 J, and 24 J. IACS UR S22 requires an average of 27 J and a minimum of 19 J for any single specimen.

### Step 1 — Compute Arithmetic Mean

$$
\overline{KV} = \dfrac{KV_1 + KV_2 + KV_3}{3} = \dfrac{38 + 52 + 24}{3} = \dfrac{114}{3} = 38.0 \text{ J}
$$

*Logic:* The mean is the primary acceptance criterion per IACS.

### Step 2 — Compare Mean to Specified Minimum

$$
\overline{KV} = 38.0 \text{ J} \quad \text{vs.} \quad KV_{spec,min} = 27 \text{ J}
$$

$$
38.0 \text{ J} > 27 \text{ J} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Mean criterion: PASS}
$$

### Step 3 — Check Single-Specimen Minimum (Worst-Value Rule)

$$
KV_{min,individual} = \min(38, 52, 24) = 24 \text{ J}
$$

$$
24 \text{ J} \geq 19 \text{ J} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Single-specimen criterion: PASS}
$$

### Step 4 — Final Verdict

$$
\text{Verdict} = \text{ALL CRITERIA MET} \Rightarrow \text{Report stamped: PASS}
$$

---

## 3.3 Heat Input Calculation from Test Report WPS Data

**Problem Setup:**
A SAW (Submerged Arc Welding) operation has:
- Arc voltage: $V = 32 \text{ V}$
- Current: $I = 620 \text{ A}$
- Travel speed: $v = 480 \text{ mm/min} = 8.0 \text{ mm/s}$
- Process efficiency for SAW: $\eta = 1.0$ (per AWS D1.1 Annex K for SAW with DC)

### Step 1 — Apply Heat Input Formula

$$
E = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \times 1000}
$$

*Logic:* The factor 1000 converts W·s/mm → kJ/mm.

### Step 2 — Substitute Values

$$
E = \dfrac{1.0 \times 32 \times 620}{8.0 \times 1000} = \dfrac{19{,}840}{8000} = 2.48 \text{ kJ/mm}
$$

*Logic:* This heat input is within the maximum allowed (e.g., 3.0 kJ/mm for DH36 plate) to avoid excessive HAZ grain coarsening.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Test Type vs. Required Report Content

| Test Conducted | Standard | Mandatory Fields in Report | Acceptance Authority |
|---|---|---|---|
| Transverse Tensile | ISO 4136 | UTS, %E, fracture location | Classification Society |
| All-Weld-Metal Tensile | ISO 6892-1 | UTS, YS, %E, %RA | Welding Engineer |
| Charpy V-Notch | ISO 148-1 | KV₂, lateral exp., temp | Classification Society |
| Bend (Root/Face/Side) | ISO 5173 | Crack length, mandrel dia | Welding Engineer + Surveyor |
| Hardness Survey | ISO 6507-1 | HV10 grid values, locations | Welding Engineer |
| Macro Examination | ISO 17639 | Etch profile, defect depths | QC Engineer |
| Chemical Analysis | ISO 14284 | C, Mn, Si, S, P, CEV | Mill / Supplier Lab |
| RT / UT / MT / PT | ISO 9712 | Indication size, location, grade | NDT Level III + Surveyor |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Test Report Generation Workflow

The Mermaid diagram below illustrates the end-to-end data flow that produces a shipbuilding weld Test Report, from initial welding input to final surveyor sign-off.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[WPS Document] --> B1[WPQR Test Coupon]
    A2[Welder Qualification Card] --> B2[WPQ Test Coupon]
    A3[Production Weld] --> B3[Production Test Coupon]
    A4[NDT Coupon] --> B4[NDT Evaluation]

    B1 --> C1[Mechanical Tests]
    B1 --> C2[Chemical Tests]
    B1 --> C3[Macro Etch Test]
    B1 --> C4[Hardness Survey]

    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    B3 --> C2

    C1 --> D1[Raw Data Tabulation]
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
    C4 --> D1
    B4 --> D1

    D1 --> E1[Calculated Property Sheet]
    E1 --> F1[Acceptance Verdict Engine]

    F1 --> G1{Verdict Status}
    G1 -- PASS --> H1[Report Stamped PASS]
    G1 -- FAIL --> H2[Non-Conformance Report Issued]
    H2 --> I1[Retest or Repair Sequence]
    I1 --> A3

    H1 --> J1[QC Engineer Sign-Off]
    J1 --> K1[Classification Surveyor Endorsement]
    K1 --> L1[Filed in Hull Quality Dossier]
    L1 --> M1[Legal Maritime Record Archive]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Information Architecture of a Single Test Report Document

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Project Metadata] --> Q1
    P2[WPS Reference] --> Q1
    P3[Base / Filler Metal] --> Q1
    P4[Process Parameters] --> Q1
    P5[Specimen Diagram] --> Q1
    P6[Raw Test Data] --> Q1
    P7[Calculated Properties] --> Q1
    P8[Acceptance Verdict] --> Q1
    P9[Non-Conformance Notes] --> Q1
    P10[Signatures] --> Q1

    Q1[Final Test Report PDF] --> R1[Class Society Database]
    Q1 --> R2[Shipyard MES]
    Q1 --> R3[Owner Repository]
```

## 4.3 Test Report Lifecycle State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Draft
    Draft --> UnderReview
    UnderReview --> Approved : PASS
    UnderReview --> Rejected : FAIL
    Rejected --> Rework
    Rework --> UnderReview
    Approved --> Archived
    Archived --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip:** The `[*]` nodes denote the start and end of a state machine. Mermaid state diagrams help in visualising the regulatory approval cycle of a Test Report in a classification society workflow.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**
> List **eight mandatory fields** that must appear on a Procedure Qualification Test (PQT) report for a butt-welded joint in a Grade DH36 hull plate.

**Model Answer (8 fields, briefly explained):**
1. **Project / Ship Identification** — Hull number, block number, contract reference.
2. **WPS Reference Number** — Unique traceable ID of the Welding Procedure Specification used.
3. **Base Metal & Filler Metal Details** — Grade, thickness, batch/heat number, AWS/ISO classification.
4. **Welding Process Parameters** — Process type, current, voltage, travel speed, heat input, preheat, interpass temperature.
5. **Specimen Extraction Diagram** — Drawing showing location, orientation, and dimensions of all test specimens.
6. **Test Standards Used** — E.g., ISO 6892-1 for tensile, ISO 148-1 for Charpy, ISO 5173 for bend.
7. **Test Results Table** — UTS, YS, %E, %RA, Charpy values, hardness values, macro-etch results.
8. **Acceptance Verdict and Signatures** — PASS/FAIL statement with signatures of Lab Technician, QC Engineer, Welding Engineer, and Classification Surveyor.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**
> Differentiate between a **WPQR (Welding Procedure Qualification Record)** and a **Production Weld Test Report**. State the governing standard for each.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | WPQR / WPQT Report | Production Weld Test Report |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Qualifies the *procedure* (WPS) before production | Verifies *actual production welds* are meeting the WPS |
| **Frequency** | Once per new procedure or essential variable change | Periodically (e.g., every 50 joints) or per block |
| **Governing Standard** | **ISO 15614-1** (or ASME Section IX) | **IACS UR S21 / S22**, **ISO 3834**, and shipyard QA plan |
| **Test Scope** | Full mechanical + chemical + macro + hardness | Reduced scope (often just tensile + macro + NDT) |
| **Signatory** | Independent third-party witness | Yard QC + Surveyor (routine release) |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> - Students frequently **omit the specimen location diagram** — this alone costs 2 marks.
> - They confuse **%Elongation (in 50 mm gauge)** with **%Reduction in Area** — read the question carefully.
> - They forget to **state the acceptance criteria clause number** when giving a PASS/FAIL verdict — the examiner expects it.
> - They use **incorrect units** (e.g., N/mm² instead of MPa, or omit units entirely).
> - They **forget to state the test standard reference** (ISO 6892-1, ISO 148-1) — this is a recurring 1-mark loss.

---

### Question Choice A (14 Marks) — *Theory + Sketch Based*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** With the help of a **labelled block diagram**, describe the **information architecture** of a shipbuilding weld Test Report. Enumerate the **ten mandatory tiers** and explain the engineering rationale for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain in detail the role of **WPQR, WPS, and WPQ** documents in the chain of welding qualification. State the relevant ISO standard for each and the conditions under which requalification becomes mandatory. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

A weld Test Report functions as the **definitive quality dossier** for a welded joint. Its information architecture must be tiered to ensure traceability, auditability, and regulatory compliance. The ten mandatory tiers are:

1. **Project Metadata** — Establishes the identity of the build (hull number, block, contract, drawing reference). *Why:* Without this anchor, the report is legally orphan.
2. **Base Metal & Filler Metal Identification** — Records the exact plate grade (e.g., AH36), thickness, heat number, and filler metal classification (e.g., AWS E7018-1 H4R). *Why:* Traceability is required for forensic failure analysis.
3. **Welding Process & Parameters** — Process type, polarity, current, voltage, travel speed, heat input, preheat, interpass, and PWHT cycle. *Why:* Allows reconstruction and replication of the procedure.
4. **Specimen Extraction Plan & Geometry** — Diagram and dimensions of each test specimen (transverse tensile, all-weld-metal tensile, Charpy, bend, macro). *Why:* Mechanical properties are location-dependent in welds.
5. **Test Method & Standard** — The governing code (e.g., ISO 6892-1, ISO 148-1, ISO 5173, ISO 6507-1). *Why:* Auditability of test methodology.
6. **Raw Test Data** — Load–extension tables, dial gauge readings, absorbed energy values. *Why:* Allows independent recalculation by the surveyor.
7. **Calculated Property Values** — Derived parameters: UTS, YS, %E, %RA, KV₂, HV. *Why:* Convert raw data into design-usable properties.
8. **Acceptance Verdict** — PASS/FAIL with explicit clause reference. *Why:* Contractual decision point.
9. **Non-Conformance Notes** — Any deviation, retest, or anomaly. *Why:* Honest engineering documentation and continuous improvement.
10. **Authorisation Chain** — Signatures of lab, QC, welding engineer, and classification surveyor. *Why:* Establishes legal accountability and chain of custody.

*(Labelled block diagram shown in SECTION 4, Figure 4.2 of these notes.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

The qualification chain in ship welding follows the triad:

- **WPS (Welding Procedure Specification)** — A *documented procedure* that specifies all essential and non-essential variables for a given weld. Governed by **ISO 15609-1**. Acts as the *recipe*.
- **WPQR (Welding Procedure Qualification Record)** — The *evidence document* that proves the WPS produces welds with acceptable mechanical and chemical properties. Governed by **ISO 15614-1**. It is the *lab test report* of the trial weld made from the WPS.
- **WPQ (Welder Performance Qualification)** — The *evidence document* proving an *individual welder* can deposit sound welds using an approved WPS. Governed by **ISO 9606-1**. It is the welder's *licence*.

**Requalification is mandatory when ANY of the following essential variables is exceeded:**
- Change in base metal group (e.g., from Grade A to Grade E).
- Change in welding process (e.g., SMAW to GMAW).
- Change in filler metal F-number (per ASME QW-432) or classification group.
- Increase in heat input beyond the qualified range.
- Change in joint type (e.g., butt to T-joint).
- Change in plate thickness beyond ±25% of the qualified thickness range.
- Change in PWHT parameters (temperature, time).

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks) — *Numerical / Analytical*

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

**(a)** A transverse tensile test specimen extracted from a butt-weld test coupon of a Grade-A hull plate has:
- Original diameter: $d_0 = 10.0 \text{ mm}$
- Maximum load: $F_{max} = 65.0 \text{ kN}$
- Final diameter at fracture: $d_u = 6.5 \text{ mm}$
- Original gauge length: $L_0 = 50 \text{ mm}$
- Final gauge length: $L_u = 61.0 \text{ mm}$

Calculate the **UTS, %Elongation, and %Reduction in Area**. State the PASS/FAIL verdict against **ABS Rule 2: Part 2** requirement (UTS ≥ 400 MPa, %E ≥ 22%). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a Submerged Arc Welding (SAW) operation, the following WPS parameters are recorded:
- Voltage: $V = 30 \text{ V}$, Current: $I = 600 \text{ A}$, Travel speed: $v = 500 \text{ mm/min}$
- Process efficiency: $\eta = 1.0$

Calculate the **heat input (kJ/mm)** and verify whether it lies within the **IACS UR S22 limit of 3.0 kJ/mm** for Grade DH36 plate. Also list **four factors** that influence the heat input. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Original Cross-Sectional Area**
$$
A_0 = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d_0^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot (10.0)^2}{4} = \dfrac{314.159}{4} = 78.540 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Ultimate Tensile Strength**
$$
\sigma_u = \dfrac{F_{max}}{A_0} = \dfrac{65{,}000}{78.540} = 827.65 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Percentage Elongation**
$$
\%E = \dfrac{61.0 - 50.0}{50.0} \times 100 = \dfrac{11.0}{50.0} \times 100 = 22.0\%
$$

**Step 4 — Final Cross-Section at Fracture**
$$
A_u = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d_u^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot (6.5)^2}{4} = \dfrac{132.732}{4} = 33.183 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Percentage Reduction in Area**
$$
\%RA = \dfrac{78.540 - 33.183}{78.540} \times 100 = \dfrac{45.357}{78.540} \times 100 = 57.75\%
$$

**Step 6 — Verdict**
- UTS = 827.65 MPa > 400 MPa → **PASS**
- %E = 22.0% = 22.0% (meets the threshold exactly) → **PASS (boundary case)**
- Final verdict stamped in the report: **ACCEPTED — PASS**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Convert Travel Speed to mm/s**
$$
v = \dfrac{500 \text{ mm/min}}{60} = 8.333 \text{ mm/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Heat Input Formula**
$$
E = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \times 1000} = \dfrac{1.0 \times 30 \times 600}{8.333 \times 1000} = \dfrac{18{,}000}{8333} = 2.16 \text{ kJ/mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Verdict**
$$
2.16 \text{ kJ/mm} < 3.0 \text{ kJ/mm} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Within IACS UR S22 limit} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{ACCEPTED}
$$

**Step 4 — Four Factors Influencing Heat Input** (to be stated in the report's notes section):
1. **Welding current (I)** — Higher current = higher heat input.
2. **Arc voltage (V)** — Higher voltage = higher heat input.
3. **Travel speed (v)** — Higher speed = lower heat input.
4. **Process efficiency (η)** — Different processes (SMAW, GMAW, SAW, FCAW) have different arc efficiencies, which must be accounted for per AWS D1.1 Annex K.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Summary for Last-Minute KTU Revision**

- **Definition:** A Test Report is a controlled, traceable engineering record of welding test results, mandatory under IACS UR S21, S22, Z17, and ISO 15614-1.
- **Three Qualification Documents:** WPS (recipe) → WPQR (recipe evidence) → WPQ (welder licence).
- **Ten Tiers of a Test Report:** Metadata → Base/Filler → Process → Specimen → Test Standard → Raw Data → Calculated Properties → Verdict → NC Notes → Signatures.
- **Key Standards:** ISO 6892-1 (tensile), ISO 148-1 (Charpy), ISO 5173 (bend), ISO 6507-1 (Vickers), ISO 4136 (transverse tensile on welds), ISO 15614-1 (PQT).
- **Tensile Formulas:** $\sigma_u = F_{max}/A_0$; $\%E = (L_u - L_0)/L_0 \times 100$; $\%RA = (A_0 - A_u)/A_0 \times 100$.
- **Charpy Rule:** Mean ≥ spec mean AND min individual ≥ spec minimum (e.g., 27 J mean / 19 J single for IACS UR S22).
- **Heat Input Formula:** $E = (\eta \cdot V \cdot I) / (v \times 1000)$ in kJ/mm.
- **Hardness Rule:** HV10 indent grid per ISO 6507-1; max hardness 380 HV10 for DH36 (per IACS W31) to avoid hydrogen cracking.
- **Acceptance Authority:** Always involves a **third-party Classification Surveyor** (ABS, DNV, LR, BV, KR) — never self-certify for marine work.
- **Pitfalls:** Forget to state the test standard → lose 1 mark; forget specimen diagram → lose 2 marks; use wrong units → lose 1 mark; forget clause reference in verdict → lose 1 mark.
- **Archival Life:** Test Reports for marine structures must be retained for **the entire service life of the vessel** (typically 25–30 years) and often beyond under SOLAS and flag-state regulations.
- **Digital Trend:** Industry 4.0 shipyards now use **blockchain-anchored digital test reports** to prevent forgery and ensure instant surveyor access.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Module:** Module 4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Acceptance Standards** in shipbuilding are the codified, quantitative, and qualitative criteria established by classification societies, national bodies, and international regulatory agencies to determine whether a completed weld joint, structural fabrication, or assembly is fit for service in a marine environment. They define the permissible limits of weld defects, dimensional deviations, mechanical properties, and metallurgical characteristics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Board Standard):** *Acceptance standards are the legally and technically binding criteria specifying maximum allowable defect sizes, types, and distribution in welds and fabricated structures, ensuring structural integrity, seaworthiness, and compliance with safety regulations.*

The principal standards applicable to shipbuilding welding include:

| Standard Body | Code / Standard | Governing Region |
|---|---|---|
| **AWS** | AWS D1.1 / D1.5 / D3.6M | American Welding Society, USA |
| **ASME** | ASME Section IX, B31.1 | American Society of Mech. Engineers |
| **ISO** | ISO 5817, ISO 10042, ISO 6520 | International Organization for Standardization |
| **IACS** | IACS UR W, UR Z, UR S | International Association of Classification Societies |
| **Class Societies** | DNV, LR, ABS, BV, CCS, NK | Norway, UK, USA, France, China, Japan |
| **IS** | IS 822 / IS 10234 | Bureau of Indian Standards |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Medical Checkup of a Ship":**
> Think of acceptance standards as the **health report card** issued after a complete medical examination. A doctor checks your blood pressure, heart rate, and cholesterol levels against **published normal ranges** (the standard). If your readings are within the limit, you are "accepted" as healthy. If they exceed the limit, you are "rejected" and prescribed treatment.
> Similarly, an acceptance standard defines:
> - **WHAT to check** (e.g., porosity, cracks, undercut)
> - **HOW to check** (e.g., RT, UT, MPI, DPI, Visual)
> - **WHAT LIMITS are acceptable** (e.g., porosity ≤ 2 mm, undercut ≤ 0.5 mm)
> A weld that passes these criteria is "accepted" for service at sea — where failure can mean loss of life, cargo, and the vessel itself.

## 1.3 Why Acceptance Standards Are Critical in Shipbuilding

Marine structures are subjected to **cyclic loading, corrosion, low temperatures, dynamic wave forces, and fatigue stresses** for 25–30 years without dry-docking. Therefore, the **consequence of weld failure is catastrophic**. Acceptance standards convert engineering judgment into **objective, measurable, and legally defensible** pass/fail criteria.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Students must understand that acceptance standards are **not design guidelines** — they are **inspection gatekeepers** applied *after* welding is complete. The hierarchy is: **Design Code → WPS → Fabrication → Inspection → Acceptance Standard**.

## 1.4 Key Parameters Governed by Acceptance Standards

1. **Weld Defect Geometry** — size, shape, distribution of discontinuities
2. **Weld Profile** — reinforcement height, leg length, throat thickness
3. **Dimensional Tolerances** — distortion, misalignment, angularity
4. **Mechanical Properties** — tensile, impact, hardness (transverse/longitudinal)
5. **Metallurgical Quality** — grain structure, HAZ integrity, absence of lamellar tearing
6. **NDT Acceptance Levels** — radiographic, ultrasonic, surface, and visual thresholds

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Structure of Acceptance Standards

The shipbuilding industry follows a **three-tier acceptance hierarchy**:

**Tier 1 — International Standards:** ISO 5817 (arc welding — quality levels B, C, D), ISO 10042 (aluminium), AWS D1.1 (structural welding).

**Tier 2 — Classification Society Rules:** DNV (Det Norske Veritas), Lloyd's Register (LR), American Bureau of Shipping (ABS), Bureau Veritas (BV). These are **mandatory** for classed vessels.

**Tier 3 — Project / Customer Specifications:** Owner-specific requirements that may be **more stringent** than Tier 1 or Tier 2.

## 2.2 ISO 5817 Quality Levels (Most Tested in KTU)

ISO 5817 defines three weld quality levels, each tied to a service severity:

| Level | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Level B (Stringent)** | Highest quality | Cyclic loading, fatigue, low temperature, offshore |
| **Level C (Intermediate)** | Standard quality | Static-loaded ship structures (hull, decks) |
| **Level D (Moderate)** | Lowest quality | Non-critical, secondary structures |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** *In shipbuilding, the hull primary structure is typically accepted at ISO 5817 Level B or C, while secondary members may be accepted at Level D.*

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Acceptance Threshold Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations govern the **permissible defect size, weld geometry, and effective throat thickness** in acceptance standard evaluation. All equations are SI units unless noted.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Effective Throat Thickness** | $$a_{eff} = a \cos\theta$$ | $a$ = actual throat, $\theta$ = bevel angle |
| 2 | **Permissible Undercut Depth** | $$h_u \le 0.05\,t \quad \text{or} \quad h_u \le 0.5\,\text{mm}$$ | $t$ = thinner plate thickness |
| 3 | **Permissible Porosity (cumulative)** | $$\Sigma d_p \le 0.04\,t \quad \text{or} \quad \Sigma d_p \le 10\,\text{mm}$$ | Sum of pore diameters per 100 mm length |
| 4 | **Individual Pore Limit** | $$d_{p,max} \le 0.25\,t \quad \text{or} \quad d_{p,max} \le 3\,\text{mm}$$ | Whichever is smaller |
| 5 | **Lack of Penetration (LOP) Limit** | $$h_{LOP} \le 0.05\,t \quad \text{max } 2\,\text{mm}$$ | Single occurrence |
| 6 | **Weld Toe Angle (acceptable)** | $$\alpha \ge 90^\circ$$ | For butt welds in fatigue service |
| 7 | **Reinforcement Height (cap)** | $$h_{cap} \le 1\,mm + 0.1\,b_w \quad \text{max } 3\,\text{mm}$$ | $b_w$ = weld width |
| 8 | **Hi-Low Misalignment** | $$\Delta h \le 0.1\,t \quad \text{max } 3\,\text{mm}$$ | Butt joint plate mismatch |
| 9 | **Imperfect Root Gap Excess** | $$e_g \le 0.1\,t + 0.5\,\text{mm}$$ | Excess root gap |
| 10 | **Crater Crack Length** | $$l_{cc} = 0 \quad \text{(Zero acceptance — always reject)}$$ | Cracks are NEVER acceptable |

> [!WARNING]
> **Vertical Pipe Ban:** All equations above use the `≤` symbol and standard symbols. **Never use the pipe `|` character** for absolute value inside markdown tables — always write `≤ 0.5 mm` instead of `|x| ≤ 0.5 mm` to prevent table-breaking.

## 2.4 Acceptance Criteria by Defect Type (ISO 5817 Reference)

### 2.4.1 Surface Defects

- **Undercut (501, 521):** Smooth transition required; depth limit as in Eq. 2.
- **Excess Weld Metal / Reinforcement (504):** Must be ground flush where fatigue is critical.
- **Root Concavity (515):** Depth $\le 0.05 t$, max 1 mm.
- **Overlap (506):** **Not acceptable** in Level B; conditional in Level D.

### 2.4.2 Internal Defects

- **Porosity (201):** Distributed porosity sum limited; individual pore size limited (Eq. 3, 4).
- **Slag Inclusion (301):** Length $\le 0.5 t$ or 6 mm max for Level C.
- **Tungsten Inclusion (402):** Not permitted in Level B.
- **Lack of Fusion (401):** Zero tolerance in Level B.
- **Cracks (100-series):** **Zero acceptance** — universal rule across all levels.

### 2.4.3 Dimensional / Geometric Defects

- **Hi-Low misalignment:** Eq. 8.
- **Angular distortion:** $\le 5°$ or 3 mm in 500 mm gauge for butt joints.
- **Incorrect root gap:** Eq. 9.

## 2.5 Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) Acceptance Standards

| NDT Method | Standard | Acceptance Criteria |
|---|---|---|
| **Visual Testing (VT)** | ISO 17637, AWS D1.1 §8 | Surface profile, undercut, visual discontinuities |
| **Radiographic Testing (RT)** | ISO 17636, ASTM E446 | Image Quality Indicator (IQI) sensitivity; defect severity levels |
| **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)** | ISO 17640, ASME V Art. 4 | DAC curve, amplitude threshold, length rating |
| **Magnetic Particle Testing (MT)** | ISO 17638, ASTM E709 | No relevant indications; linear ≤ 1.5 mm |
| **Dye Penetrant Testing (PT)** | ISO 3452-1, ASTM E165 | No cracks; rounded indications acceptable per level |
| **Eddy Current Testing (ET)** | ISO 17643 | Surface/near-surface defects |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Warranty & Insurance:** A vessel cannot be insured (e.g., London market P&I clubs) without class-society-approved acceptance documentation.
- **Litigation Defence:** Acceptance standards provide legal defence in failure investigations.
- **Inter-Operability:** ISO/AWS standards allow global shipyards (Korea, Japan, China, India, EU) to fabricate hulls that are accepted by foreign owners.
- **Fatigue Life Prediction:** Acceptance limits (e.g., toe angle, undercut) directly input into **S-N curve** fatigue analysis per IIW recommendations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-Reference:** Module 4 acceptance standards directly depend on Module 3 (NDT methods) and Module 2 (welding process defects). For KTU Part B questions, integrate defect origin → detection → acceptance.
