Once these tiny stable clusters exist, more and more water molecules attach themselves in an orderly fashion, expanding outward like branches of a snowflake (**dendritic growth**) until the entire cube is solid. This is crystallization in action.

**The Three Real-World Engineering Analogies:**

1. **People forming a queue at a bus stop** — A few individuals (nuclei) form first at the designated stop (heterogeneous nucleation site, e.g., mold wall). Others then "attach" to this orderly line (crystal growth).

2. **Crowd gathering in a stadium after a concert** — People first cluster around pillars and corners (low-energy heterogeneous sites), then the crowd grows outward until the entire open space is filled.

3. **A frozen lake in winter** — Ice begins at the edges and at floating debris (heterogeneous nucleation), then progresses inward as a smooth front (planar growth) or branched (dendritic).

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Values Used in Crystallization Theory:**
>
> - **Latent heat of fusion ($L$):** Energy released per unit mass during solidification. For pure iron: $L = 247 \text{ kJ/kg}$
> - **Specific heat capacity of liquid ($C_p$):** For pure iron liquid: $C_p \approx 825 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$
> - **Thermal conductivity ($k$):** For liquid steel: $k \approx 30-40 \text{ W/(m·K)}$
> - **Solid-liquid interfacial energy ($\gamma_{SL}$):** $\gamma_{SL} \approx 0.1-0.3 \text{ J/m}^2$ for most pure metals
> - **Melting point of pure iron ($T_m$):** $1538°C$ (crucial in shipbuilding steel metallurgy)
> - **Universal gas constant ($R$):** $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$

## 1.4 Why Crystallization Matters in Shipbuilding

In shipbuilding, every fusion weld produces a **weld pool** that undergoes crystallization. The resulting grain structure in the **heat-affected zone (HAZ)** and the **weld metal** directly determines:
- **Toughness** of the joint (resistance to brittle fracture in cold seas)
- **Fatigue strength** of welded hull connections
- **Corrosion resistance** in marine environments
- **Mechanical properties** of cast ship components (propellers, rudders)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-Step Process of Crystallization

Crystallization of a pure metal proceeds via **two fundamental, sequential stages**:

### Stage 1: Nucleation
The formation of a new, distinct crystalline phase within the parent liquid phase. Nucleation itself has two distinct categories:

#### (A) Homogeneous Nucleation
- Occurs **spontaneously within the bulk liquid** without any external surface assistance.
- Requires **significant undercooling** ($\Delta T = T_m - T$) because the system must overcome the **energy barrier** imposed by the creation of a new solid-liquid interface.
- The driving force is the **volume free energy change** ($\Delta G_v$, negative below $T_m$), opposed by the **surface free energy** ($\gamma_{SL}$, always positive).

#### (B) Heterogeneous Nucleation
- Occurs at **preferential sites** such as mold walls, insoluble impurities, oxide inclusions, or container surfaces.
- The interface between the foreign substrate and the liquid **reduces the activation energy barrier**, allowing nucleation at much smaller undercoolings.
- This is the **dominant mechanism** in all practical casting and welding operations.

### Stage 2: Growth
The progressive enlargement of stable nuclei by the addition of atoms from the liquid to the solid surface. Growth mechanisms include:
- **Planar (interface-controlled) growth** — at very low undercoolings.
- **Dendritic growth** — at moderate to high undercoolings, producing tree-like branched structures.
- **Cellular growth** — intermediate morphology.

## 2.2 The Cooling Curve (Thermal Analysis)

> [!NOTE]
> A pure metal, when cooled slowly under near-equilibrium conditions, exhibits a characteristic **horizontal plateau** (thermal arrest) on its temperature-vs-time curve at the melting point. This plateau corresponds to the release of **latent heat of fusion** at a rate exactly matching the heat extraction rate, keeping the temperature constant until solidification is complete.

For a **weld pool in shipbuilding**, the cooling curve is **non-equilibrium** — the temperature drops rapidly through the arrest region, often with significant undercooling before recalescence (temperature rise) occurs as the latent heat is released.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **essential formula set** for any KTU examination question on crystallization. Memorize these — they appear every year.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Symbol Meanings | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Volume Free Energy Change** | $\Delta G_v = \dfrac{L \cdot \Delta T}{T_m}$ | $L$ = latent heat per unit volume, $\Delta T$ = undercooling, $T_m$ = melting point | J/m³, **negative** below $T_m$ |
| 2 | **Total Free Energy Change (Spherical Nucleus)** | $\Delta G = -\dfrac{4}{3}\pi r^3 \Delta G_v + 4\pi r^2 \gamma_{SL}$ | $r$ = nucleus radius, $\gamma_{SL}$ = interfacial energy | Joules (J) |
| 3 | **Critical Radius** | $r^* = \dfrac{2\gamma_{SL}}{\Delta G_v} = \dfrac{2\gamma_{SL} T_m}{L \Delta T}$ | Stable nucleus size threshold | meters (m) |
| 4 | **Critical Activation Energy (Homogeneous)** | $\Delta G^* = \dfrac{16\pi \gamma_{SL}^3 T_m^2}{3 L^2 \Delta T^2}$ | Energy barrier to form critical nucleus | Joules (J) |
| 5 | **Heterogeneous Nucleation Barrier Reduction** | $\Delta G_{het}^* = \Delta G_{hom}^* \cdot f(\theta)$ | $\theta$ = contact angle, $f(\theta) \leq 1$ | Dimensionless shape factor |
| 6 | **Shape Factor Function** | $f(\theta) = \dfrac{(2+\cos\theta)(1-\cos\theta)^2}{4}$ | For spherical cap nucleus on flat substrate | $\theta = 0° \Rightarrow f=0$; $\theta = 180° \Rightarrow f=1$ |
| 7 | **Nucleation Rate** | $\dot{N} = K_n \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\Delta G^*}{k_B T}\right) \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q_d}{k_B T}\right)$ | $K_n$ = constant, $k_B$ = Boltzmann constant, $Q_d$ = diffusion activation energy | nuclei/(volume·time) |
| 8 | **Growth Velocity (Dendritic Tip)** | $v = \mu \cdot \Delta T^2$ | $\mu$ = kinetic coefficient | m/s |
| 9 | **Grain Size Relationship** | $d \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt[3]{\dot{N} \cdot v}}$ | Higher nucleation rate & growth velocity → finer grains | meters (m) |
| 10 | **Local Solidification Time** | $t_s = \dfrac{\Delta H_f}{C_p \cdot G \cdot R}$ | $\Delta H_f$ = enthalpy of fusion, $G$ = thermal gradient, $R$ = growth rate | seconds (s) |
| 11 | **Cooling Rate Relation** | $\dfrac{dT}{dt} = G \cdot R$ | $G$ = temperature gradient, $R$ = solidification front velocity | K/s |

## 2.4 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

> **Why does a metal undercool?**
> Below $T_m$, the **solid is thermodynamically more stable** (lower free energy) than the liquid. However, forming a tiny solid cluster requires creating a **new surface** that costs energy ($\gamma_{SL}$). Only clusters larger than $r^*$ are stable — smaller ones redissolve.

> **How does heterogeneous nucleation work in practice?**
> In a **ship hull plate casting**, the molten steel contacts the cold sand mold wall. The mold surface acts as a substrate. Solid nuclei form with a much smaller activation barrier because the **solid-mold interface energy ($\gamma_{SM}$) + solid-liquid interface energy ($\gamma_{SL}$) < liquid-mold interface energy ($\gamma_{LM}$)**. This is energetically favorable.

> **Why are dendrites common in weld metals?**
> In fusion welding of ship structures, the **thermal gradient ($G$) is steep** and the **growth rate ($R$) is moderate to high**, producing the constitutional supercooling condition that drives dendritic growth. The dendrite arm spacing directly affects the mechanical properties of the weld.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Crystallization Relevance |
|---|---|
| **Hull plate casting** | Control of columnar vs equiaxed grains in continuous casting of shipbuilding steel |
| **Arc welding (SMAW, GMAW)** | Weld pool solidification determines HAZ grain size and weld metal toughness |
| **Propeller casting (bronze/manganese bronze)** | Dendritic structure affects cavitation resistance and fatigue life |
| **Submerged arc welding (SAW) of thick plates** | Solidification cracking susceptibility depends on grain morphology |
| **Heat treatment of castings** | Homogenization annealing dissolves dendritic segregation |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Critical Radius ($r^*$) for a Spherical Nucleus

We start with the **total free energy change** for forming a spherical solid nucleus of radius $r$ from the liquid:

$$\Delta G_{total} = \Delta G_{volume} + \Delta G_{surface}$$

The two terms are:

$$\Delta G_{volume} = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 \Delta G_v$$

where $\Delta G_v$ is the free energy change per unit volume (negative below $T_m$).

$$\Delta G_{surface} = 4\pi r^2 \gamma_{SL}$$

where $\gamma_{SL}$ is the solid-liquid interfacial energy per unit area (always positive).

Thus:

$$\Delta G_{total} = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 \Delta G_v + 4\pi r^2 \gamma_{SL}$$

To find the **critical radius**, we differentiate $\Delta G_{total}$ with respect to $r$ and set it to zero (extremum condition):

$$\frac{d(\Delta G_{total})}{dr} = 4\pi r^2 \Delta G_v + 8\pi r \gamma_{SL} = 0$$

Solving for $r$:

$$4\pi r^2 \Delta G_v + 8\pi r \gamma_{SL} = 0$$

$$r(4\pi r \Delta G_v + 8\pi \gamma_{SL}) = 0$$

Since $r = 0$ corresponds to no nucleus, we have:

$$4\pi r \Delta G_v + 8\pi \gamma_{SL} = 0$$

$$r^* = \frac{-2\gamma_{SL}}{\Delta G_v}$$

Because $\Delta G_v$ is **negative** below $T_m$, we write $|\Delta G_v| = -\Delta G_v$:

$$r^* = \frac{2\gamma_{SL}}{|\Delta G_v|}$$

Substituting $\Delta G_v = \dfrac{L \Delta T}{T_m}$:

$$r^* = \frac{2\gamma_{SL} T_m}{L \Delta T}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** As undercooling $\Delta T$ increases, the critical radius $r^*$ **decreases** — meaning smaller nuclei become stable, leading to a **finer grain structure**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Critical Activation Energy ($\Delta G^*$)

Substitute $r^*$ back into the total free energy expression:

$$\Delta G^* = \frac{4}{3}\pi (r^*)^3 \Delta G_v + 4\pi (r^*)^2 \gamma_{SL}$$

Using $r^* = \dfrac{-2\gamma_{SL}}{\Delta G_v}$:

$$\Delta G^* = \frac{4}{3}\pi \left(\frac{-2\gamma_{SL}}{\Delta G_v}\right)^3 \Delta G_v + 4\pi \left(\frac{-2\gamma_{SL}}{\Delta G_v}\right)^2 \gamma_{SL}$$

$$\Delta G^* = \frac{4}{3}\pi \cdot \frac{-8\gamma_{SL}^3}{\Delta G_v^3} \cdot \Delta G_v + 4\pi \cdot \frac{4\gamma_{SL}^2}{\Delta G_v^2} \cdot \gamma_{SL}$$

$$\Delta G^* = -\frac{32\pi \gamma_{SL}^3}{3 \Delta G_v^2} + \frac{16\pi \gamma_{SL}^3}{\Delta G_v^2}$$

Taking common denominator $3\Delta G_v^2$:

$$\Delta G^* = \frac{-32\pi \gamma_{SL}^3 + 48\pi \gamma_{SL}^3}{3 \Delta G_v^2}$$

$$\Delta G^* = \frac{16\pi \gamma_{SL}^3}{3 \Delta G_v^2}$$

Substituting $\Delta G_v = \dfrac{L \Delta T}{T_m}$ and $\Delta G_v^2 = \dfrac{L^2 \Delta T^2}{T_m^2}$:

$$\Delta G^* = \frac{16\pi \gamma_{SL}^3}{3} \cdot \frac{T_m^2}{L^2 \Delta T^2}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta G^* = \frac{16\pi \gamma_{SL}^3 T_m^2}{3 L^2 \Delta T^2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Result:** The activation energy barrier is **inversely proportional to $\Delta T^2$**. Doubling the undercooling reduces $\Delta G^*$ by a factor of 4, dramatically accelerating nucleation.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Heterogeneous Nucleus Shape Factor

Consider a spherical cap nucleus of contact angle $\theta$ on a flat mold wall substrate. The contact angle is defined by Young's equation:

$$\gamma_{LM} = \gamma_{SM} + \gamma_{SL} \cos\theta$$

The volumes and areas of the spherical cap are:

$$V_{cap} = \frac{\pi r^3}{3}(2 + \cos\theta)(1 - \cos\theta)^2$$

$$A_{cap} = 2\pi r^2 (1 - \cos\theta)$$

The total free energy change becomes:

$$\Delta G_{het} = V_{cap} \cdot \Delta G_v + A_{cap} \cdot \gamma_{SL} + A_{contact} \cdot (\gamma_{SM} - \gamma_{LM})$$

After algebraic manipulation (substituting Young's relation), this reduces to:

$$\Delta G_{het}^* = \Delta G_{hom}^* \cdot f(\theta)$$

where:

$$\boxed{f(\theta) = \frac{(2 + \cos\theta)(1 - \cos\theta)^2}{4}}$$

> **Verification at limits:**
> - When $\theta = 0°$ (perfect wetting): $\cos\theta = 1$, so $f(\theta) = 0$ → no barrier (instant nucleation).
> - When $\theta = 180°$ (no wetting): $\cos\theta = -1$, so $f(\theta) = 1$ → barrier equals homogeneous case.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Nucleation Types

| Parameter | Homogeneous Nucleation | Heterogeneous Nucleation |
|---|---|---|
| **Site** | Bulk liquid (no foreign surface) | Foreign surface (mold, inclusion) |
| **Undercooling required** | Very high (often $> 100°C$ for pure Fe) | Very low (often $< 1-10°C$ in practice) |
| **Activation energy** | $\Delta G_{hom}^*$ (maximum) | $\Delta G_{het}^* = \Delta G_{hom}^* \cdot f(\theta)$ (reduced) |
| **Practical occurrence in shipbuilding** | Rare (lab experiments) | **Dominant** (every casting and weld) |
| **Controlling factor** | Bulk liquid cleanliness | Substrate surface energy and contact angle |
| **Effect on grain size** | Fewer, larger grains | More, finer grains |

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** For pure iron solidifying under homogeneous conditions, given: $\gamma_{SL} = 0.205 \text{ J/m}^2$, $L = 1.87 \times 10^9 \text{ J/m}^3$, $T_m = 1811 \text{ K}$, and undercooling $\Delta T = 10 \text{ K}$, calculate: (a) the critical radius $r^*$, and (b) the activation energy barrier $\Delta G^*$.

**Part (a): Critical Radius**

$$r^* = \frac{2\gamma_{SL} T_m}{L \Delta T}$$

Substituting values:

$$r^* = \frac{2 \times 0.205 \times 1811}{1.87 \times 10^9 \times 10}$$

$$r^* = \frac{742.51}{1.87 \times 10^{10}}$$

$$r^* = 3.97 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m} = 39.7 \text{ nm}$$

**Part (b): Activation Energy**

$$\Delta G^* = \frac{16\pi \gamma_{SL}^3 T_m^2}{3 L^2 \Delta T^2}$$

Numerator:

$$16 \pi (0.205)^3 (1811)^2 = 16\pi \times 8.615 \times 10^{-3} \times 3.28 \times 10^{6}$$

$$= 16\pi \times 2.826 \times 10^{4} = 1.420 \times 10^{6}$$

Denominator:

$$3 \times (1.87 \times 10^9)^2 \times (10)^2 = 3 \times 3.497 \times 10^{18} \times 100 = 1.049 \times 10^{21}$$

$$\Delta G^* = \frac{1.420 \times 10^{6}}{1.049 \times 10^{21}} = 1.354 \times 10^{-15} \text{ J}$$

Converting to electron-volts ($1 \text{ eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$):

$$\Delta G^* = \frac{1.354 \times 10^{-15}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 8.45 \times 10^{3} \text{ eV} = 8.45 \text{ keV}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** This extremely high barrier ($\sim 8.45$ keV) explains why homogeneous nucleation rarely occurs in practice. Real castings and welds rely on heterogeneous nucleation at much lower undercoolings.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Free Energy Barrier Schematic (Mermaid)

The following diagram illustrates the **total free energy change** as a function of nucleus radius, showing the critical radius $r^*$ and the activation barrier $\Delta G^*$.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Liquid State at T below Tm] --> B[Atoms Begin Clustering]
    B --> C[Subcritical Embryos Dissolve Back]
    B --> D[Supercritical Nuclei Grow]
    C --> E[Free Energy Decreases - Unstable]
    D --> F[Free Energy Decreases - Stable Growth]
    G[Critical Radius r Star] --> D
    H[Activation Barrier Delta G Star] --> G
    I[Volume Free Energy Term - Negative] --> J[Surface Energy Term - Positive]
    J --> K[Net Free Energy Curve with Maximum]
    I --> K
    K --> G
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Crystallization Workflow in a Weld Pool

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Heat Source: Arc or Flame] --> B[Base Metal Melting]
    B --> C[Weld Pool Formation - Liquid State]
    C --> D[Mold Wall Cold Substrate Contact]
    D --> E[Heterogeneous Nucleation at Interface]
    E --> F[Stable Nuclei Form on Substrate]
    F --> G[Columnar Dendrite Growth Toward Pool Center]
    G --> H[Thermal Gradient G Steep in Weld]
    H --> I[Growth Rate R Moderate]
    I --> J[Constitutional Supercooling Onset]
    J --> K[Dendritic Arm Spacing Formation]
    K --> L[Final Weld Metal Grain Structure]
    L --> M[Mechanical Properties of Weld]
    M --> N[Ship Hull Integrity Assessment]
    O[Cooling Rate dT/dt Equals G times R] --> H
    O --> I
    P[Purification: Refining Inoculants] --> E
```

## 4.3 Nucleation Mechanism Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Liquid Metal Below Melting Point] --> B{Nucleation Site Available?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Substrate Present]
    C --> D[Contact Angle Theta]
    D -- Theta Small --> E[Low Energy Barrier - Easy Nucleation]
    D -- Theta Large --> F[High Energy Barrier - Hard Nucleation]
    B -- No --> G[Bulk Liquid Only]
    G --> H[Homogeneous Nucleation]
    H --> I[Requires Large Undercooling]
    E --> J[Stable Solid Cluster Forms]
    F --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Critical Radius r Star Exceeded]
    K --> L[Spontaneous Growth Phase]
    L --> M[Grain Structure Develops]
```

## 4.4 As-Cast Microstructure Zones (Shipbuilding Casting)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Mold Wall - Cold Surface] --> B[Chill Zone: Fine Equiaxed Grains]
    B --> C[Columnar Zone: Dendrites Grow Inward]
    C --> D[Equiaxed Zone: Center of Ingot]
    D --> E[Final Casting: Hull Plate or Propeller]
    F[Heat Flow Direction: Perpendicular to Mold] --> B
    F --> C
    G[Grain Morphology Depends on G over R Ratio] --> C
    G --> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Description of the diagram:** In a typical shipbuilding steel casting (e.g., a stern frame or rudder horn), the solidified structure has three distinct zones. The **chill zone** at the mold wall contains fine, randomly-oriented equiaxed grains formed by rapid heterogeneous nucleation on the cold mold surface. The **columnar zone** consists of elongated dendrites growing perpendicular to the mold wall, aligned with the heat flow direction. The **central equiaxed zone** forms last, where detached dendrite fragments or new nuclei grow without preferred orientation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q: Define crystallization of a pure metal. List the two main stages involved.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Crystallization of a pure metal** is the first-order phase transformation in which a metal changes from the liquid state to the solid crystalline state upon cooling below its equilibrium melting point ($T_m$).
>
> The two main stages are:
> 1. **Nucleation** — Formation of stable solid clusters (nuclei) within the liquid, which can be either homogeneous (in the bulk) or heterogeneous (on substrates).
> 2. **Growth** — Progressive attachment of liquid atoms onto the stable nuclei, propagating the crystal lattice until the entire liquid volume is solidified.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q: Distinguish between homogeneous and heterogeneous nucleation.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Homogeneous Nucleation | Heterogeneous Nucleation |
|---|---|---|
| **Site** | Bulk liquid (no foreign surface) | Foreign substrate (mold, impurity) |
| **Energy barrier** | High ($\Delta G_{hom}^*$) | Reduced ($\Delta G_{het}^* = \Delta G_{hom}^* \cdot f(\theta)$) |
| **Undercooling required** | Very large | Small |
| **Practical occurrence** | Rare in real castings | **Dominant in shipbuilding welds & castings** |

> [!NOTE]
> In shipbuilding, the weld pool in fusion welding (e.g., SMAW, GMAW) and molten metal in casting operations always contact foreign surfaces, making heterogeneous nucleation the practically relevant mechanism.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Pattern

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Part B Module 4 questions carry 14 marks with internal choice. Students answer EITHER Question A OR Question B. Each question has sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question based on Dec 2023 pattern]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q: Derive the expression for the critical radius of a spherical nucleus during solidification of a pure metal. Explain its physical significance.**

**Model Solution:**

The total free energy change for a spherical solid nucleus of radius $r$ forming from the liquid is:

$$\Delta G_{total} = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 \Delta G_v + 4\pi r^2 \gamma_{SL}$$

where:
- $\dfrac{4}{3}\pi r^3 \Delta G_v$ = volume free energy term (negative below $T_m$)
- $4\pi r^2 \gamma_{SL}$ = surface free energy term (positive, always)

**Step 1:** Differentiate with respect to $r$:

$$\frac{d(\Delta G_{total})}{dr} = 4\pi r^2 \Delta G_v + 8\pi r \gamma_{SL} = 0$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $r$ (excluding $r = 0$):

$$r^* = \frac{-2\gamma_{SL}}{\Delta G_v}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $\Delta G_v = \dfrac{L \Delta T}{T_m}$ (where $\Delta T = T_m - T$ is undercooling):

$$r^* = \frac{2\gamma_{SL} T_m}{L \Delta T}$$

**Physical Significance:**

- $r^*$ is the **minimum radius** for a nucleus to be thermodynamically stable.
- Nuclei with $r < r^*$ dissolve back into the liquid (subcritical embryos).
- Nuclei with $r > r^*$ grow spontaneously (supercritical).
- As undercooling $\Delta T$ increases, $r^*$ decreases → **finer grain structure**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often forget the negative sign when isolating $\Delta G_v$ and write $r^* = \dfrac{2\gamma_{SL}}{\Delta G_v}$ with a positive denominator. **Remember:** $\Delta G_v$ is negative below $T_m$, so the result is positive. Always state: "Taking the magnitude, $r^* = \dfrac{2\gamma_{SL}}{|\Delta G_v|}$."

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q: With a neat sketch, explain the formation of a typical as-cast grain structure in a shipbuilding steel ingot casting. Discuss the role of thermal gradient (G) and growth rate (R) in determining the structure.**

**Model Solution:**

The as-cast grain structure of a shipbuilding steel ingot consists of **three distinct zones** from the mold wall inward:

**Zone 1 — Chill Zone (Outer):**
- Forms immediately at the cold mold wall.
- Rapid heterogeneous nucleation produces **fine, randomly-oriented equiaxed grains**.
- High nucleation rate due to large undercooling at the chill surface.

**Zone 2 — Columnar Zone (Middle):**
- Dendrites grow **perpendicular to the mold wall**, aligned with the heat flow direction.
- Grains are elongated and parallel.
- Growth is controlled by the steep thermal gradient $G$.

**Zone 3 — Equiaxed Zone (Center):**
- Forms last as the remaining liquid solidifies.
- Grains are roughly equal in all dimensions.
- Nucleation from **detached dendrite tips** and constitutional undercooling.

**Role of G and R (G/R Ratio Criterion):**

| $G/R$ Ratio | Resulting Structure |
|---|---|
| Very high | Planar front growth |
| Moderate | Cellular growth |
| Moderate-low | Columnar dendrites |
| Low | Equiaxed dendrites (CET transition) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CET (Columnar-to-Equiaxed Transition):** When the ratio $G/R$ drops below a critical value, the structure transitions from columnar to equiaxed. This is critical in shipbuilding cast propellers and stern frames, where a fine equiaxed center is preferred for toughness.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often describe the three zones but fail to **relate G and R to the structure**. Examiners specifically award marks for explaining how $G/R$ controls morphology. Always state the threshold condition explicitly.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question based on May 2024 pattern]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q: Explain the homogeneous nucleation process. Derive the expression for the activation energy barrier and show how it depends on undercooling.**

**Model Solution:**

**Homogeneous Nucleation Concept:**
Homogeneous nucleation is the spontaneous formation of a stable solid cluster within the bulk of a pure liquid metal without any external surface. It requires **significant undercooling** to overcome the energy barrier associated with creating a new solid-liquid interface.

**Derivation of Activation Energy:**

The total free energy change is:

$$\Delta G = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 \Delta G_v + 4\pi r^2 \gamma_{SL}$$

Substituting $r^* = \dfrac{-2\gamma_{SL}}{\Delta G_v}$:

$$\Delta G^* = \frac{16\pi \gamma_{SL}^3}{3 \Delta G_v^2}$$

Using $\Delta G_v = \dfrac{L \Delta T}{T_m}$:

$$\Delta G^* = \frac{16\pi \gamma_{SL}^3 T_m^2}{3 L^2 \Delta T^2}$$

**Dependence on Undercooling:**

- $\Delta G^* \propto \dfrac{1}{\Delta T^2}$
- Doubling $\Delta T$ reduces the barrier by 75%.
- This is why **high undercooling accelerates nucleation exponentially** (via the Boltzmann factor in the nucleation rate equation).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Confusing $\Delta G$ (general) with $\Delta G_v$ (per unit volume). Always specify which is which in derivations.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q: Describe the mechanism of heterogeneous nucleation. How does the contact angle influence the activation energy barrier? Show that the heterogeneous barrier is always less than or equal to the homogeneous barrier.**

**Model Solution:**

**Mechanism:**
In heterogeneous nucleation, solid nuclei form on a foreign substrate (e.g., the mold wall in a ship hull plate casting, or inclusions in a weld pool). The nucleus adopts a **spherical cap shape** characterized by a contact angle $\theta$ with the substrate.

**Young's Equation:**

$$\gamma_{LM} = \gamma_{SM} + \gamma_{SL} \cos\theta$$

**Contact Angle Influence:**

The activation energy for heterogeneous nucleation is:

$$\Delta G_{het}^* = \Delta G_{hom}^* \cdot f(\theta)$$

where:

$$f(\theta) = \frac{(2 + \cos\theta)(1 - \cos\theta)^2}{4}$$

**Proof that $\Delta G_{het}^* \leq \Delta G_{hom}^*$:**

- For $\theta = 0°$: $\cos\theta = 1$, so $f(\theta) = 0$ → $\Delta G_{het}^* = 0$ (perfect wetting, no barrier).
- For $\theta = 90°$: $\cos\theta = 0$, so $f(\theta) = \dfrac{2 \times 1}{4} = 0.5$ → barrier halved.
- For $\theta = 180°$: $\cos\theta = -1$, so $f(\theta) = \dfrac{1 \times 4}{4} = 1$ → barrier equals homogeneous case.

Since $0 \leq f(\theta) \leq 1$ for all $\theta \in [0°, 180°]$, we have:

$$\Delta G_{het}^* \leq \Delta G_{hom}^* \quad \text{(proved)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** In shipbuilding, **inoculation** (adding small particles like Ti, B, or Al to liquid steel) reduces the contact angle and provides active sites for heterogeneous nucleation, resulting in a **finer, more uniform grain structure** in cast propellers and weld metal.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write $f(\theta) = \cos\theta$ or other incorrect forms. The **exact expression** $f(\theta) = \dfrac{(2+\cos\theta)(1-\cos\theta)^2}{4}$ must be reproduced accurately. Examiners deduct 1-2 marks for errors in this formula.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning: Common Mark-Loss Areas

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks on This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Sign confusion in $\Delta G_v$** — Failing to note that $\Delta G_v$ is negative below $T_m$, and that the magnitude $|\Delta G_v|$ is used in formulas.
> 2. **Skipping the $\Delta T^2$ dependency** — The relationship $\Delta G^* \propto 1/\Delta T^2$ is a favorite KTU question; students often write $1/\Delta T$ instead.
> 3. **Confusing critical radius with nucleus radius** — $r^*$ is a threshold; the actual nucleus radius in any moment may be different.
> 4. **Drawing a cooling curve without labeling the thermal arrest** — A common 1-mark loss in 3-mark questions.
> 5. **Stating the shape factor formula incorrectly** — Use the exact expression $f(\theta) = \dfrac{(2+\cos\theta)(1-\cos\theta)^2}{4}$ with all parentheses.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap: Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Essential Points to Remember Before Exam (Crystallization of Pure Metals):**
>
> - [ ] Crystallization = Nucleation + Growth (two sequential stages).
> - [ ] **Homogeneous nucleation** occurs in bulk liquid; requires large undercooling; high $\Delta G^*$.
> - [ ] **Heterogeneous nucleation** occurs on foreign substrates; much lower undercooling; dominant in practice.
> - [ ] Critical radius: $r^* = \dfrac{2\gamma_{SL} T_m}{L \Delta T}$ — decreases with increasing $\Delta T$.
> - [ ] Activation energy: $\Delta G^* = \dfrac{16\pi \gamma_{SL}^3 T_m^2}{3 L^2 \Delta T^2}$ — inversely proportional to $\Delta T^2$.
> - [ ] Shape factor: $f(\theta) = \dfrac{(2+\cos\theta)(1-\cos\theta)^2}{4}$, with $0 \leq f(\theta) \leq 1$.
> - [ ] Cooling curve of a pure metal shows a **thermal arrest plateau** at $T_m$ (latent heat release).
> - [ ] **As-cast structure:** Chill zone → Columnar zone → Equiaxed zone.
> - [ ] **G/R ratio** controls growth morphology: high G/R → planar; low G/R → equiaxed dendrites.
> - [ ] **Dendritic growth** dominates in fusion welds (e.g., ship hull plate welding) due to constitutional supercooling.
> - [ ] Local solidification time: $t_s = \dfrac{\Delta H_f}{C_p \cdot G \cdot R}$; finer structures from faster cooling.
> - [ ] In shipbuilding: **finer grains = better toughness, fatigue resistance, and corrosion resistance** in welds and castings.
> - [ ] **Inoculation** (adding Ti, B, Nb) promotes heterogeneous nucleation → grain refinement in marine castings.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Phase Transformation in Iron-Carbon Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST831 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Phase Transformation in Iron-Carbon Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Iron-Carbon Phase Diagram** is a binary equilibrium phase map that plots temperature (ordinate, in °C or K) against the overall carbon concentration (abscissa, in weight percent, wt% C) for the Fe–C system, generally evaluated between **0 wt% C (pure iron)** and **6.67 wt% C (the stoichiometric composition of iron carbide, Fe₃C, also called cementite)**. The diagram is the foundational metallurgical tool for predicting the **phases present, their proportions, compositions, and the sequence of phase transformations** that occur during the heating (austenitization) and cooling (solidification, allotropic transformation, and decomposition) of carbon steels and cast irons used in marine structural fabrication, weldments, and hull plating.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):**
> The Iron-Carbon phase diagram depicts the equilibrium phases (Ferrite-α, Austenite-γ, Cementite-Fe₃C, δ-Ferrite, and Liquid) that exist as a function of carbon content and temperature, and the invariant reactions (peritectic, eutectic, eutectoid) that govern transformations in steels and cast irons used in shipbuilding.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Iron-Carbon Building Blocks Game"

Imagine you are stacking LEGO bricks of **two colours**: iron (Fe) atoms (grey) and carbon (C) atoms (black). The grey bricks can stack in three distinct patterns:

- **Body-Centered Cubic (BCC)** — a tight 9-brick pattern called *Ferrite (α)*. This is soft, magnetic, and loves cold temperatures.
- **Face-Centered Cubic (FCC)** — a roomy 14-brick pattern called *Austenite (γ)*. Carbon atoms slip into the empty "parking spots" in the centre of the cube faces. This is the only phase that can dissolve much carbon.
- **Body-Centered Cubic again at high temperature** — called *δ-Ferrite (δ)*, which forms right before melting.

The black carbon bricks can either:
- Hide individually inside the FCC parking spots (forming austenite).
- Stack together with three iron bricks to form a super-hard brittle molecule called **cementite (Fe₃C)** with exactly **6.67% C** by mass.

Now, when you change the *temperature* of the pile, the bricks rearrange spontaneously — this is a **phase transformation**. The Iron-Carbon Diagram is essentially a "rule-book" telling you exactly which LEGO arrangement is stable at any given (temperature, carbon %) combination.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Mnemonic for KTU Board Exams — "FERAL"**
> **F**errite (α, BCC, max 0.022% C) → soft, magnetic
> **E**utectoid (0.77% C) → pearlite forms
> **R**etained (γ at room T) → unstable martensite on quench
> **A**ustenite (γ, FCC, max 2.14% C) → non-magnetic, ductile
> **L**edeburite (eutectic at 4.30% C) → cast-iron territory

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> | Quantity | Symbol | Value | Unit |
> | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
> | Eutectoid carbon content | $C_E$ | **0.77** | wt% C |
> | Maximum carbon in austenite | $C_\gamma^{max}$ | **2.14** | wt% C |
> | Maximum carbon in δ-ferrite | $C_\delta^{max}$ | **0.09** | wt% C |
> | Eutectic carbon content | $C_{eut}$ | **4.30** | wt% C |
> | Cementite carbon content | $C_{Fe_3C}$ | **6.67** | wt% C |
> | Eutectoid temperature | $T_{eut}$ | **727** | °C |
> | Eutectic temperature | $T_{euc}$ | **1147** | °C |
> | Peritectic temperature | $T_{per}$ | **1493** | °C |
> | Melting point of pure iron | $T_m^{Fe}$ | **1538** | °C |
> | Curie temperature of iron | $T_{Curie}$ | **770** | °C |
> | Density of ferrite | $\rho_\alpha$ | **7.87** | g/cm³ |
> | Density of austenite | $\rho_\gamma$ | **7.86** | g/cm³ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Phases of the Fe–C Diagram

The phase diagram is constructed by tracking the **Gibbs free energy minima** of competing phases at each (T, C) coordinate. Five distinct phases are encountered:

#### (a) Ferrite (α-phase)
- **Crystal structure:** Body-Centered Cubic (BCC)
- **Carbon solubility:** Maximum 0.022 wt% C at 727 °C (eutectoid temperature); negligible (~0.008 wt%) at room temperature
- **Properties:** Soft, ductile, ferromagnetic below 770 °C (Curie point), low strength
- **Notation:** $\alpha$-Fe

#### (b) Austenite (γ-phase)
- **Crystal structure:** Face-Centered Cubic (FCC)
- **Carbon solubility:** Maximum 2.14 wt% C at 1147 °C
- **Properties:** Non-magnetic, ductile, the only phase that accommodates significant carbon, parent phase for all diffusional transformations in steel
- **Notation:** $\gamma$-Fe

#### (c) δ-Ferrite (delta-phase)
- **Crystal structure:** Body-Centered Cubic (BCC)
- **Carbon solubility:** Maximum 0.09 wt% C at 1493 °C
- **Stability range:** 1394 °C to 1538 °C (just below melting)
- **Significance:** Involved in the peritectic reaction during solidification of carbon steels

#### (d) Cementite (Fe₃C)
- **Crystal structure:** Orthorhombic, complex intermetallic
- **Composition:** Fixed at 6.67 wt% C (line compound — a vertical line in the diagram, indicating no composition variation)
- **Properties:** Extremely hard (~1000 HV), brittle, ferromagnetic up to 210 °C
- **Role:** Hardening constituent in pearlite and ledeburite

#### (e) Liquid Phase (L)
- Exists above the liquidus line
- Compositions: 0 to 6.67 wt% C (along the L → γ + Fe₃C line)
- Critical in welding pool solidification, casting, and weld metal microstructures

### 2.2 The Three Invariant Reactions

Invariant reactions are points in the diagram where **three phases coexist in equilibrium at a fixed temperature** (zero degrees of freedom by Gibbs Phase Rule: $F = C - P + 1 = 2 - 3 + 1 = 0$).

#### (a) Peritectic Reaction — at 1493 °C, 0.16 wt% C
$$
L_{(0.53\%\,C)} + \delta_{(0.09\%\,C)} \;\xrightarrow[\text{cool}]{\;1493°C\;}\; \gamma_{(0.16\%\,C)}
$$
- Occurs during solidification of low-carbon steels (relevant to marine grade-A, grade-B hull plates)
- The δ-ferrite is incompletely consumed, leaving residual δ at grain boundaries — a common source of weld solidification cracks

#### (b) Eutectic Reaction — at 1147 °C, 4.30 wt% C
$$
L_{(4.30\%\,C)} \;\xrightarrow[\text{cool}]{\;1147°C\;}\; \gamma_{(2.14\%\,C)} + Fe_3C
$$
- Defines the boundary between **steels (<2.14% C)** and **cast irons (>2.14% C)**
- The eutectic mixture of austenite + cementite is called **Ledeburite**
- Critical in white cast iron microstructures used for marine propeller bearings

#### (c) Eutectoid Reaction — at 727 °C, 0.77 wt% C
$$
\gamma_{(0.77\%\,C)} \;\xrightarrow[\text{cool}]{\;727°C\;}\; \alpha_{(0.022\%\,C)} + Fe_3C
$$
- The **single most important transformation in steel metallurgy**
- The eutectoid mixture of ferrite + cementite is called **Pearlite** (lamellar structure)
- Governs the final microstructure of slowly cooled carbon-steel weldments in ship hulls

### 2.3 The Critical Temperature Lines (A-Lines)

These lines track the position of the eutectoid temperature under **non-equilibrium (real) heating and cooling rates**:

| Line | Meaning | Heating/Cooling |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $A_1$ | Eutectoid temperature (727 °C) | Equilibrium |
| $A_3$ | Upper critical temp. of hypoeutectoid steels (γ → α completion on heating) | Heating |
| $A_{cm}$ | Upper critical temp. of hypereutectoid steels (Fe₃C solubility in γ) | Heating |
| $A_{c1}, A_{c3}, A_{ccm}$ | Critical temps on **heating** (c = chauffage, French) | Heating |
| $A_{r1}, A_{r3}, A_{rcm}$ | Critical temps on **cooling** (r = refroidissement, French) | Cooling |
| $M_s, M_f$ | Martensite start and finish temperatures | Quench |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **welding metallurgy**, the weld thermal cycle rapidly traverses these A-lines, leading to non-equilibrium microstructures (martensite, bainite) in the heat-affected zone (HAZ) of marine steel joints.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Lever Rule and Phase Fractions

The **Lever Rule** is used to calculate the mass fraction of each phase in a two-phase region. For a steel of carbon content $C_0$ at temperature $T$:

| Quantity | Formula | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Fraction of phase $\alpha$ | $W_\alpha = \dfrac{C_\gamma - C_0}{C_\gamma - C_\alpha}$ | In $\alpha + \gamma$ region |
| Fraction of phase $\gamma$ | $W_\gamma = \dfrac{C_0 - C_\alpha}{C_\gamma - C_\alpha}$ | In $\alpha + \gamma$ region |
| Fraction of proeutectoid $\alpha$ | $W_{\alpha}^{pro} = \dfrac{0.77 - C_0}{0.77 - 0.022}$ | Hypoeutectoid, just above $A_1$ |
| Fraction of pearlite | $W_{P} = \dfrac{C_0 - 0.022}{0.77 - 0.022}$ | Hypoeutectoid, just below $A_1$ |
| Fraction of proeutectoid Fe₃C | $W_{Fe_3C}^{pro} = \dfrac{C_0 - 0.77}{6.67 - 0.77}$ | Hypereutectoid, just above $A_1$ |
| Gibbs Phase Rule | $F = C - P + 1$ (at constant P) | Degrees of freedom |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Prohibition:** In the formulas above, vertical pipes (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) are **NOT** permitted inside markdown tables as they break the table rendering. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead. For example, use $\vert C_\gamma - C_0 \vert$ written as `\vert C_\gamma - C_0 \vert` if absolute value notation is required.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Shipbuilding Welding

- **Hull plate selection**: Shipbuilding grades AH36, DH36, EH36 (0.18% C max) are hypoeutectoid; their weld HAZ transforms via the eutectoid reaction and must be controlled to avoid excessive pearlite banding.
- **Weld metal design**: Electrodes with ~0.10% C deposit weld metal that on slow cooling forms ferrite + pearlite, providing the required Charpy impact toughness at -40 °C.
- **Cast iron propeller repair**: Eutectic reaction governs the microstructure of Ni-Fe electrodes used in cold welding of marine propellers.
- **Heat treatment of marine shafts**: Forging of marine propeller shafts (typically 0.35–0.45% C) requires austenitization above $A_3$, then controlled cooling to obtain the desired ferrite-pearlite ratio (typically 50:50).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations and Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Lever Rule

**Statement:** For a binary alloy of overall composition $C_0$ lying in a two-phase region between compositions $C_\alpha$ and $C_\gamma$ (where $C_\alpha < C_0 < C_\gamma$), the mass fraction of each phase is given by the lever rule.

**Step 1 — Conservation of Mass.**
Consider a unit mass of the alloy at temperature $T$ within the $\alpha + \gamma$ region. Let the mass fractions of $\alpha$ and $\gamma$ be $W_\alpha$ and $W_\gamma$ respectively. Mass balance gives:

$$
W_\alpha + W_\gamma = 1
$$

**Step 2 — Conservation of Carbon.**
The total carbon in the alloy must equal the sum of carbon in each phase:

$$
C_0 = W_\alpha \cdot C_\alpha + W_\gamma \cdot C_\gamma
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Equation 1 into Equation 2.**
Replace $W_\gamma = 1 - W_\alpha$:

$$
C_0 = W_\alpha \cdot C_\alpha + (1 - W_\alpha) \cdot C_\gamma
$$

**Step 4 — Expand and Rearrange.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_0 &= W_\alpha \cdot C_\alpha + C_\gamma - W_\alpha \cdot C_\gamma \\
C_0 - C_\gamma &= W_\alpha \cdot (C_\alpha - C_\gamma) \\
W_\alpha \cdot (C_\alpha - C_\gamma) &= C_0 - C_\gamma
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Isolate $W_\alpha$.**
Multiplying both sides by -1 and noting that $C_\gamma - C_\alpha > 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_\alpha \cdot (C_\gamma - C_\alpha) &= C_\gamma - C_0 \\
\therefore W_\alpha &= \dfrac{C_\gamma - C_0}{C_\gamma - C_\alpha}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Obtain $W_\gamma$ by Subtraction.**

$$
W_\gamma = 1 - W_\alpha = \dfrac{C_0 - C_\alpha}{C_\gamma - C_\alpha}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric Interpretation:** The mass fraction of a phase is the **ratio of the tie-line segment on the opposite side of the composition point** to the total tie-line length. The lever "balances" at the overall composition $C_0$.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Pearlite Fraction in a 0.45% C Steel

**Problem:** A marine shaft steel containing 0.45 wt% C is slowly cooled from the austenitic region to just below the eutectoid temperature. Calculate (a) the mass fraction of proeutectoid ferrite, and (b) the mass fraction of pearlite in the resulting microstructure.

**Given:** $C_0 = 0.45$ wt% C, $C_\alpha^{eut} = 0.022$ wt% C, $C_{eut} = 0.77$ wt% C.

**(a) Mass fraction of proeutectoid ferrite (just above 727 °C):**

At 727 °C, austenite contains 0.77% C, ferrite contains 0.022% C. Since $C_0 = 0.45\% < 0.77\%$, the steel is **hypoeutectoid** and consists of proeutectoid $\alpha$ + $\gamma$ of eutectoid composition.

$$
W_\alpha^{pro} = \dfrac{C_\gamma - C_0}{C_\gamma - C_\alpha} = \dfrac{0.77 - 0.45}{0.77 - 0.022} = \dfrac{0.32}{0.748}
$$

$$
W_\alpha^{pro} = 0.4278 \approx 42.78\% \text{ proeutectoid ferrite}
$$

**(b) Mass fraction of pearlite:**
Below 727 °C, the remaining austenite (0.77% C) transforms entirely to pearlite. The fraction of pearlite equals the fraction of austenite just above $A_1$:

$$
W_P = W_\gamma^{above\,A_1} = \dfrac{C_0 - C_\alpha}{C_\gamma - C_\alpha} = \dfrac{0.45 - 0.022}{0.77 - 0.022} = \dfrac{0.428}{0.748}
$$

$$
W_P = 0.5722 \approx 57.22\% \text{ pearlite}
$$

**Verification:**

$$
W_\alpha^{pro} + W_P = 0.4278 + 0.5722 = 1.000 \;\checkmark
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Phase Transformations on Cooling

The following matrix tracks the phase evolution of a **0.45% C hypoeutectoid steel** as it cools from 1600 °C to room temperature, with phase fractions computed via the lever rule at each stage:

| Temperature (°C) | Phases Present | Phase Fractions | Microstructural Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1600 | L (Liquid) | $W_L = 1.00$ | Fully molten weld pool |
| 1520 | $L + \delta$ | $W_L = 0.46$, $W_\delta = 0.54$ | Solidification begins; δ dendrites form |
| 1493 (Peritectic) | $L + \delta + \gamma$ | Invariant | Peritectic reaction: $L + \delta \to \gamma$ |
| 1450 | $\gamma$ | $W_\gamma = 1.00$ | Fully austenitic, single phase |
| 900 | $\alpha + \gamma$ | $W_\alpha = 0.41$, $W_\gamma = 0.59$ | Proeutectoid ferrite nucleates at γ grain boundaries |
| 727 (Eutectoid) | $\alpha + \gamma_{(0.77\%)} \to \alpha + Fe_3C$ | Invariant | Eutectoid transformation to pearlite |
| 25 (Room T) | $\alpha + Fe_3C$ (as pearlite + proeutectoid α) | $W_\alpha^{pro} = 0.43$, $W_P = 0.57$ | Final ferrite-pearlite microstructure |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Welding Implication:** In a single-pass weld of 0.45% C steel, the weld thermal cycle is far from equilibrium. The HAZ experiences rapid heating above $A_3$ (austenitization) followed by rapid cooling, often producing **non-equilibrium martensite** rather than the equilibrium ferrite-pearlite structure predicted above. This is a major concern for marine weld HAZ toughness.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Schematic of the Iron-Carbon Phase Diagram (Block-Level Architecture)

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **logical architecture of phase regions, critical points, and invariant reactions** in the Fe–C diagram, suitable for KTU board answer scripts where hand-drawing the full diagram is impractical.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Pure Fe 0% C"] --> B["Ferrite alpha BCC"]
    B --> C["Austenite gamma FCC"]
    C --> D["Delta Ferrite delta BCC"]
    D --> E["Liquid L"]
    E --> F["Peritectic 1493 C 0.16% C"]
    F --> G["Eutectic 1147 C 4.30% C Ledeburite"]
    G --> H["Eutectoid 727 C 0.77% C Pearlite"]
    H --> I["Cementite Fe3C 6.67% C"]
    I --> J["Hypoeutectoid Steel 0 to 0.77% C"]
    J --> K["Eutectoid Steel 0.77% C"]
    K --> L["Hypereutectoid Steel 0.77 to 2.14% C"]
    L --> M["Cast Iron Region 2.14 to 6.67% C"]
    B --> N["Curie Temperature 770 C Magnetic Transition"]
    C --> O["Ac3 Line Hypoeutectoid Upper Critical"]
    H --> P["A1 Line Eutectoid Horizontal"]
    I --> Q["Acm Line Hypereutectoid Cementite Solubility"]
    J --> R["Marine Hull Plates AH36 DH36"]
    K --> S["Eutectoid Rail Steel"]
    L --> T["Marine Shaft Steel 0.45% C"]
    M --> U["Marine Propeller White Cast Iron"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Safeguard Compliance Check:** All node IDs are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `A`, `B`, `J`, `R`). No reserved Mermaid keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as standalone node IDs. All labels are raw uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting tags or HTML tables.

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Phase Transformation Flow in a Marine Weld

This block diagram illustrates how a marine steel weld joint progresses through the Fe–C phase regions during a typical multi-pass welding thermal cycle:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["Base Metal 0.45% C Ferrite plus Pearlite 25 C"] --> B1["HAZ Heating Peak 1100 C Full Austenitization"]
    B1 --> C1["Coarse Grain HAZ Austenite 1100 to 900 C"]
    C1 --> D1["Fine Grain HAZ Austenite 900 to 727 C"]
    D1 --> E1["Intercritical HAZ Partial Austenite plus Ferrite 727 to 600 C"]
    E1 --> F1["Subcritical HAZ Tempered Microstructure 600 to 25 C"]
    A1 --> A2["Weld Pool Liquid plus delta 1500 C plus"]
    A2 --> B2["Weld Metal Solidification gamma plus delta 1493 C"]
    B2 --> C2["Columnar gamma Grains 1400 to 900 C"]
    C2 --> D2["gamma to alpha plus Fe3C 727 C Eutectoid"]
    D2 --> E2["Acicular Ferrite plus Pearlite 25 C Final Weld"]
    F1 --> G1["Final Joint Microstructure"]
    E2 --> G1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation for KTU Answers:** The weld metal and HAZ each traverse a *different path* through the Fe–C diagram. The weld metal starts from the liquidus, undergoes solidification, and the final pass transforms austenite to acicular ferrite + pearlite. The HAZ regions (CGHAZ, FGHAZ, ICHAZ, SCHAZ) experience re-austenitization and decomposition, often producing **localized brittle zones (LBZ)** that are critical failure loci in marine structures subjected to dynamic loading (e.g., ship slamming, wave impact).

### 4.3 Phase Transformation Cascade Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Stage 1 Liquid to delta plus L"] --> S2["Stage 2 Peritectic L plus delta to gamma 1493 C"]
    S2 --> S3["Stage 3 gamma to alpha plus gamma 900 C Proeutectoid Ferrite"]
    S3 --> S4["Stage 4 Eutectoid gamma to alpha plus Fe3C 727 C Pearlite"]
    S4 --> S5["Stage 5 Final Ferrite plus Pearlite 25 C Equilibrium"]
    S1 -. Quench .-> Q1["Martensite Mf below 200 C Non Equilibrium"]
    S2 -. Quench .-> Q1
    S3 -. Quench .-> Q1
    S4 -. Rapid Cool .-> Q2["Bainite 400 to 250 C Non Equilibrium"]
    Q1 --> QE["Engineering Consequence HAZ Embrittlement in Welds"]
    Q2 --> QE
    S5 --> SE["Engineering Conference Desired Toughness in Hull"]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the eutectoid reaction in the iron-carbon diagram and state the temperature and composition at which it occurs.**

**Model Answer:**

The eutectoid reaction is an invariant three-phase transformation in which a single solid phase decomposes on cooling into two distinct solid phases of different compositions. In the Fe–C system, the reaction is:

$$
\gamma_{(0.77\%\,C)} \;\xrightarrow[\text{cool}]{\;727°C\;}\; \alpha_{(0.022\%\,C)} + Fe_3C
$$

- **Temperature:** 727 °C
- **Eutectoid composition:** 0.77 wt% C
- **Products:** Ferrite (α) and cementite (Fe₃C) in lamellar form (Pearlite)
- **Significance:** It is the *most important transformation in steel metallurgy*, governing the final microstructure and mechanical properties of all slowly cooled carbon-steel weldments in shipbuilding.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for reaction, 1 mark for T and C values)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Distinguish between hypoeutectoid and hypereutectoid steels with one example each from shipbuilding applications.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Hypoeutectoid Steel | Hypereutectoid Steel |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Carbon content | < 0.77 wt% C | 0.77 to 2.14 wt% C |
| Microstructure | Proeutectoid ferrite + Pearlite | Proeutectoid cementite + Pearlite |
| Phase at $A_1$ | α + γ | Fe₃C + γ |
| Example in shipbuilding | Hull plates (AH36, 0.18% C) | Marine shaft steel (0.85% C tool steel) |
| Mechanical behaviour | Soft, ductile, weldable | Hard, wear-resistant, less weldable |

*(3 marks: 2 marks for distinction, 1 mark for examples)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the iron-carbon phase diagram up to 6.67% C. Mark all critical points, phases, and invariant reactions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A carbon steel containing **0.50 wt% C** is slowly cooled from 1100 °C to room temperature. Calculate:
   (i) The mass fraction of proeutectoid ferrite at 727 °C. **(2 marks)**
   (ii) The mass fraction of pearlite at room temperature. **(2 marks)**
   (iii) The mass fraction of cementite in pearlite. **(3 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** The Fe–C diagram up to 6.67% C is plotted with temperature (°C) on the Y-axis and carbon content (wt%) on the X-axis. Key features:

- **Liquidus line** (ABCD): Above which the alloy is fully liquid.
- **Solidus line** (AHJECF): Below which the alloy is fully solid.
- **Peritectic reaction at 1493 °C** (point C/H/B): $L + \delta \to \gamma$
- **Eutectic reaction at 1147 °C** (point C): $L \to \gamma + Fe_3C$ (forms Ledeburite)
- **Eutectoid reaction at 727 °C** (point S): $\gamma \to \alpha + Fe_3C$ (forms Pearlite)
- **$A_3$ line (GS)**: γ → α boundary for hypoeutectoid steels
- **$A_{cm}$ line (ES)**: Fe₃C solubility in γ for hypereutectoid steels
- **Phases marked**: α (BCC ferrite), γ (FCC austenite), δ (BCC delta), Fe₃C (cementite), L (liquid)

*(Full sketch drawn by student; board valuation expects labelled axes, all three invariant reactions, and identification of α, γ, δ, L, Fe₃C regions.)*

**(b) Solutions:**

Given: $C_0 = 0.50$ wt% C, $C_\alpha = 0.022$ wt% C, $C_{eut} = 0.77$ wt% C, $C_{Fe_3C} = 6.67$ wt% C.

**(i) Proeutectoid ferrite at 727 °C:**

$$
W_\alpha^{pro} = \dfrac{0.77 - 0.50}{0.77 - 0.022} = \dfrac{0.27}{0.748} = 0.3610
$$

**Proeutectoid ferrite = 36.10%** *(2 marks)*

**(ii) Pearlite at room temperature:**

$$
W_P = \dfrac{0.50 - 0.022}{0.77 - 0.022} = \dfrac{0.478}{0.748} = 0.6390
$$

**Pearlite = 63.90%** *(2 marks)*

**(iii) Cementite in pearlite:**

Within pearlite, the ferrite-cementite lever rule applies at 727 °C (Pearlite composition = 0.77% C):

$$
W_{Fe_3C}^{in\,P} = \dfrac{0.77 - 0.022}{6.67 - 0.022} = \dfrac{0.748}{6.648} = 0.1125
$$

**Cementite in pearlite = 11.25%** *(3 marks)*

Total Fe₃C in steel = $0.6390 \times 0.1125 = 0.0719 = 7.19\%$ of total microstructure.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**

**(a)** Explain the **three invariant reactions** in the iron-carbon diagram with their temperatures, compositions, and products. Why is the eutectoid reaction the most important for shipbuilding weld microstructures? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A marine propeller shaft steel containing **0.85 wt% C** is austenitized at 900 °C and quenched in oil. Describe the resulting microstructure and explain why such a steel is generally **not used in as-welded ship hulls** but only in forged-and-heat-treated shaft applications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** The three invariant reactions:

| Reaction | Temperature | Composition | Equation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Peritectic | 1493 °C | 0.16% C | $L_{(0.53)} + \delta_{(0.09)} \to \gamma_{(0.16)}$ |
| Eutectic | 1147 °C | 4.30% C | $L_{(4.30)} \to \gamma_{(2.14)} + Fe_3C$ |
| Eutectoid | 727 °C | 0.77% C | $\gamma_{(0.77)} \to \alpha_{(0.022)} + Fe_3C$ |

The eutectoid reaction is most important in shipbuilding because:
1. All carbon steels used for hull plates (0.10–0.22% C) and structural sections lie in the hypoeutectoid region where the eutectoid governs the final microstructure.
2. The ferrite-pearlite ratio, grain size, and pearlite interlamellar spacing directly determine the **yield strength, ductility, and Charpy impact toughness** of welded joints.
3. The weld HAZ undergoes repeated eutectoid transformation on each thermal cycle, making it the **rate-controlling step** for HAZ microstructure evolution. *(7 marks: 2 marks each for three reactions, 1 mark for products/compositions/conditions/parameters combined, 1 mark for shipbuilding relevance)*

**(b)** On quenching 0.85% C austenite from 900 °C:

- The cooling rate is too fast for carbon diffusion, suppressing the eutectoid reaction.
- Austenite transforms to **martensite** (BCT, body-centered tetragonal) via a diffusionless shear mechanism.
- The microstructure is **as-quenched martensite + retained austenite**, very hard (~65 HRC) and brittle.
- $M_s \approx 200$ °C, $M_f \approx -50$ °C for this composition (Koistinen-Marburger relation).

Such a steel is unsuitable for as-welded hulls because:
- Martensitic HAZ is **highly susceptible to hydrogen-induced cracking (HIC)** and **cold cracking** — a catastrophic failure mode in marine service.
- The lack of ductility prevents plastic redistribution of residual welding stresses.
- Charpy impact toughness is far below the -40 °C requirement of marine classification societies (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS).
- Therefore, 0.85% C steel is used for **forged propeller shafts and pinions** that are **pre-machined, austenitized, oil-quenched, and tempered** to obtain tempered martensite (sorbite) with high strength + adequate toughness, *not* in welded hull structures. *(7 marks: 2 marks for microstructure, 2 marks for martensite properties, 2 marks for shipbuilding exclusion rationale, 1 mark for shaft application)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Gibbs Phase Rule:** Students often write $F = C - P + 2$ (for variable pressure), but at atmospheric pressure (constant), it is $F = C - P + 1$. KTU expects the *binary, constant-pressure* form.
> 2. **Wrong lever arm direction:** When the alloy composition is closer to the $\gamma$ phase boundary, the lever arm for $\alpha$ is the *longer* arm, not the shorter. Many students invert this. Draw the tie-line explicitly.
> 3. **Confusing $A_1$ with $A_3$:** $A_1$ = eutectoid horizontal (727 °C); $A_3$ = γ → α completion on heating (varies with C). Do NOT mark $A_3$ as a single horizontal line.
> 4. **Missing the peritectic reaction:** Many KTU students forget the peritectic at 1493 °C and only mention eutectic + eutectoid. Always mark all three invariant reactions.
> 5. **Cementite as a "line compound":** Cementite appears as a vertical line at 6.67% C because it has *fixed composition*. Marks are lost if students draw it as a region.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:**
> - [ ] **Five phases:** α (BCC, ferrite), γ (FCC, austenite), δ (BCC, delta-ferrite), Fe₃C (cementite), L (liquid).
> - [ ] **Three invariant reactions:** Peritectic (1493 °C, 0.16%), Eutectic (1147 °C, 4.30%), Eutectoid (727 °C, 0.77%).
> - [ ] **Key compositions to memorize:** 0.022, 0.77, 2.14, 4.30, 6.67 wt% C.
> - [ ] **Key temperatures:** 727, 1147, 1493, 1538 °C.
> - [ ] **Lever Rule:** $W_\alpha = (C_\gamma - C_0) / (C_\gamma - C_\alpha)$; $W_\gamma = (C_0 - C_\alpha) / (C_\gamma - C_\alpha)$.
> - [ ] **Pearlite** = eutectoid of α + Fe₃C; **Ledeburite** = eutectic of γ + Fe₃C.
> - [ ] **A-lines:** $A_1$ (eutectoid), $A_3$ (hypo upper), $A_{cm}$ (hyper upper), with c/r subscripts for heating/cooling.
> - [ ] **Marine relevance:** Ship hull steels are hypoeutectoid (≤0.22% C); HAZ transforms via eutectoid; rapid weld cooling produces brittle non-equilibrium phases (martensite, bainite) that must be avoided via preheat and PWHT.
> - [ ] **Gibbs Phase Rule** for binary at constant P: $F = 3 - P$.
> - [ ] **Curie temperature** (770 °C) marks magnetic transition in α-Fe; austenite is non-magnetic at all temperatures.
